comment_content:
The course is relatively easy and feels very similar to the HKDSE ICT curriculum — more like IT common sense than deep computer science. The actual lecture content is not very important for grades, but the lab software skills are directly tested.

Lecture topics:
Introduction, Hardware
Software
Software (cont.)
Internet & Mobile Computing
World Wide Web
Computers in Society
Graphics
Multimedia
Copyright & Internet Security
HTML
The Digital Age

Lab topics: Portable Apps & Google Drive, MIT App Inventor, Programming Basics, Android Game (App Inventor), GIMP, MS Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Video Editing.
comment_teaching:
Not that important, as mentioned lecture content are not relevant in grading, unless you are interested, you can just slack off till lecture is over.
Can ask Kennith or TA for help in projects, but ChatGPT or Gemini is your best bet.
Mostly Cantonese + English, chill and cool overall.
comment_workload:
Weekly Labs Assignment: straightforward, can be done remotely with detailed guidance.

(*Main Workload*)Project: build an app in MIT App Inventor + make a demo video. Manageable if teammates contribute; watch out for free-riders (keep contribution records for protection).

Quizzes (after each lecture): very easy, almost free marks, but remember to double check not to miss any.

Lecture activities: participation-based, small bonus but attendance required.

Overall: light to moderate workload, well distributed.
comment_grading:
Grade Weighting:
Lab Exam: 40%
Project: 30%
Lecture Activities: 10%
Quizzes: 10%
Labs: 10%
(+ small bonuses for project leader & participation)

My Grades:
Lecture activities: 1.8 / 3 bonus
Project: 23/30 (aesthetics), 8/10 (video aesthetics), +0.5 proposal bonus → ~31/40
Lab Exam: 76.74 / 100
Quizzes: 10/10
Labs: 9/10
Overall Grade: B+

Tips:
Lab Exam is very manageable if you prepare (Excel + GIMP/Photoshop + App Inventor).
Be careful with project groups — report free-riders in peer evaluation and always keep records.
Bonus marks (project leader, lecture participation) can slightly boost your grade.

Overall, grading is generous, if you wish to score an A range, remember to put in effort!
I slacked off to much during lab exam revision, which got me a B+.

comment_content: The course is very beginner friendly, and discusses really basic things. Basic programming principles, general knowledge about computers. Can be boring if you already know some things, but it's not hard.
comment_teaching: Unlike other classes, the lectures aren't used to go over the slides. Look over the notes beforehand, and the actual classroom time is for application activities in a group (ugh).
comment_workload: Workload is manageable, just scope the final project so you don't accidentally overwhelm yourself. As long as you cover the items in the requirements, you don't have to go above and beyond to get a decent grade.
comment_grading:
Grading is fair: multiple choice quizzes about every-lecture-ish based on the lecture slides. If you miss lecture, there are videos. The project is not hard, and uses very simple programming. Make sure to read the rubric and fulfill all the requirements! Final is reasonable. Read the questions!!!

imo labs are useless and are skippable. save your time for better things (like studying for other classes) and just read over the information later.

comment_content: Content covers a few basic useful software like GIMP, Word, PPT, app inventor, excel, etc. You should already know some of these software. In the final lab exam, you can use photoshop instead of GIMP.
comment_teaching: ok ge
comment_workload: not much workload, a bit busy near the project deadline, but less workload overall.
comment_grading: Not sure about the grading, but since most student can get almost full marks on the quizzes (as those are open book and have no time limit), so all it matters is the final lab exam which test your knowledge on three software and the app inventor group project.

Lec:
Attendance will be counted

1. Introduction, Hardware

It is divided into two parts. The first is the mc questions (some may appear in some quizzes). The second one is about the lab e.g. Microsoft Word, PPT or Excel etc.

You need to find other students to work in groups of three. In the project, you have to invent an app and make a video to demonstrate your app.

Lab:
There is a lab section every week. MIT App Inventor, Microsoft Word, Microsoft Excel, GIMP etc. will be taught in the labs.

Quiz:
A simple quiz before every lecture
comment_teaching: The professor has a good accent and you can hear every word he says clearly. The teaching method is also very interesting. There is always some activity in the lectures. At the beginning of the lecture, you will be assigned to a group to compete against other groups. If your group becomes one of the winners, you will receive bonus points (which may be important for your grade).

Project:
The project is the biggest workload in this course.

Lab:
The labs can be quite time-consuming sometimes but it is also a good practice for your exam.

comment_content:
Some mc questions about DSE ICT content.

Hardware, Software, Internet, Mobile Computing, World Wide Web, Computers in Society, Graphics, Multimedia, CopyRight, HTML, Digital Age.

MIT App inventor, Excel, GIMP, Word, Powerpoint are the main testing part in the final exam.

There are some quizzes before the lecture ( do it at home not  during lecture ) and they are quite easy if you have studied DSE ICT before. Even if you don't, you can use chatgpt and the lecture powerpoint to figure out all the answers easily.
comment_teaching:
Good teaching.
Lecture activities are funny.
comment_workload: Some wordload. You need to work harder on the group project if you want a higher grade. The final exam is not that hard but make sure you have enough time to finish all of the questions. You can not skip any lectures because it counts the lecture activites.

It is an easy course so not that hard to get a good garde if you work harder on the project and exam.

comment_content:
very simple topics
helps you to gain some common tech sense and excel regularly used software and functions
basically ace everything if you are familiar with daily used tech/studied ICT
(I give B in content as it might be too easy for some students)

topics:
[labs]
-create & use Gmail, google doc, sheet, slide, form
-use Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint
-GIMP (free Photoshop)
-App inventor

[final exam]
bunch of MC question about topics taught in lecture or before lecture videos
the move on the lab questions, in which you recreate stuff like well organized excel sheet, word document format, ppt template and format, picture modified with GIMP

[lectures]
honestly, I don't remember anything at this point. they teach things like copyright, evolution of tech (from punch card to USB), simple html coding (copy n paste with little modification on numbers and stuff). just read the slides n do every before-lecture quizzes and you'll be fine in final
comment_teaching: one of the most-friendly, fun professor I've ever seen. feels like my secondary school teacher back then. at the end of every lecture, he was surrounded by students answering all the questions regarding learning material, assignments or project (or maybe making small talks idk). awesome teaching. although the videos watched before lecture can be a little boring, it's not a big deal. either watch them in 2x or scan through the slides or google the answers for the quizzes
comment_workload:
personally, I feel like this is the best course one can take for good grade while having some time to relax

-(almost) every week, you'll have to watch some video(s) about that week's topic and then finish a little quiz before lecture with just a few questions
multiple but limited attempts are allowed
-also, there're labs that one may complete alone by simply following the instructions. lab sessions do not require attendance yet can be useful if you come across with any issue that you wanna ask (which never happen to me. I asked my frd instead coz the tasks r real simple)
-the project is about creating your own app/game in App inventor, which can be simple as long as you fulfill every requirement. basically, add all the functions you are told to add (those functions r mostly taught in class/lab), add some appealing graphics, make a decent short video and try to get along with your teammates (and submit on time). not too difficult
comment_grading:
[in-class activity bonus]
but it can be pretty hard to get the bonus score in class activities if you don't have great groupmates as it's quite competitive and relies on both presentation and tech skills

[before-class quizzes, lab work, project, final]
no complain
MC questions in canvas so no grading problem
didn't release almost every score like project, final
got an A+ anyway

comment_content:
I have no DSE ICT background.

The lecture content is self-learning, you need to read it and finishing a quiz with 5 MCQ before the leature.

There is lecture activities in lecture instead of reading ppt, you will get into a group and finishing some task together (if you are shy and not that able(like me), you will need good luck and good groupmate), the fastest groups will get bouns (not affecting the grade of others, but will increase your total mark)

Project: 3 person per group, developing a Android App using MIT App Inventor.(incluing the app, and a video to introduce the app)

Lab: Can finish at home and just hand it in. Follow the instruction to finish the task with some basic functions of Microsoft Word, Excel, Powerpoint, GIMP, MIT App Inventor.

Exam: MCQ + Microsoft Word/ Excel/ Powerpoint/ GIMP/ MIT App Inventor
For refernce, we have 25MCQ + Excel, GIMP, and MIT App Inventor this semeter
The MCQ is easy, you just need to read the note before the exam and remember some important points (even though I am poor at remembering, I can answer the questions with only a few hours of studying the note)
comment_teaching: As mentioned before, there are lecture activities in lectures instead of reading a PowerPoint. Some lecture activities are interesting if you are not shy. For the content, just read the lecture note before the lecture.
comment_workload: One lab per week, one Quizzes per week(online quizzes, no time limit, can hand in twice(other set of questions)), one project for the course(Android App)

comment_grading:

But, this course does not conflict with comp1021, so if you are an engine student, I strongly advise you to take it in the first year
Prof Leung said his grading is generous, but I cannot judge with the absence of data

project:40%
final exam:30%
lab:10%
attendence:10%
quiz on canvas:10%
Bonus competition on lecture: at most 3%
Bonus-project proposal: 0.5%

comment_content:
The course covers 10 topics:

1. Introduction, Hardware
2. Software
3. Software (con'd)
4. Internet, Mobile Computing
5. World Wide Web
6. Computers in Society
7. Graphics
8. Multi-media
9. HTML
10. Digital Age

These topics are just providing basic knowledge of different aspects, and even you don't know any about computer before, it should be fine for you to catch up.

You also had to attend labs, which covered softwares like MIT App Inventor, GIMP, Word, Excel and Powerpoint. Only MIT App Inventor and GIMP would be a bit difficult for first time users.
comment_teaching: The course used blended learning mode, which means you had to watch the lecture videos before the lecture time. During lecture time, the professor would have some recap of the videos and then ask students to break into groups to finish some in-class practice. It is good for those who enjoy self-pace learning, as you can choose the speed of the videos and what time you want to watch the videos.
comment_workload: For a common core course, this course has much bigger workload, especially for the project part. The project part requires 3 people forming a group and write a program from scratch. So, if you find a bad teammates (e.g. people who doesn't know anything about coding/ people who are demanding), you will have much bigger workload. But out of the project, the workload for other part of the course should be fine.
comment_grading:
Exam: 30%
Project: 40%
Lecture activities: 40%
Quizzes (done after watching the lecture videos) : 10%
Labs: 10%

Lecture activities, quizzes and labs are parts that are quite easy to get high marks as usually extremely detail steps are given and you just need to put small effort to finish them.

Project is the toughest part of the course. Though some marking is provided, some marks are given on subjective basis, so you may need to work harder than expected to get desire score.

Exam is divided into two parts. The first part is mc questions on lecture videos. As handwritten cheat sheet is allowed, this part should be fine for well prepared person. The second part is practical exam, which test your skills to use the softwares taught in labs. As you only need to choose 2 questions out of 4, you can avoid those that are less familiar with.

Also, the prof. will give bonus mark (max. 3 points) for those participate in class or done well in in-class practice.

comment_content:
主要教基本電腦知識,例如電腦硬體和軟件,Microsoft Word, PowerPoint , Excel 和一些編程知識例如 Scratch 和 MIT App Inventor

Lecture: 每一次課堂最後都有小組活動, 要同組員一齊完成任務, 做得最好個五組(有時全部)有分加, 會計入總成績。如果你扲到三分, 會升一個等級。

TA會根據你對小組活動的責獻而對你的出席率進行評分。就算有出席課堂, 但咩都冇做, 出席分=0。

Assignment:
每星期有一份測驗, 主要考番上堂內容。頭兩次測驗可以比你做五次, 之後只可以做兩次, 計最高分嗰次。建議同其他人一齊做。

Labs:
在特定星期有lab 課堂, 比你去實踐Word,Powerpoint, Excel, GIMP, MIT App Inventor。每堂都有功課, 滿足所有要求, 就能扲滿分

盡量不要有任何錯失  , 每次Lab task個平均分都接近滿分

Group Project:
三至四人一組, 每組要用MIT App Inventor 去造一個程式和一條介紹影片。

考試
沒有Midterm
只有大考, 範圍是所有教過的課題
大考有分兩部份,第一部份是 多項選擇題,  第二部份有四條,但只叫你選其中兩條問題( Powerpoint, Gimp,. Word, MiT APP inventor)。 如果你完成所有問題, 只會計最高分嗰兩個。                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        

This course mainly teaches basic computer and coding knowledge such as computer hardware, software and coding like Scratch and MIT app inventor.

Lecture:
There is a group activity at the end of each lecture. You need to finish some tasks with your groupmates. Professor/TA will give the best 5 or 10( sometimes all) groups a bonus point. If you get 3 bonus points, a sub-grade will be increased.

TA will take your attendance in every lecture by considering your contribution in group activity. If you do nothing in lecture activity, you will get zero mark in attendance.


Assignments:
You need to finish a assignment every week, just helps you to recall the content mentioned in previous lecture. The allowed attempts in first two quizzes are 5 and then the following attempts are 2 only. It will count the highest score in the attempts. You are suggested to do it with other classmates.



Labs:
There is a lab lesson on specific weeks. You will practice basic coding skills in Scratch and MIT App Inventor and Word, excel, PowerPoint. After the lesson, you need to finish all tasks. If you fulfil all requirement, you can get full marks.

Please do not make any mistake because the mean of each lab task is nearly full.


Project:
There is a group project which requires you to make a software using MIT App inventor with your groupmates. 3-4 people per group. Besides, each group need to submit a video introducing the app.

Exam:
No midterm exam
Final exam: It can allow you to use a two-sided paper as cheat sheet and the final exam covers all topics. It is mainly divided into two parts.
The first part is 20 MC questions, the second part is about PowerPoint, Excel, GIMI and Word practicing. I only need to finish two tasks. If I finish more than two tasks, it will only count the best two tasks.

comment_content:
The course starts with easy and common sense computer stuff like Microsoft Word, Excel and Power Point. However, if you think everything will be so easy in this course, you are about to fall in bad grade.

Things will suddenly get complicated when the course requires you to develop an Android App and learn everything about GIMP. The lectures just teach you very simple however unhelpful knowledge about MIT App inventor so you need to spend much time self-studying about how to use more functions in MIT when you are really trying to develop the App project.

The GIMP is also something SUCKED that seems easy to use but hard to be pro on that. (In Chinese: 易學難精) The lectures just teach you how to use basic function like adjusting brightness or add filter to photos, but the exam needs you to do complicated stuff like background removing and layers editing. If you don't have much time or passion for practice on software using, better not chose this course.
comment_teaching: The professor is a funny guy and his English accent is good and understandable. He was very kind that he was willing to postpone the project submission deadline.
comment_workload:
It depends on whether your group have freerider.

Apart from the group project, you need to finish a quiz after self-studying some Power Point slides and hand in the lab work every week (the most torturing part is you need to get full mark on each of them).

AND you need time to familiarize each function of the software they taught if you want to get good mark in final. So I think the workload is really subjective and depends on what grade you want to get.
comment_grading:
This course requires you to do a group project (occupy 40% of the total score, while final just occupies 30%) so you should pray for having good teammates otherwise you won't have any good grade.

I messed up with my final and only got 76% score. (The final contains 20 MC questions for 40% mark and you need to do 3 task out of 4 options by using GIMP/Excel/Word/PPT for 60% mark)

And luckily no freerider in my group project so I got 92.2% of project score. Overall 88% and got B+.

So you need to be prepared that obtaining an A range will be difficult.

comment_teaching: The professor implements this "flipped classroom" approach in his teaching of this course. Materials were given before lessons and students are required to understand the topic and complete lecture quizzes before attending the lecture. In lectures, instead of teaching, activities were given to students to complete. Bonuses would be given to groups that win in these activities.
comment_workload:
Workload of the course highly depends on the project.

The project involves making an app using App Inventor and producing a video describing the app. If you have lower standards for this course, or if you have really good group mates who are good at programming, then the workload will not be heavy.

Details of project workload:
- Project proposal
- Project proposal voting and forming project groups (yes, these can earn you marks)
- Project submission
- Video presentation submission
- Inter and intra group evaluation

Lecture activities are not hard.

Lecture quizzes have multiple attempts and answers are easily found in power-points.

For labs, there are clear instructions as to how you should complete the tasks. The labs are not hard and can usually be completed in an hour. There were 10 labs in total.
comment_grading:
The grading is fine.

Labs: 10% (6.5/7)
Quizzes: 10% (10/10)
Lecture activities (Attendance!!): 10% (3% bonus max) (9/10)
Exam: 30% (see below)
Project: 40% (99.05/100) (see "workload" for details)

Exam consists of two parts:
1. MC Questions (40 Q) (40/40)
- Open book, all questions can be found in the power-points
2. Practical questions (3 out of 4 Q, tasks involving Microsoft Office tools and GIMP) (59.23/60)
- Excel questions are difficult for those who did not take DSE ICT, an advantage otherwise
- Kind of lengthy

A+

comment_content:
The content of this course it not too difficult.

If you have taken DSE, this course is basically just a summary of ICT without some boring and outdated stuff. If you get 5 or above, definitely take this course as it's very easy.

This course also teaches you practical stuff, like Word, PowerPoint, Excel and GIMP.

There are two parts in the final this semester, In the lab part of the exam, you can choose 3 questions to answer. Each question cover each practical software mentioned above.

In the multiple choices part, there are like 40 questions which you can all find the answers in all the lecture PowerPoint. Since it's an open notes exam it's pretty easy to get high score if you are careful. You have to download all the PowerPoint before the exam as they will lock all access to them on canvas. I advised you combine all slides into one big pdf and use ctrl+F (or command+F) to search for the keyword.
comment_teaching:
The prof basically doesn't teaches the PowerPoint content during the lecture. You either have to watch the per-recorded videos released alongside with the slides or just study the content of the PowerPoint in your own time.

Personally I go through it once every time I did the quiz (which was basically assignments that you have to do every week) and I think it's adequate already.

The prof did a really great job at getting us to interact during the lectures. Most of the lectures are just group discussion and work and those who completed the task will be given bonus score. More details in the grading section.
comment_workload:
Pretty light workload. There are quiz every week which you have to complete before the next lecture. They only have 5 MCQs for each quiz. Takes you like 15 mins max to complete. The quiz is done with your own free time so you are free to refer to your notes and take as much time as you need.

The heaviest workload is labs probably and they are not even that bad. Basically there are one lab every week or so (sometimes you don't have it). You could join the lab session and ask for help from TA. You could stay in there for 1-2 hours and finish it and submit it immediately so you don't need to worry about forgetting week for the rest of the week. They are pretty easy, and are all about those practical software I have mentioned (Word, PowerPoint, Excel and GIMP.). Be sure to meet all the requirements mentioned in the Lab PowerPoint and ask for clarification if you are not sure. You don't want to miss too many score with the lab.

comment_content:
COMP1001 is an introduction to computers and computing tools. It introduces the organization and basic working mechanism of a computer system, including the development of the trend of modern computer system. It covers the fundamentals of computer hardware design and software application development. The course emphasizes the application of the state-of-the-art software tools to solve problems and present solutions via a range of skills related to multimedia and internet computing tools such as internet, e-mail, WWW, webpage design, computer animation, spread sheet charts/figures, presentations with graphics and animations, etc. The course also covers business, accessibility, and relevant security issues in the use of computers and Internet.

Outline of this course:

comment_content:
A beginner course on basic computer skills and concepts. The labs focus on practical skills that can be quite useful in daily life, like Google Drive, Microsoft Word, Powerpoint, Excel, AppInventor and GIMP (photo editing). The lectures cover topics such as simple game/app programming, internet security concepts,  computer software and hardware, computer graphics and HTML. My most memorable lecture was the one on search engines. Since it is Professor Leung's research interest, he was able to provide great insight into the topic.

Content is mainly factual and not hard to understand. It should be very easy to pick up if you have any background in ICT or technology. I personally think the syllabus is very close to the ICT DSE one, so if you took ICT before it should be pretty easy to handle. Might not learn much new content though.

There's also a group project that requires you to make a mobile app and film a video for it. It's not difficult, but can be time consuming.

Generally a simple and fun course.
comment_teaching:
This course adopts sort of a flip classroom mode, you have to watch pre-recorded lecture videos at home before the lectures and complete very simple online quizzes (5 questions). During the actual lecture, the professor rarely teaches and uses most of the time for lecture activities. Attendance is counted and the activities grant bonuses so it is very important to go to the lectures.

The class is divided into about 10 large groups (pre determined, you can't form your own) and each group works together to complete lecture activities. They can be stressful sometimes as time is limited and you need to fight for the bonus points (0.5 each, up to 3%). Tasks included block programming, typing HTML, presentations etc.

Professor speaks good English, and is nice and approachable. He is very laid back and tries really hard to make the lectures fun, so the atmosphere is usually quite good. He is also willing to answer questions after class. However, he doesn't really teach much during class and sometimes rushes through the lecture activities, without ensuring that most of the class has grasped the concepts. For example, during lecture activities, since only one person needs to complete the activity for the whole group to get a bonus, he only focused on the person who completed the task while most of the groupmates had to catch up on their own. His teaching style isn't really the best you can ask for, but he is very clear when he teaches in class or in videos so that's already decent for me.

The lab TA doesn't teach especially well but he isn't bad either. He manages to deliver most of the content he needs to and it is not hard for people to follow.
comment_workload:
A bit on the heavier side, but not really that stressful. You just need to devote time to complete it.

During the term (before project), the course is pretty chill except you have to do online quiz. Only one lecture per week and one lab (which you can skip). If you do the labs by yourself, most of them take less than half an hour.

Project can technically be done in 1-2 days I think, if you really rush through it. Depending on the scale of the app, it can take way longer to complete. Filming and editing the video can also be time consuming. Also, it depends on your groupmates and the app proposal you choose to work on. Very important to find good groupmates!!!!

Workload is insane during the lecture though omg I was so stressed every week during the lecture activities if you're like me and not used to interactive lessons please beware

Overall good course though thank you Kenneth it's been a lot of fun :))))
comment_grading:
Most people say it is a good grade course. I'm not sure about that, but I can say that the grading feels quite fair.

This course is one of the less transparent ones I've taken. The professor hasn't provided any sort of statistics on the mean or SD of the project or the exams. The statistics of the MC part of the exam, labs and online quizzes can be seen from Canvas, but they are not of much significance.

Grading:
Exam: 30%
Project: 40%
Lecture activities: 10%
Quizzes: 10%
Labs: 10%

Online quizzes are often very easy and most people get 0.4-0.5 (out of 0.5) every time. They can't really differentiate the students. It is easy to get full marks for labs as long as you bother to bring your work to the lab for the TA to check. If you let him check in person, and he finds out that you made mistakes, you can change it at the lab until you get full marks. If you're confident you can do it perfectly at home (from my experience, most people find it easy to follow the tasks), you can submit your work via Canvas and you don't even need to show up to the lab.

The exam is conducted in the lab, and consists of two parts: MC and practical. I personally found the exam MC quite easy (I studied for a day before the exam). There were 40 questions and according to Canvas the max score is 39. Practical is a bit hard, you get to choose 2 from 4 questions (GIMP photo editing, Word, Excel, Powerpoint). I chose Word and Powerpoint because I found them the easiest. The difficulty of the Excel question was a bit ridiculous in my opinion. Time limit was 2 hours for both parts together, a bit tight.

Project grade is mainly made up of technical score and aesthetic scores. For technical scores, you get them as long as you complete that task. For aesthetics, you really need to work hard and see what the professor likes because he really cares about aesthetics lol

Didn't really study hard, but I had to make sure I completed all the components and tried to complete a decent project. I got 38/40 for exam MC, 6.95/7 for labs, full for quizzes and attendance plus 3% bonus. Others unknown. Finally got an A+. I think in this course hard work usually pays off.

comment_grading:
上堂既時候係做Lecture Activity，每一堂都係，可能一開頭會略略重溫返嗰個星期既Lecture Notes，之後再分組做Activity，分組唔會變，由第一堂到最後一堂都係同一堆人。每次落堂前都會揀做得最好嗰幾組，有0.5%bonus towards final grade，咁成個Sem大概有十堂既Lecture Activity左右啦，如果拎到起碼6個Bonus（3%），就直接升一個Subgrade。

以下係我既Data畀大家參考下：
Lecture Quiz（11份，5題每題0.1分）：5.3 /5.5
Lab（7個）：6.5 /7
Exam MC：30 /40（Highest: 35 Mean: 27 Lowest: 7）
Bonus：3%拎晒直接升一個Subgrade
至於Exam第2 Part、Project Proposal、整既App同附上既Video就唔知幾多分（我地係整遊戲既），Exam我係做Word同Excel，基本上應該接近滿分（好簡單，冇咩錯既空間而且一定夠時間）。而我地亦都可以睇得到成班既Proposal同App，比較下我地個App同條片都整得唔錯。

Final grade：A+
可能本身只係拎到A，但因為升一個Subgrade最後A+
同組既Groupmate一個都係A+，另一個B+，大家都升咗一個Subgrade既，Project既分應該都一樣，所以決定性既位應該係Exam。

So... consider if you want to study this course. (If you like writing app, this course will be your very first course to start with! Good luck!)
comment_grading:
Grading consists a lot of stuff... Project, final exam, lecture quiz (doing at home), lab (doing at home/school also is okay). And bonus marks will be given out in the lecture activities, so make sure that you firstly attend the lesson, then perform well with your group mates! Group mates are randomly assigned (not the project one), but luckily, I was paired up with good group mates. I think those bonus marks are also important!

Repeatedly, the project is the core of this course! You have to use your own knowledge to write an app and shoot a video with your group mates. For me, I have 0 knowledge in writing app! So I have to watch some videos to learn, and ask professor Leung & TA to give me some help. Otherwise, it is difficult to finish the app in time. Maybe our project is good and video is funny (professor Leung like funny things), I got an A at the end.

comment_content: This lecture covers basic IT knowledge, which is easy to follow. The lab teaches how to use some computer software such as Microsoft Word, Excel and GIMP. Students have to learn to use "MIT App Inventor" and design an app.
comment_teaching: Very interactive, class activities are quite interesting. The instructor is quite passionate, and always willing to help.
comment_workload:
Pre-lecture Canvas Exercise every week
Lab every week (Easy, you don't have to attend if you can finish by yourself)
No Midterm
A project that requires a group of 3 to create an app, also to make a video introducing the app. Making the app can be very tough.
Final consists of 2 parts. The first part is MC, which is not very difficult if you revised everything once. The second part requires you to finish some tasks using software taught in class. As the tasks are much difficult than the lab exercise, a lot of students found it difficult. Especially for Excel and GIMP, it is not easy. Also time is very limited.
comment_grading: I would say the grading is very good. My in-class group got all the bonus that we can get. Scored full marks in all labs, mostly full marks in pre-lecture exercise. Not sure about the project, but I guess my group did well. I can finish most tasks on the Final Exam. Got an A+.

comment_content: Very easy. For application, you only need to know about Microsoft Office and GIMP. For exam questions, you just need to memorize some main points in the lecture ppt. Those questions are similar to those in the quizzes.
comment_teaching: Good. A very friendly professor. The lecture is quite interesting as there will be lecture activities for you to get bonus marks. The lecture activities will be done in a form of a group. The professor and TAs always answer our questions and clarify some misconceptions for us.
comment_workload: Quite heavy for the project (Making an android app using MIT App Inventor). But for the others it is ok. You will have 1 online quizzes on canvas before each lecture. For the lab, it is not necessary to attend (The TA will not teach you anything but just sitting there to answer your questions). You can submit the lab work through canvas.
comment_grading: Quite good. I got A-. I have handed in all the labs and I got 94% for the quizzes. However, I do not know the grading of my final exam and project. I did not finish my final exam, with 1 task half finished. (The professor said nobody can finish the exam) And I think my project did quite well.

comment_grading:

comment_content:
Content, I believe is quite interesting. I wouldn't say it is entirely useful and valuable. It focuses on the general knowledge related to computers. But it does not help with any other computer courses you will probably take in HKUST (programming courses).

You do spend some hours doing Scratch, but because the content is not dealt in exams, most people do not have the opportunity to learn/understand basic programming. But otherwise, you will be given an opportunity to learn Excel, powerpoint, word, video editing, photoshop etc...
comment_teaching:
Not a common type of teaching/lecture style. You are required to watch video lectures outside of class, take a quiz and do lecture activities during the lectures.

The quizzes are not entirely easy. Some questions are quite challenging and vague so sometimes you need to get lucky.

The lecture activities are to give extra credits for the final score. However, most students get the full 3 points, so the bonus points do not help you a lot getting a higher letter grade.

The video lectures just read the powerpoint material... so yeah the teaching I wouldn't give full credit for.
comment_workload:
Not much workload, only when you have the project and when you have to study for the final exams, there's a lot of work to do. However, the exam takes place earlier than other exams --> you have more time to study for other exams.

If you are looking for a programming course, this is not the course to take. But if you need to take a COMP course and are not confident taking another COMP course, I guess this is the only option you have.
comment_grading:
The professor does not provide the final scores, so I don't know what the average score is and what my project score is. I prefer grading to be transparent but some reason most of the scores (over 50% are done at the end of the semester and students don't have access to them).

I don't think the grading is lenient. The quizzes are not easy to get 100%, the final exam is not easy (Excel part especially).

comment_teaching:
Instead of the traditional lecture style where you sit and listen the whole time, you'll do in-class activities during "lecture" time. So you're expected to watch the pre-lesson videos (~lecture) and do a quiz before attending the lesson since the activities are related to the content. He'll sometimes review some important concepts before starting the activity.

The lab work is quite helpful for the exam, it's best you know/understand/remember what you're doing since it'll be in the practical part of the exam. Some labs are optional but can be helpful for your project. You don't have to do it during lab session, you can send it if you want but it's safer to show up and show it to them so you won't risk mark deduction

The prof and TA are helpful and nice. Overall, the course was enjoyable, easy and useful in my opinion.

Exam has MC and practical part. Best to practice Excel and GIMP, it's where most get stuck/confused apparently

I'm not sure if attendance actually contributes to the grade

comment_workload:
Quizzes and Lab work should not be a concern as they are easy and not troublesome.
However, the group project which requires students to make an app with MIT inventor will take up to two weeks of your time depending on your level of desire to get good grade.
comment_grading: The biggest contribution to the final grade is the final exam as most students get very high score in other areas. Be advised that the application part of the final exam is extremely difficult. You need to have high level of proficiency in photoshop/excel to get high grade.

comment_content:
Mainly covers basic introduction to computers (hardware) and computing tools (basic and commonly used softwares). Materials covered were quite boring and only some sessions were fun.

There is a project which is to design an Android app by using MIT App Inventor. The grouping for the project is free and better take it with at least a friend. Bear in mind that this course is an exclusion to ISOM 2010.
comment_teaching:
The teaching was fine and the teaching assistant (Katherine) was very helpful. The lectures are usually divided into two sessions, first half is materials review (since students are expected to read or listen to the recorded lecture before coming to class) and second half is activity session, in which students are divided into groups and there are some tasks that should be finished within the time given.

The lab sessions were not so useful, but if you are not quite familiar with the materials, coming to the lab might help. Basically in the lab, there's a task that you have to finish within that week and in the lab the TA will give some hints on how to do that task. Some materials were quite hard that you need to come and they can sometimes just give the answer directly.
comment_workload: Workload was quite easy but there is a quiz every week that needs around 10-30 minutes to finish. The project complexity depends on the proposal chosen. Final exam questions were quite easy and the weight of the practical exam is higher than the MC exam, so be prepared with the practical exam (covers materials from lab).
comment_grading:
The grading was good, the contents are easy to follow. But you have to be careful in the quizzes, because sometimes the answer to some particular questions cannot be found in the powerpoint given beforehand. Or, the questions are just tricky.

Final exam statistics: Mean=70.10, Max=92, SD=13.96
Lab=10%, Lecture activities/attendance=10%, Project=45%, Final exam=30%, Quizzes=5%

In the activity session in lectures, the instructor sometimes give bonus point(s) to the group that can finish the task faster. It can sometimes be unfair and inconvenience for non-local students because if the group contains more locals than non-locals, they usually discuss things in Cantonese that the non-local students cannot help or even join in.

comment_content: Contents are okay, midway in the course things might become more difficult, but general things are okay. One thing worth noting is the quiz's answers are sometimes not in the powerpoint and google might be required.
comment_teaching: The teaching was pretty good, IMO very enjoyable. The professor records his lectures in a video and expects you to to listen to it and read the slides before going to class, but he will also wrap things up during lectures. The lectures are interactive lessons where he will give you tasks and give you credits (for those who work the quickest) which boost your final grade. This system is at times unfair since at times there are people who already knew the content in advance in the class but otherwise the course is pretty enjoyable.
comment_workload: Some find the workload pretty heavy as there are always quiz and projects but personally i think it worth it since the two worth more than the final exams (no mid term).
comment_grading:
Grading is okay. Only thing that bothers me the credits they professor gives since you can free-ride if someone in your group has the understanding advance.
If you work hard enough in projects you will most probably get full marks. For exams, if you bother to revise legitimately, you will be fine although the mean seems to be high.

Final grade A

comment_content:
I would not recommend this course for people. One does not learn anything out of this course, and the grading is awful. COMP 1021 or any other programming-related course is way better!
The content was quite simple, describing about the parts of a computer, MS Office, Google Drive, etc. As long as you have used a computer, you will have no problem with the content.
The labs were held once a week and taught about the basics of Google Drive, GIMP, Movie Maker, Word, Excel, and Powerpoint. The materials of the lab were released a day before the session and you could finish it before the lab. You could come to the lab just for attendance. However, the exam required more than the amount learnt in the lab.
There is a group project (group of 3) on designing a website. For this one will have to submit a proposal (a Word Document) on the work distribution, and present the website (a Presentation before the deadline). Remember to choose your group wisely!
comment_teaching: The teaching was alright. It was focussed on activities that help understand the concepts taught in the lecture slides. Surprise quizzes would be held, so it is necessary to read the lecture slides before attending the lectures.
comment_workload:
The workload would depend on the group members of the project. Try to avoid the free-riders.
It is manageable for the lectures and the lab tasks.
comment_grading:
The grading is Horrible. It is really hard to get a good grade.
There is only one exam, usually around Week 10 of the semester. The exam consists on the things learnt from Word, Excel, Powerpoint, GIMP, Movie Maker, and the lectures.  However, it is super hard. They ask to complete complicated tasks in Word, excel, PowerPoint, and GIMP, and answer 40 MCs on the lecture slides in only two hours.
Despite working hard on the project, the Professor and the TAs do not give good scores.
Attendance to the lectures were necessary, as surprise quizzes would be held on the lecture slides. One could also get bonus marks by doing the tasks in the lectures.

comment_content: The content is mainly the basic python programming knowledge, if you have learnt coding and programming before, it would be easy to handle, but i think with assistance of AI, there should be not difficulty on understanding the code. AI can handle all the content in this course. Topics are variable, type, for loop, while loop, recursion, class, oop.
comment_teaching: The teaching is fair, if you like to attend the lecture, go for it, COMP department professors are quite good at teaching, but if you have learnt all the concept of basic python programming, it would be a little bit necessary, since everything is easy, you can just search online or ask AI, i think that would be more efficient for learning. Remember practice is the key to success. Don't just read the lecture note and think you know everything, it doesn't work. At least type something by yourself.
comment_workload: The workload is fair, about 1 PA a week, the PA is easy to finish, just about the topic that you learn that week, nothing special, just remember to do it and everything would get full marks on that usually.
comment_grading: The grading is fair, since most of the student taking this course is from school of engineering, if you have no coding experience, it would be one the of biggest disadvantage by comparing to other student. But i think the key is to be careful enough, the content is easy but the exam content can be tricky. Understanding what the question requires you to implement is important, you can treat the exam as english reading exam, lots of words. Be aware of your time management. You may not able to finish the exam if you struggle in one particular questions, especially recursion.

comment_content: The course provides a broad introduction to Computer Science by covering multiple areas such as game programming, web programming, user interface design, and computer graphics. The variety of topics keeps the course interesting and helps students understand how computing can be applied in different contexts. Using Python as the main programming language makes the content accessible for beginners while still allowing meaningful problem-solving.
comment_teaching: The teaching approach is generally clear and structured, making complex ideas easier to understand for students new to computer science. Concepts are usually explained alongside practical examples, which helps connect theory to practice. The pace is suitable for non-CS majors, although some topics could benefit from deeper explanations or more examples.
comment_workload: The workload is reasonable for an introductory course. Assignments require time and effort, especially for students with little programming background, but they are manageable with consistent work. Overall, the workload aligns well with the learning objectives and expectations of the course.
comment_grading: Grading is mainly based on programming assignments and assessments that reflect the material taught in class. The grading criteria are mostly clear, and the assessments are fair for students who consistently keep up with the coursework. Performance is rewarded more through effort and practice rather than prior programming experience.

comment_content: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to Python programming, starting from basic syntax and control structures (if–else statements, loops, functions) and gradually moving on to data structures such as lists, tuples, and dictionaries. The use of Turtle and PyGame libraries helps students understand graphical programming, event handling, and basic UI concepts, which makes the learning process more engaging. More advanced topics, including recursion, file input/output, and basic object-oriented programming, are briefly introduced toward the end. While not all Python features are covered, the scope is appropriate for an introductory-level course.
comment_teaching: Lectures are conducted entirely in English, using relatively simple language, although the speaking pace can be fast at times. The quality of teaching materials is high, especially the lecture PowerPoint slides, which are clear, well-organized, and closely aligned with the examinable content. The course website is well maintained and updated weekly. Lab instructors explain concepts step by step and often demonstrate code directly, which is particularly helpful for students with little or no programming background.
comment_workload: The overall workload is moderate and manageable. Most learning comes from reviewing lecture slides and understanding the provided code examples rather than extensive memorization. Labs are held weekly and are mostly ungraded, but they are useful for practicing core concepts and preparing for assignments. As long as students keep up with the weekly materials and do not leave assignments until the last minute, the time commitment required for this course is reasonable.
comment_grading: Grading is based on a mid-term exam, a final exam, and two assignments. Exams are open-book but still require a solid understanding of program structure, syntax, and data structures, especially list operations. Assignments are generally graded fairly and generously if the required functionality is correctly implemented. Overall, the grading scheme is reasonable, and students who consistently follow the course materials and practice coding should be able to achieve good results.

comment_content:
If you have any coding background and are not required to take COMP 1023 and want a good grade, this course is definitely a worth-considering choice.

This course teaches Python from the basics, covering different types of variables, different types of loops and libraries (random, turtle, calendar, matplotlib).

Neither the lecture nor the tutorial takes attendance; you can decide to go or not. The tutorial is just a Zoom meeting during which the TAs will answer your questions about the lectures or the labs.
comment_teaching: Dr James Chueng's teaching is easy to understand and follow. His class is interactive, and he will have some Mentimeter questions at the end of the class, if there is time left.
comment_workload:
Assignments: There is a lab every 1-2 weeks, except during the first two weeks. Some labs needed to be handed in, some don't, as they are part of an assignment or just for you to have some hands-on experience on the content they taught recently. An assignment consists of two labs and additional requirements outlined on the assignment page. It may take less than an hour for each lab if you have a coding background, and 1 - 2 hours if you don't. Overall, it is not too tough because the deadline is usually 1-2 weeks after the lab is posted.

Midterm / final: Both of them are open-book and open-notes; you can bring as much as you want to the venue as long as it is not digital. So, you do not need to memorise any code. The midterm's difficulty is fine, while the final is tougher.
comment_grading:
There are two possibilities in calculating the score; the system will take the higher one:
- Midterm (0% / 20%)
- Labs (15%)
- Assignments (20%)
- Final (65% / 45%)

Overall, I think the grading is good, because you can do the labs as long as you want.

comment_grading:
Grading is curved. Not sure of the specific cutoffs, but really depends on how familiar you are with coding: if you are new to it, or even if you have some minor experience, A-range is practically impossible, as you are competing with students who have been coding for years. The grade consists of the following:

Group Weight
Hand-in Lab 1 5%
Assignment 1 10%
Midterm Exam 20%
Hand-in Lab 2 5%
Hand-in Lab 3 5%
Assignment 2 10%
Final Exam 45%

If final is better than midterm only final is used. The assignments are easy to get 100%, as you can use any resources and AI. Most of the students get 100% on each. I got a 0 on assignment 2 as I submitted a wrong file, which dropped my grade by a lot. My other grades were 69% midterm and 47% final. Here are the statistics:
Final:
Average: 43.7
Standard Deviation: 20.5
Maximum: 98
Minimum: 7

Midterm:
Average: 55.9
Standard Deviation: 24.7
Maximum: 100
Minimum: 7

As you can see, STD is quite high as some students know a lot, others start from scratch.
Eventually I got a B-, despite the 0 on assignment 2, so I believe B-range is quite big.

comment_teaching:
For teaching, I can only speak to Gibson Lam's teaching. I have heard stories that David Rossiter's teaching is great, but I've never attended his sessions.

Course and grading aside, I feel that Gibson Lam is actually a really great teacher. There definitely seems to be a strong passion for teaching within him and it shows well during lectures.

He himself has categorized his teaching style into "Teaching using other things instead of slides" and "Using new illustrations and examples". Which is partly true, but he does make decent use of the slides.
comment_workload:
This course does not have too big of a workload. As mentioned previously, only 3 of your 6 lab projects are required to be handed in. You can definitely get away with only ever doing those 3 but do beware of the possible use of lab concepts in the final exam.

It would be difficult to severely mess up your labs if you put in any ounce of effort into them. Depending on your understanding of the course, use of AI, etc. the labs should take you anywhere from 20 minutes to 2 hours. You are also able to attend lab session to receive some guidance.

In addition, the course is also open book. You can refer to course material and personal notes during the exams. This can reduce the strain on studying for the exam and does mean the exam covers more computational thinking and logic than memorization.

So, COMP1021 may not consume much of your time, but it also won’t ignite your interest in computer science—leaving you with little more than a handful of Python scripts and a lingering sense of disappointment.
comment_grading:
A place where this course does redeem itself a little is the grading. The grade weightings chosen are your best of:

- Midterm 24%, Lab projects 36%, Final exam 40%
- Midterm 0%, Lab projects 42%, Final exam 58%

This does mean that, if you do poorly in the midterm, you can override it with a much better final performance.

The higher weighting of lab projects is also definitely a bonus to this course. It is completely realistic to achieve full marks in all 3 of the hand-in (marked) lab projects. This does reduce the stress of having to perform excellently in the midterms and finals.

comment_content: The course introduces programming through Python in a way that is accessible to beginners. It covers basic programming concepts such as variables, conditionals, loops, and functions, along with some fun applications like simple graphics and games. The pace is reasonable for students without prior coding background, though those with experience might find some parts repetitive. Overall, the content gives a good foundation for further computer science or engineering courses.
comment_teaching: The lectures are clear and well-organised, with many practical examples shown during class. The instructor demonstrates how to apply concepts in coding exercises, which makes the material easier to follow. Sometimes the lectures move quickly, especially when introducing new syntax, but the teaching team provides sufficient support through tutorials and consultation hours.
comment_workload: The workload is moderate compared to other engineering courses. Weekly assignments take some time, especially if you are new to coding, but they are essential for building confidence. The final project requires more effort but is also one of the most rewarding parts of the course. As long as you start early and practise consistently, the workload is very manageable.
comment_grading: The grading is fair and transparent. Assessments include programming assignments, quizzes, and a final project/exam. The assignments are manageable if you keep up with lectures, and they give good practice. Partial credit is usually awarded for effort, so even if your program does not run perfectly, you can still earn points if the logic is correct.

comment_teaching:

comment_workload:
• Reading lecture slides and trying code examples is sufficient for ~90% in the mid-term and ~80% in the final.
• Preparing slides before class (hard/soft copy) is helpful, especially for beginners.
• Review after lectures should focus on syntax and structure.
comment_grading:
1. Mid-term Exam (20%) – open book, 1 hr, Week 7 (~40–45% of course).
• If skipped or if counting it lowers your grade, weight becomes 0%.
2. Final Exam (40%) – open book, 2.5 hr, covers the whole course.
• If no mid-term taken, or mid-term lowers your grade, weight becomes 60%.
3. Assignment 1: Music System (20%) – command-line program generating MIDI from text files.
• Lab 3 & 4 completion = 30% of A1; extra features = 70%.
4. Assignment 2: Game Project (20%) – Turtle-based “Space Invaders” game with event handling.
• Lab 7 & 8 completion = 34% of A2; extra features = 66%.
5. Assignment Bonus Point (2%) – awarded only if A1 + A2 < 40%. Earned by answering in-class questions (ping pong ball system).

Passing is easy, but if you want an A, it’s gonna take some effort.

If you want a higher grade, you’ll need to do more exercises, and there are a lot of exercises they provide on the course website.

comment_content:
Generally, doing those labs was pretty fun! There are 10 labs in total, where MP1 and MP2 are 2 weeks long. You'll have tutorials during the add/drop period (attendance is not required ofc), and a manual booklet and a lab report booklet will be distributed to you on Week 1 in the lab orientation session. The overall process is as follows:
1. You submit pre-lab (Part A) before your lab session begins;
2. You record all the data in your lab report booklet (Part B);
3. You analyze the data, answer the questions in the booklet (Part C), and submit both Parts B & C, Excel sheet, and sometimes Summary (< 450 words, 2-3 paragraphs) before the beginning of the next lab session.
comment_teaching: The tutorials are useless af: the prof just reads through the presentation available on Canvas. The only good thing about them is that those tutorials end by the semester's Week 3.
comment_workload:
It all depends on how you develop your habit of submitting your lab reports each week. Imo you should really make efforts to submit your Excel sheet for MP1 as perfect as you can, so the subsequent lab reports will be just [Ctrl+C, Ctrl+V]-ing your .xlsx file. If your writing sounds 'scholarly' enough, then you'll most likely get 20 or 21 every time.

After you get used to doing your monkey work with Excel, I advise you to prepare the Excel template before each lab session (with all the slopes/intercepts, parameters formulae, measurement errors, errors of propagation, and linearized scatter plot) for the four main reasons:
1) Half of the experiments require working in dark area, so you'd better type your measurements on the laptop rather than on the booklet;
2) You can clearly notice if the trend of the first 2-3 points already differ too much from the intended outcomes;
3) Submitting all the reports and summary will take you as less as only 1 evening;
4) +100 aura in front of the TA for your preparation

Alright, so, I think this lab course is totally worth its 1 credit in terms of workload.
comment_grading:
Your final grade will be determined based on (i) lab performance (20%), (ii) lab reports (60%) and (iii) summary (20%).

Overall score: LQ = 88.54, Median = 90.76, UQ = 92.78, mine = 95.75/100.
Adjusted score (we were told that it's per the average perfomance/reports score of each session): LQ = 80.49, Median = 82.51, UQ = 84.35, mine = 87.05.
Grade: A+

comment_content:
Content:
**First Module (1-5)**
**Second Module (6-10)**

1. **Course Overview**
2. **Programming Fundamentals**
3. **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)**
4. **Scope, Boolean Logic, and Branching**
5. **Loops, Shortcuts, and Arrays**
6. **OOP, Subclassing, Multidimensional Arrays, and Sorting**
7. **Strings and File Input/Output (I/O)**
8. **Event-Driven Programming and Graphical User Interfaces (GUIs) - Not Tested on Final Exam**
9. **Recursion**
10. **Abstract Data Types (Stacks and Queues)**

To be frank, the lecture materials are not enough for you to prepare for your examinations. For grade chasers, make sure you understand all the past papers. The format of exam is very similar to those in past papers.
comment_teaching:
For the video part, the prof speaks at a slow pace. To watch it comfortably, I recommend using the 2.0 speed setting. Additionally, there’s a transcript available next to the video. Please note that you can find the PowerPoint presentation in the resource page. Furthermore, you’ll need to download the example code as well.

Desmond is a friendly and approachable individual who is more than willing to answer questions. He also takes the time to explain each question in detail, ensuring that we fully comprehend the concepts.
comment_workload:
FOR JAVA BEGINNERS:
Catch up on the coding logics and Java syntaxes by working extremely hard.

FOR NON-BEGINNERS:
The workload is manageable. Focus on studying for midterms and finals.

Also, the project is not overly challenging. Simply follow the codes you learn throughout the modules, and you’ll eventually succeed.
comment_grading:
The grading system is quite favorable. Since the teaching was conducted online, it is expected that all students will receive full marks for laboratory experiments, projects, and module questions. Therefore, academic performance in examinations will significantly impact overall grades.

Desmond has indicated that approximately 25% of the final grade will be determined by exam scores. It is crucial to pay particular attention to loops and recursion in the final examination, as well as abstract data types. These topics will be extensively covered in the final exam.

Notably, the final exam will primarily focus on the second half of the module, with minimal questions regarding the first half. To prepare effectively, it is advisable to complete the past papers provided by Desmond. The average score for both midterm and final examinations was approximately 50%.

comment_content:
The course is called introduction to programming, but in fact the people taking the course typically already know Java or another programming language. If you are truly looking for an introductory programming class, I recommend COMP1021 instead.

The exam papers are far too easy, but there are many tricks that make you lose points. I find these tricks rather pointless, as any free IDE should be able to catch such errors with ease. They include:
- Hidden curly brackets or syntax errors in the LQs
- Poorly phrased questions (eg. how many times is a function called?)
- Easily testable things not taught in lectures (eg. float multiplied by an integer)

None of the content is particularly hard. The course will cover basic variables, conditional statements, expressions, looping, recursion, classes and inheritance, 1D and 2D arrays, and basic data structures (stack and queue).

If you want to do well, just make sure you do not make any careless mistakes. I have heard that even a 90% is B range in this class.

Overall, I learned very little from this class, and the exam was more focused on trying to trick you rather than testing your ability to think like a programmer.
comment_teaching:
Wilfred's teaching was alright. He teaches clearly and makes sure to point out common pitfalls for beginners. However, as I have a background in programming (as do the majority of people taking this course), he goes far too slowly for my liking (for reference, I only write a few lines of notes per lecture). He does go through examples in his classes, but they are far too basic to be of much use.

If you have a background in programming, I recommend skipping his lectures and revising the material per the slides instead.

Both of the TAs for this course are very competent. During labs, they readily go around the lecture hall to answer any questions. Moreover, they mark labs, assignments, and exam papers at an astonishing pace. I cannot commend them enough.
comment_workload:
Very low workload.

There are 2 programming assignments which take around 30 minutes each to complete and are very easy.

Moreover, there are around 10 labs, which can be completed in around 10 minutes each. Unfortunately, the attendance for the labs are mandatory, so if you don't have a hall you should definitely schedule another class on the same day.

Very little revision needs to be done as there is a reference sheet with all the syntax and you are also allowed an additional 2 sided A4 cheat sheet for both the midterm and final.
comment_grading:
Two pathways for grading:
10% labs, 5% written assignment, 20% programming assignment
If midterm score > final score: 25% midterm, 40% final
If final score > midterm score: 0% midterm, 65% final

Most people got 100%s in the labs and programming assignment. In general, people did worse on the written assignment, where the median was 41/50.

Midterm:
LQ: 68.75/100 Median: 77.5/100 UQ: 90.5/100

Final:
LQ: 74/100 Median: 86.5/100 UQ: 92.5/100

In general, people did better on the final than the midterm for 2 reasons. First, the final was arguably easier than the midterm. Second, due to the existence of pathway 2, your midterm score does not matter if you do well on the final, so we were more incentivized to revise for the final exam.

I scored 92/100 on the midterm but 97/100 on the final. Through pathway 2, my weighted score was around 98/100, and I scored an A+.

The reason I rated the grading for this course so low is because I have heard many negative things about the grading. As everyone is competent at programming in this course, the median is naturally very high. As such, even a few marks is enough to knock you down a grade.

comment_content:
The final exam skips module 8 on Simple Event Driven Programming and GUI (good). Some MCQ tricks on past papers are not taught and require extra knowledge of the specification, e.g. literals, string interning, and null values, but they count a small proportion towards marks.

Recursion is the hardest across all papers, e.g. recursion with a stack ADT, subset sum problem, and generate permutations. Solving these problems becomes formulaic since they involve iterating through the array/string ONCE and doing recurrence relationships with each item.

This introductory course introduces you to fundamental tools (concepts and syntax) but you are on your own in figuring out how to use them (DSA). It does not promote best practices either.
comment_teaching:
Desmond holds bi-weekly optional review sessions on Saturdays with around 15% attendance rates. MOOC learning content was pre-recorded 10 years ago but is still relevant given the basics of Java 8 have not changed much.

Desmond is speedy in responding to emails, allows resubmission to online lab assignments, and personally congratulates you for achieving an A+.
comment_workload:
3 semesters of past papers are given (from 2018 to 2020). 2017 paper while not available, can be found online and was used to write our mid-term. Doing past papers and knowing what concepts may be tested is essential for preparation.

The project is essentially a giant lab assignment. For labs/the project, I don't condone academic dishonesty, but JD exists if you know what I mean.

Grading is directly proportional to study time and individual abilities. But I don’t think it is possible to compete with others if you are a novice to programming with the few months you have.
comment_grading:
There was someone who got a 100 for mid-term and 99 for finals. I got 95 for the mid-term and 90.5 for finals, after bumping from 92 and 77.5 respectively after paper checking. Always attend the paper-checking session, and prepare a long defence of your misunderstood code to TAs. comment_content:
The objective of this course is to provide students with a solid foundation in programming principles and data abstraction through the utilization of Java. Throughout the course, students will acquire the skills necessary to develop procedural programs by employing variables, arrays, control statements, loops, recursion, data abstraction, and objects within an integrated development environment.

In this course, students will learn various ways to apply data abstraction in their programming projects. Here are a few examples:

Creating Classes: Students will learn how to define their own classes, which encapsulate data and behavior into a single entity. By defining classes, they can abstract away the implementation details and expose only the necessary methods and properties for interacting with the data.

Encapsulation: Students will understand the concept of encapsulation, which involves hiding the internal details of an object and exposing only the necessary interfaces. They will learn how to use access modifiers like public, private, and protected to control the visibility of variables and methods, thereby abstracting the internal workings of an object.

Abstract Data Types (ADTs): Students will explore the concept of abstract data types, which provide a high-level description of data structures and their operations without specifying the implementation details. They will learn how to use ADTs like stacks, queues, and linked lists, which allow them to work with data in a more abstract and modular manner.

Interfaces: Students will be introduced to interfaces, which define a contract for classes to implement. By programming to interfaces, students can abstract away the specific implementations and focus on the desired behavior. This allows for more flexibility and modularity in their programs.

-> heavy workload
-> easy exams (for with some background in programming)
-> useful concepts
-> suitable for people with some background in programming, better beginner level

comment_grading:
Online Activities - 10%
Lab Exercises - 10%
Project - 10%
Midterm - 30%
Final - 40%

All the online activities, the project and most of the lab exercises are graded automatically by the online system. You are allowed quite a few tries and only a handful of test cases were used, so it's safe to assume that the majority of the students are able to complete these parts with full marks. The few hand checked lab should also be easy enough to get 100% if you follow the instructions (although it does get ambiguous sometimes).

The exams are conducted on the canvas quiz system and you are allowed to use BlueJ, pdf files, google calculator and course websites. Past papers were provided, but they were not representative of the difficulty of the midterm, which was exceptionally easy, quite some people scored 100. They probably realised the issue and designed the final with greater difficulty. It was also filled with problems. The system did not recognise correct code and the questions were deleted because the program provided was not written properly. It led to great confusion during the exam and people did poorly compared to the midterm.

The grading in general is very lenient, even if you messed up and get 80 something in the final, you can still get A+. However, judging from the trend of the exams, it's likely the difficulty of the papers go up significantly and it will be much harder to get a good grade.

The workload is quite light. The deadline for everything in part 1 is before the mid-term exam and in part 2 is before the final exam. It took me 4 days in total to finish all the assignments.

1. MCs: You get around 3-5 MCs per module to test your understanding of the topic.

2. Labs: You have a lab per module, and inside a lab, you have 3-4 related tasks constituting the entire program of the lab. Each task, you write around 10-20 lines of code for each task, but the task is very easy to understand and the code logic is very simple.

comment_grading:
The grading is similar to COMP 1022P courses by other instructors in the past. Around 95% total mark can get you an A, so it's quite bad but should be fine for people who learned programming prior.

However, the mid-term exam and final exam are poorly designed.

Here is a summary of the exams' style. They are delivered online as Canvas quizzes. PDF notes, Google Calculator, BlueJ, and the course website (HKMOOC) are allowed references.

There are 10 questions in the mid-term exam and 18 questions in the final exam. Some are MCs, some require you to process the code manually and determine the output, and others require you to fill in the blanks to complete a program. Codes that aren't intended to be copied into BlueJ will be displayed as an image.

The instructor (or TA) really wants to utilize Canvas' auto-grading system, so he adds a lot of warnings asking us not to add ";", full-stops ".", unnecessary spaces " ", etc. in bold, italics, and with capitalized words. The utterly long warnings take up to half part of a question.

There is 1 error in the mid-term exam and 2 errors in the final exam, causing us to waste all our time on those questions. Combined with the warnings distracting us from reading the core part of the question, all of us become careless in at least one question. Therefore, the maximum in the final exam is 96/100, as opposed to 100/100 in mid-term exam (and 103/100 in MATH 1023 mid-term exam).

Moreover, quite a few questions don't really test our programming abilities with Java. One asks us to process a code but everything is compressed (like two methods in one line, brackets, if statements, for loops, return statements, all put in a single line, which is allowed in Java but not in Python). Honestly, it only acts as an eye test but nothing else.

comment_content:
IF DESMOND IS NOT THE INSTRUCTOR, THIS REVIEW MAY NOT BE APPLICABLE TO YOU

Course content covers basic programming from beginners level.  Even if you never had programming experience before, you will still be able to manage it.

If Desmond is the instructor, he will cover 2 more topics than other instructors, and the order of topics taught will be different.

Topic 4: Checking and Selection Statements
Topic 5: Looping Statements
<Midterms>

comment_teaching:
IF DESMOND IS NOT THE INSTRUCTOR, THIS REVIEW MAY NOT BE APPLICABLE TO YOU

Desmond is really a great instructor.  If there was an A+ for teaching I would definitely give him an A+.  He is really passionate about teaching and he will absolutely make you love programming.  He teaches simple topics from scratch and every detail is well explained.  He is willing to stay after class for a long time to answer students questions,  I’ve heard he even sends emails to congratulate students who got A+, which shows how much he cares about his teaching.  Don’t get intimidated if you’ve never learnt programming before, if you put in work and attend lectures you can definitely get A-range.

That being said, I have to say that this course is not suitable for everyone.  If you’re taking this course just to fulfil science school requirements, or simply taking it as a common core, the competitiveness is insane.  There seems to be many QFIN students taking this course during spring term too, so they will absolutely kill the grades.  If this description sounds like you, then you may consider taking COMP 1021 instead.

However, if you’re really passionate about learning programming, or you’re not worried about pulling down your GPA slightly, or simply just want to experience Desmond’s superb teaching, then please take this course.  You’ll learn so much more and so much better than COMP 1021.  If you plan to study Computer Science in the future, taking this course will guarantee a much better foundation when taking future comp courses compared to your peers who studied COMP 1021.
comment_workload:
IF DESMOND IS NOT THE INSTRUCTOR, THIS REVIEW MAY NOT BE APPLICABLE TO YOU

Labs are light workload.
The 2 Assignments may look scary at first sight but in fact you only need to write a hundred to two hundred lines of code in total.  
Midterms and Exams are very hard so you need to prepare well for it in advance.  Read the textbook if you have extra time, you’ll pick up useful additional knowledge.
comment_grading:
IF DESMOND IS NOT THE INSTRUCTOR, THIS REVIEW MAY NOT BE APPLICABLE TO YOU

Very competitive course (See teaching for more details).  Desmond promises a 28% A-range which may sound nice, but the fact is so many crazy guys take this course, so you’ll really have to work hard to squeeze yourself into the top 28%.  On the bright side, there is no limit to B range so if you’re happy with B-range then it’s ok.

Make sure you practice all past exam papers on the course website, and understand why the answer is such, if not you may be screwed.

Exams are of course cumulative.

Lab 10%
Assignments 30%
Midterm 20%
Finals 40%

comment_content:
Basic programming in Java (Note: not JavaScript), and also teach about basic manipulation of GreenFoot and Eclipse IDE, 2 programs for Java programming.

Topics include:
*variables
*objects and classes
*more on variables
*scopes

Stack is not taught in 2020-2021 Fall

My thought is that, concepts aren't hard when you practice more on Java. Looking for some extra-curricular Java tutorials may also help. If you are really not good at programming, consider COMP1021 (really), given by the following grading information.
comment_teaching:
As an Indian (presumably from Andhra Pradesh?) lecturer, Muppala's English is peppered with heavy accent. This however, doesn't affect my understanding. After all, as he's responsive to questions asked in and after lectures, clarifications can be raised without doubt. Generally, a good lecturer.

I previously heard about that Muppala shows cat videos during lectures according to a 2014 COMP1022P review on MSSSUG, but I don't observed any cat videos being shown on lectures. (after all, it's 6 years past...)

Regarding TAs, they are supportive during labs. Some of them have heavy Chinese accent when speaking English, but are generally understandable.
comment_workload:
There is 1 written assignment and 1 programming assignment for COMP1022P. You can get a good score if you can make early preparation for the 2 assignments (For example, I get full mark for the programming assignment). Workload is generally light.

There's also weekly computer labs, focusing on recent topics taught. Although for most of the time you can get easy full marks for labs, it's highly advised that you should prepare for labs early, as you won't know the difficulty of the upcoming labs (For example, a number of students and I screwed in a lab related to if/switch statements).
comment_grading:
– Laboratories (10%)
– Assignments (5+20%)
– Midterm examination (25%) (68.5/100)
– Final exam (40%) (69/100)

As per other's said before, there will be loads of programming experts in COMP1022P. This means not only the mean is quite high, but also you'll face fierce competition.

From an optimistic perspective, as there are plenty of programming experts, you can easily ask for help if you have any conceptual problems. For example, I met an experienced French student on course Whatsapp group, and he helped me a lot on understanding scopes.

I have to say that I'm very lucky to survive in COMP1022P as a programming muggle (especially when I didn't look through course reviews before pre-enrollment). In general (and for other courses in HKUST, actually), you're in great trouble if your score is under the mean.

comment_content:
Just some basics of programming with Java. The things you will learn in this course are nearly the same as HKDSE ICT Elective D and even less than that, so if you have learned ICT before, the content in the course may be familiar to you. For nearly 2/3 of the course, you will learn Java basic with Greenfoot, and change to use Eclipse in around the last few topics. Recursion will be the hardest thing in this course but is still ok if you have had the lesson.
