comment_teaching: Wilfred is good really good teacher. He was willing to answer most of the question you ask for it.
comment_workload: If you have some basic programming knowledge before you take this course, those labs and assignments may not be too hard for you to finish. But if you haven't learned programming before, those labs and assignments may be a little bit hard for you to do. With 9 labs (1 lab pre week), 1 written assignment, and 1 programming assignment, the workload is quite light comparing to other courses (like LANG1002).
comment_grading:
Laboratories (10%) (with 9 lab in total for 20-21 fall, 1/week)
Written assignments (5%)
Programming assignments (20%) (write a Mastermind game)
Midterm examination (25%) ~ With Mean of 63.6
Final exam (40%)

Those Labs can be easily getting full marks if you have put some time to finish those coding before you the lesson. With the Mid-term and Final exam, since we have online mode this year, those exams will be divided into two parts, MC and Long Question. You can finish the exam within the time limit if you have a really good programming foundation and study those past papers before you have the exam.

Since this course is harder than COMP1021, most of the people with good programming knowledge will choose this course but not the 1021. Therefore, you will see lots of programming experts in this course. If you do not have confidence in programming or you are bad at using the computer, I will not recommend you to take this course. Just take COMP1021 instead of this course.

comment_content: Desmond teaches really useful content in the course, basically everything taught are those tools that you can apply easily (eg. loops, checking statements etc). The syllabus goes from basics to the difficult ones, so it really helps build a good foundation before moving on.
comment_teaching: Desmond is an extremely good professor. If there was an A+ for me to choose for his teaching it would be A+. I am a programming dummy so during classes I would ask silly questions and ask him to repeat things that I could not catch up with. Desmond patiently answered my queries every time and would even stay behind class to explain more to me. The thing is he even encouraged me when I felt hopeless during the course, and I would say he has been more like a mentor than he is a professor. Desmond係科大既清泉 if you know what I mean.
comment_workload: The workload is alright. Yet apart from that, I would say the workload makes sense. Two programming assignments throughout the course, and if you are good at it it shouldn't take you a lot of time.
comment_grading: I would be honest, I think the grading is really fair actually. If you are good at it, you get a good grade, if you are lost most of the time (like I was), you don't get a good grade. The distribution is really fair and it is not impossible to A range, but that definitely means a great deal of effort. Some students have a programming background and it is normal for them to do a bit better but then for those who don't and started from scratch, I would say it would be challenging. Yet again, I would say I never expected to get a good grade when I enrolled in the course. I just wanted to learn. So maybe if you kept this thought in mind, the grade wouldn't matter that much after all. The course is really worthy to take and the final grade is just a by-product to me. (Not gonna reveal my grade but I failed every test, think this says a lot)

comment_content:
Non-VBA Excel Functions (week 1-3)
Cell References, Operators (^ power, <> not equal to), Logic Functions (and, or, not),String Functions (Concatenation, LEN) IF, VLOOKUP, HLOOKUP, LookUp Error

VBA (week 3-12)
Macro, VBA Event, InputBox & MsgBox
Cell References (Cells(Row, Column), ActiveCell - currently selected cell)
IF...Then, Else IF, COUNTIF
Types of Variable (Integer, Long, Single , Double, Boolean)
Loop (While...Wend; Do While...Loop; Do...Loop While, For Loops, (For...To...(Step)...Next),  For Each Loop (For Each...In...Next)
Specifying Input and Output Type, Random Numbers, RANDBETWEEN(a,b)
Local & Global Variables, Excel Objects (Range = cells, Shape), Excel Color (RGB), Doing VBA Events (.Rows.Count, .Value), Brackets for functions with input parameters and output, Private and Public Functions, GUI Control
Object-Oriented Programming (class, attributes, method)
Recursion

comment_teaching:
Professor Rossiter has a very interesting, intriguing teaching style, I have attended every lecture of him and I am really enjoy his teaching.
I think he is one of my favorite professor in HKUST over 4 years of study
Lecture videos are provided to every lecture and you can do a revision on it

Questions are asked to award the students who answer correctly and instantly on every lectures as their bonus marks  +0.4% marks per question adding up in your total score (sitting on the front and wearing a special color of cloths will help to raise your chances to answer questions)
comment_workload:
As both Midterm and Final exams are open-book, it indicates the difficulties (that's means even you have all the notes, you may still not know how to do the exam!!!)

But printing all the notes and jotting down all the examples from the lectures are imperative, especially printing all the codes of every lab session (including the last lab!)

Also, do all and print all the Midterm Sample and Final Sample Papers! Make sure you 100% understand why the answers work in a logical way.
I studied a few full days for the final exam to get a better score, to cover my low scores for the midterm.
If I continued to get a -0.83sd in the final, I would be sure I would get a C or C-

Don't look down on the Basics of the course content (the loops, if functions), please have a 100% strong understanding and practice on the basics before moving forward to the advanced

All the teaching materials and examples in lectures, labs and sample papers are relatively easier, YET the EXAMS are totally on a different level!
Again, DON'T UNDERESTIMATE the difficulty of this course!
Programming rookies get a tough time to familiarize the programming language, styles and all the codes.
You will never do a better job as great as those CS majors, people who have studied ICT in DSE. Rookies are coming to put the mean down and favor the high-flyers
comment_grading:
No attendance marks for lectures and labs, but I highly recommend you to go all for bonus marks in lectures, learning the principles and copying all the codes for the lab assignments

Total 7 lab sessions, only 2nd - 6th lab projects count the grades
5 Labs: 5 X 8% = 40%
1st and 7th Lab does not count but the 7th lab about Interpolation is extremely essential for final exam. You must go to the lab session to complete the codes and understand how it works.
The last lab is very difficult for programming rookie like me

Open Book Exams (Both Midterm & Final)
Midterm Exam: 1hr 10mins
Final Exam: 2hrs 10mins
Time is limited and harsh for rookies, please do the exams precisely with a good time management. Don't stick on the questions you don't know.
Read more codes and practice by using the Excel on hands (I got a loop question correct in the final exam, only because I had done a Excel additional practice - sourced from the course website)

If Midterm Result > Final Result
Midterm Exam: 20%
Final Exam: 40%

If Midterm Result < Final Result
Midterm Exam: 0%
Final Exam: 60%

My Case:
Lab: 500/500 = 40 (follow and copy every code given by the TA in lab session to make sure you don't have any mistakes)
Lab is important for rookies to save their grades
Midterm Exam:  56/100 (-0.83sd) (mean 72) (sd: 19.2) = 0%
Final Exam:  64/100 (-0.19sd) (mean 67) (sd: 15.9) = 64 * 60% = 38.4
Bonus mark (Answering questions in the lecture correctly and quickly): 1 question = 0.4
Total = 78.8 = Grade: B-
I only get -0.19sd in the final exam, it already grants me a B-
That's indicate you must get a mean to get a B

When you review on other comments about this course
Total: 77.2 = Grade B- (-0.3sd in final exam)
Total: 76.2 = Grade: C+ (-0.4sd in final exam)
The B-range and C-range are very narrow, the course is extremely competitive, only with total 80% secures you a B
Imagine how many programming experts in this class competing with you
Excel the course with your Logical Intelligence and Carefulness
If you have an unclear logic, you have no any programming background and you are a programming idiot like me, try your best to avoid COMP classes (you go to take other QR Common Core courses, go exchange to transfer credit, or take some distance learning courses)
If not, I will bless for you!

comment_grading:
Originally, there are two schemes and the better grade from the schemes will be calculated:
labs 40%, midterm 20%, final 40%; or
labs 44%, midterm 0%, final 56%.
In this way you can get a good grade even if you perform not so well in midterm.

However, in this semester, it is changed to:
midterm 33.32%, labs 66.68%

Also, the professor asks questions in the lecture, and randomly picks people to answer the question. You get 0.5% bonus points for each correctly answered question.

For reference, my grades:
labs 100%
midterm 91%
Bonus 2.5 (5 questions answered)
Final grade A+

comment_content:
Week 1-2: Learning Excel Features
-Cell References
-Cell Formulas (including but not limited to: SUM(), AVERAGE(), MAX(), MIN(), RANK(), COUNTIF(), SUMIF(), SUMIF(), LEN(), UPPER(), LOWER(), LEFT(), RIGHT(), SUBSTITITE(), IF(), AND(), OR(), NOT(), VLOOKUP(), HLOOKUP(), ISNA(), IFNA(), ISERROR(), IFERROR(), etc.)

Weeks after: Excel with VBA
-Referring to Cells (Cells(), Range())
-Making decisions (AND, OR etc.)
-Looping (While...Wend, Do While...Loop, For Loop, etc.)
-Making random numbers (Rnd())
-Changing the appearance of cells
-Using excel objects
-Drawing excel shapes
-Types of variable in Excel VBA

As this semester is quite special, there's only a midterm but no final exam. The above is the content for the midterm exam.

The following is the content after the midterm exam. Although not examined in this semester, here are the contents for your reference:

-More on Loops
-Using shapes as buttons
-Different ways to make a macro
-Subroutines and functions
-More on variables
-Basic GUI controls
-RGB representation
-Arrays

The content is quite useful, especially if you are from business school. However, if you wish to major in IS, you are not recommended to enroll in this course as you have to take ISOM 3400 instead of ISOM 3230 as ISOM 3230 has an exclusion of COMP 1022Q. ISOM 3230 would have a more comprehensive look on the business analytics side of excel, while COMP 1022Q looks at more of the technical side of excel and VBA. ISOM 3400 is a Python course, which is considerably harder than excel VBA.

4 Lab Assignments [40%]: 100/100 (mean: around 98)
Midterm [20%] = 90/100 (mean: 62.37, sd: 24.22, maximum: 100, minimum: 8)

[SCHEME 2]

comment_content:
Very easy.

This course is about how to use Excel VBA, mainly focusing on the coding and logic behind writing codes. They will teach you the most commonly used commands such as IF, LOOKUP. If you had taken IT courses before this course will be a piece of cake. If you hadn't this course will still be a piece of cake to you as the content requires little time to review and understand.

The first half of the course consists of some excel basic knowledge that even primary students will know if they had taken their IT courses such as the definition of a spreadsheet (which of course will not be asked in exam), basic cell formulas. Another half of the course is also very easy to understand, such as looping and basic cell formatting.

In fact, the course is so easy that you can understand all the lesson materials just by reading the lecture notes, you don't need to attend any lectures at all (in my opinion).
comment_teaching:
Streamlined and Sophisticated.

Gibson is one of the best lecturers I've seen (though to be fair as a year 1 student I haven't seen much), his lessons are interesting, used a lot of gags and jokes to stir up the atmosphere, he also uses very simple and easy examples to help students understand the concept of the class.

Questions will be asked during each lecture and if you answered it correctly you will be awarded 0.5% towards your final grade.

Each week they will have 1 lab, a mini-project/ homework that helps you understand the concepts and the implementation of codes. Without a doubt, those labs are very well organized, while doing the lab you're simultaneously learning and revising the lecture material. The lab is so good that if you've done all the labs you don't even need to read the lecture notes to learn the content.
comment_workload:
The only homework for the course is lab work aforementioned, better still only half of them are counted towards your final grade, which means there's only a lab work biweekly.

Each lab work is a simple mini-project using excel spreadsheets that takes around 1 hour to finish, such as making a search up page for MTR fare.

Lab project will be taught in lab sessions and you can finish that homework while attending that session. If you missed that session you have around 5 days to do it yourself and hand in via canvas.  Moreover, each lab will have a sophisticated step-by-step guide teaching you how to write the codes almost LINE BY LINE. Even a primary student can finish the lab under that level of instruction.
comment_grading:
This semester's grading is quite good in general.

This course was regarded as one of the best courses in UST. Easy to learn with very high grades. However, this year is an exception.

The course has 4 lab work, 1 mid-term exam and 1 final exam, by the way all exams are open book open notes exam.

This course has two ways to count your final grade. I've forgotten the exact method, but basically, final exam counts around 50%, mid-term 30% and labs 20%. The second way is final 60%, labs 40%. The instructors will automatically choose the method that will result in a higher score.

My score:
Lab total: 97.5/100

Midterm: 62/100
Mean: 62.37
S.D: 24.22

Final: 83/100
Mean: 48
S.D: 22

My Total: 84.6%, Final grade A.

As you can see the final exam is disastrous. Never have I seen a 1000 level course having a lower than 50% mean score. Bear in mind this is an open book exam. Though to be fair it doesn't really help even if you had your notes, since the exam had focused a lot on logical deduction which required a focused-mind and clear head. To conclude almost no revision is required, but you'd better make sure you're clear about the concepts of coding.

comment_content: Content is about basic VBA programming.  It is very useful no matter what school you are in.  As a business school student, I find VBA useful and applicable in my internships.  Employers really care your computer skills and your way to handle data.  Strongly encourage students to take it.
comment_teaching: Gibson is a wonderful professor.  Although it is a 9:30 class for my section, I seldom miss it. He explains all the things in a clear way.  And he makes programming a funny way with his facial expressions and wordings.
comment_workload: Workload is low.  All you need to do is to submit the 5 labs.  The other labs you can choose to attend or not.  No presentation, no project.  Final and mid term are open book, so all you need to do is to understand the content and bring everything you need to the exams.  No need to waste time copying cheatsheets.

comment_content:
The course mainly teaches Excel VBA programming, covering an overview of Excel and cells functions, VBA logic, loops, shapes and operations, as well as more advanced stuff like arrays, recursion and object, which are all rarely used in Excel VBA due to the availability of cells as object instances. 2D arrays are also out of syllabus, which comes to me as a joke, since both the other introductory courses have this programming logic.

I would say this course is useful for office and work applications, but pretty much useless for learning actual programming concepts or as a prerequisite to the much higher-class COMP2011.
comment_teaching:
I absolutely adore Gibson.

His lessons are really fun, and he can always take the seemingly complicated codes and dumb it down to a level that even junior secondary students have no trouble understanding the codes. A very good lecturer for absolute beginners in programming.

He is also pretty knowledgeable in other programming languages, and is able to present comparison of VBA to other programming languages to help students determine which programming introduction course is the most suitable for them.
comment_workload:
There is a lab each week, while you get some weeks off, so there are 8 labs in total. There is indeed some work involved, but it isn't really that hard, especially if you go to the assigned lab session.

Both exams are, as always, open book, and thus you have no need of trying to memorize the syntax and function names and details; a throughout understanding of the logic is sufficient.
comment_grading:
This course has traditionally been one of the best graded courses at HKUST, as well as the easiest among the 3 1000-level programming introduction choices. However, this semester has subverted all expectations, and I hereby announce that COMP1022Q has officially turned from an easy, good grade course into a course with terrible grading and difficulty.

As always, both exams are open book exams.

Participation bonus:
The instructor asks a question in class related to the lecture content, and the person who answers the question correctly gets 0.5% bonus each time, so my advice is to be proactive and seize the chance to answer, because Gibson allows that and he takes quite a long time to randomly pick a student.

There are 2 grading schemes:
Scheme 1: All in final
Final exam: 56%
Lab: 44%

Scheme 2: Mid-term score included
Mid-term: 20%
Final: 40%
Lab: 40$

Statistics:
Mid-term mean: 62.4/100
Mid-term SD: 24.2
Final mean: 47.5/100
Final SD: 22
Lab mean: >95/100

My score:
Lab: 100%
Mid-term: 82/100
Final: 97/100
Participation bonus: 3 times, +1.5%

A+ with scheme 2, 99.8%

I would say I got lucky, to be honest.

Workload is not high having 8 labs but only 4 is graded. There will be teaching assistants during the lab period. Attending all lab sessions is encourage so you can experience building your own program and learn from the mistakes you made in the program. You are allowed to bring books and notes into the exam, so you can find the answers from it. Although the exams are open book, you still need to understand the usage of different formulas of the program and the logic flow of different programs to get good grades. Just be careful and study all lab programs and highlight the important formulas in the notes so you can find the necessary information in the notes.

comment_content:
TL; DR: Beginner friendly (at least for 70% content), best if you have a bit of programming knowledge (C/C++, Java). Might need some time for the library.

The new course opened in 2025 Fall to replace 1021 turtles.
My background is quite a lot: ICT C/C++, Python, Java, so you could imagine a lot of concepts are actually duplicated.
Shout-out for my fellow ASSO mates.

Python is one of the important programming languages in multiple fields, even helping you in basic calculus (trust me), I would say it is worth the content and worth the time.  

### Overall topics:
Basic Syntax
Operation
Branching: if-statements (and its extend like if-else, elif etc), switch statement
Looping: For-loop, while / do-while
List
Tuple
Dictionary
Function
Recursion
OOP

3 libraries:
Numpy
Pandas
MatPlotlib

Mention some of the less-discuss topic:
### Lab: must attend for attendance, it's not a 3 hr long speech but <1hr so it's fine. The rest of the time is for QnA.
### PAs: I recommend just do not start right away once it is out but wait 1-2 days for it to settle, a bit more convenient than going back-and-forth with the skeleton (drafted) codes.

comment_teaching:
I’d say he’s top 10 in all professors I’ve met, but there are things that can be done better.

Good: makes sure you understand, reminds you of details you may not find elsewhere. I do not want to keep saying the good things because you can see it in almost all other reviews, I am here to talk about my honest feelings as a total newbie to programming.

Bad: pretends to focus a lot on exam (more on grading), but not about the content, just about some boring syntax you can totally avoid, forcing us to listen to something that is not useful anywhere outside of this course, just for him to make crazy papers to trick us. (For example when you really write code there are many ways to do something and you can just pick one way that you like, but he forces you to remember the syntax to all the writing methods and may ask you about how to rewrite a program using other syntax doing the same thing, which I think is totally useless)

I think for a new programmer, the most important thing is to hone a programming mindset and problem-solving mindset using codes. It is very different whether you can write codes or you can read and understand those codes. I’d say the lecturer spams a lot of codes without giving us sufficient motivation. He basically goes like “Show time” and then show us what happens. He has good explanation only on understand understanding the basic concepts but not on honing problem solving techniques. What I mean is that he always walks through why the program works, but never talks about why the program is designed that way and how to come up with that.

Of course I understand that he gives us a lot of self practice, and we only learn by doing, but it will also be better if experts like him can share more with us about how he approaches questions. (I’m sure he can do it well because he is a good story teller, nothing can be easier than just walking through what he has in mind with us) When the focus is about explaining why the program works, it is not really worth it to take lessons because I can just read the notes and run through it line by line (the notes are made by him too, and are good enough to a level that can replace him…), I think insights and thinking are much more precious and should be emphasised a lot more.

I believe this is important, especially for an introductory course (for courses like COMP 2711H, professor never talks about problem-solving and only discusses the beauty of the theorem and the intuition, that is totally fine because it is not an introductory course and we can learn about doing on our own, I am just claiming that it is not appropriate for the very first course in anything like programming)
comment_workload:
2 PA
5 lab exercises
Lab tutorials require attendance (non-sense)
Sometimes non-sense,, too much involvement in resolving very stupid problems rather than actually learning.
Also, much time is spent on dealing with exams with scenarios that never happen in the real world. That kills a lot of time and interest as some exam questions are pure non sense, have some memorise a lot of extra things just to tackle this exam and those have nothing to do with my programming proficiency.

For a total python newbie like me, each lab took me about 2-3 hours to complete.

PA is the ultimate devil.
PA 1 is supposed to be easy from the content covered, but turned out to be extremely tedious.
It took me 4 hours to write all the codes, and 6 hours for me to finish debugging…
First of all, as a person being “introduced” to python, it is really insane to deal with such a high intensity exercise.
However, most of the time it’s not about you not knowing some concepts, but it is about you’re not knowing something they never teach about how python works.
Second, you actually do not know what you’re doing. You are only writing some functions for them to do the work and you never have the big picture of what you are actually trying to do. They will throw a lot of arbitrary names and variables in the code but never explains what they mean. I don’t even know the type of some particular variables and have to test them out using the output (and of course when you talk about reading codes, it’s another level of trouble).
Also, throughout the process you hardly can learn about anything, and you cannot use AI because they have a very strong plagiarism platform that can check.
Usually after spending hours into doing something and finally making it, one feels rewarding as I feel like they have challenged their limits successfully and should see some evidence of improvement. However, that can never be the case here. The only thing I feel is I can finally get rid of this terrible stuff. I cannot appreciate my hard work because it is only to get through all those clumsy details and is not worth my time and effort at all except the 25% course grade.

Luckily, my major is not CS or DSCT, so this will be my only programming course at HKUST and I will never do it anymore.
comment_grading:
Mid term:
The most terrible mid term exam I’ve ever had, even crazier than real analysis, HHM’s multivariable calculus or anything. First, if you need to think, then you won’t be able to finish the paper. Secondly, if you need time to understand their instructions, you won’t be able to finish the paper. Thirdly, if you will be careless under time constraints, I can guarantee you that the number of mistakes you will make in this paper will be much greater than what you do for other paper.
Basically, what is tested has absolutely nothing to do with what is taught. The whole paper is an IQ test about programming, I can guarantee you that it has very little to do with how hard-working you are, as the sole purpose of the paper is just to give you a hard time.
For the T/F questions, some questions are out of syllabus and the concepts were never mentioned in any occasions, nor are they important, whether you know them or not has nothing to do with whether you are a good programmer.
And then the next question asks us to implement them line by line, excuse me, why don’t you just ask us the final output? What’s the point?
And the long questions were basically disaster, they forced you to use particular methods by limiting your number of lines to the answers, which is so stupid because a key part of programming is about algorithmic creativity, his practice of limiting our creativity is extremely discouraging and inappropriate for any purpose as an introductory programming course. Also, the number of questions and reading we need to do exceeded a normal time limit outrageously, come on it is not 2012H, it’s so pointless that the mean last year is 55 and they probably think it’s not low enough and decided to add two more questions to our paper, and of course our paper was much harder than last semester as well, which makes it extremely devastating to even take the exam. It is definitely the worst midterm I have ever taken.
Note: the reason why I say he pretends to focus on exam is that he always say “in the exam I can ask you …”, but he actually never does. All he asks are the Demon questions that anre extremely hard to come up with as an intro, and he has never offered any clues to help us with the exam. All he says is the basics of the basics and won’t help you get more than 30 marks in the exam.
To be honest I understand why he does this because so many people are already experienced and he does not want to make the paper too easy so those people get full marks easily, but he fails to accommodate major students’ needs and tricking us and showing off and telling us how weak we are become his whole purpose given the ratio of demon questions and terrible time constraint. This is pure nonsense

To get points for the lab, you are requied to attend the lab and finish the requirement / program, and submit it to the ZINC (automatic grading system) on or before the deadline as stated on the lab page. There are 10 lab exercises, only 5 of them will be graded. Each graded lab is worth 10 points. Only the best 4 out of the 5 graded labs will count towards final grade. No late lab assignment will be accepted.


Each lab attendance will be awarded 1 point. Since there are a total of 10 lab meetings, the maximum attendance points you can earn are 10.


To earn attendance points for each lab, you need to answer questions from the teaching assistants during your lab time. To make sure you stay for the first 60 minutes, please use the iPRS to answer all the questions. You won't be penalized for wrong answers, so we encourage you to try answering everything. At the end of the lab, your teaching assistant will randomly select half of the questions to check, and you'll earn 1 attendance point if you answered all of those. If you run into any technical issues that prevent you from submitting your answers using iPRS, please inform the teaching assistants before the lab ends. They won't be able to assist you if you wait until after the lab, and you might lose your lab attendance score as a result.

comment_content:
The title of this course is kind of a scam. This really isn't an "introduction" to python programming. If this is your first time programming, I highly suggest to drop this course and take COMP 1021 + 1028 instead to fulfill the requirements.

The content covers your standard topics of python and programming such as control flow, conditional logic, loops, data types etc. It then covers some data analysis libraries i.e. numpy, pandas and matplotlib which are useful if you are going to take AI courses in the future.

However, personally, I think some of the content chosen is of a very poor standard for an introduction course. I feel like recursion as well as the data analysis libraries aren't really very beginner level concepts. In fact, I felt some useful content such as exception handling, string manipulation, file handling etc. were left out which is much more essential than something like matplotlib for e.g.

Also pandas and matplotlib might as well have not been in the syllabus because barely anything was covered for them. It was basically a "just read the documentation bro" chapter and that's how the exam questions were set from it as well.
comment_teaching:
I did not attend a single lecture because they were boring as fuck if you already knew python well. Also I just find it much faster to read the notes and practice yourself than attend lectures but that's just my preference.

However, I did attend the labs (cuz mandatory attendance) and I'll say that the teaching was fucking dogshit, not cuz the TAs couldn't teach, but cuz of what they actually taught. As beginners, I think it is much more important to focus on how to use programming concepts to solve problems rather than focusing on how they work in depth. Yet all the lab quizzes tested dumbass knowledge like what the output of a confusing bundle of if-statements should be etc. This was useful for me, as a programmer with a solid python background, as it solidified my understanding of python as a programming language, but if you never coded before, this is just useless knowledge.

The self tests suffered from the same issues, they just tested in depth internal working of python code, not how to write python code to solve specific problems. At least the uploaded some practice problems, but that was like only 1 week before the final exam. They should have uploaded those problems way before; I think that would have helped the true beginners a lot more.
comment_workload:
Ok my grade for workload is for people for whom this course is actually an "introduction" to python. If it is, you have to dedicate hours to solving the lab exercises and programming assignments. To prepare for exams, I would suggest solving some easy leetcode problems using python as that would reenforce your python problem solving abilities.

If this course isn't an introduction to python for you, the workload will be much less, probably an A- grade, but the exams will be challenging nonetheless so still prepare seriously, especially if you are not that good at problem solving using code.

comment_content: Others have already covered the syllabus, so I'll just talk about my thoughts on it. Having already known Python before this course, I was surprised at how in-depth they went with every piece of syntax. There are situations you don't normally see in real-life programming covered just so that you know exactly what every keyword does, and what happens when you put them all together. This is up to personal preference; some people may like this, others may not. But you can't argue that this approach does train more competent programmers, even if it is tedious and difficult to master.
comment_teaching:
Desmond's teaching style is...entertaining, and also easy to understand. He draws diagrams showing how the variables store data so that you won't mess up the concepts. I really like that he uploads Zoom videos, which have much better audio quality than the typical RVC videos, and tbh I don't really go to class - I just watch the videos sped up. But the freedom of being able to pause anytime to take notes and think through the content is very helpful.

I personally haven't approached him to ask any questions, but judging by how I often see him talking to students, he's very willing to help if you run into any problems. The anonymous Piazza forum on Canvas also allows you to ask questions if you don't like talking to people face-to-face.
comment_workload:
It's no secret that this course's content is quite challenging, especially to people who have never touched programming before. Even though I've learned Python before, I felt that this course hit my sweet spot of just enough challenge that I really have to think to solve the problems. Plus there are weekly Labs (shorter exercises, still challenging) and Programming Assignments (much longer exercises, several times longer than labs, difficulty is a bit harder than labs). So quite a bit of difficult work to tackle.

It might be a good idea to get yourself familiar with some programming fundamentals first before taking this course. But as someone who took it my very first semester in uni, I think anyone can do so too; it's not the type of course you need to wait further into your academic career before taking.
comment_grading: The grading is fair. You make a mistake in your work, you get marks deducted for it. But if you are just aiming for around 80% or higher, it should be pretty doable as long as you have had enough practice and are familiar with the language.

comment_content:
Covered basic python programming syntax and concepts in 3/4 of the term. Then start teaching numpy/pandas/matplotlib in the remaining 1/4 term. I think the teaching on numpy/pandas/matplotlib are quite accelerated and inadequate in teaching time, especially when topics of other courses are getting harder near final exam to squeeze your time. You probably need to self study those libraries usage before they start covering numpy

btw i think numpy/pandas/matplotlib are useless. This is probably the last time in my life using them. Pandas' design is horrible, you will never know whether data should be inputted in column-based or row-based. The way it index data is also horrible and confusing, iloc[] ??? sucks. Should be removed from the syllabus. Thats why i gonna give an E in content.

(the following parts are copied)
1. Introduction to Computers and Programming
2. Python Programming Fundamentals
- fundamental data types
- fundamental operators like +, -, *, /, assignment operators and logic operators
- short circuiting/lazy operation of and, or
- caches of small integers
3. Branching Statements
- if statements
- match case statements
4. Looping Statements
- for and while
5. Collections – container data types
- list and tuple, and their operators, slicing
- deep copy and shallow copy in list
- dict and their operator
6. Modularization – Functions and Recursions
- functions, type hint, positional argument and keyword argument
- global variables
- passing mutable object to functions
- recursions
7. Modularization - Modules, Packages, and Libraries
- functional programming using reduce, map functions
- lambda functions
8. Object-Oriented Programming
- use of class and their "public", "private", "protected" members, name mangling
9. NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib
- NumPy, their operations and properties, and broadcasting
- introduced very minimal Pandas and Matplotlib

comment_teaching:
Desmond is genuinely a good teacher, he's always energetic and makes the lectures as interesting as possible. Lectures are recorded.

The TA however, is on the opposite side of the scale. He speaks poor English and is quite hard to understand, he's clearly inexperienced, the labs are poorly organized, and can at times be boring, which made me despise attending labs.
comment_workload:
Labs are weekly and you have to answer around 4 IPRS questions each time to get attendance points, be punctual.

4 out of 5 lab exercises are counted towards the grade, labs in the beginning are quite easy but get progressively harder, though they help a lot in getting you familiar with the topics, don't miss an exercise.

The programming assignments are quite difficult and time consuming, each can take up to 30 hours including debugging time if you're a beginner, so start early.

comment_content:
Course Description
This introductory course is designed to provide a comprehensive overview of the Python programming
language and its core concepts, such as data types, variables, operators, and control structures, as well as
the collection data types, including lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries. Additionally, it covers the language's
functions and the fundamentals of object-oriented programming. The course also explores a range of useful
Python modules and packages, such as NumPy, Pandas, Matplotlib, through meaningful examples and
applications, as well as hands-on experimentation using a well-known integrated development environment
and AI tools. Furthermore, this course will discuss good programming practices and debugging skills.
List of Topics
1. Introduction to Computers and Programming
2. Python Programming Fundamentals
3. Branching Statements
4. Looping Statements
5. Collections – container data types
6. Modularization – Functions and Recursions
7. Modularization - Modules, Packages, and Libraries
8. Object-Oriented Programming
9. NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib

comment_content:
The content is quite difficult if you don’t have any programming experience. The exams are really hard (at least for me it was) and basic understanding of python will not do you much good. On top of that, it a mandatory course for some non-CS majors so I would say it is going to very difficult for them.

It starts off rather easy. However, soon recursion, numpy, pandas and mathplotlib are introduced in a very short period of time. These topics are quite challenging and difficult to understand.
comment_teaching: The lecturer I had was really good. He tried his best to engage with students and made an effort to know about you. He will give you advice appropriately and guide you if you are struggling. However some of the TAs for the mandatory labs are very bad. The main TA for my lab was good, but sometimes he was replaced by other TAs who are terrible at teaching.
comment_workload: Workload of the course is manageable but self-study is crucial for this course. You will have labs due every 2 weeks with 2 programming assignments (one before midterm and one before finals). The labs and programming assignments are easier compared to the exams. It is possible to get full makes on them just don’t cram the night before they are due since you need to fine-tune them.
comment_grading:
I have to say grading is very good. Even if you do bad in your exams you will have 2 programming assignments, 10 labs and lab attendance to bring your grade up. Try to keep full marks on the other things. Just attend the labs and answer the IPRS questions (you don’t even need to get them correct just answer them). Lectures aren’t mandatory but labs are.
Grading is:
10%- labs+lab attendance
25%- programming assignments
25%- midterm
40%- finals

comment_content:
1. Introduction
2. Basic Programming Language Elements
- very basic stuff like main(), data types, input()
3. Python Basic Operations
- just symbols like these: + - * / % // == >= !=
4. Branching (aka Checking & Selection)
- use of if-elif-else, match-case
5. Looping Statement
- for loop, while loop, for-else loop, while-else loop
6. Collections - Container Data Types (Part I)
- list, list slicing, shallow copy, deep copy, 2D list
7. Functions (Part I)
- define a function, call a function, also teach some algorithm with if-else
8. Functions (Part II)
- global variables, list passing, parameter passing, 3 lambda functions: filter(), map(), reduce()
9. Collections - Container Data Types (Part II)
- tuple, dictionary
10. Recursion
11. Numpy
12. Introduction to Object-Oriented Programming
13. Pandas
14. Matplotlib
comment_teaching:
Cecia is a good tutor. Desmond is even better. The teaching team (including TAs) are supportive and reply to our question on Piazza very fast.
But I want to point out that reading ppts is not enough for you to learn Python. Spend some time on Youtube watch some Python tutorial video and try writing some simple codes on your own. It would definitely help you to memorize the syntax and functions.
If you think the content is easy, go self learn the difficult topics like Numpy, Pandas, Matplotlib, oop.
comment_workload:
If you know some Python, you don't really need to study the mid-term. For those who knows nothing about Python, if you don't work hrad, you are cooked. PA1 was said to be too difficult for beginners. PA2 involved really difficult oop stuff and some numpy. It really take some times to debug while doing PA. AI is always a good helper, though it may consider as plagiarism.
Final exam is tough as the 3 libraries Numpy, Pandas, Matplotlib is just a whole bunch of functions. You need to memorize some function names and know what are the parameters. The questions are also challenging, I have no idea how ppl get a 97.25/100.

comment_content:
1. Introduction to Computing and Programming Basics
   Overview of computers, basic programming concepts, and the purpose of programming languages.

2. Core Python Fundamentals
   - Basic built-in data types (int, float, str, bool, None)
   - Essential operators: arithmetic (+ − * /), assignment, comparison, logical (and, or, not)
   - Short-circuit (lazy) evaluation in and / or expressions
   - Small integer caching / interning behavior

3. Decision Making – Conditional Execution
   - if, elif, else statements
   - Structural pattern matching with match … case

4. Repetition – Loops
   - for loops (including iteration over sequences)
   - while loops
   - Loop control: break, continue, else clause on loops

5. Container / Collection Data Types
   - Sequences: list and tuple – creation, methods, operators, slicing
   - Shallow copy vs deep copy (especially with nested structures)
   - Dictionaries: creation, access, modification, common operations, dictionary views

6. Code Organization I – Functions & Recursion
   - Defining functions, parameters, return values
   - Type hints / function annotations
   - Positional arguments, keyword arguments, default values
   - Variable scope: local vs global (global / nonlocal keywords)
   - Passing mutable vs immutable objects to functions
   - Recursion: base case, recursive case, call stack behavior

7. Code Organization II – Modules, Packages & Functional Tools
   - Creating and importing modules
   - Packages and the role of __init__.py
   - Lambda (anonymous) functions
   - Higher-order functions: map(), filter()

8. Object-Oriented Programming in Python
   - Defining classes and creating instances
   - Instance variables and methods
   - public and "private" naming conventions
   - Name mangling (__variable) and its purpose

9. Python Library – NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib
   - NumPy basics: ndarrays, vectorized operations, shape, axes, broadcasting
   - Very basic Pandas: Series, DataFrame, simple selection and filtering
   - Minimal Matplotlib: line plots, scatter plots, basic figure customization(Taught in tutorial)

comment_content:
The course taught basic python concepts: types, control flow, functions, collections, classes, etc, coupled with introduction to popular python packages (numpy, pandas, and matplotlib).

Course content on python concepts were very approachable and their assignments weren't too difficult. The problematic part was the python packages: each (of three!) have their own quirks and mechanisms (e.g. numpy's indexing and broadcasting) that has nothing to do those previously learnt python concepts. Each package is a whole new surface to cover, yet they were all squeezed into just the last few lectures. The course should've allocated more time for students to digest this last part.
comment_teaching:

Programming assignments were more complex and took one/several afternoons. The assignments were always posted several weeks before the deadline so there was always enough time to work on them.

comment_content: The course content is well-structured and starts from the absolute basics, making it truly accessible for beginners. It systematically progresses to more complex topics. While the concepts themselves are not inherently "hard," the pace can be challenging as it requires quick assimilation and consistent practice to keep up.
comment_teaching: The teaching quality is a highlight of the course. Instructors are typically clear, engaging, and supportive. They explain concepts thoroughly and are responsive to questions during lectures and office hours. The use of practical examples helps solidify theoretical understanding.
comment_workload: The workload is substantial. It includes regular programming assignments, quizzes, lab work, and a final project. Some assignments, particularly those involving integrated concepts or external libraries (like numpy which is crazy hard for beginners), can be difficult for beginners, demanding considerable time for research, trial-and-error, and debugging.
comment_grading: The grading scheme is generally considered fair and lenient in terms of marking criteria. However, the course curve can feel intense due to the presence of many highly skilled students (including those with prior programming experience or exceptional aptitude). For a true beginner, achieving a top grade requires significant effort and proactive learning.

comment_grading:
The course uses "relative grading". (according to unofficial data, around 70% of students get B-A range)
It counts scores in 4 area:
Lab(including attendance(all labs) and exercise x4 out of 5)
PA(programme assignment)x2
Mid-term
Final

Both Labs and PAs provide shown test cases(50%) and skeleton codes, so it is possible to understand what it requires u to finish. There are also 50% of hidden test cases to ensure ur code is reasonable instead of by luck or hard coding. Both of them provide unlimited attempts before deadlines so u can try as many times as possible to get at least 50% marks. They do take u time to understand and finish so finish them asap is recommended. For Labs, PA1 and PA2, I got 100%, 95% and 100% respectively. So they are possible for beginners like me to finish those assignments.

Since the contents for mid-term are covered in secondary school, I found that it is impossible for me to be above upper quartile. Yet, having the new topic numpy, most ppl learnt from the beginning like me. Listening to lecturer's teaching and asking tas questions, I was above the upper quartile in the final.

The exam curve might seem to be terrifying, but it is possible for beginners to get a good grade. I got an A- in this course which was out of expectation.

comment_content:
No turtle anymore

1. Introduction to Computers and Programming
2. Python Programming Fundamentals
- fundamental data types, mutable objects and immutable objects
- fundamental operators like +, -, *, /, assignment operators and logic operators
- short circuiting/lazy operation of and, or
- caches of small integers
3. Branching Statements
- if statements
- match case statements
4. Looping Statements
- for and while
5. Collections – container data types
- list and tuple, and their operators, slicing
- deep copy and shallow copy in list
- nature of list and tuple: list are mutable, tuple are immutable
- dict and their operator, immutable keys
- list/tuple/dict comprehension (useful for one-liners)
6. Modularization – Functions and Recursions
- functions, type hint, positional argument and keyword argument
- global variables
- passing mutable object to functions
- recursions
7. Modularization - Modules, Packages, and Libraries
- functional programming using reduce, map, filter functions
- lambda functions
8. Object-Oriented Programming
- use of class and their "public", "private", "protected" members, name mangling
9. NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib
- NumPy, their operations and properties, and broadcasting, fancy indexing, slicing
- introduced very minimal Pandas and Matplotlib

PS: Talking about the notorious NumPy, it seems to be included to connect the content of COMP2211, so if you don't plan to take COMP2211 in your later semesters, it's quite natural to think that module is useless, but whatever this course tries to provide a smooth transition to COMP2211 and that's the aim of this course, so I won't say sth like teaching this module in this course is bad, but it's true that this course's content and schedule are quite immature, with a rush teaching in numpy and shift quite a long time in teaching basic syntaxes. I believe the details of its immaturity is being emphasized a lot in many other reviews.

PS: Some people are just complaining the limitation of prohibiting the use of for, while loops in NumPy question. comment_content:
This course serves as an efficient and well-designed gateway for programmers to enter the world of C. It is perfectly tailored for students with prior coding experience in higher-level languages (like Python or Java) who need to understand C's lower-level mechanics and syntax. It provides a focused, practical introduction with minimal friction.

The curriculum effectively translates general programming concepts—variables, control flow, loops, and functions—into the specific context of C. It wisely progresses into the more advanced topics that are crucial for C proficiency, such as pointers, manual memory management, and possibly structures and file I/O. The guided programming exercises are essential here, as they provide hands-on practice with C's unique and sometimes challenging features, solidifying the self-learned theory.
comment_teaching: The fully online format is executed effectively. The self-learning materials are presumably clear and comprehensive, allowing students to absorb the conceptual material at their own pace. This is well-complemented by the practical exercises. The format is ideal for self-motivated learners who benefit from flexibility while transitioning to a new and more complex language paradigm.
comment_workload: The workload is consistently light and manageable, structured around approximately one lab or exercise set per week. This pace allows adequate time to grapple with C's distinctive concepts, such as pointers, without feeling rushed or overwhelmed. It fits comfortably alongside other academic or professional commitments.
comment_grading: The Pass/Fail grading scheme is a significant strength of this bridging course. It removes performance pressure and encourages experimentation and deep learning, which is particularly valuable when tackling C's steeper learning curve. The course is clearly designed for student success; consistent engagement with the weekly material virtually guarantees a pass, fostering a supportive and low-anxiety environment.

experience in computer programming but wish to learn how to apply those programming skills to the C
language. The course covers basic programming topics, such as variables, control, loops, and functions, to
more advanced topics. Students explore these by self-learning of course materials together with guided
programming exercises. Students without the prerequisites but possess relevant programming knowledge
may seek instructor's approval for enrolling in the course. Graded P or F.

comment_teaching: The course is structured for independent learning. Each subject area is accompanied by instructional videos and sample exercises, which is a thoughtful approach. It required me several hours per week to thoroughly review the course materials.
comment_workload:
The course materials were updated weekly. Typically, it took me several hours each week to review the content, test the code examples, complete the review questions, and submit the exercises. The overall workload was quite manageable.

I spent the entire night before the final exam reviewing the material, only to realize the next day that this extensive preparation was unnecessary, as the exam itself was very straightforward...

The online exercises were simple and allowed me to easily search for the answers using Google.
comment_grading:

2.40% of the final exam
The assignments are straightforward and easy. Additionally, the final exam is quite simple.

Given these criteria, it is practically impossible to fail this course.

comment_content: I recently completed COMP1021 in the fall semester and was eager to explore other programming languages. This course seems to be a good fit for my interests. Compared to Python, C has a more complex syntax, but the concepts I learned in COMP1021 have helped me understand the use of several functions in C. Overall, the content of the course is appropriate for a 1-credit course.
comment_teaching: I had high expectations for this course, but unfortunately, I was somewhat disappointed with the delivery. While Gibson brought a lot of passion to COMP1021, I didn't feel the same level of engagement in this course. Although I knew that it was an online course, I didn't anticipate that the course materials would consist only of text, screenshots, and files. The absence of video explanations made it difficult for me to understand certain concepts at times. Additionally, the only way to reach Gibson and the TA was through email, which was inconvenient in some situations. I would have appreciated a Zoom session for inquiries.
comment_workload:
The workload for this course depends on your goals.
If you want to take it easy, the workload is relatively small.
However, if you want to ensure that you have a thorough understanding of the course materials, the workload can be quite substantial. COMP1021 is a 3-credit course with 7 labs, so one might expect that COMP1029C, which is a 1-credit course, would have 2-3 exercises. Unfortunately, there are 4 exercises. I enrolled in COMP1029C on the last day of the ADP, and I also took COMP1029V, so I felt like I was constantly fighting against deadlines. It took me about 30 minutes to read, take notes, and complete the quiz (which doesn't count towards the grade) for each section of a lesson, but it took me hours to complete each exercise.
comment_grading: Overall, the grading for this course is reasonable. To pass the course, you need to score at least 60% on the exercises and 40% on the final exam. There are four exercises in total, and they are not overly difficult. You only need to complete three of them and score well to pass the course. The final exam is a one-hour exam with 20 questions, most of which are straightforward, although a few are a bit tricky. It is relatively easy to score 40% or higher on the exam.

comment_content:
The very basics of C.

2. For those who plan to take COMP2011, taking COMP1029C beforehand does not help much. You will have a little bit of revision on loops and recursion here, but you will not learn pointers.

comment_content:
Basic C programming stuff.

If you have enrolled in COMP1021/1022P/1022Q before(which most of you did), you will not have any difficulties to understand the course materials. The concepts used in C/C++ programming are pretty much the same as python/java. You just need to learn the syntax used in C/C++ specifically.

Overall this is just an 1 credit bridging course. Only basic and fundamental concepts are introduced.
comment_teaching:
The course materials are provided through the internet. You don’t need to attend any class.

This year almost no videos were provided. The course materials mainly consisted of plain text and pictures. Everything was well-organised and explained clearly.
comment_workload:
The course materials were updated once a week. Usually, It took me 4-5 hours to read through the materials, test the codes, finish the review questions and submit exercise. The workload was very light.

I revised the whole night before the final exam. Then I recognised that it was completely unnecessary since the exam was very straightforward...
comment_grading:
In order to gain a pass, you need to fulfil both requirements listed below.
1. Obtain 60% or above in 4 assignments total mark
2. Obtain 40% or above in the final exam

Gaining full mark in all assignments shouldn’t be a problem. The final exam is very easy and not tricky at all.

It is impossible to fail the course unless you do it on purpose. Otherwise, you must be a legend.

The professor and the TA (Wallace Mak) responded to emails well with good explanation and details. Even though there was no CSE help center, the instructors of the course are very helpful.

comment_content:
This is a good introductory course to C programming languages. If you've learnt Java before, you'll find C syntax to be quite similar to that of Java. This course would be quite useful if you're gonna take COMP2011 later.

Topics included:

      🔶 For Loops

      🔶 Variable Scope

comment_teaching:
There are no face-to-face lectures.

All I needed to do was to visit the course websites and read through all the content. The concepts are all well structured and clearly delivered, so you should find no problem understanding the course content.

There are also instructional step-by-step videos teaching you how to do the assignments, so if you're stuck with the assignments you can always have a look. But of course, it would be more rewarding if you do the assignments and think them over all by yourself.
comment_workload: The only workload is the 4 assignments submitted weekly (for summer), so the workload is light for a 1-credit course. The assignments, however, are quite difficult at times. But since there are instructional videos, I had no difficulty finishing them at all.
comment_grading:
Pass or fail.

Need to get above 60% overall in the assignments (by finishing at least 3 out of 4 of them) and 40% in the final exam. These should be a piece of cake as soon as you pay attention to the deadlines and revise for the exam.

comment_content:
This course gets you with some very basic concepts for C programming, for example, input/output, loops, arrays, structure, recursion,etc. They are just pieces of cake for those who have some basic programming senses.
For a 1-credit course, it  is super easy and not time-consuming at all.
comment_teaching:
Go online and you will get everything you want.(lecture materials, exercises with video tutorial,etc.)
Gibson is just perfect, his materials are funny and easy to understand.
comment_workload: The course requires you to do 4 exercises in total within about 20 days each, but after watching Gibson's video tutorial, it would take you at most 1 hour to finish the exercises each, even if you have not learnt on that part at all. But you need to read through the web page to at least understand what was taught.
comment_grading:
it is a 1 credit course.
those who gets 60/100 on average in all the 4 exercises of the course and get 40%+ in the written final exam would pass the course.
As the exercises are super easy, reaching the 60/100 average mark is a piece of cake.

Wow.

Almost impossible to fail.

comment_content: Overall, it's not a difficult course with 1 credit. The content involves introduction and some basic functions, which are adequate to use them to make a small game. The contents are easy to understand and there are videos to help you understand better. If you just want to get to know this programming language without occupying too much time, then it's a good choice.
comment_teaching: The course is designed for self-study. Each topic will be matched up with videos and examples, which is quite considerate.
comment_workload: There are several (about 3-4) assignments to finish throughout the semester and the answer will be released afterwards. There are also a few questions for each topic to help you understand better.
comment_grading: There is no mid-term or quizzes for this course. The final exam is the only examination we have to take, which is very easy indeed. Regarding grades, there are only pass and fail for this course. As long as you have done all the assignments and review before the exam, I believe that getting a P is easy.

comment_content: Very green-hand friendly. Even to students without much programming background. Use games as assignments, which are quite interesting.
comment_teaching: Hard to judge as there's no face-to-face teaching.
comment_workload:
Just a 1-credit course so the workload is very very low.
Even lower for students with programming background, just spend 1-2 hours a week to work on course work.
Strongly recommended, just learn something during summer!
comment_grading:
Should be easy to get passed.
To pass the course, you have to finish 3 of 4 total assignments and get 60% of total marks.
And you have to get at least 40% of total scores in final exam.

comment_content: Learn some basic java language through BlueJ, you can learn text input/output, loops, classes. Array, file input/output. The most basic java language learning. Basic language learning is not very difficult, but requires some simple understanding and memorization of different language formats. The java language overlaps with many basic knowledge points.
comment_teaching: The course clearly explains the code through text and pictures. In addition, some examples are provided for you to download and learn from. In addition to the regular mandatory assignments, you can also complete some additional review questions. Very simple but also very interesting, there are many vivid examples to learn from. The professor likes to give some vivid examples to help us understand abstract concepts, and the class is very lively and interesting.
comment_workload: The workload of this course is not very large, because the students have a certain foundation. Just some basic learning about the java language, is to lay the foundation for the later a course. The amount of time spent per week is very small, and only needs to complete normal homework and watch videos. Don't worry about whether you have time to complete this assignment and course, because compared with other courses, the homework is very small. There is very little to do.
comment_grading: pass/fail. There are no specific grades, just pass and fail grades. The exam is very simple and 40%, and the questions focus on what the output is rather than asking you to write code, so some of the formatting of the code can be ignored. Homework accounts for 60 percent, which is a very large percentage. So doing homework is a very good choice, and homework can help us understand the language better.

comment_content:
Basic Java
1. Using BlueJ
2. Strings and Loops
3. Class and arrays
4. File I/O and Recursion
5. Greenfoot

the concept of class and arrays are quite useful for 2XXX COMP courses like COMP2011/2012. Somehow better than 1029C for preparation for COMP2011. (Why they don't use Java to teach OOP tho...)

If you cannot enroll due to not finishing COMP1021 by the time applying, just waive pre requisite and state you are taking COMP1021, Gibson is nice and he will usually allow you to enroll.
comment_teaching:
The canvas page might seems to be redundant for pros, but quite clear and easy to read for a Java Rookie like me.

No lessons, just read canvas page online
comment_workload:
simple HWs, can be done within 1-2 hours each week even in month-long Winter/Summer semester

Just ChatGPT or Deepseek the assignment if you have really no idea (just kidding don't, email TAs or find you peers for help instead)
comment_grading:
Pass / Fail course
40% in exam
60% in HWs (4 in total) to pass
cheat sheets can be used in final

Final is not too hard and you should be able to pass if you do some basic preparation

comment_content:
4 (actually 5, but the first is an intro) chapters in total: basic java stuffs using mainly BlueJ, and also last chapter Greenfoot;

However organization of notes is not good, some lengthy examples are tiring to read, kind of unnecessary too,
Just fking tell me the syntax and it's good enough;

Any online java lesson would probably be better than this course;

Also you cannot learn or do new homework until it unlocks the chapter, why, Gibson ?

no any lesson, just self-study (reading from notes, no videos), maybe learn from other online resources to get better understanding.

Also is Gibson so lazy? Why give homework grades so late, but give final grades so fast (just 1 day after exam) ?? Maybe he don't want to work in Chinese New year I guess.
comment_workload: Nearly no workload at all, can easily complete whole chapter in just 1 day, each homework (exercise) have about a week for you to work with, but actually only need like 15 minutes as it's just fill in the blanks if you  fast enough. 4 Homework in total.
comment_grading:
Very easy to pass:
get 60+/100 for 4 homework (each 25%)
0 effort needed to get full marks

and 40+/100 in 1 hour final exam
also very easy, even without cheat sheet
the average is like 74 ?

comment_content:
An introduction course for Java program.
This course teaches the basic concepts of Java program and it is also suitable for those who don’t have any programming background.
comment_teaching: This course is a self learning course. No lectures or tutorials need to be attended, notes will be realised by time to time in canvas. You can take your time to learn those notes, but remember to hand in your labs on time.
comment_workload: If you take this course in a regular term(fall/spring) there are about 1 lab a month and it takes more time for the labs in the later modules.
comment_grading:
This is a pass fail course.
Final: 60% (open book)
Labs: 40% ( you can only hand in 3 of them if you can make sure you can get a full mark in all 3 labs)

comment_content:
The course includes some basic knowledge of Java, e.g. loops, methods (called functions in other programming language), BlueJ, etc.
as long as you have had a touch in programming, the contents should be easy to you (and it's okay even if you had never)
You would make a little game at the end of the course and it is fun to see the result coming out.
After the course, you would know why Java is called object-oriented.
comment_teaching:
The contents are taught by texts and some examples. There is no 'teacher', so there's nothing to be graded tbh.
but the teaching materials are written clearly so that you could understand just by reading it.
i believe you could still seek out to ask questions if you have any, but i did not contact the course instructor throughout the course so i'm not sure about it.
comment_workload:
again, your goal is to get a passing score
finishing four tasks is def not difficult or time-consuming
each task took me less than an hour to finish (the first two even took less)
final exam is open-note, you could bring your own notes in hardcopy (and i rmb there was no page limit? i brought ard 3 A4 pages, if there is a limit, it should be something close to this ), no electronic devices allowed
comment_grading:
This course is pass/ fail only, so your goal is to pass (60% in total), not to answer every question or finish every task perfectly
The course includes 4 graded tasks and one final exam.
What you need to do is finish at least two (or three, sorry i dont remember so well) out of four tasks, and get 60% or above including final exam

comment_content: Basics about Java is included in this course, and the tools used in this course is BlueJ and Greenfoot.
comment_teaching: This is an online self paced course, so just read the notes and materials in the canvas. The materials are clear and easy to understand as well.
comment_workload: The 4 assignments have the skeleton code and the instruction for the assignment is very clear as well. Hence, the workload is really low compare with other coding course (as it is an 1 credit course of course)
comment_grading:
4 Assignment + Final Exam
The assignment and exam are easy as it is just a bridging course. As this is an online course, this is an open book and open notes exam.

comment_content: 非常基本的java入門。包含了在COMP1021學過的一些相似概念，相差的地方大同小異，不過是在Java上再用出來而已。建議只學過python的同學能趁winter 或 summer了解下，尤期是year 1。這對你學其他programming language 更有幫助，作為COMP2011之前的過渡不錯。而對programming已經有一定經驗的人來說，這是一個白嫖one credit 的course。無他，基本上放好小effort都可以pass。
comment_teaching: 全筆記，靠自己。沒有課堂，沒有影片。你要學的內容都要自己閱讀。對一些自學沒有信心or 自律的人來說很不友好。
comment_workload: 非常輕，唯二的負擔就是熟習java的語法同啲簡單的功課。基本上都給了指引，跟着做就可以了。
comment_grading:
有四份功課，每份功課佔分一樣，你要在功課上拿到六成的分數才能合格，即如果你做了三份功課，加起來已經60分，你可以不做第四份。
Exam要四成分數才算合格，放心，題目非常簡單，比起COMP1012 來說相當直白。加上允許帶筆記，且沒有頁數限制，你可以將筆記內容都打印出來帶去exam。但事實上沒有這個需要，輕輕鬆鬆就可以pass。

記得要功課同exam都合格先會當你完成個course。

comment_content:
Learning about basic Java programming which features the use of  BlueJ and Greenfoot. Suitable for those who are interested in learning Java and are looking for a starting point to dive into the Java world. Not suitable for those who have already learned the basics and looking for advanced content.

Rough syllabus:
1. Data types, operators, use of Java package
2. Print text and get input from users
3. If, Switch
4. For loop, While loop, Do While loop
5. Object-oriented programming in Java(class, method, instance)
6. Array in Java
7. Inheritance & Interface
8. Types of error, catching exceptions
9. Using path, read and write a file
10. Recursion
11. Greenfoot section(making a game)
comment_teaching:
Self-learning, self-pacing. You need to read through the materials on Canvas on your own. The amount of material is not heavy, about 3-4 sections every week with several examples. If you are hardworking enough, learning through all of these would be a piece of cake. Recommend actually downloading BlueJ and Greenfoot and following the examples to have a better understanding.
If you could not push yourself to study on your own, this course may not suit you.
comment_workload:
Very little workload. Only 3-4 pieces of material to learn every week, along with 1 piece of homework.

About the homework :
1. Gave you a template. You only need to fill in specific parts, just like in COMP1021.
2. Only 3-4 simple tasks. You may check your program with the given example.
3. Most of the part is copied and pasted from the example. If not, from the previous learning material. You may always find TA  for help if you really could not figure it out.
4. Make sure you check the requirement carefully.
5. Make sure you submit the correct file. (.java or .zip, depending on the homework)
comment_grading:
A Pass or Fail course. If you achieve:
1. Submitting at least 3 homework and having good scores among them
2. Getting at least 40% on the final exam
Then you would pass the course.

About the final exam:
1. Open book, open notes. So do not hesitate to make some cheat sheets to bring to your exam.
2. Mixed with MCs and Short Questions. Does not require you to write a whole program on your own suddenly.
3. Most questions are straightforward and basic. Don't be stressed - Getting 40% is all you need!

comment_content:
The contents are good but not very deep, which include some basics of Java, basic OOP, Error handling, File I/O, and probably the least useful: Greenfoot.

The course suggests you to use BlueJ for a better visualization of classes and methods but I prefer to use vscode. Still you need to know how to use BlueJ as there are exam questions on BlueJ.
comment_teaching: There's no teaching at all. You just go through the canvas pages and read the materials yourself.
comment_workload: It's a 1-credit course so there's really not much workload. Every assignments can be finished within 2 hours with the guidelines from the assignment.
comment_grading:
Easy to pass the course. Just get >60% on assignment and >40%  on final exam (which should be open book) .

I got 3/4 assignment full mark and 1 assignment 0 mark because I submitted the wrong file, and I got 72% in final exam.

comment_content:
Content is just a very simple introduction to java, designed for someone with no experience. Uses BlueJ and GreenFoot, not practical but good for beginners to visualise what they're making.

The content is presented on Canvas, and is very easy to get through (only takes like an hour maximum). There are five of these pages overall, which results in only a few hours of content for a full semester. However, this is probably in line with most other 1-credit courses.
comment_teaching: There is no teacher for the course, so cannot say. I hear Gibson is a good one though.
comment_workload:
Basically non-existent. The final exam takes like an hour of revision to pass, and was quite easy. The assignments are all quite straightforward, and just use what you've learnt from the canvas pages.
There is around a few hours of work in this course that you need to do, so very good, can finish the entire course's content in a day.
comment_grading: It is a pass/fail course so the grading is no problem. Passing involved the exam and assignments which are both very easy to do well in.

comment_content: It is a course which will bring you through the basics of java, like hello world, basic data handling and stuff. 5 Lessons in total, you will have spare time to finish each of them with half-a-day. Starting from lesson 4, you will learn some basic of OOP, which includes Inheritance, Interfaces, error handling, file input and output or even coordinate handling in a 2D world. But again, easy stuff, what do you expect from a 1-credit course and the best grade you can get is a Pass.
comment_teaching: Learn by yourself, only words are there to read and thus to teach. If you are really really struggling, you could contact TA -- Google. There're actually some real TAs and I'd say Google could do the job much faster.
comment_workload: I didn't expect to create 5 programs in a 1-credit course, so I rate it a C.
comment_grading: Easy to pass, impossible to get A. You only need to identify the syntax errors in the codes in the final. And in some extreme cases, a dry run table will get you through all the problems.

comment_content:
The content is easy and more concentrates on the usage of blueJ.
BuleJ is helpful
comment_teaching: There is no lecture and we are required to do self studying. You can ask the TA for help if you have questions.
comment_workload: There is little workload.What you need to do is illustrated clearly.
comment_grading: It is a p/f course. Programming exercises: submit and obtain 60% or higher of the four exercises total mark. That means you could submit three out of four exercises and still pass the requirement, given that you get very good marks for them. Final examination: obtain 40% or higher in the final exam

comment_content:
Am I the only one who thought this can be counted in the best 24 cred in the major selection and enrolled in this course? 🤔 (It is P/F so it don't count :P)

Simple Java using BlueJ and Greenfoot.
learn it yourself by reading the notes on Canvas. It is a Canvas page, not pdf files. There are 5 parts. Part 2~5 have a homework in the end so 4 homework in total. He unlocks 1 part every month and the homework is due before he unlocks the next part so you can't finish learning everything at once.
comment_teaching: The homework and example are easy to follow through and clear to read. but I wonder if I can remember anything after the final exam. :P So, I didn't rate it an A for teaching.
comment_workload: One part of notes and one homework per month. 3 hours per month will be enough. per month not per week so the workload is very light.
comment_grading:
It is just a Pass/Fail Course and I don't think there is anyone who failed this course

He literally wrote “There are enough easy problems to let you reach the passing mark (40%)” in the final exam. (it is an open book exam so think about it)

I have asked the professor for a few times. His explanations are pretty well. Usually, he will reply you in less than 24 hours.

comment_grading:
This is a Pass/Fail course. To pass the course, you need to:

1.
Programming exercises:
submit and obtain 60% or higher of the four exercises total mark. That means you could submit three out of four exercises and still pass the requirement, given that you get very good marks for them.

2.
Final examination:
obtain 40% or higher in the final exam.


The final exam contains 20 questions. It lasts for 1 hour. Some of the questions are multiple-choice questions and some of them are short questions. The exam is open book and open notes (digital or paper-based).

Grade: P
