comment_content:
This is an excellent, low-pressure course designed specifically for individuals with prior programming experience who need to quickly get up to speed with Python. It successfully serves its purpose as a bridging or starter course, providing a smooth transition into the language without overwhelming the student.

The content is well-structured, progressing logically from fundamental programming concepts (like variables, functions, and control loops) to more advanced Python-specific topics. The curriculum doesn't waste time on absolute programming basics, instead focusing on how to apply existing programming knowledge within Python's syntax and paradigms. The guided programming exercises are particularly effective, reinforcing the self-learned material from the provided resources.
comment_teaching: The purely online format is executed very well. Course materials are clearly organized and accessible, allowing for self-paced learning. The structure supports independent study while providing the necessary guidance through exercises and, presumably, instructor availability for questions. It's an ideal setup for someone who is disciplined and prefers flexibility.
comment_workload: The workload is accurately described as light. The requirement of approximately one lab or exercise set per week is very manageable, even for students balancing other commitments. This makes it possible to engage meaningfully with the material without it becoming a significant source of stress.
comment_grading: The Pass/Fail grading scheme is one of the course's best features. It removes the anxiety associated with letter grades and allows students to focus purely on learning the material. As noted, the grading is fair and the course is designed for success; anyone who engages consistently with the weekly exercises is virtually assured of passing. This creates a supportive and positive learning environment.

comment_content: The course covers the fundamentals of Python programming in a step-by-step manner. Topics include variables, lists and strings, loops (for and while), functions, recursion, file input/output, and an introduction to object-oriented programming. There are also some small projects like Conway’s Game of Life and using turtle graphics to draw trees, which make the material a bit more engaging compared to just reading.
comment_teaching: Since this is an online bridging course, there are no traditional lectures. All the material is provided on the CSE website, and students are expected to go through it independently. The explanations are simple and direct, so beginners should be able to follow without too much difficulty. However, because there is no real teaching or live guidance, you need to be self-disciplined. If you are juggling multiple other courses or responsibilities, it can feel harder to keep up.
comment_workload: The workload is moderate. While the overall content is manageable, some exercises can be time-consuming, especially if you are completely new to programming. If you plan your time well and steadily work through the material, you should be fine.
comment_grading:
This is a pass/fail course. The requirements are:

Submitting at least three out of four exercises and achieving 60% overall for them.

Scoring at least 40% in the final exam.

The final exam is not very difficult, and most students can pass if they’ve done the exercises.

comment_content:
Course contents are given in 5 weeks:
*Lesson 1: Basic Python syntax, variables, strings, lists, boolean operators
*Lesson 2: Function writing, variable scope, for and while loops
*Lesson 3: 2D arrays, print and clear screen, delay function
*Lesson 4: Dictionaries, user input, try-except-else, file I/O
*Lesson 5: Recursion and turtle graphics

When compared with what is taught in COMP1021 (the regular Python introductory course), I think COMP1029P retains most of the contents of COMP1021.

As most Python learners will tell you, Python is actually quite easy to learn. (Professing it is another issue tho) When comparing the difficulties of learning Java in COMP1022P as a newbie at that time (in which I took in Fall Sem in 2021), Python is really much much easier...

And as usual with other programming classes, practice helps, especially considering the fact that COMP1029P is a self-learning course. In general, the course is quite a nice filler course for the long holidays to keep your brain running.
comment_teaching:
Same as what you've seen on the class schedule and others said, this is a self-learning course. Course contents are given on a weekly basis (and you can't access future contents). The emails of both Professor Lam and the TA (Peter Chung) is given for students to ask questions.

Professor Lam gives helpful responses regarding courses, and this even extends to some more out-of-scope contents: for example, he responded to my question why Python file I/O don't work for Microsoft Word--on Sunday, which I didn't expect.

On the other hand, when I asked for a technical problem on one of my assignment, Peter Chung insisted that my code is wrong, and his responses only increased my confusion instead of clarifying my question. What's next? I still get full marks for that assignment the next day.

That said, as I only have 2 occasions to contact the professor and TA with email, my opinions here may not be too representative.
comment_workload:
In general, nearly zero workload (though more workload than spam courses like PHYS1003).

There are a total of 4 assignments since week 2, and you are given with a week to deal with each of them. In fact, they are so easy that I can finish all of them within 2 to 3 days after the assignment is released.

There will also be 2-3 review questions per week, designed to encourage practicing the course content. While all of them won't count towards the final grade (?), review questions for week 2 is a component part of the week's assignment.
comment_grading:
Like other COMP1029* courses, COMP1029P adopts the pass-fail mechanism. You get a pass if you get more than 60% for the mean of all assignments, and 40% for the final exam. For me, I get full mark for all assignments, and scored of 75/100 for the final exam.

The professor also claimed that there should be enough easy questions in the exam, which is true per my experience. At the end at least one guy failed miserably (?*2) at a score of 38 for the exam, showing even those who performed bad in programming may even have a chance to pass this course.

While meaningless to a course which don't pull the curve, most of the homeworks have a mean of over 80%, and the exam's mean is 76.92/100.

comment_teaching: I had high expectations for this course, but unfortunately, I was somewhat disappointed with the delivery. While Gibson brought a lot of passion to COMP1021, I didn't feel the same level of engagement in this course. Although I knew that it was an online course, I didn't anticipate that the course materials would consist only of text, screenshots, and files. The absence of video explanations made it difficult for me to understand certain concepts at times. Furthermore, the only means of communication with Gibson and the TA was through email, which was not always convenient. However, I found that they were friendly and responsive in answering questions. I would have appreciated a Zoom session for inquiries.
comment_workload:
The workload for this course depends on your goals.
If you want to take it easy, the workload is relatively small.
However, if you want to ensure that you have a thorough understanding of the course materials, the workload can be quite substantial. COMP1021 is a 3-credit course with 7 labs, so one might expect that COMP1029V, which is a 1-credit course, would have 2-3 exercises. Unfortunately, there are 4 exercises. Within the same semester, I also took COMP1029C, so I felt like I was constantly fighting against deadlines. It took me about 30 minutes to read, take notes, and complete the quiz (which doesn't count towards the grade) for each section of a lesson, but it took me hours to complete each exercise.
comment_grading: Overall, the grading for this course is reasonable. To pass the course, you need to score at least 60% on the exercises and 40% on the final exam. There are four exercises in total, and they are not overly difficult. You only need to complete three of them and score well to pass the course. The final exam is a one-hour exam with 20 questions, most of which are straightforward, although a few are a bit tricky. It is relatively easy to score 40% or higher on the exam.

comment_teaching:
Professor is quite nice. He answers student's questions after class or through email. His lesson focuses on examples. However, his lecture slides are not useful for revision.

The gives out coupons during lesson if you answer his questions. The coupons can be used to either obtain full marks for one question in an assignment or obtain full marks for prediction in the project. It's not difficult to get a coupon.
comment_workload:
As mentioned in the grading section, I spent quite a lot of time in learning and revising course materials. It is essential to attend the lessons or watch the recordings, as the slides are missing many key concepts and steps of the algorithm.

There are two assignments, each includes 5 questions. You can complete the assignments following the examples during lecture.

For the project, you can form a group of two or just on your own. I did the project on my own and there isn't any problem with that. You will be given a training set, and you have to predict the target attribute for the test set. You need to propose 5 models to do the prediction and submit the results. The main workload lies in the project report, which you need to state the result and findings. The project is quite time consuming.
comment_grading:
Midterm mean score, final -0.4 SD, assignments, projects and attendance full marks, eventually got a B.

To be honest, the grading is good enough given the exam scores. However, personally speaking, I'm not satisfied with the score as I spent quite a lot of time learning and revising course contents, and the grade does not reflect my effort in this course. Computer Science and Math students pull up the average so much that other students struggle to reach the mean.

The exam papers obviously include easy questions and difficult questions. The examples given during lectures are rather easy but exam questions are much more complicated. The easy ones you just need to apply the algorithm taught during lesson and you can get the marks. For the difficult questions, they are REALLY difficult. Even after Raymond's explanation, I still have no idea how that can be solved.

comment_content:
6 Main topics:

1. Association
2. Clustering
3. Classification
4. Data Warehouse
5. Dimension Reduction
6. Web Databases

They are not "evenly distributed". The first 3 are much longer than the later ones.

Content is interesting with some overlap with COMP2211 (Naive Bayes, K-Means, Perceptron). However it won't give you too much advantage as the focus of these 2 courses are quite different.

One thing that is not so nice for a "common core" is that he spent quite a bit of time about proofing the algorithms, and they are really tested in the exam. I thought a CC would be less theoretical . However, you can use a cheatsheet, so you can copy the proofs onto it without really understanding them.
comment_teaching:
Raymond is one of the best instructors I met in UST. Others have wrote a lot about this, so I want to focus on his coupon system:

A coupon is given to a student whenever he/she answers a question in class. It can be used to waive 1 (out of 5) questions in a homework (there are 2 homeworks and each time you can only use 1 coupon), or guarantee full mark in the output file of project phase 3. Therefore you can use a maximum of 3 coupons throughout the course.

During the first month, a lot of people will raise up their hands each time Raymond asks a question, so it is not so easy to get a coupon from him at that time. However, don't be discouraged. As time passes, it will become less competitive. He will begin giving coupons for participating in in-class activities or some simple questions like "do you remember your instructor's name".

Even if you can't get all 3 coupons, it is good to get at least one for the project as that part of the project is 4% of your grade. Meanwhile, one question in a homework is 1%.
comment_workload:
Only 2 homeworks and a project.

There is IPRS, but your answer doesn't matter. So you can get full mark as long as you attend the lesson (punctually). Sometimes he starts the IPRS at the very beginning and sometimes it is near the end of the lesson.

Homeworks, midterm and final are very similar to pastpaper. Having source or not determines whether you can succeed in the course.

The project can be done on your own or in a 2-person group. I did it in a group but I think the workload is reasonable even if you do it individually. One thing to note is that the instructions are not very clear so make sure you talk to Raymond and the TAs to know what they expect you to write in the project report.
comment_grading:
Assignment 10%
Project 20%
In-class Participation 10% (IPRS)
Mid-Term Exam 20%
Final Exam 40%

Mean is very high for assignment and project, so the grade highly depends on midterm and final.

It seems that this course was known to be good grade.

If you are aiming for B-range then yes, you can spend very little time on it.

However if you are aiming high then please think twice. I don't know whether his grading is stricter this year, but I got A- with overall score 90.5%, which quite disappointed me. It seems that the A-range is very narrow and B-range is very broad.

comment_teaching:
Raymond is definitely a very good professor! He explains every point in lectures very detailedly. After every lecture you can see him answering questions passionately for students.

What is interesting is that he actually ask students to vote for the format of exams (difficulty, time, proportion of MC questions, bonus question).

I suggest you vote for 0% MC and 100% long questions because every MC is about asking you calculating a lot (which is even similar to the long question) in the 4 choices, and one less long question will result in four more MC questions :(  Also, long questions could give your partial scores, which result in a higher scoring rate than MC questions.

I also suggest you vote for no bonus questions, as they take about 1-page-long words to describe the problem... If you did that, you have no time to recheck the other problems.

Another interesting fact is that you could get a 'coupon' to waive one problem in HW or getting full mark for one part in project, just by asking one question from Raymond in the lecture :)

I suggest you attain 3 coupons in the first few classes, as you could waive for 1 question in both HW (at most one in each HW) and attain full mark in the project part (which is nearly impossible to get full mark normally)
comment_workload:
Moderate workload. Workload comes only before the deadline of HW, project phases, and exams.

If you decide to team up with someone, then the workload should be very light. I decided to work on my own, but the workload is still moderate.

comment_teaching:
Raymond is an exceptional teacher. After each lesson, he stays to clarify any confusion regarding the course material, ensuring that every question is answered before the session ends. If any questions arise after class, students can easily reach him via email, and he responds with detailed and thorough explanations promptly.
A unique aspect of the course is the "coupon" policy, which has two main uses:
Assignment Waiver: The coupon can be used to waive one question on an assignment, granting full points regardless of whether the answer is correct or even submitted. This is particularly useful if you're struggling with a specific question or if you're racing against the deadline and can't complete all the tasks (not that anyone would have personal experience with that).
Project Advantage: It can also secure full marks on one part of a project, which might otherwise be challenging to achieve without utilizing the coupon.
Coupons can primarily be earned in two ways: by answering questions posed by Raymond during lessons or by providing solutions for the "in-class exercise" (which will be further discussed in the 'workload' section). However, if you’re hesitant about answering questions quickly or feel unsure about completing the "in-class" exercises, there’s no need to worry. As the semester progresses, Raymond will inquire whether students have coupons. For those who do not, he will offer opportunities to answer questions, making it highly likely that everyone will earn at least one coupon, which is especially encouraged for use in projects.
comment_workload:
The workload for this course is manageable, comprising only two homework assignments and one project over the semester.
Homework
There are two homework assignments: one before the midterm exam and one afterward. Each assignment consists of four to five questions that assess your understanding of the course material. For someone like me, who tends to learn the material while doing homework, it takes about five hours to complete each assignment—roughly one hour per question. This is relatively light compared to the two-month deadlines for each assignment.
Project
The bulk of the course workload is centered around the project, which can be completed individually or in pairs. The project consists of three phases, and as long as you follow Raymond's guidance, achieving a full score is quite feasible. In fact, the average score for the project is over 90%. As a solo participant, I spent about three days on the project, which may sound like a lot, but is actually quite light given the generous deadlines.
In-Class Exercises (Optional)
Raymond offers in-class exercises during lessons, which are discussed in the following class. If you feel confident, you can arrive a bit early to show your answers to Raymond. If your answer is correct, you can earn a "coupon." However, participation in these exercises is entirely optional; I personally never participated in them before exam revisions.
Attendance
During lessons, there are iPRS questions that grant you marks simply for participating, regardless of whether your answers are correct.

Feels like I didn't really learn anything, I just know how to run these data mining algorithm eh

Note: You NEED Microsoft Excel on WINDOWS!!! This course requires you to use XLminer which is an add-in on Excel for data mining stuff.
comment_teaching:
I appreciate that Raymond tries his best to teach us, but for whatever reason I don't really like how he explains things,
Since this is a common core course, lots of things have to be sacrificed, you would not know WHY the fuck you need to do this and do that, you would only know HOW you evaluate all these algorithms. During the lectures of more complicated topics (PCA, Bayesian Belief Network, etc) I already don't understand wtf he is talking about, despite he trying to use analogies, I just don't.

Kudos to the TAs though, they are really efficient, they marked all 200 students' midterm paper in just few hours and released the results??? holy shit. Also for project and final they marked them quite fast too.

I skipped all tutorial lessons so I don't have any comments on that. Reading the slides for tutorial is enough.
comment_workload:
I would say the course content is not difficult to understand. The work you have to do is not difficult either. However it is just *tedious*, since you don't need to write program for this course, that means you have to run the algorithms taught in class... by hand!!! For careless person like me this was especially painful. Maybe you can just write programs for doing questions in assignment, but in the exams you need to do it by hand anyway.

The part annoyed me most is the project, where you need to predict income using real life data in XLminer. There are 3 phases.
Phase 1 is the easiest, just convert 2 csv file to an Excel file, follow Raymond's instructions and you should (hopefully) get full marks.
In project phase 2, you need to show every steps you did for setting up the algorithm of models used, there's probably a easier way to do this but I followed Raymond's instructions which is screenshotting every buttons you clicked, very tedious.
In project phase 3, Raymond's instructions are quite vague to be honest (for me at least), but you don't need to worry that much, just follow them to your best and you should likely get full mark too.

Another rant is that, for assignment and project, why the fuck do we still need to hand in HARD COPY???? What the fuck??? We have Canvas for handing in work for fuck's sake, it's 2024. My project is >100 pages long and horray my $20 gone just from printing all these craps alone.

For midterm and final exam, like I mentioned before this is just a common core course, they won't ask you concepts in-depth, just memorize how to do the questions and you should be fine.
Oh and Raymond would let you vote for exam formats, it could be unpredictable, so do more past papers pretty please :)

Raymond is a great teacher. He could explain the content well in layman's language. The class is interactive due to the existence of "Coupon" (Will be further mentioned later). Also, he will reply to your email quickly if you have any questions.

You can vote for the details of the midterm and final exams (e.g. Proportion of Multiple choice and long questions, Allowance of cheat sheet, bonus question)

In this course, you can obtain a "Coupon" by answering questions in class. Raymond will ask many questions in class, and most of them are very easy. Using "coupon", you can waive a question in an assignment (at most one for each assignment), or use it to get full marks in the predicted attribute files in project phase 3. So getting three coupons is recommended. At least getting one as it is impossible to get full mark in phase 3 without using coupon.
comment_workload:
Light workload, you can choose to form a 2 people group for the project or do it on your own. Even if you choose to do the project independently, it can be done within a day.

There are only two assignments, 5 questions each. You can waive at most one question for each assignment using coupon.

comment_content: Let me be clear: Many courses merit scorn, but this is unquestionably not one of them. Therefore, my review is intended to contradict the unfavorable review. It may be written in an overly enthusiastic manner, but as I promised, this is a fantastic course.

comment_workload:
The existence of a "coupon," which has two uses, is a special policy that applies to the course.
First off, you could use it to waive one assignment question, in which case you would receive full credit regardless of whether you provided the right answers (you can even not answer the question at all). This might be helpful if you were having trouble with a particular question or racing to finish the project before the due date and realized you couldn't do it all (definitely not personal experience).

comment_teaching:
Explanation of concepts:
To be honest, the lecture notes were a bit messy, but Raymond explained the concepts very clearly by using Zoom annotation and Microsoft Whiteboard. He used in-class exercises to ensure that we understood what he taught.

The speed of replying email:
The speed of reply always shocks me because Raymond replies to emails insanely quickly. One of the experiences is that he replied to my email within 10 minutes after I had sent it to him and the content of his email was longer than I expected.

Accent:
Raymond has a bit heavy Cantonese accent, but it is acceptable generally because he repeated the content and concepts quite many times.

TA Team:
It is worth praising all the TAs in this course because they were efficient, which I did not expect. They replied to emails quickly and released the scores of assessments (including mid term and final exam) within 48 hours.

Freedom/Flexibility of mid term and final exam:
We could vote on the format of Mid Term and Final Exam, such as the length, difficulty, and bonus-questions-or-not of exams.

By the way, I think Raymond (Prof. Wong) may be one of the best professors/lecturers in UST because he really spends time on teaching and taking care of students inside and outside of the learning aspect.

comment_content:
This course Uses XLMiner in Excel, NO PROGRAMMING is required.

= Topics overview =
1. Association rule mining
- Finding frequent patterns and association rules
- Apriori algorithm
- FP-Growth algorithm
2. Clustering
- Grouping things together
- K-means clustering
- Hierarchical and divisive clustering
3. Classification
- Predicting using a decision tree constructed based on historical records
- Decision trees (ID3, C4.5, CART)
- Bayesian classifier (Naive Bayes, Bayesian belief network)
> The project revolves around this topic
> IMO the hardest to understand is the Bayesian belief network, which is taught out of order just before finals
4. Dimension reduction
- e.g. 2D data representation into a 1D data representation
- PCA Principal component analysis
5. Data warehouse
- What pre-computed results to store in the DW to reduce response time
- Greedy algorithm for which view to materialise
6. Web databases
- Ranking webpages
- HITS algorithm
- PageRank algorithm
> Requires very basic matrix knowledge, which they will teach as tutorial content

= Opinions =
If you really hate coding in COMP1021 and can put up with manually going through algorithms, this course could be an alternative to fulfil QR common core.

Interesting subject to learn even without prior knowledge, and you'll learn how powerful data really is and why companies crave them.

Quite a portion of the course is manually running through algorithms, which may get tedious with many steps, especially during the exam. Even though this course doesn't expect any programming, I ended up spending more time coding some of the algorithms that print every step in detail, and some of which required way more advanced python packages than COMP1021. Was coding them that worth it? Sort of. It acted as a tool in reinforcing the content and saved a bit of my sanity, yet wasn't a significant advantage for exams.
comment_teaching:
Do not worry if you know nothing about the course, because I didn't either beforehand. Explains everything very clearly and in detail, and repeats until the concept is understood. Would answer out-of-scope questions after class if the student is interested. His English is a bit broken but is legible.

As for the seemingly long PowerPoint slides, need not fear. Most of them are just step-by-step screenshots of using XLMiner.

Very efficient TA team, releasing results within days of submitting assignments and even exams.

Tutorials are (mostly) optional, and they would notify you about the topic of the upcoming tutorial session via email so that you could choose to attend or not. If you couldn't fully grasp the content in the lecture, tutorials are helpful.

Some of the tutorials will teach content that is assessed in the exams but not taught in lectures. They will explicitly note which ones are particularly important.
comment_workload:
*Online semester, special arrangements may be applied*

= Assignments =
One for each half of the semester, on content before and after the midterm. They encourage you to complete the relevant questions right after they've taught them, so the workload is spread out evenly.

= Project =
Group of 1-2 members, freely formed.
Spans the entire semester, but most of the work is concentrated in the last month (Phase 2 and 3).

Phase 1: Excel
Simply convert their text files containing the training and test data into one Excel file, one is expected to get full marks on this. Be careful to match their specifications EXACTLY including case sensitivity.

Phase 2: Design report
Try many different classification model setups, and pick 5 to put into the design report.
The most exhausting part is the step-by-step screenshots on how to use XLMiner for pre-processing and each classification model.

Phase 3: Final report and output files
One week immediately after the submission of phase 2.
Adding to the phase 2 report, with the results of each classification model and two examples manually demonstrating how the model predicts.
comment_grading:
*Online semester, special arrangements may be applied*

Assignment 10% (5% each)
Project 20%
In-class participation 10% (iPRS participation 5%, Camera turned on 5%)
Midterm 20%
Final 40%

= Coupon system =
Raise up your hand to answer select questions or show your solution to in-class exercises, you'll get a coupon, very easy to get.

Coupons can be used to waive one question in an assignment and get full marks for that question or used to get full marks for the output files in project phase 3 (which usually isn't easy without coupons).

Make sure to have THREE coupons for the entire course, which they pretty much guarantee you to have since there are so many opportunities to get one, and even create trivial in-class activities for those who haven't got any.

= Exam format =
They let you vote on:
- The proportions of MC and LQ (e.g. 20% MC 80% LQ)
- The length of the exam
- The proportion of question difficulty (e.g. 80% straightforward 20% not-straightforward but easy)
- Bonus question or not (+10%)

= Stats =
Assignments and the project have the means at 90+% of the max score, so you essentially need to get full marks for a good grade.

Midterm:
- Min Max 9.5 & 100
- Mean 54.32 / 100
- SD 17.87
- "Effective" score due to the tightness of the online exam +20

Final:
- Min Max (Before re-mark) 21 & 190
- Mean 108.33 / 200
- SD (Before re-mark) 34.81
- "Effective" score +40

I got full marks for all assignments and project phases, midterm ~+0.43 SD, final ~+0.77 SD, and got A- in the end.

Raymond welcome students to ask question freely during the lecture. If you have questions just raise hand and ask him. He is willing to answer all kinds of questions.

During the lecture, Raymond will give "coupon" to those who have answered his questions. 1 Coupon can be used to waive 1 question from a homework to get full mark. Also, it can be used to waive the prediction result part in the project, which means you can directly get full score even your prediction result in the project is a crap (or you can even not hand in the prediction text file :D).

Don't worry that you cannot get a coupon from Raymond lecture as he will ask many questions during the lecture time and most of them are very easy even primary students can answer them. So don't be afraid of it. Besides, Raymond will send email to ask those students who have no coupon to register and let them answer the questions sometimes.  You can take the chance of this to get a coupon for your project if your hand speed is not as fast as other students in the class.

Raymond powerpoint usually consist of many slides to show you how to use XLMiner step by step. But I think many of them are actually redundant and can be simplified. So don't be scared by a vast amount of powerpoint slides, most of them are just XLMiner demonstration part :D
comment_workload:
Workload is mainly come from the exam, homework and project.

There are 2 homeworks in this semester, each of them gives you nearly 1 month to finish (maybe?). You are definitely having enough time to finish it on time. The questions are not difficult, but require repetitive calculation and long steps, which may cause you to have careless mistakes. Be careful of it.

The project is not difficult also. You can form 1-2 people in a group to finish it.
Phase 1 is handing in a Excel file which is adding the column names to the data in txt tile originally. You can finish it within 10 minutes.
Phase 2 is handing in the design report. You need to give 5 models to do your prediction. For each of the model, you need to clearly write down the parameters and steps so that TA can follow what are you doing exactly. It is not difficult but may cost you some time because you need to do many screenshot D:
Phase 3 is the final report. You just need to add the results from each of your model in phase 2 and write down the conclusions. Again it is not difficult. Generally I just copy and paste the results of my model and change some wording to finish this part.

It is easy and your report just follow the format given by Raymond then it is okay. You don't need to consider the design and formatting of the report.
comment_grading:
Don't know the detail cutoff, but seems quite lenient generally.

Grading Scheme:
10% Participation (Requires screenshot in lecture iPRS and answering the iPRS)
10% Homework (Easy, but the wording is quite long)
20% Project (Easy, separated in 3 phase, Raymond will teach you how to finish them and you can just simply follow his given format to get full marks here)
20% Midterm
40% Final

This semester midterm consists of 50% MC and 50% LQ, which is quite challenging

For the final exam, it is 100% LQ.

Both exams are in tight-time schedule, open-book and open to online materials. It is recommended to use some program or excel(?) to help you solving the question. Otherwise it is quite impossible to finish the whole paper in the final exam.

You can expects the participation, homework and project mean is around 90-95%. So the things determine your grade is midterm and final.

Getting +1.3 sd from midterm and +1.5 sd from final giving me an A+, which is not very useful for general grading. You can refer to other review to know more about the grading. I believed that the grading is generally good.

comment_content:
Let me put this straight, many courses deserve their hate (looking at you, phys1112), but this is definitely not one of them. Therefore, this review is to counteract the negative review, and this review might be in a slightly overly positive tone, but I promised you, this is indeed a great course.

Content:
1. Association (Apriori Approach, FP-growth Approach)
2.Clustering (k-mean, dendrogram)
3.classification (Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, NN Classifier, Neural Network, Recurrent Neural Network, SVM)
4. PCA
5. Data Warehouse
6. Web Database

Never heard of these materials? Me neither!
However, don't get discouraged by these topics that sound difficult, they are not as horrendous as they appeared to be. Most of the topics could be easily comprehended and understood after you attended the lessons (maybe with the exception of PCA, which is quite confusing for me). After you learnt about these concepts, you should be able to grasp an idea about data handling.
comment_teaching:
Raymond is a great teacher. After the lesson, he will stay to answer any confusion about the course material, and he will not end the lesson until every questions got answered. And if you have questions after the lesson, just connect him through email, he will provide in-depth and detailed answer pretty quickly.

There is a unique policy about the course, which is the existence of "coupon", which has two usages.
Firstly, it could be used to waive one question in an assignment, you would automatically obtained full points for that question whether your answers are correct or not (you can even not answer the question at all). This would come in handy when you feel struggling about a certain question, or you are rushing the assignment before the deadline and realise that you could not finish all of them on time (definitely not personal experience).
Secondly, it could help you to automatically obtain full mark in one part of the project, which would be otherwise hard if you do not use the coupon.

There are mainly two ways to obtain the coupon, which are through answering Raymond's questions during the lesson, or providing solution for the "in-class exercise" (further discussed in 'workload'). However, don't worry if you feel you could not raised hand to answer question quick enough or not smart enough to complete the "in-class" exercise. Towards the end of the semester, Raymond would start to ask students whether they have coupons or not, and if you don't, he would give you opportunities to answer his questions. Therefore, you are almost guaranteed to at least have one coupon (which is highly encouraged to be used in project).
comment_workload:
The workload is not that heavy, for the whole semester, there are only 2 homework and a project.

HW: for the homework, there will be one before the midterm exam and one after the midterm exam, which test your knowledge about the course materials, each of them consist of four to five questions. For a slow learner like me, I can complete it within about five hours, i.e. one hour for each question (but you need to take consideration that I often missed the lessons and would just learn the material when I do the homework), which is relatively small workload when compared to its deadline (about two months each).

Project: Most workload of the course comes from the project. The project could be done in group of two or one(i.e. only yourself). The project consist of three phases, which as long as you follow Raymond's guidance, would be very easy to obtained full score. As a matter of fact, the mean of the project is more than 90%. For the whole project, as a one man group, I worked about three days, which again might seem much, but is actually quite light given the lenient deadline.

In-class exercise(Optional): In-class exercises are given by Raymond during a lesson, which would be discussed in the next lesson. If you are confident, for the next lesson, you could arrive the class a bit earlier and show your answer to Raymond, if it is correct, you could obtain a "coupon". However, this is totally optional and I personally never did the In-class exercise before revisions for the exams.

Special mention: attendance. Although the course should returned to face-to-face next semester, I think this semester attendance record system are still worth to mention. Your attendance will be recorded if you upload a screenshot of the lesson after the class. Also, during the lesson, there will be some iPRS questions that would grant you mark as long as you answer them (regardless of whether your answer is correct).
comment_grading:
Assignment 10%
Project 20%
In-class Participation(I.e. iPRS and attendance) 10%
Mid-Term Exam 20%
Final Exam 40%

For assignment and project, they are not difficult to obtain high score as long as you are willing to put some effort, as the markings are quite lenient.
For In-class participation, erm, sleep well and don't wake up late, I guess?

For examination, there are quite a few points to be discussed.

The examination method: Raymond would allow students to vote for their desired exam method (letting us to choose the mark distribution for MC and long/short questions, ranging from 0% to 100%). For midterm, people choose 50% of MC and 50% of long/short questions, and many were disappointed with it as they feel like the MC part was difficult. As a result, the final exam consist of only long/short questions and no MC. I was in the minority side of this situation as I preferred some MCs, but hey, to each their own, and so I would just try to give some objective description about the MCs and long/short questions, and it is left for you to decide how you want your exam to be.
MC: the MC would consist of some conceptual questions, some standard question that require moderate level of calculation, and some questions that are actually compressed long question that you would need to go through like a normal long question to get the desired answer (which I think are the reason of MC being considered difficult).
Long/short questions: The long/short questions are mostly standard and straightforward questions that revolve about the taught materials, which are mostly extremely similar(or even identical) to past paper or exercises given during lessons, some questions that required proof(most are mentioned during class), and some unseen explanation question that test your understanding about the course (which are the questions that distinguish the level of students). Long/short questions would allow you to obtain at least some score as long as you write some steps, but it would be more lengthy to complete one question as you need to write a lot of words, which might cause you to have less than sufficient time to complete the whole paper.

Little tips about the exam:
1. DO THE PAST PAPER AND IN-CLASS EXERCISE! This is the utmost important tip. There are many similar questions between the past paper/in-class exercise and the actually exam paper, so as long as you complete those things and memorize how to do them, you should be fine.
2. Remember the proof. There are few proofs about the materials that Raymond would bring up during the lessons, remember to copy and remember them as there is always a proof question in the exam.
3. Try to make some programs to help you do the calculation. (This tip might not be applicable next time this course is offered as I don't know whether the exam will still be carried out online). The exam would almost always required many calculations for some questions, which would be impossible for people to finish manually within the time limit. Therefore, you should make some programs that help you do those tedious calculations. Don't worry if you do not have any programming background, Excel could do the same computations after you put formula into the cells.

The grade (total, mean, sd)
Midterm: (100, 61.41, 15.02)
Final exam:(200, 117.42, 38.46)
Assignments have mean ~90/100 and project has mean ~95/100
I got +1.3sd for midterm and +1.0sd for final, and got an A.
But before you boo me and call me biased, may I mentioned that there are reports of people that got mean in final yield an B+, and people that get -0.5sd for final yield an B. So I think it is not a lie that the course has good grade.

comment_content:
You will learn some common data mining techniques and algorithms.

However, this is not a math course. So, you won't be learning about the mathematical principles behind these algorithms.

This isn't a coding course either. So, you won't be learning how to implement these algorithms in code.

What else is left to learn? How to "run" these algorithms by hand! Sounds fun? You will be performing repetitive, mechanical calculations by hand to solve problems that would take a computer 0.01 milliseconds to solve.

If you can write code to solve these problems, however, you can own those SBM noobs who can't code, and laugh as they take 30 minutes to solve a problem on the final that took you 1 second to solve, cos you wrote a program for it.
comment_teaching:
In terms of teaching, Raymond has a lot of room for improvement. However, he is nice and gave me a good grade, so I will give a C here instead of E.

Raymond is really nice and will answer your emails promptly. The teaching team is also efficient and finishes marking exams/assignments in 1-2 days.

However, Raymond is really terrible as a teacher.

Raymond forces you to attend nearly every lesson. You need to submit screenshots of your Zoom attendance 15 times and answer 20 IPRS questions. At 1 IPRS per lecture, you can only miss 3 or 4 lectures.

Attending Raymond's lectures is actively detrimental to my learning progress. Once in a while, some disruptive students will ask some stupid questions and he will go out of his way to answer them. Literally, 90% of the questions raised in lectures were asked by the same single student. However, Raymond is too nice to tell him to just SHUT UP.

Raymond also has a  coupon system where you get a coupon if you answer a question in class which you can use to get full marks on a homework question. Raymond wants to give every student a chance to answer a question in class, so he goes out of his way to ask a lot of trivial questions. Interactiveness is good but Raymond is really taking it overboard to the point that it is disrupting the flow of the lecture.

Moreover, Raymond talks so slowly that I drift off and lose focus every 10 seconds. If you are an individual of higher intelligence whose brain operates on a higher frequency and therefore prefers watching lecture videos at 2x speed, too bad! Raymond forces you to attend his lectures.

Furthermore, Raymond has a fetish with long documents. His powerpoints will make you want to rip your eyes out. They are usually 100 slides long. He likes to animate his powerpoints FRAME BY FRAME. 80% of the slides in his powerpoints do not contain useful information. Moreover, your project report will be around 50 pages long, mostly consisting of screenshots detailing how you use the data mining tool. Therefore, you will spend more time furiously flicking your mouse scroll wheel to navigate the document instead of actually typing stuff.

You would think a CSE professor would be more tech-savvy. While UST's LANG courses are completely useless to us undergraduates, I think Raymond will really benefit from attending a LANG course to upgrade his presentation skills.
comment_workload:
Workload is pretty huge.

If you want A range, you must do at least 5 or 6 past papers, and write code for every single question type. I think there are several question types but each type has a couple variations, so you will need to write a few dozen programs in total.

Raymond sets the deadline for homeworks and project reports at 10:30 in the morning. If you are someone who cannot focus on a homework assignment unless the deadline is less than 6 hours away, your sleep schedule will suffer.

The homeworks are time-consuming, and you need to triple-check your answers. The project report is also tedious. You need to use the data mining tool and paste screenshots of every step, i.e., every button you click.

Projects requirements were not clearly communicated. The TAs will even penalize minor formatting mistakes. If your solution isn't a carbon copy of the official solution, you will get points deducted.
comment_grading:
In terms of grading, this course gets an F for fascism.

Example from the final exam:
Q: Is it possible to find X? If yes, what is X? If no, explain.
A: X = 3.

Wrong! -1 point, "missing answer to yes/no question"

Welcome to North Korea!

The exam format was highly inconsistent. The midterm was 50/50 LQ MC with NO mechanical questions that you can write programs for, while the final was 100% LQ with 2 questions worth 40% of the total solvable within 1 second with pre-written code. The midterm got me good and I barely got 50%, but luckily I had a good comeback in the final. Personally, I do not appreciate this kind of inconsistency in exam format, as students can not adequately prepare for the exam by doing past papers. This is another reason this course sucks. You never know what Raymond is going to throw at you next time.

Statistics:
10% HW Full
10% Attendance Should be Full
20% Project nearly full
20% + 40% Midterms and Finals +0.9 SD averaged
->A-

Luckily, the final is worth twice as much as the midterm, so you can make a comeback if you screw up in your midterm.

Raymond lets us vote on the format of the exams. For the midterm, some naive kids voted for 50% Long Q 50% MC.
As a result, the midterm was very fun. MC is very fun because it's all-or-nothing, no partial credit. I only got 3/10 of the MC.

Raymond also removed all MC questions from the past paper. Maybe he is too lazy to design new MC questions and reuse them every year. Too bad if you want to do past papers to prepare for the MC part!

Finals were 100% Long Q. 10 questions 20 marks each. 2 questions can be solved with code right off the bat, so 40 marks there. For the rest, most were copied from the past papers. Raymond didn't even bother changing the numbers for one question, so I just copy-pasted the solution from a past paper. (It was open book)

1. Association

It was the first time for me to get to know more about data visualization in a course. The topics are interesting and managable. You get to know more how to analyze data using XLMiner or even calculating the datas manually.
comment_teaching: Prof. Raymond is a really good teacher. He is passionate and always patiently explains every concept again and again. In addition, he often searches for different ways to elaborate the concept, which made me understand the concept better and memorize easily.
comment_workload: The workload is okay. There are 2 assignments (one before midterm, and another before final). There are also a project (it is seperated to 3 phases. It seems quite a lot to do in the semester, however, those assignments and project actually make you understand and practice more about the concept. Additionally, the questions occurring in the assignments are quite similar to those in midterm and final. Moreover, you can actually get good scores in assignments and project quite easily if you are willing to work on and complete them.
comment_grading:
Assignment 10%
Project 20%
In-class Participation 10%
Mid-Term Exam 20%
Final Exam 40%

For assignment:
The questions are similar to the questions professor showed in class. If you are familiar with the content in class, you are able to manage the assignment. If you still cannot do the questions by yourself, you can also refer to the slides uploaded in the class website. (The slides show step-by-step guide for how to solve a problem.)

For project:
My partner and I spent half a day dealing with it. It is not very hard, but you have to spend some time to transfer or reduce the data.


For in-class participation:
You have to answer in total 20 iPRS questions (whether answering wrong or right does not matter.)

Overall:
The scores of assignment, project, and the participation are not that hard to get, so most of the students can get very high score in these parts. Therefore, I think the midterm and final actually decide your grade.

comment_content:
Contents:
- Association Mining
- Clustering
- Decision Trees
- Naive Bayesian Filter and Bayesian Networks
- KNN, basic neural network
- Some measurement on the classification performance.
- PCA
- OLAP / Data warehouse
- Web Database
Most of the concepts taught in the course are definitely helpful in data analysis. Definitely a good course to learning Quantitative reasoning(QR).
But remember, this is an introductory course. Some of the explanations in advanced topics may not be well explained. If you want to know more, you should take the data mining course.
comment_teaching:
Raymond explains concepts clearly and logically so that students in other disciplines will also understand. He will also spend time talking about some of the rationales behind some data mining techniques.
Occasionally he may spend extended duration on some of the concepts which are already taught in previous lectures.
comment_workload:
I would say the workload is reasonable.
There are 2 assignments. Each assignment has around 1 month to do it.
There is 1 project which has 3 phases spread across the semester. During the project, students will analyze real-world data using XLMiner with specific technique learned in lectures.

For the midterm and final exams, you are allowed to bring cheatsheets. Remember to print every important concept taught in the lectures, and play around XLMiner, as those will be tested.

comment_content:
Since everyone else wrote about what the course content includes, I won't dive into it cause its unlikely to change for the next decade to come. The course content is quite interesting in some ways and allows students with no computer science background to understand and possibly be able to communicate (if you understand the course) with others on data mining. There is no coding or any sort of understanding of it for this course.
On another note, some of the content of this course could really be used in real life or at least understand parts about the real life. For example, one of the data mining methods (Principal Component Analysis) requires the calculation of the Eigenvalues which basically allowed me to say "Ayyy.. I know that" when I was watching Avengers Endgame.
comment_teaching:
Professor Raymond is a very passionate professor and has really cared for the students' success in his course. While he obviously can't give every student an A+, he has provided as much support as he could to help students understand the course and gain extra credit. The way to gain extra credits was so easy it would be stupid not to go for it.
Even though the professor's teaching was very good, his teaching assistants were as passionate nor cared about the students as much. They basically read off the powerpoint and made no efforts in making the tutorials more interesting. So I don't really see a point of going to the tutorials since you can just read the tutorial notes yourself.
comment_workload:
The assignment workload could get a bit much if you don't properly organize your time throughout the semester especially if you don't understand the course content very well.
The project workload is pretty chill, you basically just follow the steps given and you will score a 100% no problem. So for most time, relax and organize your time to finish the work. Professor Raymond normally sends out reminders letting the students know the different milestones in class thus allowing students to do the assignment questions right after the lecture while the knowledge is still fresh.
comment_grading:
I think the grading was quite fair and allowed students who aren't super good at test taking to balance their grades out with the group project and the assignments.
In some aspects, the assessments given could be way harder than the content taught in class but as long as you do the past papers, it is unlikely that you will score low in the exams.
Do keep in mind that there are computer science major students in the course. And for a student without any knowledge in the area, it could be intimidating to see someone code in the lecture theatre. But don't worry, you aren't competing with those guys, there is no way you can beat them with the same effort they put in the course.

comment_content:
An elementary level course on data mining. Topics included:
1. Association: Apriori, FP growth
2. Clustering: k-Means, hierarchical
3. Classification: Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, k-Nearest Neighbor, Neural Network, concepts like true positive, accuracy, etc.
4. Data Warehouse
5. Dimension Reduction (Principal Component Analysis)
6. Wed Databases

The difficulty of most contents are well-selected to be a common core level. You may feel it is a bit easy if you have a solid background on math or computer science. There are some topics that you may have not learned before (for example, basic matrix operation), but those will be covered in the tutorials.

Using XLMiner in Excel is one of the highlights of this course. It is a tool for you to perform data mining in Excel. Some tutorials may be used to teach the way to use XLMiner. Raymond may also mention it himself during lectures.
comment_teaching:
Raymond explains things quite clearly, and he is willing to answer any questions regarding the content of the course. He is probably famous for replying emails with a fast speed. In addition to that, his replies are clear and rich in content. Just read other course reviews to see how nice he is.

I didn't attend any tutorials not about XLMiner, so I cannot give any review on that. For those tutorials about XLMiner, the TA goes through the steps of doing different data mining techniques slowly. This may be helpful if you can't really understand the procedures or you are lazy to learn it yourself.
comment_workload:
Assignments: I do not have "sources", and it spends me about 3~5 hours for both. Some questions ask you to use XLMiner, so that may waste you a bit of time.

Project: Not sure if I treat it too seriously, but the workload is not as light as other people suggested. Phase 1 is easy for everyone and it does not cause you more than 30 minutes. Phase 2 is the design report. Mine contains 14 pages including screenshots from XLMiner, which causes me at least 5 hours. Phase 3 is the final report, which can be based on the design report. Mine contains 21 pages including screenshots from XLMiner, which causes me 8~10 hours.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme:
Assignment x2 10% (easy to obtain high score)
Project 20% (easy to obtain high score)
In-class Participation 10% (iPRS)
Midterm 20% (Mean: 65.76/100, SD: 15.88, Max: 98)
Final 40% (Mean: 125.33/200, SD: 30.03, Max: 196.25)

Get coupons by answering questions during lectures. These coupons can allow you to waive a part of the assignments and project and guarantees a full score. Raymond will even "force" you to obtain some coupons at a later stage of the course, so it should be easy to obtain them.

Getting a course total of 96.7% yields an A+. It is lucky to have an A+, but probably due to the significant increase of difficulty in the final exam. Your score is especially outstanding if you perform well in the final exam. A reminder for anyone taking this course: the time for exams this year are quite insufficient, so try to rush over the questions.

comment_content:
Don't get confused by the course title, it's just "Introduction to Data Mining". Topics include:

1. Association
2. Clustering
3. Classification
4. Data Warehouse
5. Dimension Reduction
6. Web Databases

This course focuses on the steps to do the actual data mining, but not the concept behind. In this course, you will be trained to be a machine for processing some data. Don't expect that you will be able to do something cool after this course. You will only learn how to do some specific data processing steps for an extremely specific and simplified scenario.
comment_teaching:
Raymond explained the data mining process clearly in a step-by-step manner. He assumed we have no prior knowledge, know nothing about high school maths, know nothing about computer science. He spent way too much time to elaborate some of the details which may make you feel boring.

But still, I am quite impressed his ability to explain complicated concepts. The content is as easy as ABC after he has explained it.  I should quote from Prof. Leung from PHYS 1112, "You have learnt it in kindergarten."
comment_workload:
HW are easy. Just copy the HWs in the previous offering. They are mostly the same.

Project is easy but a little bit time-consuming. You have to capture screenshots for ALL steps of building your classification model so the TA can follow exactly what you performed.

For exam preparation, doing 4 years of past paper should be sufficient for getting a decent grade. Most questions come from past exam, DO copy them in your cheatsheet or you probably will regret.
comment_grading:
The grading is not as good as the previous offering. You need to work much harder than before to get an A+ in the course. It is heard that the mean only gets you a B in this semester compared to B+ in the previous ones.

The exam difficulty has increased a lot, you have to complete the question quite quickly in order to finish all. The final question involve solving a system of linear equations (instead of simple arithmetic in previous semesters). I am bad with those maths and made careless mistakes in calculation costing me 10 points in final. And also another 6 point because not finishing the clustering in time due to that equation solving. It seems the paper test your maths and time management skills instead of your knowledge about the topics.

HW: 10/10
Project: 20/20
IPRS: 10/10
Midterm: 93.75/100 (Mean: 65.76/100, SD: 15.88) => 18.75/20
Final: 175/200 (Mean: 125.33/200, SD: 30.03) => 35/40
Overall Grade: A

+1.7 sd in both midterm and final still yields an A. I don't think that can be classified as a good grade course, right?

EDIT: Please don't get me wrong. To be fair, this is not a bad grade course, and there are much more courses with grading worse than that. Based on past experience and other reviews (in previous offerings of this course and other courses), +1.0~1.2 sd should at least yield an A-, +1.3~1.5 sd should at least yield an A, and +1.6~1.7 sd should at least yield an A+ for a course with better grading. What I really meant is the grading scheme is not as good as before, and it is still reasonable in some sense.

comment_content:
The contents include:

1) Association
2) Cluster (k-Means, Dendogram)
3) Classification (Decision Tree, Naive Bayes, NN Classifier, Neural Network)
4) PCA
5) Data Warehouse
6) Web DB

The content is extremely fruitful so you may have a difficult time studying for your finals, but still it is a great course. The concept itself may feel difficult at first, but if you listen to the lectures, the professor makes the course material understandable (which you don't see too much often here at HKUST).
comment_teaching:
Best teaching at HKUST so far. He is a very passionate lecturer; he is willing to embrace all students who cannot follow along with the course pace. He is very open to questions, so if you email him about any question, he will respond extremely quickly. (BTW I don't think he sleeps early. His emails come at like 1,2 am in the morning)

There are some math involved in the course and because he knows most students cannot follow, he slows down and explains the same thing many times. (Especially at the first few lectures in association rule, he will repeat the calculation like four times. But later he will speed things up a little bit...)

Some people might find his accent/pronunciation funny. He pronounces "sheet" in a very concise way, and he uses the word "coupon" a lot and it sounds like "Q-pon".
comment_workload:
Not too much.

There is a participation score but you just need to have 20 units of iPRS questions, which is easy to get. You don't have to go to every single lecture. Some lectures have 3 iPRS questions so by the end of the term, there is no need to go to the lectures. You can watch the video link provided by the department (which is actually better) when studying for the exams/assignments.

Assignments are short, you can waive one question with a Q-pon. You get a Q-pon by answering questions during the lecture. You will need about 4 to go through the semester (1 per assignment/project). I ended up gathering five in the first two weeks and didn't participate after then.

There is a project but... I don't think the grading is very harsh. Didn't expect the mean to be as high as 90.79 but it's not very difficult, just do what he teaches you in the lectures (takes about 4~6 hours I guess if you do it alone).
comment_grading:
Just like any COMP classes, the grading is very objective. If you deserve 100, the professor and TAs will give you a 100.

I suggest that when you get your assignment scores, project scores, exam scores, you should take a look and talk to the professor if you think you deserve more points. The professor is open to changing scores if you deserve more (and if you can elaborate your answers).

Project: 100/100
Midterm: 97/100 (was 94)
Final: 195/200 (was 189)
Participation: 10/10
Assignment 1: 10/10 (was 9.5)
Assignment 2: 9.6/10 (was 9.0)

I don't know what my letter grade is yet but probably many other students have high scores as well.

The mean scores are:

(Naive Bayes, Bayesian Belief Network)

comment_content:
3 assignments and 1 final project. The first assignment is making a mash-up of the audio from one source with the video of another. The second is using green-screen techniques to combine characters from different media into one setting. The third is making a music video mashup incorporating elements from multiple songs or remixing a given song. The final project is basically do whatever you want, there are no rules (except be really creative). Each submission requires a 1-2 minute video of your work, and a corresponding 1-2 minute commentary video describing your creative and technical choices (except the final project, which is 2-3 minutes for both the video + commentary). You can work on the video yourself or in groups of 2 people (if you want to have a more manageable workload highly recommend you find someone to work with).

I can totally see people submitting the same types of content for all 4 assignments, as the criteria for all of them were pretty loose and are generally quite friendly towards whatever your passions or interests are.
comment_teaching:
A course that rewards those who self-learns everything and/or already have a developed hobby in music and video making.

Almost no in-class teaching was done. Prof. Horner does enthusiastically go over other people's creations (from this semester + previous semesters) in class and gives out a lot of artistic and a few technical suggestions, but these largely do not really help that much beyond video ideation. The comments he gives you after you submit your video are all just positive remarks like "Great techniques!" or "Amazing idea!", don't expect to receive actual feedback for your video (courtesy of the TA's) until after your video is shown and peer-reviewed.

Other people previously claimed that there were tutorials provided which help students who don't have that much experience get started. My opinion is that this is only to a small extent; these tutorials aren't really that helpful if you want to really impress them and get a decent grade, they either just suggest a few techniques and tools or they're way too esoteric for most people to understand, and many of them really are only for one purpose (ex. they provided a lot of deepfake AI tool tutorials for the final project, which wouldn't be that useful if you don't want to do something like that). Especially noteworthy if you aren't coming from a field or profession where sound/video design is researched or video editing is used, or you aren't a graphics design major at heart, most of the tutorials also use apps and features which either are pretty finicky to use or turns out are behind a paywall. In fact, in my opinion it is really difficult to obtain a decent grade in this course without spending several hundred dollars on video and audio editing software/AI tools (ex. video editing tools: Final Cut Pro is a one-off 2300 HKD purchase, the standard subscription for Capcut premium is 60 HKD/month which you have to purchase to do literally anything (seriously, even splitting audio and video from a clip requires Capcut premium), Davinci Resolve is free but most advanced techniques besides plugins require a subscription that costs 230 HKD/month, the full range of adobe software requires at least a 400 HKD/month subscription to use etc., iMovie is free to use if you have a Mac but I do NOT recommend it for this course as it is so basic and there are so little features).

Of course, you could have a really creative premise that compensates a lack of technology with either a really good storyline or clever artistic choices, but this is HARD to do especially without making it look or sound like it's "low-budget".

As I am only taking this to complete my last Broadening CC requirement and I have some basic experience in video editing (due to some random stuff i did for a university club a few years ago), the only two subscriptions I purchased was a standard plan for Runway ML (so I could survive assignment 2) and Capcut Premium, both of which I swiftly cancelled after I finished my final project (Audacity, GarageBand (for Mac users), and the base version of Davinci Resolve were free). Even then, I had to rely on my experience on playing an instrument for several years and having a decent knowledge in music theory for assignment 3 and the final project to get a good grade. I recommend you take this course if you're already passionate in video editing and music creation and have or know how to use several of the tools recommended by this course, if not RUN AWAY.
comment_workload:
4 video projects only, seems light right?

NO.

Firstly, the assignment due dates are absolutely atrocious. Assignment 1 due in the middle of add/drop period, assignment 2 right before midterm period, assignment 3 in the middle of midterm period, final project right after midterms end. I constantly found working on these videos got in the way of a ton of other stuff I was doing (for a year 4 student working on postgraduate/job applications, and I anticipate most people taking this course is year 4 given how fast COMP1943 turns from available to waitlist during the first day of course reg, this is borderline hellish).

Also, one only realizes how much effort you need to put into each video when you actually do it. Having the idea is one thing. Executing it is a whole other situation. Prof. Horner and his TA's value quantity AND quality, videos that approach the 2 (or 3) minute limit and are full of artistic details and techniques. Even if you spent a massive amount of time and energy on your assignment, or you have a really good idea, they won't judge you on your idea alone if it looks like you had trouble achieving what you wanted to do.

Don't sleep on the commentary video either, to get a higher grade its not really that suggested to just add subtitles to a running clip of your workspace, instead a separate edited video that explains every important thing you did. The first two assignments I just added subtitles to a video recording of my Capcut workspace for my commentary (because I ran out of time) and neither were received very well. Some people went above and beyond and made their commentary video seemingly more amusing and detailed than their actual assignment, especially if their original video revolves around a theme/subject that isn't very well known by many people (ex. certain sports like F1 or football), but this obviously takes a lot of time.

(also, quick PSA but please do not just throw a ton of prompts into GenAI tools and make slop videos, it gets extremely boring and repetitive after several watches)

I only spent around 8 hours working on Assignment 1, as the prompt wasn't too difficult to achieve and I already knew before the class began what I wanted to do. However, I had to spend almost 20 hours for Assignment 2 to learn Davinci Resolve, masking, green screen etc., and the TA's still thought my video was pretty mediocre. To make up for this, I spent a bit more time (~25 hours) composing and remixing the song for my Assignment 3, which paid off. Finally, I spent around 30 hours total for the final project making both my audio and video components. This course ain't for you if you don't have any free time or if you value your sleep schedule (although during midterm season most people probably don't value it anyways), I had to pull near all-nighters for these assignments.
comment_grading:
3 assignments (10%, 20%, 25%), 1 final project (35%), Peer and research ratings (10%, basically graded on completion unless they notice you're spamming)

Grade boundaries: A range was generally around 4.1-4.3, B+ range 3.5-4, B range 3-3.5, C+ range 2.5-3, C range 2-2.5. The mean generally hovered around the B-B+ range, which is actually quite good for HKUST standards, but for this course if you want A-range you need to either do something related to music or video creation you already have a lot of experience in and/or spend a huge amount of time and effort (see workload).

Also, no matter how bad your video might seem to you, send it to Prof. Horner anyways, you get a bonus if you allow your video to be shown in class and peer-reviewed.

comment_content:
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp1943/

(For the rest of content please goto "Grading" session)

The course provides you free environment for video editing and appreciate each other's work. You have to edit 4 videos in one semester and your work would be shown to the whole class for grading and feedbacks.

You have to be dedicated in put your effort in every assignments and everyone could see that in the lecture. If your work is "SUPER INSPIRED", it would have a chance to be demonstrated in COMP4441 next semester as an honor.

Meanwhile, you could get inspired by different creative ideas and approaches from your classmates, TAs and the Professor throughout their feedbacks, the past work demonstrations and different student's commentary work.

Lastly, takeaway suggestions to enjoy this course (there's other suggestions in other sessions):

0. Don't be hallucinated with the 'Good grades', have a try ONLY if you're interested and willing to learn editing videos.

There's a lot of obviously disappointing works and AI slops in this semester. Some of them are even off topic and lack of efforts----even condemned as 'a waste of time' according to some classmates.

Just remember, your work would be shown in front of the class.

1. Take this course with your friend, or make a friend in this class who know video editing or even start a learning club LOL

2. Avoid free-riders, especially when your group-mate is not your friend.

3. Respect each other's work. I've seen a student trying to attack me and my friends, also disrespecting Vocaloid MVs as "boring as hell". Please have a positive attitude.
comment_teaching:
I see there's some comments claim that "There is no teaching". I have some counter-arguments.

Please remember this is not a traditional course that professor reading powerpoint slides in dead tone.
Professor Horner would bring you through all the "Best of the Past" works (with commentary) for different MIAs, and give his insights with suggestions. And after you submitted your work, Horner, TAs and your classmates would give you feedbacks that would help you for a better polished product.

Just be active, go ask Horner and TAs when you're free, when you're in need. Like in emails or right after lectures .etc. Trust me, you would learn way more than just listening to reading powerpoints.

FYI, both Professor Horner and the TAs are friendly, just don't be afraid. You could even make friends with them :D

The tutorial materials are actually available but not much being mentioned from the TAs, I hope they could mention more about this. The tutorial materials are very useful. e.g. the RVC stuff.
comment_workload:
The workload depends on these major factors:
Your current state, your expectation, your motivation, hence the dedication.

Your current state:
Do you have any basic skills for video editing?
Do you know how to have a good storytelling?
Do you have crazy ideas/inspirations in your head or in your storage? (Like pranking Horner and UST, or merging Harry Potter and UST?)
Do you have any preferred type of editing? Like meme compilation? Your own animation? Vocaloid MV? A parody or even a GenAI work?

Your expectation:
If you have a good taste and expect a "masterpiece" that's similar with the viral vids from YouTube, NicoNico and Bilibili. Please get ready to suffer from learning many skills from different YouTube tutorials. You should be ready to try and error, meanwhile looking at the deadline coming to you.

If you don't to be so 'churred', lower your bar and accept the fact that 'I can't make things perfect, but I could take my advantage'. Just choose a skillset that you're good with, like, music composition and just focus on it. Way better than start from nothing.

It would be a big red flag if don't have any expectation, your work won't be polished well if you don't push yourself.

Your motivation and dedication:
If you really love the idea and urges to execute that idea well, usually huge motivation would leverage to a MASTERPIECE. The consequence is huge amount of countless nights non-sleep.

I was amazed by one of my classmate that dedicated to personally cosplayed and filmed 星街すいせい's ビビデバ , that's a lot of effort, damn.

(I see there's a Final Year student saying "As a Year 4 student, it’s really suffering to do the project", I feel very hilarious as he delivered a very poorly-performed work and making excuses, meanwhile I'm a Year 4 student too and even having credit overload. )

It's easy to get a pass. But we all could see your effort and attitude in your video.

However, if you want to ace well. Get ready for 20+ or even 50+ hours churring for the A range or even 'high'.

For example, I had to learn Vocaloid cover, pitch tuning, After Effects .etc for my MIA3, and I made my Vocaloid Cover MV from knowing almost nothing in weeks. And getting a very good grade.

I've sacrificed 3-5 nights sleepless, dozen cans of Monsters and midnight screams.

So, manage your workload and time well, learning a new editing tool/skill requires a lot of effort and time. Please reserve at least 20+ hours per work. Add the number up if you're learning a new tool like After Effects or FL Studio or 3D rendering.
comment_grading:
In this course, you have to hand in 4 works in total, also some research ratings, here's the composition:
3 Assignments (45%: 10, 20, 25%),
Project (35%),
Peer & research ratings (10%)

The research rating would come from the research released by the TAs in Canvas, just follow the instructions and submit them on time. Then you could get easy full marks on this part.

For each assignment/project (what we called "MIA"), you had to submit:
1. a 2-3 minute work ("masterpiece")
2. a directory commentary video of your work, "includes inspirations, creative process, storytelling, video and music magic tricks, and other cool secrets." according to the source.

Your MIA's grade would come from 3 factors:
1. Professor Horner
To be honest, grading from Prof. Horner is kinda biased: he has a strong preference in so-called 'wacky-twists', in which does not favor in any really slow-paced or 'plain' work. Please have some mental preparation.
2. TAs.
3. Your peer grading:
It's done by Canvas quiz in every lecture: it would count toward your attendance so please do the quizzes.
All you have to do is to grade everyone's work out of 5 (refer to the following rubric) and leave your comments.
However, there's some students that just leave the comments empty and randomly give marks so that they could fake the attendance :(. It could strongly affect the totals marks given to everyone.
So, please do attend, enjoy the lecture, and do grade and comment your peer's work :) That would be helpful to everybody.

Marking rubric:
Wow-factor of creation video (and behind-the-scenes video), count out of 5:
5/5: Super inspired
4/5: Very inspired
3/5: Inspired
2/5: Somewhat inspired
1/5: Uninspired
0/5: Not submitted

You could either submit your work in two ways:
Plan A:
Your work would be played in class, in front of Horner, TAs and your classmates, could get a maximum of 5/5 (FULL MARK).
Strongly recommended if you want to ace the course. And you would get the feedbacks from Horner, TAs and your peers.
Plan B:  
Your work would NOT be played in class, but you could only get a MAXIMUM of 2/5. DON'T take this plan unless you just want a pass or you're too shy or your work is too bad——take a try in Plan A is always worthy.
And you would only get Horner's comment ONLY.
