comment_content:
There are 3 assignments: The first one getting us familiar with video editing, the second one focusing on the use of greenscreen & the 3rd one on music remix. Then, a final project that utilizes anything and everything you've learned throughout the semester. For the lecture content, there is nothing--LITERALLY NOTHING--but watching what other students from the same course have done. (your peers & the works from previous years) Oh, and you also have to do peer reviews as well for each assignment.

Basically, just make sure you're making fun videos--everyone's review is subjective, and that includes the professor & TA as well!
comment_teaching:
There is no teaching from the professor aside from his commentary on each videos shown in class, since he believes the best way to learn is to learn from others.
Despite that, I LOVE this professor a lot! He'll patiently answer any questions you throw at him, asking him for advices and stuff. Plus, he's very lenient if you got any problems with your submissions. For one of our submissions, my group uploaded the wrong commentary & for the final project, we weren't able to finish the video in time. However, the professor was very nice about it when I emailed him.
Plus, it is very effective to "learn" from what other people did as well, as he explained what he liked & disliked from different videos each time, it's easy to understand how his grading works after the first submission--which is, effort is key!
comment_workload:
This is my own subjective opinion only, because my experience in video editing is little to none :p
The workload for this course is extremely intense, if you're not familiar with video editing beforehand, and are aiming to get good grades. For each of the submissions, you have to make a 1~2 minute video (2~3 for the final project), and a commentary video on what you have created. Basically:
-You better have some creative ideas, and don't be afraid to express them. Otherwise, picking the right topic for each video may be quite exhausting & time-consuming!
-The professor values the level of detail in your videos quite a bit, stuff like matching the BGM frame-by-frame, how you apply greenscreen/other techniques to make the video that much funnier/realistic...so if you want to get the good scores, prepare to work sleeplessly/over a few days on the videos : ) I didn't sleep AT ALL the day before each submission.
-Get yourself a teammate. The work can be done by a group of 2, so find a friend & you'll half the workload. I sure relied on my friend quite a bit, couldn't have gotten this far without my bro. (Sike, both of us still went sleepless working on it, so yeah)
-The main video is important, but the commentary may be even more important. The commentary can be a pretty fun video filled with memes or a bit more serious (like an actual commentary), but whatever your style is, you'll want to pick out some of the most important/overlooked details from the main video & explain how those moments were made, instead of just replaying the entire video with some subtitles going over it :p

TL;DR, if you want to enjoy a common core with light workload, this is NOT the place for you. If you like video editing & are willing to either spend 15~20+ hours on each assignment on average, or is OK with getting a lower grade, then this will be a fun course for ya :)
comment_grading:
You'll get in A-range as long as you're in the upper quartile after everything is evaluated. The professor & TAs seemingly grade things based on 2 aspects: creativity and how much you focused on the small details. Though, it's all subjective so your work may not be liked.

My grade (LQ/Median/HQ)
Assignment 1: 3.84/5 (3.95/4.26/4.51)
Assignment 2: 4.64/5 (3.7/4.09/4.38)
Assignment 3: 4.76/5 (3.5/4.12/4.55)
Final project: 4.81/5 (3.68/4.03/4.49)

Because they didn't like our first assignment, we adjusted our styles, put in a lot more time & effort into the subsequent submissions. We worked for about 30 hours nonstop for the final project, and at least 15~20 hours for both assignment 2 & 3 each, hence the much higher scores.

Still, the hard work paid off well for us. Me & my friend both got A+
:)

comment_content:
I don't usually write course reviews but I love this course so much that I am writing a really longggg review here ^^

basically, you need to submit 5 assignments in the entire semester (so around 1 every 2-3 weeks), the first 4 will be a video around 1-2 minutes, and the last one will be a video around 2-3 minutes.

You will be editing videos using films / dramas / games / anime / anything with audios (self-composed /songs /anything). Although there are topics for each assignment, you can pretty much do anything you want with any material you like as long as it's somewhat related to the topics. For each assignment, you also need to submit a director's commentary video where you show what effort you made

I used Da Vinci Resolve, audacity and BandLab. But you can use anything you want

According to the professor and TAs, the content and format of COMP 4441 and COMP1943 is almost identical
comment_teaching:
don't expect any actual lectures on video/audio editing. TAs may do a few short tutorials on basic techniques but that's it. The professor will be playing videos edited by students in previous semesters or showing everyone's assignment and comment on what he/she did well in the assignment. Watching others' work is so much more effective to learn than having long lectures on techniques and theories in my point of view ^^

really love how he is always supportive no matter how bad a video is lol the TAs are very fun as well
comment_workload:
it depends. for example,
(i) how much effort do you want to put in each video?
- you can do a lot of edits and create a lot of new things, or you can always do really quick editing, it's entirely up to you
-> most of the time more effort = people being more impressed by your work and giving you higher marks, but if your ideas are smart, you can get a really high score (even higher than cases where people put a lot of effort but no one understands the effort)

(ii) are you interested in video/audio editing?
- I find video/audio editing really fun so I did not consider the work as workload at all. I almost treated the assignments as leisure activity lmao
- but if you hate it, I don't see why you want to get in the course in the first place
comment_grading:
I had an A+, and I think the course is a good grade course since I had no video editing experience before at all

50%- 4 assignments (all my regular assignment had marks very near the mean, one below mean and other three is slightly above mean)
40%- Final assignment (5.08/5, mean 4.2, highest 5.4)
10%- Peer evaluation (full mark for me)
10%- Listening test (bonus I think?)

For the assignments, make sure to submit them to canvas and to the prof through email. You will get a base mark no matter what if you only submit to canvas, but people who sent the prof an email will have their video shown in class and will get bonus marks (the amount of bonus will depend on what mark the prof/ta/other students gave you, but you will get some no matter what). Almost everyone would send the professor emails, so please do so as well. No one will make fun of you :)

The 10% peer evaluation is just surveys where you give marks to your peers' work. You will get the marks if you did them on time (and you didn't just give all full marks or zeros)

The listening test is not actually a test, it's more like a survey, nothing will be tested. If you didn't just submit random answers so you can finish the test asap, I think you'll be fine

tips:
(i) some reviews say audio editing is not important, I agree you can get high marks by just using a song in the background if your video is good. However, I'm sure people would be more impressed if you showed your effort to create original stuff, even if it doesn't sound professional

(ii) although of course it's important to put effort in your regular assignments, make sure to make your final assignment a really good one, since it can affect your grade hugely

(iii) you never know what the prof/ta/students like and will be impressed by, so SHOW ALL YOUR EFFORT ON THE DIRECTOR'S COMMENTARY, EVEN IF THE EFFORT SEEMS NOT WORTH MENTIONING TO YOU
-> in some sense, the director's commentary is a lot more important than your actual assignment
(especially if you did some impressive stuff, it's not surprising if some people didn't get what you did if you did not emphasize your effort in the commentary)

(iv) don't worry if you are new to editing. you will pick up things quickly when you start your first assignment

comment_content:
Basically you need to submit 5 videos (10 if you also count the director's commentary) throughout the semester (every 2-3 weeks). You can do it on your own, or work with another classmate. Also remember to do the peer review quiz each lesson.

And here are the projects we had worked on this semester:
1. Magic Mashup Trailer (remake a trailer in another style)
2. Cyberpunk: Suspended In Time (include some slow-motion video and relevant sound effects)
3. Funk’n Fight Remix (Fight scene with funky music)
4. Breakup Remix (make a breakup MV for a pair of weird character combo)
5. Final Project- Pick 1 out of 2: a. Lip Reading Music Video; b. Sequel Mashup Trailer

The first 4 regular homework "requires" you to make a video under 2 minutes, while the final one "requires" 2-3 minutes. But, as long as your video is good, exceeding the time limit is not a problem, my 4th assignment that is 2:30 long even got the highest score in class.

Here's my advice before you read the rest, make sure you are somehow interested in video/audio editing before picking it, this will hugely impact how you feel about the workload and content of this course, because video editing can be quite time-consuming and complex.

If you are inexperienced or less-experienced, but don't mind those difficulties and still want to try, you definitely have interest and should take this course, but also look out of experienced editors grabbing away your chances of getting As, their works can be close to professional. Plus, this course is not a video/audio editing course, you will have to figure out in your own.

In my opinion, people with video-editing experience have a very great headstart, those with musical skills have also quite a nice headstart too.

As you read along the reviews, you will realise the "creative sound" element seems to be quite minimal. In my opinion, yes and no. We need to express the creative sound design via video, that's why video-editing is important, and one of the important factors of video editing is sound design, so put more emphasis on this sector will help your video to be better.
comment_teaching:
The prof played videos on each lesson, showing good works from previous semesters, and also reviewing everyone's video for peer review with short comments. In some classes, the TA will give a small tutorial on using some video/audio softwares, a helpful introduction for beginners.

It's pretty much self-learning, and try your best to learn and be inspired from others; but if you have any questions, make sure to email.
comment_workload:
TL:DR-
1. You better have some interest in video/audio editing first
2.  Work with your heart, but work even more on the commentary

Some basic info about me: I am a final year, and have editing experience for 5 years because of YouTube hobby, but I haven't edited any kind of music videos until this course, and it was sure fun and expanded my editing potential. I used Davinci Resolve as the video editing software, and used Audacity to help with some audio edits. Davinci Resolve is a godsend if you are into video editing and special effects, it's free, stable and equipped with so many features.

1. This may sound condescending but 90% of students attending would be not very experienced, this had given me a huge advantage in terms of editing efficiency and quality. However, as the semester goes, some of their work have shown significant improvement, it was very exhilarating to me as an editing lover, and definitely pushed me to the limits.

So how's the workload? I would say very little. Each video I spent around 3-5 days, a total of 40 hours to finish. Now some of you may say "wow that's a lot", but this time of work is very typical for video editing, if you are going after perfection. Plus, I don't treat them as homeworks, I treat them as personal projects for fun. So ask yourself, how would you view them as? And do you have the time for that?

2. If you have the interest and the time, have fun, be creative and work it with your heart. a trick I can give you is to work even better on the director's commentary, here's why- to let the viewer better understand your work and effort, and like your work even more. My commentaries on the last two assignments were more detailed and got nearly the highest score.
comment_grading:
50%- 4 Assignments
40%- Final Assignment
10%- Peer Evaluation
10%- Listening Test (Yea, I think this is actually a bonus 10%)

For the assignments, always submit them on both canvas and email, this will give you a bit bonus score (because I think 99% of the class did that). The works that are sent to email will be showcased in class for peer evaluation.

I suppose many of you would be curious what that listening test is all about, don't worry about that, as long as you attend and complete it, you will get full score, the test is more like a survey in my opinion. I will not tell what actually it tests, just not to ruin the surprise (or maybe the topic will be different from mine).

Grading wise, as many people has commented is very LENIENT.
The mean for each assignment is around 4.3/5, not data on the SD, but the highest score is usually 4.9 to 5

I am above mean in all assignments, with 1 highest in class, and my final assignment is 0.07 points away from the highest. Got A+

comment_teaching:
You mostly learn from your own experimenting and experience, just search online if you don't know how to do anything. There are also (optional) tutorials where the TAs teach some of the more basic techniques.

There's not much teaching because you can choose to use any software you want, so what you learn is up to you.

Most of the class time is spent reviewing other people's work to learn from them.
comment_workload:
The time you spend on it depends on how much you want to mix or compose music, and how many effects and cuts or sound effects you want to add.

Usually if you add more stuff to your submission you'll get a higher grade. But if you're able to produce a good submission without much effort then good for you. You could spend 1-2 hours every 2 weeks, or work for hours every single day if that's how you want to spend your time. The longest I've spent is probably around 23 hours on one submission for what I consider a masterpiece (it got really high marks).
comment_grading:
Andrew Horner is a really nice teacher and tries to give as many high grades as possible.

You get a base level grade for handing in your work to canvas. You can also send your submission to the professor for each assignment to get bonus marks (pretty sure everyone does this). Your submission (if you sent it to the professor) will be played in class for everyone to see (don't worry about it it's not scary). Then the professor will say something nice about your work, and move on to the next submission.

Grading is subjective to the professor, the TAs, and partly from the class. There is a canvas quiz each class and each student can give a grade to each submission. Your grade for each submission is calculated by finding the weighted averages of these.

The final project has a higher grade weighting than the other assignments but the topic is more vague so you can do almost anything you want.

I spent time to make good submissions so I got A+. I had a friend take it and spent around 2-3 hours per submission (so like 15 hours of actual work in the whole semester) and got a B.

The course is boring as hell. Taking this course is a waste of your life. You shouldn't take this course as your CC no matter what. For those AI major students? Good luck. I can say that majority is not listening during class.

And do you know why I marked the midterm in the course overview? It is because all content after the midterm is not assessed (since there is no final). I am sure that although there is no quiz on such topic everyone will pay attention in class. ;>
comment_teaching: I am perfectly fine with professor Xiao. Although she mostly only reads ppt, but she is very kind and helpful if you have any issues.
comment_workload:
The workload is high. You have to complete 4 quiizes. Each quiz has 3 long questions (or should I call them essay questions?) and you have 15 minutes to complete them. The quizes are open book, fortunately, although the reading materials is very lengthy. Using AI generated content is prohibited in the quizes and will instantly gets you a 0.

Then is the news presentation. You have to conduct a 3 minutes presentation on a recent news (at most 2 weeks in advance). Slides are not compulsory, but should get you some extra points on clarity and content organization.

And we also have reading response. You can choose from multiple reading materials and write a critical reading response about it. The reading materials are extremely lenghty, leading to difficulties in summarization of the content (since we have a word limit of 1000 words. How on earth am I able to produce something worthy with such a pathetic word count?).

We also have debate. Students are put into groups to do debates on topics created by other students and TAs. The quality of the question varies. Some can be really bad and some are really good and thought-provoking.

Coming with debate is the group and individual debate review. You and your groupmates have to be the judges of one of the debate rounds. A detail guide will be given in the debate tutorial, so not much to worry about. After that, you still have to write a debate response on one of the debates.
comment_grading:
The grading is magical. The marking of the majority of the work (quiz, reading response, presentation, debate, midterm) is subjective, and I actually don't know what the TAs and professor want. I only know that I still got a B even though I failed my midterm (20%) and failed my debate review (15%).

Yeah also participation is important. Don't miss any classes and please be active and engage in discussions if any.

comment_workload:
The grading scheme is a mess. Tons of little sections and one real pain: a formal debate. Yep, an actual timed debate with multiple speakers. In the free-debate bit everyone gets 5 minutes total, but you must swap every ~30 seconds. If your teammate can’t read a timer and hogs 2+ minutes in one turn, congratulations! You just lost half the 15% debate mark.

Quizzes are “open book” but you get only 10 minutes. Three questions are based on a ~10‑page PDF and expect quick, precise answers; the fourth asks you to respond to a Netflix documentary shown in class. Good luck doing that well in 10 minutes.

There’s also a 30-minute “news” presentation at the start of many lectures that must cover AI news from the previous 14 days only. If you present on Nov 20, you can only use items from Nov 6 - 19, which makes finding relevant AI news a matter of luck some weeks.

This is treated as a common-core course, but the workload and micro-requirements feel totally inappropriate and needlessly strict.
comment_grading:
The grading scheme is a ridiculous patchwork: Participation (attendance) 15%, Quizzes 10% (four at 2.5% each), AI news presentation 5% (individual), Reading response 15% (individual) plus 5% peer review, Case study analysis 15% (individual) plus 5% peer review, Debate (group) 15%, and Debate review (individual) 15%, total 100%.

Anything that has a peer review means that 20% of that item’s weight comes from an anonymous classmate while the TA accounts for the other 80%, so your final mark on a task can be dragged down by one random student’s whim. For example, if the TA gives you 10/15 but a classmate gives 5/15, your weighted score suddenly becomes 9/15. They say you can appeal anonymous reviews only for “obvious inconsistency” or “unreasonable/unrelated” comments, but who decides what’s unreasonable?

These tiny percentages seem insignificant on their own, but they pile up and can wreck your final grade. I was pretty much average on everything (except the debate, where my team got crushed because one single "smart" person hogged two minutes in the free-debate slot and half our team score evaporated), and even being near the mean didn’t spare me from the system’s hair-trigger penalties.

comment_content:
Don't believe his syllabus list.

Here is what you will learn:
- How to argue with Dekai
- Dekai's book on AIs being your children
- How to review Dekai's TED talk
- How to bullshit to get participation
- How to use Dekai forum that we never actually used

Life changing experience:
His motto is "AIs are your children". I would like to thank Dekai for enlightening me, because Neuro-sama is now my wife.

Bonus: He teaches some actual ethics

=====

Usually the lectures will follow a formula.
1) Unscheduled Dekai rant on random topic
2) Watch Dekai's TED talk
While loop:
3) He asks us for our opinions, you raise your hand to speak out to the class.
4) Other classmates will respond to your comments, and the chain continues until Dekai doesn't like one of the takes and begins debating with that one student.

Judging off of purely the contents, the best parts of this course are the discussions we have. He brings up issues and we discuss. Wish we just skipped all the foreplay so we had time for longer and better discussions. But this part of the course which I liked isn't really sunshine and rainbows either (read Grading).
comment_teaching:
Dekai is legitimately a very active teacher. There is never any dead air like the other profs in UST and you won't be bored. He is the type of teacher who would explain everything in great detail.

Problem is he talks about things that are COMPLETELY UNRELATED to the "syllabus materials". He does not explain what AI means until mid semester, he does not introduce ethics terms until late semester. The first few weeks of the course consisted 30 mins of rants about the latest AI trends. The one I remember the most was on Elon Musk buying out Twitter. He dragged this on for so long because someone replied to him with a different POV. Isn't this a credit bearing course? We have a midterm right? Can we please get back to the planned contents?

Discussion is a very big part of the course. I did not talk too much when compared to the rest of the class. Some students are very active and I enjoy listening to their opinions. Dekai's comments, are very biased to (that particular side).

Small rant:
Dekai went for a "United Nations" meeting and we skipped 2 weeks of lessons, and he comes back telling us we need to come for lecture at 7:30AM. We thought it would be on Zoom, but NO you have to come back F2F, and remember, if you don't come back, no participation score, bad grade gg. (My friend lives on the other side of HK, he has to wake up at 5 for Dekai).

Also, there was one lesson where he just "decided" to not show up, I only had his lesson that day, what a waste of my bus fare and time.
comment_workload: Virtually no workload. Just remember to come to lecture every time. Participation is important.
comment_grading:
Participation, Quiz, Homework, Midterm, Final Project(Video)

I'm not rating it lower for grading just because it is very easy to rig the system to get yourself higher grading. Otherwise, the grading for this course is disgustingly unfair.

For the midterm, remember to study on the different ethics terms. if you pay enough attention during class, you would have learnt basic bullshitting skills, and you'll probably be fine finishing the midterm with good scores. If you have done DSE, the question type is similar to Liberal Studies data-based questions.

================
Examples:
Q1:   Fox News Article reporting on Facebook Whistleblower
- What the inherent biases seen in this report, and what are the consequences of reporting like this?

Q2:  Some researcher's thesis
- Why AI cannot do critical tasks such as judgement?

Q3:  Report on Google's ethical board not including African Americans
- Do you think representation is important in such companies' ethical boards? Argue based on either one: deontological, virtue ethics, consequentialist
================

For the final video, make sure you stand out. He never tells us what the marking rubric was, and I still to this day dunno how I did for my video.

Homework is very simple. Just need to read some articles and find biased arguments. (I still don't know how that directly relates to learning about AI ethics but ok).

Quizzes are not quizzes. They are questionnaires from Dekai about stuff like your decisions on the trolley problem. For one particular quiz, we had to watch a Netflix documentary at home, come back and take a quiz on the contents of the documentary. Some of us are poorL and don't have Netflix.

A large chunk of the course grade is class participation. This leads to very unfair grading. Here is why.

1) If you are not comfortable talking in front of the class, even if you have a lot you want to say, you will never get the chance to participate. Hence, bad grade gg.
2) If your reaction time is not above upper quartile, Dekai will forget that you have raised your hand and you will never get the chance to speak. Hence, bad grade gg.
3) If you raise your hand for too long, Dekai will forget that you raised your hand and you will never get the chance to speak. Hence, bad grade gg.
4) The class time is very short, if someone argues with Dekai for too long, everyone will lose their chance to participate, and you will never get the chance to speak. Hence, bad grade gg.

If you REALLY want to take this course, here are some tips:
A) Whatever you say does not matter, just raise your hand as soon as you have the chance.
To put into perspective:
- Well thought out argument: 1pt
- Repeating ideas: 1pt
- Water is wet argument: 1pt

B) If someone begins arguing with Dekai, don't wait for your turn and just jump in and argue with them. It helps with the discussion anyway to have more people talking. The best discussions we've had are always the ones with the whole class arguing, no regard for raising hand. Remember to say who you are before talking and you'll still get the pt.

C) Agreeing with Dekai will shorten the time you spend your turn, and will give your fellow classmates more chance to get their point. Also Dekai will have a good impression of you.

D) Remember to check if the TA recorded your mark last lecture correctly at the start of the lecture.

We've had pretty good discussions this semester, hearing my classmates' hot takes on topics like AI uprising definitely helped me gather better perspectives on these issues.

But sorry this is all credit bearing, and Dekai's grading system is rigged. Don't ruin your grade for some "good discussions".

Follow these suggestions and you'll survive.

• Introduction

• Weak AI, Strong AI & Superintelligence

• Privacy, Safety, Security
• Unconscious VS Inductive VS Algorithmic Bias

• Information Disorder

• Conscious AI
• Empathetic AI

This course critically surveys the fast moving, urgent, emerging area of AI ethics. AI is explosively disrupting every sphere of our work and lives. Cambridge Analytica and fake news bots. AI driven social media displacing traditional journalism. Drone warfare. Elimination of traditional jobs. Privacy-violating advertising. Biased AI decision/recognition algorithms. Deepfakes. Autonomous vehicles. Automated hedge fund trading. No area remains untouched. Policy think tanks, governments, and tech companies around the world have started paying serious attention to AI ethics. How will human civilization survive the rise of AI? What are the new rules? What are the ethical frameworks needed to avoid extinction? What are engineers’ and entrepreneurs’ ethical responsibilities?

comment_teaching:
Each week we cover a different aspect of AI ethics and society, with the class structured in two halves.

The first half begins with a short provocation such as a TED talk, to create a controversial context for discussion and debate. We then review the relevant literature, illuminate major concepts, and critique them. The first half finishes with a questionnaire/quiz that tests your understanding and poses self-reflection challenges.

The second half exercises these concepts. We may do case studies, breakout groups, collaborative mind mapping, and other interactive work to help concretize, explore, and internalize the issues and challenges. Individual contributions to these exercises over the course of the semester are a large part of the assessment for the course.

Provocative content,
Discussion-based
comment_workload:
Very low,
Assignment 1: News content map
2: one midterm mid-length written question based questions
3: final presentation of the news content map
comment_grading:
Virtually 45% from in class participation, which is counted for every point you raised in class or on the online forum

Your grade will be determined by a combination of factors:

Grade weighting
Class participation ~25%
In-class exercises/quizzes ~20%
Assignments ~20%
Midterm exam ~10%
Final presentation ~25%

comment_content:
Given that this was the first offering of this course as a CC, future offerings should be better with less news analysis, more concepts, and a textbook (he claimed that it'll be published in 2022).

Issues investigated with excess effort:
• Information disorder: The inability of algorithms to prevent the spread of misinformation and disinformation
• Unconscious bias, inductive bias and algorithmic bias
• Remarks: These investigations began in Week 2 and weren’t complete until Week 10 (6 weeks later than the schedule)! The professor attributed this to the spike of Facebook controversies during the semester.

Issues investigated with reasonable effort:
• Creative AIs
• Strong AIs

Issues investigated with insufficient effort:
• Artificial children
• Artificial gossips
• Prescriptive/descriptive and deontological/consequentialist/virtue ethics
• AIs and weapons

Kind of useful concepts (but made shallow effort to cover them): Trolley problem, Scissor statement

Case study: Current news (e.g. Covid vaccines, Facebook)
• A LOT OF COURSE HOURS (equivalent to 5-6 complete lectures) originally intended for lectures on important concepts were spent to discuss tech news (with the most emphasis on controversies of Facebook), a significant amount of which was irrelevant to AI ethics (e.g. TRUTH Social, Apple VS Epic, China's crackdown on how long kids can play mobile games per week, you name it)

Remarks: I use "investigated" above because the professor didn't come up with a takeaway message after the discussion in each lecture, and you have to come up with your own.

The course description did say, "This course critically surveys AI ethics ..." The course greatly achieved the intended learning outcomes on analysis of AI ethics, but failed to achieve the rest (comparison of existing AI ethics frameworks, propose designs for an ethically aligned system, ...).
comment_teaching:
Enrolled in the course without reading reviews about this course to find out that the professor is somewhat biased (despite teaching you what is unconscious bias). He tries to persuade you to think that
• the media is biased against vaccines; and
• MAMAA (except Microsoft) are evil and untrustworthy.

Lectures mostly involved discussion on ethical issues related to AI and analysis of recent news. Very few concepts that aid analysis were taught. There wasn't enough evidence to rate how he 'taught', but if you take part in the discussions during the lessons, he can inspire you to investigate the problem from a deeper perspective. Oh, and classes often overran.

P.S. To those who read the reviews of COMP4901M (the former code of this course) and COMP4221, the professor is finally willing to share his slides to everyone after the class!

- - - - -

TA: Serkan Kumyol
The TA’s English couldn’t be understood by at least half of the class. And he gives vague instructions on assignments that may change in the last minute...
comment_workload:
Forum participation: You probably need to spend at least 10 minutes on each comment on the forum to ensure that it’s decent enough to secure your score. Some students set the bar really high though.

Readings (most of them are news articles discussed in lectures): Very light if you don't read them. Almost all of them weren't used in the lectures except the one that introduce them, despite being "required readings"… Some are really inspiring and worth reading though.

In-class exercises: Just fill in the Google forms (only 2 in this semester) that he asked you to. They're simple to complete.

Group assignments: Light. Create a mind map on Covid vaccines memes (and news) and revise it around once per month. The only task in which you may struggle is following the format.

Mid-term exam: Write as many short essays (12 to choose from, some have higher scores than others) as you can. Each one is hard enough. Worse still, some are on topics that aren’t even discussed in the class, so there's no way to prepare for them except reading a lot…

Final group presentation: Medium. Convert some nodes on your mind map into HTML NEWS ARTICLES (1-2 each) and do a 5-minute presentation on all of them in class. Lighter if you know how to write HTML documents natively.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading
• ≥85% = A range
• ≥75% = B range
• ≥65% = C range
• ≥55% = D range

Participation score (25% of course grade): Backfired!
• Some students are so keen to earn these points that the professor often struggles to move on to the next topic, has to decide a cutoff for maximum participation scores, and award the extra points students earned as bonuses (with lower weighting).
• If you don’t think you can think fast and vast enough in class, rely on posting your views on the online forum to earn the scores (there’s a catch - you can only get a point if the professor thinks your comment is good enough). Expect a lower weighting though.

In-class exercises (20%)
Group assignments (20%)
Mid-term exam (10%)
Final group presentation (25%)
See Workload for the details of the above items.

My grades: The grades in the above components weren't announced (except the participation score). I got a median participation score, received decent verbal feedback right after the final presentation, and eventually got a B. Given the competitive nature in class, as a Year 1 student, I'm satisfied with it.

comment_content:
The following are the main topics covered in this course:
1- C++ Basics (intro to coding, c++ syntax, types, variables, etc)
2- Control Flow (if statements, while and for loops)
3- Functions (pass-by-value/reference, Recursion, etc)
4- Array (1D and 2D, C-string)
5- Scope
6- Struct
7- Pointer (spent the most time on this topic, l/rvalue, address, "pass-by-pointer", dynamic memory, linked lists, etc)
8- Class
9- Stacks and Queues

Overall, I would consider these topics to be easy and quite basic, as I've already learned about many of them.

Some topics that are more confusing, especially if you have less coding experience, are Recursions and Pointers.

With recursions you have to change the way you think about problems, so I'd suggest spending a bit more time on this topic and do some practice problems and code to get familiar with it. I have learned about recursions before, so I did not struggle much on it.

Pointer is a new concept for me, so I had to spend a bit more effort understanding it, but overall I think it's very simple conceptually. The difficult part is being careful with unusual cases, such as const pointer vs pointer to const and pointers to pointers, and all the cases with linked lists such as the head and tail.

Due to the nature of coding, I'd highly recommend messing around with code on your own. The lecture slides provide lots of example code that you can copy and experiment on. It's useful in helping you build up an instinctive understanding of the C++ syntax and to think like a computer.
comment_teaching:
Dimitris Papadopoulous is a great teacher. I love the way he teaches, especially with how he encourages us to ask questions. He is very engaging and fun to listen to. He also asks good questions that are not on the lecture material that get my head scratching and help me understand the material deeper.

The lecture slides are well made and contain everything you need to know for the course (plus some optional extra info). You do not need to attend lectures to succeed in this course, but in my case I liked Dimitris's teaching so I joined most of them. Like I mentioned in the content section, there are also lots of example code that I suggest you copy and practice with.

Overall, the course content is structured clearly and helpful in understanding. You can do well on the exams just by studying the lecture slides and past-papers.
comment_workload:
I have previously learned Java and self learned Python, so I was able to understand most of the course's topic without spending any time studying. I also spent relatively little time studying for the exams, instead choosing to focus on other classes. For those with less coding experience, your workload may be greater in this aspect.

Labs are super easy, less than 5 min for the first 2 labs, and less than 30 min for the rest.
PAs may take more time. For me, it took like 1-2 hours just to read and fully understand the assignment scenario and what it wants me to do. The actual coding is not too difficult once you understand it, though it may still take quite a bit of time depending on how many tasks are in the PA. The last PA took me a lot of time, maybe like 7+ hours or something like that.

Like I mentioned in the grading section, be careful on the labs and PAs. Aside from hidden test cases, you're allowed to check your score before deadline. As such, you should aim to get 100% on the public test cases. If you're short on time, feel free to use AI to double check your code. Do not use AI to do your assignments, it is not worth it. If, even with ample time, you're unable to do the labs or PAs without AI, you're most likely gonna struggle on the exams since they contain questions that are similar in style to the labs and PAs (though not as long or complicated as the PAs ofc).

comment_content:
The content of the course is comprehensive and truly accelerating in terms of programming skills. It covers all features of control flow, functional programming, recursion (including backtracking and other advanced topics), pointers, dynamic memory allocation, C String features, basics of OOP and basic data structures like linked lists, stacks, queues and BSTs.

Despite the fact that content seems a bit voluminous for beginners, you can get any grade (even solid A or A+) if you read Horstmann, review PPTs, do past papers and solve LeetCode problems in a proper manner and pace.
comment_teaching: I would like to honourably mention my instructor, James Cheung, because he provides a lot of comprehensive materials and stresses extremely non-intuitive edge cases. It was very helpful because, actually, all you have to do to outcompete your peers in midterm and final exams is to complete all short questions without any error. Since they are tricky and meticulously designed, you have to grasp the concepts totally.
comment_workload: Workload is average for CS courses. QA sessions during your labs might be very stressful. Some TAs may urge you to answer the question quickly, some are truly meticulous and may even laugh at you, clicking on your name and “Pass/Half/Fail” table in the sheet. Frankly, they are just joking, but it can be a bit scary (Әйгерім-абла, сәлемәтсіз бе!). Anyway, instructors and TAs are not mean and generally are supportive. Attendance is needed for your labs only, but I would recommend to attend lectures as well.
comment_grading:
Grading schemes seem to be fair. Since the number of students enrolled every semester is high (approximately 500), some rough overall score cutoffs might well be handy. For your reference:

~89-90% A+
~84-85% A
~79-80% A-
…

Please note that such cutoffs are rough and are applicable for the grading scheme:

12% Labs (8x1.5)
24% Assignments (3x8)
24% Midterm (please note that the max score for the midterm is generally not 100, it is lower, so every single error on your midterm affects your overall score dramatically).
40% Final (Anyway, if you are a sigmaboy or gigachad, you may try to farm some kind of comeback aura on your final)

comment_content:
The content is great. C++ is fun. However, at first it might be confusing to people who used to program in Python (like me). In Python, the syntax is very casual. But in C++, it is very strict.

Many people think "pointers" is the hardest topic. I agree, but I think the hardest thing to fully master is "at what circumstances there is an error".

In exams, they can give you a VERY STRANGE CODE (I bet no one will ever code like that) and then ask you whether there is a compilation error / runtime error. I HATE THESE QUESTIONS THE MOST. I will never understand why I need to know whether there is an error when I never code like that. It is like asking a Chinese person about Spanish grammar.
comment_teaching: Cecia teaches very well. Very clear, very engaging.
comment_workload:
8 lab assignments, 3 programming assignments (PAs).

In my opinion, all (except the last programming assignment) were easy and can be finished within hours.

The last programming assignments took me exactly 2 whole days (A whole Saturday and a whole Sunday) to complete. Including debugging, which took me 6-7 hours.

I heard other students complain about the workload of the last programming assignment. The professor replied that typically, 2 days aren't enough for the last assignment.

Since there are hidden testcases, you never know whether your code is really correct, even if you can pass all the shown testcases. Therefore, you really have to be VERY careful when you code.

Some of my friends passed all the shown testcases on the first attempt. But they aren't lucky enough to pass the remaining ones. I, on the other hand, was lucky? Or unlucky? Depending on how you think about it. It's somewhat like 賽翁之馬.

I was unlucky that for PA3, I failed almost all the testcases. And I need to debug case by case (each debugging only solves one or two testcase). And that's why that took me 7 hours. However, after all these debuggings, I was so lucky that I passed all the hidden testcases. I got 100% for all labs and PAs.
comment_grading:
Midterm and Final exam. I got 99% in the midterm and 95.25% in the final. A+ as expected.

The highest score in the midterm and the final was 99% (that was me) and 95.7% (that was my friend). The good thing here is that the marks are divided into many fragments. Even if you are careless about something, you only lose 1 or 2 marks at most. (In contrast, in COMP1021, mistyping one letter can cost you 8 marks --- I experienced it.)

I think I also got the highest overall score in the course. (Yes, I'm showing off)
My overall score is 97.83%, while the highest score shown in Canvas is 97.8%. It looks like Canvas is correcting the high score to 1 decimal place. So I am not the highest if someone got 97.84%.

comment_workload:
Workload is heavy and that's why it is 4 credits
For Spring semester:
1 Quiz, 1 Midterm, 1 Final
8 Labs, 3 PAs
Quiz occurs in 2nd week of semester, and it only covers first few topics

Midterm lasts for 2 hours and final is just a longer midterm with 3 hours including more topics

first few labs takes less than one hour to do but the ones with pointers takes hours to fix memory leak and heap overflow...
remember to attend the labs as they have attendance

PA1 is very easy and should take less than 3 hours
and the rest are PAIN, which lead to the title

PA2 is about "Simplified" Mathematical Expression Evaluator,
Here are the tasks you need to complete in this assignment. In chief, you are required to implement:

A set of recursive math utility functions that are used to calculate the values of mathematical functions, such as square root and power.
A set of recursive string utility functions that are used to manipulate strings, such as a parse_number function that parses a string like "3.14" to a double value 3.14.
An expression evaluator function that evaluates a mathematical expression.
An expression parenthesizer function that adds parentheses and removes extra spaces to/from the expression to make it more readable.
Task 1 and 2 are fine but task 3 and 4 are very painful as you have no idea how to achieve these task with recursions, no loops allowed! I spent my whole weekend and few days around to finish the PA, working 8 hours straight per day, INSANE. I feel my sanity jump mushroom after finishing PA2. The UGTA Harry Li makes this PA, so I will leave a song here for him.
聽我說謝謝你 因為有你 溫暖了四季
謝謝你 感謝有你 世界更美麗
我要謝謝你 因為有你 愛常在Harry
Xie Xie Ni (Thank You) Harry for making my sanity jump mushroom
Initially I want to give Workload a B but considering PA2 it should be C now, following the course title C++
I'm psychologically traumatized!!! (我有心靈創傷啊!!!)

comment_content:
Refer to other posts for the syllabus. Theres a lot of content to be covered.

Generally, the hardest content before the midterm for me was recursion. After the midterm, really really focus on pointers, and dynamic memory. But honestly, how I view it is:

Before the midterm, all the topics are kind of within their own bubble and can be understood separately. But the content after the midterm is very very inter-related. Things like function overloading aren't standalone topics because they teach you how constructors of classes can be overloaded later on. The entire topic of dynamic memory and pointers has so many other applications as well, etc.

My advice is that before the midterm, you can focus on each topic individually, and you'll be fine just practicing a couple past papers and COMP2011 self tests (on the course website>resources).

However for the topics after the midterm, I think you should really try to see the bigger picture of what you're learning. Especially if u plan on proceeding to 2012.



One of the key tips to understand with cpp is that you really need to understand how things work under the hood. Its not like python where you dont really need to think about how the computer program REALLY interacts with your computer.

For example, in python, you wouldn't ever see a statement like 1+'a', because how does that make sense right? how can you add a to 1? But in cpp we learn how these characters are all binary numbers that have equivalent integer values, so here the character 'a' can be turned into an integer and be added to 1.

Memory allocation, in particular, is key to being able to do well in 2011. In python, you dont ever have to explicitly allocate memory spaces for anything. In fact, we dont even really consider how the program interacts with memory at all. But CPP lacks a lot of the conveniences of languages like python, so u will need to learn DEEPLY how memory works for different variables and data structures.

comment_content: The tutorial sessions are enjoyable and provide a welcoming introduction to programming in C++. The instructor creates a lively and interactive learning environment, which makes the material more approachable. However, the curriculum is somewhat limited in scope, focusing primarily on fundamental concepts such as basic C++ syntax, pointers, classes, and stack operations. While these topics are essential for beginners, the tutorial could benefit from covering more advanced or practical applications to better prepare students for real-world programming challenges. Expanding the content to include topics like dynamic memory management, inheritance, or common data structures would greatly enhance its educational value. Despite this, the tutorial remains a useful starting point for those new to C++

comment_content:
Took the course during the first semester through c-placement test. If you have any prior experience coding, even if not in c, I recommend you take this course rather than 1021 which is quite boring - unless you want a strong grade boost. Comp 2011 is an actual programming course. It goes over more complex computational thinking concepts such as recursion, pointers, and object-oriented programming (although the last doesn't go too in-depth). In my personal opinion, the course is pretty well thought out as well. You will have to do multiple lab assignments (most of which can be finished in an hour, maximum like 4 hours for people who've coded before). The longer extended assignments (the PAs) are a lot more complex, and also a lot more interesting to work on as you are actually coding something relevant (war simulator, minesweeper).

Besides the labs, there are the usual midterm and final, with finals taking 50% of the entire course grade.
comment_teaching: Its alright, all the bases covered. Prof CHUNG, Albert tries his best to keep the class engaged by breaking down concepts more simply. His teaching style is a lot more on-the-white board, but his English is plenty decent and you can understand everything as well.
comment_workload: If you have prior coding experience the workload is extremely low compared to other courses. I was able to cruise through most of the course without much problems. Attendance isn't mandatory, though the weekly lab assignments are. However, those are really not hard, and actually quite enjoyable to work on anyways. No complaints. If you haven't coded that much besides the pre-requisite courses, I don't have much of a reference point but I don't think it will be THAT much homework.
comment_grading: The grading is pretty typical UST, nothing exceptional. I got 2stdv above the median for midterms, and 1.5 stdv above the median for finals, with a final grade of A.

comment_content:
Most contents are already there in many other reviews. This is copied from others:

Part 1: Basics and Controls
Part 2: Function (Introduction)
Part 3: Array
Part 4: Recursion

Part 5: Scope
Part 6: Function III:
  - Function declaration
  - Function overloading
  - Default Function Argument

Part 7: Separate Compilation
Part 8: C++ Structure

Part 9: Pointers (Intro)
  - lvalue & rvalue
  - dereference a pointer
  - "const" in pointers initialization
  - Array as (constant) pointers

Part 10: Pointers (Usage)
  - Dynamic allocations (on Heap)
  - Problems on dynamic objects:
     ! Dangling pointer
     ! Memory leak
  - Linked List (LL)
  - LL + Binary Tree
  - Dynamic 1D and 2D array

Part 11: C++ Class (Intro)
  - Class == Struct with its own functions
  - keywords "public" and "private"
  - Constructors
     Trigger when a new object is created
  - Destructor
     Need when the object has pointers
  - Accessor
     Always followed by "const"
     Should NOT modify its data members
  - Mutator
     Opposite to accessors
  - Scope operator

comment_content:
1. Introduction to computer programming
2. C++ basics: basic syntax, data types, operators
3. Control flow
4. Functions
5. Array and structure
6. Recursion
7. Scope
8. Struct
9. Pointers
10. Dynamic Data
11. Class
12. Stack and Queue
13. File input / output

This course provides basic programming knowledge of C++. It begins by teaching the foundation programming logic, such as variables and data types, loops and conditionals, functions and arrays, and Classes, which are just different in syntax. After week 6, the concept will be more difficult because the logic of recursion sometimes is really hard only using the mind to think out the picture, struct, and pointers are the new things who only learn about Python, the dynamic array also needs to handle the memory leak in the same time, and it also includes the data structure link list, queue, stack, and the binary tree.
comment_teaching:
My teacher, Ki Cecia Chan, has a clear teaching style. Although she sometimes teaches at a slower pace, her use of examples helps clarify concepts that we might be missing. She also follows the content in the PowerPoint presentations and uses a C++ shell to demonstrate key ideas.

If you are good at self-study, learning it by yourself is more efficient. If you encounter problems, you can refer to the video recording or attend the live lecture to ask questions.

The lab sessions consist of two parts: The first part is a 15-minute tutorial. After the teaching, the TAs will select half of the students to answer questions based on that week’s lab, making attendance important.

comment_content:
bg: 2nd yr COMP student, getting A in COMP1021, no more previous programming experience, average IQ???.
friendly reminder:
1.If this course is not needed to fulfil your requirements, I generally don’t recommend taking it unless you achieve A range in COMP1021.
2. If you are a yr1 student and aiming to get into CSE, consider your own situation carefully before enrolling in this course, so that you won’t jeopardize your semester and cga.(I know that some of you are geniuses and can easily handle the stuff)

Now let's go back to the content.
Week 1-3:
Basics, Control Flow. Very basic knowledge about C++ programming, but still lots of details to notice if you only did programming on Python previously.
Week 4-6 (before midterm):
Function (Basics and Recursion), Array. The 2 most difficult topics before the midterm are recursion and array. It’s not easy to think recursively as our brain isn’t designed to think that way. Array have many rules to follow, and it’s very easy to make mistakes like segmentation fault.
Week 7-10:
Scope, Struct, Function (overloading, declaration, default arguments). This part is quite decent compared to others, and it covers some knowledge that you need to know before learning class, pointer.
Week 11-14:
Pointer, Class, Stack & Queue. This is the most challenging part of this course. Lots of concepts and rules are introduced in this part. Personally, I hate the combination of dynamic memory, string and class the most.(Actually I had a similar question about that on the final and messed it up - losing half of the marks on that 18 pts LQ)
comment_teaching:
lecture:
Dimitris’ teaching is decent. He speaks English with a distinct accent, making ‘r’ sound very special, and his tone and talking speed are a bit different from typical English. But I can understand most of that after two or three lectures. Anyway, He is very passionate about teaching and he would like to make interactions with students, fostering a lively classroom environment. After the lectures, he is also friendly to answer questions.

lab:
We have a lab every week except the midterm week, and the lab is actually a half-an-hour tutorial. During the lab, contents that are helpful for programming but not tested in exams like debug and separate compilation are taught by TA. In our section, the TA is Peter. Peter is an interesting guy who likes making political jokes. His teaching is ok but a bit rushed, leaving no time for us to try on our own. In every lecture, Peter uses some memes about our people’s GREAT GREAT Leader Xi (and sometimes Kim) to make us feel engaged, demonstrating that HK is a place embracing free-speech.
comment_workload:
1 lab per week + 3 programming assignments
The workload is even huge compared to other 4-cre courses. Each lab typically took me 2 hours to finish, except the one about recursion which was very annoying to understand the logic. For the PAs, the first two took me 3 days, around 8 hours each to finish. The last one, I spent a week, around 12 hours to finish, including a day purely for debugging (why are there so many memory leaks and segmentation faults…?). Also, since the difficulty of the content, I also spent 1-2 hours after each lecture to revise.
comment_grading:
lab(12%):
Each lab is counted as 3 marks:
1 mark for attendance, recorded before lab session;
1 mark for lab assignment, which is like a homework available 1 week before lab session and due after the lab session;
1 mark for Q&A session, where students are selected to answer a short question about lab assignment.(once every 2 weeks, if the student is not selected, the 1 mark will be counted base on lab assignment)
The total 12% is given according to the percentage of your lab mark.

PA(24%):
Each PA is worth 8%. Before the ddl, about one-third of the test cases are provided for testing, and others are hidden test cases. The PAs are easier compared to previous semesters, with the mean of each being nearly 90.

Exams: hand-written exam with short questions and long questions. In short questions, the format is usually like, providing a program and asking for the output, or filling in the blanks of a piece of code. Every question requires significant effort and can be very annoying. For long questions, the format is like writing a function so that it can work as expected. Since you cannot use a computer, every minor mistake or carelessness during the thinking process will get you into trouble, and you cannot vertify it by running on the computer.

Midterm(24%): mean-37/70 sd-12
The midterm is 2-hr long, testing the content during week 1-6. There are around 10 questions in total, with 6-7 short questions and 3-4 long questions. The most challenging questions of midterm are an SQ about reference variable, a LQ about recursive function, 2 LQ about array.
Final(40%): mean-51/100 sd-23
The final is 3-hr long, testing the content throughout this semester. There are around 10 questions in total, with 6-7 short questions and 3-4 long questions. The most challenging questions of the final are an SQ about scope, pointer, and array, an SQ about loop, a LQ about recursive function, 2 LQ about dynamic array.

B is given to students getting mean of the exams.
A range is 1SD or above.(it seems like I’m near the cutoff of A range, with total 81.5%)
/*update: final grade is A-, I guess I'm slightly above the cutoff, maybe few pts. one of my frds got b+ with 77.5%*/
To pass this course, students need to have total score >= X%, weighted exam score >= Y%, where X is below 50, and Y is below 30.

comment_content:
The course format includes lectures and labs/tutorials. Attendance is not required for lectures but is required for the labs/tutorials. Attendance for the labs is very strict and accounts for large portion of that lab's score. There is one quiz, one midterm, and one final exam. There are a total of around seven labs, but this depends on the time available. Each lab requires a few hours to complete and includes instructions. Also, there are three programming assignments, which may take up to a few days.

The TA's were very helpful. Even if you had difficulties while doing the assignments, they would still give you hints. They aren't allowed to directly help you solve the problems, but their hints are more than enough for you to figure it out yourself.

The syllabus includes the basics of C++ programming (e.g., syntax, the concept of compilation), control, functions, scope, pointers, recursion, stacks, queues, structures, classes and arrays. There are also self-study topics which include file input and output, separate compilation and the string class.

The first half of the course was not too bad. When they get to topic on pointers, stuff gets pretty difficult. But the instructors give plenty of past papers, which I found to be very helpful.

Note: although the materials may seem okay, the problems in the exam are much harder.

Note: although it was said that the self-study topics will not be assessed, they are still somewhat included in the exams.
comment_teaching: This instructor's explanations were very clear. Although our section was relatively slow compared to the others, the pace was just right. The instructor encouraged students to ask questions, which was good. For harder topics such as linked lists, she also made her own slides with her own examples which I found very helpful. She is also very responsive to emails and would give thorough explanations.
comment_workload: For a 4-credit course, the large workload is understandable. The labs were almost weekly. There were no readings required, but the suggested textbook was very helpful.

comment_grading:
I got a B+, but that really isn't important.

POV: You're Year 1 looking to get into CS, just got past your fall semester probably with an A range in 1021/1022P, found it easy and/or fun and want to take more coding courses. Now, if it wasn't for the fact that you need GPA to actually choose your major, you probably would've already decided to take 2011, and I would fully support that decision. But the matter of fact is, you still need to keep your GPA high which is exactly why I discourage you from taking this course unless you have a very good reason to. Basically all of the engineering introductory courses are way easier than this course even if you like programming over those subjects. If I had the chance to choose again, I would just wait until Year 2 to take this course and just pre-enrolled in something like CIVL1100 instead, even though I didn't plan on choosing CIVL as my major. Hell, I'd even rather just have one less course in my semester to focus on my other subjects and get a better grade on those instead. In my opinion, its almost never worth it.

If you're still determined to take this course and are like "Nah, I'd win", then I salute you and wish you good luck. Put in consistent effort, its not like 1021 where you can go all in on your exam. I got below mean for my midterm as a result of winging it and not being used to having to write code on paper instead on a computer, and thats basically the end of my hopes of getting an A range even though I didn't realize it at the time. I tried to fix it and did well in my finals (82), but that wasn't enough to get me an A range which was unfortunate. Anyways that's enough of an old dog's rambling. Just know, even though you are smart, so is everyone else in the course. The only edge you can have over them is putting in more time and effort than them consistently.

This course focuses on a systematic view of C++. Unlike the useless COMP 1021, this course provides you with detailed step by step about the operation of C++ as a programming language. comment_content: The content of the C++ programming course was generally quite good. It covered all the important concepts and topics related to C++ programming, including variables, data types, control structures, functions, classes, and object-oriented programming. The course materials, including textbooks and lecture notes, were comprehensive and provided a solid foundation for understanding the language. However, there were a few areas where the content could have been improved, such as providing more real-world examples and practical applications of C++ programming.
comment_teaching: The teaching in the C++ programming course was satisfactory. The instructor had a good understanding of the subject matter and was able to explain the concepts clearly in class. They were knowledgeable and approachable, and encouraged students to ask questions and seek clarification. The instructor also provided helpful resources, such as coding exercises and programming challenges, to reinforce the learning. However, there were times when the pace of the lectures was a bit fast, making it challenging to fully grasp some of the complex topics.
comment_workload: The workload in the C++ programming course was quite heavy and overwhelming. There were numerous assignments, projects, and coding exercises to complete within tight deadlines. The workload often required long hours of coding and debugging, leaving little time for other coursework or personal activities. It would have been beneficial if the workload had been more balanced and manageable, allowing students to delve deeper into the concepts without feeling overly burdened.
comment_grading: The grading in the C++ programming course was average. The criteria for grading were communicated at the beginning of the course, and the instructor provided timely feedback on assignments and tests. However, there were instances where the grading seemed inconsistent, with some assignments receiving higher grades than expected based on the feedback provided. Additionally, the grading rubrics could have been more detailed and transparent to ensure a fair assessment of students' work.

comment_grading:
Weighting:
Lab:9% (6 Graded, 2 Attendance only)
PA: 24% (3 Total, 8% each)
Midterm: 27%
Final: 40%
*Graded labs are worth 3 points (1 based on ZINC result, 1 attendance, 1 from Canvas Quiz (Will only be given if attended Lab))
*Ungraded labs are worth 1 point (1 attendance)

Stats (My Raw Grade / Max Score / Weighted Score):
Labs: 19.75 / 20 / 8.875%
PA: 298 / 300 / 23.84%
Midterm: 93.25 / 100 / 25.1775%
Final: 103.5 / 110 (100 in practice, read note) / 41.4%
Total: 99.305%
Final Grade: A+
I think you can get A+ if total ≥ 90%, similar to COMP2011

Note: For some reason, the maximum score for the final is treated as 100. That extra 10 points is treated as bonus, i.e., you can get 44% at most (assuming you get full mark)

Personal Advice:
1) Canvas Quizzes in labs will reference lab content, but also the related topic, i.e. Remember to check both lab programme and the lecture notes, in case they ask for some specific behaviour.

2) Occasionally, the labs and PAs instructions may be a bit unclear, ask on Piazza! The TAs will usually reply within a day or two. Remember to check your code to make sure it fits updated requirements. (Forgot to check in PA1 and lost 2 pts :( )

3) Write you own test cases! The public ones on ZINC are not  always exhaustive.

4) Study well on Constructors, Destructors, Move Semantics & Copy Elision. This sem, ~10-15% in midterm and final are related to them. They will ask for the outputs which depends on which constructor is called. It is very easy to make a mistake, which in turn cascades into other mistakes. The question in the final has a point only awarded ONLY IF you get ALL output right.

5) The exams' programming questions doesn't tend to stray too far from lecture notes, but read the instructions and sample outputs carefully. Seriously, sample code + sample output + instructions are often 3 - 5 pages long, but implementation of tasks are fairly easy if you understand the question.

课程内容有些错位，这是因为诸如STL、static关键字以及namespace等实用性强的内容被安排在课外自学环节，而非作为授课部分详细讲解的核心内容。相比之下，面向对象编程（OOP）部分（p.s.仅限于对象生命周期）的讲解则非常详尽（占比70%以上，甚至有点过于详细，而完成UROP，甚至完成COMP 2012自己的Lab，PA，还是都不需要知道这么细致的关于面向对象编程的内容，感觉这门课的很多考题设计出来就是为了卷而卷，死抠犄角旮旯的C++语法细节，而不详细讲解C++在项目中更加实用的部分（string类库，STL容器，智能指针，多线程等等）。而且上课的时候没有提到类似虚函数表以及实现的的底层细节）。此外，数据结构占比差不多30%，讲了动态数组，单链表、双链表，栈、队列，二叉树（循环版本的前序中序后序遍历、递归版本的各类遍历、层序不包括），二叉搜索树 BST，哈希表（拉链法、开放地址）的增删改查，算法题讲得很少，完全不包含图，Trie 字典树，并查集，线段树 / 树状数组，LRU 缓存等更多的内容，感觉作为“数据结构”有点名不副实。
comment_teaching:
Core Issue Overview  
A notable disconnect exists between teaching and examination: Although Cecia provides detailed explanations of code specifics during lectures, there is a significant gap between the course content and the actual requirements of the examinations.  

Recommendations  
1. Necessity of self-directed learning: Relying solely on classroom content is inadequate for examination preparation. Additional independent study and practice are required to bridge the gap.  
2. Limitations of past examination papers: The provided past papers have limited alignment with the challenges of actual examinations. Complete reliance on them for preparation is not advisable.
comment_workload:
The workload for COMP 2012 is relatively heavy.
The first PA (Programming Assignment) is a review of pointers and linked lists, covering the final chapters of COMP 2011.
The second PA focuses on practicing object-oriented programming (OOP).
The third PA involves developing a small-scale ticket booking system, utilizing data structures and concepts such as binary trees, generic programming, and hash tables. This assignment is particularly demanding but serves as excellent practice for strengthening programming skills.

It is important to note that after submitting each PA, there are numerous hidden test cases to consider. I lost a few points due to some tricky hidden cases, but all my PA scores were above 90/100.

Additionally, each lab session is followed by a corresponding quiz, so thorough familiarity with the lecture slides (PPT) is essential.
comment_grading:
The overall grading composition for COMP 2012 is as follows:
Lab: 9%
Programming Assignment: 3 × 8% = 24%
Midterm Exam: 27% (Mean: 53/100)
Final Exam: 40%

My performance:
Lab: 8.775/9
Programming Assignment:
PA1: 96.8/100
PA2: 92.3/100
PA3: 99.1/100
Midterm Exam: 80.9/100 (Mean: 51.69/100, Upper Quartile: 71.95/100)
Final Exam: 57.35/110 (Mean: 47.5/110, Upper Quartile: 71.03/110) – I FUCKED UP THE FINAL. The additional 10 marks are added directly without adjustment, making it 57.35/100.

Overall: 76.614/100, Letter Grade: B+ (COMP 2011: A-)

The A range accounts for less than 15% of students, essentially reflecting an absolute grading system (overall score > 80). The midterm exam tested the object construction and destruction process with and without RVO (20 marks) and additional destruction details (10 marks). The final exam similarly covered object construction and destruction processes related to Move Semantics (16 marks). Most students were unable to finish the final exam. Additionally, the final included extensive content on Lambda expressions and function pointers (16 marks), which were only briefly covered in tutorials without in-depth study. The past papers provided on the official website are not fully representative, as the actual exam was significantly more difficult and broader in scope. I am in the CPEG program. If you are not in COMP or CPEG, it is advisable to consider taking an equivalent course at another university for credit transfer, if possible.

考试中要求学生像编译器一样手动书写长达30至40行的对象销毁流程代码，这在期中（有RVO和没有RVO的对象销毁）与期末考试（Moving Semantics输出对象生命周期的代码）中均有体现（占比20分以上，完全把考生当成编译器来搞，感觉挺无理取闹）并且期末考试大量考察上课没有大量练习（Lambda表达式），以及在Tutorial上一笔带过（Function Pointer）的内容（两者一共16分），感觉教考分离，内容丰富程度和考试方式远远不如COMP 2011，考试的时候问你“茴香豆的茴有几种写法”，现代版科举考试，（COMP 2011虽然比较难，但是可以学到很多基础算法方面的内容以及指针的底层原理，是真的可以入门C++以及编程的好课，COMP 2012纯粹无理取闹，为了卷而卷（A range < 15%），没苦硬吃！）如果不是Computer Science或者是Computer Engineering的话，请谨慎选择！如果是Computer Science或者是Computer Engineering的学生，也请仔细斟酌一下！

comment_workload:
For a 4-credit course, the workload is quite fair. I don't remember the exact number of labs, but we had three programming assignments (PAs), each of which required more than a day of focused work. Some of my friends, who were preparing for other important commitments, struggled to manage their time and ended up resorting to using other people's work. As a result, they either received close to 0% for those PAs due to inability to debug them or faced a -8% penalty for plagiarism.

The last PA is usually significantly harder than the first two. My advice is to work on your C++ skills daily for at least an hour and to manage your deadlines proactively.

Where can you learn? Practice with LeetCode questions to reinforce your basic knowledge, or dive into deep study sessions on cppreference (which is a fantastic resource). While books are helpful, they may not provide the same excitement, so I personally prefer YouTube videos. One great channel to check out is Cherno.

Dear students,

We believe that we were among the first batch of instructors (back some 10 years ago) who asked the university to help record our lectures and released them in a timely manner so that students had another way to review their understanding of the course materials.

In the beginning, it seems our lecture videos did us good. They are never meant to be a  replacement of attending lectures in person, which to us will serve various educational objectives such as real-time discussion (or Q&A), meeting/knowing your classmates, etc. We notice that some students want to have the videos sooner to review the lectures.  We are willing to balance the educational benefits of lecture videos and decide to release the lecture videos every 2 weeks (biweekly) now. The videos of lectures given in the first 2 weeks of this semester are now available at the Canvas' homepage.

Again the lecture videos should be used to review the lectures and not to replace the lectures.

comment_content:
<I can tell you all focus on the GRADING part.>

Basically the same as noted by others (and past semesters).
Minor changes on progress of teaching and content distribution between Mid-Term and Final.
You can find the full catalog in others posts. Here's a simplified version:
A) C++11 new features → range-for-loop / auto & lambdas / ...
B) function pointers (i.e. void (func*) (int, int)) [remark: said not to be tested this year, but know for your own good]
C) OOP: class & struct(ure) → access control / inheritance / *dynamic binding
D) templates
E) data structures → binary & binary search tree (AVL balancing algorithm is optional) / hashing [remark: basics like link-list are covered in COMP 2011]

* The recent exams favor Binary Trees though.

For your own interest, visit (https://github.com/ADMINGUOYU/COMP2012-HKUST-) to find out more about COMP 2012 this semester. (including my implementation of Labs and PAs, some random notes within class codes and sample programs in some of the mid-terms and finals.)

comment_content:
You know how in 1021 and 2011, you're basically doing "toy problems", where most labs and assigments ask you to finish making a game on the terminal, but nothing really practical?

Well, 2012 is finally the so called "practical programming course", where instead of focusing on foundational techniques, you are now tasked to implement programs using OOP concepts. While 1021 and 2011 are fun to learn, I never felt that I made anything "real" with most of their labs, and I think this course fixes this issue for sure. Do we still print stuff on the terminal? Yes that's true, which isn't super practical; but are the concepts we learned real world techniques? YES.

Const-ness and Lambda
Constructors & Destructor
Inheritance and Polymorphism
Generic Programming
Binary Search Tree
Hashing
rvalue Reference and Move Semantics

I do wonder why they cut AVL tree and STL though, which are also quite useful to learn.

comment_workload:
Something I like about the midterm and final are they don't test trivial and random shit, instead focus on making an entire system the way you like. If your codes can make the expected output, then it's good to go. 2011's final (in my experience) consists of random shit that is absolutely useless to ask, and forces you to do certain things the way they wanted, which sucks when I already figured out what to do but I have to fill in the blanks. I have attached this sem's 2012 final for your reference :)

Anyway, here's why the PAs suck:

1. Lack of public test cases

I get the idea that we should make our own test cases, but we should at least have some format to base off of right? Like there are like 40 methods we need to implement in PA1, but there are 10 public test cases, which some methods didn't even get tested. So now we have to guess how the outputs should look like, and pray ZINC has mercy on us.

2. It's a clusterfuck

PA1 and PA2 are fine, while PA1 is huge, you do know what you're doing. PA2 is fun, teaches you how to trade stock.

PA3 is uhhhhhhh, one of the PAs of all time. The task is to make a Efficient Database Simulator with a shit ton of trees and hashtables. Maybe I'm naive, but I don't think that's how database is implemented? Like the class data structure is there's an index tree where each node stores a binary tree and each node over there stores a hastable for storing entries, which idk, already exists in another hashtable in the class? If you don't undestand anything I just said, nobody does when doing the PA.

The feeling I'm getting from this PA is it is testing knowledge for the sake of testing it, so it's straight up unrealistic for an actual program to look like this. It took me 4 days with constant attention to finish the fucking thing.

comment_teaching:
Given that Desmond is not teaching COMP2012 in the near future due to some program requirement adjustments in CSE, this is just for reference:

If you wish to get a good grade, I would strongly recommend you to enrol in or sit in Desmond's class, what you would gain from his class is MORE than you can expect and worth it.

He has given us clearer explanations on the topics taught in the lecture notes, given us additional and supplementary exercises to practice at home, sent us emails to explain certain topics, and taught us something interesting that is not in the syllabus. Overall, his teaching is comprehensive.

You might want to argue that he shouldn't teach something out of the syllabus, but we are not here to study for good grades. If there is something interesting that may benefit us for our future, I'm willing to learn more, instead of just striving for an A range.

Though it's sad that my PAs 1 and 3 ended up like this (feel sorry about that tbh), Desmond is really a good instructor who cares about students, unlike some who just care about students' performance, but he also cares about our well-being. Instead of a professor, he's more like a mentor to me.

I won't be able to earn an A range in COMP 2012, but still, I've still managed to get a score higher than the UQ, which is totally unexpected, but the efforts and time paid are worth it, as a wise man once told me:
"It's okay to have a not-so-good grade, but at least you have learnt something. " (Tsoi, 2025)
Yes, my PA scores are sh*t, but I've proven myself that I've learnt a lot through the final exams, no matter what letter grade would I eventually get.

Just want to say to Desmond, 喺我心入面你係最好嘅，無論有幾多人鍾意你教書，幾多人唔鍾意都好，希望你可以堅持落去。呢間大學，以至呢個世界需要更多好似你咁嘅老師。Desmond, 多謝你🥹 Virtual grade A++++++++++++ to u!! <3

Lastly, for students taking COMP 1023 and COMP 2111, wisely make your choice of instructors. :)

Assignments:
Before describing the assignments and my experience with them, I'd like to say that the quality of the TA team is not as good as COMP 2011 in Fall 2024/25, given that the instructions in both PAs and Labs are unclear.

The workloads of PA1 and PA2 are similar, with PA1 being more difficult. The difficulty and workload of PA3 can be described as "hellish", given that it's the most challenging PA over the past decade, according to one of the instructors.

And as for PA3, as I was busy working on the functions of my society (literally a function on the deadline day of PA3) and many deadlines, I failed to submit my PA3 on time (and there are also compilation errors -_-), so...... my problem only, deserve it hehe QwQ

It's not very difficult, I would say, if you revise it with average effort, you can still manage to get a mean from it.

Final Exam:
Easier than I expected, except for Question 3, which you have to pay extra attention and carefulness to the outputs, and Question 7, which most students found difficult with the insert() function.
*With the dire score due to the PA experiences, I started revising just minutes after finishing the previous exam, and just slept for 1.5 hours a night before the exam started.
