comment_content:
As you might have guessed from the title, I have a story to tell. Yeah, but I’m gonna save the details for the “workload” section if you are curious about it. For now, I just want to remind you to strictly adhere to the honor code and avoid plagiarism.

Right, let’s come back to the main topic.
content covered:
Lambda, Constructor/Destructor, Inheritance and Polymorphism, Generic Programming, Binary Search Tree, Hashing, rvalue Reference and Move Semantics.
Optional(briefly covered in lab tutorial, not tested):
make, namespace, static, Standard template Library(used in PA3)
2012 delves deeper into materials you have learnt in 2011, especially those introduced after 2011 midterm like pointers. Unlike 2011 which included IQ-style questions like recursion, 2012 focuses more on mastering ‘object-oriented programming’. You can expect greater emphasis on working with “heap”. So make sure to consider carefully about the memory allocation and wisely manage the structure when programming.
comment_teaching:
Lecture:
Dimitris is still Dimitris––If you like him, you like him.
1, He speaks English with a Greek accent and his talking speed can be a bit fast sometimes. So it takes some effort to get used to his English.
2, He tends to emphasize on some important or difficult parts and his pace is also a little bit fast. So it’s not very easy to stay focused throughout the entire class. But it’s definitely rewarding to attend his classes.
3, He enjoys interacting with students. Sometimes, when discussing the output of some examples, he expects answers from students before he gives an explanation. Moreover, he encourages us to pause and ask questions if anything is unclear.

Lab:
Just like 2011, attendance for labs is required.
Vinayak’s session is solid–He can deliver materials clearly, making them useful.
comment_workload:
The workload is even worse than 2011. So try not to take this course with other courses with a huge workload, otherwise life will be hard.
Lab: 8 labs in total. Most of them are fairly easy, but each still takes around 2 hours to finish. I’m not sure whether they can make some changes according to 2211, maybe 4 graded labs, 4 ungraded??
PA: 3 PAs in total. Each of them had some minor mistakes, taking 1-2 days to be fixed after it was released, so it feels like the teaching team wasn't very responsible. First two were manageable, each took around 3 days(15 hrs) to finish. The last one was designed by ‘notorious’ Brian(known for PAs made by him, hahaha). It took me 5 days(30 hrs) to finish that last PA. The structure of the PA was bizarre as hell: you have to build a Binary tree, which stores binary trees, which store hash tables, which store binary trees. 5 levels!!! If you want to know more about the structure, please refer to the attached file shared on Piazza(source: God Yu). The funny thing was that someone crashed out and swore at the teaching team directly on Piazza…Crazy.

My story:
I don’t want to blame my friends for what happened cuz I also hold responsibility, I just hope you can learn from my experience and avoid making the same mistake.
It was actually related to PA1: To be honest, there were a few functions in PA1 that were tricky to deal with. So after I finished, my friend reached out to me and asked questions about some functions. Since we chatted online, it was too hard to describe a 100-line function within minutes, so I explained with screenshots. A few days later, my friend came back again and asked for my code to cross check. He promised me he wouldn't copy it so I sent my file.
A month later, after finishing the 2012 midterm in the morning, I took a joyous nap. But I was shocked when I opened my mailbox afterward—you know what email I received. Panicked, I asked my friend for his last submission. At first glance, I thought it was a mistake cuz when I clicked ‘compared with selected’, the page was overwhelming with differences everywhere, even the indentation was different. I was furious that time like an innocent found guilty, thinking: diuu!!! Goofy plagiarism detection system, causing me troubles. However, that night, as I calmed down and analysed our programs, I noticed something suspicious: My friend had written some functions exactly according to my logic, line by line. The only differences were variable name and indentation… "OMG GGs." Deep copy is still copy, right?:) Upset? Annoyed? Frustrated? For a few nights after knowing that, I couldn’t fall asleep because of the worry.
Anyway, I appealed in hope of receiving a lighter penalty and went to a meeting with Desmond. To demonstrate how sophisticated the system is, Desmond used the system to check our work in front of me again. It flagged our work with a 90% similarity score. He told me that the system has already been in use since he was a student, and has been continually updated over time. So don't try your luck on a system that is probably older than you!(oh btw Desmond is a Young Guy!! :)) I pleaded for leniency from Desmond, he told me that it was also a difficult decision to impose a penalty on me, but for the sake of fairness and consistency in handling plagiarism cases, he couldn’t make exceptions. In the end, we agreed on the penalty: 0 for PA1 with additional 5 marks deducted from total.
Before the meeting ended, Desmond said something that has stuck with me ever since: "If your friend doesn't know what's wrong with their code and wants to see yours, tell them to ask us for help." So please, learn from my mistake: Do not share your code with anyone for any reason!!!
comment_grading:
The labs are pretty easy so make sure to get full marks. The first 2 PAs are manageable. But the third one– designed by Biran–is really challenging.
The exam is similar to the one in 2011, featuring weird short questions and hard long questions. It’s wise to skip some short questions you are not sure about and do some long questions first. Each of LQs is basically a condensed version of lab, with those long functions left to finish. So if you can finish labs on your own, you should be able to handle at least 60-70% of functions in LQs.

comment_teaching:
I have not seen two professors who were this passionate and helpful as Brian and Desmond. Through and through they answered my silliest questions in and after classes. They would be more than willing to set up office hours if you think you need more time with them to understand certain topics.

Desmond is popular for a good reason, he is enthusiastic while teaching and makes classes so fun. His classrooms are always full, so you can imagine students not being bored at all. Brian on the other hand is careful, yet well-paced, is meticulous and very friendly. If you want more curated response to your questions you may find Brian really helpful. I believe one would be lucky to have either of them teaching this course. Honestly they should receive an A+ for their teaching and resourcefulness.

comment_workload:
I spent nearly 50% of my efforts and study time on this course. As I mentioned, they expect you to know the minutest of details, be it pointers, const expressions or pointers to const expressions, you need to be really quick witted and regular to make sure you know everything. PAs are not easy either, they make you think A LOT, and a lot of planning has to be made before you write your solutions (or it could be just me being a slow student ;)). Lecture slides won't be enough as you'll need to be creative in every way possible thinking of any scenarios out of the ordinary to perform well.

PAs would be released 2 weeks before deadline, and lab assignments are weekly, requiring attendance. The "practical" parts would give you a good run for the scores, so don't take them lightly. And you will need A LOT of exposure to past papers and discussions to decently prepare for midterms and finals if you are a below avg student like me ;)

comment_content:
1. Review about class in COMP2011. New features in C++11, const-ness.
OOP:
2. Constructor and Destructor, including conversion, copy, and other constructor.
3. Inheritance Polymorphism and Dynamic Binding (You won't forget it !!)
4. Generic programming, Operator overloading, and Friend declaration.
Data Structure:
1. Binary Tree, BST, Hashtable. 除此之外没啥新东西。
Others:
Rvalue reference and move semantics.

很好地锻炼了一个没有什么编程基础的人的抗压能力，心态调整能力，团结协作能力，和代码熟练度。C++的特性使得debug变得更加磨砺心性。同时也能让你初步认识到自己是否拥有成为一名软件工程师的基本素质。比如，当客户给你同事一个不明不白的需求和指示，而恰好你的同事在项目进行一半的时候提桶跑路。此时你的任务就是帮同事的项目擦屁股。你是否有能力完成这个项目并且想到所有bug可能出现的测试情况呢？以上这些大抵就是这门课培养学生的核心素养能力吧。

comment_workload:
A heavy workload can arise from a combination of factors that impact individuals and teams in various settings. One primary reason is increased demand for products or services, which can lead to more tasks than usual due to seasonal trends, new projects, or expanding markets. Additionally, staff shortages often exacerbate the situation; when there are not enough employees to manage the workload, remaining staff may be required to take on additional responsibilities, resulting in heightened pressure and stress. Inefficient processes also play a significant role; outdated technologies or poorly designed workflows can create bottlenecks, leading to longer task durations and a backlog of work.

Moreover, the complexity of certain projects can contribute to a heavy workload. Tasks that involve technical challenges, regulatory requirements, or the need for collaboration among multiple teams often require more time and effort to complete. Poor time management can further complicate matters, as individuals may struggle with prioritizing tasks or managing their time effectively, leading to a buildup of work and missed deadlines. Frequent interruptions from meetings, emails, or other distractions can disrupt focus and extend the time needed to complete tasks. Unrealistic deadlines set without considering available resources can leave employees feeling rushed and overwhelmed, while a lack of support, such as insufficient training or resources, can leave them feeling unprepared to handle their responsibilities.

In summary, a heavy workload can significantly affect employee morale, productivity, and overall job satisfaction. Identifying the underlying causes is crucial for organizations to implement strategies that alleviate pressure and promote a more balanced and effective work environment.
comment_grading: Despite achieving a score above the mean, the grade received is a C+, which indicates a disconnect between performance and evaluation standards. This situation can be frustrating, as it suggests that while the individual's performance is better than average, it does not meet the higher benchmarks set for a more favorable grade. Factors contributing to this outcome may include the grading criteria being overly stringent, the distribution of scores among peers skewing the average, or the grading system itself not adequately reflecting the level of effort and understanding demonstrated. Such a result can lead to concerns about fairness and the effectiveness of the assessment methods used, prompting a reevaluation of how grades are assigned in relation to actual performance.

comment_teaching:
Why is Desmond the best instructor that I have ever learned from in HKUST?

In terms of technical efforts, It’s clear he has tried to make the tragic idea of sitting through or watching lectures as smooth as possible. Crisp and clear sounding mic? Done. Punctual lecture recording uploads? Done. Lecture recordings with mic connected to the audio? Done. Lecture recordings all compiled in a website? Done. Lecture recordings all compiled in a website plus progress of lecture slides (e.g. topic XYZ p.23-48) next to each video? Done. What more can I ask for? I have not once waited for him to upload his lecture videos if I missed class. And for revision, if I forgot some topic, I didn’t have to spend an hour opening fifteen tabs of zoom recordings to figure out which video it was.

Desmond is unparalleled, compared to most instructors. The others simply don’t pay attention to minute details like this that make it easier for students to consume their knowledge.

His teaching is also powerful. Each sentence he speaks is so dynamic and meaningful; he could literally become a voice actor. There are some professors who speak like a robot for half the lecture to explain a topic, and then suddenly express a slight hint of their quasi-personality to show that the topic they’ve taught is indeed, remarkable or interesting. Desmond doesn’t speak like a robot at all. He teaches with the curiosity of students in mind and in a way, he is sort of performing when he does so.

Aside from his oral delivery, his ideas are very intuitive. He is not reading lecture notes most of the time but instead giving these very simplified and dumbed-down programs. Surprisingly, his programs cover most of the lecture notes. He has consistently (and jokingly) thrown shade on some of Professor Mak’s complicated example programs that reside in the lecture notes, which I live for.

When Desmond teaches, he has shown immense empathy to the students. For one, I think he knows that people lose focus normally. Every time I lost focus in class, Desmond would literally repeat his latest sentence, word for word, as if he could sense that I was zoning out to another dimension. On a serious note, I think if I had some attention deficit, this class wouldn’t be that bad. This obviously may have increased the time of the lectures but I don’t think Desmond cares. I mean, it is clear that he isn’t there because he is a professor and needs to finish the syllabus before the end of the semester because it is his job. He is there because he wants to teach. Desmond also knows that students struggle and acknowledged the difficulty of the course on multiple occasions. He has made it so clear that help is found whenever needed and this made it much easier to complete this course.  

Desmond also has a website where you can see his weekly schedule. If you can’t make it to his office hours, and you missed TA’s office hours, you can email him to perhaps book an appointment and your name will be on the schedule. Like what? This guy is just so detailed.

Overall, I think Desmond is an excellent teacher and he knows that. I hope he never stops teaching.

comment_content:
Asides from teaching you many useful C++ features, I think a big focus is on language concepts and how to explain various C++ standards e.g. why shouldn’t you declare a pure virtual destructor without implementation in a class hierarchy?, why can’t you use a rvalue reference to initialize another rvalue reference ? etc.

Of cause as the course title suggests, they will also teach you some useful data Structures like BST & hash table and you will have plenty opportunities to practice applying those data structures in PA and labs.

You’ll find out in other courses many more application of those data structures like hash table can be used to improve run time of sorting. Overall the content is very important and interesting.

Exams are quite competitive, in addition to assessing your mastery on course material and problem solving skills, they also test your stamina ie. Can you code for long hour even when you’re tired? exam is ~3 hrs you know.

Also in exam time allocation is key, I got low mark in final because I spent too much time challenging the hard question and rushing through easy questions, this resulted in tons of careless mistakes. So allocate your time more rationally.

Speaking of the final exam, in the last question (AVL), If you have very good concept of pointers (COMP2011 topic) I think you can solve the problem without using any AVL technique which is out syllabus. So make sure you do through review on pointer and how they may be creatively used.
comment_teaching:
Very good teaching from Desmond, he explained complicated topics in a way you can understand e.g. when he taught move semantics, instead of following the slide he gave us the big picture (resource stealing) first, he also illustrated using actual examples.

Also his passion in teaching is highly contagious, you'll suddenly be very motivated too when you attend his lecture lol

TA team is also very good, especially Dinh Anh Dung (the lead TA i guess) he answer questions on Piazza very fast and concise. I also like the 2 PAs he designed which is PA2 tournament matching and PA3 tic tac toe with minimax, at least for me they're really fun.

In PA3, interestingly the TA made an important logical mistake in the hash table optimization for alpha-beta pruning that made the algorithm incorrect,  however the mistake is ... super tricky and technical, so (unsurprisingly) no one (a whole class of few hundred people!) found out until the TA later found out himself lol. But that error is really just an exception, they don't make mistake 99% of the time. Still when you're doing assignment, you should think more critically and actually understand the purpose of the code and the logic behind it. I too thought I'd throughly understood the code, but I still couldn't spot that huge mistake, so I actually didn't throughly understand the code ... lol

There're around 700 questions on Piazza and only 13 unanswered. So the teaching team did a great job.
comment_workload:
5 mins lab attendance each week and  3 PAs. On average, you can finish labs within 1 hour PA within a day, but I always spent additional time to triple check and make sure there's no mistake.

Also do more past papers to train intuition on exam questions, this's key to answer questions very fast in exam. Note that intuition != logical thinking and there're good videos on Youtube explaining how to take advantage intuition in competitive exams. But over reliance on intuition may backfire as it's error prone.

comment_content:
Based on my understanding, the structure of CPP can categorize into more than six parts. Type, Function, Objects, Inheritance, Operator overloading, Template(STL), etc. Each abstracts the language features into a fundamental concept (not necessary) for all Object Oriented languages. In COMP 2011, you are supposed to have a solid understanding of Type, Function, and Struct. In Comp 2012, you will learn the rest of them.

Some syntax
int array[5];
int const* const* p = new int*;
int (*arrPtr) [5] = &array;
int (&arrRef) [5] = array;
int* (&ptrsRef) [10] = ptrs;
int (*fptr) (int, double) = function;
int (*const fpArr[]) (int, int) = {f1, f2, f3};
int (*fptr) (int) = [] (int x) -> int {};
int (* func(int))[10];
comment_teaching: Suppose you are interested in the CPP language itself. Then, I strongly recommend the book "C++ Primer," which elaborates on more features and detailed explanations. After this book, Effective CPP by Scott Meyers should be your best choice, which gives you suggestions on how to write clear and compelling codes. Finally, if I remember correctly, there is also an exprimental course in UST for those interested in Compiler, Comp 4901N Principles of Programming Languages. Sadly I haven't taken this course yet. So I don't know the details.

comment_teaching: Desmond focuses on the logic of each piece of knowledge(in his word, xxx is sth beautiful) and likes to repeat some important points(sometimes in English, Putonghua and Cantonese) and demo important procedures like ("Can we go into the body now? No, we have to first initialize all its data member!",  bst node delete bst tree, node, tree, node,tree....) This teaching style really facilitates my understanding of new stuff. Honestly speaking, although the content in comp2012 is a bit less harder than the pointer part in 2011 when you first learn it, the overall difficulties is much harder than 2011. Although some students with good foundations and talent may find that the speed a little bit low, I first think that if desmond spent less time in saying sth like " do you want to learn sth new please say yes!", we can speed up our teaching progress and there will be no addition class( I don't really hate addition class but when it is conducted on Fri evening or the day when you have a whole day class it really breaks your mood.), when the difficulties of the content is increasing, it acts as a buffer for you to recall and catch up. The power of brainwashing is so strong and I believe everyone who takes his class will remember some typical lines.Another feature of his class is that he encourages us to response and ask questions in class and it is really beneficial to learn from classmates' questions. Maybe for some students they don't like this kind of hyperbole, but for students who get below mean in 1021 this teaching style really enables us to have a better understanding of new content and builds a good foundation.

comment_teaching:

comment_workload:
OK for a 4 credit course.

Labs do not take very long to complete and is not too difficult, often its just completing a simple class definition.

Assignments are also manageable, however time needs to be spent debugging and making sure you are ready for all of the test cases. In my opinion, 2012 has easier assignments than 2011, since what you need to do is more clear in this course.

Exams require you to be very clear on the concepts related to the course, and doing the past papers is extremely important since the questions in the exam are closely related down to the way they are asked. comment_grading:
3 Programming assignments (10% each): 3 assignments, each about a different area, (for this semester: class, inheritance and BST), difficulty is acceptable other than assignment 2 where the TA made the assignment far too complex and had to spend about a week reworking the assignment to make it easier.

Laboratory exercises (10%): 1 lab per week, finish the code and attend the session, you may be asked a question by the TAs regarding the lab, the questions are mostly easy and TAs are lenient with the score, not difficult to get full marks here.

Midterm (20%): True or False questions and short answers, mostly about constructors and destructors. Doing the past papers here is key.

Final examination (40%): Same as the midterm, covers the entire course equally, again doing the past papers is key.

I got slightly above mean in the midterm, mean in finals, attended all labs and got around 95% in all 3 assignments, ended up with a B, decent grading.

comment_content:
Continuation of COMP2011
1. Intro, review, const-ness, lambda
A brief review of what have been learnt from 2011, introduction to lambda expression.

2. Constructors and Destructor
OOP concept. It is very important to distinguish different kinds of constructors and their use-cases. Like: why a conversion constructor only take one parameter? Ans: for implicit conversion when we are doing something like: Word a = "abcd"
This topic plays an important role in the next topic when discussing Inheritance (like order of constructions), do remember to do more practice and understand each constructor thoroughly

3. Inheritance and Polymorphism
OOP concept again.

4. Generic Programming
The power of template for code re-using. Do pay attention to the syntax.
Operator overloading is a very important part of this course. Make sure you know how to write the function declaration of each operator (like << , [ ] requires two function signature...)

5. STL
Application Programming Interface (API) for extremely useful data structure (vector, stack, queue, map...) You don't have to memorize all the things, but better get more practice.

6. Hashing
More theoretical. Something you will see again in COMP3711. Very basic and there is basically no Math behind this chapter. (They just teach you the method)

7. Binary Search Tree
Another data-structure.

8. Rvalue reference and Move Semantics
Better use of resource: temporary objects, move constructor, move assignment operator
comment_teaching:
It is the first time Cecia teaches this course. Probably because Desmond won't teach 2012 next year.

Since my COMP2011 was taught by Cecia, I get used to her pace and style. I would say Cecia is a great instructor in this course also (Besides Desmond :D).

I don't know how do people learn programming, but for me it starts from the basic: make sure you understand every line of code, what is the meaning/usage of such design, what if I change something, will the program still works? (You have to be "naughty" and use your imagination to change the codes and give an explanation of the new result. 中文講就係舉一反三)

Therefore, Cecia should be the one you look for if you want your basics to be stronger. Usually she will give a more simpler example compared to the lecture note when starting a new topic. And there will be some "tricky" polling questions that allows you to understand more about the mechanism.

Since it is her first year teaching COMP2012, her pace is a little bit slower than expected. Resulting in rushing the last few topics, but I think it is still ok to handle given you have solid understanding in all the topics before.

I don't have any experiences on programming besides the intro course. But from COMP2011 to 2012, two A+s, the only thing I can do now is a big appreciation on her ;)
comment_workload:
The workload of this course mainly comes from PA and lab that requires you to catch up with course material.
PA1, 3 are easier but PA 2 and 4 are much difficult. Especially PA 4 costs me around 2-3 days to finish.

The labs are quite boring. In Comp2011 we usually have some logic demanding labs that requires like 2-3 hours to finish. However the lab this semester is too easy. Maybe you change 2-3 lines of code, or copying other functions to finish the tasks. Usually it takes 15-60mins for each lab. Sometimes you don't have to understand anything new to finish the lab.

The way I learn 2011/2012 is develop reasonable explanations for all the phenomenon. (Not applicable for everyone, just if you are interested.)
E.g. It is very hard to memorize the order of construction/destruction
Then you can think of building a house. You have to gather all the components before you can really have a house. If your house requires a component from other class (own other class object), then ask someone to build the component for you (class constructor); If your house is base on another house (inherited), then ask other to build the base for you first (base class constructor), then you can stack the components one by one and finally create a house.

By some "stories" or "analogy", try to come up with a reasonable mechanism for all the things in this course. Just like building a house, your base determines how tall you can build.

comment_grading:
Originally there are mid-term exam and final exam.

Due to the sudden change to online learning, the final exam is removed, and the score distribution is changed to an extra assignment and more weighting of previous stuffs. That's what the nightmare happens (specifically for this semester):

The midterm exam was not giving enough time for students to finish in normal writing speed. (If you think you have finished half of the pages in 45% of time. you are not suppose to finish it on time before the exam ends), so most students complained that the exam cannot even be finished, this caused the grade less dependent on the course expected outcomes, but rather whether you realised that you need to write extremely fast from the start of the exam. Everything sucked. Many students had reflected this situation to Desmond, and he promised to set the questions better in the final exam. However, fate gave no chance for everyone. There was no exam, meaning no hope for rescue, since basically people can get close to full marks in homeworks and labs, so it became the so-called 一試定生死 (a single exam deciding your fate) and the worse is that this was unexpected. That made you suddenly lose all your passion into the course since you knew it is of no return, and your grade was going to be rotten. You knew this is destiny, and you knew this is unfortune. That's all.

comment_content:
(The midterm wasn't conducted in this sem due to online teaching mode, so this is my assumption of the topics covered before and after the midterm in normal teaching schedule)   
Before mid-term:
0. Revision of COMP 2011 (const-ness, dynamic array, linked list & C++ Class Basic): since this course is the continuation of COMP 2011, lecturers will review on the topics covered after the mid-term of COMP 2011 to ensure everyone is ready for what is coming.  

1. Constructor & Destructor: You have seen them for the first time in COMP 2011, but they actually take several forms. You are going to understand how these forms of Ctor and Dtor mix up in the object construction and destruction.

2. Inheritance and Polymorphism: The lecturers from now on will groan about code re-use and introduce these two major OOP topic in order to clear way such frustration, they are also reflected in reality too!

3. Generic Programming: I have mentioned the hatred of the lecturers against code re-use. With templates, we have virtually no other means of duplicating similar-looking codes. If you don't think certain operators (+,-, +=, <<) work well for user-defined data types, you can even re-define them by operator overloading.

After mid-term:
4. Hashing: the mechanism behind searching. Without coding, this is the easiest data structure to learn.

5. Binary search tree (BST): The pain of recursion curses my body once more when implementing this data structure. You are also going to learn the three ways of traversing the tree, it's great that it couldn't be directly examined in the online exam!

6. AVL: Just BST plus balancing, so make sure you understand BST beforehand.

7. rvalue Reference and Move Semantics: the story of temporary objects attempting to extend their lifespan with rvalue reference but end up being abused by move semantic. This final topic can possibly "move" down your grades if being taken lightly of, be cautious!

comment_teaching:
Ah the teaching. As I always said: 厲害了，我的龍.

I have attended all his lectures except for one. And I would say that, his teaching is actually not that bad. His accent is ... Interesting, but I have no problem understanding. He sometimes give us the motivation behind something, like for operator overloading, we want the operation to be closed (return value be the same type), and he goes off to say something about group, rings and fields, etc. Definitely not useful for exam, but interesting.

Speaking about usefulness in exam, oh my god there is not much. Often he goes over the important topics so fast that you couldn't see his taillight. Inheritance is done in ONE lecture. You read that right, ONE, as in less than two, so that there is more time for him to talk about the extra stuff. The thing is, he will go on to say that something is not important, but then that something appeared in the exam! I guess you can see that the exam papers are prepared mostly by Desmond, and not Long. For example, he said that private and protected inheritance is not important, so he didn't go over it. Then in the exam, it appeared. Surprise! Long said that he looked over the paper to make sure that only the content taught by both lecturers will appear on the exam, but the word 'TAUGHT' is used very loosely, as some topics are technically 'taught', but even as a person who attended almost all of his lectures, I don't remember it (E.g.: const-ness). When I asked him, he said: It was in one of the page in my slides! 厲害了，我的龍. He often talked about the concept, and rarely the code, which is basically what the whole exam is about.

By the way, around midterm, only 20 people are present. Near the finals, 5. 5/around 90 people attended his lectures. I guess the rest watched or attended Desmond's lectures (the smart choice). Keep in mind though, this is a 9am lecture. But I couldn't help but imagining the face of him when he invigilate the final exam (for the students from his lecture) and saw that holy shit, there were 90 people registered in his session.

What I would suggest is, to just go with Desmond's lecture or videos. If you are really stubborn like me and stuck with Long's lectures, do the past papers. It will save your ass in the midterm and final. It was only when I was attempting the past papers do I realize how screwed I am, and how 厲害 my 龍 is.

I guess one of the problems of this course is that, questions in exam are often stuff that Desmond have mentioned in his lecture, but not in Professor Quan's.

The labs could be time-consuming, so at least read the site before coming. Not much to say.

comment_content:
This course covers the major material present in both COMP2011 and COMP2012 (and slightly beyond). The breadth of these topics is wide but lies heavily on C++.

A list of covered topics is as follows:
- Introduction to computer programming
- Fundamentals of C++
- Flow control
- Arrays
- Functions, scope, and recursion
- File I/O
- Pointers
- Structs
- Classes and Objects
- Inheritance and polymorphism
- Generic programming
- Standard Template Library (STL)
- Static data members and member functions
- Linked lists
- Stack and queue
- Binary search tree
- AVL tree
- Hashing

The topics closest to COMP2011 are up to 'Classes and Objects' and were covered by Week 7, whilst the topics closes to COMP2012 are up to the 'Binary search tree' are covered by Week 12. The final Week 13 covers the remaining two topics.

By taking this course, you are expected to already have a strong programming foundation. The course restricts enrollment to those who received an A grade or above in COMP1021, but attainment of this is in no way indicative of one's ability to succeed in COMP2012H.

For this reason, if you enjoy programming and have a passion for computer science, this is a course that will further stimulate and grow this passion.

The key topics to watch out for in this course are:
- Fundamentals of C++ (and all of its quirks)
- Classes and Objects (+ Structs)
- Generic Programming
- Standard Template Library (+ Stack/Queue)
- Linked Lists
- Binary Search Tree

Such topics are heavily emphasized in the Mid-term and Final examinations, so it is important that you have a thorough understanding of them.

Reflecting further on what I've learnt through the course, I feel confident saying that I've gained a decent understanding of C++, object-oriented programming, and the key data structures.

The programming assignments and labs that you are required to do are very well thought out. It is clear that the teaching team have put their time into delivering tasks that both teach you plenty and challenge you.

Programming assignments and labs aside, the examinations are still a key part of the course. They are difficult. Whilst there are some common 'template' questions that you will see in all the sample papers, they'll only make up about 15% of the exam. The remaining questions are, admittedly very well crafted, long form questions that require deep algorithmic thinking and application of knowledge.

Those who have a tendency to cram the content near the exams may find that real practice, repetition, and use of the concepts is required to excel.

In all, the content is deep, but a lot will be learnt.
comment_teaching:
It seems a lot of other reviews were incredibly critical of the teaching in this course. In honesty, I'd probably have to disagree with some of the things said. I find it hard to hold a negative position of any of the teaching that went on.

When looking at the teaching, we obviously have the professor, but we also have the rest of the teaching team. Given their presence in the course, it would be a disservice to not include them in an evaluation of the teaching.

I had Professor Quan. I'd start out by saying that he's a pretty good teacher when it comes to the bigger picture or more abstract understanding of the topics. His English does have a noticeable Beijing taint to it, but his time overseas has certainly allowed him to gain a perfectly acceptable command of the language. There is a certain passion that Quan seems to have when teaching, but it does pain me to see 90% of students attend lectures just to work on their laptops.

A lot of other reviews have noted that what is taught in the class doesn't necessarily reflect what would be expected in the examination. The reason for this being that the professor does not write the exams or programming assignments, which is left up to the teaching team. This aspect is undoubtedly true, but I don't believe it to be a problem that is easily remedied, or a problem at all.

Despite the ~4 hours of lectures per week, it would be impossible for the professor to go deep into every specific topic due to the need to cover each of the 17 key topics. Instead, it seems the lectures are positioned to introduce the intuition and concepts behind each topic, to which you'll need to go off and explore them yourself.

This is where the rest of the teaching team comes in. They produce excellent Labs and Programming Assignments which allow you to identify areas you may lack practical understanding of before the exams.

As mentioned, the teaching team also write the midterm and final exams. It should be noted that the questions on the exams will (should) only be based on the content of the slides. There may be times where an exam question is based on a slide that was only briefly covered, but there were no instances I found where topics that weren't mentioned on the slides were tested.

On the topic of the slides however, they are pretty terrible. I would not be surprised if I heard they were produced two decades ago. These slides cover the topics, but they are a pain to look at with that repulsive purple gradient. The writing is poor and the code is not formatted. If there is one thing Quan should change immediately, it is those god-awful slides.

A remedy to this would be to refer to slides in COMP2011 and COMP2012, or to even reach out to previous professors of the course. I never ended up doing so, but, had I do this again, I would not pain myself with Quan's slides.

Given the terrible teaching that is seen in a lot of other courses at this university, it is very difficult to say that COMP2012H is poor when it comes to teaching. You will be taught the content, but you must ensure that it is sufficiently supplemented with your own self learning.
comment_workload:
It is of absolute importance that you understand what you're getting yourself into when you enroll into the course. Around 8 credits worth of material will be covered in this 5 credit course and it is recommended (by the course) that you spend around 15-20 hours per week on it. If COMP1021 was your first exposure to computer science, save yourself the hassle and do not enroll for this course. The pain of learning programming for the first time in this accelerated programme is certainly not worth it.

As far as the workload goes though, the course had 4 programming assignments and 8 labs (7 graded) to complete. The amount of time you need to spend on these depends on what you aim to get out of completing them.

If you are strictly out to get full marks on the programming assignments and labs, doing the bare minimum (as I had unfortunately), it should take around 3-6 hours to complete a programming assignment and 1-2 hours to complete a lab.

On the other hand, if you intend to design a well crated solution that goes well above what is technically required. You can expect to spend days working through the programming assignments and around half a day on a lab.

The programming assignments are very well guided and there is no ambiguity when it comes to what exactly is expected of you. Every function/method you are to implement is explicitly stated, and the test cases for scoring are solely based on them.

In addition, studying for the exams can take a lot of time if you expect to review topic by topic. There is a lot of content to cover, so if you rely on rote learning, expect to spend an unreasonable amount of time on this course. Otherwise, it is best to gain a good holistic understanding of the topics and 're-derive' concepts as needed in the exams.
comment_grading:
If there is one thing that is undoubtedly good about the course, it is the grading. There is no other course where you can score just around the mean and still be within reach of an A-range final grade.

When you take this course, it is best to assume that everybody else will be getting full marks on their programming assignments and labs. It is, for this reason, important that you ensure you perform adequately in the exams.

In both the midterm and final in the Fall 2025-2026 exams, the average mark sat right around 40%. Naturally, there are some people who are just absolutely dialed in during the exams attaining the upper 90s. But it should be understood that performing near the mean in this course is not a disappointment.

What I believed to be terrible scores on first viewing after receiving the dreaded 'grade updated' Canvas notification, turned out to be scores that still left me in the A-range for this course. You will be competing with very competent people, and the grading completely reflects that.

It is very refreshing to see that COMP2012H does not see its students as cattle satisfying a normal distribution. The university's capped A-range quota is a nonsensical grade-inflation remedy which I've never understood. To think that students are reduced to a bell curve is disheartening, it is a true killer of wonder and passion. Thank god such rules do not apply to honors courses.

comment_content: The content in the course is substantial. It covers basic programming constructs like loops, conditionals and variables in C++. Most of this is covered in the first two weeks, then functions specifically, pass-by-value and pass-by-reference is done. This topic almost always comes in midterms. The next major topic is pointers. It includes function pointers, dereferencing, pointer operations and array-pointer duality. The last part covered before midterms was classes. The content covered before midterms was relatively simplistic, however, the time to develop proficiency in those topics was less than sufficient. After midterms, we delved deeper into object-oriented programming covering the three main OOP concepts: encapsulation, inheritance and polymorphism. And apart from OOP, we did data structures and algorithms like Stacks, Queues, Priority Queues, Heaps, Binary Trees, AVL Trees and Graphs. Note that this year was the first year where graphs were introduced.
comment_teaching: I didn't attend many lectures, but Quan Long taught considerably well, however, obviously he could not have covered the entire content within the scope of the class with just a 1 hour 50 minute lecture session. He is also good at remembering faces.
comment_workload: I found the PAs to be quite difficult. Most people used AI or collaborated for their PAs, however, I did not do so. Apart from that, the content was a lot especially in the given time constraint. That said, one recommendation would be to use a variety of books to study since the slides are not enough.

comment_content: Focuses on introducing students to the fundamental concepts of object-oriented programming and data structures. It aims to provide a solid foundation in these areas, which are essential for building software applications and solving complex programming problems. The course may cover topics such as classes, objects, inheritance, polymorphism, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, and algorithms for searching and sorting.
comment_teaching: Depending on the instructor. However, common teaching approaches may include lectures, in-class demonstrations, code walkthroughs, and hands-on programming exercises. Some instructors may also provide additional resources such as online tutorials, textbooks, or supplementary materials to support learning.
comment_workload: It is expected to be a moderately demanding course, given the complexity of the subject matter. Students are likely to spend time both inside and outside of class working on programming assignments, studying the course materials, and practicing coding exercises. It's important to manage your time effectively and allocate sufficient hours to understand the concepts and complete assignments.
comment_grading: Depending on the specific instructor and course requirements. Typically, grading is based on a combination of assignments, quizzes or exams, projects, and class participation. It's important to review the course syllabus or consult with the instructor for detailed information about the grading scheme.

comment_content:
The lecture full course menu:
1. Course introduction
2. C++ constants, variables and operators
3A. Program flow control
3B. Integrated problem solving: Animal counting
4. Function call and parameter passing, and function overloading
5. Array
6. Recursion
7. struct
8. Pointer to object
9. Linked list
10. C++ class
11. Object constructor and construction
12. Inheritance and examples
13. Generic Programming
14. Standard Template Library (STL)
14B. STL examples
15. Stack and Queue
16. Trees, binary trees and search trees
16B. BST and AVL tree
17. Hashing
17B. Hash performance

Of course these are only the go-to topics, and there are a lot of stuff that goes between, like "order of construction and destruction", or "rvalue reference and move semantics". So as you can imagine, the course content is dense with little room to breathe (for beginners). That said, the "H" in the course code meant that the course has a certain expectation.

Also, did you know that you can just log in to https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2012h with a valid CSD account to see for yourself, even if you aren't enrolled? I used to find that amusing.

Anyway, if you could write *correct* C and debug by yourself, and have the patience to read through all 100+ answers on a Stack Overflow question to sieve for the gold, chances are that maybe the course materials wouldn't be that alien after all. Maybe it would make sense. Maybe you would even find enlightenment in learning how things actually work.
comment_teaching:
Honestly, I didn't really go to lectures that much, because I already knew most of the stuff written on the lecture notes, thanks to Stack Overflow. But Gary's teaching is definitely not bad. I didn't feel that bored listening to his lecture despite having already known most of stuff, so there's that.

During breaks, a lot of students came up to ask him questions, and he seemed passionate to answer, and particularly didn't mind indulging himself into confusing warnings and errors thrown by the compiler.
comment_workload:
This is a course that worths 5 credits, so go figure. The weekly assignments are a massive burden: even if I know how to do it, it still takes a lot of time for me to debug and sanity check. Plus, no one writes completely correct code straight from the start. The tasks are naturally time-consuming.

But what is even more burdening are the people that finish the assignment within the starting day and actively ask the lecturer for more difficult work, in turn for extra bonus. I guess this course not only takes a toll on your free time, but also on your mental wellbeing.
comment_grading: Grading is different than COMP2011 and COMP2012, so it's difficult to judge. The graded test cases are all hidden and the visible ones do not count towards the score, so even though the all weekly assignments weighted 65%, it still takes effort and is not very possible to get full marks (unless you exhaust all input possibilities or prove your code is correct with math, idk). The final weighted 35% and imo is also hella difficult. Bonus might provide "fine grade adjustments" but I never know what it meant. My guess is you will probably get A range if you hit mean or above.

comment_teaching:
Gary's teaching is quite good (although I skipped quite a few lectures, and mostly did PA in the ones I attended). He likes to respond to student questions on Piazza during lessons, which helps everyone learn. He is also not afraid to talk about more topics in depth, which is a plus. Also, he is quite approachable both before and after class, so ask questions if you have any.

The TA team in general is quite good as well. Although some of the assignment descriptions or the sample program may have issues, they responded to them promptly. They are also quick to reply emails (especially when you need to appeal PA multiple times due to compilation errors) and are active on Piazza.

Overall, the entire teaching team is enthusiastic in teaching and answering any queries you might have.

Note: Lab sections are taught by TAs to cover additional topics, unlike COMP 2011/2012's labs which take attendance. The attendance rate of labs are quite low throughout this semester as a result.
comment_workload:
This course is famous for its workload, and it's not difficult to see why. Weekly programming assignments are challenging to complete (although there are 2.5 weeks per assignment, the assignments are released weekly, and you really do not want to be behind schedule for them). Not to mention the bonus that you might want to work on.

Ask yourself these questions before taking this course:
Is it necessary for you/ interesting for you, or are you simply here for the grades? Note that you can take COMP 2011 in Year 1 Spring and COMP 2012 in Year 2 Fall to satisfy the same requirements at the same time for COMP/CPEG/COSC (and if you can do well in this course, you can likely do well enough to get A ranges in both COMP 2011 and COMP 2012). The grading you get here might be lenient, but not as good as 8 credits of As.

Do you have prior programming experience (preferably in an OOP language)? It is almost mandatory for you to have some, as COMP 1021 is definitely not sufficient for you to survive in this course (unless you are a genius).

Are you motivated enough to work *extra* hard throughout the semester? The workload is not trivial and you are advised to do every programming assignment when it's released. Do not deadline fight these or you will definitely be in trouble. Also, consider taking a reduced credit load (<= 15 credits). I took 18 credits this semester and the workload is immense. Taking COMP 2011 and COMP 2012 means that you can have more time to strengthen your foundations and have a better experience overall.

I'm not trying to dissuade you from trying this course - quite the contrary. If you are still interested after reading that, come and take it! It will be an unforgettable experience. Odds are, you'll pickup something new no matter what your skill level is.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme:
65% Best 10 out of 11 PAs
35% Final Exam

The main difference with Desmond's COMP 2012H is a conspicuous lack of labs, mid-terms, or the collaborative project. Don't worry - the weekly programming assignments are quite enough to ensure that you have quite a lot of hands-on programming experience.

List of PAs: (PAs with Bonus indicated with *)
PA 1* - Calendar (Basic I/O, arrays, and control flow)
PA 2 - Text Processor (C-style strings and arrays)
PA 3* - Nonogram (Recursion)
PA 4* - Integer Factorization (Nominally speed programming, but became a algorithm-finding competition)
PA 5* - Deque with Sentinel (Structs, pointers and dynamic memory)
PA 6* - "Mini" Database (Linked list hell)
PA 7* - Wordle with Qt (GUI framework Qt)
PA 8 - "Lord of the Rings" Turn-based Game (Inheritance and Polymorphism)
PA 9* - Skiplist and "Object" for C++ (Pointer hell, dynamic binding, operator overloading and friend)
PA 10 - Data Preprocessing for AI (STL)
PA 11* - Dictionary with Trie (Abstract Data Structures)

PAs 3, 6, and 9 are notable for their difficulty (especially PA 9).

Bonuses are either based on speed (PA 3, 4), correctness and conciseness (PA 5, 11), or extra features (PA 1, 6, 7, 9).

Note that 3 penalty-free late days total across all assignments are given to all students.

According to Gary, bonuses may improve your grade if you have a sufficient amount. However, take into account that bonus might be difficult or challenging to do, and you might not want extra workload during the semester.

The final exam was quite astonishing in its length and difficulty, which can "allow students to shine" (Gary, 2022). I doubt more than 10 students finished the paper in the tight time limit.

Update: The teaching team added 10 marks per person for the final exam for "effort", but still no one had a score above 100. You can see how difficult the final is.

2nd Update: Grading seems to be pretty... unusual. Mean is B while UQ is A+, which makes me wonder how the grades in the middle are given.

Note that the mean is ~6 total marks below median, similar to the gap from median to UQ.

It has been confirmed that around 50% of the class got an A range.

comment_content:
Horror's OOP and Data Structures

This semester's COMP2012H is quite special. Traditionally, 2012H always consisted of lots of labs, 4 PA's, a group project, and a final. However, this semester, there are 11 *weekly* full-fat PA's (yes, you read that right) and a final exam. Take that how you will.

To be honest, being forced to write so many programs eventually makes one quite comfortable with programming in C++.

Overall, the content is very useful and interesting and will become a solid bedrock foundation for future CS courses. It is worth it to learn the content well.

Content (copied from website):
- Introduction to computer programming
- Fundamentals of C++: data types, variables, operators
- Flow controls
- Arrays
- Functions, scope, and recursion
- File I/O
- Pointers
- lvalue references
- struct
- Class, object construction, iniitalization, and destruction
- Inheritance and polymorphism
- Generic programming
- Standard Template Library (STL)
- Static data members and member functions
- Some features in C++11
- rvalue references and move semantics
- Linked lists
- Stack and queue
- Binary search tree
- Hashing

Additionally, practical skills like separate compilation / Makefiles, speed programming and Qt (2012H only) will be taught in the labs.
comment_teaching:
Gary is a nice guy and his teaching is good. As for the pace, I honestly can't comment because it really depends your prior knowledge. It will be far too fast or slow for some people, and just right for the others.

In addition to teaching the standard course material, it seems he frequently reads Piazza and takes the first 15-20 minutes of each lecture to answer common questions from there. Overall, it's quite a nice experience.
comment_workload:
Quite intense.

Doing one full PA a week is already quite difficult. Additionally, if you wish to do the bonus tasks, you will often spend more time on them than the core tasks.

Despite this, I think the workload is smaller than Desmond's 2012H, but only by a small margin. It's still the insane workload you expect from this course.

If future iterations of 2012H are like this, my suggestion is to do the PAs as soon as they're released, which is 2.5 weeks before they are due. This way, you won't have to stress about them the day they are due, and gives you some leeway in case of unforseen events or if the PA is unexpectedly difficult. (I'm looking at you, PA9...)

Moreover, you have to look at it contextually... COMP2012H is usually taken by CS students in their Year 2 fall semester. In this semester, they will also take a linear algebra course, COMP2711(H), and LANG2030(H). These courses are also difficult for many students. Together with 2012H, it makes for an extremely high-workload semester.

comment_content:
This is a course that combines COMP 2011 and COMP 2012. Some topics such as C++11 features and move semantics are not examined.

For the final exam, I highly suggest to do the past paper to get familiar with the question types, since there are many tricky questions. (e.g. questions such as order of object constructions / destructions, and the algorithm used for AVL tree also confined to the one taught in class, etc...)

One important thing about the group project is to make good use of data structures and OOP techniques. It doesn't matter how good your program look, how fancy your GUI is. The most important thing is to write good codes (codes that are reusable and sustainable). Our group focused too much on making the GUI look good, but neglected the use of data structures, which got us a below mean in the project.

comment_workload:
Perhaps the heaviest in UST. 4 PAs, 1 group project, 1 final exam. (No mid-term due to online teaching)

PA1: minesweeper
This is the trickiest PA. I spent over 1 week to debug my program, even then I still couldn't get full mark. There are many hidden test cases which will fuck up your program. However, this PA is a good training to your intuition in data structures and algorithms. (You were taught an algorithm for solving the minesweeper game, and you need to translate the algorithm into C++ language).

PA2: proof assistant
Even today I still don't understand what the fuck is happening in this assignment. Basically you need to write a program that prove logical expressions (the one you learn in COMP2711). It uses some abstract data structures to manage the input/output. I am very bad at math and just follow the instructions and somehow I was able to finish this assignment.

PA3: a mini turn based game
This one is quite straight forward, you just make use of OOP techniques and write a mini game. The test cases are pretty obvious and intuitive, therefore many people get full mark in this assignment.

PA4: GUI version of PA3
Self-learn Qt and then turn your PA3 into a GUI version. At first it was difficult (learning Qt from scratch), but after picking up the basics, you will finish it in no time. (I spent first 3-4 days looking at the documentations, got confused (finish 10%). When I realize what was happening, I spent 1-2 days to finish the whole program(finish remaining 90%))

The group project is about making a program with GUI (games or minitools or anything you like). Desmond said that the project is not competitive (everyone got decent grade). Depending on what you are trying to make, the project may take 3 days to 2 weeks+ to finish.

comment_content: This course is an accelerated and intensive course on concepts and techniques behind object-oriented programming (OOP) and data structures using an OOP language. It covers the major materials of COMP2011 and COMP2012, and its curriculum is designed for students with excellent programming background or substantial programming experience. Topics include: functions; pointers; abstract data types and their class implementation; static and dynamic construction and destruction of objects; data member and member functions; public interface and encapsulation; class hierarchies; polymorphism; inheritance and dynamic binding; standard template library; generic programming using templates; object-oriented view of data structures: linked lists, queues, stacks, trees, and their algorithms such as searching, sorting and hashing.

comment_content: Condensed, but covering most materials in COMP2011 & COMP2012, plus lots of extra knowledge on practical programming.
comment_teaching:
Desmond is a good teacher indeed. I could feel that he really wants to teach us something useful, and is willing to help us in learning.

Although this course covers materials of 2 courses, the teaching speed is actually moderate. Demond spends much time on reviewing previous lectures and teaching code examples. What makes it possible to cover all the materials is time (discussed in the workload section).

What I like most about Desmond is that he is always there to help. He would stay for 30-60 minutes after each lecture answering questions and always reply emails with most detailed explanation.
Besides, he sends quite a lot of emails to further elaborate topics in classes, usually with code examples. So you can definitely learn much more as you want to.
comment_workload:
It is reasonable to have heavy workload for a course covering materials of 2 4-credit courses, but the overall workload is definitely worth more than 5 credits.

We have 2 regular lectures and 1 labs every week, each lecture of 2 hours and labs mainly acting as Q&A sessions - sounds pretty acceptable. However, since we only have 8 lab tasks, almost all the rest labs are used to deliver lectures. Moreover, Desmond just loves his job TOO much and is always willing to offer additional classes. We've got 5 or 6 additional lectures this semester, each of THREE hours.

Apart from lectures, we also have 8 lab tasks - each costs you several hours, 4 PAs - each can take up 1-3 days and even more on debugging, and a group project - takes around 1 week if you do want to be good.

However, somehow, I still found the workload to be moderate because I really enjoyed the PAs and the project. Therefore, take this course only if you are really interested in programming, or you certainly get frustrated.
comment_grading:
I hesitated on this section but finally gave an A, because the grading is actually very fine, as promised by Desmond - I got an A- with a overall grade slightly below mean, which means that over half of the students are in A range.

What makes me hesitate is that people in this course are all experts with A or above grades in introductory programming courses, so everyone deserves a good grade. And the grading scheme is also kind of unsatisfactory. Almost everyone gets nearly full marks in PAs and labs, and the group project grades have a very small SD, but the final exam is quite difficult and super tight in time. Therefore, your overall grade almost merely depends on your final exam, although it only counts 36% of the total grade.

comment_content: This course covers the major materials of COMP2011 and COMP2012, and its curriculum is designed for students with excellent programming background or substantial programming experience. Topics include: functions; pointers; abstract data types and their class implementation; static and dynamic construction and destruction of objects; data member and member functions; public interface and encapsulation; class hierarchies; polymorphism; inheritance and dynamic binding; standard template library; generic programming using templates; object-oriented view of data structures: linked lists, queues, stacks, trees, and their algorithms such as searching, sorting and hashing.
comment_teaching:
Desmond is a very good teacher. He is always ready to help us learn more. In class, he demonstrates most of the concepts by coding them. After class, he often sends us emails to elaborate some topics discussed in class. You can always reach him or any of his TAs for any confusion you may have or anything you want to learn extra.

I would recommend taking this course because it encourages to learn more and the grading is generous (see GRADING section). So if you think that you can manage the workload, definitely give this a try.
comment_workload: The workload is kind of heavy. There are 4 PAs. Even if you have experience in programming, the PAs may take you 3-4 days. Besides, there are 8 labs, which are helpful in practicing the concepts you learn in class. Moreover, there is a group project. You may need form a group of 3-4 for it (you can work independently in some cases). Finally, there is a final exam. Due to pandemic, in my time there was no midterm. I'm not sure whether this is the case for other semesters.
comment_grading:
Each PA is worth 8% of the total marks.

Each lab is worth 1%. So, 8 labs are worth 8% in total.

Group project is worth 24% and the final exam is worth 36%.

The letter grades are given very generously. Throughout the course, Desmond emphasized that this course is for learning not for competition. And this was reflected in the grading. He gave more than 50% of the students an A  range grade!

comment_teaching:
​​Desmond老好人了，上课讲得很细，课后问答很有耐心，邮件秒回，另外还会经常群发邮件解释一些上课没讲到或不够清晰的点，这些邮件一定要看，很可能成为考试考点。两个TA也很负责，虽然TA的workload很大，但问他们问题都是会回复的。

comment_workload:

由于没讲算法，PA基本是模拟题，可能会有DFS或简单数据结构，题目冗长，编程细节非常多，对以前学过竞赛的同学可能也会比较麻烦。建议在做PA时练习写代码之前整体把控代码布局和细节，以增加代码的一次正确率，这能显著减少debug量，也对考试中手写代码的正确率很有帮助。

Of course, this is an honor course. Heavy workload is expected.

comment_teaching:
Come on guys, that's Desmond!!!! I don't think I need to say more! Most wonderful teacher I've ever met.

Two TAs are Bing Yen Chang, and Zhang Jingyang. They both have solid knowledge of C++ programming and are willing to help you out. Very nice guy!!
comment_workload:
Oh, that's really heavy. This is 5-credit course and everyone is excellent, so that they perhaps made everything harder.

Labs are relatively easier, but some of them still have long codes and you need to FULLY UNDERSTAND the requirement and know exactly what you need to do, which is really really really important!

Assignments include long codes and are really difficult.
PA1 is about using recursion to build and solve Minesweeper game. 0.5~1 weeks required.
PA2 is a mini-tool for prove(sth. in COMP2711) and this one is really difficult and tricky, as you'll encounter countless memory leakage, and it takes a long time for you to understand each instructions' meanings, as the description is not clear, you need to try on demo program yourself. 2~2.5 weeks required.
PA3 is a game, relatively easy, aiming at letting you get familiar with OOP. 0.5~1 weeks required.
PA4 is PA3's game with GUI. This one is difficult as well, as nothing about Qt is taught during class, so that you need to read documents and do google on your own. GUI is nothing like what you've done with C++ before, so you need to work hard. 1.5~2 weeks required.

For the group project, you need to submit a proposal first and it'll be assessed by teaching team, after which you'll receive feedback of your idea.
The program of group project is a hard time. Get prepared to spend days and night on programming and debugging it. After submitting your code, your group need to do a presentation and explain your usage of OOP/Datastructures and your game's features to Desmond, Bing Yen and Jingyang.

Final exam is difficult and the time is not enough. All you can do is fully understand what Desmond taught during class and understand all labs. Problems cover OOP, Data Structures, in lecture slides and labs. NO COMP2011 knowledge has been tested directly in final exam this time. (But there was before)

comment_teaching:
Desmond is an epic guy; he tries to be funny in class to keep you awake, and he will not be annoyed if you send him many emails to ask things. He is very passionate about teaching. He will even reply to emails on weekends. And he will prepare many supplementary notes on his website, which are very useful for revision. The last time I met someone this passionate about teaching was in secondary school.

He talks a bit slow, thought, so for the latter half of the semester I skipped his lessons and watched the videos at 2x speed, which is more efficient (for me), since I can't focus during the real time lessons anyway and would start playing with my phone or something. (sorry, Desmond!)

Definitely take this course just to know Desmond. He is the most epic professor you will ever see in UST. Even though taking both COMP2011 and COMP2012 would be better for your GPA (if you can get A in COMP2012H you can probably get A in 2011 and 12), I recommend taking this course just for the epic learning experience.

The TAs, Bingyen and Peter, are also epic. They will respond to your emails promptly.

Bingyen is very lenient with grade appeals. For my PA1 I originally got 0 marks because I implemented a very stupid loop which takes 3 minutes to complete and caused the grading system to timeout. Since technically it should work, Bingyen approved it and I got 14/20. Then, I completely rewrote part of my code to get another 4 marks, and Bingyen approved my appeal again, bringing my score up to 18/20. Even though I did spend a lot of time fixing my code and  I stuck to my original approach instead of copying from the solution, Bingyen is pretty epic for approving my appeal.

Peter is also epic, one time I was struggling with a lab and I emailed him for help, and he told me to send him my code and he immediately pointed out the mistake.

So, the whole teaching team is very nice and professional, I would give them A++!!!!
comment_workload:
The workload is not very epic. It is two 4 credit courses squeezed into one. Taking more than 10 credits with COMP2012H (so 15 credits in total for the semester) is a huge mistake, as I found out. I took 18 credits and nearly worked myself to death. I was walking up 8am and working until 11pm every day for the entire semester.

The labs took me around 6 hours each, and the PAs took around a couple days to a whole week to finish. If you are an epic genius you may spend less time.

PA1 (minesweeper solver) was a nightmare. I spent more than a week on it. I also decided to be a smart ass and used loop instead of recursion, which resulted in my program timing out (see above) and a lot of trouble with fixing my code and appealing. Don't be a smart ass like me. Recursion is good, recursion is very healthy for your sanity.

PA2 (proof assistant) was slightly better. I made a careless mistake which caused my program to fail for a special test case and costed me some points though. So, try to consider all the test cases.

PA3 and 4 (turn based war game) were better. There were no insane special test cases you need to consider. If your program produces the correct output then you're done. However, the amount of coding to be done is massive, so I still spent a couple days on each.

The group project gave me PTSD. I unintentionally kind of free-rided it. My groupmates started working on it 2 weeks before the deadline without telling me. After finishing PA4 I decided to start working on it (it was 1 week before the deadline) and I found the code 50% completed. So I freaked out and was worried that I was going be considered a free-rider and get a mark penalty. Well, I tried my best to contribute in the last week and we eventually got a ~95%, so I can't complain.

Edit: you can find one of my groupmate's review here. I'm the bad teammate that he mentioned who proposed features when the deadline is near. Well, according to his review he got an A and I got an A+, so who is laughing now? *evil cackle*
comment_grading:
PA1 7.2/8
PA2 7.54/8
PA3 8/8
PA4 8/8
Labs 8/8
Project 22.9/24
(PAs, Labs, Project all slightly above mean; most ppl get close to full marks)
Finals 75.5/100 (+0.722 SD)
Mean ~65, SD ~13

Got an A+ at the end, so quite unexpected. But I think my score should be close to the A+ cut-off, so it was a close call. I thought I was going to get an A, so I mind-blown when I received an email from Desmond saying I got an A+. Yes, he sends you an email telling you what grade you got, which shows how epic he is.

I though I screwed up my finals for sure. I had 3 essays to finish during the last week of lessons and we only had 3 days of study leave, so I ended up with only 2.5 days to study for the finals.

I also made some dumb mistakes during the finals, like spelling val as value, capitalization, etc. which costed me at least 5 marks. I also forgot to insert one value in the question about AVL tree which costed me 6 marks. The canvas exam was a bit awkward. 2 of the the questions gave you a header file and asked you to implement the functions. You had to keep scrolling up to see the main question then scroll down to the test boxes. It would have been a good idea to write down the details of the given classes like the data members on a piece of paper so you don't need to keep scrolling up and down.

comment_content:
Basically what my fellow classmates have posted.
You will fall in love with this course i.f.f. you love programming. Namely, if you don't enjoy programming, even if you could handle the workload before the midterm period, you might eventually feel stressed out.

comment_teaching:
Really enjoy taking Prof. Desmond's class. He demonstrated the concepts with examples in detail and kindly reminded us the important parts (prof: "If I were you, I would circle it and draw three stars around this keyword!"). Also, he is very talkative, easy-going, and a great listener to students' needs. Extension of deadlines is negotiable. He did a great job in creating an interactive and comfortable learning atmosphere in spite of our lectures conducted via Zoom. He encourages us to rethink the purpose of every line of code and logic behind the usage of data structures when approaching a problem.

I was amazed by his enthusiasm from his welcoming emails (3 emails about additional C++ concepts on average per week). He is very responsive to emails and questions during classes. Just write him emails or interrupt him politely during class to clear your doubts if you have any.

For the TAs, Bing Yen and Peter have been really responsive to email/meeting requests as well. Sometimes, the task description of lab and assignments are not that clear but make sure you ASK THEM ASAP!

From group projects, students also get to learn a lot not only from the talented/hard-working peers but also practical usages of C++!
comment_workload:
Tremendously heavy, more than I expected, probably because all the reviews here were written by students who have already learned OOP/are talented/are passionate about programming.

I recommend students learn C++ in advance. (I regret that I did not spent more weeks during my summer to finish up all the online C++ lecture videos of Stanfxxd CS106B...) Also, I advise that students should know their learning capacity well. Otherwise, students might end up struggling like me:

- PAs:
I spent at least 1.5 weeks on average to finish PA1, 2, 3. Unfortunately, I was unable to finish PA4.
- Labs:
I spent at least 3 days on average to finalize each lab submission. Minimum time spent to submit one lab is 5 hours. Lab works were easier when the semester was coming to an end.
- The 3 holidays (National Day of PROC, the day following the Mid-Autumn Festival and the Double 9th) are my saviors. Otherwise, I might beg for deadline extension for every hw submission...

Honestly, as a student who studies in a non-CS major and is probably not talented enough to take this course without prior OOP coding experience, I think COMP2011 + COMP2012 probably would be better in terms of the learning experience.

Of course there are people who are talented enough to pick up concepts easily without prior experience in OOP programming, but just make sure you know your strengths and limits before taking this course. Lol.
comment_grading:
Prof. said roughly 50% of students get A range and he rarely gives a C range.

Do the past papers at least 1 week before the exam. Learning resources are abundant but they require time to digest.

Review the concepts right after class/on the same day, and start working on lab. Don't be ddl fighters!

The marks for each PA and lab are fair, and Desmond is a lenient guy.

I didn't do so well for PAs, one of them below mean and on average above mean a little(though the mean was pretty high), including the group project. But for the final exam, I got about mean+1.3sd, and got A+ at the end. There was no midterm this semester.

One thing to notice. We used ZINC, an automatic grading system, for submitting and grading labs and PAs this semester. Usually, each task had several test cases, and for each test, it is either full mark or zero. For PA2 I made a mistake for one single task and failed 7 tests, costing me 20+ marks. So it won't hurt to be really careful and check every task thoroughly(maybe by writing and running your own test cases) before the latest submission.

comment_workload:
**If you don’t have enough time to do PA, email Desmond to ask for extending deadline, he will extend it**

Lab: labs are pretty easy, usually you just need 1-2hrs to finish. And starting from this semester, auto grading system ZINC is used, so lab sessions become optional.

Project: If you don’t have prior experience on GUI Programming / doing programming project, I will recommend you to learn QT and do it early, otherwise you will deadline fight for that. For project presentation, I think they expected to be overran, but I will suggest you don’t bullshit to much on your product demo, demonstrating OOP skills and data structure are the most important thing.

Final Exam: Start revision early and make sure you understand how to implement those data structure. I’m sure that some of you have experiences on programming, so you may feel easy for COMP2011 content. But the exam is not just asking you OOP skills, but also data structure. And you may be required to implement any data structure during the exam. And those questions are tricky so make sure you do all past paper especially COMP2012 and COMP2012H.
