comment_content:
You will love this course if and only if you love programming in C++.

This course is a paradise for those who love programming, especially in C++. This course provides almost all knowledge about basic C++ programming, from basic procedural programming to advanced OO design (e.g abstract classes, templates). On the other hand, it is tedious for those who have less programming experience or little interest in programming. There are tons of material covered in a single semester: 21 sets of slides not counting the extras.

The course materials are extremely well prepared. They explain the concepts clearly. There are lots of examples provided and Desmond will cover some extra ones in class. The materials suffice for those aiming for a satisfactory understanding and good enough score in exams. I personally found that not enough and I spent lots of time reading cppreference.com .

IMO, this course should be renamed to "Honors C++ Programming and Applications in Data Structures". It's more about the OO programming in C++ instead of introducing the OO paradigm.
comment_teaching:
Desmond, I know you will read the comments here.
I just want to say. You are a really cool guy indeed.

Desmond spent lots of time covering the little details and some fun corner cases. It is understandable that not everything can be covered in class and many cases are missing. :P Nevertheless, it really motivates me to dig deep into the C++ standards and appreciate the beauty of C++. Why the language is designed in a way that this case causes an error but not another?

I remember Desmond told me about the g++ compiler not following the standard. One of them is about defining templated operator both in-class and out-of-class (and the operator is called). I thought, is it really the case? Although I did not find out the standard incompliance, I found a really interesting thing. g++ warns about not compliant to ISO C++ standard if the non-templated operator is defined both in-class and out-of-class, but it is not the case when the operator is templated. The latter case is indeed compliant to standard! That is a function template overloading, which the in-class definition is more specialized than the out-of-class one, so it won't be ambiguous when the operator is called. :O
(Maybe I will later write a little snippet and attach it here, but I believe no one would be interested :P)

Desmond is famous for his timely reply of emails and interesting teaching style, so I won't repeat too much in this review. He also explains the concepts clearly with easy to understand examples. But most importantly, he is really good at maintaining a good learning atmosphere so you want to constantly learn more.

tl;dr Desmond AA AAA! (in Desmond tone)

Also, I would like to thank Bing Yen, who is the TA of my lab session. He is a nice guy to talk with! He did his undergrad in UST and is a quite knowledgeable person. You can discuss nearly anything about 2012H or even about some of your other courses. He prepared the materials quite well, and the tasks are interesting (like the chess in PA4, you can play chess with him too!). Most importantly, he gives good grades :P

I didn't get in touch very often with the other two TAs and the materials (assignments & graders) are not prepared that well. I believe they are both nice guys. I want to thank Guangneng who patiently listen to my explanations and give me back 2 points in exam checking despite I wrote long and messy codes for the LCA.
comment_workload:
Just a fair warning: if you don't fully enjoy programming, you will hate the workload (and the whole course).

I would rate the workload A if this course changed to 8 credits.

For me, a relatively experienced programmer in competitive programming (I have used C++ as my primary programming language for 4 years) and a team member of HKUST programming team, the workload is just at the right level, barely manageable in other words. I have to spend lots of time in this course despite my good programming background.

There are a total of 4 PAs, 1 PP and 8 Labs. Labs won't take much of your time (~1 hr workload each) but the assignment and the project are quite heavy.

The first PA is sudoku, a very easy and standard recursion task. It takes only a few hours to finish but I didn't pay attention to the handling of small case alphabets and got below mean......

PA2 is about a weird doubly link list stock system (it is quite like persistent data structures). I tried to speedrun PA2, it took me about 7.5 hours of continuous work. Even more time is used for writing test cases and stress testing to debug the program. I also found the bug in the demo program which I spent some time typing up emails explaining to Desmond and TAs about the situation.
(I believe stress testing and writing test cases are overkill, the grader grades on simple cases anyway)

PA3 was released near midterm, it's a pipes game. I finished the programming part fairly quickly but the documentation part was a nightmare. I spent about one to two days time (not including breaks) to type up a 23 pages (about 5k words) report explaining everything in the program, glad Desmond extended the deadline by 2 days.

PA4 is chess. It took me about 13 hours to complete the program and an extra 5 hours for the full 10 points bonus. Although it took much longer than PA2, working on this is way more fun and less frustrating as you can test play it while coding.

Regarding the project, you have to form a team of 2-3 ppl and propose a topic by yourself. The workload depends on how far you want to accomplish. You don't need to work really hard to get a good score, it is more like your personal aim instead of grades. I spent hours and hours on the project because I enjoy working on it. You can look at the commit history for details. But really, I hate documenting it...... I spent two days seating at LC working from 9 to 9 writing docs for each function using doxygen.

In case anyone is interested in our project, here's the link:
https://github.com/STommydx/photo-editing-software

Although the project got graded, I sometimes will work on fixing some bugs or on some new minor features (just for fun). So, feel free to report bugs or even open PRs ;)

Protip: Image Processing is a good topic if graded by Desmond (he said he would give 20 points bonus!)
(利申: graded by Bing Yen but not Desmond)

Aside from lecture and time spent on labs and assignment, I also spent a lot of time reading cppreference.com and testing extra programs by myself. I didn't count how much time I spent. Just a reference, most of the links of the C++ language in cppreference are deep blue after the semester.

The workload is demanding but rewarding. I did learn a lot while I went through all the assignment and project madness.

Just a final note, I wrote up many snippets to help myself (and my friends) to clear the concepts. I thought I better not waste it and post it here :)

C++ Exercise (covers a lot of tricky cases not mentioned in lecture):
https://gitlab.com/snippets/1788807
https://gitlab.com/snippets/1788814

Arrays & Function parameters (extends from a case Desmond mentioned in lecture):
https://gitlab.com/snippets/1776698
https://gitlab.com/snippets/1776751
https://gitlab.com/snippets/1776862

Constructing Binary Trees with Pre-order & In-order Traversal using BST (a weird but interesting solution for lab 8):
https://gitlab.com/snippets/1784383

Lambda closure type (have you ever thought of the type of lambda? can they be copied?):
https://gitlab.com/snippets/1776478

comment_content:
The content is divided into 3 major components:
1. Procedural programming (Programming in a C-ly way)
2. Object oriented programming (Programming in a C++-ly way)
3. Data structures (Programming in a programmer-ly way)
Simple data structure that are ubiquitous such as binary trees and its variants, stacks and queues, hash tables.

The course puts heavy emphasis on the syntax and usage of C++, therefore it seems likely that one of the goal of the course is to pave way for subsequent courses which requires adequate programming skill.
comment_teaching:
The instructor is passionate in teaching and he developed his own vivid and lively style of lecturing. He has a good handle of lecture time as he is able to speed up and slow down in appropriate circumstances. For example, he tends to recapitulate and explains slowly when the class stumbles upon a difficult or confusing topic, and skims through straightforward and unimportant parts in the lecture notes.

It is sugestive, to the teaching ability of the instructor, to note that despite the bad time slot allocated to the lecture sections (9am lectures!), the attendance, although not required and neccessary (since lecture videos are posted), still reaches 80% throughout the semester.
comment_workload:
The "best" part about this course is its insane magnitude of workload. The assessment consists of a weekly lab demonstration and 4 programming assignments. On top of that is the self-proposed project in which students has to write a nontrivial program in a group of 2-3.

The weekly lab works are highly nontrivial and time-consuming. The explanations in the lab pages are lengthy and the work required substantial. Some labs are a foreshadow of the following week's lecture as students are expected to use knowledge that has not yet been taught.

The programming assignments, though not gratuitous, are convoluted. The odd numbered assignments (Sudoku solver and generator, and pipe game) are a little bit easier than the even numbered ones (A toy stock database and a player-to-player international chess game).

The toy stock database can be summarized as a double linked list of linked lists of linked lists with nodes linking to the second level linked list.
                                 linked list ------------------------
                                        ^                                 |
                                        |                                  |
Doubly linked list ---> linked list <--------------------

The chess game is, well, the international chess game with the standard rules.

The writer took other courses with high workload such as common cores with writing assignments and math titled courses. The write begs you not to repeat the same mistake :).
comment_grading: Lenient. Anyone could receive A- or above if he/she is not falling too far behind the progress and ability of the mainstream student. It is speculated that the median of the grade is A since the writer, though mediocre and average, still got an A.

comment_content:
Content is just simply 2011 + 2012. Very fast paced, but still manageable. The instructor had very tricky syntactic errors introduced in class which were not included in notes, and they were quite helpful as well.

You learn basic C++ syntax (2011 part) then go into the world of pointer, then into OOP (class, objects, inheritance / dynamic binding, templates / generic programming, etc.). OOP part is tricky and equipped with more concepts than the others, but rather intriguing.
Then there is the data structure part, which introduces basic data structures (stack, queue, tree, hash, etc.) they are not that difficult to understand, though hard to implement for yourself, so it requires hard work to make it yours.
comment_teaching: Desmond is a very good lecturer. Though he may sound a bit weird at first, you will grow to like him for his childish yet powerful explanations that will never leave your brain. I guess this course's material, style, and rating vary greatly depending on who the lecturer is; I guess it is good to have Desmond.
comment_workload: The only downside of the course (along with its fast paced teaching for some people) is crazy workload. Don't expect you can handle this course's workload while doing other high workload courses, that will ruin your grade, health, and life. The course itself will take a great deal of time away from you, so only those who are prepared to suffer should take the course.
comment_grading:
Grading is the best part of this course: not saying that other parts are not good, grading is unbelievably good here.

Basically the scheme was

4 Assignment (7% each)
1 Group Project (12%)
Lab (10%)
Midterm (20%)
Final (30%)

Many of the assignment and lab had bonus tasks, which can make up for your missed point in the criterion (assignment bonus will only be added on assignment score, not any other)

The means in both exams are higher than expected, as it is a H course. But if you get your assignments, lab, project done well, you will totally be fine.
I had both MT and Final below mean by a little bit, but still got A range.

comment_content:
Since the syllabus of COMP 2012H is the combination of COMP 2011 and COMP 2012, there will be a lots of concept needed to study.
The content is related to "Programming" more than "C++" so the use of data structure and algorithm is the major part of this course.
However, if you do not have experience in C++, you must have a hard time in this course.

Before Mid-Term:
Simple C++ syntax ( if for while pointer recursion etc. )
Simple Data Structure ( e.g. linked list )
Simple Sorting algorithm ( e.g. merge sort )

After Mid-Term:
OOP ( class inheritance etc. )
More Data Structure and Sorting algorithm ( e.g. tree heapsort )
comment_teaching: The instructor is nice and have a set of good powerpoint which is enough to learn about the topics. However, I think the time is not enough for a student to learn during class. There are too much things in one lesson. The process of the course is very fast. One lesson can cover 2~3 topics. If you not familiar to C++ or OOP, I think you should read some books beforehand.
comment_workload:
There are 3 assignment.

The first one is very easy. I will say it just a practice.

For the others two assignment may need about 25+ hours to finish.

The second assignment is the hardest one. It is about the use of the data structure. Since there is some bonus marks for the speed of the program, some research at the Internet is required.

The third assignment is related to OOP. I think it is much more easier than assignment 2 but still have some difficulties. Since the topic of the assignment is game, it is a bit more interesting than other assignment. Since there are some restriction by the provided code and requirement, the .
comment_grading:
There are 1 mid-term exam and 3 assignment and 1 final exam.

The mid-term exam is not difficult.
If you know C++, you could got well in the short question part.
However, the long question require a bit more which need you to write good algorithm by yourself.

For the final exam, it is similar to the mid-term but contain the difficult part of this course, like STL, class, heap.

I got both mid-term and final above mean.
And the all three assignment almost full marks (many people got near full since there is bonus marks).
Then got an A so I think the grading is really good.

comment_content:
Covers basic programming concepts, and OOP. Basically 2011 + 2012. Nothing non-standard. Apart from the 2-courses-crammed-into-1 aspect, one would mistake this as a non-honors course.

Refer to Kabul's review for details.
comment_teaching:
Consider this: How does a course go off the rails?

Two of the many answers might be that 1) the instructor is incompetent, and that 2) the instructor is irresponsible.

Either one is enough to derail an otherwise mediocre course, but in this case, they are true at the same time.

Quan is not familiar the particularities of C++, or an effective communicator behind the podium. His slides are god awful — incomprehensible, ancient, with errors all over.

More egregious is his utter lack of concern for the effectiveness of any aspect of the course. Quan only gives lectures. Labs are out of his purview. Assignments are out of his purview. Exams are out of his purview. In fact, he hadn't laid eyes on the midterm paper before we were done with midterm. This inevitably led to a total disconnect between teaching, and testing — we were grossly unequipped to do well in the exams, whose purpose seems to be to differentiate those that are proficient C++ programmers from those that aren't. It would be unwarrented praise for the TAs to say this course is run by them (a few aren't helpful, at all), but it certainly is not run by Quan.

It's been a month since the semester came to an end, but I still feel a tinge of fury, which I know is shared by many of my classmates. I wasn't even burned by the grade.

Keep out.
comment_workload:
The second assignment is fairly intensive, requiring ~40 hours of programming.

In total, this course took me ~80 hours of study time, excluding lectures (which I didn't attend).
comment_grading: There are a lot of awful things about this course, but grading is not one of them. I only have limited data, of course, but top ~15% got me an A+.

comment_content:
This course is designed for students with substantial programming backgrounds. No newbies please. You'll flunk the hell of whole course.

There are 3 main parts.

Basics
🔷 1.1 Introduction
🔷 1.2 C++ Basics
🔷 1.3 If-else Statement
🔷 1.4 Loops
🔷 1.5 Functions
🔶 2.1 Pass by Reference
🔷 2.2 Arrays
🔶 2.3 Recursion
🔷 3.1 Miscellaneous Flow Control
🔷 3.2 Local, Global Variables and Scope
🔷 3.3 More on Functions
🔷 3.4 Separate Compilation
🔷 3.5 File I/O
🔷 3.6 Structures
🔶 3.7 Pointers
🔶 3.8 Dynamic Objects

Object-oriented Programming (OOP)
🔷 6.1 Classes and OOP
🔷 6.2 Abstract Data Types (ADT)
🔶 6.3 Classes with Dynamic Members
🔷 6.4 List as ADT
🔶 6.5 Overloading
🔷 6.6 More on Classes
🔶 9.1 Generic Programming
🔷 9.2 Standard Template Library (STL)
🔷 9.3 String
🔶 10.1 Inheritance
🔶 10.2 Polymorphism

Data Structures & Algorithms
🔶 4.1 Lists
🔷 4.2 List Algorithms
🔷 5.1 Algorithm Analysis
🔷 5.2 Merge Sort, Insertion Sort
🔶 7 Lists, Stacks, and Queues
🔶 11.1 Trees, Binary Trees, and Binary Search Trees (BST)
🔶 11.2 AVL-trees - Part I
🔷 11.3 AVL-trees - Part II
🔶 11.4 Heaps, Heap Sort, and Priority Queues
🔶 12 Hashing
🔷 13.1 Graph and Breadth First Search (BFS)
🔷 13.2 Depth First Search (DFS)
🔷 13.3 B+ Trees

Pay attention to topics marked with 🔶.

Among all the 3 main parts, the most difficult one is certainly data structures and algorithms. Make sure you spend time on topics related to implementation and manipulation of data structures like linked lists, stacks, queues, binary trees and heaps.

The PowerPoints have been among the messiest I've ever seen - they just switch from topic to topic without any linkage or transition in between. Horrible.
comment_teaching:
The professor only skimmed over the "philosophy" but never explained how to implement them. Lectures are futile - don't even waste time on attending them. Better rely on self-studying.

The TA's are even worse. They just released assignments without previously building a viable program themselves, and it turned out they'd overlooked a great deal of important details in the assignment description. I dunno how much time I'd wasted when they repeatedly altered the requirements of the assignments thanks to inaccuracies in their description. Fxxk it. I had to start over again.
comment_workload:
The TA's released only 3 assignments throughout the semester, but the second one was crazy - absolutely not something that could be possibly done within the 2-week time constraint they'd given. It was basically a bigDecimal class using a linked list to store an infinitely long decimal. The algorithms required for decimal operations were especially complicated and most likely you'd end up with numerous bugs that just crashed the program instantly.

The lab tasks were another headache. While some of them were trivial and could be done within merely 15 minutes, a few of them were suddenly crazily difficult and could never be done within the 2-hour lab.
comment_grading:
Exams are mind blowers. While the lecturer taught easy concepts in the classes, the exams were on a completely new level - we were required to complete complicated algorithms which even honor-stream students couldn't handle. The midterm was already a disaster - mean score was only 5X/100, and note that's for the top students who got A's or A+'s in the 1000-level courses. The final was even more demanding and as anticipated, I crashed my final exam miserably.

I ended up in a grade B. What could I have expected? No complaints.

comment_content:
Before MT:
Constructors / destructors, static classes, inheritance, polymorphism, virtual functions, generic programming, iterators, STL functions

Big O / theta notation, sorting algorithms, binary and AVL trees, BFS / DFS, directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), hashing

comment_teaching:
You can tell he is very enthusiastic, and often talks a lot about his own experiences. He is also very lenient, e.g. 5 days of late submission quota which is later increased to 7 days.

However he stutters a lot when speaking English which makes me difficult to follow what he is saying.
comment_workload:
Labs: not only are they useless but they do not correlate to the lecture content. For example constructors are covered in the first / second lecture, but the lab about constructors are held after midterm. Most people spend time there to do their project anyways.

Written assignments: questions are all from the textbook, so sometimes you cannot just rely on his notes to do the questions. If you really have no time then just search for the backuped 2013 course website with model answers in Github.

Projects: not extremely difficult but requires a lot of time, especially the last one. I myself spent a whole weekend doing nothing but coding for nearly every project. However, they are really useful for giving you hands-on experience. To save time I think it is better to plan beforehand (how many classes, implement what functions in which class etc.)
comment_grading:
Since the scores of all students are released after final, I searched my friend's rank who got A+, and found out that there is actually more than 30% of students who got A+.

Most of the weighting goes to projects. The TAs however will just deduct 1-2 points for each bug, so most people end up with >90% for projects. Hence, to outperform others in this course you will need to score high in MT and finals. Though I see no point in being the top scorers; you don't need to be one to get a good grade.

On the other hand, you may get B / C range if you don't do well enough in the course.

comment_content: The content focused on basic of C/C++ in the first few weeks, such as pointer and class. Later on there were more about OOP, similar to the content of COMP 2012, but conducted much more faster. After midterm, the course was about basic algorithms and data structures.

comment_content:
This course is an accelerated and intensive course on concepts and techniques behind object-oriented programming (OOP) and data structures using an OOP language. It covers the major materials of COMP2011 and COMP2012, and its curriculum is designed for students with excellent programming background or substantial programming experience. Topics include: functions; pointers; abstract data types and their class implementation; static and dynamic construction and destruction of objects; data member and member functions; public interface and encapsulation; class hierarchies; polymorphism; inheritance and dynamic binding; standard template library; generic programming using templates; object-oriented view of data structures: linked lists, queues, stacks, trees, and their algorithms such as searching, sorting and hashing.
The drawback of course content is that it doesn't cover c++11 and c++14 features.
comment_teaching: The professor explains the course content sufficiently well.
comment_workload: The workload is relative heavy. There are 6 course projects in total. Students build a micro scheme interpreter step by step through the whole semester, applying concepts and ideas learned from lectures.
comment_grading:
The course will be graded on a curve, but no matter what the curve is, I guarantee you the following.

If you achieve 85% you will receive at least a A grade.
75%  B  
65%  C  
55%  D  

Your grade will be determined by a combination of factors:

Midterm exam ~20%
Final exam ~25%
Participation ~5%
Homework ~40%
Labs ~10%

comment_content:
Content is great. We learnt a lot of interesting topics surrounding the field of AI, including Naive Bayes, K-nearest neighbours (tho I heard that in the coming semesters, linear regression might replace KNN in the syllabus), K-means clustering, and artificial neurons. Some of the more advanced topics include image processing and convolutional neural networks. Lastly, we learnt minimax and alpha-beta pruning, which are search algorithms for games.

The lecture slides are designed very well.
comment_teaching:
The instructor teaches VERY WELL. She always teaches in a way that makes it easy to understand complex concepts. She also answers students' questions very patiently. Oh, and she always gives us 5 minutes break in the middle of the lecture, which really helps me when I need some more time to understand the concepts or when I need to ask her questions.

HOWEVER, the TAs are BAD. Basically, the main purpose of going to the labs is just to take attendance. The lab's teaching TA's voice is basically as low as if it is only listenable to the people who are no more than 0.5 m away from him. Most of the time, we can't even notice that he started the teaching because, yeah.

Also, the assignments are so problematic. I will explain more under the "WORKLOAD" part.
comment_workload:
Assignments and labs are SO PROBLEMATIC.

Many bugs in their skeleton, many unclear instructions, many untaught things which appear in the assignments. They really expect us to deep-learn the unclear documentation on some library functions that are OUT-OF-THE-TEACHING-SYLLABUS.

Half of the last programming assignment was about the preprocessing of texts. Well, you might think --- yeah, that looks important in AI. BUT, if we have just 1 out of 30000 results that is [NOT WRONG, but a little DIFFERENT from their result], then good game. **IT IS NOT ABOUT RIGHT OR WRONG ---- IT IS ABOUT GUESSING THE TA'S MODEL ANSWER'S THINKING LOGIC.** The instructions are not clear enough that the preprocessing can be done in MANY DIFFERENT WAYS, in which you need to try like a thousand times to get it done in the way the TA wants you to do.
comment_grading:
I got an A+, but at what cost?

Each of the 2 programming assignments, which are DESIGNED BADLY (I'll explain more under "WORKLOAD"), contributes to 15% of the course grade. That means 30% goes to programming assignments.

Midterm and Final are like 20% and 40%, respectively. Labs are 10%. Labs require attendance, and also the 4 best scores out of 5 lab exercises are counted.

I got like 98 marks in the midterm (which is quite a lot higher than the mean). But what does that mean? 20%. Only. The midterm is like treating us as if our brains were AI. A lot of calculations that can make your brain explode. The time limit was short as well.

In comparison, failing just ONE test case in a programming assignment costs you 1% deduction in the whole course grade. Which is equivalent to losing 5 marks in a midterm exam. What does that mean? A very complicated calculation that would drive you crazy might earn you 2 marks in the midterm. Yeah.

comment_grading:
Desmond promises 25% A range so its extremely good grading. But bare in mind that people taking this course has an extended major in AI. So be prepared as they are generally more competitive than other students. Anyway, these are my marks: (LQ/Median/ Mean/UQ/Max)
Lab  full marks
PA1 16.5/16.5 (15/15.95/15.54/16.5/16.5)
PA2 14.36/15 (8.54/11.25/10.1/12.32/15)
Midterm 84.7/100 (49.35/60.25/58.93/70.25/92.7)
Final 90.75/100 (54.33/69/64.07/76.45/94.5)
Course Total 92.6/100 (65.65/75.5/71.87/81.56/95.92)
Grade A+

I would like to share some of my thoughts on how you could excel in this course. (Just personal opinion)
Firstly, as mentioned above, you should try your best to learn vectorization. You have to use it in labs, pas and also exams. I know that it is extremely frustrating to learn it as a beginner in coding, but this skill is extremely important in this course. I also struggled a lot in learning it and eventually I became good enough to be able to figure out labs and pas on my own. (I know the logic to doing it but sometimes have minor bugs in my code. Background: COMP student who have taken COMP2011 and 2012 already)

Exams: Spend time to really understand the concepts and memorize small details in slides. The Exams are pretty concept oriented and some questions are just math problems. I still remember we have to prove mathematically why a perceptron with sigma activation function cannot deal with linearly inseparable data points. If you are not good at maths, um... good luck.
For me, I think I understand each topic thoroughly, so I pretty much just write whatever I think on the exam paper. And most of them turn out to be correct.
Also, there's always one coding question taking up around 14-16 marks of the whole paper. My advice would be to NEVER give up. Even if you do not know how to do it at all, scribble something random. If you left it blank, the tas can't give you any marks. I remember writing a completely wrong solution on my coding question on final exam, and was expecting 2/6 at most and probably 1/6. But the TA gave me 4/6, which is ridiculous. I think because most people are leaving it blank, they are extremely lenient in marking those questions. So NEVER, NEVER, NEVER leave those questions blank.

I did bad in midterm. I misunderstood the focus of the exam. I put more effort and did better in final.
Now, in my point of view:
1. There is a Python programming problem, and a hard subproblem is worth 6-7 points, so it is important to practice using broadcasting proficiently to deal with it.
2. Generally speaking, pay attention to python coding, such as building models and performing operations like reshaping, to avoid getting stuck when using these lines.
3. Besides the above, I think one can treat this course as a Liberal arts minor course, like 史地政 courses in high school. Recite the characteristics of different topics, familiarize yourself with terms and vocabulary to express yourself clearly.

下面我将吐槽一下PA2。
public test case测的时候什么问题都没有，hidden test case全是问题。我记得官方说hidden test case的设置目的是防止hard code，那么为什么难度的差异这么大呢，为什么public test这么不反映问题呢？这造成，我（我猜也包括一些同学，但此处不敢代表所有人）对于hidden test的难度一无所知，不知道努力/优化到什么程度才算ok。比如，test case11对efficiency的要求很模糊，先说了可以不vectorize，又说推荐优化，结果却是不vectorize就会挂。accuracy 75%同样如此。提交时无法知道模型质量，只能出分后抓瞎。ps，判断保留哪个视频这种事情，又没有明确进行提示，每个人的理解各不相同，（尝试起来又很耗时，所以我不想尝试所有可能，）让这种个人理解而非学习/代码能力的事情影响作业成绩，是否区分得有些草率了？

comment_workload: To be honest, the labs and PAs in this semester were not easy to manage. Basically, each assignment primarily focused on applying vectorization, i.e. NumPy functions to handle arrays. It’s more about math than coding! I feel like there isn’t much connection between lecture materials and homeworks. Is it just me? When doing Labs and PAs, I had to search online and ask AI frequently for some Numpy or other AI methods that I was not sure about. Lab 2, was designed to be extremely hard as a convention.(Desmond said he doesn't want the hard topics of different courses to overlap, so he made the earliest graded lab to be the hardest) The subsequent labs were easier than lab 2 but still not that easy. For example, it took a lot of time to run the model in lab 8, I even needed to create another google account for free GPU. For PA 1, We were required to write around 25 functions for vectorization to implement KNN and K-means. Because of the difficulty, the teaching team extended the deadline and gave bonus to students submitting before the original deadline.(10% on PA1 score that can be used to offset deduction on PA2) PA 2(designed by ‘notorious’ Brian, Jesus the 2 lowest-scoring PA I did this semester were all made by you, another one being in 2012, thank you :))) is based on the scenario of autopilot, focusing on image processing and CNN. We still had to write some functions for vectorization. Moreover, we needed to train our own model to detect the directions, which was very time consuming. The worst part, however, was that the provided test cases were almost useless, so we had to manually check everything ourselves.

comment_content:
Probably the best COMP course in terms of content. Useful, practical, and interesting. In a nutshell, it's mostly introduction to AI, ML and image processing without using advanced math, which is perfect for someone like me who knows nothing about before. And I think taking this course alone is already enough for you to intern for some entry level data science stuff.

That said, this course is definitely not a easy course. The most difficult part for me is programming with Numpy (yes, the first chapter), you need to have good multi-dimensional thinking skills to solve the programming problems. I did well in 1021 with Turtle, but here I feel like an idiot with Numpy :( Hope the new COMP1023 will help people prepare more for it.

comment_workload:
The workload is not light, but A here cuz I don't wanna break my straight A rating for the course :)

Lab:
I spent the most time on Lab 2 (the first graded lab) cuz it requires only Numpy programming. Other labs (except Lab 9 Minimax) allow other libraries so are more simple.

PA:
That's where the pain comes from. PA1 and PA2 are two different types of pain.

PA1: You have to complete all tasks in Numpy, and no for loops is allowed for most tasks. I used several blank sheets just to visualize 3D array operations. Now if you see people drawing random squares and cubes on paper in LC, you could guess what they're doing.

PA2: To get full marks your model has to get >=75% accuracy. Imagine spending 20 minutes to train your model and it only got a 60% accuracy, then you repeat this process again and again for 50 times but it's still below 75%. (Or worse, it got a 99% accuracy and obviously it’s overfitting.) As Google Colab limits daily GPU use, I've opened several new accounts just for working on this PA.
Finally I still couldn't get 75% accuracy, but fortunately I still got full marks for PA thanks to early submission bonus in PA1.

comment_content:
In short, I have learn a lot initiative idea of various AI algorithm and coding method where mainly for classification and only high school level math are required to understand most of the things in this course.
---
You will first learn some advance python in first two-three weeks since the basic python in COMP1021 is not enough to help us write AI code. Where here will introduce the mindset of vectorize (Writing code without using for loop), which is very important technique for your coding later in this course/other AI course.*

Then you will learn various of AI algorithm: (Arrange in taught order)
Naïve Bayes
K-Nearest-Neighbors Classifier
K-Means Clustering
Artificial Neural Network - Perceptron
Artificial Neural Network - Multilayer Perceptron
Digital Image Processing Fundamentals
Convolutional Neural Network
Minimax and Alpha-Beta Pruning
Introduction to Reinforcement Learning (Self-Study)^

Where most of the AI algorithm taught here is classification algorithm, which is rarely seen because other foundation course are most likely focus on regression algorithm.

And course instructor will try to tell you the initiative idea of how the algorithm come, when and why we use the algorithm, instead of just reading notes and letting you recite the algorithm for no reason.

At the end of this course, you will learn some AI ethics where I treat it as common sense.

If you want to know more about the algorithm (e.g. how to proof for the error function). Course instructor also provide addition material for you to read, which won't cover in exam.

* It might be remove from the syllabus from future offer semester and these part of content will be move on to COMP1023 (New course that start offer in 202526 Fall)

^ As *, it might be cover in lesson when the remove of advance python in future offer semester.

comment_content:
Check other reviews for table of content.
Overall I would say the course is well designed to give you a taste of some fundamental AI. My reason for taking this course was just to see what it's all about and get a high level idea which the course did quite well.
That being said, bear in mind that this course will cover lots of topics but can't go too deep. Hence, there will be lots of black box. For example you'll just be told to use some specific activation functions and some specific number of layers without explanation.
comment_teaching: I mean it's Desmond, do I need to say anything?
comment_workload:
Overall the workload is medium, or at least it's as much as should be expected from a 2xxx comp course.
The labs are doable within a few hours and the PAs are doable within a day. One thing to note is that the difficulty of this course decreases over time (since we later use libraries :p). So PA1 is probably as hard as this course gets (which is not too hard).
Another thing is that desmond takes extra classes every other week since he goes very slow.
The concepts are not really hard to internalize, but there are many concepts.
Having said that, Desmond gives a lot of time for everything (so much that I forgot to do one of the labs o.O) so you can usually do coursework whenever you have time.
comment_grading:
For a non honours comp course, the grading is as good as it gets. Desmond promises around 25% A range.
Firstly, there's lab attendance marks which I dont like at all (even tho I still skipped because the weightage is not too much)
You can check the weightages in other reviews. Pretty much everyone gets full on labs and nearly full on PAs so let me just comment on the exams.
The mid term was garbage. Just basic arithmetic but really fast. I believe it was made by one of the TAs. But the final was made by desmond and it was really good. There were easy questions for everyone to grab and some harder conceptual questions to made a good curve. Make sure you conceptually understand the high level ideas to perform good.

comment_content:
You have near zero AI knowledge (like me)? well, you're at the right place, this course is for you

The course title "Exploring Artificial Intelligence" really lives up to its name, here you'll be exposed to (at introductory level) a wide variety of AI/ML techniques and their applications in various fields e.g. predicting your grade, find optimal move in some games, classifying images

Each topic is introduced with rich examples, diagrams and graphics, which is highly beginner friendly. You also have hands-on opportunity to implement AL algorithms in python either from scratch or with library like keras.

comment_teaching:
Desmond is very supportive, in class he always try to give satisfactory answers to questions from students, you can sometimes find him chatting with students after/outside class too.

comment_grading:
I didn't get full mark in PA and labs. Only around 5 and 10 marks above UQ in mid term and final respectively. To my great surprise the letter grade is A, very lenient !

(by the way, physical attendance required for labs this sem)

One thing to note is that this course is calculation focused, so do more dry run on examples when you're doing revision e.g. given data, manually do prediction with naive bayes, manually do convolution ... also make sure you can type very fast with your calculator (which you'll bring to exam)

▷ Introduction to & History of AI

    • Python basics (COMP1021 + List comprehension, set?, parallel iteration, class)

    • ***NumPy*** nD array reshaping and broadcasting can be confusing but are very important to fully understand!
▷ Naive Bayes Classifier

▷ Minimax & Alpha-Beta Pruning
    • AI in perfect information, zero-sum, deterministic games like tick-tac-toe
▷ Ethics of AI

Overall very practical and interesting content, and can be useful for capstone projects. Mostly on machine learning (a subset of AI), only minimax & alpha-beta pruning is classic AI.

A little overlap with COMP1942 (a data mining course) on Naive Bayes, KNN, and K-means clustering, but since this course does coding, I'd say 2211 is more practical, while 1942 is more in-depth with the theory and manual execution of the algorithm.
comment_teaching:

comment_workload:
5 graded labs (~bi-weekly) + 2 PAs

= Time & Braincells spent =
Can be time-consuming and even frustrating to figure out. Lab 2, 4, and PA 1 were the most painful since we manually implement the algorithm with only NumPy while being unfamiliar with it. Later on, we can use libraries like SciKitLearn and Keras, BUT fine-tuning the training model hyperparameters will take the majority of the time instead of coding.

= The code =
The programs were saved in Jupyter Notebooks (.ipynb) and executed on Google Colab. No setup is required for GColab but for later labs where you need to train a model, you need to be wary of the limited GPU usage quota. If you have a decent PC, I highly recommend setting up your local Anaconda environment and VSCode so that you can train on your own GPU w/ Tensorflow GPU (which in my Lab 8 is 4s/epoch on RTX3060 compared to 11s/epoch on GColab's Nvidea T4). Yes, it will be quite a hassle, but you will thank it for speeding up training by a margin and allowing you to test more model configurations.
▷ Anaconda Navigator: https://docs.anaconda.com/free/navigator/index.html
(Note that Tensorflow GPU needs to specifically be installed through your environment's terminal and then "pip -install tensorflow-gpu". It might be different for you, but that's how I got mine working)

= ZINC =
Be VERY CAREFUL in checking your code! ZINC will NOT generate a grading report when you submit so you won't know if your code is bugged or wrong. Batch grading in 2211 has crashed ZINC multiple times this semester :P.

= Labs (G for graded) =
1. NumPy
2. Naive Bayes Classifier (G)
3. KNN
4. KMC (G)
5. Simple Perceptron
6. MLP (G)
7. Digital Image Processing Fundamentals
8. CNN (G)
9. Minimax & Alpha-Beta Pruning (G)
10. Reinforcement Learning (Optional, after the exam for additional self-learning)

= Programming Assignments =
PA1 KNN Classifier for Sentiment Analysis
PA2 Retinal Vessel Segmentation using CNN
comment_grading:
= Grading scheme =
▷ Lab exercises 10% (5 graded labs, 2% each; 4 ungraded labs additionally)
▷ Programming assignments 30% (2 PAs, 15% each)
▷ Midterm exam 20% OR 0%
▷ Final exam 40% OR 60%
(The more beneficial one will be chosen for you automatically)

Desmond promised 25% A-range and ~46% B-range THIS semester. It is pretty competitive with many apostles coming for Desmond's teaching (seeing that the classroom of 126 capacity was packed on the first day and the quota had to be raised two times from 80 to 120).

Exams pretty much determine your grade, and you should **focus a lot on the concepts** instead of programming since ~75% of the exams will be short questions where you have to explain things. Often I feel stupid for not being able to answer those with total confidence, and since math equations and library functions (w/ documentation) will be provided, study even more on the concepts and rationale instead!

I got an A-, but considering my scores, I reckon I'm right on the edge of the A range.

Probably the hardest topic in this course is the first one, Python Fundamentals for AI.  In this topic, you will learn Numpy, and most importantly Numpy broadcasting.  Lots and lots of practice and experience is needed to master broadcasting, and whether or not you master it will make or break your grade.  Personally, I found it challenging because it's not related to programming, instead it really needs imagination.  You need to be able to visualize 2D or 3D data in your head and think about how you can reshape/ multiply them together to achieve your goal.  So the 2 semesters spent in 2011/2012 won't help you.  9 out of 100 marks from midterm are related to broadcasting, and whether you master it or not will determine if you pass many test cases for PA1.

comment_content:
An introductory course to A.I. I tend to divide the course into four parts:

----- Part Zero: Some soft stuff -----
-> History of AI (First part of the course)
-> AI ethics (Last part of the course)

----- Part One: Simple algorithm -----
1. Naive Bayes (Evaluate probability to predict class)
2. K-nearest (Predict class by majority voting)
3. K-means (Do grouping to a set of data)

----- Part Two: Artificial Neural Network -----
4. Multilayer perceptron
5. Image processing
6. Convolutional neural network

----- Part Three: Game Theory related -----
7. Minimax
8. Reinforcement learning (Optional)

Personal comment:

1. Before Naive Bayes, an introduction to Python is given (with many extra lessons!) Therefore there is no need to worry if you are not familiar with Python.
2. This is really an introductory course, in the sense that the requirement of math is even fewer than Andrew Ng's AI course in Coursera... But I find this course better anyway.
3. The part for reinforcement learning is actually a good starter to learn (discrete time) "stochastic process", which is an interesting topic in math. I personally find the related material in this course is more readable than that in MATH3425...
comment_teaching:
1. Enthusiasm: Desmond's enthusiasm in teaching makes the class lively. Because of that, this is the first course that I seldom feel bored or refused to attend the F2F class.

2. Extra material: Desmond is willing to distribute advanced detail to related topics through email. This is frequently done in first half of the course.
comment_workload:
Assignments: Only 5 labs and 2 programming assignments are required to submit. The labs are simple.

The only tricky assignment in this semester is PA1: It requires you to do modification to your code well to get full score, which strictly needs to apply vectorization everywhere instead of loops; PA2 is another extreme: You even do not have to think because the architecture is given - Because of that, this PA is even easier than regular lab...

Preparation for exam: Since necessary math formulas and codes commands are given in exam, the only thing which is essential to memorize is the rationale behind each algorithm. Indeed there are some other tricky things like broadcasting rule, do convolution or calculating number of trainable parameters and so on, but they can be understood once you are willing to practice.
comment_grading:
1. As usual, Desmond's course promises as large A range as possible.
2. Warning: This semester is more competitive than the last one! Given that the midterm exams in two semesters are about the same (in terms of style, difficulty...), last sem's mean is ~65, and this sem's mean is ~80...
3. However, the final exam in this semester is much harder. It requires deeper understanding (than last sem's final paper) to get high score, and there are more parts requiring you to hand-write codes.

Update: Fast grading! The final statistics is released in 3 days. Mean ~60 (confused, since Desmond says 59, but Canvas says 56), SD ~14, Highest score is 86. Comparing to the result in previous semester, you can see that this year's paper is apparently more challenging.

comment_content:
This is the first offering of this brand new course, the original content plan was the following:

1. Brief History of Artificial Intelligence
2. Naive Bayes
3. K-Nearest Neighbour
4. K-Means Clustering
5. Perceptron and Multi-Layer Perceptron
6. Fundamentals of Image Processing
7. Convolutional Neural Networks
8. Minimax and Alpha-beta Pruning
9. Reinforcement Learning (Value Iteration)*
10. Artificial Intelligence Ethics

*Later on, desmond realized that there is just not enough time to cover reinforcement learning and decided to leave that to summer.

As a brand new AI related course designed with a course code less than COMP4211 and COMP3211, I can see that desmond tried his best to maintain the balence ---- he tried to make the content decent but at the same time not too complicated to scare students away. So I should say the content is well designed and brings a good overview to different aspects of AI and ML.

I do think there are two possible improvements to the content:
1. I undestand things are meant to be kept simple so as to not scare students away, but there are some math that isn't that complicated but from my perspective should not be skipped. Such as for the backpropagation of MLP, the formula provided in the slides were just like things to simply memorize (although memorization was not required as the formula is provided in exams). But in fact that formula should only be applicable to sigmoid activation since even the derivative was pre-computed as a part of it. It's not even the general learning rule. The math behind this is simply chain rule, which is something understanable as long as we took MATH1013 I suppose(which is probably a course every single ust student takes?) So maybe it's better to explain about the deatailed math behind back propagation as it is essential for understanding gradient descent behind MLP.
2. The Numpy tutorial was really good. It is almost same as the python tutorial of CS231n. But I really also hoped to learn how to properly use pytorch or tensorflow when enrolling this course. Seldom do professional researchers use Keras in machine learning research, it's just too wrapped up and provides little flexibility. Pytorch and Tensorflow (not tensorflow keras) plays a greater role. I hope that the main library chosen for the PAs can be changed into pytorch or tensorflow in the future.
comment_teaching:
Desmond is always passionate about teaching. I can feel that he just literally loves to teach. He tries his best to make sure that every concept is explained clearly, and also engages actively with the chat  to answer questions. So very good teaching!

The only downside is that he always asks "Am I going too fast", when in fact, probably he knows it, that he is going too slow. Too much time are spent on telling jokes in Cantonese and Putonghua. And then additonal lectures need to be offered, and one topic had to be left till summer. I mentioned this in the review of another desmond's course and got a great bunch of down votes though. I don't know if everyone else find this amusing but for me sometimes it just really feels time consuming and just encourages me to watch recordings in 2x speed=(
comment_workload:
Workload can be divided into Labs, PAs, and exam revision.

10 labs, 5 out of which are graded. Each lab takes around 1-3 hours to finish depending on complexity.

2 PAs, both graded. PA1 was obviously more complicated and takes about one whole day (from morning to night, non-stop). But PA2 can be done in just 2 hours, it was even simpler than lab6 and lab8 which was probably the two most lengthy labs.

Expect one day each of full revision for the midterm and final exam. Pay attention to calculating image convolution and calculating number of parameter in CNN and MLP layers. (by one day, I also mean one whole day, from morning to night, non-stop)

Therefore, the overall workload is very small.
comment_grading:
Desmond is probably the most lenient grade giver in CSE.

He gives 25% A range in COMP1022p and COMP2012 (Since that is already the maximum A range allowed for non-H courses according to ust policies) (BAD GUY UST, allow for larger A range nah! :<) and gives an astonishing 60% A range in COMP2012H (because that's an H course and this is allowed). And Desmond also promised 25% Arange for this course as well.

So you can see that desmond is really pushing to the limit against the policies to give more As. Indeed a lenient grade giver.

Also I am so grateful that he provides 2 grading schemes to provide us a try and error chance in the midterm since there weren't any pastpaper available. It was so so so thoughtful of him to provide the second scheme so that we get a chance to get used to the exam question types for this course. One thing I learnt from this is don't give up even if you failed in the midterm. Cause if you prepare well for the final you can still do a great job. Thank you again desmond for the 2 schemes option!

Scheme 2: Lab(10%) + PA(30%) + Final(60%)

comment_content:
The topics cover a large range of AI techniques and applications, which may seem quite challenging but as they said in the course description, the content does not go too in-depth, giving you the gentle introduction you need to get excited about AI. Labs and PAs give you the chance to apply what you have learnt.

The course will help you begin communicating with others about AI, and also gives you an idea for what techniques are available to use for your own projects. It is a great intro course to anyone who is not too familiar with AI, but wants to learn what's available in the field.

You will need a good Python foundation to begin with, **familiarity with Numpy is preferred**. Lectures will include python code explanation, and the final exam will also involve writing your own code. Although the lectures reviewed Python at the beginning of the course, Desmond goes quite fast, basically like a COMP1021 crash course.

Keyword Syllabus
- History of AI
- Naive Bayes
- K-Nearest Neighbour
- K-Means Clustering
- Perceptron and Multi-Layer Perceptron
- Fundamentals of Image Processing
- Convolutional Neural Networks
- Minimax and Alpha-beta Pruning
- Reinforcement Learning (Value Iteration)
- Artificial Intelligence Ethics

Even though the lessons are in online-mode, his lectures are extremely lively, continuously asks the class questions and responding to questions in chat. He is very open to questions after lesson, even if it is out-of-syllabus. I can only imagine how much fun it would be when the lessons are held face-to-face.

comment_content:
Very rewarding, content/understanding-heavy AI crash course. Even if you come into this course knowing nothing about AI like myself, you will end up being able to understand most AI research, freely converse about it, and apply it in many areas of your future projects.

I would only recommend this course to anyone with a good background in programming and is willing to learn AI. That is, if your programming experience is only limited to COMP1021, you may find this course difficult, especially when you get to Numpy. I'd argue you cannot focus on the ideas of AI itself if you are hindered by the programming.

Python skills:
- Python basics
- Numpy
- Scikitlearn
- Keras
- Tensorflow

Content:
- Definition and history of AI
- Naive Bayes Classifier
- K-Nearest Neighbours
- K-Means
- Perceptron
- Multilayer Perceptron
- Convolutional Neural Network
- Minimax / Alpha-beta pruning
- (Not taught this semester) Reinforcement learning

The hard theory and maths behind the more complex stuff will not be tested, but supplementary slides were provided for more advanced students.
comment_teaching:
Desmond is very lively and explains concepts well. He repeats concepts to make sure you understand them. He's also open to questions after the lecture and also responds to emails timely.

I would recommend staying after class to participate in post-class discussions and Q&A's. You can also attend his Zoom office hours if you just want to chat with him, even if it may be off-topic.

The pace was not fully optimised this semester as this is the first offering. However, I am sure these issues will be ironed out in future semesters.
comment_workload:
- 9 Labs, 5 of which are graded
(There were 10 labs planned, but we ran out of time and couldn't do the reinforcement learning lab)
- 2 programming assignments

There may be supplementary lessons (sometimes at 8PM-10PM) that you are expected to attend.

The labs are a good way of learning and applying the ideas taught in class. They are submitted to ZINC, and your submissions are auto-graded every time you upload, unlike COMP1021.

The programming assignments take some time, and you *are* expected to develop your own test cases. They require a solid understanding of the material to complete. Also, as another review mentioned, try to get them uploaded to ZINC before the deadline, because the server tends to get overloaded near the due date.

PS. If you have a computer with a CUDA-supported GPU, I would recommend setting up Jupyter up on your local machine and doing the Labs/PAs there instead of Google Colab. It will make the code for the later labs/PAs run much faster, and of course you have no GPU quota unlike Colab. They even set up a 3080Ti for the grading of Lab 6 :P

Grading Scheme: (official)
Condition to Obtain A:
If your course overall is larger that mean+2sd, or your course overall is larger that 90, you would get an A. (if your course overall is larger than 95, you would definitely get A+.)
In general, mean is C+, mean+1sd is B, mean+2sd is A.
Bonus Part:
You have bonus part in project. At most you can add 2.25 points to your overall score. Cindy would first cut the letter grade without the bonus mark (i.e. >87 get A-). Then they would add the bonus to your overall score to check whether your grade would change. (i.e. you get 86, then you would receive B+ without bonus part. however, with the bonus, you would get A-, which is very good.)

comment_content:
Syllabus:
(1) Introduction to Digital Logic
- Logic Gates, Truth Functions, Logic Function
- Combinational Logic
1. Multiplexor, decoder, PLA
2. Minterm / Maxterm, Canonical Forms
3. Boolean Algebra
4. K-map
- Sequential Logic
1. S-R latches
2. Gated D-Latch, Master-slave D Flip-flop
(2) Data Representation
- Signed and Unsigned Integer (2's complement)
- Floating Point Numbers (Single-Precision and Double-Precision IEEE754 Floating Point Standard)
- Characters
(3) MIPS ISA and Assembly
- Instruction Set Architecture, MIPS, Registers
- Endianness
- MIPS basic instructions
- Implementation of MIPS Instructions (R-format, I-format)
- Addressing modes
- Procedure
(4) Computer Arithmetic
- 2's Complement Arithmetic
- Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
- Multiplication
- Division
(5) Processor
- Single-cycle Datapath
- Single-cycle Control
- MIPS Pipeline
- Pipeline Control
- Pipeline Hazards (Forwarding, Stall)
(6) Memory Hierarchy
- Memory
- Principle of Locality
- Cache

In general, we will deal with some digital logical circuit, then we deal with calculating numbers in binary and writing instructions in MIPS assembly language. Finally, we implement MIPS in processor and memory.
All of these will take some time understanding. It is best if you don't skip any lectures or at least spend some time every week to catch up.

comment_content:
1. Digital Logic : it's my first time learning logic gates and using them to perform various operations. Before exam, I  recommend you prepare some tricks to simplify complicated boolean expressions.

2. Data Representation : pretty interesting to see how data are represented at low level like using IEEE 754 scheme and 2's complement. Also  here you will deal with bit array all the time so before exam, I  recommend you prepare some tricks to quickly manipulate powers of 2 e.g. \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}2^k = 2^{n}-1 (tho this actually comes from geometric series).  

3.  Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) and MIPS Assembly
     Language : pretty interesting to learn Assembly
     Language, it lets you experience a new style of programming. Before exam, I recommend you to equip yourself with ability to read & understand a block of MIPS code without any hint, you should be able to tell what the code is doing precisely.

4. Computer Arithmetic : here you'll learn ALU and how to implement arithmetic like multiplication & division with ALU. Before exam, I recommend you familiarize yourself with the every step of those algorithms including the states of the operands.  

5. Processor and pipeline : here you learn to implement various MIPS instructions with hardware. Before exam, I recommend you add additional hardware to the processor and implement more MIPS instructions, just play around.

6. Memory System : we only learned up to directed mapped cache this semester. Dr. Lam said if there's one thing that you should take away from COMP2611, then it has to the principle of locality (temporal and spatial). This principle is applied everywhere in CS and even other fields.

comment_content:
Six major topics:
1. Digital Logic (Boolean Algebra, Different Logic Gates, PLA, etc)
2. Data Representation (2's complement for int, IEEE754 for float, ASCII for char, etc)
3. MIPS ISA and Assembly (essentially just MIPS Programming)
4. Computer Arithmetic (ALU, addition, subtraction, optimized multiplication, optimized division)
5. Processor (From Single Cycle Datapath to pipelined)
6. Memory (Locality and Hierarchy)

Also I heard rumors that the course will switch from MIPS to ARM in 1-2 years? Maybe MIPS is out of date now.

comment_teaching:
\\ Lecture in Recording Form //

Alex's teaching style is perfect if you
watch his lecture recording only.

In the recording, you can adjust
the timeline and play speed as you like.

I set the speed to 1.25x and
skipped all the blowing water parts.

During the lecture, he directly draws
pictures and do calculations on the slide.

As a result,
he often made the notes messy.

You may pause and try to digest his
notes in the recording.

Also, he will review and try to summarize
the materials in the previous lecture.

If you are confident in what he is reviewing,
you may simply skip them since he is just
repeating what he said previously.

----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

\\ Lecture in Real-time Live //

Biased Comment Erased :)

You may join his class during Add-drop
and see if you enjoy his real-time teaching.

----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

\\ Overall Comment //

--- [ 1. Lecture ] ---
Join Real-time Live or Watch Recording.

Never skip them unless you can digest
ALL details in the Lecture Notes.

--- [ 2. Tutorial ] ---
Self-study is recommended.

If you found the topic too difficult.
Watch the recording or go to Piazza.

--- [ 3. Labs ] ---
Self-study if time allows
and you want to learn more.

Otherwise, just ignore most of the labs.
They are useless for HW and Exams.

"Debugging on MARS" is important for
debugging your MIPS HW & Project

Lecture 一定要去，

comment_grading:
Pretty standard grading.

Programming project (15%): Done entirely in MIPS (a custom version of MIPS is used for this) where you finish a functions in a simple game and make a flowchart explaining the game functions, difficulty is pretty low, referencing how the given functions work is very helpful in finishing the project.

Midterm (30%): MC questions, and then long questions, doing the past papers is important since the questions are very similar.

Final (40%): Same as midterm, emphasis on the course materials taught after the midterm, more challenging than the midterm due to the difficulty of the material.

comment_grading:
The grading is not as bad as the others say, and is actually quite good.

Project 15%
Homework 15%
Midterm 30%
Final 40%

I got full marks for the project and homework, 0.5 bonus points in the project, and +1.2 SD for both midterms and finals.
The overall course grade was 93.5 -> A+

The exams are very straightforward. No tricky questions. Everything in the exam was covered in the lectures. If you review the materials thoroughly, you can probably score at least 80%. The only reasons you can have points deducted are either because you ran out of time (the exams were one-way-forward) or careless mistakes. Since you can't go back to previous questions in the exam, you may be tempted to triple-check your answers, but don't. You will end up running out of time.

I recommend reserving 3 days for revision for the midterms and finals. Moreover, the exams were open-book, but only printed materials are allowed, so a good cheat sheet will help a lot.

You can get up to 5 points from your project bonus. That's 5 points to the overall course grade, not the project grade, so it is totally worth it. You need to submit a proposal first, but there is no penalty if you can't finish what you propose or decide to work on something outside your proposal, so you can propose as many items as you want. The final bonus grade depends on what you end up being able to implement.

comment_content:
1. Digital Logic: Gates, Combinational Logic, Boolean Algebra, Sequential Logic
2. Data Representation: Binary Integers, Two's Complement, IEEE-754 Floating Point Representation
3. MIPS Instructions: Arithmetic, Branch, Loop, Implementation (Representing instructions in binary format), Procedures, Addressing Modes
4. Computer Arithmetic: Sign Extension, Overflow Detection, ALU, Multiplication Hardware, Division Hardware, (Optional) Floating Point Arithmetic
5. Processor Datapath and Control: The steps taken by computer to execute instruction

7. Memory Hierarchy: Cache Architecture (Block placement and identification), Block Replacement, Write Strategy, (Optional) Virtual Memory

Basically all are new things except the first topic. Need to pay attention to and remember many subtle details.

comment_grading:
This semester both midterm and final have a relatively low mean (both 60 something) because
1. Given the length of both papers, the time limit appears to be insufficient (the instructor announced to give 10 more minutes near the end of both exams)
2. quite a few of the questions are set based on scenarios with which we are not familiar, e.g. tracing an extended JK flip flop (essentially a JKJ flip flop) to find its truth table, a revised multiplication hardware based on combinational logic (not the same as the one in lecture notes), control signals for imaginary MIPS instructions, etc.
3. the above point essentially means the teaching team really wanted to test us more on understanding instead of memorisation (of course you still need to remember certain things...)

In short, if you want to do well in exams, you have to understand comprehensively the concepts they taught (in a way that you can comprehend an entirely new scenario, not just those typical ones in the lecture notes), do fast and do very carefully.

Each topic is equally important, but I would suggest you pay particular attention to the followings:
1. Combinational Logic and Sequential Logic: Some important aspects of these logics are not mentioned in the lecture, but the teaching team may still test you on them. Try to read other textbooks, not the one suggested in this course, to understand more.
2. IEEE-754 Floating Point Representation
3. Addressing Modes (especially PC-relative and Pseudo-direct) in MIPS instructions
4. ALU
5. Processor Control

Statistics:
Midterm (30%): mean 63.27, sd 17
Final (40%): mean 65.13, sd 22

My result:
Midterm: 65.5/100 (+ 0.1SD; I remember I couldn't understand one question on combinational logic even though I spent like 20 minutes to read it. Plus I made many careless mistakes)
Final: 88.5/100 (+ 1SD)
Homework and Project: nearly full mark
Grade: B+ (Overall ~85/100)

The grade is as expected. But given the difficulty to do well in the exams, the grading shall be considered slightly strict.
