comment_content:
COMP2633 offers diverse, week-by-week cybersecurity topics that progress from foundational CTF skills to advanced exploitation techniques. The first two weeks provide basic training with hands-on exercises to familiarize students with the competitive programming environment and fundamental CTF concepts.

The course follows a dual-track system where Track A covers binary exploitation topics (Reverse Engineering, PWN, symbolic execution) while Track B focuses on application security (Web Security, Cryptography, SQL Injections, Forensics).

Topics include advanced exploitation techniques like ROP (Return-Oriented Programming), format string attacks, RSA cryptanalysis, shellcode development, and server-side attacks. This structure exposes students to comprehensive cybersecurity domains used in real CTF competitions.

Week 0: Course Introduction
Week 1: Basic Training - Linux 101
Week 2: Basic Training - Network, Python, and PWN
Week 3: Reverse 101 (Track A), Web Security 101 (Track B)
Week 4: PWN 101 (Track A), Crypto 101 (Track B)
Week 5: Reverse 102 (Track A), SQL Injections 101 (Track B)
Week 6: PWN 102: Shellcode and lazy binding (Track A), Cryptanalysis (Track B)
Week 7: PWN 103 Return Oriented Programming (Track A), Client-Side Attacks (Track B)
Week 8: tryHarder on your midterms
Week 9: PWN 105 Format String Attacks (idk where did PWN 104 go) (Track A), Fantastic RSA and How to Attack Them (Track B)
Week 10: Stack migration (Track A), Server side attacks (Track B)
Week 11: Grab some coffee for continuing your CTF life
Week 12: Symbolic Execution (Track A), Forensics and Steganography (Track B)
comment_teaching:
Dr. Alex Lam does not directly teach the course; instead, COMP4633 students (HKUST Firebird Core members) serve as "Helpers" who lead instruction. While these peer instructors cover fundamental concepts and provide helpful explanations, the course heavily emphasizes self-directed learning.

The helpers demonstrate genuine effort to explain basics and problem-solving approaches, but you must independently bridge the gap between foundational knowledge and the advanced techniques required for homework completion—Google is your best friend. These peer instructors are valuable resources who showcase diverse problem-solving methodologies and can significantly accelerate learning for motivated students.

Since this is an online course, active participation in the online class chat room and the Firebird Discord channel is essential for connecting with experienced members and potential future teammates. Don't be shy—all the "bigbigs" (high-achievers and experienced CTF players) are approachable and friendly. The Firebird community functions like a big family where members share daily experiences and willingly help others when asked. This collaborative environment provides invaluable learning opportunities beyond formal course materials.
comment_workload:
Workload perception varies significantly based on individual goals. Students aiming merely to pass with minimal CTF engagement may find the workload manageable, while those competing for selection into COMP3633 (Firebird school CTF team) face substantially higher demands, so i gave it an average grade: C.

For students with serious competitive ambitions—like me determined to join the Firebird school team—the workload extends far beyond course requirements. These motivated students invest substantial effort into external CTF competitions, applying skills learned from the course and COMP4633 peer instructors to earn rankings and recognition.

The intensive self-study and competition participation, while demanding, yields tangible rewards through competition placements and demonstrates the practical value of knowledge gained from both course materials and advanced student mentors.​

The competitive environment attracts high-achieving students, including Dean's List recipients, creating opportunities for peer learning and collaboration with skilled teammates. Weekly exercises with 2-week deadlines require continuous engagement, and the self-study component demands significant time investment for students unfamiliar with security concepts. The course serves as preparation for competitive cybersecurity events, making the workload proportional to one's competitive ambitions rather than course requirements alone.
comment_grading:
The course uses a Pass/Fail grading system requiring only 60% out of a total 150% to pass. The breakdown is generous:
​
- 50% for class attendance

- 50% for Track A exercises and homework

- 50% for Track B exercises and homework

Although I was on study leave and couldn't officially register, I stayed active and ranked No. 2 on the training platform by solving most challenges. From my experience, you can easily pass just by maintaining full attendance and grabbing the accessible "free flag" exercises without needing to master every hard challenge.

I honestly think being a 0-credit course is a huge advantage. There is absolutely no pressure to fight for a letter grade or view other COMP2633 students as competitors. Instead, the environment encourages everyone to share ideas and help each other grow, since helping a classmate doesn't hurt your own chances of passing.

comment_content:
In this course, there are 2 tracks: track A and track B. The content of the tracks are different. You have to choose at least 1 track.

In Track A, you will learn about reverse and PWN. I choose track B so I doesn't have much to say.

In Track B, you will learn about Web, Crypto and some forensics. If you wish to take track B, taking COMP 2711H will be extremely helpful for Crypto. COMP 2711 also helps as well.

I have to say in the majority of the course, what you learn in class won't directly help you finish you homework, especially the first 2 weeks. You have to conduct your own research online, which is common in CTF competitions. Instead, the teching gives a framework for organizing knowledge, as well as some directions for you to conduct your own researches.
comment_teaching:
The teaching have little impact on your learning. At the end of the day, self-learning is required and encouraged.

The courses are teached by COMP 4633 students, all of them are highly skilled CTF players. The lecture takes around 2 hours for a track. I don't suggest you taking both tracks, but if you do, there are recording of the lessons in the course web.

There is also a welcoming CTF event (which is really fun) and a discord server for this course. I highly suggest you to join both, especially the discord server where you can worship bigbigs and ask questions about CTF directly. You may also find writeups of past assignments and enchance your knowledge as well.
comment_workload:
I love CTF, so I think the workload is moderate. But if you don't like CTF, this will be pure hell.

You have 2-3 questions a week for 1 track. You will never know how long does it takes to finish one, and the problem-solving process can be extremely discouraging (for example week 7 track b hw2).

However, the questions are quite interesting and challenging. I am sure that solving these questions are benefitial.
comment_grading:
Pass or fail only, so chill if you accidentally enroll this course and fail to drop. To get a pass, you will need to get at least 60% of the total scores. You can achieve this by handing in all attendance flag and homework flag.

For those who are hoping to achieve more (namely COMP 3633), pass is not enough. You have to perform well in Firebird internal CTF.

comment_content:
Course content can be found on https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2633/

This course covers a lot of topics, from binary exploits to cryptography and web web security. It will certainly be tough if you have not much previous knowledge (As it's like a bunch of courses scrambled into one).

You can chose from two tracks:
Track A - binary exploits, reverse engineering
Track B - Web, cryptography, forensics
After add-drop period, each track will include a 2 to 3-hour online lecture every week, although occasionally there will be breaks (e.g. during mid-terms, competitions)
comment_teaching:
Teaching is done by COMP4633 students, who are very talented and actively participate large-scale CTF competitions.

There will be 1 online lecture per week (except for break periods). Usually it's 4-6 hours long at night. Technically, you do not have to attend the lectures and you can watch the lecture videos online. Either way, I suggest you actually follow the lectures, and get hands-on experience as they teach. Only by that will you be able to learn valuable CTF skills.

The helpers are very helpful and they will assist you on Discord when you don't mind reaching them for help in private chats.
comment_workload:
Tldr: The more you want to learn, the higher the workload

If you want to complete most of the challenges (For people who aim for taking COMP3633, this is a must), you will need to spend a lot of time every week. Some homework challenges will surely take hours to solve.

However, if all you need is a pass, the workload for this course is low.  

Considering this is a 0-credit course, I will rate this an E.
comment_grading:
Grading is solely based on online CTF exercises completion.

For every week, usually, there will be:
* 2 Attendance challenges - 1 in track A, 1 in track B, you only need to solve 1. They will guide you step-by-step on lectures and they often provide you the flag (the solution) during lectures.
* 1-2 Exercises per track - they will lead you to complete it during lectures and they sometimes provide you the flag
* 1-2 Homework per track

Completing all attendance will give you 50 marks, and (exercises + homework) worth 50 marks per track.
The passing score is 60, so you do not need to complete a lot of tasks to actually get a pass.

The homework, especially those after mid-term periods, are significantly hard to solve. In this term, there is one homework challenges left unsolved (No one was able to solve it) and many challenges with less than 10 solves.

comment_content:
Since every other review is warning people who haven't played CTF, I write this review for the future students who have already played CTF, as I have (poorly as it may be) in secondary school.
If my review isn't sufficient, you can dm me @wylited on discord.

Alongside COMP2711H, this is the best course I've taken in my first semester at HKUST.

This course is pretty invaluable in improving my skills in CTF and competitive cybersecurity, as I have never formally studied these topics before.

The setup for the exercises and homework is excellent, and they provide two tracks A and B to accommodate for specialized players.
Track A: pwn, reverse
Track B: web, crypto, forensics

Check the attached image for the exact contents.

I think that both tracks are pretty introductory, if you really want to compete in the HKUST Firebird team, you should be trying to solve both tracks and learn as much as possible.
comment_teaching:
Although Alex is a great guy, the teaching is conducted by the very handsome COMP4633 students, these students are very experienced in CTF and highly skilled at the fields that they teach.

Although the lectures may last quite a long time (4 hours on Zoom call from 6 to 10 pm) I recommend you don't watch the full 4 hours directly and watch the recording instead.

I also recommend you pause the lecture and try doing the attendance and exercise work by yourself.

The helpers (don't call them TA's) of comp2633 are really quite helpful in helping you complete exercise and homework if you are struggling. They are quite approachable on discord and even during lessons, the vibe is quite chill and some students (not me because I am sleeping at 10pm) may stay back to chat with the lecturer.

I highly recommend taking the time to socialize with these "bigbig" as their experience and advice is invaluable.
comment_workload:
Just passing this course is trivial, copy flag from the lecture videos.

If you want to 100% the exercises and the homework, the workload of this course is equivalent to a 4 credit course.

I took this course along 18 other credits and despite having background in CTF I didn't have enough time to complete all the tasks.

My recommendation for completing the workload is to do the exercises by yourselves while referring to the slides and properly understand the content, and then try to do the homework. Usually the technique needed to solve the homework is covered in the slides.
Otherwise, you can ask the helper in charge for that week about your solve strategy, they won't directly give you the answer, but they will give you some guiding hints that are very useful.

comment_content:
This course covers a lot of the basics (and a bit more) for CTF competitions. It’s great for beginners in cybersecurity, but if you’re aiming to join the CTF team, you’ll either need some prior skills or be ready to work hard. Topics include:
Reverse Engineering
Pwn (Binary Exploitation)
Cryptography
Web Security
Digital Forensics

If you have a CSE account, you can check the official course page
https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp2633/
comment_teaching:
The course is taught by senior students who are solid at explaining the material. They use good PPT slides, and you can ask questions during Zoom sessions (recordings are available) or on Discord.

While they are knowledgeable and effective at explaining concepts, they don’t really teach you how to solve actual CTF problems. At their level, most questions are just pattern matching. For beginners, the problems may feel like black boxes. You’ll need to learn how to ask better questions and break problems down to find a direction.

Additionally, while the course introduces some tools, better ones are often available online, so you should google it yourself.
comment_workload:
The workload is chill if you’re just here to learn and have fun, expect to spend 1-2 hours a week watching the Zoom sessions and submitting the attendance flag (literally just copy).

If you want to improve or join the CTF team, plan on completing 3-4 tasks weekly, which might take 0.5–2 hours each, depending on your skill level. If you’re not proud of your ability to "Google it", just do the attendance flag.
comment_grading: Only Pass/Fail. Attending the Zoom sessions and submitting the attendance flag is enough to pass. However, if you’re aiming to progress to COMP 3633 or join the CTF team, you’ll need to put in significantly more effort.

comment_teaching:
" Is you to help others but not others to help you " "No one will teach you knowledge in the industry " said by the professor in the first lesson . Yep , you can imagine what of kinds of teaching of this course.

It is all about self-learning . When you want to seek help from the senior students , the most possible response you get is "try harder" or "lay harder" . You can also get many ridiculous jokes instead of advices .

comment_workload:
"It is a 0 credit course but the workload is like a 4 credit course " said by the professor . Yep , it is the workload .

You have to finished 1 attendance exercise to prove that you have attend the lesson and 3 HW each week. The attendance exercise is quite easy . You can just the follow the steps in the zoom recording and do it (yep , just copy cat) . However,  the HW is very difficult . comment_content:
This course basically teaches you how to play Capture the Flag (CTF), a kind of cybersecurity competition (as if it isn't clear enough from the course name).

For the first 2 weeks, there are basic trainings that familiarize you with basic CTF skills, including Regex, Linux commands, Python, etc. After that, the course is split in to 2 tracks, namely track A and track B, which are about different topics. Track A is about reverse and binary (pwn), while track B is about web, cryptography (crypto) and a little little little bit of forensics towards the end. Forgive me for being unable to provide detailed teaching content of each category since I don't want to be wrenched by helpers.

Us 2633 students joke about this course being a CS major speedrun, which honestly isn't very far from reality. The content is very broad and deep, so you might want to try really hard and spend a lot of time to understand them if you want to get anything more than a pass.

Little appendix about some terms (even if you don't understand them now, you will eventually understand if you enroll into this course):
Flag: a string that you need to find and submit in order 7to get scores
Reverse: reversing codes from binary files like .exe and find a correct input to get the flag
Pwn: gaining partial / full control of remote systems by exploiting unsafe codes
Web: getting private information by exploiting vulnerabilities in websites / servers
Cryptography: the cryptography you learn in 3632 and/or 4631 idk (not cryptocurrency)
Forensics: retrieving deleted / hidden information inside files
comment_teaching:
Alex is not the one who teaches, it's actually COMP 4633 students (helpers) who do their presentations and teaches us. (they are paying to teach instead of being paid to teach so please send love to your helpers if you are going to enroll in the future UwU :heart::heart::heart:) All of them tried their absolute best, and the powerpoints are very rich in content and clear enough to understand. Unfortunately, they are most likely still not enough for your homework (welcome to the world of cybersecurity), so Google is your best friend.

Lectures are conducted in zoom, and they last for 4 hours (1800-2200). A lecture is separated into 2 halves, 1800-2000 is for track A, 2000-2200 is for track B, so you don't have to attend the whole lecture. After each lecture, lecture videos will be uploaded to the course website, so you can choose to watch videos instead of attending lectures. I highly suggest attending lectures though since you can chill with other students and spam :place_of_worship: in zoom chat.

The overall lecture atmosphere is very casual and relaxing, and you can chat freely with other students, helpers and even management team members who pop out of nowhere during and after lectures. There is a discord server for the HKUST Firebird CTF team and COMP X633 series, which you are highly encouraged to join. You can easily contact other students and helpers to ask for help with in-class exercises and homework, but unless you are completely lost, they usually respond with "try harder" or one or more :blobcattryharder: emojis.
comment_workload:
If you want to find out how the coursework looks like, I suggest you visit the intro website and take a look at the challenges in it: https://intro.firebird.sh/challenges?

==========
"We expect a 3-credit,
COMP 4000-level workload
in this 0-credit course."
~ Alex, at the start of 2633
==========

Alex literally told us that the workload is a solid E. The old course codes for the COMP X633 series are COMP 4901_, which kind of explains the high workload of this course.

All exercises and homework are CTF challenges. For the first 2 weeks, there are 2 exercises and 3 homework each week. For the rest of the course, there are 1 exercise and 1-2 homework for each track each week. Exercise flags can usually be copied from the lecture videos, but you are mostly on your own for homework. For a CTF noob like me, the easier ones usually take me multiple hours, and the harder ones take me multiple days, so unless you are already a CTF god, don't expect yourself to be able to finish all coursework.

In short, only enroll if you are interested. And if you are interested but don't want to be like me who sacrificed all COMP 1021 lesson time for dealing with CTF, you might not want to set your goals too high in this course.
comment_grading:
The highest you can get in this course is 150%, 50% from attendance, 50% from track A and 50% from track B. You only need 60% out of 150% to pass the course so it is extremely easy to pass, but it's quite difficult to get really high scores.

For attendance, you are expected to hand in an attendance flag, which is shown in lecture videos, within 3 days after a lecture, and each flag counts 5%.

Coursework of each week counts 10%, which includes both demo-ed exercises and homework. For each week in the first 2 weeks, 10% is distributed evenly between all challenges. For each week afterwards, track A and track B each counts 5%, and the 5% is evenly distributed between all challenges in the track.

Technically, if you just want to pass, you only need to submit all attendance flags and copy some exercise flags, which don't really take much time. However, if you want to score higher, it's a completely different story. Check the workload part for details.

TL;DR:
Total 150%, Passing 60%/150% (0 cred P/F)
Attendance 50%
Basic training 20%
Track A 40%
Track B 40%

P.S.: If you are interested in 3633 or 4633, you will have to score really really big in 2633 to increase your chances to get into them, as 3633 and 4633 are invitation-based.

comment_content:
The following key topics are taught:
- Propositional Logic: Including operators, equivalences, proofs using laws (without truth tables), and rules of inference.
- Predicate Logic: Including quantifiers (∀, ∃), expressing statements with predicates, negation of quantified statements, and formal proofs.
- Sets and Functions: Including set operations (intersection, union, complement, subsets), proofs (direct and contrapositive), countability/uncountability of sets (e.g., natural numbers, functions from N to N, binary strings), and bijections.
- Counting Principles: Including permutations, combinations, binomial coefficients, arrangements with restrictions (e.g., adjacency, no two alike adjacent), unordered pairs, palindromes, and paths in graphs (without reusing edges).
- Combinatorial Proofs: Proving identities using combinatorial reasoning (e.g., involving subsets and distinct elements).
- Inclusion-Exclusion Principle: Applied to counting problems (e.g., digits in numbers with specific constraints).
- Pigeonhole Principle: Applied to grid-based problems (e.g., colored cells sharing corners).
- Probability: Including basic probabilities, binomial distributions, and scenarios with independent events (e.g., catching flies).
- Random Variables: Including expectation (E), variance (var), linearity of expectation, independence, and comparisons (e.g., E(X^2) vs. E(XY) for dice rolls).
- Graphs
- MOD
- Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)

comment_teaching:
Papa has a really strong accent. Due to this, he usually falls behind Kenneth in terms of pacing. In fact, Kenneth's classes got 1 week of break in the very end, while Papa's class just got into the second half of graph theory, and uses the very final lecture for a review along with a past paper.

However, you can still access Papa's slides and watch his RVC recordings(well, this also apply to Kenneth's) for his lectures, so if you're already confident with Kenneth, you can still know the extra stuff in Papa's classes, and vice versa. Besides the lectures, in-class exs and HWs, there are also some other helpful resources, such as Kenneth's old lecture videos, tons of supplementary exersices(reviews for midterm & final, and extra problems on several topics), Papa's additional slides as I've mentioned before, tutorial problems, past papers, and several problems from TAs and UGTAs before the final exam.

Nonetheless, I'd also recommend the past papers for deeper understanding and problem solving of the content

comment_content: Teaching about logics, sets, counting, number theory, algorithms, recursion and graphs. The difficulty on the content is a little bit hard, but still acceptable. Also there is in-class exercise during each lesson, which contribute to 10% of your grade.
comment_teaching: Although he has a quite strong accent, he used his heart to teach and added many contents that are not in the original ppt but are really useful
comment_workload: 4-5 homeworks in total, so about 1 to 2 per month. Each homework takes about 3 to 5 hours to finish but some questions in the homework is a bit tough.

comment_content:
Topics:
Logic, Sets and Functions, Counting, Probability, Number Theory, Algorithms, Induction and Recursion, Graphs

The content of the course is not difficult if you take your time to understand the content. Some topics (like graphs, algorithm and number theory) can be tricky if this is the first time you encounter content like this (especially the proofs for these topics). I recommend taking a look at the textbook in the syllabus and do practice questions in there, and learning some common tricks for each topic.
comment_teaching:
The teaching is good, but occasionally you might not understand Dimitris's accent, and he tends to make mistakes in his derivation which can leave you a bit confused. Also occasionally you would have to do in class exercise out of class because the professor went overtime.
(also because of the in class exercise, even though there is technically no attendance, you need to attend your lectures)
comment_workload: There are four homeworks and they are medium in length. Each take maybe around 2-3 hours to finish?
comment_grading:
In-class Exercises 10%
Homework 25%
Mid-Term Exam 25%

A relatively easy 4-credit course (honestly feels like 3 credits) because I think most people good at the subject do 2711H instead. Many questions in the mid term and final are simply going through mechanical steps.

comment_content: The course covered a wide range of foundational topics in discrete mathematics, including logic, set theory, functions, relations, combinatorics, graph theory, and recurrence relations
comment_teaching: The instructor demonstrated strong command of the material and consistently emphasized conceptual clarity. Lectures were well-organized, often beginning with intuitive examples before moving into formal definitions and proofs.
comment_workload: The workload was demanding but manageable for a comp course
comment_grading: The grading scheme balanced exams, homework assignments, and in-class exercise. Homework was graded fairly, with detailed feedback that highlighted both strengths and areas for improvement.

comment_teaching:
Professor Kenneth Leung demonstrates a remarkable commitment to student engagement in
COMP2711 L2. Although the classroom is full, he pays careful attention to each individual to
help them immerse themselves in the lecture. His integration of gaming, puzzles, and other
compelling questions with the course's complex subject matter is highly effective. Professor
Kenneth Leung further supports learning through his readiness to answer questions postlecture and via Telegram, and through thoughtfully designed assignments and in-class
exercises. Providing all learning materials at the semester's outset is another testament to his
dedication, allowing students the flexibility to learn more effectively.
comment_workload: In class exercises are not difficult to handle. Homework is much more challenging, if you really want to learn the insight of each detail, you should put some time and effort into self-learning by reading textbooks, doing exercises. Add oil! Overall, it is ok to handle.

comment_content: The content that is taught in COMP2711 is fundamental to computer science and provides a comprehensive spectrum of knowledge. It solidifies our foundation of mathematics and computer science and really trains our brain to function in a mathematical way.
comment_teaching: This lecturer is quite helpful as he actually teaches the content and explains the hard sections of the content. He would use whiteboard to draw diagrams to help us visualize the complex knowledge to ease it. Whenever we have questions, he would also answer the questions and address the needs of the students.
comment_workload: This course has quite a heavy workload as the materials are taught in a fast manner such that there is loads of self-study needed. Aside from the self study, there are mandatory in-class exercises to do in lectures. There is also homework every once a few weeks, but it is quite busy!
comment_grading: The professors are lenient on grading as some may have missed the first few exercises due to add/drop period, and they would allow late submissions and still count the scores. Other than that, the homeworks are also promptly graded, which is decent.

comment_content: The course content covers core knowledge points including logic and proof, sets, functions, algorithms, counting, induction, recursion, probability theory, and graph theory. In addition, the course introduces propositional logic and the use of truth tables, which strengthen students’ ability to reason and construct rigorous arguments. This course has weak relevance to calculus, and the logical thinking it cultivates is of great help to the development of problem-solving skills in subsequent programming learning.
comment_teaching: The instructor explains the concepts in detail, emphasizing both theoretical understanding and practical applications. The teaching pace is moderate, and in-class examples are provided to reinforce comprehension. However, the in-class exercises are sometimes repetitive, focusing more on basic skills rather than challenging applications. Moreover, the results of in-class exercises are counted toward the final grade.
comment_workload: Each class includes in-class exercises that must be submitted on the same day. These exercises help students consolidate what they have learned, but may pose some pressure for those with weaker mathematical backgrounds. The professor has mentioned that they will try to help such students “survive” the exams. [However, if you are not good at mathematics or have weak logical thinking skills, this course is definitely not as easy as you might imagine.]
comment_grading: The grading scheme is relatively clear, consisting of homework, quizzes, a midterm, and a final exam. The exam difficulty is moderate, primarily testing fundamental understanding and basic problem-solving skills rather than highly complex questions. Overall, students who keep up with the course should be able to achieve good grades.

comment_content: the course content covers core knowledge points including logic and proof, sets, functions, algorithms, counting, induction, recursion, probability theory, and graph theory. This course has weak relevance to calculus, and the logical thinking it cultivates is of great help to the development of thinking in subsequent programming learning.
comment_teaching: each class has a large knowledge capacity, and the teacher's teaching pace is slightly fast. Supporting exercises are arranged in class, but the difficulty of the exercises is relatively low. In addition, students' attendance will affect their final grades.
comment_workload: the course has abundant teaching content, and the actual teaching content far exceeds the scope mentioned in the course description. Students need to complete exercise tasks in class, and due to the complexity of the course concepts, they need to spend more time reviewing after class to consolidate the knowledge they have learned.
comment_grading: I don't know but the course involves many concepts, but the overall difficulty is not high. This course has low requirements for prerequisite courses, and the learning situation of prerequisite courses has little impact on the study of this course.

comment_content: as a 4 credit course it contains a lot of seperate topic, but trust me most of them wont be tested in mt and final (including plenty of proof of thorem), so a good approach is to have a quick look on the past paper then u will know what is actually important for a good grading
comment_teaching:
Kenneth is a very chill guy and having his lecture is like Dayo Wong's stand-up comedy.

TA is quite helpful and you can ask any question regarding assignment or learning in the telegram group, they are more than willing to help.
comment_workload:
As a year 1 business school student with zero programming background and knowledge (even at the end of the semester I dont know how to code), I would say learning in this course at the beginning will be very tormenting as it includes material in programming like algorithim. Also I need the help of ai in some of the in class exercises as I cant follow the class material at the beginning. But once you re watch the recording and try to revise them regularly, the knowledge itself is not that hard, even though some of them do, trust me they wont appear in the exam.

So even zero coding experience can get a+ in this course, nothing to complain. I think those non year 1 engineering students will have an advantage in term of workload of learning, but that's all, they wont have much advantage in term of exam at least for this semester.
comment_grading: This semester the mt and final is extremely easy compared to past semester and you can tell it from the mean of mt and final. However, given that I all just slightly higher than uq in both mt and final and even up A+ which I thought should be A-, what can u say, what a wonderful grading (4 credit course even has a higher weighing in CGA)

comment_content: Kinda difficult. Discrete math itself is not an easy topic.
comment_teaching: Good. Lectures are interesting and relevant. I like the examples and stuff. It's like it makes discrete math less alien like. There's also fun puzzles and games. But I feel like the teaching speed is a bit too fast for me as I don't have a background in discrete math.
comment_workload: Medium. Have to go home and review some topics so I don't forget them next class. And also because the teaching is fast. Maybe previewing content is a good idea.
comment_grading: Very competitive because there's a lot of CS students

comment_content:
Syllabus:

Pre-midterm:
Logic:
- Propositional logic
- Predicate logic
- Inference and proofs
Sets and functions:
- Injection, Surjection, Bijection
- Cardinality
Induction and recursion:
- Induction, strong induction, double induction, structural induction
- Recursive definition and step
Number Theory:
- Modular arithmetic
- GCDs and congruences (Chinese remainder theorem)
- Cryptography (Extended Euclidean algorithm, RSA)

Post-midterm:
Algorithms:
- Big Θ, big O, big Ω notations
Counting:
- Basic counting (Sum rule, product rule)
- Permutations and combinations
- Binomial coefficients (Pascal's identity)
- Inclusion-exclusion principle
Probability:
- Intro to probability (Expected value, variance)
- Conditional probability (Baye's theorem)
- Random variables (Binomial distribution, geometric distribution)
Graphs:
- Graphs (Bipartite, K, C)
P.S. order of topics may change from semester to semester, but the overall content doesn't change much.

This course teaches the fundamentals of logical reasoning and such, good and useful content.
Favours people who took M1/M2 in DSE (or just people with background in statistics/probability in general)
comment_teaching:
First half is taught by Yi Ke. Yi Ke's teaching makes already convoluted topics even harder to understand. You really gotta study hard for midterms because you can't rely on his teachings alone.

Second half is taught by Dr. James Tsz Him Cheung. James teaches way better than Yi Ke and the things he talks about actually makes sense. Also eager to help students after lectures end.

Arman is one of the TAs which is always fun, but tutorials don't count attendance so can be skipped.
comment_workload:
5 HWs with varying lengths, some HWs (*cough cough* induction) will take you forever to do, while others can be done within 2-3 hours at most.

Since the counting and probability taught are very fundamental, people with any background in statistics will find those topics extremely simple.
For people without background, a lot of extra work might be required to catch up to people who do.

For midterms and finals, not much can be done for revision other than doing past papers due to most questions being proof-based (other than probability and counting). Cheat sheets are only there to help you remember the sheer number of laws and theorems learnt throughout the course.

comment_content: COMP 2711 focuses on essential discrete mathematics concepts tailored for computer science applications, covering enumeration techniques, basic number theory, logic and proofs, recursion and recurrences, probability theory, and graph theory. Students will learn counting principles, permutations, and combinations alongside foundational number theory concepts like divisibility and modular arithmetic, crucial for algorithm design and cryptography. The course emphasizes logical reasoning through various proof techniques, including induction, and explores recursion with methods to solve recurrences relevant to algorithm efficiency. Additionally, it introduces probability theory, addressing random variables and distributions, as well as graph theory, encompassing graph algorithms for searching and optimization. Overall, the course equips students with the mathematical skills necessary for advanced topics in computer science and enhances their problem-solving abilities in a technology-driven landscape.

comment_teaching:
The teaching of this course is fabulous for me.
Lecture: Kenneth is good at teaching stuff like that. During the lecture, he can provide interesting examples to help us understand some concepts or theorems.
“I’m bigger than Desmond.” — Kenneth
He is very passionate about teaching and students feel engaged during his lectures. However, his pace is faster compared to other sections, and he sometimes directly skips some slides he deems not important or not difficult. So a brief preview before class is recommended.
Tutorial:
Tutorial is also engaging. This semester, Arman attended most of the tutorials. When he came, we played some interesting yet tricky games. Those who can solve the game can have small prizes. Besides that, the questions in tutorial notes are also well-crafted and suitable as an exercise.
Adding:
They have a telegram group chat where we can discuss exercise questions and share ideas with others, useful for assignments and revision.

If you like skipping lectures, you’d better ask your friends to send the in-class exercise to you. If you aim for good grades, you need to spend more time in the exercise so that you can familiarize yourself with what formula/algorithm/rule to apply during exams.
comment_grading:
COMP course gives COMP grade— wide B range, but fierce competition for A range, so I may just leave a B here.
10% In-class exercise
25% HW
25% Midterm
40% Final
In-class exercise: It’s more like attendance since the exercise itself is very easy to deal with, and Kenneth even gives some hints so that everyone in the class can manage it. Only 80% of the exercises are counted for grade, which means you can still get the total 10% course grade if you miss 20% of the classes.
HW: totally 4 hw sets and each of them contains around 8 problems. You can spend 2-3 days(6-8 hrs) working through all the questions you can, then discuss the questions that you can’t solve in the group chat, TAs are very friendly and supportive to give hints. Assignments are made easier compared to previous semesters, with the mean of each set around 95.
Midterm: 2 hrs in total, containing around 8 questions. Every question is LQ, you need to provide all the steps of your solution. They will give you scores as long as your logic makes sense. The most challenging questions are about counting, combinatorial proofs. They made the questions easier compared to previous semesters, so the mean is kinda high—68, and SD is 20.
Final: 3 hrs in total, comprises around 10 questions. The format is similar to midterm. The most challenging questions are about probability, modular arithmetic, and algorithms. Final is also easier compared to previous semesters—mean is 70 and SD is 19.
me: total is around 98%, I will change “grading” into E if I don’t get A+. I’m not here to flex and I genuinely hope you guys can all do better than me, so good luck.

COMP2711 is a required course for DSCT/COMP students. It focuses on a wide range of concepts, but not in-depth, related to discrete mathematical tools in computer science.

If you have taken MATH 2411, you might find this course to be relatively easy.

However, taking this course in the summer term might not be a good idea, as the time is insufficient for revision. We end class on Friday, but we have the final exam the next day, which is Saturday.
There are around 600 slides waiting for you.

Most of the workload comes from completing past papers. You will need to spend about three hours on each past paper after finishing a day of lectures, which lasts three and a half hours.

comment_content:
The course covers the same topics as in a regular semester (they didn't even skip graph theory like they did in the spring semester). But the homeworks and exams are extremely easy compared to that of normal semester. So it might be less preferable to someone who prefers rigor. But I assume those guys will take 2711H anyways.
Although there are many topics, they are very introductory and don't go much in depth (though the questions can be quite challenging), so you would likely need to take other courses such as MATH 4141, MATH 3121, and MATH 2421 to gain a deeper understanding. I believe there is significant overlap with MATH 2421, so if you are a DSCT, COSC, or just a student interested in these subjects, you should definitely consider taking them together.

One interesting finding: The syllabus is an exact copy of UC Berkeley's Math 55 https://math.berkeley.edu/courses/overview/lowerdivcourses/math55
This is actually a good thing since UCB is known for being a strong CS school, and I don't mind because I don't have to pay $60,000(USD) per year and can still learn the same content.
comment_teaching:
On the first day of lecture, I remember Professor Tai saying, "I have taught this course 10 times," so my expectations were quite high. Unfortunately, they fell short. She struggles with pacing her lectures, frequently stumbles over words, and as a result, might skip crucial parts if she runs out of time or takes an extra 10 minutes to finish.

If it weren’t for the in-class quizzes, I would have only attended the tutorials. In fact, I highly recommend attending all tutorials led by Arman. He is clearly passionate about the subject, provides key insights, and emphasizes intuition in his explanations. He might also drop occasional hints about questions that could appear on exams (as he did for the midterms) during the tutorials.
comment_workload: I believe the workload in the summer semester is lighter, as the homework, midterms, and finals are much easier. So, it really depends on the grade you’re aiming for.

I am writing this review just after taking my final exam, so the final results haven't been released yet (I’ll update this later if I remember). There will be some exchange students also taking this course, so the mean might not accurately reflect the true UST average. Still, it’s quite competitive to score above +1 SD, as indicated by the high standard deviation and self-selection bias (students who choose to take a course during summer vacation may be more motivated than the general population, but that's just my guess).

Midterms:

    Mean: 60.84
    Median: 61
    High: 99
    Upper Quartile: 82.5
    Low: 0
    Lower Quartile: 41

While these scores might seem high compared to a normal semester, the questions were also significantly easier. The true UST mean (if you exclude exchange students) might have been 65+.

Note: I don't hold any prejudice against exchange students; they often spend less time studying and don't stress about grades as much as UST students, since they want to explore Hong Kong.

For this semester, more weight was given to quizzes, but the way the professor conducted them was less than satisfactory. For instance, she would allocate only 3 minutes for some questions that clearly required 5+ minutes of calculations. Additionally, some students skipped lectures and relied on friends to notify them when quizzes would be held. I noticed this because one time there were only 38 students in the class, yet 44 respondents after the quiz ended (simple pigeonhole principle). Given how quickly some students submitted their quizzes and got 100, I suspect they used ChatGPT, which is clearly unfair since quizzes are worth 18% of the grade.

comment_content:
I may be biased as I was taught under Mr. Leung's teaching, but the content was not too difficult. The topics taught were:
Logic, Combinations, Probability, Number Theory, Algorithms, Graphs
In terms of difficulty, (in my opinion) they would be arranged (from easiest to hardest)
Logic < Graphs < Algorithms < Number Theory < Combinations < Probability
The topics taught in:
Logic-- are relatively easy, just make sure not to make careless mistakes for these questions
Graphs-- are mostly "proof" questions. They won't ask you much about graph theory as it will be discussed more in year 3
Algorithms-- understand clearly what big O,theta, and omega are, and you're good to go
Number Theory questions are usually about decrypting texts and stuff like that, which are things that you can memorise. So these questions are practically free marks (unless you're careless)
Combinations and Probability-- now these questions are always tricky. A particular TA named Arman always creates creative and tricky questions. You cannot do these questions through sheer memorisation. You have to use your brain and aimlessly think of methods, which is why it is the hardest.

Comp2711 has a telegram group where you can ask questions to the TAs, and usually get an instant response, which I love.
comment_teaching:
I find Kenneth Leung's teachings to be very interesting. He is always very happy when he discusses the topics at hand. He makes these very simple analogies on certain complex topics to make us better understand them. I believe his teaching style is excellent, clear to understand as well as entertaining.

The TAs were all very helpful, especially Arman. Arman, from my experience, replies a lot more in the telegram group than other TAs. He sometimes sends custom questions to the group as extra practice questions for the exam. These custom questions may be hints to certain questions in the exam, so make sure to pay attention to what Arman writes. But Arman is also infamous for making hard questions in exams so :thumbs_up:
comment_workload:
In terms of the frequency of assignments, it is not a lot. There are only 6 homework to hand in. However, most of the time, the homework would usually take 1 week to finish. I would often find myself doing the homework overnight (like a proper UST student). But I wouldn't say it is "stressful" as there are only 6 homework to hand in.

It's good to keep in mind that the "complexity" of the homework questions is MUCH harder than those in the exams. A lot of us were very scared by the midterm because we thought the midterm questions were as hard as the homework ones, but they weren't really.

comment_workload:
Workload is quite heavy. Homeworks are immense and took me 1-3 days to finish, the techniques and theorems may demand a lot of time to digest.

It is clearly aimed at students without a strong background in informatics or mathematics competitions: if you are just a student who got A- or above in your MATH1014 course, workload will be high due to the homeworks and the trade-off mentioned.

Despite the fact that your background affect your performance significantly, the main purpose of the course is to teach you solve basic mathematical problems applied for computer science. So, since they are not really overloaded with the concepts, the key factor affecting your grade is your accuracy since every single mistake is costly in terms of scores and penalties are generally harsh.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme for the term:

20% Assignments
35% Midterm
45% Final

Examinations include both proof-based questions and calculations regarding the topics mentioned above. Proof-based questions are not complicated as long as you are familiar with the content and structure your proofs in a precise and appropriate manner. Students should review all the tutorials, past papers and PPTs before the exam - and it will be fine. In addition, homeworks are designed for the preparation to the exams since the basic solution techniques are the same for both HW and exams.

Despite the description in the syllabus, letter grades are assigned according to the curve. The grading policy is a trade-off. On the one hand, A-range is approximately 55-60%. On the other hand, means and medians for the course are high (roughly about 80 and 85, respectively).

Therefore, if you do not have any profound understanding of the concepts before the course, you might well get A- if you put in some effort and B+ without too much effort because B+ range is also wide. However, the competition for solid A and A+ is enormously fierce since the vast majority of the students are those who are familiar with the concepts well like informatics or math Olympiad participants, even IOI/IMO.

comment_teaching:
Arya is the reason I come to ust instead of skipping lectures because he teaches concepts super clearly, so I don't have to read the slides again to understand the content. He is also very enthusiastic and passionate, and his lessons are quite engaging. The best part about him is that he tries to present proofs insightfully. For example, he uses Euler's totient function to show how someone can come up with RSA cryptography instead of just restating the ppt proof.

Tbh tho if you're a fast learner or have previously learnt the materials in primary school then Arya's style might not suit you because his pace is quite slow for an honors course. Prof Arya’s teaching is great, really passionate and cares about math beauty (especially in combinatorial proofs), despite previewing ppt before each lecture, he always manages to say something I never thought of, and he tells a lot of jokes, (one time accidentally say “what the f…” and took it back hahahah) Also, he and the TAs reply to emails fast.

Update: His teaching gets better as the stuff gets harder. After mid terms, we started combinatorics and probability. I actually learnt the probability properly in here but not in ISOM 2500 (business statistics) which I learnt nothing there. Also, he asks us to first make a guess, and then do the math rigorously so ensure that we are thinking on the right track, this mindset is really good and makes us do maths more mindfully, really really appreciate his teaching!!

Update (towards the end of sem): Prof Arya didn’t manage to finish all of graph theory. As set theory was excluded as well (but the concepts related to injection and surjection are included in combinatorics), we can see that 2711H covers less materials than 2711, but has more in depth analysis of other key topics.

(Update): on the last day of the course he asked if the materials are too difficult, but then he said but it’s too late to drop the course. What a good way to end the course. Hahaha
comment_workload:
2 assignments before mt and 2 before finals, released 14 days before submission and you know exactly when they will be released in the course syllabus.
Before mt, as I have some prior knowledge, I usually finish the assignments within 2 days after releasing, so the workload is light for me, but things are getting much harder after mid term, so wish me luck.

(Update): hw 3,4 are wild. I have no idea about half of the questions and I’m so cooked. It took me 5 days to finish each of them, and the solution is amazingly simple and elegant. (Btw, it is usually the case that you can finish one problem with half a page, if you’re writing pages of calculation,  you’re definitely on the wrong track, or at least, not the optimal track) Also, the assignments perfectly complement the materials from the lecture and extend it a little bit, I can feel the professors passion about utilising every opportunity to help us learn more and deeper.
comment_grading:
Mid term:
Mean: 80.71, sd: 17.2, median: 85, UQ: 97, mode: 100, yes, 10 people out of 90 got 100 marks. It’s insane, I shouldn’t be here.
The grading of questions are really really lenient, try to write some definitions out even if you have entirely no clue. I was one step away from finishing the last question, but they regarded my answer to be totally correct.

Before finals, Prof Arya announced that the final would be super hard as we performed too well in mid terms (he wanted the mean to be 70 but we got 80, so he would make the final mean at around 55-60). Actually quite scared, let’s see what happens.

Final exam: mean 75.68, sd 20.68, median 80, UQ 90.5.
However, despite Prof Arya saying it would be difficult, the final exam was very easy, I’d say much easier than hw, I struggled a lot with the assignments, and after struggling, you’d find the exam paper very easy. However, please make sure to understand the tricks from the assignment as they will appear exactly in your final, and he even have us doing it twice in the last 2 Qs. This year, 2711H paper is way easier than that of 2711. There’s really no reason not to take this.

2711 is 2.5h for 11Q and 2711H is 3h for 8Q. Also, we were not tested on materials before mt at all but 2711 did. We didn’t have graph at all but 2711 did. So now you understand the difficulty of 2711H, plus 50% A range, is there any reason not to take this?

My score:
Assignment (20%): 20/20 (mean is around 18)
Mid term (35%): 97/100 (I forgot the proof of gcd)
Final (45%): 100/100 (really easy if you understand all pp and hw)
Total: 98.95%
Grade: A+ (I definitely cannot get A+ in regular 2711)

Please note that they care a lot about how you present your proofs/steps. Even for non-proof questions, it’s the safest to write down all the steps and formulae with justification, and explain how you got each step to the next step with a very short sentence or a bracket next to the steps to indicate some properties. As for algebraic steps, they deduct marks if you skip a lot of them as they think you may not know how things cancel out or combine, you just see the final answer (proof questions) and pretend you did it

comment_teaching:
I really like the way Amir teaches. His lectures start with understanding a problem and then go on to solving it, so that knowledge can be presented in a form suitable for entering the brain. He gave almost every theorem understandable proof, and the matching examples are sufficient, multi-angle and not excessive.
So I enjoyed his class.

As for the formal assignments, they should be handed in latex, which I think is good practice of latex ability.
I also like the process of doing homework. Difficult questions can get students to discuss spontaneously.
A wrong question got me thinking for a long time, but it wasn't that necessary.

At first I was still working on the Problem Sets, and then I stopped working on it. I fell behind about 5, but I almost made up for the final week.
TA’s lectures are not as good as Amir, but at least their academic ability is good. Later, I found that the answers they wrote were also quite convenient to read.

Exams are fun. It brings together the content of the lecture in a way I never thought possible.

Through this course, I found that learning professional knowledge can be as interesting as listening to talk shows and playing games to fight monsters. Thanks Amir for not only bringing me knowledge, but also bringing me interest.

comment_workload:
你说的对，但是2711H是一门由Amir自主开发的全新开放思维冒险课程。课程开设在一个被称作HKUST的幻想世界，在这里，人们将被要求引导算法之力，完成小学奥数。你将扮演一位名为「Computer Scientist」的神秘角色，在自由的学习中邂逅不会计算、但一起绽放笑容的同伴们，和他们一起找回年少自信。（听说学2711H的同学都天生爱笑：）
毫不夸张地说，2711H是一门为大一新生量身定做，被大二学长强力推荐，零门槛，难度适中，给龟easy，考试人性化的优秀课程。Amir随性幽默专业的谈吐，更让学习变成一种享受。经常上2711H的同学大多眼神锐利，极度自信，且智力逐渐增加。上2711H会重组大脑结构，让小脑变为大脑，最后完全变成「Computer Scientist」。上2711H的学生具有强烈的数感，人一旦开始爱上2711H，就说明这个人gpa通通都是上等，这辈子肯定在UST大杀四方，对世界格局起到决定性的作用。
一个不喜欢2711H的人，无非只能有两种可能性。一种是不认为省队竞赛题、IMO试题与小猿口算难度相当的人。因为自己没有能力抽出一点时间去读每周几本书的课余小任务等自身因素，而把做不出题归咎于题目难度，他们的人生是失败的。如果你学三周2711H却不能拿信竞金牌，只能说明你没有思考足够长时间的week problem set；如果你学三周2711H没能参加IMOCMOUSAMO并拔得头筹，只能说明你对Amir的recommend book list视而不见。第二种可能：没有感受到Amir个人魅力的人。那么这个人的思想境界便低到了一个令人发指的程度。一个人不喜欢Amir，只能证明此人个人品味之低下，是阴暗的，是扭曲的，是无法融入社会的。Amir怎么你了，我每天18小时观看Amir课程回放，每节课能学到2到3年世界前列的数学研究成果。我半节课学完逻辑门，1天能熟练使用latex，2节课掌握数归良序递降，3周精通图论数论，我一门语言都不会，却能轻松理解DFS、BFS、动态规划以及各种NP完全问题，虽然我只读大一，但已经超越了中国绝大多数人（包括你）的水平，换算过来最少也是Yau的徒弟了，这便是我骄傲的资本。这恰好说明了2711H课程使学生体现出来的谦虚和自信，这也造就了这个课程学生互帮互助的局面。Amir负责课上输出优质内容，学生负责课后形成良好的学习氛围，如此良好的课程模式令那些绞尽脑汁却找不到人听课的教授艳羡不已，令lang课group discussion只能听到2711H讨论声的老师自惭形秽。可以说，选课不选2711H，就像四大名著不读红楼梦，说明对于数学造诣和自我修养不足，理解不了算法与哲学统一魅力的高雅艺术，它只能看到外表的数字和计算，参不透计算机异或内核，只能度过一个相对失败的人生。

comment_teaching:
Amir is a walking aura emitter. His teaching is one of its kind. I have never seen anyone in my life teach at the same level as him. He has the ability to explain a difficult concept in a few minutes with extremely precise language. He is like Desmond, but he teaches at 3 times his speed. How he does it is by presenting his logic flow in an ordered manner, splitting each problem into smaller problems, and solving each sub problems to prove the actual question.

I also like his approach of teaching. He basically just gives us problems in lessons, explains how to do it, and move on to the next problem. This way, the content is easier to understand and you also have the opportunity to think on your own before seeing the answer.

I also feel like what I learned from him is more than knowledge. It's a mindset. It's an approach to difficult discrete mathematics problems. Whenever I struggle in some problems, I try to think what Amir would do. I would rewatch his videos and learn the way he proves. At some point, I just realised the method of proofing became part of my toolbox. If the hammer doesn't work, I will try using the screwdriver. I will keep testing until it works, and it really sped up my problem solving process as I no longer have to sit in front of the problem and wonder how I am suppose to solve it.

By the way, please check out his youtube channel if you want to have a taste of what his teaching is like or want to pre study 2711H. It's very challenging but worth watching. I think the only part he didn't teach well was number theory. The rest of them were all very good.
comment_workload:
The workload is supposed to be E. The reason I gave it an A is because I didn't want to mess up the overall rating of the course too badly as I know this version of 2711H is not gonna appear anymore.

As the other reviews had mentioned, there are 10 books amir told us to read, additional videos each week to watch and problem sets every week. The workload is not for humans. I could study this course alone in the whole semester and I wouldn't be able to finish all the work he gave us. So I basically gave up on doing any of them after the fifth week and hope that everyone else is doing the same. I think Amir gave us so much exercise and resources because that's how he studied when he was a student? I am just guessing though. Anyway Amir had high expectations of us and we failed to meet it.

comment_content:
Reminder that this course in the future is not going to be as hard as this term. (Unless there is an even "crazier" professor than Amir which is very very unlikely.) This course was really something special. A lot of things have covered this term. It was fun definitely. I love math after all. But to master each topic in a week and train hard. That made my enjoyment throughout the course decrease day by day, week by week. The course this term has a lot more connection to higher level math courses (Real Analysis, Combinatorial Analysis, Game Theory, etc. just look at the syllabus and you will understand.)

Here is the syllabus and after that there will be the schedule for the whole course.

Syllabus:
    Proofs and Reasoning: Propositional Logic, First-order (Predicate) Logic, Quantifiers, Proof by contradiction, Mathematical Induction, the Pigeonhole Principle, the Invariant Principle, the Extremal Principle, ...
    Set Theory: ZMC, Peano's Axioms, Dedekind Cuts, Relations, Functions, Ordinals and Cardinals, Russel's Paradox, ...
    Enumerative Combinatorics: Rules of Sum and Product, Permutations and Combinations, Inclusion-Exclusion, Catalan and Stirling Numbers, Generating Functions, Recurrence Relations, Double Counting, ...
    Probability Theory: Discrete and Countable Probability Spaces, Axiomatic Probability Theory, Measure Functions, Lebesgue Integrals, Conditional Probability, Independence, Random Variables, Bayesian Reasoning, Expectation, Stochastic Processes, Markov Chains, Markov Decision Processes, ...
    Number Theory: Divisibility, GCD, LCM, Primes, Congruence, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Theorems of Fermat, Euler and Wilson, Quadratic Reciprocity Law, the RSA and El-Gamal Cryptosystems, ...
    Graph Theory: Adjacency, Walks, Paths, Cycles, (Strong) Connectivity, Distance, Trees, Spanning Trees, Bipartite Graphs, Matchings, Shortest Paths, ...
    Game Theory: Combinatorial Games, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, Normal Form (Nash) Games, Nash Equilibria, Correlated Equilibria, Infinite Games on Graphs, ...

Some of them are not covered by Amir, but you are required to read the books to learn it.

Here is how the flow so far according to how Amir teaches (From first lecture to latest lectures):

Logic and Proofs: Propositional Logic, Predicate, Quantifiers, definitions of natural numbers ,Peano's Axioms , Induction, Well-ording , Infinite Descent , introduction to Pigeonhole Principle, Strong Induction, Extremal Principle.

Combinatorics in general: Counting, Permutation, Combination with and without repetitions, Principle of Inclusion and Exclusion. More about Pigeonhole Principle.

Big focus on Graph Theory: theorems for graphs, isomorphism , path, walks, connected graphs, connected components, trees, cycles, algorithms on creating trees, bipartite, eulerian circuit, Hamlitonian cycle/graphs, graphical graphs , Havel Hakimi Algorithm ,Minimum Spanning Trees, Kruskal's algorithm, Prim's Algorithm , directed graph, DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph), Topological Ordering, DFS, BFS algorithms, Huffman Coding algorithm, eccentricity, shortest path, dijkstra algorithm, matchings, Hall's marriage theorem,König-Egeváry theorem, Gallai Theorem , vertex covers, edge covers, independent sets, network flow, ford-fulkerson algorithm, stable matching and graph coloring.

Number Theory: extension to Peano axiom to integers rather than natural number,definition of negative numbers, definiton of operations on integers, absolute value,divisibility, gcd, relatively prime/coprime, Euclidean algorithm, lcm, prime factorization, fundamental of arithmetic Congruence, Modular Multiplicative Inverse, Chinese Remainder Theorem, Fermat Little Theorem, Euler Theorem, Wilson's theorem, Diffie-Hellman-Merkle Key Exchange (Symmetric key), Elgamal encryption.

Set Theory: Naive Comprehension/Naive Set Theory, Russell's Paradox, Defining language for Set Theory, ZFC 10 Axioms (Extensionality, Empty Set, Unordered pair, Union, Comprehension, Powerset, Infinity, Replacement, Foundation, Axiom of Choice (there are 5 variations of this axiom)), Cardinality (e.g. Countable and Uncountable sets, Cantor's diagonalization, Taski's Theorem, Schroder-Berstein Theorem), Defining Rational and Real numbers (Dedekind cuts, supremum and infimum).

Probability Theory: Lebesgue Measure (Not heavily covered but it turns out to be very important in a hw), Measurable Space and Probability Space, Conditional Probability, Bayes Theorem, Independence, Random Variable, Linearity, Expectation, Stochastic Process and Markov Chain (Definition and representation in graphs especially having infinite walks in a graph), Reachability event, Buchi set, . (There are other topics Amir didn't taught in class like Variance and the distributions but they are required.)

Game Theory: Combinatorial Games (Nim with 2,3 and n heaps), solve games using Topological ordering in a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) and XOR operations, Sprague-Grundy Theorem, One shot games (Prisoner's Dilemma, Pollution game, Battle of sexes, Matching Pennies), Pure Nash Equilibrium (Using Dominant strategy to solve game), Mixed Nash Equilibrium (Using Probabilistic strategy to solve games), Nash Theorem, Arena, Reachability game (similar to Markov Chain), Buchi game.

There are also other topics that have connections to this course some are required some don't. (Control Theory, Group Theory, Measure Theory, Linear temporal logic, Ordinality of Set Theory, )

There are a lot of books to read. And most of them are not friendly to beginners at all. There are alternatives but these books goes deeper than any books I found in the library. Of course you go look at the "handbook of Discrete Math" but it doesn't really give you strong idea of why this or that theorem is true. So you still need to read the books.
I like Chen and Koh book.

Discrete and Combinatorial Mathematics by Grimaldi
Concrete Mathematics by Knuth et al
Principles and Techniques in Combinatorics by Chen and Koh
A First Course in Probability by Ross
Elementary Number Theory by Burton
Set Theory: A First Course by Cunningham
Introduction to Graph Theory by West
Algorithmic Game Theory by Nisan and Roughgarden
Problem Solving Strategies by Engel
102 Combinatorial Problems by Andreescu and Feng
Winning Ways for Your Mathematical Plays by Berlekamp et al

There are also some videos TAs or Amir might share but I recommend watching more that are not provided by them since a lot of the topics / questions really require guides or clarification. There are a lot of online resources related to this course so use them wisely! There is too many channels I can recommend and there are a lot of the topic this course has covered. So I recommend watch the videos in categories (Like watch videos about proofs like MIT courses, organic chemistry(Probably the weakest channel with proofs but very basic), TrevTutor, Math Sorcerer.)

If you have read this far, here is a reward to you. His website to his course with the recordings. https://amir.goharshady.com/teaching/honors-discrete-math
comment_teaching:
Amir is a good and fun teacher. However, the pacing is really fast. And don't expect the notes by Amir and TAs to be detailed. (He is pretty lazy when it comes to writing notes but he wrote so much in problem sets/homework/exam questions idk why lol.) TAs simply just copy Amir notes and take some words from him in the recording. So, make sure to take notes as well. I had to rewatch lecture notes and read chapters on books multiple times to get a grasp on a single content. Also for those who don't know, all tutorials sections are just additional lectures for students to attend and they are mandatory. So if you miss one lecture, you are in a disadvantage unless you have a very good background on Discrete Math before hand. I highly recommend you guys to ask TAs and Amir for help. They are friendly. But make sure to book the meetings asap.

TAs are good. I had problems from listening to one of the TAs since he don't speak English well. But I still think he is a decent TA. Since he patiently answers your questions. The TA tutor sections are also very crucial for the course. So attend them as well. If not watch the recording.
comment_workload:
This course has ridiculous workload. Not only you have lectures that you cannot skip. You have extra exercises that are required to do. Especially counting problems. In week 2, Amir have given us over 400 problems and some of them are advanced questions involving group theory. This is impossible to do and I only managed to do 1/4 of it from sacrificing my daily time and skipping lectures.

There are totally 12 problem sets with 10 problems each. They are not required but very highly recommended. The problem set are usually hard, there are easy ones. But when it comes to counting/principle of inclusion and exclusion/pigeonhole principle/number theory, Amir loves to add Olympiad questions to it and they are generally very hard. I believe the TAs just copy the solutions in the internet as their solutions. (And how they find the observation to solution is really unclear.). So expect to spent a long time to finish these questions as well. I didn't do problem sets after set 7 since I fumbled other courses while mainly handling this course.

The homework at the beginning is pretty easy as long as you do some combinatorics questions. After that it is not easy. So you might spent a few days if not weeks to solve 2-3 questions. Btw, student can only submit their hw via latex, so if you have no knowledge on it, have fun spending extra time on it. (I stared at the screen for days. Always thinking the solutions while eating, walking, laying on bed or taking a bath. It took me a long time to get them right.) Don't forget, there are questions that you need to learn how to answer in the internet as Amir didn't taught them much. You need to give correctness on your algorithm, proving it is correct. Read/watch Measure Theory and Markov Decision Process.

There are many books you can read but they are high recommended again (The books are in the contents part since they are pretty much compulsory). But Amir also shares videos or extra reading materials. The videos are usually long but you can skip them if you want to. I watched and read all of them.

Oh yes, we cannot forget about the study part even though we did a lot already. There are a few ways you can study. Either you watch videos relate to it or watch other courses taught by Amir. It is very easy to see the similaries and there are exam papers publish publicly there. (No idea why no sample papers for this course. smh).

For those who dislike the tremendous workload like this, don't take this course. Although Amir is no longer teaching in UST, he is still a great teacher. He just likes to make things 100x harder.
comment_grading:
Homework 40%
Mid Term Exam 30%
Final Exam 30%

The homework policy is quite generous in general, you are allowed to submit twices for each homework. After the first submission, you get feedback from TAs or Amir and then resubmit it. The score for each homework is the maximum of two attempts.

As for the homework difficulty, it is generally hard after homework 1.
HW1: 10/10
HW2: 8.6/10
HW3: 10/10
HW4: 10/10

HW4 is where you need to understand Measure Theory and there is literally nothing you can do except googling.

As for the mid term, well it was pretty devastating. Even though it is easier than weekly problem sets, it is generally hard. The mid term covered EVERYTHING before the the date of the mid term exam, that means the lectures for that week also counts into it. Also, the weekly problem sets solutions are quite late to come out. I understand that TAs are very busy but I wish at least there are guides on how to do these type of questions OR just give us a sample paper like they did in 3711H.

As for my score, I got 6/30, my friend had 8/30. (Yes, I am terrible. I am sure most of the students here have higher score than me. I had mental breakdown before I had the grade and asked TA for help to save me from failing the course.)

As for the final exam, while it is easier, it turns out to have question on calculating which is unexpected. I was expecting something similar to the problem sets. So I didn't score perfectly on that part. Same goes for Combinatorics. Other than that, it was not that bad. I got 16.5/30. Much much better than mid term.

I got A- in the end. THANK YOU AMIR YOU ARE THE BEST!!!

comment_content:
[Some words after watching Amir's offering]

Apparently Amir's version is way more all-rounded than the usual offering, in terms of the depth and breadth of course content. In the typical version, the teaching sequence is

Propositional logic -> Number theory -> Combinatorics -> Probability Theory -> (Optional) Graph theory

But Amir's version has the sequence of

Propositional logic + Induction (a part of Peano's axiom) -> Combinatorics -> Graph theory + Graph algos -> Number theory + Cryptography -> Axiom of completeness -> Probability -> Stochastic process -> Game theory

If 3711H is "a feast for algo geeks", then 2711H shall be "a fine-dining for algebraists, analysts and algo geeks" since the non-graph-theoretic topics are really foundations for mathematicians living on each side, while the graph theory topics can be treated as a taster of 3711H since the related core topics are covered with some proof of correctness, but without the important C++ implementations and runtime analysis.

Initially I didn't understand why graph theory was introduced at the beginning of the course. After covering topics like Markov chains and game theory, I realized that there might be no better teaching sequences for putting graph theory elsewhere.

If you wish to go further based on the course content, the following courses are highly recommended -

(i). The Honors analysis sequence (1023 up to 3043) will help you continue completing the "axiom of completeness", as well as why measure theory and Lebesgue integration theory are necessary in modern analysis. I would say solely based on 2711H, students may feel difficult to see why Lebesgue integral is important and necessary.

(ii) The discussion on elementary number theory is just telling one fundamental fact that "\mathbb Z" is a Unique Factorization Domain (UFD)". The Honors (and graduate-level) algebra courses (3131 + 5111) will explore more algebraic structures and similar properties; Also many modern crypto algos are based on commutative algebra and algebraic geometry.

(iii) The measure-theoretic probability (2431 + 5411) courses will make use of what you learnt in the Honors analysis courses and rebuild every concepts in classical probability. Without such foundations, the famous central limit theorem and the laws of large numbers (and related variants) cannot be understood rigorously.

(iv) For the graph-theoretical topics, indeed 3711H is the best continuation.

Like 3711H, I admire the hard efforts made by Amir and his teaching team in this course.

[End]

1. Official textbook: Notes on the course website, and his own lecture - The content taught in class might be different from that in the notes, and not all stuff is taught.

2. Course structure:

========Part One: Pure Logic stuff=======

TL;DR: Literally basic building block in math.

i. Propositional logic, inference,... (Most useful takeaway: p implies q iff not q implies not p - A logical mistake that everyone makes in daily life)
ii. Set and cardinality
iii. Function (Mapping between sets), and injective, surjective, bijective...

==========Part Two: Number Theoty===========

TL;DR: Basic number theory.

iv. Mod arithmetic and number theory (Euclid's algorithm, Fermat's little theorem, C.R.T... - One shall study abstract algebra topics like group and ring for further study)
v. Cryptography (RSA)

========Part Three: Counting principle==========

TL;DR: One of the hardest types of question appears here! You have to prove identity, usually combinatorial approach instead of analytic. It is quite difficult because you may not figure out the correct scenario for the proof!

vi. Permutation and combination, binomial coefficient
vii. Inclusion-exclusion principle
viii. Analysis of algorithm (Big-Oh, Big-Theta, Big-Omega...)

=========Part Four: Probability======

TL;DR: Discrete random variables. The only continuous stuff taught is normal distribution.

ix. Random variable
x. Expectation and variance, conditional probability
xi. Special distribution (Bernoulli, binomial, geometric, normal...)

=========Part Five: Others=======

xii. Strong/weak induction
xiii. Recursion
xiv. Graph theory

comment_teaching:
The professor explains the concepts in much detail (as expected for a honors course) and often emphasizes the importance of thinking about the same problem in different ways, so as to build a richer mathematical sense. Possibly as a result, the proofs in class may differ from the ones displayed in the lecture notes posted on the course website. General recommendation is to not skip class, although the professor said himself that it is totally alright to just read the lecture notes. If you do attend class, be warned that using mobile phones is frowned upon (laptops are fine).

On the other hand, the tutorials are just about average. What happens is that several problems are selected from the question bank (which will be posted on the course website beforehand) to be explained by the TA, which is, frankly, quite boring. If you have done all of the questions in advance and checked your answers, the tutorials can be skipped. Only attend if you have questions.
comment_workload:
There are 4 assignments, each with around 10 questions. Depending on the difficulty, each assignment can take anywhere from 4 to 16 hours to complete (assuming that you do not search online for answers). Both handwritten and printed work are accepted (hard copies only).

Considering that I also took COMP 2012H during this semester, the workload for this course isn't really that much... right?

If you are not interested in discrete mathematics, taking COMP 2711 may be a better idea.
