comment_content:
The course provides a gentle introduction to students who are interested in further study in computer science the mathematics which commonly appears in their studies. As suggested by its title, this course revolves around the discrete part of mathematics, namely:

1. Logic
2. Number Theory
3. Counting and permutations
3. Discrete probability
4. Induction and recursion
5. Graph theory

The course enables students to develop their mathematical maturity as the content is rather rigorous and formal, though the instructor also succeeds in telling the motivations and intuitions behind formal definition and proofs.

The instructor does not cover all of the graph theory content in the lecture notes because of time constraint.
comment_teaching:
Despite the instructor's Indian accent, his English is easy to comprehend. His teaching is well structured and can be easily followed. He does not like to teach using slideshows. Instead, he loves to use the whiteboard to write the contents down. Some of the content are not present in the lecture notes, which is the same set of notes used in the non-honor version of COMP2711, so do remember to attend the class and take notes. In the mid-term examination, the professor posted a question on the proof of Fermat's Little Theorem, which of course has been taught but only written on the whiteboard! If one wants to avoid losing easy marks it is advised that you do take notes.

He is really knowledgeable in the subject matter since he is a seasoned veteran in the field of algorithms. He offers insights to problems and concepts which could have been very difficult to grasp for inexperienced learners.
comment_workload: The workload is light as students are only expected to complete and submit four assignments consisting of around 5-10 questions. The questions are challenging yet approachable and insightful. It is expected that the time one spent on this course is mostly due to revision as the instructor provides a large set of problems for us to practice. He provides incentives for the students to practice those questions by reusing them in the written examinations. (He said this in class)
comment_grading: The grading is lenient. The instructor said in class that he will give all As. Despite being outperformed by a quarter of the students, the writer still managed to get an A+. Therefore, at least a quarter (25%!) of students gets A+ in this course.

comment_content: Same as 2711. Maybe a little bit deeper, but basically the same.
comment_teaching: He is a great teacher. Shows everything step by step and very clearly so that everyone can understand. The lectures are quite different from the notes on the course website, but in the essence, they are the same. Still, it is very recommended to attend the lecture since you can gain much deeper understanding of the concept much easier.
comment_workload: All you have to do is just study for the exam plus just 4 assignments. The assignments tend to be quite hard and mean is very high either: still, if you search online, you have access to most of the answers, and even the lecturer do not stop you from doing so. He thinks it is one of the ways you learn.
comment_grading:
Grading is great. More than half gets A range. The scheme was

MT 35%
Final 45%
Assignments 20%

The exams have high means, yet if you study the notes, examples in class, past papers, and especially the Question Bank and Extra exercises carefully, you will be able to get high marks.

comment_content:
0. Basics: Sets and Functions (self-study)
1. Logic and Proofs
2. Combinatorics
3. Discrete Probability
4. Number Theory and Cryptography
5. Induction, Recursion, and Recurrence Relations
6. Graphs (wasn't covered thoroughly due to time constraint)
comment_teaching: One of the best instructors from CSE department. Sunil is not just knowledgeable, he can really explain mathematical concepts in an intuitive and organic manner. He's inspiring and humorous as well. I went to every lecture, overall very enjoyable.
comment_workload: 4 written assignments (feel free to use LaTeX) in total for this semester. Originally it was supposed to be 5, but it was reduced to 4 in the middle of the semester. For each assignment, we had slightly less than two weeks to solve 10++ problems. Better start early because every assignment will definitely have some difficult questions. Pay attention to basically all examples used by Sunil, they might appear in the midterm/final.
comment_grading:
HW 20%, Midterm 35%, Final 45%
Probably one of the most generous grading scheme among courses offered by CSE department. Admittedly most students taking this course are quite diligent and smart, and this drives up the mean scores easily, but if you can keep up with your peers, it's very likely for you to get A-range.

comment_content:
Discrete mathematics needed for the study of computer science:
1. sets, functions;
2. propositional logic, predicate logic, rules of inference, proof techniques;
3. pigeonhole principle, permutations and combinations, binomial coefficients, inclusion-exclusion principle;
4. probability theory, Bayes' theorem, expectation, variance, random variables, hashing;
5. cryptography and modular arithmetic, Euclid’s division theorem, multiplicative inverse, divisibility, RSA cryptosystem, Chinese remainder theorem;
6. mathematical induction, strong induction and well-ordering property, recursion, recurrence relations;
7. graph representation, isomorphism, connectivity, Euler and Hamiltonian paths, planarity, graph coloring.

(1) is not formally taught in class, basically ur knowledge in thz topic is assumed, meaning that u would hv to do some self-study. Btw it is not difficult, just some terminology.
(2) would be a really conceptual topic, no matter how much or how less u memorised, as long as u can use the logic rule to solve problems is ok. Btw I think thz topic would be a good math foundation for one considering taking 3000-4000 math ug courses.
(3) (4) is just slightly > dse lv core math + m1, with sth new like pigenhole and inclusion-exclusion. Btw thz part also overlaps with some math 2000 lv stat prob course, (2411, 21, 31...), which are also in comp curriculum. I think the cse dept should really re-consider to construct a better course structure...
(5) is about mod operation. Comp 1000lv prog course gives u insight, but this course gives u a more in-depth knowledge. Better take it serious for its wide application in many other Comp theory courses or even courses in diff subj, e.g. math courses like Abstract algebra, Number Theory.
(6) is just an extension of Dse lv m2 version MI. Again, what's inmportant is the idea. The stupid work flow and clumsy format would not really bother u much once u get the whole picture
For (7) I think prof usually doesn't hv too much time to cover it. Just like in thz sem, basically he just introduced some terminology and definitions. But those fancy terms are stll important. What u hv to do to deal with thz chapter is memorising stuffs as much as possible. In thz way u cen prevent any "surprises" in final exam.
comment_teaching:
I think prof’s teaching is quite well, but one may first listen him speaking eng with a quite strimg accent..

One thing worth to mention is, prof has a quite
old-style teaching. For most of the topics, he would like to explain by writing everything on board, but not by using ppt slides or showing his work on projector. Plus he does not really hv a nice handwriting. So one might expect to be really concentrated in the lesson to get the idea.

Another thing is, some notes or ppt slides he provides in advance are just other prof’s work. And he just provide to us for ref, but does not choose to follow them strictly. For some topics like (5) concerning number theory and (7) graph theory he even told us to ignore those slides, but instead search Rosens textbook for the definitions and thms he illustrated in the lesson. So u may really want to get a Rosen e-book just in case. Luckily there are some free online versions ~

And he somehow hates people using smart phone in class, but he just managed to accpet student using computers... So u either use phone in secret or use ur computers in class  for learning or other(?) purposes.

Overall his teaching is good and the course really equip u with solid knowledge of some discrete math tools. But somehow u hv to get used to the prof’s teaching style.
comment_workload:
For the assignments, he usually delivered it 1-2 weeks before. Like those in C++ class, u may not be able to finish it in last minutes. We are talking abt some REAL stuff here. So just be early planned and better prepared.

One thing good is, prof allows or u may say he encourages u to hand in a printed version of ur hw. Plus he specifies that poor hand-writing in hw might cause a lower marks. So u can actually use MS Word or Latex to finish ur hw, which is good for a bad writer like me.

For the difficulty lv, some of the hws would be as difficult as those math honor courses (1023, 24, 2131,...). But as the course title suggested, it is also a H course, so u just gotta deal with it.

To sum up, u might want to expect to hv a huge workload if u r not really that familiar with math. Even if u r, u still need to work hard to compete with others
comment_grading:
20% for assignments (4-5 / sem)
35% for mid
55% for final
A bit diff from most of the math courses

As far as I know, most of the people taking thz course would get A range. But i think it is just becoz of their strong academic background...
U know, nds and even some locals are really gd at math and logic...
(Thz might explain why as an average-lv HK-er, I only can get a A- grade...)
(Btw no offense.)

One thing gd is, prof is willing to provide pp for us to prepare for mid and final. He may even send u pp in last several sems, as long as sby request in class. And some of the Qs would really re-appear in ur mid / final paper,
meaning that u would hv less risk relative to other comp courses when dealing with exams.

Overall, hard-working can help for obtaining gd grade, which is good for a comp course.

comment_content: The course mostly cover the content of 2711, i.e. logic, combinations, discrete probability and number theory and  Arya also gives some insight into graph theory, which you may find it useful in 3711.  It discusses some proofs, like those combinational proofs of combination identity and you are required to understand them and prove them in the exam. Some useful techniques may be taught and required to practice in your homework, but you are not required to think out very fancy or wield techniques.
comment_teaching: Arya has his accent, but once you get used to it, you must like it. His teaching style is relatively relaxing and interesting and he is quite nice to answer questions after class and are willing to praise you for your small findings.
comment_workload: Throughout the semester, you will be distributed 4 to 5 problem sets, which you may need to turn in after one or two weeks. To practice on them is quite helpful for your exam.  Generally speaking, its workload is smaller than any other honor course's.
comment_grading: The grade is really good. Arya promised in the first class that, if you spend the same effort, you won't get a worse grade than those in 2711. Many students get A arrange.

comment_content: The content includes logics, combinatorics, probability, number theory, recursion and graph theory. These contents are very interesting if you like math.
comment_teaching: Arya's lectures were very easy to understand, and he was engaging students into his lecture, which was good. I think the TA was quite smart, and his materials clearly helped with understanding the course materials.
comment_workload: In my opinion, the workload was quite light. For this semester, you only needed to do 4 assignments, each assignments with around 10-15 questions. If you paid full attention during the lecture and reviewed the lecture notes, the assignments would not feel very difficult. Also, you are given about 2 weeks to finish each assignments, so you do not need to rush in time.
comment_grading: The grading was amazing. I think if you get above mean in  mid-term and final and all the assignments, you will get at least A. If you do better than that, like getting around 0.5-0.6sd above mean, you will get A+. However, note that the mean on the exams and assignments tend to be very high, since this is an honor course.

comment_content:
MATH course with COMP course code. The content is similar to the non honor version:
Basic logic, proofs; combinatorics, discrete probability; Number theory, cryptography; Induction, recursion, Big O, omega, theta notations; Graphs.
The prerequisites are not really needed because no calculus are required, but good problem skills are (good DSE core combinatorics and probability knowledge would be a great plus).
The content is appropriate even for a year 1 student like myself.
comment_teaching:
HIs teaching is really good and the content is good. He encourges thinking about a topic or question deeper because this is honor class. He is funny as well. Although he has an accent, it is comprehensible.
The ta is just ok, we only do a few questions in the tutorial, looked at the answer for a little while, and move on.
comment_workload: OH BOY DOES HE MEAN IT WHEN HE SAID THAT THE WORKLOAD IS HIGH. There are 4 homeworks, with about 9 - 14 days to finish the homework, which consists of about 10 - 15 question. Those questions are very hard, really require you to spend a whole night to think about, doesn't work, and try another way. I ended up googling some of them. And only some of the questions were graded each time, and they chose the easy one. If you did well on the questions that they didn't mark, well, bad luck for you.
comment_grading:
The midterm have a mean of 78 and a sd of about 15, and the exam have a mean of 81. I got slightly above mean for both, but below mean for all homework, but still got an A.

It is worth mentioning that the midterm is heavily "inspired" by the homework, as prof pointed out. I was shock though, seeing the same question about proving the Chebeyshev Inequality (worth 10 points), and overall are quite similar to the homework. The exams are just a memorization fest where you write out things you memorized from the notes. Just read all the notes and you are done.

comment_content:
To quote from the course homepage: "Discrete mathematics needed for the study of computer science: sets, functions; propositional logic, predicate logic, rules of inference, proof techniques; pigeonhole principle, permutations and combinations, binomial coefficients, inclusion-exclusion principle; probability theory, Bayes' theorem, expectation, variance, random variables, hashing; cryptography and modular arithmetic, Euclid division theorem, multiplicative inverse, divisibility, RSA cryptosystem, Chinese remainder theorem; mathematical induction, strong induction and well-ordering property, recursion, recurrence relations"

We were supposed to learn about graphs too but we didn't have enough time so the professor just gave an introduction to the topic.

The concepts were easy, but the actual applications were difficult and complicated. Very interesting nonetheless.

~90% of exam questions are taken from homework and lecture notes. Although Sunil emphasised that we should understand the content instead of memorising, but at the end it was still a memory contest more than anything.
comment_teaching:
Excellent professor. Clear and humorous teaching. Never seen such a high attendance in a 9am class. Sunil is the kind of professor who makes you want to go and check the class schedule website and see whether or not you can enroll in his other courses in the next semester.

Our TA was Yuchen Mao, who was also excellent. He responds quickly to emails and teaches clearly.
comment_workload:
5 written assignments in total, we were given 1-1.5 weeks to finish each.
Assignments were very challenging, quite a few assignments I had to finish by googling the answers.

Don't take too many credits along with this course cause it's quite demanding.
comment_grading:
Good grading, I believe almost everyone got A-range.
Above mean = A+
Mean = A
Below mean = A-
(but just don't exploit Sunil's good grade and get ridiculously low scores, someone in our class didn't attend the finals, and idk what grade he/she got at the end)

comment_content: The content is same as the non honors course, but taught at a different pace. The content is majorly about algorithms, probability and running time.
comment_teaching: I absolutely love how sunil arya teaches! His teaching is organic and has a very good flow i.e. he wont teach something simple and expect you to understand a ver hard concept from that. He explains everything step by step and gives a lot a practice!
comment_workload: Heavy! But worth it if you really want to learn all the concepts properly!
comment_grading: He needs you to have at least a B+ in the previous MATH Courses. Absolute grading: most people get A range or a B+.

comment_content:
Very similar to normal version of COMP 2711. Lecture notes are from normal version COMP2711 at 2014 Spring. The course is totally MATH course. Extra topics (graph theory) may discuss during lecture time but no notes, have to attend and jot down by yourself.

Book is not required but may useful for handling assignment problem. Book name: Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications 7th edition, Publisher: McGraw-Hill
comment_teaching:
Lecture will elaborate all terms and theories, and may show you the correct way to solve problem. Teaching style is quite traditional, by writing everything on board instead of PowerPoint, but won't boring and easier to follow. Tutorial will discuss the use of theory taught in lecture to solve problem.

The only drawback is Prof. ARYA spent too much time on early topic, causing no time teach topics behind (some of graph theory are skipped and some of them are concluded only in 1~2 lectures.) But overall, his teaching is good for students from all background to follow, and wanna everyone understand before goes on.
comment_workload:
Total 5 assignments, each assignment consist at least 10 questions, some of them consist 20+ questions. They are extremely hard, some of them require extra knowledge which is not included in lecture to solve it. Assignment will be released 2 weeks before due date.

Assignment count for final grading 20%, and some theory not being discussed in lecture but existed on assignment may also included in exam. Have to work extremely hard for good grade.
comment_grading:
Very good grade, ~75% or above can reach at least A-.

Grading: 20% for assignment, 30% for mid-term, 50% for final.
Mid-term and final may also cover topics in assignments (may not be discussed in lecture), but exam questions are easier than assignments.

comment_content:
Content is more less than COMP2012. Just copy the slides from previous year.

comment_content:
🚩 Issues:
- Too much overlap with COMP2012. It’s not worth taking if you’ve already taken COMP2012.
- Only learned something new during the last lecture of the semester (e.g., multithreading).
- Final exam felt disconnected from the lecture content.
- The final was extremely challenging, and your grade depends heavily on your performance in it. You must be super familiar with Java syntax.
- You need to act like a human compiler to predict outputs and identify compilation errors. ⚙️

🔍 Example Questions:
- Many questions ask which version will compile and produce the correct output.  
  Example:  
  - How to use `PrintWriter` inside a `try-catch` block to produce the correct result?  
  - Which of the following will equal `b`?  
    - char(a - 1)
    - char(a - '1')
    - char('a' - 1)

📝 Format:
- 20 MCQs in the final exam.
- Some questions were harder versions of ❗️class examples❗️and quizzes.

➡️ Quiz Question Examples:
1. Which of the following statement(s) will compile successfully and print `1` when executed?  
   - A. `System.out.print(1.toString());`  
   - B. `System.out.print('1'.toString());`  
   - C. `System.out.print("1".toString());`  
   - D. `System.out.print(((Integer)1).toString());`  
   - E. `System.out.print(Integer(1).toString());`  
   - F. `System.out.print(((Character)(char)1).toString());`  
   - G. `System.out.print((1 + "").toString());`  
   - H. `System.out.print((1 + '').toString());`  

2. Consider the following program in `MyClass.java`:

   ```java
   public class MyClass {
       public static void main(String[] args) {
           try {
               System.out.print(1/0);
           } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
               System.out.print("A");
               throw new RuntimeException();
           } catch (RuntimeException re) {
               System.out.print("B");
           } finally {
               System.out.print("C");
           }
           System.out.print("D");
       }
   }
Which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

A. The program compiles successfully and prints "ABCD" when executed.
B. The program compiles successfully and prints "ACD" when executed.
C. The program compiles successfully and prints "AD" when executed.
D. The program compiles successfully but prints none of the above.

3.Consider the following incomplete program in MyClass.java:
java

class BaseClass {
    void print(String s) {
        System.out.println("BaseClass" + s);
    }
}
class SubClass<E> extends BaseClass {
    void print(E e) {
        System.out.println("SubClass" + e);
    }
}
public class MyClass {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ___________________________
    }
}
Which of the following way(s) to fill in the blank will produce a program that compiles successfully and prints BaseClass1 when executed?
A. SubClass<Integer> obj = new SubClass<Integer>(); obj.print("1");
B. SubClass<Object> obj = new SubClass<Integer>(); obj.print("1");
C. SubClass<Object> obj = new SubClass<Object>(); obj.print("1");
D. SubClass<Object> obj = new SubClass<String>(); obj.print("1");
E. BaseClass obj = new SubClass<String>(); obj.print("1");
F. BaseClass obj = new SubClass<Integer>(); obj.print("1");
G. BaseClass obj = new SubClass<Integer>(); obj.print(1);
H. BaseClass obj = new SubClass<Object>(); obj.print("1");
I. BaseClass obj = new SubClass<Object>(); obj.print(1);


4.Consider the following incomplete program in MyClass.java:

interface MyInterface {
    int compute(int x);
}
public class MyClass {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyInterface i = _______________________;
        System.out.println(i.compute(10));
    }
}
Which of the following way(s) to fill in the blank will produce a program that compiles successfully and prints 100 when executed?
A. x -> x * x;
B. x -> return x * x;
C. x -> { return x * x; };
D. new MyInterface() { int compute(int x) { return x * x; } };

5.Consider the following incomplete program in MyClass.java:

public class MyClass extends Thread {
    private String name;
    static Integer count = 0;
    public MyClass(String name) {
        .this.name = name;
        count++;
    }
    public void write() {
        System.out.print(name);
        System.out.print(name);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new MyClass("A").start();
        new MyClass("B").start();
        ___________________________
    }
}
Which of the following way(s) to fill in the blank will produce a program that compiles successfully and prints either AABB or BBAA but never ABBA or any other combinations when executed?
A. ;
B. public void run() { write(); }
C. public synchronized void run() { write(); }
D. public static synchronized void run() { write(); }
E. public void run() { synchronized (this) { write(); } }
F. public void run() { synchronized (count) { write(); } }
comment_teaching:
- The lectures were very boring. 💤Although the professor had an American accent, which was nice, it didn’t help much with engagement. 😕
- The teaching style didn’t match the difficulty of the exam. It’s disappointing that she used relatively simple examples during the lectures that were easy to understand, while the final exam was much harder. 😔
- It would be much better if she taught harder material in the lectures, so it would be more similar to the final exam. 😩 It would also be helpful if she taught us how Java compilation works. That would improve the learning experience.

comment_content:
Looks like introduction to Java but its actually not.

Content
1.intro to java
2. obj and classes
3. strings
4. inheritance, polymorphism
5. exceptions, text I/O
6. abstract class, interfaces
7. generics
8. inner class
9. functional programming and lambda
10. event-driven programming (little content from JavaFX)
11. parallel and concurrent programming

Generic and multithreading parts are really confusing even with the examples given.
Need some knowledge from comp3511 if you have never learnt multithreading before.

Lab
There are few optional labs in the beginning of semester with the info of useful tools like git or debug mode. Lab practise let you pratice the topics learnt during lessons.
comment_teaching:
The prof gives examples for each topic and they includes the tricky part (and also how Java treats stuff). The lectures are OK.


The lab only went throught the lab powerpoint about the tasks and oftne finish in 5-10 minutes. Attend the lab in person if you want to ask questions.
comment_workload:
Pop up quiz that release during lesson (can be done within the day)
Weekly labs
2 Programming Assignment (around 3 weeks time)
One final

Both PA and labs can use AI tools.
But the PAs are really heavy and hard. The first PA often collide with other tasks during those weeks and not much test case is given.  The instruction are often unclear and require explanation from the TAs.

PA2 for me is a disaster as most tasks are generic and we have not learnt multithreading at all during release. I dont even know what DAG and data depency graph is at that time and I used a lot of support from online sources and claude. The tasks are pretty abstract and hard to figure out what to implement. Especially last tasks requires running time speed up (lenient grading used already).
comment_grading:
No midterm.
8% canvas quiz
8% lab tasks
16% PA1 (class,parsing,list usage,etc.)
24% PA2 (generic,multithreading,synchronization,etc.)
44% Final (Mean: 42.5 (much higher than last year),SD: 14.7)
Open book, open notes final

Both final and canvas quiz are in MC but it may have more that one answers. If the final answer contains any wrong answer then it will give you a 0 for that question. Really harsh system especially with answers that look really similar and you thought they would work the same way but actually not.

I got C+ as my final was around lower quartile.

comment_teaching: Prof Shen has a very fluent English and strong American accent, which makes it comfortable to listen to her teaching. She basically uses Charles's ppt and switch to her own to demonstrate some corner cases and tricky examples. However, the UGTA often asked hard questions during class and Jiasi could not answer it. Also, the English accent of UGTA is really awful. I barely understood what he was talking about almost every time he asked a question. This makes it kind of waste of time.
comment_workload: Light workload comparing to COMP2012. Labs are very simple. You can finish each lab within an hour every week. PA1 is about very basic java features. You can easily finish it within an afternoon. PA2 is a lot more challenging, especially those new to functional programming and parallel programming. I spent almost a week to finish PA2. The good thing is that this course allows the use of AI. You can lighten your workload if working properly with gpt. However, the exam has totally nothing to do with the labs and PAs. The best tip I can give about preparing for the exam is to review sjs's examples several times until you fully understand every page. This helps your exam A LOT. After that, the exam questions might not look like as hard as it seems.

comment_teaching:
Prof. Shen's lectures are very laid-back. The iPRS questions (correctness is not important) generally contribute to the lectures. At the beginning of the semester, given that it's her first semester teaching, she did not know how to pace her content, and ended up finishing the first three lectures worth of content in one. As someone who likes programming and has a decent knowledge in OOP, it was fine since the content was about C++ vs Java. If she did this in later topics, however, I'm sure the effect would not be similar.

She didn't really know what she wanted to cover at the start, but as the semester went along she definitely got more grasp of the entire teaching thing. One thing to take note of is that her research area involves quite a bit of artificial intelligence. So in her lectures she sometimes demos concepts using chatGPT. I don't know if it's because of our lack of reaction, but she stopped doing it after a while.
Starting from the middle of the semester, she found that she would be going through the contents too quickly, so she would prepare examples to run and demonstrate in-class. These examples sometimes are on par with COMP2011/2012 "give me output"-question level, so it is quite useful for practice.

For those who are concerned about her accent, it's completely fine in my opinion, her accent doesn't affect her delivery at all.

The lab exercise are one of the best parts of this course. Since the LA section was scheduled at 09:00, the TA allowed us to attend through Zoom (very nice). The lab exercises are also designed in a way where you progress it through the semester in one GitHub repository. The instructions are mostly clear and it does mostly make use of content in the course, I do wish more time was put on concurrency though.

Right before the final exam, she also practiced some past paper questions with us, which is something I find interesting and useful.

The 2 TAs and Prof. Shen all respond to emails quickly whenever I bother them, so that was nice.

comment_grading:
8% iPRS attendance
21% PA1
21% PA2
8% Labs (11 of them)
42% Final exam

For iPRS, you only need to answer, it doesn't matter what you answer. She does these anytime in a lesson, usually half-way, so be on the lookout for that. She wrote, and I quote, "To get full marks for lecture participation, you must participate in at least 15 (out of ~20) of the in-class exercises." I answered 22 times so she asked around that many times. (I attended most lectures)

The 2 programming assignments aren't difficult. They aren't trivial, but they are definitely not heavy in terms of workload. You would do both PAs with a GitHub repo each.
One good thing about it is you can use any libraries you want (not sure about external ones aside from JUnit, as JUnit is essential for the testing), so you don't have to manually declare array of fixed sizes for tasks. I am definitely not salty over not being able to use vector in COMP 2012 PAs. At the start of the semester the teaching team mentioned about a bonus scheme for PAS, but that was never executed so I have no idea about that.

Labs, as I stated in the teaching section of this review, were very good. Combined with the PAs above, you actually learn some software engineering. So... for those CPEGers wanting to learn a bit of SE concepts and wanting to avoid COMP 3111(H) (which is smart), you could learn a bit from here.

The final exam. We were assigned the 08:30 timeslot, so we already weren't fully awake. Combined with the fact that it was open-book (hard-copy), we were scared. She released Desmond (yes, that Desmond)'s past paper from the one time he taught this course, and that paper was... quite difficult. What Prof. Shen gave us put Desmond's in the medium difficulty category. Truly an MIT-level paper. (Context: Prof. Shen got her PhD from *the* MIT)

The final exam paper goes like this: Full MCs, every question has at least one correct answer, meaning for a question with ABCDEF, there could be 6 correct answers.

The scores you get are calculated this way: If you answered any incorrect option, 0. Else, the score you get is FLOOR(s*x/n), where s is the score of that question, x is the amount of correct options you answered and n is the amount of correct options. An example, if a question worth 3 points with choices ABCDE has AD as its answer, if your answer has B, C or E, 0. Else if you answered A or D, you get FLOOR(3 * 1 / 2) = 1, if you answer AD then 3 points.
Another example, question worth 6 points has 8 options ABCDEFGH (yes one had 8 options) with answers ABDEG, you answered 2 correct, AB, for instance, you get FLOOR(6 * 2 / 5) = 2 etc.
Hope this is clear enough.

I've never felt chills over a final exam paper. It genuinely tests how confident you are with your knowledge in Java and how much you know. (particularly if you over-rely on libraries)

Oh, I must mention, our final exam didn't test anything regarding JavaFX, labs and PAs didn't do anything with it either (Glad I basically gave up that part)

Attendance - 8/8 (answered 22 times, which is far over the needed amount)
Labs - 11/11
PA1 - 100/100
PA2 - 94/100
Final exam 42/100

This resulted in an A-.

comment_teaching:
The teaching quality improved significantly throughout the semester - possibly caused by her lack of teaching experience before.

In the beginning, she had a weak grasp of lesson planning and often speedran the contents of last semester's slides. She is also a ChatGPT enthusiast (ChatGPT was used to fill in the gaps when she can't figure out what to explain) and wasn't very confident.

She improved quite a bit during the semester as she got adjusted to teaching. She was able to prepare her own examples for the trickier points of Java's behaviour, and preparing her own materials enabled her to structure her teaching a bit better. At the end, she was at least an above average prof when she figured out she's doing.

The labs prepared by the teaching team were generally pretty good, and provided hands-on experience to add on the topics presented in the lecture.

B for showing improvement throughout the semester and at least trying to do her job properly (which some people *cough cough 3111* were unable to do).
comment_workload:
The workload for this course is actually really low.

The labs are not difficult (mostly) and can be finished in under an hour if you're unfamiliar with the topic.

The assignments are very easy unlike COMP 2011/2012/2012H assignments. There are not a lot of hidden test cases to screw you over, and you can leverage the entire Java standard library to make your code more concise. It should take you no longer than 5 hours to finish one if you have listened to the lectures.
comment_grading:
When combined with the low difficulty of the assignments and labs in general, the grading scheme is heavily skewed towards the (explosively difficult) final exam.

8% Attendance (15 iPRS responses out of a total of ~25 are enough for the full score, graded on response and not on correctness)
42% Programming Assignments (21% each, 2 assignments)
8% labs (11 Labs)
42% Final Exam

Assignments
=================
Assignments are actually smaller than normal COMP 2012(H) PAs despite carrying a large percentage of the grade. The first assignment was on general OO design and Java collections. The second was on  event-driven programming and multithreading. None of them were too tricky nor difficult - the most annoying thing might've been PA1's logic.

Despite mentioning a "bonus scheme", none of thee PAs had any bonus whatsoever. Maybe an oversight?

Final Exam
=================
The final exam was where the "hell" in the title came from. It was a 2 hour pure MC test, and every single question ranged from "moderately difficult" to "ungodly hard". The MCQs nearly all had multiple answers, and the marking scheme strongly discouraged guessing - any wrong answer in a multi-answer question instantly made you lose all marks for the entire question, and your answers are graded on floor(correct * question_score / total_correct). The entire thing combined led to the lowest mean (30.7/100) I've ever seen in an HKUST exam, and possibly the hardest final paper I've had the (dis)pleasure of sitting through.

This is not to say that the final was set badly though. Each question definitely examined how clear your concepts are - get them slightly wrong and you're screwed. It is a great way to make sure you've got most of Java's trickier behaviours down.

The final exam is the real grade differentiator. Since everyone gets full marks in the PAs/labs/attendance, this means that each mark in the final matters much much more than anything else. The "one-exam-kills-all" approach is why the grading is a C here.

Getting full for labs and attendance, 99/81 for the PAs (oops), and 54/100 for the final results in an A.

From syllabus:

Bonus Scheme

Objective: Encourage students to further explore beyond the programming assignments
Means: When you have completed a bonus task in the assignment, you will get the bonus points accordingly
Bonus scheme: Be used as a "safety blanket" on the assignments capped by the maximum score (e.g. 100). It motivates hard-working students without sacrificing the less hard-working students
Examples:
If a student got 95 out of 100 for the basic tasks, but he got 6 bonus points, the final score will be: MIN(95+6, 100) = 100
If a student got 100 out of 100 and got 0 bonus points, the final score will be: MIN(100+0, 100) = 100

comment_content:
Same as previous semesters, basically teaching COMP2011 and 2012 but in Java, with the main addition being multi-threading and interface, which are quite java specific

You can definitely say that you are a competent Java programmer after taking the course
comment_teaching:
SCC quality teaching as usual, enthusiastic, patient, and knowledgeable in both java and software engineering in general

He's always explaining why the java designer make restrictions/syntax as they are, rather than just talking about the resulting designs

TAs are quite friendly, so do actively find them for help if you are stuck in PAs or labs.
comment_workload:
Main workload are the PAs, where you really need to start earlier (at least 1 week before deadline) to finish it. Rather than being tedious, they are generally shorter than the 2011/2012 ones, but require good understanding of the concepts and lots of googling in order to know how to implement some parts.

Labs generally take <1 hour to finish. 1 or 2 of them may be harder, but you can always ask the TA or even the prof for tips.

Final exam is open-book where you can bring anything (in paper) into the exam venue, so you don't have to spend time memorising the stuffs. The exam questions are quite tricky, so you should spend some time trying different special cases before the exam.
comment_grading:
42% assignment (3 of them, each 14%)
42% final (3% each for 20 MC questions, 40% for short questions)
8% lab (nearly all people get full marks)
8% quiz (you get full mark by participating in the zoom poll, you can actually take this attendance at home rather than having to be physically present in the classroom)

Unlike the chaotic last semester, the grading is clear and fair at least. I think it is way better than expected, I didn't even get upper quartile but got an A in the end.

comment_content:
The first half part is OOP in java. I think it is easier than C++ syntax-wise.

Then we have javafx, easy but kinda useless, just need to memorize things.

The real challenges starting from multi-threading to lambda.
comment_teaching:
Although the prof is not humorous as Desmond, you can feel the passion of him.

He's well prepared before lesson, he would like to try to explain why Java is designed this way so we can memorize more easily & guess how will Java behave in other similar new situations.

He also provided many well-designed quiz in every lessons. Those quiz will make you realized you don't understand the topic well enough although you think you are.
comment_workload:
Quiz is free to get, as long as you answer anything in the zoom poll in the beginning of the class, you will get the point.

Lab are easy, it should take around 2-3 hours for each.

The workload mainly come from assignments.
Each took around 2-3 days to finish.
There is no hard algorithm you need to write. The difficulty come from learning some Java features yourself.
There are some java18/javafx features not covered in the class but required you to use in the assignment. It took some time to learn.

For the exam, it depends on what grade you you want to get, personally I spent 3 days studying before the exam.

comment_content:
Java programming, consider it an extension of COMP2012.

Quite the overlap with C++ OOP in the first half of the semester, with new contents in the second half. Overall it is quite nice picking up a new language.
comment_teaching:
Good.

While he's no Desmond, Professor Cheung's lessons are interesting, with good live coding demos, useful quizzes and good explanations on topics. There's a quiz every lecture to count attendance, but it is conducted on Zoom so technically you don't have to be there.

However, it is recommended that you do attend the lectures because they are worth the time and since there are no lecture recordings.

Tutorials and Labs can be skipped fully, just complete the Labs and hand them in, the tutorials simply explain what to do in the Lab, which isn't necessary.
comment_workload:
Typical for COMP course, similar to COMP2011/12.

All labs take around 1-2 hours to complete, PAs may take a week or so.
comment_grading:
In-class Quizzes and Participation (8%): 2 quizzes every lecture, conducted on the Zoom, the questions are very useful for clearing up concepts, answers for them are released making for great study materials as well.

Laboratory exercises (8%): Simple labs, just follow the .md file in the project and complete them.

Course assignments (42%): 3 PAs, all around the implementation of a Sokoban game, PA1 plays in the terminal, PA2 runs in a GUI and PA3 implements a multithreaded replay system. Again all are simple and most people get over 90%.

Examination (42%): 1 final exam, unfortunately this is where the course gets worse. 20 MCQs, and 5 short programming questions, however all 5 short questions about generic programming, no multithreading, no javaFX, no inheritance. The MCQs are very difficult with multiple correct answers, and you get 0 points if you choose just 1 incorrect answer. Average is around 50%.

1% total below mean, C+, bad grading.

comment_teaching: Theaching is OK. Even good for UST. You can skip lectures and work on materials on your own. It could be even better than wasting time in the classroom.
comment_workload:
Do not listen to other reviews in terms of workload. Again, for many asians a task which is not mandatory means NO TASK. That's a silly approach. In fact, you need to spend 4-5 hours on materials+tasks+lab every week.
IF YOU WANT TO DO WELL IN THE EXAM:
There are so many past papers! Start a long time before the exams and it will help you a lot! Many students will look only on 10% of them. You can be better!
comment_grading:
Grading is good. Average = B. Labs are the thing you should take seriously and have 100/100. They are not difficult but time-consuming. Many local students want to finish them as soon as possible to go and play computer games. So, you can beat them in this part being very careful and attentive. The competition is extremely high in this course, so you have no right to make mistakes. There are also 2 grading schemes because some students (me including) have never experienced tests in programming world and can perform not satisfying their plans. You know, asians can do well without understanding much because they have tests in their DNA. That's why having 2 grading schemes is a good idea.

My results:
Labs - 100/100 (Avg: 92)
Midterm - 60/100 (Avg: 62)
Final: 79/100(Avg: 70)

comment_teaching:
One of the best professors I met at HKUST.

Professor Cheung is a very passionate professor, and his classes are of high quality. He has a deep understanding of software engineering, explains things clearly, and will always tell you HOW and WHY programmers (especially Java developers) do this instead of just teaching things.

He is willing to answer all your questions and respond to emails quickly (I usually get a reply in 10 minutes).

It's also valuable that he teaches the latest Java version with new features included.
comment_workload:
I think the workload is good. PA1 is a bit complicated since you need to think of all stuff clearly and there are a lot of codings. I think finish the PA is easy but it's hard to make it elegant.

PA2 and PA3 are shorter, as long as you understand the content, you can finish it very quickly.

Perhaps one of the workloads is the polling before each class (since 1 of the 2 classes is 9:00 AM), but as long as you participate in the polling, you get full marks.

The final exam is very difficult (if you have experienced his polling questions, you will know). There are 26 multiple-choice questions, each worth 2 points. Multiple correct answers are possible; you will lose all points if you select any one of the incorrect answers, and will get 2 * x / y points if you select x correct answers out of y correct answers. (This is really a workload for TA to calculate scores lol)
Remaining 48 points are programming questions, hand-written codes. They are basic stuff, and you should be okay if you pay attention to his lectures.

comment_content: Basically cover almost all important concept of java, beginner can manage to follow the content very well. Everything is not hard to understand and is covered in detail. It is not in a rush. I am very satisfied.
comment_teaching: I love the professor very much. He is fun and kind. However, there are too many mistakes caused by the TA team. I think they can be more serious and more careful as their mistakes are causing a lot of trouble for many people. I see people very mad about that.
comment_workload: In terms of a COMP programming course. I think the workload is very acceptable. The assignments and labs are quite easy and straightforward. The instruction is however not adequate in my opinion. You may have to send quite a lot of emails to ask for details.
comment_grading: As everything is quite easy, there is a huge competition in the course. You will assume you won't get a good grade if you make too many mistakes. Basically, you have to be perfectly careful if you want to win the course!

comment_content:
The lecture contents were okay. Students are assumed to be familiar with basic programming concepts like OOP, functions and variables. Java specific details about these concept were taught. Some new concepts untouched by COMP2011 and COMP2012 like producer/consumer, multithreaded programming are present.

The slides generally were adequately paced. Although you may need to chew on specific slide and look for online resouces to get a full grasp on more complicated subjects.

The assignments were too trivial and instructions are often unclear.
Important java specific concepts were not tested thoroughly in these assignments and you won't be improving significantly after completing them. Only the first assignment (OOP) put up to a moderate challenge and the others were a breeze.
Instructions were often unclear and leave some questions unanswered. You can ask the TAs or figure it out yourself. The added benefit is that you have to be more concious on the general behaviour on you program, but those who require detailed instructions may find it difficult.

The labs were actually harder and will test your knowledge on taught matters. There were some instructions given but you will have to figure out many things out by yourself.

C for actually teaching me java and got me an internship xD.
comment_teaching:
I never really paid attention in lectures so I can't comment on them.

But HOHO, the teaching team is very SUS.

Wrong/bugged files were given for labs. Test cases for assignment 4 are actually wrong and doesn't evaluate your code correctly. Wrong questions delivered for mid-term. Different exam length for different groups...

and they forgot to schedule my mid-term makeup session even if I informed my unavailability on the day that it was announced. Leaving me very confused when they released the mid-term marks. I've also heard that the new makeup mid-term (which I took) were harder than the original one, so that is a bit unfair for me.

Overall its like they make a mistake on every announcement and had to do a makeup for every real-time assesment that they made. It's like the professor never talks to the TAs and all of them are very SUS.

Rated E for Ejected.

comment_content:
不要抱有任何期望。

👎👎👎👎  𝗔𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 👎👎👎👎
Assigments 設計奇差，指引唔清晰，朝令夕改，製造混亂，未做好本份就搞 Bonus 分制度。例子就係一個 Split string task 要用 Delimiter 將一條 String 拆開，然 Print 一行一個。好啦做完出 Grade 見到最淺個 Task 被人扣咗分都打個突，結果原來 TA 就唔畀你 Replace delimiter with "\n"，佢一定要你轉做 array of String 再用 for loop print 出嚟。但份 Assignment 冇話過一定要先轉做 Array object，只係有簡短 Description, Input 同 Output 畀你去理解，結果你用咗 String class 嘅 replace method 就要扣你 5 分。明明份 Assignment 第一頁就講明係俾你熟悉 String API，但原來暗啞底唔用得 replace，反智到無倫。借問聲 Java 幾時將 replace 從 String class 入面剔除咗？既然你有咁嘅 Requirement 點解唔喺 Skeleton code 度擺埋相應 Test case 去消除 Ambiguity或者喺 Specification PDF 度 Explicitly 寫清楚？

👎👎👎👎 𝗠𝗶𝗱𝘁𝗲𝗿𝗺 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝘀 & 𝗙𝗶𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺 👎👎👎👎
有兩個 Midterm exam 一個 Final exam，每次都整一個 makeup version 嘅 exam。其中一個 Midterm 就係畀錯 Test file，令到 Test case 冇可能會 Pass 之餘你要浪費一大堆時間去 Debug。

最離譜係 Final exam 有兩位 TA 畀咗佢地組 20 分鐘去考，但其他組只係得 15 分鐘，事後出 Announcement 道歉都仲厚顏無恥地話其實 20 分鐘嘅人表現都唔係比 15 分鐘嘅人好，所以其實 20 分鐘都冇乜著數嘅，繼而叫覺得受影響嘅人 Join 返個 makeup exam 啦，但其實個 Makeup exam 都只會係畀你 15 分鐘㗎渣。

每次出錯都有 Makeup，但就無考慮過新安排到底公唔公平，明明自己出錯仲成日講成係 COVID period 畀學生嘅 Flexibility，恩賜嚟仲唔快啲講多謝。考試設計又係一個問題，分組考 20 條 True or False，運氣成份佔多，根本就考核唔到學生對 Java 嘅認知。

利申：我冇參與過任何 Makeup

👎👎👎👎 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 𝗶𝗻𝘁𝗲𝗿𝘃𝗶𝗲𝘄 👎👎👎👎
考完 Final 突如期來話要搞個 Code review interview 同 TA 一對一深情對話，話要捉 Plagiarism 喎。好啦我好彩本身冇Reg咁多科，但我都戥其他人慘。仲有抽到個口音重嘅 TA 連問題都聽唔明咁你就自求多福啦。好啦搞完一大輪 in 完，到頭來就話取消。白 in。

利申：in 完我好好彩係冇俾佢扣到任何分，但成件事根本多此一舉，話突擊其實都係有時間準備，成件事個成效同製造出嚟嘅麻煩衡量下就知根本多餘

📣📣📣📣 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝗰𝗹𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 📣📣📣📣

Garbage.

What I mean is garbage is just garbage.

Professor is a DJ. He knows how to play music but doesn't know how to teach Java.
e.g., he cannot answer students' questions, and his eclipse always gets a problem running the demo code.
We all know most of the professors in HKUST read ppt only, but he can't even read ppt in a fluent way.

Of course, there are in-class exercises, but they are useless. Those in-class exercises will not help your final exam since the final exam is purely T/F.

In conclusion, his arrangement and teaching are even worse than Dekai's. Sorry, Dekai.

comment_content:
To be honest, the content of Java is very important and interesting, but the powerpoints are horrible to read. Prof directly copied and pasted Desmond and Alex's PowerPoint, leading to an awkward situation where he cannot understand and explain many questions in the PowerPoint. Most of the time, I am self-learning via youtube and Bilibili.
There are four programming assignments. All of the four assignments are extremely near 100 points since they are just too easy and with too many bugs. I don't really understand why a COMP 3xxx course's programming assignment only tests a student's ability of how to use String Class's default method. Except for PA1, all the remaining PAs are just copies of the demonstration code from the textbook. Even the labs are more difficult than PAs but the labs are derived from the past years' lectures. TAs are too lazy to prepare anything solid.
comment_teaching: Don't read the PowerPoint and say "syntax sugar! more fun!" please. It should be better for the professor to do the research rather than teach students. To be honest, these four TAs are maybe the TAs with the most dazzling resume. But it doesn't influence that they know nothing about Java and know how to communicate with each other in teaching students. The happiest time of their cooperation should be finding a way to pull down the scores of some unlucky students, so as to complete the course better. What's more, improve your spoken English please!
comment_workload: After the final, we suddenly received an announcement that we need another code interview, due tomorrow. I mean what the fxxk? most of the students still have many exams to finish. They change the grading scheme implicitly only because TAs found that most students' overall grades are beyond 95, and they need a reason to drag down your students' grades. Then a clever and lovely professor came up with a solution: 60/109 students were picked up and interviewed after the exam period, and I got deducted points hahaha. TAs: even if you pass the final test and got 100, your implementation is not correct, you forgot to test this feature. But why you say if you can pass the given test.java your PA4 grade would have no problems when we submitted the PA4? Different TAs will ask you different questions, and your final grade will deduct. For the rest of the 49 students, I should say that God really blesses these lucky dogs.
comment_grading:
The most interesting part. My mom always taught me to tolerate others' fault, but after seeing these four TAs I suddenly understand not every man in the world has a mother.
If you are a born cheater, I should say that this is definitely the best course prepared in CSE for you. We have two mids and one final, and all of them have a make-up because of TAs' stupid mistakes. All three exams have many sessions, and if you can get the test paper from your friends, Congratulations! Because all the papers are remarkably the same. And you can do the test 1 hour earlier than others. The final is a 15 minutes' 20 T/F questions, but hey how can TAs make stupid mistakes in this simple test? 1. Well first, they got the wrong answer for the grading. 2. Then they suddenly found that two TAs mistakes up the exam period! and one-half of the students got  20 mins to finish the exam, too good! 3. Students took the test behind can directly got the paper from the previous session! 4. one-half of the students got  20 mins to finish the exam, unfair! How to fix that? We can give all the students to take make-up in 15minutes with much similar questions. Anyone with basic logic and common sense knows that the best remedy is for everyone to take the test again or give everyone a 20-minute make-up. But they don't want to. I don't know how they got into graduate school and Ph.D., Canvas shows that the students who take make-up are 3 points higher than the previous test on average, some of them even get high.
The overall mean on Canvas is 95.4, but the real one is much higher. 99 A range, 97 B range, 95 C range. Nice and fair grading indeed. I am ready to get C range now. Thank you TAs!
Update(WOW! PA4's grade is not decided by the result of the test case, but the result of the interview. I got 40 points deducted! Good!)

comment_teaching:
Since this part is ultimately long so I put the central idea first : When you see this prof teaching this course, PLEASE DON'T REG. THIS GUY IS DEKAI 2.0. Wait for SC Cheung or Alex. Or if you don't mind your elective requirement then just find another elective. FreeCodeCamp is your good friend for learning Java. Anyway, STAY AWAY FROM THIS PROFESSOR!!!

Great, talk about the teaching, I am indeed speechless but I still try to describe it out with words so that other people can know what is happening.

First, talk about the professor, he is a quite chill guy. He plays music before lecture starts. However, he speaks very very slow. Even I play the lecture recording in 1.25x I still find no difference from a normal person talking. Also, he hesitates a lot. These make me hard to concentrate. Moreover, it seems like he doesn't know Java very clearly. Quite often when students asked questions, he didn't know how to answer. I think he is not a really professional Java programmer.

However, the lecture is not the worst thing. The worst thing is his course management strategies.

The most annoying thing is, his plan keeps changing. At the first lecture, he proposed for 3 PAs and a regular final exam. But at a particular instance of the semester, he changes his mind by adding one more PA (which due during final exam period), shrinking (not cancel) the scale of final (in terms of both amount and grade proportion) and adding unknown amount of assessment tasks. THANK YOU PROF

Ok so these are still not quite a big deal compared to the below things. Here comes the real annoyance

When everyone feels happy and relieved about the semester finally ends...

Professor : SUrPRisE MoThERFuCkER

Guess what. He add an assessment task (2nd coding interview) AFTER THE FINAL EXAM, without any prior notification. It just comes randomly, arbitrarily, out of thin air. WHAT THE FUCK?

Originally, the coding interview was announced on 26 May and due on 27 May (I am not lying, one day due). This triggered a lot of students because 27 and 28 May still has final exam (For me I have COMP2211 and COMP3111 final in these two days) and the due date is short. Therefore, he changes his mind AGAIN. Instead of asking every students to submit a respond document, he randomly picked 60 students (in 109 students) to have a Zoom interview on 30 May, which is today.

I just can't get it why he insists to hold a interview RANDOMLY and AFTER FINAL. Do you know how I feel about that?

I think many of you have played New Super Mario Bros Wii right? When I played it for the first time, I feel relieved that Bowser is finally defeated in the last castle stage. However, a witch comes out and after some magic, Bowser recovers and become giant. When I see that I think : OMG I still need to beat Bowser again? That's my feeling

For some translation : When I feel relieved that final period ends, this course ends, the whole semester ends, this motherfuxker gives out a pop-up assessment, which frustrates me deeply. I just want some rest.

Also, I can't get it why he randomly picked 60 people only, which will make the situation more unfair.

Another thing really triggered me is that, even though the course is in complete chaos and professor is clearly torturing us, he still thinks that he is a nice person, these things help us and he does a good job. He claimed that these policies are helping us to "overcome the hard covid period" and "give us more practical practice for Java" . I just got annoyed by these nonsense.

Then, the TAs, which are the assistants of professor torturing us. They are not only useless in the lab (Only repeat the ppt), but also nearly made mistakes in any part of their work, which worsen the situation. They are like coming to troll and sabotage us. Here are some examples :
1. Labs contained many bugs
2. PA contains BUGS and WRONG TEST CASES
3. In the midterm coding demo, they gives out WRONG TEST CASES as well, which seriously affect the judgement of students under such a pressure test
4. In the final, two of the TAs mistaken the exam time from 15 mins to 20 mins

I think these mistakes are very ridiculous, unreasonable and unprofessional. I have never meet a programming course which contains these huge amount of mistakes (We all should appreciate the professional COMP2011 and COMP2012 teaching team).

So everything went wrong, and how did the professor deal with it? Make-up. Make-up. Make-up. Anything went wrong just make-up. All midterm and final has make-up (Not maximum policy, so if you screwed up in make-up. Congratulations, you waste time and effort in decreasing your score). I just feel tired when we have 4 midterms and 2 finals in one course. When I am dealing with this course, I hope my life can also have a make-up then I will straightly jump mushroom right now. Unfortunately this is not true.

Although prof try to compensate us by offering make-up, I just got annoyed by them. Instead of make-up everything, he should ensure things do not go wrong (Just look at 2011 or 2012, will these programming course be this chaotic?). This is the duty and responsibility of being the course coordinator. It is infuriating that the teaching team made a lot of mistakes but students need to take the accountability and join the make-up. Why? What did we do wrong? We did nothing. All mistakes come from your fucking stupid teaching team. And now we need to waste more time and effort to prepare for those make-upssssss in order to compensate your fault?????? Moreover, the repeated mistakes also implies that professor and TA never learn from history, which is very disappointing.

Also, although the professor tries to make the course more fruitful and more practical, unfortunately, they need to be executed CORRECTLY. Otherwise, it will backfire. This professor genuinely does not know how to manage a course but he keep proposing more ideas to make the situation even worse. Currently, everything went wrong and every student in this course is suffering. This course is off the track in any possible direction and it is full of unfairness. It is a catastrophic failure. If you cannot ensure things can be executed correctly, then please don't propose them out. Just adopt the usual course structure. Your proposed changes just created a more unfair situation and tortured students more. WHAT IS THE POINT?

The only good thing from this course is that the professor opened a complain board (forum), where students can complain anonymously. GOOD, because now everyone can see how we suffered from this piece of shit. Everyone can see how desperate, helpless, frustrated and angry we are.

Very probably the fucking prof and TAs deal with none of the complaints; but have been reading it with a big smile on their face, just like those celebrities watching player falling from the glass bridge one-by-one in Squid Game.

If you are interested, please access the website below (I swear it is not virus or rickroll. If it is then i get five Ds in this semester)

Website of complain board : https://xoyondo.com/mb/2WXq6OJsZWP5cZk

I use 3 songs to summarize this course. Prof I recommend you to play them in your lecture because they fits you:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=X-KwYX2u8e4
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gkTb9GP9lVI
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_B0CyOAO8y0
(ps : 1.25x speed is recommended for the third one)

(I can't believe I used half hour to criticize a course...But I am very angry about this course so I just did it. If here has option of F I will definitely give all Fs)

(Update 31/5/2022)
Now the prof made a new BIG TROLL. For our PA4, he proposed a new marking scheme today suddenly. Aside from the correctness of the code (output checking), now the semantics are also take into account. Specifically, "Whether your code is robust and close to the best practice for the programming task" (From prof. email). That is, if your output is correct but you write spaghetti code then you will still got point deduction.

(The above coding review part is applicable for non-interview students only. For interview student, they will consider interview performance instead.)

In 109 of students, 78 students got marks deduction, either -12 marks or -24 marks out of 100 marks

I GOT DEDUCTED BY 24 MARKS. WOW

I mean...it is good to ask student not to write spaghetti code and practice for a good programming habit. However,

YOU NEVER MENTION IT BEFORE

How can your marking scheme change suddenly after we submitted the assignment? Does that even sound fair??? University always emphasize honesty and warns student not to cheat, but now this professor shows ZERO HONESTY and cheated on us.

I start suspecting the whole teaching team did these things deliberately due to some reasons (Revenge?). I can't imagine the whole teaching team is stupid as fuck and did EVERYTHING wrong.  Moreover, the teaching team start trolling and sabotage us after the SFQ is due so that we cannot change the ratings. GOOD. I will blacklist this prof forever.

Anyway, I am done with this course. As long as you don't give me F then I am contented.

(Update 31/5/2022 13:27)
Fine, since too many students complained, the professor finally gives up this new policy
comment_workload:
1.) 9 Labs (2 can be skipped) + 4 PAs
Usual programming course work. Nothing special.

One interesting thing is, for this course, labs are even harder than PA. I don't know why. Moreover, in this course, labs and PA lacks instructions. Quite often I just got lost when reading the lab ppt or PA description. TA are also useless so you will got lost easily.

2.) Lecture exercise
a.k.a. attendance. Not every lectures have but it is pop-up randomly so you cannot skip class unless you have friends to carry you. Even though you submit piece of shit you still get the point.

3.) 2 midterm coding demo (+ 2 make-up)
Midterm exam. However, it is in coding format. Specifically, you will get the problem description at the beginning of the midterm. Then, you need to write programs to solve it and submit to Canvas. The time limit is short, if you are not familiar enough you may got stuck and cannot finish the tasks.

Unfortunately, the logistics are in a mess. As mentioned in "Teaching" part. TAs perform badly in handling the exam as they can gives out WRONG TEST CASES. Moreover, we are divided into four timeslots to attend the exam. Some students claimed that the problem in different time sessions are same or similar and this may lead to cheating or unfair situation. As a result, both midterms got make-up

4.) Final (+ make-up)
As mentioned before, 20 T/F questions. Lottery time

Also, as mentioned before, due to two stupid TAs mistaking the exam time limit and the subsequent unfair situation, we got to make-up AGAIN

5.) 2 coding interview
You will need to propose modifications or new features to existing lab tasks or PA (Maybe prof or TAs are lazy to design the tasks for next year). The first one you need to submit document. The second one is Zoom interview and only 60 out of 109 students need to attend.

------------------------------------------------------------
As a 3 cred course, the workload is somewhat equivalent to or even more than comp2011 or comp2012, which are 4 cred course. And the number of exams...BRAVO

Moreover, I think the toughest workload is that we need to bear the repeated and repeated mistakes made by the TAs and the unreasonable arrangement from the professor. The psychological impact and frustration is much more heavier than those physical workload. At the end of the course, my heart is just tired as fuck.
comment_grading:
1. Labs 10%
2. Lecture exercise 10%
3. Programming assignments (10%+15%+15%+15%)
4. Mid-term coding show (5%+10%)
5. Final exam (10%)
6. Coding interview (??%+??%)
7. Bonus of finding error (+2% max)

The only thing I would say is : GARBAGE

The proportion of daily assessment and exam assessment is 75:25. For a programming course, it is a nightmare. Because everyone will just get full marks in daily assessment, which means everyone has got 75% by default (If not then you have already died). Moreover, the mid-term coding shows are also easy. Many people just get >90% or even get bonuses.

So the only component to determine your fate is the final. And guess what is the format?

20 T/F questions, in 15 mins

Great, my grade just comes from luck. Even if you don't know something, you can treat it as a random lottery and you may still get good grade because P( getting a question right) = 1/2. RIDICULOUS.

As a result, everyone will very likely to get >90%, and imagine getting overall >90% but ends up a C range, how will you feel?

Someone makes joke:
100% : A
99% : B
98% : C
97% : D
<=96% : F

I doubted whether it is joke now

These assessments and grading scheme just failed to discriminate students' ability so they are indeed TRASH.

Moreover, if you ask why do I put question marks in the proportion of coding interview, that's because even the professor nor the course website didn't mentioned the proportion at all. The two coding interview just popped out randomly. We are being told and threatened that they may affect our grade and if we don't do the interview then we will receive marks deduction. That's all. It is non-transparent as fuck

Ok so now I finally know what the coding interview is for. Please see the updated part (the last paragraph) in "TEACHING" (Update 31/5/2022)

Grade has not been released yet. After released I will update. But being a newbie of Java and make quite a few mistakes, very probably I will get C range. RIP my cga

Even though the grade is unexpectedly good, I will still regard this course is a trash course since the arrangement is in chaos and ridiculous.

comment_content:
Build on prior knowledge of Java programming.

comment_workload:
I found the workload not too hard, the Labs are very easy - with mandatory attendance, althought you do not need to submit the lab during the lab session which meant that a lot of students ended up just going to the lab session in order to sign their name.

comment_content:

comment_teaching: Amazing professor who explains everything very clearly. I highly suggest going to the lectures because they are actually really helpful.  His English is good as well. TAs were very nice and supportive and open to questions/help.
comment_workload:
Workload is quite light.

In class quizzes: in reality, this is just attendance. The quizzes are done in PRS but you just have to respond to get the points (no need to get correct answers).

Labs: Attendance every week. Not difficult tasks. Have to submit either during lab session or to CASS by the weekend.

Assignment: Quite time consuming, so don't leave them to do at the very end. 2 assignments in total. First assignment is due after midterms, and focuses on classes/inheritance/interface etc. Second assignment is due before finals and focuses on GUI, event driven programming, and some multithreading

Midterm: ~1 hr long. Open notes. Half MCQ and half code writing. MCQ is quite confusing (can choose multiple) and need thorough understanding to choose all correct answers. Code writing is straightforward. The javadoc for necessary functions are provided.

Final: Similar but 3 hours long

comment_content:
Up-to-date content that most important Java updates have been well covered. Good for beginner, and also informative for students who already has a little Java background.
Java10 this semester, also very little Java11 (released in Sept 2018) content (not examined).

Since COMP2011/2012 provided most of the OOP syntax and characteristic, this course focuses on the OOP features that is unique for Java or not covered in COMP2011/2012.

Major Topics:
Polymorphism, abstract class, interface, GUI using JavaFX, event driven programming, lambda/high-order functions
comment_teaching:
Detailed explanation. You will gain a lot by attending the lectures.

Prof. Cheung encourages students to in in the Java developer's perspective to inspect a particular feature.
He also likes making unusual operations to Java and ask "what will this output". It is reflected through his iPRS and exam questions.
comment_workload:
Similar to but a little lighter than COMP2011/2012, which is medium for a 3-credit course.
Lecture iPRS attendance.
12 labs.
2 programming assignments.

Attendance is recorded as long as you submitted an answer of iPRS, regardless of correctness.

Labs are much simpler than COMP2011/2012 lab. Each can be finished in an hour.

Each PA requires around 8-20 hours to complete (including debugging and document works)
comment_grading:
For exam difficulty:

Desmond tends to make easy but tricky MCs, lengthy and time-consuming Long-questions.

Prof. Cheung prefers making deep and difficult MCs, which usually require choosing 2-5 answers from 5 choices and 1 wrong answer causes 0 mark in the whole question.
Long-questions are much shorter but focused. A 4-credit question can test in various concepts and require students to be extremely careful.


Overall grading:

Fair, and better than UST grading standard
iPRS 100% attendance.
Assignments full mark.
Mid-term above mean 1.5 SD.
Final above mean 1 SD.
A.

comment_teaching:
Professor Cheung did have passion in each lesson. He would prepare a lot of things to the lectures such as demo programs and extra exercise.

One bad thing was his boring tune and lesson atmosphere which often made me fall asleep.
comment_workload:
2 iPRS quizzes were provided in each lesson, making the lessons compulsory.

Lab attendance was compulsory too. After each lab tutorial, you would have 1-week time to finish the lab work.

2 PAs were related. PA1 was a text-based Sokuban project in which TO-DO parts focused on the logic part of the game. Based on PA1, PA2 was a GUI version of the game in which TO-DO parts focused on the GUI part of the game, event handling and multi-threading.

Basic Github skills were required.

As 'Content' mentioned, Midterm focused on upper half of the course, and Final covered all things with great focus on Event Handling~Functional Interfaces. One thing I want to mention is the MC part of the exams. Be careful as correct options are more than one. If you pick more than correct answers, you will get 0 mark.
