Grade: B+

comment_content:
Very fruitful and rich content. We learned a brand new topic almost every week. Many other reviews have listed all topics, so I will not repeat them. Generally speaking, the course teach you Scala and a lot of magical ways to write code. From my perspective, the content is very interesting. It covers many interesting ideas that you can see in modern programming languages. They can be completely different from Python and C++, so it can be challenging if you only have experience in those two programming languages.

Some people might think the content is useless. I admit that a small part of the content, such as logic programming, is like art. It is actually beautiful, but only a small number of people can admire it, and it cannot help you make money. However, a large proportion of the content is useful in the sense that it can be migrated to other programming languages or tasks. For example, you can see pattern matching in many other languages (and potentially even C++ in the future!). Also, observer and signal models are widely applied in frontend frameworks. In summary, if you view the content from a wider perspective, not just from Scala, you may find it is even more useful than you think.
comment_teaching:
C for lectures. I have to say LP does not teach very well although he is a good person. I feel like he assumes us to have decent knowledge in programming languages, and does not explain everything in a way that most of the students can understand. For a student who does have some prior knowledge, the lectures may be enjoyable. However, if some student does not have any background, they may feel they are not the right audience of the lectures and the teaching is too fast. In that case, attending lectures is probably not more helpful than reading the slides.

A+ for lab sections. I think this course has the best lab section design in all courses I have taken. The labs took place in one of the CS Labs. In half of the weeks, we are assigned some coding tasks and write actual Scala code. In the other weeks, we were divided into groups to complete written questions on the whiteboards. For both formats, the TAs would walk around the room to see how everyone is doing and whether someone need help. At the end of each section, they also explained the solution. The lab sections help me a lot in understanding the topics, practicing writing Scala code, and even preparing for exams. They are very interactive and meaningful, which is extremely rare in the department. Especially, I would like to worship Anto Chen here for his fantastic teaching, patience and helpfulness.

Besides, the teaching team created a discord server for us to ask questions and have discussions. The instructors and TAs are also reachable there, so the support we can get is more than sufficient. Recordings were also posted.
comment_workload: There are only 3 PAs, so the workload from homework is not high. However, if you want to understand everything in the course, you need to put in a lot of time and effort. For me, the workload is higher than 3711 but lower than 4211.
comment_grading:
- 30% PA (3 PAs in total, 10% each)
- 30% midterm
- 40% final

PAs are very interesting and reasonably challenging. I think they have top tier design among all COMP courses. The hardest one is PA3, which has a lot of small points that are easy to forget.

PA1 (Trees) : LQ=9 / Mean=9.31 / Median=10 / UQ=10 / High=10 | Me=10
PA2 (Tic-Tac-Toe) : LQ=9 / Mean=9.40 / Median=10 / UQ=10 / High=10 | Me=10
PA3 (Lisp Interpreter) : LQ=9 / Mean=9.23 / Median=10 / UQ=10 / High=10 | Me=9

I checked some reviews from previous semesters and I believe PA3 was the same as last year. However, the stats were much higher, so I really suspect that some students used AI (or answers from previous semesters) to complete the assignment.

Midterm and Final are kind of hard, but more manageable than 3711. The main challenge is the 2-hour time limit. I think the questions are fun and well designed. One or two of the questions can be found in the past papers provided by prof, so do check past papers. However, don't rely on past papers too much. For example, Lisp almost always appear in the finals of previous semesters but did not appear this time, but logic programming, which never appeared before, was included this time and made up a lot of points. A bad thing I would like to mention is that the exams only covers several topics in the course, while others are not tested at all (as I wrote in content section, we have a brand new topic almost once a week), which makes the exams not that comprehensive.

The grading of this semester is kind of puzzling. The stats of the midterm is so low compared to previous semesters that LP decided to turn a 10-point question into bonus, and the total marks of midterm changed from 40 to 30. They also gave some bonus points in the final, but I don't know how it worked. LP said the course uses criteria referencing instead of a curve to assign letter grades, so the changes in scores mentioned above would improve everyone's grade, although I don't fully understand how. Anyway, the stats below, if I understand it correctly, already include bonus, and the scores higher than full marks (30 in midterm and 40 in final) are normalized to full marks.

Midterm : LQ=11.75 / Mean=18.81 / Median=20 / UQ=26 / High=30 | Me=30 (originally 35.25)
Final : LQ=13.13 / Mean=21.93 / Median=22 / UQ=28.88 / High=40 | Me=40 (originally 43.5)

p.s. I met a lot of daai lou in this course, so do be careful >.<

comment_content:
My god it feels like a cult when reading these reviews.

I want to remind everyone who is thinking about taking this course: usually reviews are pretty polarised, either they are really happy with the course or they wanna flex their grades so they write a review to compliment, or because they hate the course so much so that they need to express their anger; in many cases, they will be biased, thus it is very hard to judge whether a good course in good or not.

Take for example comp 3311, which is universially hated by everyone because the professor sucks and the slides suck; but after taking it, I don't believe that is the case here. While the professor does look like sleepy joe, he still puts effort into expressing new ideas just like a normal human would, and while the layout of the slides are legit ugly, the content is actually quite fulfilling that they are really good for self study.

What I'm trying to say is, a single review can't and will never show the full picture. Even though I will try to be as objective as possible, I will indirectly say something biased thus leading to anger to those who genuinely love the course. For those who taken have this already, please don't dislike this review because you disagree, instead leave a comment and have a valuable discussion.

Alright let's actually talk about comp 3031.

Lecture 1: Introduction & Scala Basics
Lecture 2: Tail Recursion & Function Values
Lecture 3: Classes 1
Lecture 4: Classes 2
Lecture 5: Bounds, Variance, Lists
Lecture 6: Tuples, More on lists
Lecture 7: Reasoning about lists, Maps
Lecture 8: Comprehensions, combinatorial search
Lecture 9: Queries, Random Generators
Lecture 10: Monads, Structural induction on trees
Lecture 11: Lazy Evaluation
Lecture 12: Type-directed Computation, Contexts and Type Classes 1
Lecture 13: Contexts and Type Classes 2
Lecture 14: State, Application Example
Lecture 15: Reactive Programming
Lecture 16: Lisp & Scheme
Lecture 17: Interpreting Scheme
Lecture 18: Logic programming 1
Lecture 19: Logic programming 2
Lecture 20: Concurrent and Reactive Programming (1/2)
Lecture 21: Concurrent and Reactive Programming (2/2)

This might be one of the most fulfilling course I have ever seen, there is just so much content covered that I think if you have coded with Scala before I think you will really enjoy this course by learning the fundementals of a programming language itself. And that's the problem, you need to have Scala experience in order to enjoy this. Unless you're a genius and/or you have a lot of time (I don't, I have 19 credits this semester and I'm terrible at time management), you will be overwhelmed by the sheer amount of stuff you need to have deep understanding of.

In some sense, even though they're mixed together, you can think of this course as 2 parts, Scala and Programming Principles, just like comp 2011 and comp 2012 with C++ and OOP. So this is an issue isn't it? The course is labelled as a 3 credit course even though you're basically taking comp 2012h but in Scala. Sure the workload in lower (more on that later), but there is a legit a language gap (no pun intented) when learning the content, which is why I think a lot of people are frustrated with the course; for me, because a lot concepts are taught using Scala, it feels like I'm learning math using Japanese while learning Japanese at the same time.

Looking back, I don't think the content is bad, but I think the course needs to have an overhaul to adjust to students who don't have a strong background in non-imperative programming languages, especially when the prerequiste is literally C++. Think like this, I mostly play osu!, so even though I'm very good at aiming, I am extremely bad at playing CS2 because I cannot convert those mouse skills when playing matchmaking and I don't have the basics of FPS games down. It just wouldn't make sense to play mm without even knowing the basics of controlling a gun.

So would breaking this course into 2 courses be better? Maybe? While learning Scala separately would certainly close the gap, I certainly understand that this would waste a lot of time for some who wants to take comp 4121 in spring. What about just skip Scala and use Python/C++ to learn programming paradigms? Again maybe..., but this would make a lot of changes to the course though (and the professor hates C++ apparently). I will let the instructor solve this problem.

Pattern matching changed my life btw
comment_teaching:
Man.

comment_workload:
As a 3000 level course, around B.

The 3 homeworks are all programming assigments, with all 3 trying to applying concepts taught in class.

PA1: making trees using pattern matching, not bad but because I don't have experience in Scala, it took me almost a week of pain and suffering to finish it.

PA2: making tic tac toe game and somehow using comp 2211 stuff (minmax and pruning) and lazy evaluation to create a ttt AI. Took 2 days as I was slightly more comfortable in Scala.

PA3: modifying Lisp Interpreter to add extra functions. Again almost a week of pain and suffering because again, I lack fluency in Scala so I didn't know what to do until :worship_q: taught me how to do it.

One more thing: labs/exercise sections are mandatory but they don't count for anything, but holy shit are they hard af. My advice is really take this time to ask TA on ANY question, and you might just able to enjoy this course.

Edit:
Just a quick note here, by B I mean PASSING, if you wanna get A range, you will need need quite a lot more time self studying, making this D or even E

comment_content:
The course goes over mainly functional programming and some data structures, alongside with some other programming paradigms over the end of the semester generally using Scala.

The first half of the semester focuses on functional programming with an in-depth dive into various parts of the Scala programming language. Refer to 151044's review for the exact topics (the list of topics here is a LazyList.)

The midterm was rather unconventional this semester, but it was surely interesting. As usual, it has a focus on (higher-order) functions, subtyping and variance, pattern matching. There's also a question on laziness and I had much fun figuring out the one-liners :P

Hasn't had the final exam yet and will update this review when that happens.

The course has much richer content than others, but the examinations don't really cover everything (not enough space...). Monads (simply monoids in the category of endofunctors) were not tested this midterm.
comment_teaching:
Professor Parreaux teaches quite well! The concepts covered in class are elaborated nicely, the materials were well-prepared and he answers questions. A 10-minute break is featured between two "sections" of the lecture :D

This year, the professor added examples of "implementation of similar concepts" found in the course in other programming languages like Haskell, OCaml, Rust, etc. which is quite interesting in my opinion. Notably, he left the slide on C++ empty because it is an unreasonable language.

The professor and the teaching team are readily available to answer questions in the Discord server, whether it was questions about the lectures, assignments or general chatting.

The labs are well delivered which is quite a rarity. They alternate between computer and pen-and-paper sessions. In the former you are given a small programming tasks in Scala to complete individually; while in the latter people form groups to work out exercises on a whiteboard (good practice for examinations honestly).

However, the pace of the lectures could be quite fast and may be hard to follow. Reviewing concepts after lecture is definitely a plus.

Oh, and, C++ is an unreasonable language (why am I taking 2012 with this ._.)
comment_workload:
Only aiming to do well in this course then the workload is not too high. The practice papers provided should be enough as for topics covered in the examinations.

If you aim to grasp ALL concepts from the course then... good luck, it might take some time.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme is as follows:

30% programming assignments (10% each)
30% midterm examination
40% midterm examination

Lab (LA1) attendance is mandatory and will be taken.
There is another LA2 section for those who have documented time conflicts and watch the recordings of the labs instead.

Assignments are tricky yet interesting. Make sure to think before writing any code - don't yolo it. Assignment 1 was the implementation of a simple tree data structure. Assignment 2 was about tic-tac-toe and required implementing minimax and alpha-beta pruning (COMP2211 flashback moment). Assignment 3 was the Lisp dialect (Scheme--) interpreter, and required implementation of some additional features.

comment_content:
An introduction course to programming language theories taught in Scala 3. You are expected to get familiar with basic Functional Programming concepts and tricks, subtyping and generics, basic structural proofs for immutable data structures, lazy data structures, and etc. You will need to implement a simpliefied Scheme interpreter from skeleton codes.

The course also quickly goes over the topics of Logic Programming, Asynchronous Programming, and Actor Systems.

This course is interesting if you want to learn about programming language theories. You may refer to the book *Types and Programming Languages* by Benjamin C. Pierce as a complement of the course materials, or if you want to learn the course without taking it.

The teaching team hosts a Discord server where you can discuss the course contents with the professor/TAs/classmates, as well as ask for hints for assignments. The TAs give timely and helpful responses.

comment_content:
Content focuses mainly on using Scala as a tool for learning about functional programming and some of the theories used in understanding this paradigm. Other paradigms and languages are touched on near the end of the semester, like Prolog and Lisp, but it is still very much connected to Scala and how to implement this with Scala.
The very last topics (Actors, ...) are hard to understand and taught through very fast, but they are also not tested.
comment_teaching: The teaching is adequate and the prof will answer any questions if you have them. There is also an official discord where the prof, TAs and peers will help in answering questions and homework.
comment_workload:
There is almost a lab every week, but the work or attendance is not graded so just treat it like an useful reviewer.
There are 3 programming assignments that have about 2 weeks for each, and the assignments are not hard to the point they require all those days to work on them. A lot of guidance is given in the instructions for the assignments and you can also just ask for help in the discord.
There is a midterm and a final, both openbook. Since there are past papers given on canvas you can get a good feeling of what would be tested and it would not be hard. This is especially true for the final because the hardest parts to understand/implement are not tested, so just referencing past papers' ranges is a great way to review and wouldnt be too heavy.
comment_grading: Grading is ok, with most of it being on the exams and 30% on the assignments.

comment_content:
The course is largely inspired by the book SICP (Structure and Interpretation of Computer Program) and the eponymous course at MIT. It uses Scala instead of Lisp, which I think isn't a very good choice, since Scala is much more convoluted language with a lot of syntactic quirks, but it may be easier to learn for people used to Java/C++.

If you have prior knowledge of SICP, it's really easy. If you don't, that's still ok, because Prof. Parreaux goes quite in detail in explaining all the necessary concepts.

Given that the topics covered in the course are quite foundational in computer science, it's probably a good idea to take this course to learn them.

Topics covered include: Scala, functional programming, higher-order functions, referential transparency, currying, logic programming, concurrency, object-oriented programming, type system (generics and covariance/invariance) and writing a Lisp interpreter.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Parreaux is really good, he explains things in detail. All lectures are recorded.

Speaks good English with a slight French accent. As a proper Frenchman, he has a moustache.
comment_workload:
The assignments require some effort. For the exams, you don't have to memorise any material (you can print a cheat sheet), but you do need to understand it.

Btw, everybody signs an attendance sheet in the labs, but when I asked the TA in person about attendance, he told me that they actually don't do anything with the attendance sheets, so it's not a problem if you skip the labs.

comment_content:
As the title suggests, this is a course on the general principles of programming languages (but we somehow ended up doing everything in Scala anyways....?)

The first half of the course is a in-depth dive into different parts of the Scala 3 language, with a focus on the functional aspect of it.
Topics of this part include:
Evaluation strategies (Call-by-name and Call-by-value)
Higher order functions (Functions returning functions and currying)
Immutability and Object-Oriented Programming in Scala (Abstract classes, immutable data structures such as Cons-Lists, and companion objects)
Common Functional Constructs (Map, Filter, Reduce, Folds, and Pattern Matches on case classes)
Subtyping and Variance (What is the type of a function or a method? When is a function a subtype of the other?)
Proofs on Immutable Data Structures (Lists and Trees, Structural Induction)
Maps (the Dict kind in Python) and For-Expressions (For-loops, but functionally)
Monads, Monoids, and Higher-Kinded Types
Lazy evaluation (Lazy lists and infinite sequences)
Context management in Scala (the using and given constructs)
State and Referential Transparency

The second half of the course is a whirlwind tour of other programming paradigms, but still grounded in Scala because that is the language we use to implement most of these paradigms (and definitely *not* because LP likes Scala):
Reactive Programming (Observers and Functional Reactive Programming)
Lisp and Scheme (Both writing programs in Lisp and implementing a subset of Scheme using Scala are included)
Prolog (Logic programming, matching and unification)
Parallel Programming (Futures and their composition, Async callbacks)
Actor Systems

The midterm focused on pattern matching and higher-order functions (which are going to be your bread-and-butter), subtyping, and structural induction.

The finals focused on pure functional programming (moving a imperative program to functional), Lisp (Writing and translating programs in Lisp with Lisp), the using construct, and functional representations of booleans (Church booleans).

The assignments and labs had a heavy focus on immutable data structures (to the point it was an inside joke of mine to refer to this course as Data Structures in Scala).

Overall, the course is *extremely* content rich, and certainly a must-take if you're interested in functional programming and/or interested in learning more about the listed topics.

Also fair warning: This course is quite hard.

(Don't take this course if you hate Scala).
comment_teaching:
Prof Parreaux (LP) taught quite well. He elaborated on the concepts presented quite nicely. The teaching materials were well-prepared, and had a few exercises for you to work out if you want extra practice. He was willing to answer questions or slow down his pace when we're confused about something (for example the topic on variance and subtyping). He (and his teaching team) was readily available to answer questions on the course Discord server, whether it be course concepts, assignment questions, or general chit-chat.

The labs were well-delivered and interactive (which is quite a rarity in HKUST). They were divided into exercise sessions (where a few of us formed groups and worked out pen-and-paper exercises related to the course material on the lab whiteboard), and the labs (which were less interactive (but no less interesting) and focused on individual completion of Scala coding tasks).

One nitpick about the lecture delivery is that the pace is quite fast - you'll end up confused even if you're trying to follow along. Spend some time clearing up the concepts and you'll be fine.
comment_workload:
If you only aim to do well in the course, then the workload is moderate - do the assignments a bit earlier, do the practice paper(s) that LP provides, and you should be good to go. Although the provided midterm prep questions were... not on the same difficulty level as the actual midterm, they did prepare us for the coverage of the midterm quite well. (Perhaps it's good to note that LP's exams can be.... interesting when he puts his mind to it).

Trying to grasp *all* the knowledge in the course, however, will drop the workload somewhere around a C or a D. Quite a bit of the course materials are not examined, and you can get a nice grasp on what would according to the sample papers. That means that there are actually lots of things that aren't examined, and some of them are still a mystery to me after the semester (the part of higher-kinded types, actor systems, and functional reactive programming is still confusing for me after the semester).

Unlike what I've read in the other course he teaches (COMP 4901U), the labs here aren't work-intensive. You show up, finish whatever is being asked of you, then you're good to go. Remember to sign the attendance sheet though.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme is normal, per COMP course standards:
30% Assignments (10% each)
30% Midterm
40% Finals

Lab attendance is mandatory and will be taken, though not counted towards the final letter grade. No idea what happens if you miss one - though try not to since they are quite fun.

Assignments are surprisingly tricky and require a lot more thinking before writing any code - the assignments are like COMP 3711's in this regard. Assignment 1 was relatively easier (a n-ary tree), Assignment 2 was painful (a Rope data structure which had tons of special cases), and Assignment 3 (extending the Scheme interpreter to support objects, pattern matching, and call-by-need) required quite a bit of time in order to work out where even to begin. Plan your time accordingly.

Church booleans? Fun!... but quite scary in the exam, especially the subtyping question). comment_content:
For the actual content, please refer to the other reviews. These are my opinion on the contents:

Overall, the course focused too much on "compilation" of the languages (at least for my liking), rather than important concepts in those paradigms that we would actually apply irl. Quoting another review, if you don't want to be a human compiler, do not take this course.

1. Functional Programming (SML)

On the choice of the language, SML is quite an old, I would even say outdated, language. There are more prominent purely functional programming languages out there such as Haskell which have much more use then SML.

As an intro to functional programming, the course content focused too much on syntax and how the interpreter works (type inference) rather than important FP concepts like partially applied functions and currying. Instead, the only FP concept I have learnt (which is already known by all CS student) is recursion.

However, it still managed to cover popular FP functions such as map and filter, so I would not regard this part as completely useless.

2. Grammars and Expressions (Flex and Bison)

IMO easier part of the course, may be (or may not be) useful in programming projects. Check Github and you will find some projects using Flex and Bison, tho not a lot of them.

3. Logical Programming (Prolog)

Again, a lot of syntax and inner workings of the interpreter (prolog search tree). My main complain is that the course material failed to capture the different mindset in prolog from C++ and even Functional programming. By focusing on syntax, it is hard for an average student like me to catch up with how think in a way that helps me to code in prolog. The low average score of PA2 clearly reflected this failure. Prolog is a beautiful language, and this course did not do it justice.

4. Procedural Programming (parameter passing and scoping)

Purely conceptual, little to no coding. This part teaches parameter passing methods that are more niche. May use these concepts in some languages, but not a lot of them.

5. Parallel Programming (CUDA)

This is the part I found the most fun in learning. An intro to parallel programming in CUDA, multi-thread programming for the GPU. Because the GPU has much more threads than the CPU, it is more suited for parallel programming, and CUDA is the more popular one for such a use. The lecture mainly used matrix computation as examples, which is quite clear and illustrative.

All in all, if I could turn back time, I would not have taken this course.

comment_content:
This course teaches you different programming paradigm and more philosophical way of programming languages. It also check whether you are clever enough to learn other style of programming within one semester.

1. Functional Programming (SML)
Programming Language with only recursion, definitely nightmares for those who loves looping. As a strong typed PL with no memory for variables, SML eliminates all side effects that may occurs in procedural programming.

2. Grammar, Regular Expression (Flex and Bison)
The more philosophical way exploring the system of defining grammars, lexically analyse whether an input string belongs to the grammar (Parse tree, Regular Expression, CFG).
The second part consists of a practical use of defining your own grammar by:
Flex (Fast lexical Analyser): Analyse whether the input string matches the grammar.
Bison: Provide semantics for calculation.
(You have to write some C/C++)
With the above two tools, you can define your own grammar, and do whatever tricks you want to strings input (the PA of this chapter ask us to implement a program to play with the number of atoms in an organic chemical compound, which is super fun).

3. Logical Programming (Prolog)
At this point you have to completely switch your mindset, I would say functional programming is somehow similar to procedural programming (you call functions, execute line by line). However, Prolog presents a different way of thinking, instead of {input -> output}, Prolog put emphasis on relationship, which makes it powerful (I am still stunned by its powerful way of querying), but also extremely difficult to write.

4. Procedure Activations
Comp2011/2012 may cover some of it, mainly about how to call functions (by value, by reference..., which is related to lvalue and rvalue) and how activation records are created. Introduce C procedure in a more deep way.

5. Parallel and Concurrent Programming
Using Java as an example of parallel programming. As this course is teaching the "principles" only, so you don't have to know how to write java code. Also Comp3021(In Fall Semester) will have a more in-depth analysis on this topic.

6. GPU Parallel Programming (CUDA)
Very interesting topic but it is only a very brief introduction to CUDA programming. You can feel the power of parallel programming and it is an art to allocate recourses for each thread as performance can varies a lot. You have to use C/C++ to implement it.

comment_content:
First of all, NO FINAL EXAMS DURING PANDEMIC. This totally turns the course into an assignment-based learning one, BUT, in programming assignment 2,3, we have some subsequent pop-up quizzes requiring us to give sketches on like parsing tree and execution flow, which is not too difficult as compared to the exams.

I think overall before starting on this course, you should have some background knowledge.
- data structure, c/c++ programming, hardcore recursion [COMP2012]
- relations, perhaps some algorithms (COMP2711/3711)
- scheduling, synchronization, threads (COMP3511 and/or COMP3021)
[..] refers to prerequisites, and (...) refers to prerequisites or corequisites. Interested students are strongly encouraged to study similar topics at the same time at different courses to gain more perspective.

Anyway in the following I would briefly talk about mainly the assignment topics, but not those which would only come up in the exam questions.

For conciseness I shall use the following header notation for each assignment topic.
  {{ number }}. {{ topic }} - {{ Language }}

1. Functional Programing - Sml
If you have previous experience in es6 nodejs or python functional programming, it would be easy for you. The only trouble is you would have to get into a old-school weird SML language in the assignment. This year the sml pa is associated with a in-class demo.

2. Program (Source code) parsing - Flex, Bison
This topic is about parsing a "statement" in a typical program. From simple examples like "1 + 2" to "1 + 2 * (3 - 4) / 5", you would be taught how to translate such statements, by setting up a set of proper interpretation rules (grammar) with appropriate syntax units and operators (tokens), together with the consideration on like associativity and precedence issue. Btw just in case you wanna get involved with more theoretical material about grammar, you could take another course COMP3721 which is also a fun one :) Assignment on this topic in this semester is also easy. This year the flex-bison pa is associated with a in-class quiz.

3. Logical Programming - Prolog
It is like SQL, but not exactly the same. In this topic you completely switch your mind from variables and functions into relations. So instead of "f(input) { return result; }", you would be thinking how to write "f(input, result) :- ...". Btw this is not supposed to be a difficult topic, I mean most people take this course as elective in year 3 or year 4, with probably their first course on discrete math on previous semester already. BUT, assignment in this year is extremely tough. And after two-week efforts we get another "simple" in-class quiz :)

4. Concurrent Programming - Cuda
As I am told, this topic in usual circumstance is taken as extra topic and hence would not appear in programming assignment typically. But here we are :) In this part you would trained into thinking in a way that, tasks on large array can be separated, so that each of your computer processors can share some workload, instead of one single program running one single large array data in a slow sequential manner. This is particularly similar to the idea of "map" in functional programming. I mean once you realize this, the labs / assignment somehow becomes easier for one to tackle. The programming assignment comes with a graded presentation.


Learning material:
SML: Chapter 1-9, 11 (about 100 pages) at http://www.cs.cmu.edu/~rwh/isml/
Flex-bison: First three chapters at https://web.iitd.ac.in/~sumeet/flex__bison.pdf
Prolog: Functional helper doc at https://www.swi-prolog.org/pldoc/man?section=libpl
Cuda: Official site at https://developer.nvidia.com/CUDA-zone

comment_content:
This course gives an introduction to different programming paradigms (e.g. FP, LP) and some core concepts in programming language design (e.g. grammar and expression, scoping, parameter passing mechanism). For a programming nerd like me (see my COMP 2012H review lol), I truly enjoy the topics.

Part of the course is about alternative programming paradigms. You have to wrap you head around. The for loops are gone. They becomes functions and logical relations. How to write a program using functions only? You will be amazed by how weird but fun it is.

The other part is programming language design. Although you would not learn how to write a compiler in this course, you will learn some of the components of it. How would a compiler parse your program source file? How can the compiler determine which variable is which? Aside from the mechanisms, you can get a taste building your own little programming language without having to struggle to build a parser from scratch. Flex & Bison are pre-written parser/scanners that you will learn for implementing a language.

I always think programming is art. It feels so cool to code up your program in a special and neat way. This course can give your more inspirations on such possibilities. Cited from another review, "anyone loves programming should take this course".

But of course, don't take this course if you hate programming. lol
comment_teaching:
Luo's teaching is very clear and detailed. She goes over the materials in a good pace which an average student would be able to follow. She also supplements her teaching with lots of programming examples. Some of the cases are quite inspiring and would make you think more about the language/mechanism.

Lecture videos are also provided. They are extremely useful, especially if you cannot pay enough attention in morning classes or get lost during lecture time. However, I suggest attending the lectures because the screen is usually rendered poorly in the videos.

The TA reads out the lab materials word by word. Don't expect too much for the lab. Attending the lecture and trying out the materials by yourself should be sufficient.
comment_workload:
There are a total of three programming assignments in the course, and nothing more. Each assignment is only a few hours work if you follow the course closely. And, those are programming assignments but not Q&A boring copy & paste.

For preparing midterm and final, doing a few years of past papers should secure a reasonably good score. The length of midterm paper is about 1 hour. It won't cost you too much time for preparing the exams. Three years of past papers are provided in the course website (you don't even need to spend time petergao/coursehero!).

As mentioned, lecture videos are provided so you can learn according to your pace.

comment_content:
This course is well structured and will teach 5 paradigms of programming languages, most of them you have never heard before, really can test whether you are a good programmer and capable to learn a totally different style of programming language, as everyone is at the same starting line.

1. functional programming (SML)
    programming language cannot use loops, all recursion.
2. gramma and expression, language parser
    (BNF notation + regex + C =  Flex and Bison)
3. logical programming (prolog)
    programming using predicates, again no loops.
4. procedure activation (C)
5. parallele and GPU programming (CUDA)

1,2,3 are big topics and the topic of the assignments.

midterm covers SML, BNF, and regex, final covers all.
Both exams are closed-book, closed-notes
the style is very similar to past paper, so doing more past paper can secure your grade

Goes over non-trending programming languages: SML for functional programming, FLEX and BISON to learn about lexers and parsers, and PROLOG for logical programming.
Its a brief overview of each language without too much in-depth details and concepts.
Though it was interesting learning new languages, it could have been better with more detail.

comment_teaching: If you've taken one of those courses taught by Kenneth, you probably know he likes blended learning aka watching videos by yourselves. He always reassures his students that "most of the students who took his courses walked away with a happy face" which I kinda agree but I would not take this course twice even with a fair grade. comment_workload:
The workload of the group project is very heavy. Our project is to build a Library system using Java. There are 3 types of users: Librarian, Student/Staff, and Author. Each of us responsible for one user. System Requirements Specification consists of a class diagram, a use case diagram and a requirement specification. This is still quite easy, all you need to do is to make sure that your diagrams align with those you see in the exercises.

The terrible nightmare starts from the implementation part. all of your teammates have similar but slightly different tasks from yours. That means there would be 3 different versions of implementation of the same stuff with same functionality. The git tree would even messier than tokyo train map. So, you have constantly communicate with your teammates to make sure that you are on the same page. Also, the requirements are unclear and ambiguous so you need to search in those 1000 unorganized telegram messages with information scattering around. even the TA who are answering those questions got confused for a few times, not to mention the students. Testing is another hell as you find your branch coverages is much lower than the standard (75%). Highly recommend to minimize the branches in your code otherwise you would stuck in infinite loop. After this project, i only get better in prompt engineering but not in coding nor database. (GenAI is allowed)
comment_grading:
The grading is surprisingly good as long as you finish all those quizzes (two chances would be given) and in-class exercises (full marks upon submission no matter you are right or not). The labs are no brainer, just follow the steps. The Quiz (Midterm) and Final exam are also very easy, pure memory test. The most uncertain part is the group project but luckily it only worth 20%. My suggestion is do try all possible scenarios on your system and think of reasonable justifications for your design choices. The TA is gonna ask a bunch of questions in the demo so do prepare for that.

Labs: 10%

Project (3 people in a group)
- Activity 1: System Requirements Specification 5%
- Activity 2: System Implementation & Testing 15%
Final Exam: 40%

The group project take some of the workload tho, but if you really follow software development process, it take you 4-5 hours per week to finish it

comment_teaching:
Kenneth's teaching is OK, he effectively conveys the principles of software engineering through both videos and lectures. I would rate him a B.

He values communication with students, so he established a Telegram group. Unfortunately, it has not worked as well in this course due to issues with the main TA.

I must express my concerns regarding the main TA's attitude. When students asked legitimate questions about project implementation, he often responded dismissively with phrases like "Use your logic," "Think for yourself," or "Did you attend the lecture?" Most students were not asking trivial questions; they were seeking clarification on important aspects of the project. As a result, many turned to the UGTA, Erastus, who was much more helpful (deserved an A+ in teaching).

In Activity 1, our group lost points for not including the use-case diagram in their specifications. The main TA refused to reconsider the assignment requirements, insisting that it had been covered in the lecture by Kenneth. Fortunately, we were eventually awarded the points we deserved as the diagram is optional.

The main TA's attitude was even more concerning during demo testing, where he was unresponsive to our explanations and often complained rudely. Even when we tried to explain our work patiently, he would ridicule our efforts. Despite our attempts to show respect, it was not reciprocated.

The only satisfactory part for the main TA is his teaching during labs, he was not reading the slides and explaining quite clearly.

While the project offers meaningful practical experience in software engineering, the lack of support hindered our understanding of project requirements.
comment_workload: Most of the workload comes from the team project. I advise starting early and allocating enough time for creating test cases, which are more time-consuming than implementation itself. Overall, the workload is manageable for a 4-credit course.

The exams are not technically challenging, as they focus more on understanding concepts. The mean score for the midterm quiz was quite high. 1 Score 1 Subgrade.

comment_content:
The UML diagram makes us think about the world in a certain way.
^ This is an actual answer from an actual quiz.

COMP3111H is the exact same as COMP3111, aside from additional requirements in the project.
I would like to warn that if you can, don't take COMP3111 here. Transfer it if you can, or simply don't take it if you're not a COMP student. While software engineering is important, this course is not what you seek.

A lot of the details of how screwed up the course is, H or non-H, can be found in 151044's review:
https://ust.space/review/COMP3111H/view/aAK2cfIms2IT7bp1ChJTnuiZgcj2q5p4


1 - Lecture
This course is supposed to teach you:
UML Modelling
Software Development Methodologies
Modelling: Domain and Use-Case Modelling
Functional and Nonfunctional Requirements Capture
Implementation (Refactoring and Debugging)
Testing (Unit, Integration, and Systems)
Design Patterns

What this course actually taught us:
Some resemblance of the above topics, and
how incompetent a teaching team can be.

Being Kenneth's course, he incorporates this blended-learning mode, where he reuses videos from previous years to give lectures, has pre-lecture quizzes, and does "in-class exercises" during the lecture periods.
I have no comment about arrangement. Whether this works is subject to personal preference.

However, I have great doubts about the arrangement of these topics.
This is a software engineering course, and there is a programming project. However, useful things for this project, such as design patterns, are taught near/after the deadline of the project. This just seems counter-intuitive.

Overall, the lecture topics are just confusing when delivered by Kenneth.


2 - Labs
The labs are... kinda useless
There were a total of 6 labs that needed to be submitted
Lab 1- GitHub Basics (very very basic)
It was useless. The TAs do not follow normal Java programming practices. You have to copy code provided. Copy, as in manually typing after they included an image of the code. Frustrating, to say the least.

Lab 2 - Java Programming
They give you a portion of the slides from Desmond's first lecture of COMP3021, and then tells you to copy badly-written Java skeleton code, then implement something trivial, and doesn't actually teach Java or familiarize you with it at all.

Lab 5 - GitHub Group Repository
Project groups are formed at this point. Groups are supposed to create a dummy repository and implement some questionable code. Then we are required to submit a screenshot of a table summarising the division of labour, and commits? I fail to see the point of this as our repository has the TAs as collaborators.

Lab 6 - IntelliJ debugger
This is the best (or rather, least horrifying and stupid,) lab the course had to offer. They included a bug in the given file, and you're supposed to fix it. They wrote code in an extremely horrifying manner, which was questionable, but other than that this actually could teach people something.

Lab 7 - Unit Testing
This was pure garbage. We're supposed to write tests with JUnit 4 (which is old) , but the given files has JUnit 3, 4 and 5 simultaneously loaded.
I have no idea how that even happened and it is, of course, completely unacceptable.

Lab 8 - Reflection
This is a LANG assignment. We're supposed to write a short reflection essay on what we learnt from the previously labs.
Also completely unacceptable. There is 0 use.

3 - Project
It was interesting. (derogatory)
Imagine a box containing everything you learnt in the lectures or (if any) labs. Now throw them all out the window.
Time to use Java (COMP3021) to write a project of a game that utilizes ALGORITHMS (COMP3711). This is not acceptable for a course with COMP2012(H) as its prerequisite.
The grading scheme of the project will be mentioned in the grading section of this review.

Labs (10%)
Project (20%)
Midterm "Quiz" (20%)
Final (40%)

I find it appalling that the % of the finals is double of the project in a Software Engineering course.

The main issue with the grading of the entire course can be summarized with 2 points
1 - Subjective
2 - Ambiguous

Another thing is, programming quality is graded by how the project performs, and not the actual programming in the project.

The unit testing part of the project also made no sense. We're first given a portion of the score after having a line coverage of 20%, but then we're ranked by our code coverage? And if 2 groups have the same coverage, they're ranked by a test-to-function ratio that limits both the numbers of tests AND the number of FUNCTIONS in your code. This makes no sense, as you're telling us not to generalize the same codes and repeat them inside other functions.
Not to mention that the whole unit testing score was supposed to be ranked, and we all know ranking in HKUST is a very good idea. (It is not)

Overall, I disagree with the marking scheme, and regret suffering for this as it seems like the scheme was designed to trick and keep students in pain.

I would like to add that Kenneth has used "give us a real view of the industry" as, perhaps, an excuse to whatever this course was, and I have no comment.

I would at least accept it if they knew what they were doing, but it was evident that they did not. Even a UGTA complained about the course management.

comment_grading:
I hate the project and I hate the marking scheme. The marking scheme is designed in a way that encourages us to do EXACTLY THE OPPOSITE of what is deemed "good practices of software engineering" in class, e.g. refactoring code, using design patterns, writing comprehensive unit tests. Instead, the project marking scheme requires us to minimize our test cases, make every member variable public instead of using get/setters, and discourages code maintainability for the sake of test coverage.  In one of the functions, we are graded based on how few test cases other groups implement (and the penalty is quite heavy too - if you rank the 70% percentile, you final marks will get discounted by 70%). This not only promotes unhealthy competition (I tried asking other groups how to optimize test cases and got a cold reply), but also makes the outcome of our grades very unpredictable. The TA also frequently changes project requirements and only announces important updates on telegram, instead of via email or via canvas.

Students are not provided with clear guidelines with how to draw diagrams such as the UML (the instructor essentially brushes it off with saying they will try to be lenient in the grading) but marking turned out rather strict and somewhat arbitrary, and missing a single association in the midterm costs 4% of the total paper.

This is on top of the fact that COMP3111 is a COMPULSORY course, and I think all of these combined makes COMP3111H a terrible experience overall.

comment_grading:
I give an A here to address how large the A+ range is. After revealing the score for the final, I think my letter grade could be between A- to A, but eventually turn out to be A+. If you want to know how the A,B range is distributed, you might need to refer to other posts.

My suggestion on getting a good grade is:
1. For midterm study quiz questions and beware of important parts in the slide, try to memorize them if possible. Midterm is easy and shouldn't get a low score
2. For project, try to get almost full mark. Many teams get full mark so I believe if you scheduled it well it shouldn't be hard to get almost full. Apart from following the provided instructions, it is important to build the project in a way that makes sense to you in each part. Eventually there would be a project demo where you need to explain why you design to do something.
3. Final: It's the most important part, there are really long descriptions for the problems for drawing diagrams. To study it's better to get used to capturing necessary information for each diagram in a long description. I think it's indeed a little bit based on luck but that shouldn't influence too much if you study well.

comment_teaching:
Indeed, Kenneth is generous and tried his best to make us to have a good grade and reasonable amount of A-range as possible. He also tried his best to make the lecture videos with quality in the summer so that we can have a more relax-paced blended learning mode course.

Then, onto slides. It is colourful. Just WAY too colourful. Sometimes some unimportant words are highlighted, which can confuse us when making notes. Also, as it is pdf version, the words that are supposed to appear after animation will not be there in the pdf.

The TAs. Well, their tips on project are so useful (sarcasm intended). "The truth lies in the dataset, make sure you provide the most COMPLETE and ACCURATE data", that's basically what exactly what our TA reiterates every time we asked questions about the project. comment_workload:
The workload I would say it is slightly tight.

For quizzes, it is given 2 attempts and the correct answer will be shown. So in fact, student can basically randomly answer the question in the first attempt, and memorize the answer, and answer the correct answer on 2nd try. In fact, we don't even need to watch the lecture video to do the questions.

For class exercises, Holy cow. The time provided is very tight. Especially for students who do their work on paper (i.e. me). Let's say you were provided 20 minutes to do the exercise, you need to leave about 5 minutes to scan and upload your work. So you actually have 15 minutes to do.
Luckily, you don't have to get the exactly correct answer to get the score. They give you score if you are doing something similar. If so, one can simply randomly draw some diagrams that's looks like what the question wants and get the score.

For labs, they teach you something that are rather simple, like how to make GitHub repo, how to do commit, how to use Eclipse (Though I used IntelliJ in the end lmao), how to code some basic Java, how to make unit test blah blah… Those things are actually easy if you have some basic knowledge on those, like done a project (e.g. COMP2012H), or went for internship before.

For project, seriously, I say it is quite cursed. As I said in grading section, my group lost 1.5 marks as a punishment of generalizing the use-case diagram, while the lecture telling us to generalize cases that are too specific. Their borderline on this really shifts back and forth.
For the second activity, I must say the scale is small. The workload is about like 3 PAs from Java Programming course combined. Therefore, we can basically done the main core in 2-3 days. We even upload Gantt chart and Burndown chart that is made in like 2 hours by checking against our GitHub commit log, and still get full marks on documentation.
Sarcastically, that "truth" within the dataset, according to our TA said, actually means everything. We did not filter out any inaccurate data (e.g. sudden drop in number in "accumulative" data) and still get full mark in source code part. I searched up on the origin of the provided dataset, there already exist some errors like the aforementioned one. Indeed, complete and accurate data is what the client, i.e. the TAs want, but I must say this tip really put me in worry for sometimes.

Exam, even though it is open notes, I am totally speechless. Kenneth even give us tips on exam, trivialized some questions on the exam. But still, ambiguity exist in the exam.
Firstly, the UML diagram ones, their model answer added multiplicity next to aggregation, while the lecture told us that aggregation already implicitly gave multiplicity so it is not necessary to write it down.
The State Machine Diagram question even give a quite misleading instruction, causing most of the students got it wrong (According to TA in-charge) and lose 1.8 marks (Indeed it seems small, but remember it is about 0.3 SD in this exam. It contributes to 0.72% of total grade score and, in this case, may cost you a sub-grade!).
These places surely makes the exam itself more than just cursed.

comment_content:
If I have to conclude this course: For those who have Software Engineering experience before may/may not learn something from this course; For those who have no idea what software engineering is, you will still know nothing after this course.

Software Engineering is a very interesting topic, unlike Civil Engineering, they may visualise the structure, calculate the forces, stress/strain, and usually they know can the building withstand different shocks in design stage. However, Software Engineering is a completely different topic, there is no single "best structure" to tackle certain problems and usually we cannot foreseen how does the final system behave, or ensure it is bug-free. It is an artwork.

However, this course probably was designed in the worst way of teaching Software Engineering, cover everything but not deep enough, not even at a point to understand why I have to do these things.
------
The Lecture Contents are mainly:
1. Modelling Software System
This is an important topic, may be it sounds a little bit stupid to draw those class diagram, but it is a way that developers communicate, when you work in the industry with larger system/complicated structure, drawing class diagram is important for communication.

2. Software Development
Introduction to some software development pattern but too brief, basically if you have not participated in that pattern before, you will not know why it is good/bad.

3. System Requirement Capture
4. Implementation

5. Testing
Testing is taught in a dumb way that basically it covers those basic ICT stuff and teach you to draw more diagram. They also teach Integration Testing Using Stub which is very important if you are in Test-Driven-Development, but they omit the important part that is the implementation of Stub (Loosely-coupled principle).

6. System Analysis and Design
Design Pattern should be the most valuable thing you can learn from this course, some principle like "loosely-coupled", "composition rather inheritance"... are taught, but only a very brief introduction. BTW his slides is using all the images and derivation of a book: "Head First Software Development" which is a starter-friendly one. Check out this book to learn something valuable.

7. Some QA and Management Stuff
------
Kenneth's slides are colourful, hard-to-read and vague. You may always find some statement like: "Ignore the inappropriate requirements". Thanks for that information.

I have no big problem with Kenneth's slides since it is quite hard to cover all important material within 1 semester, it's fine. However, the arrangement of project is really a mess, I will talk about that in Teaching part.

If you do not have any Software Engineering experiences, when you read the slides and attend the lecture, you may think: why am I wasting time for this????
Indeed, studying this course without any prior knowledge just like reading a random book written by a random guy, and follow his rules to complete some tasks without any rationale/understanding. But believe me (not Kenneth kind of "believe me"), Software Engineering is a very important topic and it is way more fun than what you experienced in this course.

For those who want to take this course (CPEG/ IT minor) and really want to learn solid Software Engineering, don't register this course, go find an internship instead, it is a waste of time and effort.
comment_teaching:
Let's start with Kenneth. Kenneth is nice and he always try to please students, that makes this course a bit exam-oriented since he always say "this will be in the final exam", but still I think he really want students behave well and get good grades (he even provide hints during the final exam).

For the TAs, let's start with the labs.
We have 8 labs this year and each lab is teaching something that will be used in the team project. Something like Git and GitHub, JUnit, JavaDoc...etc, but don't expect to learn Git inside this course, they merely teach you the command to stage/commit/branch/push/clone, without much rationale behind it.
I am pretty sure some TAs are not well-prepared because there is some bug during lab7, the TA claim that it works on last year, and finally a student help the TA solve the problem. And the problem is "deprecated module", sarcastically happening in a "Software Engineering" course.

When we have questions about the lab, usually the TAs they are not quite sure about how to deal with it. I guess it may be the problem that they are selecting Eclipse as an IDE, and I wonder who is the genius that come up with the idea using Eclipse for this course. Google: "why Eclipse IDE is bad", you will know how painful it is doing all the stuff using Eclipse.

The next one is Project, I am speechless with this project.
The first problem is that the project scale is too small, I am not talking about the number of team members in each team but the tasks we are doing can basically done within 2-3 days, which does not really have to be a Team Project.

The next problem is interesting, this course is Software Engineering. So I expect we are doing something like modular development (every teammate do one feature and merge together). However, it is not what the TA expect. When someone asking whether they can use separate controller classes for different tasks, the TA said he suppose us to do all our development inside one class. WTF???? Don't they know there is something call MERGE CONFLICTS???? No single company will code like this.

Finally, in the grading of the project, this course is Software Engineering. So I expect they are checking whether we are doing some Software Engineering Best Practices (comments, organised, easy-to-read code, refactor code...). But NO, they don't look at the source code, as long as your application is working and you submit all the requirement documents, you are good to get full points. WTF??

Every Team has a TA as Team coach, but actually the team coach is not necessary except you can ask about his/her grading criteria, that's it.

So what is the point of doing this project???
comment_workload:
This course has basically no workload, or if you compare it with other courses, it worth at most 3 credits.

Out of those 3 credits, 2 credits come from Eclipse.
It take you some time to boot up the whole IDE, and when you add some files into the workspace folders, you are very likely not to see them in the IDE. Then you have to shut down Eclipse and open it again. No Auto-Complete means that you have to type every fxxking character. When you edit some code you try to see its effect, it takes Eclipse some time to figure out the build gradle file, and run the gradle project. And there is a very high chance to fail since you did not set up the build path correctly. What is buildpath?? Sorry, it is not taught in labs. Find the solution yourself.

When you face some problem in Eclipse and try to StackOverflow, usually there is no question asked for your problem cause any sensible programmer would not use Eclipse IDE anymore. If you can find something, there is a high chance that the answer is: it is the intrinsic design problem of Eclipse, remove all folders from workspace and restart Eclipse.

There can be so many cool things that this course can do, e.g. Remove all the lectures and focus on one big project. The team coach guide you to finish each tasks and teach you some Software Development Practices, also your team can choose a development pattern you like. Having meeting with Teach Coach every week to report progress and ask for suggestions. Finally, assessment based on the practices used, the way the team tackle problems...

Of course there are still a lot of ways that this course can be conducted, just not the current way.
comment_grading:
---Update after grade release---
To be honest, this should be the most "good grade" course in CSE department, of course here my definition is the overall grading instead of whether the grade reflects ability/hard work, scoring mean in this course yields B/B+, me personally get mean in exam, project slightly above mean but I miss 1% in quizzes. Total score of 88.xx% that leads to a B+ grade which is unexpected.

However, it is still worth mentioning the exam format.
This year Kenneth tries to avoid ambiguity, so instead of drawing the whole class diagram, he gave a skeleton diagram and we fill in the multiplicity into it. There are several reading comprehension, weird questions and ambiguous questions.

When I am doing the exam paper, I feel so disappointed, feeling like an idiot fooled by others to take an exam of searching stuff from notes (the exam is open-notes), reading passages to fill in the blanks, and draw a state-diagram to some "SOFTWARE SYSTEM" which is just a vending machine. I want to give up the exam several time as it really does not examining anything. If you ask experienced FAANG Software Engineer to take this exam, believe me, they will also get the mean mark.

They release scores for individual problems, and this is the first time that I get every single questions so close to mean. Not only me but also my friends, everyone is getting similar scores on each problem. The reason behind this is that TAs are just following Kenneth's marking scheme, and his marking scheme is crazily picky and not reasonable. In the appeal section, one TA also thinks that our consideration is correct, but Kenneth just don't think it is necessary, the TA has to follow the marking scheme and give out points.

The course is a good grade course in the sense that, every person gets the same score, the s.d. of final exam is around 6 out of 100, which is super crazy for a COMP course. Kenneth does keep his promise to maximize A/B ranges, and put as much B+/A+ as possibles. That makes 88% gets B+ but 92% gets A+.

For those A-rangers, it is a hard course to get A, but if you are just an average person and don't mind learning nothing, register this course.

comment_teaching:
Kenneth as a professor is well prepared and very accommodating. He produces videos for each lecture in advance, and lectures are usually spent working on exercises to help with better understanding the concepts mentioned in the lecture.

When setting deadlines for assignments and group project, he is willing to consider the opinions of students before deciding on a final date.

He also tries to make the lectures engaging and is a easy lecturer to approach and interact with.
comment_workload:
The intensity of the workload is not high, but there is always something you need to complete, whether it is quizzes or class exercises.

The quizzes are one time only, so it is best if you watch the lecture videos and read the lecture notes first before attempting them.

The class exercises may be difficult and usually require you to draw diagrams. But in turn, correct diagrams are not necessary, Kenneth just wants to see that you have attempted the exercises. Not only that, Kenneth usually shows and explains what a model answer might be, so if you are lazy, you can wait and just copy his diagram.

The project requires a bit of coding, should be easy if you have any knowledge of programming. If you don't they'll teach you all you need to know in labs so don't worry.

The final exam was open-booked, but most of the questions required you to determine whether the given diagram was drawn correctly, and asked you to drawn what you think is the correct diagram if you think the given diagram is incorrect. Since interpretation can play quite a big factor when deciding how a diagram should be drawn, be prepared to appeal if you think your diagram was wrongly graded.

All in all, this course is one of the easier courses in UST in my opinion, and if you have the opportunity to have Kenneth as your lecture, you should pick him. Good luck!

comment_content:
1. A lot of theory
To be specific, a lot of diagrams drawing and a lot of software management(?) stuff. Although some more practical things such as scrum, testing and design patterns are also covered, the project is just too small a scale for these things to be utilized.

2. Bad lecture notes
The lecture notes are the most deceptive one I have ever seen. They are deceptive in the sense that, at the first glance, you may think they are structured and detailed and summarized what Kenneth has said, but when you really read them, you would figure out these lecture notes are in fact so badly designed....

comment_teaching:
I would classify the 20 something lectures into two types: diagram drawing lecture and powerpoint reading lecture.

1. Regarding the diagram drawing lecture
Kenneth is quite interactive in this regard to answer students' questions, but this is just because his standard on diagram drawing is just so ambiguous and students have many questions on his standard or perspective. Honestly, even after hearing all his clarifications, I don't still quite get what he actually wants.

2. Regarding the powerpoint reading lecture
Well, I must say, sometimes he did clarify some points he is previously reading. But most of the time, he is just reading aloud, "with a smile", just like what other reviews have mentioned.

3. Exam-oriented?
"Class diagram is important, because I guarantee that there will be one question in the final exam asking you to draw class diagram"
"You should pay attention to use-case diagram, because I guarantee that there will be one question in the final exam asking you to draw use-case diagram"
"You better understand what is state-machine diagram, because I guarantee that there will be one question in the final exam asking you to draw state-machine diagram"
"You should practice drawing flow graph, because I guarantee that there will be one question in the final exam asking you to draw a flow graph"
Does he imply that these things are useless when he didn't test these in exam?

4. Do you want good grade?
"Do you want tips on the exam? Do you want me to give out better grade?" asked Kenneth during exam review, with a BIG smile. Unfortunately, I personally dislike his way of asking these questions.

Even though I am being quite critical here, his teaching is actually better than average (I would leave you to judge whether his teaching is exemplary). I also see Kenneth's and Nam Kiu's (the TA) effort in trying to help us as much as possible, where they open a Telegram group for students to ask about the project or anything, and they attempt to prevent freerider from ruining other members in the project.
comment_workload:
When Kenneth's COMP2711 is a 4-credit course with 2-credit workload, then his COMP3111 is a 4-credit course with 3-credit workload. Here is the main workload:

1. Attend his lesson to gain access to his in-class exercise, and finish the exercise

2. Labs: as I have mentioned, each lab can be finished within half an hour, assuming you didn't encounter any technical problems

3. Project
The project just gives a taste of how it feels when you code with others. It is not complex enough to render those design pattern or testing things useful, and it is quite fine even when you write sphagetti code or create a god class. The main workload actually comes from self-learning JavaFX, JUnit and Javadoc, if you didn't take COMP1022P or COMP3021 before.

4. Exam
It just didn't deserve your time of revision.

Indeed, the course does have a bit of workload when you need to manage your project and meet the deadline, but in terms of a software engineering course, this workload is just nothing, because the project scale is small. The "A" for this column is given under the context of a software engineering course.
comment_grading:
First 50% = In-class ex. + Lab + Project
Easy marks. Some review said the project is just a COMP2012H assignment. I don't agree. I would say that the project is even easier than a COMP2012 assignment, just that you need to self-learn JavaFX, JUnit and Javadoc, but for the purpose of the project, they are not difficult at all. (Well, the caveat is you have experience in C++ in COMP2011+2012/2012H.)

Another 50% = Final Exam
Well, this is the interesting part. First thing first off, this is a game of pure luck. There is basically no use to review the lecture notes or the in-class exercise (except the MC questions where you have the exam review to refer to), because they didn't give you sufficient evidence to judge Kenneth's standard on diagram drawing, or inferring functionality and requirement. This also means that it makes no sense to talk about the difficulty of the exam, since you are literally doing guessing in the 2 or something hours.

This leads to the second thing: everyone's mark is just so concentrated. It's like 5X to 7X marks out of 95. Everyone is just guessing. You get a slightly higher mark because you are a slightly better guesser, not because you know slightly better the course material. I also remembered one question in the exam is autograded. What is the consequence? You just see a whole column of 0s for that question in the mark sheet given out by Kenneth. Really fucking interesting.

And that leads to the third thing: there is no point talking about the mean or SD. Your grade is determined by your rank in the exam, and the rank is determined by your luck. Because of this, I would give a "D" in this grading column.

But I decide to give out a "C" for this column instead, because Kenneth's grading distribution is really lenient.
First 50%: got 49.1% (I lost that 0.9% just because my use-case specification does not include an alternative flow)
Second 50% (final exam): 65/95 (so that I rank 20 out of 100 something students)
Grade: A

However, I won't speak in favor of this course's grading scheme just because I got the second best grade possible, since I don't really know how the marks are earned or lost in the exam, and I don't think it is reasonable when your luck is a decisive factor of your grade. (Remember his COMP2711, when your carefulness decides your grade?)

comment_teaching:

comment_workload:

Learning and debugging the first two IMO can be quite time consuming.

See GRADING for further break down.
comment_grading:
I will preface by saying that I skipped classes A LOT, and didn't revised for the final (out of self-destruction, and disgust for this course). I deserve the B+. But still, here comes my wall of rage.

Full marks on everything except the final where I got 71/100 => B+. FUCK! I have a friend who also received full marks for everything else and 80.5/100 for final, and he got A+. So, it's like, what, total marks 85 = B+, 90 = A+ !!? This is not a reasonable reflection on students' performance.

Moral of the story: Be careful with Kenneth Leung's courses. He makes everything so freaking simple, everybody got full marks, and in the end losing a few marks due to silly mistakes for the final with cost you a letter grade.

PARTICIPATION (full mark):
Just hand in 12 in-class exercises and you're done.

LAB (full mark):
Basic Java programming. Basic Git usage. Very straightforward, each can be completed within half an hour.

PROJECT (full mark):
Basically make a desktop app. If you are comfortable with Java and GUI programming this is simple. The 'documentation' part is a no-brainer. They don't even care about the quality. As long as it's there and it kind of make sense then you will get full mark.

FINAL (71/100; weighted 50%; around mean)
MC worth about 30% of the paper. Kenneth will give you the questions and answer as revision exercise before the final.

The rest are utter mess! Ambiguous load of crap. They are long version of various in-class exercises. Usually involves drawing flow-chart like diagrams, and categorizing the components of problem statements. There are no unique solution! Different people can interpret the statements differently. To give you a feeling: during the final, people are raising their hands requesting for clarification non-stop for 3 hours. The girl in front of me raises hers > 10 times. And only Kenneth is answering because the TAs are just as confused. During the paper checking session, people were swarming the TAs and the assistant professor trying to explain why they think their answer is right.

This course is NEVER about realistic software engineering practices, it's all about EXAM TECHNIQUES. So be sure to attend the classes to get a feel of what Kenneth wants.

About the planning, documentation, and much more etc. You will find you know more behind that a project is not only coding but much managements behind. comment_content:
A lot of boring theory stuff about software engineering. Not as practical as others claimed. Useful development approaches in real life like agile and scrum only get mentioned briefly. Topics include:

Modeling Software Systems using UML
- Software Development
- System Requirements Capture
- Implementation
- Testing
- System Analysis & Design
- Software Quality Assurance
- Managing Software Development

For previous semesters, you can get a taste of software engineering by staring everything from scratch to build a working project. You have to think about the structure, and you have to think about how to build a good UI, and much more. After Kenneth have revamped the course, the project becomes like like an COMP 2012(H) assignment, you just copy and modify some things in skeleton code. You can attain full marks without considering any design patterns or OO concepts, or even with spaghetti code. (you won't get penalized for bad code!) I personally think the project in COMP 2012H is more like an software engineering project. That project requires you a good OO structure and the program have to be well-documented.
comment_teaching:
I would say most reviews have Kenneth's teaching skills overrated. He is not bad compared to other lecturers, but he definitely is not as good as how the other reviews claimed.

All he did in the lecture is reading the powerpoint with a smile. He did not elaborate the points too much, and most of the time he just read out the content without emphasizing the main points.

I don't get how to draw a good class diagram or a state machine diagrams. Both Kenneth and the TA said it is ok as long as it is reasonable. But I don't quite get what is reasonable. I just cant extract the main points from the problem statement.

It is good that he provide some in-class exercises to let you know what can be the question type in exams. But still, there are some variations in exams.

Sorry to all Kenneth fans. He is nice and passionate but he really can't make me understand the content. He did give good grades tho in some sense.
comment_workload:
The workload is average. It is not too much nor too heavy.

Both in-class exercise and lab require attendance. You can get full in in-class exercise by completing 10 exercises in lecture. Not all of the labs are mandatory so have a look on the course webpage regularly.

Although most parts of the group project are graded individually, the workload still depends on your groupmates. Having a good groupmate, especially one that keep track of the progress and requirements well, do help quite a bit. Conversely, if you got a freerider, you still have to do some of the group stuff by yourself like the unit test and coverage.

For exam preparation, recite the MCQs Kenneth gave (he claimed all MCQs are from the review, but somehow some of them aren't) and the three design principal of design pattern (that three points in the in-class exercise). There is nothing else that you can do to prepare. There is no use doing exercises without Kenneth/Kevin's marking scheme.
comment_grading:
Most students got full marks in project, and full mark can be obtained both in-class exercise and lab by sufficient attendance. The overall grade mostly depends on the final exam.

The final exam is mostly based on luck. Most students including my friends and myself do not have complete understanding of the concepts like drawing class diagrams and state machine diagram. If you draw the thing that he expected, then you will get the mark. But you can never guess the marking scheme because there are insufficient examples to judge that. The only thing you can do is memorize all the MCQ Kenneth gave and the three principals of the design pattern (you must use the EXACT wording from the in-class exercise to get the mark!).

In-class exercise: 10/10
Labs: 10/10
Project: 30/30
Final exam: 90/100 (Mean: ~70, SD: ~10.8, Max: 91.5) => 45/50
Overall: 95/100 => A+

My grade is probably not that useful for judging the grading scheme. I will ask for my friends for more statistics about the grading and post an update here.

EDIT: My friend who got 80.5 in final (+1sd) and everything else full. Got A+ also. Extremely good grade.

EDIT2: Another friend: final mean + else all full => B+
