comment_content:
At the moment this course has (at least) two versions. I took the Fred one, but also learnt a lot about the Kim one.

Common:
Software design principles and patterns.
Software development workflows.

The common things are basically memorization, like most of the COMP courses in UST.

Fred: lab and project is ASP.NET WebForms website. You learn latest C#, T-SQL and write a somewhat complicated website to satisfy a long and vague requirement document. Closer to large, old, non-Internet enterprises in the real world.

Kim: lab and project uses latest frontend libraries (e.g. ng), No-SQL db and Java to build an app with both web and Android clients. You have more freedom (and obligation) to show your creativity. Closer to newborn, Internet enterprises.
comment_teaching:
Fred is rather nice, despite being a bit arrogant.

You can expect clear explanations. The in-class practices are quite useful for understanding the content and preparing for the exams.
comment_workload:
No matter who you choose, you should expect somewhat heavy workload for the course project.

To ease your life, find teammates that are NOT FREE-RIDERS (you shall be yr 2 or yr 3, so you do have some friends to work with, don't you). Even better, find people with prior software development experience to avoid the entire team running into troubles.

Exams are easy, but some questions feel like lottery. Try to memorize everything and you will be fine.
comment_grading:
Personally, I prefer Fred when it comes to grading.

Fred: grading scheme is clear and "definitive". There is good reason for each point you lose in your project (output does not match requirement). Although the requirement statements are vague, you can clarify them with dozens of emails during the semester. In other words it is possible to get full mark in project if your group programs well.

Kim: TAs' taste will have a big impact on your grade. How is your poster? How is your unique feature? What's your effort of implementing if you use another library? These subjective things can lead to rather unfair outcomes. To make things worse, different TAs have different standards because they did not get on a common ground before grading (Fall, 2015-16). And the professor did not participate.

comment_workload:
Workload is large so I want to give some opinions to handle them well.
(Suppose the content will not change next fall sem)

Project :
There are 3 Phrases.
1) Create Slides for your group application
2) Test Cases & Git commit history
3) Presentation with Video instead of Real DEMO    (this year)

Make sure you can get FULL MARKS in Phrase 1 & 2. Because they are easy for you to get these marks. even 1 mark is important in this course.

Exam :
1) No multiple choice question
2) Memorizing is required

Possible Question(s) :
Midterm
1) State the Git flow for large project
     - Remember Code Review is required
2) Given this situation, which software development process is suitable
    - If you know the requirement of the product well,
      Waterfall may be a good choice.
    - If you always change the requirement,
       Agile may be a good choice ..

3) Draw UML for Design Pattern (observer pattern..)
    - You can find some UML example in notes
4) List 2 Linux Commands and the use of them
    - for example:
      1) command : cd
          Use : change directory
5) What is functional/non functional requirement ....
      - give an example about these requirement
Final
1) What is software testing? Advantages?
    - you can find them in notes
2) Design Pattern Questions (with UML)!!!
    - Ask you to write an adaptor class
3) Understand the design principle for each pattern
3) What is Assertion? What is Error handling?
    - How to do Error handling?
4) Basic Path Testing
5) Symbolic Testing
6) What is refactoring? Advantage?
7) White Box Testing V.S. Black Box Testing

Conclusion :
If you want to learn something in this course, Focus on the project with your teammates. You can learn how to use git, how to do testing, how to work with your teammates and some software development tools such as Slack and Github.

You have to know the format of final presentation.
For example:
If you just present using a video, you should not spend much time doing validation and backend stuff because no one will check your code and see if your codes are good or bad. You should do your layout beautiful and create more gimmick things.
I waste more time on improving code structure and work flow but no one cares them. (We thought the format was REAL DEMO)
(Remark: At least 2 presenters in your group)

If you want to get a good grade,
- Memorizing the lecture notes
  (examples and definitions) is needed.
- Go to Review Lecture.
- Get Full lab points
- Get Full marks in Assignments
- Try to get Bonus points if you have time
  (But it is very difficult)

I hope these opinions can help reduce your workload because you can allocate time efficiently. But If Kim sees this review and totally change the papers, ...Sorry!

My overall marks is 92%

comment_content:
Warning/TLDR: The Honors designation does not matter here aside from extra project requirements. Stay away if the same people are teaching. (This also goes for CPEG students, who should stay away at all costs).

The course content attempts to cover important methodologies and methods about Software Engineering, but with varying levels of success for different topics.

A short topics list for Lectures:
UML Modelling
Software Development Methodologies
Modelling: Domain and Use-Case Modelling
Functional and Nonfunctional Requirements Capture
Implementation (Refactoring and Debugging)
Testing (Unit, Integration, and Systems)
Design Patterns

While I appreciate the importance of these topics, the way they are presented and examined is completely irrelevant to our future work. One of the (correct) MC answers was "the UML diagram allows us to think about the world in a certain way". Another one is "They help novices act like experts". Why are these things we must memorize to take the exam (as it appears on the exam verbatim), when any reasonable examination for a software engineering course should focus on an application of the principles taught? I have absolutely no idea.

Labs attempted to teach you the basics of the tools needed to do the project, but with insufficient examples, poor documentation, and completely useless learning activities (the final lab had you write a reflection of >250 words about "what you have learnt in the labs". What is this, LANG?). More on the lab quality in Teaching.

The project, apart from being mismanaged, requires an impressively wide skill set even if you are not doing the Honors version of the project. It requires fluent Java (COMP 3021), GUI Programming (in Swing or JavaFX) (COMP 3021) and Algorithms (COMP 3711). While I am fortunate enough to use Java as my main programming language, and have taken COMP 3711 in the previous term, the requirements are unreasonable for a course with COMP 2012(H) as prerequisite. It is unreasonable for the teaching team to expect us to have all these skills to finish the project, while giving us next to no guidance.

Overall, a large portion of the contents were either useless, vague and broad without much applicability, blatantly wrong, or far out of the scope of this course.
comment_teaching:
For Kenneth's lecture teaching, I do not have much to complain about. It's acceptable, covers the main points, and gives you the answers you need. He is also willing to answer your questions after class and is responsive to student queries by email - which I do appreciate. He did extend the deadline of the project due to popular demand, which is quite nice of him as well.

However, the project destroyed any goodwill I had for this course. Even though the lecture teaching is around a C, the project is an F---- (or XF, if you agree that the teaching team cheated us out of a reasonable software engineering course). The teaching team demonstrated the importance of software engineering methodologies in project planning... by making an utter mess of the project to be the best counterexample you'll ever need.

The project issues caused by the teaching team can be broken down into three categories: Mismanagement, Outdated and Poor Practices, and General Language Competency Issues.

The teaching team mismanaged the project so poorly that they had to revise the specification for it five times (and once in November, when people should at least be halfway through it). The original specification, uploaded in early September, was riddled with errors and inconsistencies. Aside from the grammatical mistakes, the most important problem was flipping the character that the user controlled.

In response to this, the teaching team released a "correction notice" which only changed the wording but not the logic of the specification. But this change does not only change the sprites of the game. Rather, it fundamentally changes the logic behind the game. Despite having updated the specification again, the lead IA, Eden Wong, refused to release it for some reason that only he can comprehend. One of our fellow classmates (on his own volition) created an additional clarifications document after repeatedly meeting with the teaching team, but this document, along with other miscellaneous changes, was never announced via email, nor Canvas. This reflects the lack of professionalism from the entire teaching team.

The last amendment to the grading scheme is done three days before the extended deadline, and it changes the unit testing grading scheme for honors students drastically – they proposed that the “honors portions” can be excluded from the computation of the “test-to-function ratio” (see Grading). If that was ever on the table for the three months that the project was released, then our design decisions would be completely different. As it stands, it would require an extensive amount of refactoring for this change to be practical in our project source. It is, again, blatantly unprofessional for the teaching team to expect us to be able to change our code structure at will, with such short notice, in order to correct the bias in their grading scheme.

What made the project's issues worse was that Kenneth was not actively involved in most steps of it - when we booked an appointment with him, he was surprised by some of the changes that the TA team had made, and agreed with us that some of the restrictions were unreasonable. The teaching team also had poor intra-team communication - different parts of the project were worked on by different people, but there was little communication between them. This means that the people nominally in charge of the project (IA Eden and Kenneth) were unable to answer specific questions about subcomponents of the project, despite supervising the design of the entire product. This (ironically) could have been avoided by some integration testing for the project team to coordinate everyone’s work, but was somehow overlooked by a team of people who were teaching us project management and software engineering.

Ethically, some of the decisions made by the TA team were also very questionable. They have failed to attribute the authors of the original code for the sample snake game and maze generator project. Although the snake game project was MIT licensed, the maze generator was not, and is by default All Rights Reserved. The fact that the teaching team did not fork the repos, instead choosing to upload them directly without a link to the original repos showed that they had no intention to attribute the original authors. This sets a terrible precedent for students - doing similar things in a non-educational setting can easily lead to IP infringements, not to mention the ethical issues of using someone else's code without permission.

The teaching team's lack of recent Java knowledge also led to multiple questionable decisions regarding language use and (specifically) unit testing.

The teaching team has, on multiple occasions, failed to teach Java effectively – they did not follow proper Java naming convention in the labs, nor did they offer tutorials for what is necessary to complete the project. The teaching team consistently starts their classes with a lowercase letter (class main), and some method arguments are uppercase (int power(int Base, int Exponent)). Instead of focusing on fixing common Java bugs like failing to handle null, or teaching us how to trace data flow, the lab on debugging renamed all the variables to junk values like iii, ii to show “bad coding style”, and asked us to debug code which did not follow any style guide instead. The only lab on GUI programming used JavaFX, but the “skeleton code” (detailed below) uses Swing (another GUI framework). This means that most students are woefully underprepared for the course project – they had to learn much of the skillset necessary on their own, while the teaching team taught poor practices which hindered the learning progress of students.

The given Snake Game project (supposedly used for skeleton code for the project) was horribly outdated and poorly maintained - it was last updated 8 years ago, and the Java language has evolved a lot since then. The project had multiple issues including using magic constants, abuse of static state, and poor encapsulation. Using this project as a template will teach students who do not know about Java multiple poor practices. We were also (before negotiation) required to use JUnit 4, when JUnit 5 (with many more features and lambda support) was released 6 years ago. The given lab files (for teaching unit testing) also (somehow) had 3 versions of JUnit bundled. The suggested library for testing UI code (FEST) was last updated 10 years ago, had a defunct website which points to nothing, and does not cater to JavaFX users. Every single one of these issues could have been prevented if the teaching team had practiced proper dependency management, which is, again, something that you would expect the people who are running a software engineering course to be aware of.

The unit testing requirements again highlighted their misunderstanding of the Java language - when provided with an example of an exploit of the unit testing grading scheme, the TA in charge John Li ignored it and created something which is unsafe Java as an example of why this is not a problem. He then proceeded to argue that the code is equivalent when his code crashes if the wrong kind of object is passed in. When we showed that they are indeed not, they responded by telling us the grading scheme is "to let you explore different syntax sugar in writing Java programs".

A quote from him highlights this line of thinking quite well (slightly paraphrased): "If you managed to cram everything into one line, you are a good Java programmer which should get more marks!" He actively encouraged us to write code which can fit in one line by abusing ternary operators, lambda expressions, and other language features which should not be used in this manner in order to minimize the lines of code written. This implicitly encourages us to write unreadable code in order to achieve higher marks.

They have also instructed us to avoid encapsulation and use public fields and functions only in order to make testing easier. While this is excusable for a small programming assignment in a general programming course, this is a software engineering course. It is obvious that we should not violate OO design principles when actually doing a project in an OO language. The cognitive dissonance of learning some useful principles in the lecture (defensive programming, encapsulation) but being asked to violate them repeatedly in the course project is jarring – as if what I’ve learnt is completely irrelevant in practice.

The general language competency level of the teaching team is low, especially when you consider their English proficiency. On asking specific questions about the project's changes, we were met with the following response: "Think: Makes sense? Have fun? Design is professional?" We were then instructed to modify the project instructions as we seem fit. This is counterintuitive and very vague. In the future, if we modify our client's requirements without asking them, we are obviously asking for trouble. This means that the course cannot prepare us for the industry, which is what a properly designed software engineering course should do.

Their English proficiency sometimes hinders our understanding of what they mean, something which is readily visible in the labs and the project description. The condition in which these documents were released would be much improved if the teaching team had used basic grammar tools.

Update (12/5/2024): IA Eden is "no longer an IA." (Kenneth, 2024).
comment_workload:
The workload is objectively a C, and (personally) an E.

The workload for the pre-class quizzes and the in-class exercises are not high, provided that you copied down the answers Kenneth has provided in the lecture. For the pre-class quizzes, you have two attempts to finish it. Therefore, you can randomly guess answers on your first attempt, and get full scores for your second attempt. The workload here is not large, but can get annoying, much like the rest of this course.

The midterm quiz has a higher workload simply because you must memorize a lot of answers which seem to be common sense but instead violate common sense. You can get a taste of revising for it by the answer I provided above: “the UML diagram allows us to think about the world in a certain way”. The annoyance of revising for it is the main workload, as the quiz is short and relatively easy.

The project (and the related issues it caused) were the main reasons why I rated the workload an E personally. Due to the lack of clarity of requirements, we were forced to ask the teaching team for clarifications repeatedly, but that only resulted in more vague and incomprehensible answers (see Teaching). Dealing with their changes as they come constituted a major part of the workload. The project itself requires you to self-learn a lot of frameworks if you do not have the experience, which could be rather intensive if you started late.

What ultimately made me gave the workload an E was my protracted discussion with the teaching team in order to get a reasonable grading scheme for unit testing. It took me multiple hours to convince the teaching team that their scheme is flawed, and two more hours of meetings in order to reach a compromise. In the meetings, we were asked to provide a reasonable grading scheme. Putting aside the impressive ability for the teaching team to pass their workload to us when they encounter an issue (I believe designing a proper grading scheme is their responsibility), the extra workload this has given me during midterms period is enough for me to personally give Workload an E.
comment_grading:
A D for the grading scheme, E for the midterm quiz and final, and F for transparency and honesty.

Grading Scheme:
Pre-Lecture Quizzes (5%)
In-class Practice Exercises Assignments (5%)
Labs (10%)
Project (20%)
Midterm Quiz (20%)
Final (40%)

Ask yourself this question: What kind of software engineering course only has 20% allocated to the project? For a topic as practical as software engineering, it is completely unreasonable for the mark allocation of the practical component to be 20% only.

20% of the grade is basically free (if you went to the lecture and copied the solutions to the in-class assignments), and the differentiation in the midterm quiz is extremely low (the difference between the median and the LQ score is only ~4 marks, for a midterm quiz with 75 as the full mark). This shows that the mark distribution of the course would be so tight that a few careless mistakes can instantly crash your grade.

The final exam was even worse than the midterm exam – while the midterm is boring, the final exam was a LANG exam in disguise. The problem statement for both long questions was unnecessarily long and filled with awkwardly written sentences. Much of the given text (spanning 5 pages in total) was completely irrelevant to the question. This meant that both long questions were effectively reading comprehension questions which did not assess our ability to engineer software effectively. Rather, it effectively assessed our reading speed and ability to copy pointless information from a piece of text – exactly like reading comprehension tests.

The teaching team tried to increase the differentiation of scores by adding numerous asinine grading components to the project - but none of them were anywhere near meaningful work which contributed to our understanding of software engineering, unit testing, or anything of that sort. Most of the grading items were vague and not quantifiable: what is a "trivial commit"? What is "unprofessional writing"? What is "unprofessional or non-user-friendly design"? We were given no descriptors as to what precisely defines these terms, which makes the overall project grading opaque and unclear. Some of the work is also meaningless – we were required to screenshot the coverage of all our tests, and the tests themselves. For honors students who were required to implement more features than others, this caused us multiple days of work – our 155 tests amounted to 300+ screenshots. Compiling the final report in LaTeX took us more than 2 minutes, and produced a ~130-page monstrosity. As I’ve asked the TA in charge privately, they don’t check all our screenshots – if our project is done acceptably, they will take our numbers for granted. This means that they have wasted multiple days of work from my team for no tangible benefit aside from stroking their desire for formality, as the work done does nothing for our final project grade. I find the blatant disregard of the value of our time utterly reprehensible.

Specifically, the unit test requirements are downright ridiculous. We were required to test our own private functions (or make all of our data members public (which is obviously a terrible idea)) and private constructors for utility classes. None of these make sense as a target to test, but for the purposes of the project we were forced to do so. The teaching team also encouraged us to remove error handling code for edge cases in order to achieve a better "test-to-function ratio".

The "test-to-function ratio" was invented by the teaching team as a metric to break the ties in unit test coverage. Strangely, it ranked our performance by the least amount of tests written, and limited us to only four calls to the target function per test. A comprehensive unit test suite should be able to cover all the corner cases, instead of only some of them - but this is not what the grading scheme encourages us to do. It encourages us to skimp on testing by cramming all the conditionals into one line, and only testing one of them. It also encourages students to not reuse their code, since extracting common behavior into methods results in a worse test to function ratio. The issue with this is that we are ranked with other students, which in turn encourages us to abuse unit testing by not comprehensively testing our code.

At the project appealing session, we discovered that -
1. The project guidelines are intentionally vague in order to increase the differentiation between students;
2. Achieving the requirements of the project alone does not give you the full mark; and
3. The marks given for each subjective component will not be disclosed (such as why some marks are lost) despite multiple requests.

This means that the teaching team was deliberately dishonest with us - they misled us with a confusing and vague project specification, graded our project with multiple subjective factors which were deliberately not made clear, and, most egregiously, refused to be transparent when we requested justification for our grading. With a marking scheme which is not public (I observed a 2-page internal document at the checking session, and the TAs claimed that there was a "complex process" which they refused to disclose in fear of student appeals) and filled with subjective factors, not even knowing why or how some marks are lost is equivalent to so-called "black box grading", with which the teaching team can assign scores and grades arbitrarily.

The very fact that the grading scheme is designed to reward students for writing unreadable (see Teaching), unmaintainable, and unsafe code shows you how much this course can teach you about software engineering.

Update: Slightly below UQ (87.24%) yielded an A. Still, the rubbish grading scheme means that whatever grade I'm given doesn't really matter - it's mainly a matter of luck and/or memorizing useless information anyways. Also, there's still an issue of "1 percent 1 subgrade" - my friend with ~-1% from my score got an A-, which means that the grading is probably a disaster given how close the scores are (~8% from LQ to UQ).

Stay away.

But most of the material is useful for programmers, such as teaching different design patterns (Observer pattern, Factory pattern) and teaching object-oriented graph drawing (UML model). By comparing with other COMP courses, this course does not teach much specific knowledge but you can still learn some software engineering skills.
comment_teaching: LEUNG, Wai Ting is a nice professor. Students can contact him via telegram to ask questions related to the courses. Besides, TA is also nice that if you have any questions related to the lab, you can contact him for help.
comment_workload:
Assignment:
In-class exercises, quizzes, and labs are really easy to do.
The group project takes around a whole week to do. By comparing to other COMP courses such as COMP2011 and COMP2012, the workload is like a doubled-PA. However, COMP3111H has one group project only and the deadline has been postponed for 3 to 4 days. Thus, the workload of the project is not heavy.

Class:
Instead of the regular class, each student has to watch two extra videos a week for two weekly quizzes. These videos may take you an hour a week, but still an acceptable workload.
comment_grading:
Kenneth promised that >90 = A in telegram while the class overall average is 88. The grading is really nice.

10% in-class exercises
10% quizzes
10% lab
30% project
40% exam

Almost all of the students can get a full mark in In-class exercises, quizzes, and lab. Don't be too lazy, then you can easily get a full mark here.

The project is a bit difficult but the grading is still nice, there are a few groups can have a full mark in the project.

Impossible to get a full mark in the exam, but the class average is quite high (around 75). If you can get a full mark in other components, getting an average score in an exam is not terrible.

comment_content:
Basical software engineering concepts, only qualitative analyses, no quantitative analyses. So you can say that it's like a business management course, no coding of any language is taught, surprising, isn't it ? But the result is that you can only learn a little about software engineering, even if we only talk about the management part.

Ever since online teaching, the faculty has adopted flip mode, which means the main course material is delivered through pre-recorded videos, and they require you to watch 2 videos before weekly lecture, and finish 2 quizzes, each consisting of 5 single multiple choice questions, which they say are easy, but according to most students' feedback, at least on of the questions in each quize are quite vague and hard to do right. They put some in-class exercises during the lecture (usually 2), and require you to submit them after lecture for attendance.

All in all, I dislike flip mode, because sometimes when you have questions regarding the real course materials in the pre-recorded videos, you cannot ask the professor directly. Writing e-mail every time can be annoying, and it's easy to forget about the questions when you attend the lecture to do some "practical" exercises.
comment_teaching:
3111 and 3111H track students are taught together, the only difference is that H track students have a slightly more challenging project and "better grading" (said by the professor) than non-H track students.

Since it's flip mode that they delivered the course, the teaching is only about in-class exercises.

You'll feel that the conventions of drawing a class diagram is unclear and it's often the case that whether you are right or not is totally up to the professor and TAs.

Other in-class exercises are fine though.
comment_workload:
Workload is not high, as long as you know how to use github and code Java.

The main problem is about project.

This semester the project is a Gradle based data analysis application. If it's the same when you are taking this course, then good luck with Gradle + Eclipse, because many students and I all encounter misterious problems when setting up the environment and coding, that even the TAs cannot solve. Then who do you expect me to approach, father of Java, Eclipse developer or Gradle developer ?

Another serious problem is that the teaching team acts as the client/stakehoder and all student teams act as "Party B", who's gonna be responsible for everything that goes wrong. We are just beginners learning how to be a good software engineer, OK ? Not some employees that are disposable and can be used as tools.

One little problem in the project (which is due to the vague requirement that they put on canvas), and they deduct 20 points from 100. I run out of words to express my disappointment towards this course.

TLDR, the workload is not high, but whether it'll be turned into a good result, you never know.
comment_grading:
This part is what I'm so disappointed about.

Grading scheme : (directly copy and paste from canvas)
Evaluation: Pre-Lecture Quizzes (10%), In-class Practice Exercises Assignments (10%), Labs (10%), Project (30%), Final (40%)

Professor adopted a mixture of both relative and absolute grading as a result of the whole class has done a good job.

Well, here's some fun facts :

Overall 89.93% == A
Overall 85.26% == B
Overall 81.69% == C+

It doesn't matter which one is mine, what matters is that this shows that most students' overall lies in 80%~90%, and the professor has to decide who gets A+ and who gets D-, thus you have D- to A+ in just 10%.

Grading of the final exam is totally dependent on professor and TAs, because there's even no standard or preferred  solutions provided after exam. How can you appeal ? You can only wish and you are doomed, that's it.

Professor Kennethe said "I'm generous", but actually not.

comment_grading:
3 assignments: 20%
Midterm: 25% (Everything up to local search)
Final: 55% (Everything up to logic)
Mean/Low/LQ/Median/UQ/High:
A1: 98.25/79/99/99.75/100/100
A2: 95.28/0/98/100/100/100
A3: 95.06/49/95/99/99/100
Midterm: 80.95/49/74/85/90/99
Final: 73.12/39.5/62.31/76.5/85.25/96.5

The assignments are mostly trivial and poorly designed. I have disagreements with one of the solutions in the assignment and tried to argue with the TA through email, and the TA seems to have trouble understanding English and just did not answer after a few turns of 雞同鴨講 discussions.

Also, the assignment solutions are released very late (within one week before final exam) and are only released because I urge the professor to do so. The scores for assignment 2 are released after the final exam scores, which shows the incompetence of the TAs.

Since the assignments and midterm are too easy (mean = 80 is even higher than my COMP3511 open-book midterm in previous semester), the final is made slightly more difficult, but the overall scores are still very high, so you really need to be careful when doing the exams. The exams need you to recite the algorithms in order to run them for a few iterations on paper. You also need the update rules for the reinforcement learning part, so please recite them as well. Very few COMP2211 type theoretical questions are asked in the exams. No sample papers are provided, and the sample questions are too few (2 questions each for each exam) and too simple, so please don't trust your exam will be that simple.

comment_content:
Attached is just some notes I made about CSP.
Update: They seem to follow previous syllabus in 2024-25 Spring

Syllabus:
Topic 1: Introduction to AI
Topic 2: Machine Learning
- Linear Regression, Linear Classification
- Non-linear Features
- Neural Network
- Computational Graphs
- K-means
- Gradient Descent, Stochastic Gradient Descent, Mini-Batch Stochastic Gradient Descent
- Generalization
- K-Fold Cross Validation
Topic 3: Problem-solving by Search
- Depth-first Search (DFS)
- Breadth-first Search (BFS)
- Iterative Deepening Search (DFS + BFS)
- Uniform-cost Search (UCS)
- Greedy Search
- A* Search and heuristics used
- Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)
- Local Search
(1) Hill Climbing Algorithm
(2) Simulated Annealing Algorithm
(3) Local Beam Search Algorithm
(4) Genetic Algorithm
Topic 4: Markov Decision Processes and Reinforcement Learning
- Uncertainty and Markov Decision Process
- Policy Evaluation
- Value Iteration
- Policy Iteration
- Model-based Reinforcement Learning
- Temporal Difference Value Learning
- Temporal Difference Q-Learning
- Feature Representation for Approximating Q-Learning
Topic 5: Games
- Zero-Sum Game and Adversarial Game Tree
- Minimax (Adversarial Search)
- Depth-limited Minimax
- Alpha-Beta Pruning
- Expectiminimax
Topic 6: Probabilistic Reasoning
- Uncertainty
- Bayes' Nets (Not tested)
- Bayes' Nets Inference (Not tested)
- Naive Bayers Classifier
Topic 7: Logic
- Propositional and First-order Logic

==After this, I feel that each lecture can be a whole course==
My opinion: Seriously wtf that difficulty is not for 3211 students

Topic 8: AI Application
- Computer Vision Application
(Semantic Segmentation, Fully Convolutional, Skip Connection, Dilated Convolution, ASPP, Mask-RCNN, Self-Supervised Representation Learning)
- Vision-Language Application
(Open World Learning, CLIP)
- Robotics Application
(SLAM, Visual Odometry, VSLAM, Pose-Net, DeepVO, SfM-Learner)
Topic 9: Conclusion (?)

In lecture, prof will teach you more on the theory stuff of the AI.
Applications are not tested (Thankfully)

In tutorial, TA will teach you how to apply what you learn, including implementing them in Python. You will use Python library like PyTorch.

Knowledge from other courses that could be helpful:
COMP 2211: Basics of Python, Minimax, alpha-beta pruning, Naive Bayes, CNN
COMP 2711(H): Time (and space) complexity, expected value
COMP 3711(H): DFS, BFS, pseudocode understanding, optimality
MATH 2011/2023: Vector calculus (lmao)
MATH 2411: MLE

Courses are supposed to spark your interest or teach you something useful. This course taught me something else entirely: how a professor can treat students like background noise.

This is one of the worst professors at HKUST, hands down.

I waited half a year to write this so it wouldn’t be a heat-of-the-moment post. Nope. I still remember every detail. He never answers emails himself. Every time he “replies” he just forwards the message to a TA with a canned note like:

Dear <TA Name>,
Can you reply to this student?
Thanks.

Cool, professors are busy, fine. But when the course’s midterm had clear grading errors that affected at least five students, we brought it up respectfully to the TAs and got dismissed with attitude. The TAs acted like they were the ones in charge, and then told us, “Calling him down here is useless, because we are in charge of the grading.” What? TAs more powerful than the professor now?

That line escalated things, so three of us went to his office. He basically told us, “I am doing my research, can you email me instead? I will look into it.” Which is the same empty loop as his forwarding habit, email gets pushed to TAs, TAs brush you off, repeat.

After us knocking on his door like three more times, he finally came out and fixed the grading mistake. But why should students have to spend extra time chasing down mistakes that are the course staff’s fault? In other classes you don’t need to fight to get a simple correction.

Thanks to this course I learned how some professors (maybe just him) treat teaching like an afterthought. If you’re thinking of taking his course, rethink it. And then rethink it again. I don’t want to see headlines about students jumping off because of negligent instructors.

I’m an Ext-AI student. I don’t hate CS or AI, but because of this experience I won’t touch his or COMP courses again.

James (if you’re reading this), seriously, go back to your research. Stop killing students’ grades and time. You’re not cut out for teaching. If any future students drop the course or worse because of how you run things, that’s on you.

This university clearly values professors’ convenience over students’ learning. Thanks for the lesson. I’ll never forget it.

comment_content:
These are the brief content for this semester. Not very difficult things, but take time to remember to digest.
But most of the things seem not that related to AI... The course name is better viewed as "COMP 3211 - Fundamental things BEFORE you learn AI"
You can have a look at them and decide whether you are into those things:

1. Simple Intelligent Agents
    Designing Agent Controller by Rules: Programming agents (like a thermostat or a simple game bot) using specific rules it must follow. For example, "if the room temperature is below 20°C, turn on the heater."
    Supervised Learning: Teach an agent by giving it examples. Each example has inputs and the correct output (like teaching a child with flashcards). Over time, the agent learns to predict the output from the inputs.
    Genetic Programming: Create programs or agents automatically by mimicking the process of natural evolution. Programs evolve over generations to become more effective at performing tasks.

2. Search
    Uninformed Search: Do not have any extra information about the states beyond what’s provided in the problem definition. It blindly searches for the solution (like looking for an object in a dark room).
   Heuristic Search: Using clever guesswork, to make searches more efficient (like playing "hot and cold" to find an object).
    Adversarial Search: Used in scenarios like chess, where you have an opponent. The goal is to find the best move considering the opponent will also be trying to outsmart you.
    MDP (Markov Decision Processes): A mathematical framework for modeling decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision-maker.

3. Learning
    Supervised Learning: As mentioned, this involves learning from labeled data—data that has the answer key.
    Unsupervised Learning: Here, you work with data without labels. The goal might be to identify patterns or groupings in the data.
    Reinforcement Learning: Learning based on feedback—it’s about taking actions in an environment to maximize some notion of cumulative reward.
    Rule-based Learning: This involves learning the rules that govern a system from examples or through direct programming of rules.

4. Knowledge Representation, Reasoning, and Planning
    Knowledge Representation: How to store knowledge in a computer so it can be used efficiently.
    Reasoning: Deals with using the stored knowledge to answer questions or solve problems.
    Planning: Involves creating a series of actions to achieve a specific goal, like planning a route for a robot to follow.

5. Multi-agent Systems, Game Theory, and Auction
    Multi-agent Systems: Systems composed of multiple interacting agents. They can cooperate, coordinate, or compete with each other.
    Game Theory: The study of mathematical models of conflict and cooperation between intelligent rational decision-makers.
    Auction Theory: Design and analysis of auction mechanisms, focusing on how goods or services are allocated and at what prices.

6. Uncertainty
    Situations where it's impossible to know exactly what will happen. AI systems, like humans, often make decisions under uncertainty. Techniques like probability and statistics are used to handle uncertainty in AI.

comment_content:
1. Introduction
2. Reactive Agents
-> Perceptron
-> Genetic programming

-> Alpha-beta pruning

-> Auction
7. Knowledge Representation and Reasoning
-> Propositional logic
-> Predicate logic
8. Reasoning Under Uncertainty
-> Bayes Theorem (Conditional probability)
-> Bayes Network
-> D-separation

The key topics are listed above.

(Disclaimer: I am not ordered or paid by Desmond to commercial or boast his AI course. But...3211 is kinda the AI intro course before 2211 comes out. I think it is not bad to compare the "AI intro course in the past" and "Current AI intro course" and see which is more suitable as "AI intro course")

In my opinion, this course is a hybrid of COMP2211, COMP2711 and any game theory course (IEDA2010, etc.) with some extra materials, but the contents are explained in a more theoretical and mathematical way. This course covers broad topics related to AI (But not limited to machine learning). If you have some experience with AI before, you may be able to know more about the theory behind these topics. However, it is not suitable for beginners. It is because the coverage is too broad while the ideas are explained in a too abstract and theoretical way. Each concept is not explained practically and in-depth enough.

For example, both 2211 and this course covers perceptron and minimax / alpha-beta pruning. However, this course only covers single layer perceptron. The activation function are all binary function (if sum>=theta then true, otherwise false). Other activation function like Sigmoid, ReLU are not taught. For minimax and alpha-beta pruning, they only appear in the lectures notes for a few pages. The prof explains the minimax and alpha-beta pruning algorithm in an too abstract way but he didn't even give one complete tracing example for us (Then somehow the exam requires us to trace it...damn 2211 really saves my life).

Moreover, this course does not teach you any coding. No labs, no hands-on experience. However, they will appear on the assignment. You need you figure out what are you doing by yourself, which is not friendly for people who are not familiar with AI.

As a result, I feel like I didn't learn many things as I do not have a big brain to digest that much abstract and mathematical ideas efficiently. I always get lost in the lecture. In terms of AI knowledge, I think I have learned more from 2211 rather than from this course.

What I want to say is that: If you are new to AI, you will easily get lost in this course and lose interest in AI quickly when facing a bunch of abstract ideas and math notations. Therefore, if you are new to AI, I strongly suggest you to take Desmond's COMP2211 first (Enjoy Desmond class :) ). Desmond tries to eliminate all the abstract ideas and math in his class. You will have a more in-depth understanding, as well as hands-on experience regarding AI. This may somehow enhance your interest on AI rather than being ruined at the beginning. Desmond really gives us a better starting point for learning AI :) .

Moreover, make sure you have taken COMP2711 before so that you can know the logic and probability parts well (There are many overlaps). If it is available, having studied game theory from other course like IEDA2010 is also an advantage (If you know what is Nash Equilibrium then it is quite enough for prior knowledge).

If you can gain some solid foundation before taking this course, you will have a large advantage as the exams focuses back on practical application while the lecture gives you a bunch of abstract ideas. For me, my prior knowledge in COMP2211, COMP2711, IEDA2010 really helps me a lot in performing in this course as when I attend the lecture, I always get lost because of the abstract ideas and the prof bad delivery...

One significant example is that in this course, K-means clustering is not explained in-depth. It only appears in the ML lecture notes for 1-2 pages and the prof passes it very quickly in the lecture. However, in the final exam, the prof gives us a surprise by asking us to trace the K-means clustering algorithm. Fortunately I studied that in 2211 so I just finish it easily. Desmond really carry 10 points in my final exam!

Therefore, I would say thank you Desmond, thank you Kenneth, thank you WANG, Xin

This course is a standard introductory course to AI. It covered most of the basics for an AI system and you would be able to learn how to build some simple AI systems (such as a simple pacman agent).

Syllabus:
1. Introduction
2. Reactive Agents
3. Agents that Search
4. Markov Decision Process and Reinforcement Learning
5. Introduction to Machine Learning
6. Knowledge Representation and Reasoning
7. Reasoning Under Uncertainty
(copied from other reviews, modified according to the current semester's syllabus)

comment_content:
Dekai's interest lie in natural language processing, and that really shows in this class. A ton of AI techniques are introduced, but they are usually in the context of translation or processing languages. People that have taken the course have probably heard "Time flies like an arrow" or "Fruit flies like an apple" a billion times. (BTW in case "fruit flies like an apple" doesn't make sense to you, a fruit fly is a type of fly.) There is also some random Chinese thrown in there sometimes, but it's not strictly necessary to know it to understand the class. It'll just increase enjoyment of the material somewhat.

There are two major topics in AI: searching/updating and knowledge representation. For searching/updating, you'll revise naïve search methods (BFS, DFS, Dijkstra's) and then be introduced to heurstic search, EM algorithms, forward backward algorithms, Knuth's algorithm, etc. For knowledge representation, you begin with hidden Markov models (HMMs) and then weighted finite state automata (WFSAs), unrolled HMMs and FSAs, progressing to AND/OR graphs and finite state grammars (FSGs). There is also a small taste of inversion transduction grammars.

Oh and because it's Dekai you also have Scheme thrown in there randomly in the beginning.
comment_teaching:
If you can't tell, I'm a big fan of Dekai's teaching style. He's really enthusiastic and is willing to force students to break that mold of "never asking questions" a lot. He will try his best to force you to speak in his class. His classes have a sort of "American" atmosphere to them, if that makes sense.

He also loves going on tangents related to his research, and he loves functional programming and cool things. He also makes use of the whiteboard a lot, which really beats the "reading the powerpoint" professors. I'm sure you know which ones I'm talking about.
comment_workload:
There was one programming assignment that was really easy if you knew a small amount of Scheme. The other homework is written and really easy. I think pretty much everyone just got those marks for free as long as your answer makes some sort of sense.

As long as you don't mind attending every lesson and copying diagrams on the board while listening to really awesome material about AI the workload is really light. No need to study very much for either the midterm or the final. The homeworks are easily completable within 30 minutes, and there are not that many.
comment_grading:
I ticked "attendance", but there is no attendance in the course. However, if you don't attend class, you'll be 100% screwed for the midterm and final simply because the notes are not going to be online and are only given when the topic is fully covered. He also does examples on the whiteboard, which are NOT in the notes, and he will test you on them.

There are also a bunch of other smaller assignments that apply what are taught in class. Those are not very difficult and can be done in about 30 minutes.

I didn't study for the midterm outside of just doing the assignments and taking notes in class and got a really decent grade, so the midterm is not very hard. The final exam is open handwritten notes, and since I took notes for every class I did really well. There was a scheme question at the end, but since almost everyone forgot their scheme they were just given marks for attempting it.

The distribution of grades is standard Dekai:
Midterm exam: ~20%
Final exam: ~25%
Participation: ~5%
Assignments: ~50%

Since it's Dekai, you have his typical marking scheme, which is a curve with the following guarantee:
> 85% is at LEAST an A
> 75% is at LEAST a B
> 65% is at LEAST a C
> 55% is at LEAST a D

comment_content:
Overall I think the content is quite good to introduce you to database. After taking this course, you can expect that it is not too hard for you to design database tables (so that it's not confusing and scattered), write SQL queries, and make a simple application built with it. Quite practical in my opinion.

I somewhat integrated some of the concepts from this course to organize my COMP3111 project (yes, pls use proper .db file or at least .json, don't use cursed .txt databases. Integrity constraints will also help you a lot)~

I do agree that second half feels like something that you won't need to know if you are just using a database. But I think it is a good addition for the completeness of the course. For example, in file organization you can understand why indexes are actually important, in transactions you can learn how database actually handles concurrent users in a large application. As a plus, you also get some experience to work on assigment4 on integrating your knowledge to actual ODBC application.

However, the course does not really go into NoSQL queries under Prof Wilfred.

== Contents ==
- E/R Diagrams
- Database Schema
- Relational Algebra
- SQL Queries (Big Topic! Was also on the Finals)
- Functional Dependencies (In different offerings, the amount of this topic in midterm varies. Also a big and rather confusing topic the first time you encounter it)

[Midterm is arranged around here]

- Physical Database and File Organization
- Transactions (How Multiple Users can be handled)
- NoSQL and MapReduce
- Database Connectivity (ODBC, JDBC, etc. -- just theory)

=== Comments on Topics ===
The part regarding E/R diagram might be intimidating at first. The assignment description was also not that clear (however, I think writing assumptions + asking questions helps). However questions in exam are generally not ambiguous and clear. I also find E/R diagram to make more sense than *cough* UML diagram from COMP3111 (btw note that the cardinality constraint, when you use numbers, if flipped -- you might take COMP3111 at the same time if you are a COMP student)

btw In general if you find the assignment description confusing just ask. The teaching team and Prof is quite nice. (emailed Prof on a weekend to ask about what he meant on assignment 3 🫠🫠🫠, he replied :3)

Schema is an OK topic, just remember how to convert, understand primary key, foreign key, etc.

Relational Algebra and SQL is slightly challenging. I don't know proper SQL before the course. My suggestion is to practice SQL a lot, places to practice: https://www.sql-practice.com/ and before exam you may try questions from UWash CSE 344 Exams (I cannot find the link when writing this review, let me update later 🤔-- the questions here are far harder than 3311). Then, you just make sure you can remember RA notations and you're fine.

FD is uninituitive at first, but you can understand it from attending the tutorial and do the problem set slides on the course website. Finding extra practice problems online also helps.

Physical Database and File Organization is quite confusing (mostly because the questions in the tutorial is quite different). The more important topics are actually (according to past papers and the exam):
- Calculating blocking factor and how many pages needed to store it
- Calculating a single Nested Loop, Sort-Merge, Hash Join Costs
- B+ Trees dry run
See the past paper (released arround exam time) and learn why the answers are the way they are.

Transactions needs some practice, but just try to learn the rules and pay attention in the tutorial. Also locking protocol can be confusing, take some time to review it. If you are taking COMP3511 at this time, some intuition from OS topic on concurrency will help.

The rest are mostly theories (NoSQL and Connectivity, they will appear mostly in MCQs except MapReduce).

Some exam questions solutions are indeed misleading, you can ask Prof about it (and Prof also admits that there are mistakes).

comment_content:
After this class is over, you will still be wondering what a "Database Management System" is.

Content up to the midterm covers:
- E-R Diagrams
- Relational Database Design
- Relational Algebra
- SQL
This content is fine, although you should probably learn some SQL before taking this course, because the midterm scores are competitive.

Post-midterm content up to the final covers:
- MongoDB
- Indexing (B+ tree)
- Query Processing
- Transaction Management

Side note, MongoDB is straight up the worst language I have ever used, the syntax is so unbelievably annoying. It does have some similarity with SQL, but they will not explicitly explain that to you.

At this point, every lecture is just straight up useless since they do not actually teach the DBMS architecture properly. I would STRONGLY recommend just watching Berkeley's CS186 course on YouTube.

https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLYp4IGUhNFmw8USiYMJvCUjZe79fvyYge

Like, I went to every lecture and understood nothing. Then, before the final, I watched the Berkeley CS186 videos and finally learned what the fuck an "index nested loop join" was, among various other things.

The content of this class (DBMS) is very useful and important in industry, but the way they teach it is just astonishingly abhorrent, to say the least. If you are able, please take this class at a different university to save your sanity.

comment_content:
Don’t be fooled by the occasional positive reviews of this course—I can’t imagine how it received any praise at all. If you dare to enrol, your experience will be nothing short of “eye-opening” and “unprecedented”—but not in a good way. I’ve asked plenty of my classmates, and NOT A SINGLE ONE had anything positive to say. Let me be clear: I’m not targeting those who left good reviews, but please allow me to say this—please DON'T blindly praise the course just because your grade is satisfying or you happen to like the subject. The truth is obvious to anyone paying attention. Of course, if ur an undercover agent planted by the instructor to write glowing reviews (just hypothetically speaking), then I’ve got nothing else to add. That’s beyond words.

This course is supposed to be foundational for full-stack development, yet it’s been handed to an instructor who is nothing short of a disaster. The lecture notes are a complete mess. Let’s start with the obvious errors. Especially in the material after the midterm, there were at least 5 glaring mistakes. These aren’t small ambiguities—they’re clear, misleading errors that should never have made it into teaching materials. Some of these mistakes are in example answers, and even key formulas are wrong. This is not an exaggeration; it’s all backed by evidence—just check the lecture note PDFs or the Canvas discussion boards.

What’s worse is that most of these mistakes were never fixed. The teaching team didn’t bother to correct them, even when students pointed them out in discussions. When they did respond, it was in a one-on-one reply buried in a thread, as if they were quietly whispering corrections in secret. So, let me ask: do you not care if the rest of the students remain misled? I never imagined that mistakes this serious wouldn’t deserve a public correction.

Here’s the most ridiculous example: one of the most misleading formula errors was corrected via a mass email ONE HOUR BEFORE THE FINAL EXAM. This error had already been flagged on the Canvas discussion board days earlier, but the TA’s response was a single reply to the student who raised it. Then—silence. Nothing. The issue disappeared into thin air until that last-minute email... I was outside the exam hall discussing the formula with a friend; he hadn’t seen the email and had memorised the wrong version.

And then there’s the utter chaos of the lecture notes themselves. They’re so badly organised that they’re practically unusable. A single lecture’s content is scattered across multiple pages, with titles that give no clue about the content. Is this some twisted attempt to confuse us? To artificially make the course seem harder with this jumbled mess? At one point, I even suspected the instructor had copy-pasted random notes from others without bothering to organise or check them. But even that doesn’t explain the SHEER NUMBER OF ERRORS.

In short, if you’re unlucky enough to take this course, do yourself a favour and ignore the lecture notes entirely. Even looking at them feels like an insult to the subject. Instead, search online forums for clear, logical explanations of the material. If you want proof of what I’m saying, just finish learning on other platforms and revisit the genius lecture notes. You’ll see the chaos, the errors, the confusion—and you’ll thank yourself for not relying on the lec notes to learn.
comment_teaching:
Honestly, writing this section feels like giving him more credit than he deserves—it’s a waste of my time and effort. The ONLY REASON I’m forcing myself to finish it is to make sure that anyone even slightly thinking about taking his course will give up on that idea completely.

Let’s start with how he gives lectures. He’s the perfect example of a “PPT reader.” Now, you might think there’s nothing special about PPT readers since you’ve probably seen plenty of them here. But no—please stick with me—because this man takes being a PPT reader to A WHOLE NEW LEVEL.

First, you’ll need at least 2–3 lectures just to figure out what he’s even saying. Ever heard someone talk in their sleep? That’s basically him. His tone is flat, his pronunciation is so unclear, and his voice never changes—it’s always at the same dull pitch. I suspect he’s afraid that changing his voice even slightly might accidentally wake up the students who’ve already fallen asleep.

Sometimes, his words don’t match the PPT exactly. That’s because his PPTs come with carefully written scripts. If you compare the scripts in his PPTs to what he says in the lecture recordings, you’ll see he reads them out word for word. One line that stayed with me was: “Any questions?”—yes, that was ACTUALLY WRITTEN in his script. Imagine how much effort it takes to write something like that! I have to give him credit—no detail is too small for this man. Oh, and here’s a little suggestion: next time, for a course like this, just hire any English-speaking person to read the slides aloud. It would be much cheaper than hiring a “distinguished professor” for the job. (Apparently, he eventually realised he forgot to delete the scripts from the PPTs, because later uploads didn’t have them anymore. )

Now let’s talk about his Paper Checking Masterclass. For a while, I thought his poor lecture style might be because of a limited grasp of English. But no—when I talked with him in Chinese, his thoughts were JUST AS MESSY. From his exam questions, I can tell he tried to play it safe by sticking to the style of past papers. Yet somehow, even with that, he still managed to write vague, confusing questions. The whole paper-checking process became a joke where students had to explain the questions back to the teaching team. Of course, the teaching team first insisted the questions were fine, with ziyi being the most stubborn of all. But in the end, they gave in and returned the points we deserved.

One particularly memorable case involved a clearly incorrect question on the final exam. Ziyi, however, stood his ground and refused to admit it. It got so bad that EVEN THE TA'S were too embarrassed to let it slide—one of them actually argued with ziyi on our behalf. Sure enough, several days later, many students got the points they were owed.

Oh, and let’s not forget his famous coupon stunt. I heard that in one lecture, he gave out a coupon to students who attended, allowing them to skip one question on the final exam (you can probably confirm this on USTSpace). Naturally, like most students, I didn’t attend that lecture, so I didn’t get the coupon. About this, I just have to say: absolutely amazing. Ziyi dared to add secret perks to the course without telling everyone, setting a new standard for secretly rewarding students. It’s his 1st year at UST, and I can’t imagine what kind of person thinks this is ok. Was this his way of punishing students who didn’t show up to his lectures? Was he trying to force us to attend despite his terrible teaching? Did he even think about the consequences of CHANGING THE SYLLABUS without clarifying it beforehand? Instead of trying to improve his teaching, he resorts to cheap tricks. I honestly can’t see how any responsible teacher would ever do this...

Some people say he’s new and deserves some patience, but let’s be clear—he taught in Australia for several years before coming here. This isn’t a rookie mistake; it’s a HABIT. I have no idea what’s going on in this man’s head: lectures that could put anyone to sleep, a refusal to admit mistakes when marking, and shady coupon distribution. This is ziyi—just the PLAIN FACTS. If you’re thinking about taking his course, the decision is yours.

Finally, let’s talk about the teaching team as a whole. Most of them can barely communicate properly in English. Their replies in the discussion forums are slow, unhelpful, and often miss the point. You can clearly feel the frustration and negativity in the questions students ask—it’s obvious in every word. But even with all of this, nothing compares to the disaster of ziyi’s teaching.

comment_teaching:
Professors: LIU Ziyi

- Clear Projection of Voice
Ziyi is from mainland. I first thought his accent maybe unclear from my experience of attending lectures from mainland instructors. However, Ziyi has a very fluent speech and good projection of voice that I think he is different from other professor that I have met before. I can hear what he is saying easily even when not looking at the notes.

- Interesting teaching styles
Ziyi used many interesting examples to illustrate the abstract concepts in the lecture notes that is not mentioned in the notes. For example, he used the course registration system as an example to show how the ER diagram is drawn out instead of the plain definition of relationships and entity. I think this help me a lot in understanding the lecture materials and help me learn better.

- Quick response on questions
Ziyi always answer question raised by students after class. Even the students of next class arrived, he is willing to answer all of the question outside the lecture hall. Besides, he always respond to my email in a few hours. This shows he is really a enthusiastic and responsible teacher.

- Reinforcement in lecture
Since this is the first year for Ziyi to teach in HKUST. At first, I think he is not quite used to teach in a large room and face to a lot of student. He is first more focus on the teaching material part instead of the interaction between students. He finishes part of the material and ask student if we have any questions. Sometimes I cannot catch up well in certain part and not understand the part well. Later on, he includes more interaction with us so that we can catch up more easily and answer our questions more timely, which make my learning process faster. Besides, he tried to draw diagram on the whiteboard at front to show to all students at first. A student cannot see it clearly and run out to the front to take photo. After that, professor try to use the projector to display the drawing on paper, which I think he is good at reflecting his way of teaching and try to reinforce his lecture in certain way so that he can help student more in their learning. I appreciate that a lot.

- Bonus point
Ziyi has done a very interesting thing that I haven't seen other professor did throughout my entire UST life. Let me provide some background information first. This course does not require attendance in lecture, tutorial or lab session. So there are not many students attending the lecture. There are only around 20 students inside the big lecture theatre(LTA) which is for 400 students. As there is a serious attendance issue in the course. Professor gave a "COUPON" to each students who attended the lecture in one day. I was like wtf is a coupon when I heard him mentioning the giveaway. He gave each of us a coupon that can remove one question in the final exam. That means we can get some free mark in the final exam for free FOR FREE. He is the most generous and handsome professor in the department. No. in the whole campus. Just kidding. Desmond is better than him, hehe. But I really shocked when he give me the coupon as I have never seen such bonus of attending the lecture in a course that does not require attendance. I think he is really good, and I hope that he can give me more coupon that can be used in other courses.

Database Management Systems

-- drawing ER diagram after reading comprehension of the question
Relational Algebra (RA)
-- another expression of SQL
Structured Query Language (SQL)
-- select from where ...
Relational Database Design
-- how to reduce redundancy but keeps functional dependency
*** Midterm test everything above ***
Storage and File Structure
-- how data is saved and retrieved(a little bit comp 2611)
Indexing
-- many different ways to reduce page I/O cost of retrieving data
Query Processing
-- highly related to indexing
Query Optimization
-- if you fail indexing and query processing, you cant understand anything here
Transactions
-- finally new easy things
Concurrency Control
-- how to run multiple programs at the same time while making sure everything reads and write in correct order
Recovery System
-- how to undo or redo after something crashes
*** Final test everything above ***
NoSQL Databases
-- not graded, yay

The content of this course is really huge, it is just like two course combining each other, you really need some time to understand it. Also, boring is definitely a problem when having too much information.

For the final, in past paper, you will see some question which is so called 玄學 (metaphysics by google translate) like calculating cost, join in QEP. However, for this professor, as what he said, his final exam does not require calculator. He doesn't require us to calculate join/select cost. He just ask for the principle and formula of the join which already teach us in tutorial and lecture. For query optimization, what you need is assumption base on the rule in notes, not imagination. His final exam is really better than other professor design since every question is reasonable.

But there are still some problems in the course. The answer of final and midterm exercise is not released. How do we know are there any mistake when the TA revising the examination papers? Also, the exam format is change before an hour of final. We suddenly require to finish the exam in word. However, a question require us to use some symbol and we don't know how to type it. comment_content:
DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE IF YOU'LL NOT WORK WITH DATABASES IN THE FUTURE! This course is very tough, very time-consuming and I DON'T recommend taking this for fun!! There are other fun things you can do with your life 🙂

(🔥 = Difficult/Confusing)
1️⃣ ER Model & Database Design
2️⃣ Relational Model
   🔹 Reduction of ER model
   🔥🔥 2NF, 3NF, BCNF
3️⃣ Relational Algebra
4️⃣ SQL
   🔹 Basic operations
   🔹 Aggregate functions
   🔹 DDL & DML
[📝 Mid-term]
5️⃣ Storage & File Structure
6️⃣ Indexing
   🔹 Single & multi-level indexing
   🔹 B+ tree index
   🔥 Hash index
7️⃣ Query Processing
   🔹 Selection cost & external sorting
   🔥 Join cost
   🔹 Projection, set operation cost
8️⃣🔥🔥 Query Optimization
9️⃣ Transactions & Concurrency Control
   🔹 Serializability
   🔹 Two-phased locking systems
   🔹 Timestamp protocols
🔟 Database Recovery
NoSQL Databases (not tested in final)
[📝 Final (cumulative)]

The first half of the course (before MT) is highly practical. It first teaches you the ER Model (similar to class diagrams in COMP3111) and how to extract information from a problem statement. Then, it teaches you the Relational Model, ie. converting the "class" diagram into different tables. Finally, there are 3 lessons on Oracle SQL where you learn how to make queries, create tables and insert table data.

However, the second half of the course is a LIVING HELL since everything is theoretical. You'll learn the data structures and algorithms used behind database management system and it's difficult as fuck 😪. The query optimization part is the most difficult and confusing since there're endless possibilities to evaluate a query and you need to provide the most optimized plan to process it. Most of the time I don't know what the fuck I'm doing 😩😩😩😩
comment_teaching:
TLDR: Professor is a nice guy with very fluent English. However, he basically SPEEDRUNS every lesson since there are too much content to cover and around 70% of the time you'll feel completely lost and confused at the end of the lecture 😫😫

📃 Teaching Style
He's fine in explaining the concepts on the slides and sometimes he emphasizes on some tricky parts and concepts. He is also very responsive to student's questions in Zoom (this semester is online due to COVID) and he can answer all the questions relatively quickly. However, every lesson is packed with rich content and he loves teaching at an INSANELY FAST speed, which is IMPOSSIBLE to catch up unless you're a genius. Every lesson feels like a world-record speedrun attempt and I usually get completely lost at around half way though the lesson 😪😪

The craziest lecture is lecture 5 on 2NF, 3NF and BCNF. Someone on YouTube has made a 6 hour playlist on the exact same content but the professor managed to cram all of that in a 1.5 hour lesson 😈. Just watch this playlist instead: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLdo5W4Nhv31b33kF46f9aFjoJPOkdlsRc

📃 Teaching Materials
The teaching materials are well designed in general. Each lecture has its own lecture slide, in-class worksheet and a worksheet solution (released at the next lesson), and they are well designed. However, the slides are a bit confusing to understand for the second half of the course.

📃 Tutorials & Labs
The "teaching" in tutorials is kinda shitty since the TA just reads out the tutorial slides and the exercise solutions. The labs exercises are pretty useful for you to practice writing SQL and PL/SQL though.
comment_workload:
TLDR: Feels like a 4-credits course. Shit ton of things to be submitted and requires lots of time to revise and study 😪😪😪.

📝Lesson, tutorial and lab exercises
This is fucking insane. We need to complete and submit a worksheet (1 or 2 pages) for every lesson, tutorial and lab (9 labs in total) at 11pm within the same date. These exercises can take from 5~10 minutes to as long as 1 hour to complete if you're answering them seriously. Since everyone get lost at the end of the lecture, you're forced to revise the lecture slides within the same day in order to know how to do those exercises and this is very time consuming.

📝Project
The project is also very time-consuming and each task takes me around 7~10 hours to complete. The first task is to draw an ER model based on a problem statement, which is quite difficult since you need to pay attention to many small details. The second task is even more annoying since you need to go through the problem statement multiple times in order to extract all the constraints that needed to be set when defining the tables in SQL. The third task is a little bit better, but we still need to write 40 short to medium SQL queries in a C# file.

📝Mid-term and Final
Since I got confused in almost every lesson, I need to spend a shit ton of time to revise the lecture slides when preparing for the mid-term and final exam. The final exam is also cumulative so I need to spend extra time revising for the first half of the course.

In summary, this feels like a 4-credits course. In fact, I've completed several 4-credits course like COMP2012, COMP3111 and MATH2411 and NONE of these courses have higher workload than this course

Although there are a few mistakes in the lecture notes, overall the lecture notes are well-designed. The content before midterm should be easier.

Most of the topics appeared in lectures will be in the exams. Tutorials just repeat the lecture content right after the lecture ends. Labs are not included in the exams. Attendance is not mandatory. The reference book is not mandatory.

You need to complete a worksheet after each lecture, tutorial and lab and submit on that day.

These worksheets are easy. They will be graded on efforts instead of correctness so full marks for sure. Very similar problems will also be demonstrated on class.

Skipping class is not a good idea. You don't get to watch the lecture videos shortly after the class. If you skip one, you probably can only rely on the lecture notes to complete the exercise before the day ends.

comment_teaching:
The PowerPoint slides are very well-designed; you can learn 90% of the course just by reading them.

The professor explains the concepts in great detail and is more than happy to answer questions during and off-class. He also periodically issues surveys to check on our progress and receive feedback on the course, which is a great plus.
comment_workload:
Exercises: You are supposed to complete all of them during class, so the take-home workload is minimal.

Projects: The SQL statements can be a debugging nightmare, but otherwise they are quite straightforward. Each project will not take you more than a full day's work.
comment_grading:
[Grading Scheme]
- Lecture, Tutorial and Lab Exercises (10%)
Lecture and tutorial exercises are handed out during almost every class and are normally due by the end of class. Some of them are done individually but most have to be done with another student; otherwise, only half credit will be given. Lab exercises are released during lab and are due by 11 pm on the same day.

Grading is based on effort. You can get either full credit, half credit or zero credit for each exercise.

- Projects (30%)
There are three projects, each taking up 10% of the final grade. The projects have a common theme and are progressive, meaning that each project builds upon the previous one. Do not worry; a sample project is given so that you will not have to rely on your own work.

- Midterm Test (20%) & Final Exam (40%)
These cover all of the previous lecture content. The final exam places emphasis on the latter half of the course though.

[My Grades]
Exercises: 100/100 (Mean: 92.5)
Projects: 281/300 (Mean: 258.0)
Midterm Test: 90.0/100 (Mean: 77.4)
Final Exam: 92.0/100 (Mean: 71.8)
Overall: 92.9% (Grade: A)

It is quite difficult to achieve A+ in this course.

comment_content:
Very hands-on and practical. We've got to learn how to manage a relational database from design to implementation using Oracle. While DBMS technology is actually quite old, it's still very widely used in a lot of companies and I'm sure most of us would find it useful.

The highlight of the course is definitely SQL. Don't underestimate its difficulty - SQL grammar is simple, but its usage is tricky. The logic of formulating SQL is often not that trivial and requires some little twists or tricks.

After the midterm, the content then moves to the physical level of the database. This part is more theoretical and conceptually demanding. Basic understanding in algorithms and data structures such as binary trees, B+ trees and hashing would help.

Wilfred also introduced NoSQL this year. But due to time limitation, not much implementation could be taught. If you're interested, you can move on self-learning and playing around the more trendy technologies like MongoDB and Hadoop.

1️⃣ Introduction to DBMS
2️⃣ ER Model
3️⃣ Relational Algebra
4️⃣ SQL
      🔶 DDL, DML
      🔶 PL/SQL, Cursors, Triggers
5️⃣ Normalization
      🔶 3NF
      🔶 BCNF
6️⃣ Physical File Organization
      🔶 Indexing
      🔶 Query Optimizer
      🔶 RAID
7️⃣ Transaction Management
      🔶 ACID Properties
      🔶 Recovery
      🔶 Concurrency Control
8️⃣ NoSQL Databases
      🔶 BASE Principles
      🔶 MapReduce
9️⃣ Database APIs
      🔶 ODBC

This is a real introductory course - you're only expected to have basic C++ programming and algorithm background, which I think are reasonable. I've seen so many other courses that unrealistically expected us to have already learned the thing in order to take the course. Ridiculous. This course, while still difficult, has a much more manageable learning curve. You don't need to know DBMS beforehand in order for you to take this course.
comment_teaching:
Not quite sure why so many students bitch about Wilfred's teaching (because he gives bad grades?) I personally love his teaching - He's very beginner-friendly, starting from the basic and walking through with you all the necessary steps. (I also took his Java course in my year 1) He's deffo a lot better than the usual type of professors that treats everyone as genii and casually skips details in explanation.

comment_workload:
✏️💻 30% 4 Assignments (5% + 5% + 10% + 10%)
✏️ 30% Midterm
✏️ 40% Final

The first 2 are written assignments - pretty simple and can be finished in about 1 or 2 hours each. The 3rd assignment is on SQL, including database definition and writing complex queries. The 4th one is a C++ programming assignment on ODBC. These 2 programming assignments will take you longer time and make sure you've got enough test cases to check for the robustness.

The marking on the assignments is quite strict. You either get full marks or 0 in each task. Usually no partial credits will be given.

Overall the workload is manageable.

comment_content:
这门课的主要内容:
midterm前:
- ER model
- Relational Model
- Relational Algebra
- SQL
- Functional Dependency
- 3NF, BCNF

midterm后:
- Memory Hierarchy
- Database Indexing
- Query Processing + Optimisation
- Transaction
- Concurrency Control
- Database Recovery
***我们的ppt跟很多不同大学的笔记一模一样，相信教授在做这些slides时也花了很多功夫上网参考其它大学的database课。

作为elective的课，从midterm前的SQL query到midterm后的database理论，这门课结合了大量关于database的知识。有的童鞋可能会说，midterm前学的更为practical，以后做工才可能会用到，而midterm后的都是以后不可能会用到的。我只能说，这样想的童鞋不如自己去自学SQL，相信能节省更多时间，不必花一门课、一个学期去学database。再次强调，这是一门elective课，不可能只想要进来划划水，学写一些SQL就算。这门课除了要教童鞋们写SQL之外，更多的是要provide童鞋们在database方面一个更全面的认知。

虽然有时我也不太赞同教授企图把很大量的知识都挤在一门课内教完，但我认为大多数知识都是重要、有用的，只不过会让人有些消化不过来。无论是以后想往database方向发展还是只纯粹想学学database的童鞋，都强烈推荐上这门课，感觉上读CS的人都应该要对database有不错的认识。

忠告：这门课不适合抱大腿和刷grade
