comment_content: OS content itself is fair and quite important.
comment_teaching:
Pretty bad. The prof is like doing TED, trying to practice his speaking skills. But I would say what makes the delivery so poor is the slides.

I have no idea who wrote the slides, but the slides are full of long texts. Studying the slides is essentially reading a book. These kinds of slides are definitely not suitable for in-class teaching, especially when the instructor does not want to align exactly with the slides. If you fail to understand a single idea you are very likely to lose track of the entire lesson.

Also, the slides are very unstructured! Some concepts could have been explained much more clearer with simple patterns like branching, table, etc. The slides are so unstructured that it looks like a transcript of the lecture’s oral presentation. This just won’t work for students to understand! Let alone there are a lot of semantic ambiguities that drive you confused and puzzled, which can only be clarified with AI and Google.

Overall, DO NOT expect to have a great learning experience with the slides. They are completely trash.
comment_workload: Depends on studying the slides? If you can read and understand the slides pretty well, congratulations this course does not have big workloads.
comment_grading: I have not received a grade yet. The homework and PA look standard so far. But I do not expect some good grades as indicated by other reviewers.

comment_teaching:
I think the biggest problem with the lectures is that our professor doesn't speak that clearly. I'm not quite sure how to describe it, but for me, I feel like I have to really try and concentrate to understand what he says sometimes. Besides that, I think his teaching is alright.

Lecture notes are quite good. There are many useful diagrams and all content is neatly organized. They are pretty much the only materials you will need for revision.

(I didn't really go to lab so I can't say too much about them. The sessions with Peter Chung were pretty good, the rest were kinda meh)
comment_workload:
Homework and PAs are quite straightforward, with a lot of the steps/ideas found in the lecture notes, so as long as you double check and don't make mistakes you should get close to full marks for both.

Midterm and final are both open book, meaning that you can print out anything you want and bring them to the exam (in fact, one of the MCQs I've seen in the final straight up asks you to look at a certain lecture note slide.) This reduces the required revision time a lot.

These two exams are quite easy as well, with a lot of simple questions just asking you to implement or calculate something by hand, and relatively few tricky conceptual type questions. This does mean that the mean/average will be quite high, so do try your best.

I think the overall grading is very good as the instructors maximize the A-range and make their effort to push up your sub-grade.
Just above UQ for few marks can have A grade, not A-. Even you have a bit lower than UQ can also have A- if your daily assignment is excellent. My friend say that the B-range even larger, if you really fried in one of the exams (almost LQ) can also have B/B-.

But need to beware that this course is full of CSE students which make this course very competitive. The contents are not very difficult, in additional of open book exam will make the mean to be very high, also the SD is very small. You will totally be in disadvantage if you (probably >=1 subgrade lower than deserved)
1. Get low score in ANY of HW/PA.
2. Perform bad in exam due to some other factors such as you slept not well or the questions are not your cup of tea etc.
3. Lag behind in process.
4. Get many careless mistakes.

From Midterm Exam, there is some tracing problem related to CPU scheduling, you need to apply many algorithms to find what CPU done in almost 50-100 time units! You may easily get careless mistakes.
For Final Exam, there are even more tracing problems, for example this:
MC10. Consider the following page access reference string:
1 2 3 4 2 1 5 3 4 2 5 3 4 6 7 0 3 6
7 1 8 3 6 1 8 9 2 3 1 2 8 1 0 8 3 1 2 8 7 and assume that 4 frames are allocated to the process.
Using the Least Recently Used (LRU) replacement algorithm, determine the total number of
page faults that will occur for this reference string.
A) 23
B) 24
C) 25
D) 26
You really need to do many tracings (this problem 39 times >.<) and computation, really need to be careful.

This leads that I am stressed and cautious in the whole semester qwq. I can see that some classmates overall only lower than me by 2 marks yields an A- or even B+. Only small difference will lead to a sub-grade.

comment_content:
Content is quite a lot and kind of dry but I believe those are important concepts any CS students should know especially for those who want to work in the field of Computer Systems or software developers.

I think the key here is that modern OSs use "multi-tasking" (CPU frequently switching between jobs) so that you can play game and watch Youtube "at the same time". You'll learn how OSs achieve this, how OSs deal with the various problems that arise and how to optimize in this course.

In labs, you can also learn C programming, terminal commands (csh) in Linux.

comment_content: This is an introductory course on operating systems. The topics will include the basic concepts of operating systems, process and threads, inter-process communications, process synchronization, scheduling, memory allocation, page and segmentation, secondary storage, I/O systems, file systems, and protection. It contains the key concepts as well as examples drawn from a variety of real systems such as Microsoft Windows and Linux.
comment_teaching: Professor Li is excellent at teaching as a new professor. He always gives us some interesting examples in the difficult concepts and always say“ for example,...”. There are also funny interactions(like calling students to write number on the paper mimicking the process in the OC process) and communications in the class.
comment_workload:
There will be 3 project assignments (PAs): Linux Shell, MLFQ, and Simplified Memory Management. The code skeletons are well-designed and students just need to finish the main part and check the logic with the given inputs and outputs files, and some hidden tests should also be considered during the homework.

The mid-term and final exams do not have any content overlap. Besides, the question type and order follow the previous exam papers. The homework is also similar to the exams.

comment_teaching:
Not really a fan of Li Bo's teaching. He rambles quite a bit and I found it hard to pay attention. I found it more than sufficient to read the slides and the textbook, of which takes about 25% the time of watching the actual lecture.

However, sometimes Li Mo (the other instructor for 3511) covered for Li Bo. He was much more interesting and said things which made us question and correct our understanding, which is what a great teacher should do.
comment_workload:
Very low workload. Just do the homeworks and PAs.

The homeworks take quite a bit of time if you don't keep up with the lectures, but otherwise they're not too bad. They can also serve as a tool for you to catch up with the lectures if you skip them.

For the exam, you don't need to memorize too much, but make sure to dry-run the scheduling algorithms a few times so you can do them quickly and accurately. Also, try to actually internalize the concepts so you can do the MCQ and non-computation questions more efficiently. Remember, time is very limited in the exam.

comment_content:
I understand that you're currently taking a course and found the midterm exam challenging. To prepare for the final exam, it's essential to expand your knowledge and understanding of the course material. Here are some suggestions to help you in your exam preparation:

Review lecture notes and course materials: Go through your lecture notes, textbooks, and any supplementary materials provided by your instructor. Make sure you understand the key concepts, theories, and formulas covered in the course.
Identify areas of weakness: Reflect on the midterm exam and identify the topics or areas where you struggled the most. Focus your study efforts on these areas to improve your understanding and performance.
Seek clarification: If there are specific topics or concepts that you find confusing, don't hesitate to seek clarification from your professor, teaching assistant, or classmates. They can provide additional explanations or resources to help you grasp difficult concepts.
Practice with sample questions: Look for practice questions or past exam papers related to the course. Attempting these questions will familiarize you with the exam format and allow you to apply your knowledge to solve problems similar to those that may appear on the final exam.
Form study groups: Collaborating with fellow classmates can be beneficial. Organize study groups to discuss and review course material together. Explaining concepts to others can deepen your understanding, and your peers may offer different perspectives and insights.
Utilize additional resources: If you feel the need for further explanations or examples, consider utilizing online resources, video tutorials, or academic websites related to your course topic. These resources can provide alternative explanations and additional practice opportunities.
Create a study schedule: Plan your study sessions in advance and create a schedule that allows for regular review and practice. Breaking down your study material into smaller, manageable chunks can help prevent procrastination and ensure comprehensive coverage of the course content.
Take care of yourself: Don't forget the importance of self-care during exam preparation. Get enough sleep, maintain a healthy diet, exercise regularly, and manage your stress levels. Taking care of your well-being will contribute to better focus and concentration during your study sessions.
Remember, effective exam preparation requires consistent effort and dedication. Give yourself sufficient time to review and practice, and don't hesitate to seek help when needed. Good luck with your final exam!

comment_content:
Content is surprisingly boring for an operating systems course. Covers basic OS concepts and simple implementation notes for most of them, but does not go too deep. This makes the course quite memorization-heavy, which I dislike.

Content list:
Operating System Structure (Syscalls, OS Interfaces, and Design Principles)
Processes (Creating Processes, fork(), and Inter-Process Communication)
Threads (Threading model, signalling, fork() and exec() in a thread)
CPU Scheduling (Scheduling Algorithms: Real-Time, Multi-level, and Simple Schedulers)
Synchronization (Critical Section, Mutual Exclusion, Locks, Semaphors, Reader-Writers Problem)
Deadlocks (Resource Allocation, Banker's Algorithm)
Main Memory (Allocation Strategies, Segmentation, Paging and the Page Table, Translation Look-Aside Buffers (TLBs))
Virtual Memory (Page and Frame Replacement Algorithms, Thrashing, Working-Set models)
Disks (Hard Disk Principles, Disk Scheduling, RAID)
File Systems (Mounting, Sharing, and Accessing Files, Disk Organization of File Systems and their Implementations)
Protection (Access Rights)

Some of the content is useful (the synchronization parts helped me understand how threading actually works in other programming languages), but most of it feels like "OS trivia" that we're supposed to know for the exam only. Parts of it are pretty "common sense" if you're a CS student whose daily driver is some variant of -nix.

Note: The midterm this year only covered up to CPU scheduling due to slow lecture progress by the other sections, so this semester's midterm past papers did not have synchronization. Expect to see synchronization in the midterm in a normal semester.
comment_teaching:
Dr Li taught quite well. He was able to adequately pace himself, his English is decent, and he often uses the whiteboard to illustrate some relatively harder concepts. His lecture session was almost always ~1 chapter ahead of the others, which meant that we weren't rushed for time.

The assignments and PAs were adequately prepared, and the turnaround time was acceptably fast. Most of the TAs were responsive and were able to answer questions about the assignments.

The B is for failing to proofread the final exam properly (there were three typos which affected the final, and we had extra time added because of that).
comment_workload:
Workload is moderate.

The assignments did not take long to do - 3 to 4 hours at most. They are not difficult, and some of the question types may appear in the exam. You are advised to take the assignment solutions into the venue.

The PAs gradually decreased in difficulty throughout the semester. The first PA (a simple shell) is the hardest if you haven't figured out how to chain the dup()/dup2() syscalls properly. The other two (a Multi-level feedback queue and a malloc() implementation) were very easy, and probably even easier than a normal COMP 2011/2012(H) PA.

The workload to revise for this course is low given that everything is open. Make sure to do the past papers to get a grasp of what will be asked in the exams, since the question types are quite similar each year.

(If you are going to take this course in the coming year, maybe go grab this year's "past exams" now, since they publish the previous semester's exam as "past exams". Grabbing it now gives you an additional set of materials to prepare with.)
comment_grading:
Grading might be better than previous semesters, but the tight grade boundaries remain - making mistakes in your assignments and PAs (worth 50%) can and will drop your grade rapidly.

Grading Scheme:
20% Assignments (5% per assignment, 4 throughout the semester)
30% PAs (10% per PA, 3 throughout the semester)
20% Midterm
30% Final

The assignments and PAs were not very difficult, nor were there annoying corner cases like in COMP 2012(H)'s PAs. The mean for the sum of both components were 47.07/50, meaning that you cannot make any careless mistakes.

Both exams were open electronic notes, meaning that you can bring your laptop in without printing all the teaching materials. They were also non-cumulative, so you can forget about everything before the midterm in the final. Although this helped with doing the paper tremendously, this led to a very high mean (74.39/78.39 before appeals for final/midterm respectively). This caused the subgrade intervals to be much smaller than other courses.

Some (anonymous) statistics:
94.7 (A+)
92.9 (A-)
~92 (A-)
88.711 (B+)
86.4 (B)

Overall course mean: 85.467

This means that grades are distributed such that there are only ~2 marks difference per subgrade. You can judge for yourself whether the grade distribution has improved or not, since it does seem that mean is at least B- now.

Feel free to comment if you have additional information about grade cutoffs, or your own score/grade.

comment_content:
There are mainly 2 parts of content. The first half is about processes and threads, and the second half is about memory and storage.

I found the first half to be practically useful, since multiprocessing and multithreading are very important when writing performant codes, particularly for AIs and games. It will come in handy when you study machine learning courses later, particularly for hyper-parameter tuning.

The second half talks about how the OS internally organize memory and storage. It may be useful to learn the terminologies, but the algorithms taught in this part don't really have much practical use, at least for me.

comment_grading:
4 assignments (20%, 5% each): some MCQs where you can copy the answers off the lecture slides, and then calculation questions.
Averages are high for all other than the 3rd homework where most people struggled with the synchronization problems (including me).

3 Programming assignments (30%, 10% each): Implement a simple version of concepts mentioned in class (pipes, scheduling and memory allocation for this semester), all 10 test cases are given, and to be honest they are quite easy.
Averages are very high, with a majority of students getting full marks.

Midterm (20%): Open book, open notes, 20 MCQs, and then calculation questions. The MCQs just require you to search through the slides (you are allowed to search within the PDF). For the calculation problems going over the past papers and the homework questions helps a LOT, you would know what and how they are going to ask since there's not much to ask. MUST NEED TO BE CAREFUL! Simple addition mistakes can lose you a lot of easy marks.
Averages are pretty high (67/100)

Final (35%): Same as midterm, 20 MCQs, and then calculation problems, only covers the latter half of the semester. Again going over the past paper and homework means you would know what is going to be asked. MUST NEED TO BE CAREFUL! Simple addition mistakes can lose you a lot of easy marks, particularly on the final since page replacement and disk scheduling questions are very easy to be careless on.
Averages are also high (69/100)

Overall it's more important for you to be careful rather than knowing the material well, so I don't think the assessment is very good, with 10~% over mean I got an A-.

As described, the course teaches concepts and approaches to OS design, such as:
- Kernel structures
- Multiprogramming / concurrent execution of programs
- Multithreading and concurrent data access problems
- Data synchronization and deadlocks
- Memory allocation and virtual memory
- Secondary storage mechanisms  
- File system designs
- Resource access control

Some parts of this course feels like an extension of some Secondary School ICT topics that were quickly skimmed through (e.g. Purpose of OS, HDD mechanisms), but most of these are probably some supposedly "common sense" for COMP-related students.

A bit more focus are put on these (because they are easier to test? lol):
- Process Scheduling Policies
- Memory Swapping Policies
- (HDD) Disk Reading Policies
- Fork and Pipes
- Data synchronization
- Deadlock detection algorithm / Banker's algorithm
comment_teaching:
Charles tries his best to make this course as appealing as possible. Like mentioned by the other review, he made an anonymous "complain board" (sic) for ways to improve the teaching, and he also tried to make students (at least f2f ones) actively revise by setting up bonus questions (which started as giving bonus course points but was scrapped due to complaints of unfairness for non-f2f students).

I don't know how this course previously went, but judging by the slides (which Charles couldn't really do anything with) there's probably some improvement (e.g. skimming through the more "trivia" parts like how actual OSes implement some of the concepts). It is still a bit hard to revise properly because of that though.

comment_workload:
Workload is medium

1. Homework
4 homework, not very hard. Usually there are both computation / algorithm question and essay question. The latter one is troublesome because you don't know what they want. However, essay questions appears less frequently in exams.

Moreover, many questions are just copied from internet and you can copy them as well for securing your marks (After reading GRADING part you should know these marks are important for your grade)

2. Project assignment
aka, programming assignment. You will use C (not C++) so try to get familiar with the syntax. All test cases will be given beforehand so you can debug more easily as there won't be crazy hidden cases to screw you up like COMP2011 and COMP2012

There are two PA. Usually the first one will be something related to API and system call, which is very different from PA in COMP2011 or COMP2012 so it will be harder.

But the second one, for this semester it is Banker's algorithm. Once you know how the algorithm works and the coding part is just same level as COMP2011 (some array manipulation)...  

3. Midterm & Final exam
Since it is open-book, print every lecture notes out. Also, there are chapter summary (which I suspected that it is copied from textbook) available but they are not that useful, because the WHOLE PAGE ARE WORDS. You won't want to reference it during exam. Try to make more concise summary by yourself. Although this will cost you some time, when you finish making your own "cheat sheet", you should have finished going through all lecture notes. It will be more efficient than just simply reading.

Key thing for exam is : CAREFULNESS. After reading GRADING part you should know -- Careless mistakes can kill you very easily.
comment_grading:
1. Homework (20%)
2. Project assignment (30%)
3. Midterm (20%)
4. Final exam (30%)

Assignment costs 50% so don't be lazy and think exams can carry you.

Never trust people who give A to this part or said it is good grade. It is just an illusion of survivorship bias.

People who give A to GRADING and said good grade is just because they get an A range in the course. If you look at their score, mostly are overall score 95% or above and get A+. That is equivalent to "Your mother is female", which is useless and nonsense

The truth is that the grading is ultimately crazy. Once your score falls below 90%, your grade drops very quickly, The interval of a subgrade is only like 1% to 2% (Reference from Goop, 89% B+, 87% B, 85% B-, 83% C+). Moreover, mean score is C+, a clear evidence of broken grading

When you feel satisfied for making an overall score 80% and gets above the mean score, sorry you still get a C+ only. Grading is a complete disaster. The return is likely not proportional to effort so don't put that much effort on this course

I got overall 85% and only got B-. I thought I finally make a good shot in COMP course but eventually the shxt grading just make me losing hope on any COMP courses.

comment_teaching:
Judging from no reviews about Charles in this course, I think this is the first time he teaches this course. I must say that despite the course having rather boring contents, Charles still tried his best to make changes to the course.
Firstly, Charles tried not to read everything from the slides and call it a day. He once used programming demonstration to explain the concepts of process, which I think is an advancement, judging from how lecture notes is probably direct copy from textbook (I will put my theory on this later).
Also, he makes coffee questions every lecture in order to make the lecture less boring, and, encourages students to self-study after lecture. He also set up a complain board for us to point out the problem of the course, and try to tackle them down.

For the slides, it is a total disaster. Wordy, and the main points are all well-hidden inside the slides. To add insult to injury, when I search up for references to questions in homework, I found the slides of the OS courses in other universities, and they have exactly the same slide contents. Same examples, same images, and even some with the same theme (with dinosaurs at the corners). This is how I come to the theory that this course's slides are probably direct copy from textbook, judging from the textbook recommended to us also have a dinosaur as cover.
comment_workload:
The workload in this course is actually light. I downshifted the grade to D here because of the depression the exams gave me (This is personal, I know).

Homework is basically quite easy if you ask. As most MCQs and the LQs can simply Google and find the answer. For the calculations and worked questions, just double check and you will be safe.

PAs is also another easy work to handle. Since all test cases for the PAs are released and no hidden cases, just follow the lab slides and it can be done easily. Remember don't hard code as you need to explain how it works. The only challenge is your own sanity against segmentation faults without knowing why.

For the exams, they are open book and non-accumulative. For the lecture materials, only chapter summary is enough. Another thing that is recommended to bring in would be the compilation of LQs from homework and past papers. For midterm, lab materials (Especially process and pipe ones) are also recommended.

The midterm. Sorry let me put this personally. Who the F*CK come up with the idea on expressing a timeline in a TABLE????? Why you have to be WEIRD and not following the past papers and just use a number line to express real-time scheduling?????? Have you ever studied data visualization before??????? (Actually me neither, but I was just being salty so yeah) I lost 8 marks because I can't determine whether the column t = 1 means the instant right before t = 1 or the instant right after t = 1. I know I was being like an idiot in that, but oh well. I pretty much got a mixed feelings after the midterm marks was released. (The question is put at attachment, if you are curious)
OK, now to be serious. Since the exams are open-book this time, the questions this time are more programming based. As I am not familiar with the lab materials, another 8 marks was down the drain. As the exams of this course in future semesters are probably also open-book, so remember to study, and better bring the lab materials for the midterm.

The final is basically similar to midterm, just a race of carefulness. Again, this time it also involves more programming. Since the final exam is non-accumulative and post-midterm labs are just lecture reviews, there are basically nothing special needed from the labs.

comment_workload:
TLDR: Average workload, each HW could take you 3~5 hours, PA1 is difficult by PA2 can be completed within half an hour.

📃 HW
Each homework has around 15 MC questions and some LQ. Some questions can be answered by copying and pasting lecture note content, while some are more conceptual. Each HW should take you 3~5 hours so it's not too bad.

📃 Project (PA)
PA1 is quite complicated as it involves POSIX APIs like fork(), but you can complete it within 3 hours. PA2 involves implementing a simple algorithm (Banker's algorithm). It's even easier than the PAs in COMP2111 and this should take you 30 minutes.

📃 Studying
You need extra time revising the lecture notes since it is basically a clusterfuck of concepts, jargons and "fun" facts. You may want to spend extra time on fork() and semaphores since the exam questions can get hard.

comment_content:

Process and Thread
Memory and Management
Security and Protection (new topic this semester, spanned just 2 lectures though)

C and UNIX platform programming were chosen as the tools for the projects this semester, which in my opinion is much better than using those educational OSes like in previous offerings.
Project 1: pipes for processes (in command line)
Project 2: malloc/free simplified
I think these are more useful as a starter to OS programming and design.

Despite a compulsory course that might appear boring at first sight, 3511 is pretty important in letting us understand how computer systems work and demystifying what we took for granted for high-level programming. Clearing up these concepts are essential in learning about OS design, systems programming, security, networking - and the performance issues in system design. So I think it's a good experience overall.

The reference book Three Easy Pieces seems to be a nice resource to learn more if you had the time.

comment_content:
Content is pretty easy and lays good foundations

It covers everything exactly as shown on the profs website, in order at:
https://www.cse.ust.hk/faculty/cding/COMP3631/

For those with weak math background, I recommend extra study on Modular arithmetic, and to understand the math basis of RSA and El-Gamal well from the slides and YouTube etc.

ONLY AROUND 20-25 SLIDES PER LECTURE :0 so it is very easy to catch up. Since there is so few slides, they can be dense at times with a lot of notation that you should wrap your head around as early as possible so you can follow during class optimally.

I say its "outdated" because your grade is 56% homework... which everyone AI's and gets nearly full marks. I assume this course has been running long before AI made differentiating students on homework obsolete. IMO it should be changed if they want to force students to struggle with problems.

(If its like this next year you would be a fool to not check your answers with AI on the homeworks)

No midterm, 4 homeworks, open book final with laptop and wifi off.
comment_teaching:

comment_grading:
Grading is "absolute" so no curve allegedly. But they still cant give everyone an A+, but really the lowest you will get if you try is a B range.   

56% 4 homeworks, 4% Attendance, 40% Final

Since everyone gets near perfect on the homework, the final is all you really have to snatch up that 25% A range.

The final is OPEN BOOK AND LAPTOP NO WIFI OR AI. So just organise it well and you will be fine.

COMPLAINT: They did not enforce the no wifi or AI rule at all!!!  there where no checks and in the 3 hour exam they barely looked at your screen. I suspect people cheated which is unfair and selfish  when we are competing for As.

I ended up with a B+ with 100/100 in all 4 homeworks and 71/100 Final. Which I'm very happy with since my final wasn't the best. Not sure about attendance but I think I got all 4%

i agree that the assignments are quite important with this grading scheme. Can't lose out too much on the assignment as they have a higher weighting than the final exam.

I got a B+ from getting 73 in the final exam with full attendance but 97% assignments only. I personally think i scored pretty low because it was completely open book and Ding's marking are quite generous. So i think you can definitely do better than what i did.

comment_content:
Contents are a brief overview of the field of Cybersecurity, focusing on breadth more than depth - each topic was not really taught in-depth, but the coverage was quite wide.

Topics:
Security Mindset
Cryptography:
    - Symmetric Key Cryptography (Stream and Block Ciphers such as AES, DES, Feistel Ciphers, Data Integrity with Checksums)
    - Public Key Cryptography (RSA, Diffe-Hellman Key Exchange, Signing with RSA)
    - Hashing (Hash functions such as MD5, SHA, Birthday Problem)
    - Reverse Engineering (Linear and Recursive Disassembly, Type Recovery, Mitigations for Reverse Engineering)
Malware (Worms, Viruses, Trojans, Backdoors, Ransomware, Malware Detection, Encrypted and Metamorphic Malware)
Software Cybersecurity:
    - Buffer Overflow (Heartbleed, Stack, Heap, and Stack Frames, Stack Smashing, Unsafe C functions, Stack Protection Mechanisms)
    - Obfuscation (Layout, Data, and Control Flow Obfuscation, Opaque Predicates, Effectiveness of Obfuscation Techniques)
    - Fuzz Testing (Mutation-based and Generation-based Fuzzing, Black/Gray Box Fuzzing, Code Coverage)
    - Miscellaneous Attacks (Memory Leaks, Use-After-Free, Double-Free, Format String, SQL Injection and Defenses)
    - Taint Analysis and Information Flow
    - Side Channel Attacks (Timing- and Cache-Based Side Channels, Flush-And-Reload, Power Analysis)
Network Security:
    - Authentication (Passwords, Dictionary Attacks, Salts, Biometrics and Biometric Efficiency, 2FA)
    - Network Security (Network Topology, Protocols such as HTTP, SMTP, OSI Model, DNS, TCP, ARP and Attacks)
    - Authorization (Types of Firewalls, Firewalk, Signature- and Anomaly-Based Intrusion Detection Systems)
    - Protocols (Replay and Reflection Attacks, Challenge-Response, Nonces and Timestamps, Session Keys, Perfect Forward Secrecy, SSH and SSL)
Blockchain (Distributed Ledger, Proof-of-Work, Mining, Double-Spending)
Machine Learning Security (Poisoning Training Dataset, Adversarial Examples, Membership Inference, Fuzzing ML Systems)
Smart Contracts (Non-Determinism, Reentrancy, Ordering Dependence, Ownership Transfer, and Function Delegation Attacks)

There were lots of topics, but most of them were only a few slides deep. If you're interested, the Prof links a few papers per lecture which might be interesting for you to look deeper.

The most mathematical part would be the part on cryptography - some of COMP 2711's modular arithmetic makes a reappearance, and you should familiarize yourself with the math behind the probability of hash collisions. The rest of the content is quite memorization-heavy, but you're (mostly) expected to know the principles, and to know how to apply them to unfamiliar principles.

Note: The examples, assignments, and exams all use C to some degree. Familiarity with the language is a plus, and you may need to compile some C in the assignments and debug/ point out vulnerabilities in a given C program in the exams.
comment_teaching:
Prof Wang was enthusiastic, and had a slight accent which did not impede understanding too much. He delivered the lectures with a drawing pad to illustrate his ideas, and uploaded these additional notes after the lecture. I tend to lose focus a bit too easily though - so I mostly self-studied using the PPTs and some Googling. He was willing to answer questions after the lecture, and was quite flexible with course logistics (he allowed me to take the final without much fuss even though I dropped my student card earlier in the day. Thanks!)

There were two TAs who were in charge of the first half and the second half each. The first TA was a bit stricter in marking our homework (but released the solutions much more promptly), while the second TA was much more lenient. Both were friendly and were willing to answer our questions in the paper checking session.

While the teaching quality is not exemplary, it's far from bad. B for doing their job acceptably.
comment_workload:
The workload for this course is pretty low in general. You need to show up in order to get the questions for the in-class quizzes, finish the assignments (which do take some time), and review by memorizing a bunch of the concepts presented. Not too work-intensive, and won't be a burden on your time.

There's an optional hacking practice released by the Prof which I did not attempt.

You might want to read some of the additional reading materials - a paper on side-channel attacks on machine learning models were the basis of one of the final questions.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme:
5% In-Class Quizzes (Graded mainly on effort, 5 over the semester)
40% Assignments (4 Assignments, 10% Each)
25% Midterm Exam
30% Final Exam

Assignments
======================
The lecture notes were not sufficient for completing the homework sets. You definitely need to Google quite a bit in order to learn more if you want to complete the homework (and think of the implications of the concepts presented in the lecture). Assignment 2 had a "programming part" which tasks you with using buffer overflow to "attack" a problematic program written in C, which was quite fun.

I suspect the 2nd TA didn't grade our homework too closely - there was barely any differentiation in the 3rd and 4th set.
Assignment Statistics (Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max):
HW 1: 19/89.5/90.51/97/100/100/100
HW 2: 122/185.5/182.34/190/190/190/190
HW 3: 30/59/58.16/59/59/59/59
HW 4: 39/41/40.96/41/41/41/41

Exams
==========================
Exams were a mix of T/F, MCQs, and Free Response questions. The marking scheme wasn't very precise, and reasonable answers were accepted. The questions were a 50/50 mix between simple recitation of concepts and applications of those concepts to new scenarios. The questions are quite akin to "blowing water" in a sense - if you are not good at elaborating on abstract concepts with concrete examples, you *will* be in trouble.

Exam statistics (Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max):
Midterm: 28/51.25/56.02/57.5/62.75/71/78
Final: 40/76/86.02/90/99/108/108

Since the assignments account for 40% of the grade, and are pretty bad at differentiating our performance, the exams are the main differentiator. This also means that the averages are high (~85% as per my calculations), which might mean that grade boundaries are tight. It sometimes boils down to "how to write the answers so the TAs accept them" more than anything else - so keep that in mind when doing the exams.

B+ for ~1 mark below UQ. Acceptable, though not exactly great grades.

comment_content:
Wang Tao focuses on "Principles" of cybersecurity, while Wang Shuai's content is more biased to "Practice" and "Examples". The theoretical part of the course is cut to a large proportion, and replaced with content related to his own research — Cybersecurity in Software Engineering.

If you would like to learn more about software safety, security analysis, and possible leak holes in different applications, this course with Shuai is an eye-opener for you.

A brief outline of the content
1. Introduction (same principles)
2. Symmetric Keys Cryptography
3. Public-private Key Cryptography and Hashes
4. Reverse Engineering
5. Malware and Attacks
6. Software Obfuscation
7. Fuzzing, Taint Analysis and Side-Channel Attacks
8. Biometric Passwords
9. Network Security
10. Blockchain and Smart Contracts
11. IoT and Machine Learning Security

Upon the topics, I think (6), (7) and (11) are unique topics of Shuai.
comment_teaching:
Not bad, if you are fine with his accent. His lecture is slightly boring but informative. He is eager to know any feedback on his teaching and content as he does not have much teaching experience. And he responds to emails very quickly.

In the lecture, he explained things pretty well and usually discussed a lot using the whiteboard. Content of elaboration written on the whiteboard might be tested and not necessarily mentioned in the notes. So attending the class is important not to mention the in-class quizzes.

General speaking, Shuai is not bad, though some may suggest Tao teaches better.
comment_workload:
Fair.

4 Assignments: each ~8 long questions and can be finished within 3 hours. For some questions you are expected to google for answer/hint. Shuai likes to ask whether some techniques solve/improve certain issues very much. Some questions in the assignments can reappear in midterm/final.

In-class exercise (attendance): just to answer 2-3 simple questions. They can be found easily in the notes.

Optional Project: Some web penetration to the OWASP project. It requires a lot of self-learning and effort for trial-and-error. It provides an opportunity for beginners to keep in touch with tools in the cybersecurity field like elasticsearch (logging tools), Burp Suite (web hacking), hashcat (password crack), etc. For the solution, Google it and you'll find everything.
comment_grading:
Don't underestimate the importance of assignments and participation. They matter a lot in this course, especially when there are only 3 in-class in my semester (edited: you will get 7 + number of completed quiz for your mark.)
10% - Participation and in-class quizzes
40% - Assignments
20% - Midterm exam
30% - Final exam
4%   - Optional Project

Shuai tried to distribute good grades but got rejected by the department. In the end, he strictly follows the distribution suggested by the university.
~15% A
~35% B
~40% C
If you get mean or slightly above mean, you can expect a C+/B-.

comment_content:
This is mostly a conceptual course about cybersecurity. Lots of interesting theories in cybersecurity are introduced. There are five main parts in the course:

1. Security basics: CIA principle, threat models
2. Cryptography: Stream and block ciphers, RSA, hashing
3. Software security: Buffer overflow, obfuscation, malware, security testing
4. System & web security: Authentication, access control, side channel attacks, firewalls
5. Security on emerging platforms: Blockchain, smart contract, IoT, machine learning

Have you ever imagine you can infer the password by recording the sound while encrypting? How to fool a machine learning AI to label a dog as 'cat'? How does a hacker steal all the cryptocurrency from badly designed code? Cybersecurity is always an interesting topic in computer science. For the level of this course, it only gives a general introduction but not heavy maths nor programming. It should be suitable and fun for most CS students.

The only hands-on experience for hacking is the optional project. We are given a vulnerable website to conduct different types of attacks. For example, bypassing the login, stealing the passwords, or even hang up the site. Although the way to hack is not covered in class, it is fun to explore around and learn by yourself. You will be rofl when you have found a stupid vulnerability in the site.

P.S. <b>Be ethical. Don't attempt to hack others without permission.</b>
comment_teaching:
The prof. seems to be a nice and passionate guy. I haven't talked with him in person. But he sometimes asks us questions and tells some funny things in class. He also left a "well done, thanks for your effort." comment on my exam submission. Maybe I did well in questions that are his research topic. (My classmate and I found his research paper when searching for info in final lol) I only sent him email once and he did not respond. Maybe he missed it by mistake.  

He teaches okay. He is not a "PowerPoint" reading guy. He usually will put some time explaining the slides. But it is dubious whether everyone could understand the explanations as they are quite messy.  And his tone is ..., I think I slept quite a few times in his lecture. :C

Martin, the course TA, is a weird but funny guy. He likes joking about how lazy he is. "The worse TA in UST" he claimed. He don't know how to teach and always give out the answers "accidentally". He gave us some midterm tips but they are not that useful......  Anyway, I admire his courage to speak "______, ______!" when he was testing the mic. Hope he won't be silenced or get fired.
comment_workload:
The workload of the course is moderate.

The assignments are quite long. You usually cannot find the answer directly in the slides. You have to recall the things talked in class and understand the content in order to answer. Alternatively, you can ask Martin to get the answer directly......

As the prof. is new, the course format has revamped and there isn't any past paper. Most likely, you won't have materials for revision. I think reviewing the materials once should be fine since there isn't anything more you can do. Just write the answers and hope for the best.

There are in-class quizzes. The dates of the quizzes are not announced. The questions are easy (~2 questions, answers can be found in slides) as those are simply for attendance checking. Make sure to attend the lectures or ask your friend to submit it for you.

The optional project took me two days to complete. You have to do self-learning for the project. With hints from Google and some trial and error, it should be feasible to complete the project. It takes some time to get the attacks right. It is fun to craft up the attack tho, and you will feel a sense of accomplishment when you successfully attacked the target. I AM A MASTER HACKER HAHA! (just kidding)
comment_grading:
The grading curve seems to be similar or better than a standard course. However, being one of the top students is not easy that it requires some luck and effort.

The midterm and exam is quite difficult. Since there is not any paper checking session (and answers are not given), you would not be able to argue your marks. IMO the TA seems to be quite strict with the marking, you won't be able to get score for alternative answers. As this is a cybersecurity course, the questions are mainly case-studies which multiple answers may arise.

There is an optional project that can pull up your score. It seems only a few people have attempted it. It is difficult for first time hacker (like me). But still, there should be no problem completing it with some googling (for hints). The weighting is quite high (completing it equals 15 points in midterm!) so it should secure you from getting a bad grade. My scores in midterm and final are under the top (max score) student quite a bit but I still got an A.

comment_content:
The course is divided into 3 main sections:

1. Advanced pwn learning
------------------------------------
In the first few weeks, the pwn coach Chengfeng teaches the basics of heap pwn and reviews some hard heap pwn CTF problems. Content is definitely interesting for pwn players, unfortunately I am not one of them so I cannot provide more details.

2. CTF participation
------------------------------------
After learning heap pwn, we will be given a long (>1 month) break to participate in some designated CTF events. This year, each of us has to get a flag in 2 out of 9 CTFs to get full participation score, and 7 out of 9 CTFs to get full bonus score. However, most of us did not care about the grading that much and participated in all of them (8 out of 9 since the last one is delayed all the way to August). Be aware that some CTFs may overlap with your midterm and final exam schedule.

3. Presentation
------------------------------------
After most of the designated CTFs have ended, each student has to do a 60-to-90-minute presentation on a specific topic of their own choice. There is a huge list of topic provided, but you are allowed to do your own topic after getting approval from Alex. Students also have to choose one of the designated timeslot for their presentation on a first-come-first-served basis.
comment_teaching:
Same as COMP2633, Alex is not the one who is teaching.

In the advanced pwn learning section, the pwn coach Chengfeng will be the one teaching. Chengfeng is a very strong CTF player who specializes in pwn, so he knows a lot of stuff. He has a little accent, but it does not stop him from teaching in a clear way, much much better than a lot of the PGTAs out there at least.

In the presentation section, the students will be the ones teaching, and all of us did a good job. It may be heartbreaking to see your fellow friends struggling to communicate in English or having problems with time management (and maybe with stove flame in their kitchen), but you can always lend a helping hand. It does not add onto your scores whatsoever, but you can become a good person and everyone will like you :blobcatmeltthumb:
comment_workload: There is virtually no workload except one 60-to-90-minute presentation, which you have more than 1 whole month to prepare. A lot of us started doing the presentation after midterm, which is more than enough to finish a very high quality powerpoint and design some CTF challenges for demonstration. If you are not as dumb as me who literally started doing my presentation powerpoint 1 day before the submission deadline, had to work overnight non-stop and still only be able to submit an unfinished product 2 minutes before the deadline, you will be completely fine. I find the difficult part to actually be compressing the length of your extremely content-rich presentation to under 90 minutes, instead of adding filler content to extend it to over 60 minutes.

comment_content:
The review is for MAIE5209 Design and Analysis of Algotirhms which is not listed in ustspace so I write it here. If you learned algorithm course in mainland China in undergraduate, you will find it easy to get through. Even if you have not learned algorithms before, you can always believe in Kenneth's teaching, the use of toys, vivid examples and videos will guarantee you a happy journey of learning.
Differently designed from simliar courses in mainland, the course structure is better optimized discarding some unnecessary and unrelated things. I have long believed that algorithm classes should analyse about algorithm itself instead of in-class and homeworks with obscure and tedious coding and some unrelated data structures. And Kenneth's class structure made it. Also it does not need textbooks. He said that the money used to purchase textbook can be better put in steam to buy a game. I like the idea he holds.  
Content:
Intro to Algorithms(time/space complexity), Inference, Induction, Recursion, Intro to Graph, Mergersort, Inversion, Subarray, Multiplication, Master Theorem, Randomize Algorithms, Quicksort, Heapsort, Linear Sort, Greedy, Stable marriage, Gale-Shapley, Huffman coding, DP, MST, Bellman-ford and Dijkstra.
comment_teaching: Prof.Leung Kenneth is really a good teacher and he listed the kind of questions and provided us great complementary materials to review before mid-term and final exam. In classes the algotirhms are intepreted to details which is not difficult to understand.
comment_workload: The workload is light and you almost need do nothing in common weeks.
comment_grading:
Better than some other courses, the grading creteria of algorithm classes are fair and equal. But it does not mean getting scores are hard. In contrast, most of the mid-term and final questions are basic.
Homework(23.625/25) : 2 homeworks each consisting of 5 questions. No need to coding, using derivation by handwriting can get the points.
In-class Exercises(4.375/5): 8 in-class questions related to slides.
Mid-term(29.1/30): (1-sided A4 cheating sheets permitted) Mainly covered complextiy, Induction and Recursion. The hardest 2 questions are about a. finding a circle in a given graph(stroke) in-deg/out-deg of each nodes b. divide-and-conquer question analysis in a 1-D array
Final(38.8/40): (2-sided A4 cheating sheets permitted) Added mainly about master theorem, 1-D DP and Greedy. Hardest question: A Graph problem(combine BFS and DFS)
I got A+ in this course. As we know ust has a tight graduate line of 2.875 and it's a really joyful thing to receive an A+ in this semester.

comment_content:
mathematical analysis of algorithms (summations and recurrences), advanced data structures (balanced search trees), algorithm design techniques (divide-and-conquer, dynamic programming, and greedy algorithms), graph algorithms (breadth-first and depth-first search, minimum spanning trees, shortest paths)

comment_content:
Syllabus:
1. Introduction to Algorithm Analysis
- Big O, Big Omega, Big Theta
- Selection Sort, Insertion Sort, Wild-Guess Sort
- Best-Case, Worst-Case, Average-Case Analysis
- Complexity comparison
- Pseudocode
2. Divide and Conquer
- Recurrence (Expansion and recursion tree)
- Binary Search
- Merge sort
- Inversion problems
- Contiguous subarray problems
- Multiplication problems
- Master Theorem
3. Randomized Algorithms
- Probability and expectation
- Quick sort
- Expected case analysis
- Randomized Selection
4. Priority Queues
- Heap (Insert, Extract-Min, Increase-Key/Decrease-Key)
- Heapsort
5. Non-comparison Sort
- Decision tree
- Counting sort
- Radix sort
6. Greedy Algorithm
- Interval Scheduling
- Fractional Knapsack
- Interval Partitioning
- Huffman Coding
- Stable matching
7. Dynamic Programming
- Top Down and Bottom Up DP
a. 1D DP
- Rod Cutting Problem
- Max Sum with Non-adjacent Elements Problem
- Change-making problem
- Weighted Interval Scheduling
b. 2D DP
- 0/1 Knapsack Problem
- Longest Common Subsequence Problem (Both contiguous or non-contiguous)
c. DP over Intervals
- Longest Palindromic Substring
- Matrix chain multiplication problem
- Optimal Binary Search Tree
- Nussinov Algorithm (RNA max base pairs folding algorithm)
8. Graphs
- Fundamentals of Graphs
a. Visit Graphs
- Breadth First Search (BFS)
- Strongly Connected Components
- Depth First Search (DFS)
- Cycle Detection
- Topological Sort
b. Spanning Tree
- Minimum Spanning Tree
- Prim's Algorithm
- Kruskal's Algorithm
c. Shortest Path
- Bellman-Ford Algorithm
- Topological Sort Searching
- Dijkstra's Algorithm
- A* Search
- Floyd-Warshall Algorithm (Finding shortest path of all pairs)
d. Maximum Flow
- Flow and cut
- Residual Graph
- Augmented path
- Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm
- Max Flow Min Cut Theorem
- Applications

Overall, it is a very comprehensive course. It is understandable that you may not be able to catch up so many contents. I know the number of contents this course has is already quite appropriate (given that I have audited Amir's 3711H, which is a fking nightmare in terms of workload). There are so much more you can learn from algorithms but unfortunately cannot be fit into 3 months of contents.
comment_teaching:

comment_workload:
The workload for this course is very high.

There are 4 hw assignments in this course. Each Q can take you at least a day to do (or maybe I'm just stupid). Many of the questions can be found from website, but it is best if you understand it for appeal, just in case if your TA do not understand your solution. You can see from the distribution, the mean is fking high for each of them. Try your very best on all of them. There may also be a bit more workload from the appeal. Just see attached on how I explain during appeal.

Mid-term Exam is relatively easy to revise, because they move the greedy algorithm to Final instead. As a result, they would try to find stuff to test from limited topics. Try to remember the proof of correctness and complexity as best as possible.

For the same reason, final turn out to be pretty hard to revise. Greedy algorithm, DP and graphs, there are just so much you need to memorize. But this doesn't mean you can ignore the maximum flow part. So... try your best to prepare... I lose quite a lot of marks because I forgot what max flow min cut theorem is.

comment_content: Many classical and important algorithmic topics are covered in this course, including asymptotic notation and analysis, sorting, selection, divide-and-conquer, randomization, shortest path problem, greedy algorithm, dynamic programming, graphs, etc. You would learn a lot from the lecture notes and doing homework.
comment_teaching: Prof. Siu-Wing is very expert in topics involved in this course, and he teaches very carefully and fluently, but the speed may not be so friendly to new beginners without much backgrounds. But overall the quality of teaching is nice for you to learn much if you can capture most of the content during lecture.
comment_workload: The course involves 4 written assignments, midterm exam and final exam. The overall workload is appropriate given the fact that you need to master so much content taught by the instructor. For each assignment, you basically need to solve 4 to 5 problems with many potential subproblems, the problem types include designing new algorithms, analyzing complexity, proving correctness and stating the rationality of your idea and writing some pseudo code. No language-specific implementation and coding is needed. You are typically required to demonstrate your capability to solve and analyze algorithmic problems and designs for this course.
comment_grading: I am not certain about the grading policy and grading range of this course, because I am still studying it.

comment_content: The contents are an excellent starting point to start prepping for job interviews. It starts from the basics such as different sorting methods, heap, greedy, and gradually moves to more advanced topic such as dynamic programming, then it gets easier after that as it gets to graph theory.
comment_teaching: Cheng Siu wing's teaching is incredible. He dives deep into the details, but sometimes it gets hard to follow. I suggest that you read the lecture material before attending the lecture, otherwise your attention span will drive you away from the lecture :)
comment_workload: There are 4 homework and it takes some time to do. But most of them are from the materials taught in class, you just need to match the problem such that it can be solved using the known algos in class.
comment_grading: When I enrolled this course I thought I would get really bad grades(like C+), but after taking the midterm, I realized that the exams are not that far of from the lecture materials. Yes it is hard, but as long as you understand the slides, do the homework, you should be fine. I got (A+) in the end :)

comment_content: This course has a lot of content, a lot of which is unintuitive and difficult. I spent a long time trying to come to grips with many of the concepts covered in the course, but even after completing it much of the content escapes me. Questions much like the ones covered in this course appear in interview questions and leetcode puzzles though, so its well worth your time to try and understand everything. If you have the right type of tism it can also be quite interesting.
comment_teaching: Siu Wong's lectures were really good, but he does go quite fast despite the content being almost impossible to follow if you haven't seen anything like it before. I would suggest watching the lectures online and really taking your time with it.
comment_workload: If you don't know anything about algorithms going into this you will probably have to sink ungodly hours into learning things and doing assignments. Assignments in particular take a long long time to complete, even while working with others and looking for help online. Start early on them, and start easing yourself into them as soon as they drop. These are not things that can be done the night before. Doing well in this course has all to do with experience in solving algorithm questions, and the course page offers many practice questions to work on. It's difficult to gauge how long these should take but if you want to do well you should solve as many of these as you can manage.
comment_grading: Grading is tough and the exams are very difficult since you need to produce algorithms in a very short period of time.

comment_teaching:
Prof. Cheng (former asso. Dean of SENG and Head of CSE) is not only an expert in algorithm, he's also a great teacher. He explained each topic very clearly. Also since he always stimulate us to think (and solve those 'IQ questions') his class is not boring at all.

The lecture is a perfect balance of intuition and rigorousness, in the sense that you can easily get the main idea, while not losing too much rigorousness.

Most of the TAs are Phd students/candidates, I think they wrote the HW solutions, which are pretty clear and smart. They're also patient and willing to listen to your arguments.
comment_workload:
HWs were quite challenging to me, but it's ok, no challenge no fun. Now I'm very glad that I faced those challenges, didn't give up and learned a lot.

Here're some tips for HW which may be helpful:
1. easy to hard: HW are on the hard side, you may not want tackle them immediately when you just learned a new topic. Try practice on easier questions first to gain some experience and level up.

2. lecture: review lecture/slides/tutorial, there're maybe some surprises :)

3. research: You may be able to find solutions to similar problems or related theorems in journals, which may give you more ideas. This may be much more time consuming than you think, since you need to understand and analyze huge amount of information. I only recommend this a last resort because most of the time the solution is much simpler.

The exams were much easier, but you need to make sure you're very familiar with materials in lecture, slides and tutorials. There's a high chance similar questions (or their extensions) will appear.
comment_grading:
I'm likely ~10 marks below the high, still get same grade as him/her, very lenient !

Some mistakes I made in exam, hopefully it can help you:

1. run time comparison: looks simple, but can be very tricky e.g. they mix big Omega, big O together. I drew conclusion too fast, lost 8 marks.

2. initial call: built a function f(i,j), but didn't tell TA what's the initial call e.g. f(1,n), lost 2 marks

3. brief explanation: designed a greedy algorithm, but didn't fully explain the rationale behind, lost 3 marks

4. read question carefully: not just me, in final many classmates also miss read S'\subset (V \ S) as S' = V \ S. All of us gave the same (totally) wrong algorithm ...

Exam can be stressful, try your best to control yourself

comment_content:
Contents are the same as any standard algorithms course.
Big-O, Theta, Omega (and the Master Theorem)
Divide and Conquer (Binary Search, Mergesort)
Randomized Algorithms (Probability, Quicksort)
Heap (and Heapsort)
Linear-time Sorting (Counting and Radix Sort)
Greedy Algorithms (Stable Marriage, Huffman Encoding)
Dynamic Programming (1D, Over intervals, 2D)
Graphs (Basic Properties, BFS, DFS)
Minimum Spanning Tree (Prim's, Kruskal's)
Shortest Paths (Bellman-Ford, Dijkstra's, Floyd-Warshall)
Maximum Flow (Briefly in 1 lecture)

Overall, pretty applicable to many problems you'll see.
comment_teaching:
Despite having a 9am Friday session, Siu Wing was a decent lecturer who could keep my attention and is generally easy to follow. He does tend to ask questions to students to illustrate some of the more tricky points - interactive, but perhaps not the cup of tea for everyone.

One not-so-nice thing is that he tends to skim over chapters when he doesn't have time - so I got quite confused at the last chapter on maximum flow. Overall his lectures are well-paced though, with decent examples for you to revise to.
comment_workload:
The four assignments constituted the majority of the workload. Don't be fooled by the low number of questions - all of them require significant time to work out (especially the proofs). You are advised to at least read through the homework when it comes out and think about it in your spare time. Find groups to do your homework as well - some of the more difficult questions would definitely benefit from you bouncing ideas off each other.

From someone who's definitely not a math guy and got overwhelmed by the homework: it's okay if you find the homework difficult. The mean of the exams are way lower, and you can get your marks back there. Good luck!

comment_teaching:
Professor Cheng's teaching is indeed quite interesting. I think he really wants you to understand the ideas taught in class, and goes into great detail with lots of diagrams and examples. He also sometimes throws in a few jokes to keep the lecture interesting.

He will also sometimes ask the class questions and may randomly pick people, so be prepared to answer if you attend the lecture.

However, sometimes he rambles too much, so it can be hard to pay attention. It also means he sometimes has to rush to finish the remaining content (or just skip it).

The tutorials are not that useful, but it's good to review the tutorial slides before the exam.
comment_workload:
The majority of the workload will come from completing the four biweekly assignments.

Each assignment has 5 problems, usually with one easier problem (such as just running an algorithm or solving recurrences), 2-3 moderately hard problems, and 1-2 really hard problems. Depending on your ability, it may take anywhere from a few hours to a week to complete.

(Some questions in the assignments would be a better fit for MATH 1023 than COMP 3711...)

Certainly, I will say you should take this course with some friends or teammates. You are allowed to collaborate on assignments, so it will be good to bounce some ideas around. Some of the assignment questions are genuinely really hard.
comment_grading:
The grading is curved but quite reasonable.

30% Written Assignments x4
30% Midterm
40% Final
(No programming assignments)

The midterm and final are much harder than Golin's and Dimitris's, since almost all the questions involve designing an algorithm and proving it. Don't even bother doing past paper analysis; it won't help. However, in return, the letter grade is more lenient (I think the A-range is wider).

In the final exam, the marks are very sparsely spread out. However, maybe 20% of the total marks can be obtained by just reciting the lecture notes.

comment_teaching:
The teaching pace is quite slow as the professor is trying to explain a lot of details of the algorithm. Sometimes he will ask some questions that clarify the correctness of an algorithm but most of the class doesn't get what he's trying to ask which is kind of funny. (because he doesn't want to reveal too much in his conceptual questions)

He tried to make some jokes during lectures occasionally e.g., "two-child policy" in the correctness of huffman-encoding. He also repeatedly asked us to not think about algorithms during DP lecture in the course "Design and Analysis of Algorithms".

The PowerPoint of both lecture and tutorial are very-well designed like all COMP courses. The teaching of lecturer is in general good and the teaching of TA is not bad either.
comment_workload:
Given that I have some experience with algorithms before, the workload is average but you really have to attend lectures except if you already know the content very well.

I'm a year 1 student so I waive COMP2012/2012H to take this course. It's certainly ok for you to do so since the course has nothing to do with C++. What you need is a little bit of "mathematics sense" and a basic idea of what complexity means. I also don't think COMP2711/2711H is any helpful to this course so you may waive that as well if you are really interested in taking this algorithm course in year 1. COMP2711/2711H is more helpful for COMP5711/5712.

4 assignments throughout the semester. Some problem in assignment is quite difficult and may take you some time to figure out. On average, each assignment takes me about 5 hours to finish (most of the time is spent on formatting latex). I got almost full marks in asm1-3 except I always forgot to state the running time of some trivial algorithms. I also run binary merge tree wrong in asm4. That causes quite a lot of marks so you need to be careful and double-check your assignments since mean is usually around 80-90%.

Both mid-term and final exam is of moderate difficulty but time is quite limited. The TAs is lenient on marking though. You don't need extremely rigorous proof. I tried to give formal proofs in mid-term, ending up not having enough time to finish some easy problems.

comment_content: Standard algorithm stuff, following the textbook Introduction to Algorithm.  Time complexity, sorting, divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming and graph algorithm (the content of graph algorithm is largely simplified, and lots of proof have been omitted). All the contents are just primary stuff of algorithms, it's a good start if you like algorithms, but taking this course is far from even a shallow endeavor to actual algorithm research. You still need a lot of practice on the Leetcode problems if you want to enter big-name IT companies even though you earn a high score in this course.
comment_teaching: Cheng, Siu Wing teaches the first half (before dynamic programming), while Dimitris teaches the second half. Admittedly, Cheng, Siu Wing is indeed an excellent researcher in algorithms,  but his teaching is a nightmare. He speaks monolithically, trying to explain every algorithm, but with too much lengthy background introduction. It is very hard to get the core idea of algorithms if you just follow his lecture.  His lecture will only get you familiar with each algorithm and academic terms, but you really need to work harder by yourself after a lecture to gain a more insightful understanding. So my suggestion would be not even attending his lecture. Besides, the midterm problems set by him are more of a testing of reading and understanding of English than actually solving the algorithm problem: a single problem itself is more than 1 page long! I don't know why he did this to us, because as far as I know, only a few students taking this course are actually English native speakers. Dimitris teaches pretty well although he is not an expert in algorithm study,  it's worth attending his lecture. He will give you inspirational insight into algorithm design (especially for the dynamic programming part).  And the HW and final exam problems set by him are more reasonable, some of them come from Leetcode problems.
comment_workload:
Extremely high!
4 assignments in total. Each takes a few days to finish if you are new to algorithms. Being an undergraduate student in cse at ust is so tough, and the average GPA is too low!
comment_grading: The mean is about B-, and the median is about B. A+ is extremely difficult, but A- is Ok if you work hard. A suggestion would be to take courses offered by foreign professors in cse department if possible because they generally grade well. Hong Kong local professors are usually tough on grading and stick strictly to assignments, grading, and other policies.

comment_content:
Among all CS compulsory courses, this one(3711) and COMP2011+12/2012H, in my opinion, are proboably the most important courses. The content of COMP3711 basically never changes since it's about "classic algorithms", not the ML or DL algorithms that could be changing and developing everyday nowadays.

Main Topics:
~ The idea of divide and conquer
~ The idea of greedy algorithm
~ Dynamic programming (1D/2D/interval)
~ Graph algorithms (from BFS/DFS to shortest paths)
~ Max flow problems

From my perspective, "divide and conquer", "greedy" and "DP" are about getting the idea, or the approach of thinking, while Graph algorithms really seems like a game of memorization, well at least I wasn't smart enough to figure out any "generic" idea behind them.
comment_teaching:
Ever since entering ust and getting into CS major, I have been imagining taking this course from Prof Golin, because every rumor I heard about Golin is about how clear his teaching is. And I know he normally teaches this course. And also most rumors I heard about prof Papadias is how weird his accent is and how difficult it is to follow his lectures. So at first when I saw it was prof Papadias who was going to be the course instructor this semster, I was very disappointed and really wanted to drop the course.

But it turns out that Papadias's teaching is just great! Yes, he has a strong greek accent, but it's absolutely undestandable, it's even easier to follow compared to the Chinese accent of some professors I think. Aside from the accent, everything else is very clear. He explains each concept clearly and in an organized manner, he also give plenty of examples to help us understand. And despite this was an online semester, he fully utilized the zoom chat to make the lecture as interactive as possible. I haven't really taken a course from Golin, but if all rumors about how good Golin's teaching is are true, I think Papadias's lectures are every bit as good as Golin's lectures.

But there are two major difference between Papadias and golin's style. (See Grading and Workload section)
comment_workload:
"The workload of comp3711 is isane", this is what I heard for a thousand times before taking this course. However, the workload was ideed not small, but not that "insane".

This is probably another important difference between Golin and Papadias's style. Golin emphasizes a lot on proofs. He has a very standard and strict requirement on writing rigrous proofs for many kind of algorithms, which could often end up in pages. But Papadias has an more "algorithmetic focus". He doesn't focus that much on proofs, but instead on the intuition and design of algorithms. So Papadias's assignments are lengthy, but the difficult part is about coming up with the idea of the algorithm, not writing pages of long and rigorous proofs. So the workload is actually smaller than I imageined.
comment_grading:
Usually if Golin is the only instructor of this course or coteaching this course with other instructors, golin will be the course coordinator, that means he gets to decide grading scheme. And Golin's style is to give a so called "guarantee grading". (you may probably find the details about this in comp3711 review on Golin) And that "guarantee" is a "lower bound guarantee", so as long as your overall score is high enough, you can secure an A range. But when Papadias is the only insturctor of the course (and hence the course coordinator), he doesn't have this "guarantee" scheme. Instead, it's pure curved grading.

And it turns out that because the final exam is much easier this semester, the mean reached an astonishing 80 (which is probably never seen in comp3711 history I think), with an sd of 17. The mean of the final was even higher than many of the homework asignments.

But despite that, since Papadias already gave an A range of 25%,  which is normally the largest A range allowed according to ust policy (Bad Guy UST, why not allow for more A), I should say the grading of this course (at least this semester) is very good.

And I also heard that the mean is B, while Golin claimed his mean is usually C+/B-, which is "standard ust", quoted from Goin.

comment_content:
This course is mainly about algorithm design and analysis. You will learn about how to calculate the space-time complexities of different algorithms.
This course should be very important for those who want to apply for ibank internships or start your journey of LeetCode, as all the fundamentals are taught in this course. We do not have COMP3711H this year. Therefore it may be better if you study this course in Yr2 Spring if you really want to get a job in the Fall recruiting season.

1. Divide and Conquer, different sorting.
Introduction of different sorting algorithms. Learn how to use recursion to deal with problems. Some other sorting algorithm like quicksort, heapsort, linear sort are also taught.

2. Greedy
Be greedy and take the best one among all choices in each steps, but...how do you define "best"? Very hard to proof its correctness, usually simple in implementation.

3. Dynamic Programming
Very powerful tool that usually performed in optimization problems. Bottom-up approach of building up solutions compared to divide and conquer (top-down approach). The recurrence relationship will be key in this topic.

4. Graph algorithms
BFS, DFS, Minimal Spanning Trees (Prim, Kruskal), Shortest Path algorithm (Dijkstra), Max-Flow-Min-Cut theorem, Ford-Fulkerson Algorithm.
The Max-Flow-Min-Cut is the best thing I learnt in this course, an effective tool solving many other problems.

5. AVL Trees
Balanced BST. Relatively easy.

6. String Matching (Finite-automata)
How does the computer do searching when you ctrl-F?
comment_teaching:
Golin is quite good in teaching these things but he just explains something easy by a harder mathematical language.

Every lesson, he speaks relatively slow, but as the class progress, he get excited and speaks faster and faster which takes you more and more effort to understand what he is talking about.

He taught great before Graph Algorithms, but once he starts Graph Algorithms, he start messing a little bit, somehow he speaks too fast that makes a lot mistakes and has to speak again. He always said: "ignore what I said in the last minute." and starts again. Maybe you can go the other section at this topic.
comment_workload:
The workload depends on your mathematical background, compare this course to those strict, rigorous math courses (e.g. MATH2033), this course is relatively loose in grading our proofs. Usually you still get full marks in your assignments/exam if you leave a bit loophole in your proofs (even one time I know my proof is not complete, but the TA still give full marks).

The assignment 1 will be a big bomb for those who doesn't know how to write proofs, as somehow it requires a bit mathematical background to finish those upper bound/lower bound proofs (limit of sequences, epsilon-delta will help a lot).

As the course progress, the assignments are easier, it no longer need an a-ha moment, but simply something covered in class.

For the final exam, I am disappointed by its format, being a past-paper analysis course. There is one question from last year past paper directly, and mainly the proof questions comes from those already derived in lecture notes (Greedy proof of interval scheduling, Comparison-based sorting algorithms lower bound). What is the most disappointing is, there is only one question about graph, to draw BFS and DFS diagrams.

I study my exam by understanding those key insight of each problem and proofs and I write the complete proof by those key insights then since I don't believe the exam will be a reciting game. However, for those who cannot regenerate the proof by key insights, congratulations, you can spend days and days reciting all the proofs and past paper. They are lenient in giving us the past papers. I would say this is good in some sense as hard work can really get you a good grade, since the professor promise for a grade lower bound, it doesn't hurt anyone that much.

But I just don't believe that this is the correct way to examine students in a relatively hard course. Or maybe it will be a completely different story in COMP3711H but I don't have a choice this semester. I admit that in terms of hard work, I do not deserves an A+ compared to those who recite all materials.

Aim A range 嘅，notes 要全部理解到，可以唔睇notes 咁講返一次啲algo 做緊咩，點樣proof correctness 。DP 應該係final 入面可以考到最難嘅嘢。少少睇運氣，但可以靠操 pastpaper 練返嚟，來來去去都係嗰個format，做多幾條就大致get 到個思維。

剩低BCD range 取決於你勤唔勤力，notes 睇唔明就死背死操，功課都係抄㗎啦～ 。有啲成日問嘅proof, （e.g. greedy == optimal？，dijkstra，comparison-based sorting nlogn lower bound）睇唔明都最好背咗先，睇返pp來來去去都係考得嗰啲。Huffman Coding，Optimal BST 呢啲proof 個人覺得太難所以比較低機會喺final 到問，識trace algo 就ok。之後tracing 呢啲 “送分題” 就盡量執，DP唔識做都寫一大堆廢話落去，有3分同情分 (out of 15)。稍微簡單啲嘅algo題係D&C，唔擅長寫algo嘅可以喺呢到入手先，做pp做到熟咗先去試DP。

簡單講 (Assume HW 90%+)
A+：All full
A- or above：啱晒啲tracing 題目 + lecture有教過嘅proof + 2～3題 algo
B+：啱晒啲tracing 題目 + lecture有教過嘅proof + 0～1題 algo
B- or above：啱晒啲tracing 題目 + lecture有教過嘅proof
C- or above：啱晒啲tracing 題目（唔使太驚，識人final 4x 都C+）
D：可能抄晒啲assignment，final 亂寫啲嘢攞同情分就已經夠

無source就真係自求多福，我都幾肯定成個course入面唔到10%人係自己做asg，當中大部分都係nds / MPhil / PhD，唔撚信全部人都自己做asg 嘅話會有90% median。

comment_content:
Compulsory COMP course. Basic algorithm stuff, including
divide and conquer
greedy algorithm
dynamic programming
graphs...

3711 is almost a pure math course. It focus on proving the correctness rather than programing the code. All algorithms are described in pseudocode and there is no programming related content in this course, so if you are trying to pass a technical interview, you may need some extra exercise to write the algorithm in programming language.
comment_teaching:
Golin is good at teaching and he answers questions posted to Piazza very quickly (like in 20 min?).

Tutorial: I nearly skipped all. TA will explain some tutorial questions but i think you can read them on your own. There is a website where you can find all questions from tutorials and past exams along with explaining ppt slides.
comment_workload:
Terrible
The first 2 assignments are fine because the course just begins and the contents are still basic things like big-Oh notation and binary search.

However, hw3 is totally a nightmare. It consists of 9 DP problems and each one of the problem have some sub-problems and some sub-problems even have sub-sub-problems. It requires a good understanding on DP concepts and tricks. Do start it early otherwise you will be struggling with DDLs and midterms from other courses.

Graph questions arrive in hw4 & hw5. This time you will need to be familiar with the graph algorithms taught in class especially how they are proved and the correlated lemmas.

Piazza is a useful platform for this course. You may ask questions  or view questions asked by others. Professors will answer you very quickly and they will provide hints, which can save you lots of time in doing the homework. Besides, you may find the "prototypes" of some hw questions in tutorials.

Final lasts for 3 hours and has two parts. Part 1 contains easy questions and slightly modified questions from past paper. Part 2 will have some new questions. Answers should be hand-written on pieces of paper then scanned and uploaded.
