comment_content:
The content of the course is actually some practical programming skills and hacks, and we have to design and analyze the running time of the programs.

Contents include:
Comparison sorting algorithms (Insertion, Merge, Quick, etc.)
Divide and Conquer (Recursion)
Maximum Subarray Problem
Multiplication
Randomized Algorithms and Random Variables
Heaps
Linear time sorting algorithms (Radix, Count)
Greedy Algorithms (and Huffman Coding)
Dynamic Programming (1D, 2D, over intervals)
Graph Algorithms (BFS, DFS, etc.)
Minimum Spanning Trees (Prim, Kruskal)
Shortest path/Pathfinding (Dijkstra, Floyd Warshall)
Max Flow, Maximum Bipartite (Ford Fulkerson)
Stable Matching
AVL Trees
String Matching (Naive, Rabin Karp, String Match Automata)

Optional: Hashing
comment_teaching:
In my opinion, Golin must be one of the best tutors teaching this course.

Not only his answers to students' questions are detailed and quick, but the explanations to the concepts were also clear too.
comment_workload:
The workload of this course is an absolute nightmare. Although there are no group projects or presentations, the homework itself is enough to give the experience of hell.

There is 5 homework, with the 3rd one being double-sized, which is assumed to alleviate the workload of the course by replacing midterm with it. The homework has many questions asked for proof, which makes students found frustrating and took a long time to do.

For me, it cost me the whole easter holiday to finish HW3 and finalized HW5 one day before the deadline (And not to mention that the deadline of HW5 was extended for 4 more days before the release of the homework. This means, if the deadline wasn't extended, I am unable to finalize it into the best version).

During the period of HW3, some claimed that they "transcended to another dimension", had mental breakdowns, and even stated HW3 is "scarier than horror films". (I wasn't joking here, they really put these statements on Piazza!) You may imagine how insane these assignments can get.
At last, the teaching team came up with an agreement with the students to put up fewer algorithm design problems in the homework (like 50:50 ratio with problems requiring running algorithms).

You may think that since the homework is hard, and so does the final exam. However, the final exam is much easier than the homework. One reason is that it is a closed book exam, even though it is "more orientated to algorithm design questions".
Prof. Golin stated that since the questions on homework are too dense (i.e. takes too much time to think), they appear on homework but not exams (even for questions similar to the ones in homework).

Side note: For Non-CSE students who somehow wish to take this course (e.g. CPEG students who want to take it as an elective), please think thrice before actually taking it, especially please evaluate the expected workload first.
comment_grading:
The grading scheme in this semester is as follows:
55% Assignments
45% Final Exam
Notice that this course adapt the "guaranteed grade range" scheme. This means if you get a certain grade percentage, you will be guaranteed to get a range of grade:
>= 60% Guaranteed D-range
>= 70% Guaranteed C-range
>= 80% Guaranteed B-range
>= 90% Guaranteed A-range
The thresholds shown above will be adjusted at the end of the semester according to the actual performance of students (usually downwards).
The mean/median of the course is usually at B- to C+.

My grades are as follows:
Homeworks (total): 580/600 (mean: 482.4)
Final exam: 71/100 (mean: >63.9)
(At the time they finish grading, they announce that the mean is 63.9. The final mean should be slightly higher due to appeals and incorrect solution for Q1)

Final grade: B+ (85.1%)

comment_content:
Syllabus:
1. Divide and Conquer
2. Master Theorem
3. Randomized Algorithms
4. Many Sorting Algorithms, e.g. Quicksort, Heapsort, Linearsort etc.
5. Balanced Binary Search Tree
6. Greedy Algorithms
7. Huffman encoding
8. Dynamic Programming
9. Graph search Algorithms
10. Shortest Path Algorithms
11. Maximum Flow
12. Stable marriage

Content is difficult and complex, probably will make students who are not good in mathematics very tough.
comment_teaching:
Professor speak in a fluent English. He will do the algorithm analysis step by step to show students how the algorithms working by writing some notes on the slides during lecture.

If you are passionate in learning algorithms and you are smart then it will be easy to follow professor as his explanation in contents is detail.

However, the sound of professor makes me feel sleepy in every lessons. D:
comment_workload:
Extremely High.
There are 4 assignments in this semester. All of them are not the easy job. You may need to spend time to search the relevant materials to help you solve the questions in google.
Some questions can be directly searched so that you must give the relevant source and use your word to write it to prevent plagiarism if you use it.

Assignments can be done in collaboration, so finding your friend to think the question together is a good way to solve it. Remember to do acknowledgement.

Each assignments costs me at least few days to do it, especially the dynamic programming assignment. It is very tough and that TA cannot give you any help if you are stuck in that assignment. So the assignment difficulty also depends on the kindness of TA.

Final is quite tough. Make sure you know all algorithms taught in lectures and how to use it. Preparing earlier will be better as course content is quite a lot.

E for dynamic programming assignment. Excluding it, it will be a C.

comment_content:
1. The content is actually simplified
In particular, this course has greatly simplified the discussion on depth-first search and topological sorting, and didn't talk about the notion of NP completeness. But given the CSE course pathway design, this simplification is actually reasonable.

2. You better do self-learning before the start of the semester
Just because the content is simplified does not mean the content is simple! This is especially true when you are just an average student without experience in algorithm class (like me), since this course covers many algorithms in a rapid pace which, I think, an average beginner could hardly catch up. I would advise you to AT LEAST do the following before the semester starts:
- Get familiar with all divide-and-conquer algorithm and sorting algorithms (by get familiar, you should also understand all their properties and proofs)
- Grasp a basic idea on greedy algorithm and dynamic programming (e.g. the greedy choice property and the optimal substructure property)
- Get familiar with these graph algorithms: BFS, DFS, Topological sort, MST (Prim and Kruskal), SP (Dijkstra), APSP (Floyd-Warshall) (Again, by get familiar, you should also understand all their properties and proofs)
Although these topics comprise almost all the content of the whole course, I guarantee that you would have a much smoother and easier course experience by doing this. (I guarantee that my guarantee is better than Kenneth's guarantee!) The contents of this course are not easily absorbable for an average student.

3. Prove by contradiction and M.I. (and its variants) are very important to this course.

4. Better pay attention to these topics (and their variants) when preparing for the final exam (the following is essentially a summary of the kind of questions that has appeared in the exam set by professor Golin and Ma):

- Asymptotic notation and recurrence
- Properties of sorting algorithms (e.g. best-case, average-case, worst-case running time, and stability)
- Sorting lower bound (comparison tree model)
- Radix sort on integers of bounded range
- Indicator R.V.
- Divide-and-conquer algorithms (e.g. subset-sum, x-discounted MCS, and designing the variants of binary search)
- Greedy algorithms (e.g. interval scheduling, interval partitioning, subsequence, minimum stopping)
- ****Dynamic Programming**** This topic is VERY IMPORTANT, since questions of this topic are usually the killer (e.g. longest monotone sequence, shuffling, minimum cost itinerary, x-discounted MCS, doubled string)
- BFS, DFS and Topological sort (How to apply or modify them to fit in other situations)
- MST (the cut lemma, prove by contradiction and counter-example)
- Dijkstra algorithm (its correctness proof)
- Variants of Floyd-Warshall algorithm (e.g. all-pair max-bottleneck problem)
- Application of maximum flow
- Dry-run of these algorithms: min-heap, AVL tree, sorting algorithms, Huffman tree, BFS, DFS, MST (Prim and Kruskal), SP (Dijkstra), Max-flow and Bipartite matching, Pattern matching automaton
comment_teaching:
I don't think I need to repeat how good the teaching is.

However, I want to address one point that is not mentioned by any other reviews. I really like the marking scheme with the detailed marking notes that is really a great plus for this semester. comment_workload:
Assignment:
Need a lot of time to improve the writing of proofs even though some questions have source. (Some source actually does not provide proof of correctness, which means you might need to write up the proof by yourself!)

Also need a lot of time to do dry-run! What is the meaning of executing an algorithm by hand on a graph with 10 or something vertices, when the idea of the algorithm can be clearly captured with examples of a much smaller size? What is the meaning of executing dynamic programming algorithm by hand where you need to dry run the for loop for more than 100 times, and at the end, everyone just codes the problem? What is the meaning of constructing a huffman tree with more than 20 nodes? .....

Exam:
Better prepare early and spend enough time understanding all past paper problems. That is quite tough.

comment_content:
Unlike its course code, it's more like a MATH course actually. Throughout whole semester, the course is all about conceptual ideas and mathematical proofs for specific algorithms. Coding is not necessary but it may be useful when you're doing "worked" problems in assignments (you will see in WORKLOAD sections).
COMP2711 is the prerequisite of this course.

Syllabus:
0. Course Mechanics
1. Introduction (Simple Revision of COMP2711)
2. Divide & Conquer (DaC)
3.  The Maximum Subarray Problem (DaC Application)
4. Integer and Matrix Multiplication (DaC Application) + Master Theorem
5. Randomized Algorithm
6. Quicksort and Linear-Time Selection
7. Priority Queues, Heaps and Heapsort
8. Sorting in Linear Time (Counting Sort, Radix Sort)
9. Greedy Algorithms
10. Huffman Coding (Another Greedy Algorithm)
11. Dynamic Programming (1D)
12. Dynamic Programming (2D)
13. Dynamic Programming over Intervals + Matrix Multiplication Application
14. Introductions to Graph
15. Basic Graph Algorithms (BFS & DFS & Topological Sort)
16. Minimum Spanning Tree
17. Shortest Paths
18. Maximum Flow
19. Maximum Flow Application
20. Stable Marriage Algorithm
21. Binary Search Trees Operation + AVL Trees

(Extra Material: Introduction to Hashing)
comment_teaching:
Lectures:
Professor Golin seems to be a native English speaker. He articulates all the concepts in details while maintaining the overall teaching pace quite well.
Don't hesitate to ask question if you're confused. Professor Golin is always willing to answer your concerns in details. One of the best instructors in this virtual semester I met.

Tutorials:
Tutorials are conducted in Q&A mode this semester i.e. TA would not teach/explain the tutorial notes unless you ask them about those problems.
However, solutions would be provided and they are usually quite detailed, I think it's not difficult to self-study so I haven't attended the tutorials after the first one.
comment_workload:
My suggestion: DO NOT take this course if it's virtual semester. It's assignments are really time and brain cells killer...

There are relatively more "worked" problems in assignments this semester.
E.g. Given a graph with 10+ vertices. Find out minimum spanning tree / all-pairs shortest paths / max flow. We must show every substeps in details (the parents and distance form after adding a new vertices).
Using coding to finish assignments is a good approach. You can choose if you prefer to code or write it by hands (iPad recommended).
I have no idea if TA has done these problems by their own in advance. Doing such tedious process with so many times is kinda useless. I mean, the given data set could be smaller and we can still have a good practice on this concept.

Professor and TA may think that as we almost stay in our home all the time, we would have much more time to spend on our assignments.
Well, it's actually true. But workloads become disastrous when all professors think in that way.

To deal with these stupid tedious worked questions, I have almost no time for reviewing contents.
It's not about if you're a genius or not. It's just about time management and your patience.

comment_teaching:
Prof. Golin has good working ethics.
First, he knows how to teach. Generally, whenever he goes over an algorithm, he runs step-by-step example to give you intuition.
Second, he values feedback. Almost every lecture he stays behind and answer questions in details. And when I say answering questions, he really try understanding your hardship, for example, making wrong assumptions, lacking essential knowledge. Most of the time it isn't about the complexity of content but we, students, forgetting the essential assumptions.
Third, he answers email. I heard from him that quite some students simply take reference for their assignments, especially given this special semester where we have 7 assignments in total. So, I basically send him emails every time we have an assignment since I literally do each of them myself. Of course, they aren't dumb questions and I don't recommend you sending him dumb questions also. And he answered each of them timely and precisely. When I say timely, it is like within 2 hours even at night time like 2200. To be honest, this is admirable.
Last but not least, he encourages questions. So for example, since I was so busy and literally started revising 5 days before the exam and I got some questions on pastpapers, he literally told me to have a Zoom meeting for like half an hour for clearing all concepts.
To be brutally frank, this is an introductory course. So the content isn't that "exciting". But you can see his enormous effort in making sure that students truely understand a concept both intuitively and rigorously.
comment_workload:
Now, it really depends since to be honest, you can really copy all the references into your assignments and get it done. You can also text some of your friends that have taken this course previously. I mean, there are literally multiple ways for scoring high in assignments, which are the most important thing to establish your concepts.
At the end of the day, it is your motivation. I spent a lot of time in doing all assignments by myself as well as perfecting them.
Also, please note that I type it in Latex. So it takes extra time again.
Not to mention the working problems in assignments where I coded the solution. Man, you can really decide the workload.
Second, tutorials.
There are literally on average 8 weekly tutorials. Not going to lie but if you cannot grasp the concept right after the lecture, those tutorials will simply overwhelm you.
And they are important since assignments maybe related to them!!!
And so the last thing is final.
Well, you literally need to memorize all the stuff, for example, some proofs so as not to screw yourself when there is such a question.
And there are literally 16 pastpapers offered by the professor.

so as i said, this course can be tough if you take it seriously which I believe most don't.

comment_content:
Mainly on techniques of algorithmic design, correctness proofs and running time analysis. Content is very difficult and needs a lot of time to digest. But it's indeed one of the most important courses for COMP students.

There are several main parts.

1️⃣ Fundamentals
🔶 Asymptotic Analysis
🔶 Master Theorem
🔷 Selection Sort
🔶 Insertion Sort

2️⃣ Divide-and-Conquer
🔶 Binary Search
🔶 Mergesort
🔷 Towers of Hanoi
🔷 Counting Inversions
🔷 Maximum Subarray Problem
🔷 Integer and Matrix Multiplication

3️⃣ Randomized Algorithms
🔷 Hiring Problem
🔷 Generating Random Permutations
🔷 Birthday Paradox
🔷 Coupon Collectors Problem
🔶 Quicksort
🔶 Randomized Selection

4️⃣ Other Sorting
🔶 Heapsort
🔶 Counting Sort and Radix Sort

5️⃣ Greedy Algorithms
🔶 Interval Scheduling
🔶 Knapsack Problem
🔶 Interval Partitioning
🔶 Huffman Coding

6️⃣ Dynamic Programming
🔷 Fibonacci Numbers
🔶 Rod-Cutting Problem
🔶 Weighted Interval Scheduling
🔶 0/1 Knapsack Problem
🔶 Longest Common Subsequence
🔷 Longest Palindromic Substring
🔶 Optimal BST
🔷 RNA Secondary Structure
🔷 Matrix-Chain Multiplication

7️⃣ Graph Algorithms
🔶 BFS and DFS
🔶 Topological Sort
🔶 Minimum Spanning Trees
🔶 Shortest Paths
🔶 Maximum Flow
🔶 Stable Matching

8️⃣ AVL Trees

9️⃣ String Matching
🔷 Naïve String Matcher
🔶 Rabin-Karp Algorithm
🔶 Matching with Automatons

🔟 Hashing (not tested in exams)
🔷 Chaining
🔷 Open Addressing
🔷 Universal Hashing

Pay attention to topics marked with 🔶.

Overall a very fast-paced course with lots of content packed in just one single semester. Dynamic programming and graph algorithms are the most difficult parts of all. Proofs (of anything) are extremely annoying, and may cost you a lot of points if not done properly. Make sure you spend time memorizing the main flow of the proofs.
comment_teaching:
Prof Ma's teaching is okay. Good preparation, clear explanation, though her pace is pretty fast. You need regular revision in order to catch up with her pace.

Tutorials are usually a recap of what is taught in the lectures with additional examples.
comment_workload:
The workload mainly lies in the 4 written assignments. The assignments are very challenging and usually take 1 or 2 days to finish.

Exams are closed book and there are a lot of materials to revise, so it takes much time for preparation. Make sure you do enough practice cuz some of the exam questions are actually directly copied from the past papers and tutorial questions with minor modification.

comment_teaching:
Prof Papadias is a very good lecturer and teaches every concept and every example very clearly. He makes sure everyone understands his logic before moving on. He sometimes explains uses examples from outside the slides so make sure to attend his lectures. He has a strong Greek accent but is very easy to understand.

Prof Qu unfortunately has subpar English and mumbles his way through each lecture, merely reading off the slides (unlike Prof Papadias who solves every example on the whiteboard with the class). So if you have a choice between the two, I strongly suggest taking Prof Papadias' class.

TAs' quality differs significantly as well. Some of them are very unprepared for the tutorial sessions, so go attend a tutorial session to see if the TA is good at explaining. Otherwise don't waste your time by going there.

comment_content:
Content covered is very suitable for an introduction to algorithm design - relatively basic but interesting, including big-O notation, sorting algorithms, algorithm paradigms (divide and conquer, dynamic programming) and graph theory.

It takes some time and effort to properly understand all the concepts, but once you do, it is very easy to follow and intuitive.
comment_teaching:
Teaching is adequate - sometimes difficult to understand, but the instructors and TA's are more than willing to explain in more detail or repeat their explanations.

Just remember to speak up, otherwise the first explanation is sometimes half-hearted.

The lecture notes are mostly self-explanatory and covers all the materials, but a practice is important, because the lectures only teach concepts, but the exam question test the application of those concepts.

Skipping lectures is okay, but it is advised to read through the lecture materials first, because some concepts can be harder to understand.
comment_workload: The workload is very acceptable. The 4 homework assignments come after a major section of topics has been thoroughly taught in lectures and tutorials. The later homework assignments make good exam practice.

However, the mean of the final is higher than the midterm, so if you want to get good grade in this course and you are not genius, work harder in the midterm because a lot of midterm questions are DIRECTLY copied from past papers and tutorial solutions provided in the course website.

I get marks above mean in the midterm but lower mean in the final exam, passing the assignments. I expected to get C range but finally a B range grade! Thus, don't get scared by the difficulty of this course at first!

comment_content:
Other comments have gone into great lengths on the syllabus of this year's COMP3711H, so I will only give a brief summary here, with some personal comments.

Before midterm:
- Overview on runtime analysis
- Basic data structures (heap, binary search tree, AVL trees)
- Graph algorithms (DFS, BFS, Dijskstra's, Bellman-Ford)
- Greedy algorithms (knapsack problem, scheduling)
- Dynamic programming
- Misc. (Partial sums, segment trees)

After midterm:
- Amirtized analysis (union of disjoint sets)
- String algorithms (tries, suffix trees, Ukkoken's, KMP)
- ...and some 3721 knowledge (DFAs and NFAs, regular languages, Turing completeness, NP-hardness and completeness)

...and these are just the main concepts. Many micellaneous algorithms were introduced along the way, especially in tutorials (btw, sidenote, if you are going to take Amir's 3711H, don't skip tutorials, they are very useful). Do not try your luck if you aren't prepared to spend at least 10 hours per week on this course, or you will die a horrible death. You have been warned.

Yet, if you are geninely curious on algorithms and ready to dedicate the time and effort, this course is suitable for beginners, despite the fast pace of the course.

comment_content:
1. Overview: Roughly speaking the course gives a great coverage (roughly 90%) of the book CLRS, aka the most famous undergraduate-level algorithm textbook. Other good references are

- Prof. Golin's past 3711H notes, most of which are helpful for content before midterm. I guess there are still very few topics that the past 3711H covered (but not in this year) like fast Fourier transform and stable marriage problem. Speaking of Golin, I guess he is also keeping track of this course (even though he has left HKUST) since his name appears in the codeforce participation list.
- Prof. Dekai's past 271 notes. Although Dekai is notorious, still I realized his note is worth taking a look.
- Past 3711H exams are not quite helpful in overall since the style of questions are quite different. In particular, since Amir is a competitive programmer, the questions are designed in the respective way.

As a math and non-cs student, the most important takeaways for me are

i. Learn C++ - I guess this is one of the most significant distinction between this year's 3711H and its previous offering (also regular 3711), namely with providing C++ implementation of algorithms. As someone who didn't even take 2011 and knows nothing about C++, I find this course a good starting point to learn such a programming language.
ii. Recognize the difficulty of solving "discrete problems" - In fact many problems and settings for modern mathematics are made to be neat and too good to be true, so as to avoid dirty and technical difficulties.
(In particular, combinatorics plays a key role in some situations when we really choose to face dirty stuff. One example in my mind is the importance of combinatorics in random matrix theory and free probability, which comes from taking out "commutativity" which is taken for granted in standard situations like real line)
In the world of algorithms, the core is to face these difficulties. I would say my mathematical background doesn't quite help me a lot to understand the complicated course content (comparing with non-math students).

Speaking of prerequisites, I find most of us mentioned that 2711/2711H doesn't help a lot for the algorithm course. I would say I find discrete math really get involved in the following places:

i. Runtime analysis, especially the proof of Master's theorem, amortized analysis and so on
ii. Mathematical concepts and facts regarding graphs and trees
iii. Understanding the computation theory part, especially the 3-SAT problem
iv. A "mathematically-prepared mindset" for understanding the course content, especially in the dynamic programming part or do proof of correctedness using induction and so on

2. Course Content. I tend to divide the course in the following parts (not in teaching order):

===I. Runtime Analysis===

TL;DR: First foundation of algorithm analysis. The analysis of runtime can reflect the "efficiency" of algorithm.

- Basic definition of runtime: In this course the concept of space complexity is not as emphasized as runtime, although in some exam questions the space complexity is taken into consideration.
- Master theorem: Give a general formula to calculate runtime from a given form of recurrence relation
- Amortized analysis: In some case considering worst-case runtime is too "extreme" or "pessimestic", and this can reflect the "more realistic" runtime in some situations.

===II. Pure Array-sorting Algorithm===

TL;DR: Various classical sorting algorithm for arrays are introduced. This section is quickly gone through since graph algorithms are more important and efficient to implement, and graph algos are the main characters in this course. Without further conditions, it can be shown that the best possible runtime for array sorting must be O(n log n), so such kind of problem is somewhat "not interesting".

- Selection sort (Find the smallest number and switch to 1st position, then second smallest and switch to 2nd position and so on)
- Bubble sort ("Bubble up" the largest number to rightmost position, and so on)
- Mergesort (Divide the array into singletons, assemble the adjacent singletons while sorting)
- Quicksort (Find pivot, divide the array based on the pivot, do the same thing to the subarrays)
- Radix sort (Sort the array by looking at the last digit, second last,..., first digit of each number in the array)
- Divide and conquer (Generalization of idea of Mergesort; Should be treated as a methodology like DP)

===III. Basic Graph Algorithms===

TL;DR: Graph algos are more efficient, and many data structures are implemented using the idea of trees.

- Definition/facts of graphs, introduced in guest lecture
- Binary tree, BST, AVL tree
- Splay tree (Implemented in homework)
- DFS and BFS (Two fundamental graph-search algos; Super important!!!)
- Topological sort (Find underlying order in DAG)
- Dijkstra algorithm (Find shortest path)
- Tarjan algorithm (Detect strongly connected components)
- Minimum spanning tree and Kruskal algorithm (for finding MST)

===IV. Dynamic Programming===

TL;DR: As mentioned, DP is not a specific type of algo, but a general methodology like divide-and-conquer.  DP is so important that it can be applied in many situations and greatly reduce time complexity, but usually the correct scenario is usually difficult to be figured out.

- The general approach is to divide a problem into subproblems, and write down a recurrence relation and solve it mathematically. Of course speaking of recurrence relation, initial condition (base case) is essential.
- An essential tool for implementation is "memoization" which aims to reduce the number of unnecessary calls of recurrence function.
- Explicit examples: Prefix sums, Set covering, LCA...

===V. String Algorithms and Trees===

TL;DR: Algorithms for problems like string matching/common substring with trees.

- Segment trees (Label nodes with intervals)
- Tries/Prefix tree
- Suffix tree and Ukkonen's algorithm (for building suffix tree)

===VI. Computation Theory===

TL;DR: Discussion of highly theoretic topics like computability of problems, Turing machine, hardness/completeness and so on. The famous debate of P != NP or P=NP comes from this part.

- Regular expression (Homework)
- Halting problem and computability
- DFA and NFA; For those who have learnt stochastic processes, Markov chain is a good example of automata.
- Turing machine and Turing completeness (Important example: C++)
- P and NP problems, NP hard and NP complete
- Discussion on some NP-complete problems like 0-1 knapsack

===VII. Others (that I don't know how to classify)===

- Greedy algorithm which uses the idea of "local optimality". For those who have learnt some machine learning, gradient descent is a good example. Again this is a methodology like DP and divide-and-conquer, but not an explicit algo. Usually greedy algorithm is applied in optimization problem, just like the purpose of gradient descent.
- Huffman coding (An application of greedy algo)
- Randomization of algo, which plays a minor role in this course
- Some computational geometry (Homework)
- XOR problem and centroid decomposition, which applied some divide-and-conquer idea
- Sparse table
- Maximum flow and maxflow-mincut theorem
- Combinatorial game theory
comment_teaching:
I would say Amir is a combination of Desmond in CSE, and Ivan Ip in MATH. While Amir is willing to give many extra coding lectures (to implement algorithms introduced in class) like Desmond, he is able to maintain a quick pace of teaching and striving for mathematical rigor like the style of Ivan Ip.

All TAs contribute a lot for content like guidance of codeforce questions, and  enrichment of mathematical background like graphs. I do admire the efforts being made in this teaching team.
comment_workload:
There are multiple sources of workload which makes it undoubtedly worth a C in this scope:

1. My unfamiliarity on C++, which gives difficulty for me to follow the implementation of algorithms in the early stage of the course, and also tackling codeforce questions
2. Tremendous amount of course content - There are 46 regular lectures (coming from 3 lectures per week), plus weekly tutorial. I would absolute agree that the workload is moderate if this course is worth 5 or even 6 credits, but being 4 credit is too insulting for this course.
3. Codeforce questions: Although answers are always available on the internet, it is worth thinking for a while for some questions.
4. Homework problems: In fact there are some conceptual backgrounds of most homework problems, like splay tree in homework 1, or sweeping line method in homework 2 and so on. Without realizing these backgrounds, thing might be significantly more difficult.
5. Preparation of exam: Actually not everything should be prepared to be super familiar eventually, thanks to the flexibility of the exam. Precisely, you can choose 2 out of 3 big problems to answer in the exam paper, in order to completely avoid something you are not familiar with (like NP-completeness or amortized analysis in final exam).
comment_grading:
The grading is structured as 40% homework, 30% midterm and 30% final.

The exam papers are so difficult that, if you can perfectly finish 2 out of 6 (sub)problems plus being partially corrected in other subproblems, then you are able to be above mean and be safe in A-range. This is for midterm and I believe that there will not be a significant change for such grade distribution in final.

One thing that makes me a little bit unsatisfactory is that, there is a make-up exam for those who cannot attend midterm, but according to the paper released, the makeup paper seemed not significantly harder than the original one which I think causes unfairness.

This is more a mathematical course than a COMP course ( at least when doing the assignments ), you are expected to write very strict and ( not too but still ) complicated proofs in assignments.

SO if you don't do well at MATH and especially hates writing proofs - well, take care :/  Getting start in writing a perfect proof and writing dozens of them hurts.

However, these contents are not hard to understand. BTW if you are not very into algorithms and proofs - take COMP3711 instead. The contents should be basically the same.

comment_content:
"The course is running BFS but not DFS."

This course covers lots and lots of algorithmic contents. From basic greedy algorithms, to advanced graph algorithms such as max flow. The content covered is mostly the same as the non-honor version, but with more proofs.

Due to the wide range of content, most of them are not discussed in depth. Even worse, for those the topics with depth, you don't have time to digest the topic before moving on to the next one. I think it would be better to have fewer topics with more examples and techniques.

Moreover, the course is supposed to put more focus on the tools of proofs, but not skimming through algorithms. The course just simply assume us to knowing how to write a mathy proof in advance. I believe the problem is the key steps of the proofs are not highlighted during lecture. So we will easily get stuck or end up with a proof that is not rigorous enough.

It would be much better if this course can be splitted into two separate course. One is for introduction to the algorithms and the other one is advanced algos/data structures with analysis. The latter one should include things like basic reductions or some simple complexity theories. This is an honor course and it supposes to introduce things that are more difficult.

Pre-requisites:
Logic - implication, inference rules, iff, ...;
Data structure - pointer, linked list, queue, ...;
Math - probability, expectation, indicator rv, ...;
Proof - induction, contradiction, ...

Here are some key topics.
(Before midterm exam)
1. Sorting algorithms <- diff math and data structure
2. Decision tree <- BST, AVL in comp2012
3. Divide-and-conquer algorithm <- recursive def and sol to problems
(Rmk: D&C’s application includes FFT algorithm, which is sth related to complex number a+bi, and the root-of-unity concept.)
4. Greedy algorithm <- to prove a “take-all-we-can-now” algorithm is the best (or optimal) algorithm (may not always appear in midterm exam)
(Rmk: Pf here reqs careful induction/contradiction.)

(After midterm exam)
5. Dynamic programming <- to use memory or storage to eliminate recursive computations
6. Graph algorithms <- including BFS, DFS, ...
6.1 Minimal spanning tree <- Dijkstra, Prim
6.2 All-pair-shortest-path <- Folyd-Warshall
6.3 Max-flow-min-cut <- Ford-Fulkenson
7. Extra topics like hashing, not appearing in any hws or exams.

comment_content:
The subject matter of this course is undoubtedly important, covering major areas of theoretical computer science such as automata, formal languages, computability, and complexity theory. The issue is not so much what is being taught, but rather how the material is presented.

One major concern is the format of the learning materials. Instead of providing well-structured lecture slides or clearly organized teaching notes, the course depends heavily on text-based PDFs and annotated handwritten materials. For a course built around abstract definitions, formal reasoning, and long proof-based arguments, this format raises the barrier to understanding. In practice, students often need to spend significant time decoding the presentation of the material before they can even begin learning the concepts themselves.

In addition, the course assumes a high level of comfort with mathematical abstraction and formal proof techniques. For students outside the Theoretical Computer Science track, moving from basic logical foundations to reductions, decidability arguments, and complexity classifications is far from straightforward. As a result, although the content is academically valuable, the overall learning experience can be quite difficult for a large portion of the class.
comment_teaching:
In terms of teaching, the course seems to place more emphasis on in-person participation than on making the material easier to learn. Some of the supplementary explanations, annotations, or classroom clarifications do not appear to be made available in a complete and systematic way. This puts students who cannot rely entirely on physical attendance at a disadvantage. The issue is especially serious for postgraduate students, many of whom are also managing research, TA duties, or project work alongside coursework.

Another problem is that the course delivery is heavily text-driven, with limited use of structured explanations or more intuitive teaching aids. Theory courses are already demanding by nature, and without a clear framework, visual organization, or step-by-step examples, students can easily get lost in technical details. As a result, a great deal of time is spent trying to interpret the material rather than actually learning it.

The teaching experience would likely improve a lot if the course were designed with greater attention to accessibility for students from different backgrounds, for example through clearer slides, better-organized content, and more guided explanations of difficult ideas.
comment_workload:
The workload of this course feels disproportionately heavy relative to its credit value and practical return, especially for postgraduate students. The burden comes not only from assignments, but also from the amount of time required just to process and understand the course materials. In other words, the difficulty lies not simply in how much work there is, but in how costly it is to even get to the point where meaningful learning can begin.

Although the homework may look manageable on paper, the abstract presentation of the course and the lack of sufficiently clear supporting materials make even straightforward tasks far more time-consuming than they should be. For students who are also expected to maintain research productivity, this course can take up a substantial amount of time that would otherwise go to reading papers, running experiments, or advancing thesis work.

What makes this even more frustrating is that the high time investment does not necessarily translate into better outcomes. Because the material is highly theoretical and the assessments are demanding, students may still struggle to achieve the required grade even after putting in significant effort. For students outside the theory area, this creates a combination of high stress and relatively low academic return.
comment_grading:
The assessment structure of this course feels highly demanding, with very little room for error. This is particularly stressful for postgraduate students who must obtain at least a B grade in order to satisfy program requirements. The exams, especially the midterm, appear to be very challenging, and the overall student performance suggests that the level of support provided during the course may not be well aligned with the rigor of the assessments.

This concern is even more important because, for some postgraduate students, the course is not just another elective or background requirement. It plays a meaningful role in their academic progression, including preparation for later stages such as PhD candidacy. Under these circumstances, maintaining a strict grading standard without corresponding instructional support can create an unfairly high-risk situation.

I am also aware of several postgraduate students who were unable to achieve the required grade. This suggests that the problem may not simply be a matter of individual effort, but rather a broader mismatch between the course design, the assessment expectations, and the academic backgrounds of the students taking it. For a course with such an important gatekeeping function, a more balanced grading approach and stronger academic support would be highly desirable.

comment_content:
While the curriculum covers foundational topics, including Finite State Automata, Context-Free Grammars, Turing Machines, and Complexity Classes (P, NP, PSPACE), the delivery way is significantly outdated.

- Lack of Accessible Materials: The instructor does not provide standard lecture slides. Instead, students are expected to learn from PDF text sheets and handwritten annotated notes. This format is inefficient for learning and makes it unnecessarily difficult to parse abstract theoretical concepts.

- Abstract Complexity: The content requires a high degree of math familiarity. For those not specializing in Theoretical Computer Science, the jump from basic logic to complex problem reductions and decidability proofs is steep and time-consuming.
comment_teaching: The teaching approach appears designed to force physical attendance rather than facilitate understanding. The decision to withhold certain notes or annotations adds an extra layer of difficulty for PG students who may have research or TA commitments. The reliance on a "text-heavy" approach rather than visual or modular slides makes the learning curve much flatter and more frustrating than it needs to be.
comment_workload:
The workload is excessive, particularly for PG students. Between deciphering the text-only notes and completing the "straightforward but non-trivial" homework, this course consumes a disproportionate amount of time that could be spent on research.

In addition, the abstract nature of the problems means that "putting in the hours" doesn't always guarantee a passing grade, leading to high stress and low ROI for non-theory students.
comment_grading:

I am personally aware of several PG classmates who failed to achieve the required grade. For a course that serves as a prerequisite for PhD candidacy, the lack of a grading scheme that acknowledges the different standing of PG students is a major concern.

comment_content:
This course gave me a new perspective to think and argue mathematically about computation and decision problems. Overall the content is very interesting and stimulating.

This first part of the course introduces several mathematical models (DFA, CFL, TM) to describe computation and investigates the computation power of each such model e.g. given a string w does there exist a DFA that can tell (yes or no) whether the length |w| is a prime number ? (indeed this question is asked in the mid term)

The important techniques here to show a model "cannot answer" a decision problem are the pumping lemmas (for DFA, CFL) and diagonalization principle (for TM).

The second part talks about computational complexity. There're lots of open questions here e.g. Is there a TM that given a graph, can tell (yes or no) if there's a clique of size k in polynomial time ?

As you can see due to many unknown answers, the best we can do is to compare and group problems together. We put
each decision problem into classes like P (solvable by TM in polynomial time), NP (solvable by NTM in polynomial time) and LSPACE, NLSPACE, PSPACE (space complexities) based on what we already know.

With the technique of reduction for each of those classes we have a set of "hardest problems" in the class e.g. for NP it's NPC. This way if someone can show a problem in NPC is in P, we'll immediately know all problems in NP is in P (a huge result !)

Aside from the HWs that don't really weigh that much, the grading mostly depends on presentations on the lectures taught. But the grading seems to be at mean for like 80% of the students anyway, which means almost everyone gets an A-, probably a handful of B+, A, and A+.

Upon request the TA did give some response on how the presentations are graded, which besides whether "one covers everything taught in the lecture" (remark: The videos are like ~12 minutes long covering 7-8 lectures), it is graded based on:
- whether you "show knowledge of the content taught"
- whether you "can provide examples and explain them"

Doing the "coverage" part gives you mean, and doing the extra points allegedly gives you higher than mean. I did exactly that and I got lower than mean.

The TA also provided a high-mark student example (while stating that "most highest mark students don't want to show their work"), but the presentation literally reiterated the lecture notes (and recycled the examples, verbatim) with some colored slides, which the professor states as "good organization".

And from a discussion with the professor, he seems to really like this grading format and will not change it anytime soon.

comment_grading:
Random grading under a strange scheme.

4 Homework Assignments: 4% x 4 = 16%
Part 1 video presentation:  30%
Part 2 video presentation:  24%
Part 3 video presentation:  20%
Class participation:  10%

This is a CS theory course, so I do not think this makes any sense. What kind of CS theory course counts attendance, and uses presentations as its major evaluation scheme. And, seriously, there are no exams, in this course?

The main point of the presentations is compressing everything he has taught in 15/12/10 minutes, and you are graded probably by how well the ppts are designed, whether you look good, or simply the mood of the grader. You don't need to think, just compress everything.

There is a similarity check for each presentation and homework, and harsh penalties apply. BUT, it never works! On the one hand, you may get high similarity scores for original works, due to the nature of the contents in the course, and, the assignments are never changed over the years. They rerun Turnitin after the DDL so that the similarity scores go up again, and that's why I messed up HW1. On the other hand, those who are really good at plagiarism have tricks to circumvent the similarity checks. They may "borrow" the presentation materials from those in the past offerings with good grades, and use their tricks to get high scores and low similarities at the same time.

The attendance policy is also crazy. You are regarded as present iff you log in to the zoom. This means that if you attend in person with focus, but somehow the zoom got logged off, you don't get the attendance mark. If you "pretend" to attend by logging in your zoom in your bed, you get the attendance mark.

Believe me, many guys who got good grades do not understand most part of the course. They just reverse engineer according to the ridiculous grading scheme. I would be kicking their asses had there been an exam.

We had three homeworks of  medium difficulty, it took me one day to finish each of them. The homeworks allows students to directly apply the concepts learned in the course and understand them better.

Instead of a final, students are required to work in groups of 2 and make 3 video presentations to explain the contents of the course.
The course is divided into three parts:
Part 1 - lecture 1 to 13 : 15-minute video
Part 2 - lecture 14 to 20 : 12-minute video
Part 3 - lecture 21 to 23 : 10-minute video
This is challenging because the videos should cover all materials seen in the corresponding lecture and the video's target audience are new students about to study the material.

A similarity score is computed for each submission and it can sometimes be challenging to be under the threshold because your transcript for the videos essentially quotes the course contents.

I put a C Grade for the workload because while the difficulty is not that high:
- writing transcripts meeting the similarity score requirement
- creating the slides for the videos
- getting a record where you speak very quickly without stammering (you usually need several takes)
- editing the video
does take a good amount of time.

comment_content:
Main Outline of the Content:

Part 0: Preliminaries
Set theory, function, relation, language, countability

Part 1: Automata Theory
1.1 Regular Language (RL), Regular Expression, Finite Automaton, and their equivalence
1.2 Context-free Language (CFL), Context-free Grammar, Pushdown Automaton, and their equivalence
1.3 Closure Properties of RL and CFL
1.4 Pumping Lemma of RL and CFL

Part 2: Computability Theory
2.1 Turing Machine
2.2 Church-Turing Thesis
2.3 Recursive (Decidable) and Recursively Enumerable (Semi-decidable) languages
2.4 The Halting Problem
2.5 Reduction

Part 3: Complexity Theory
3.1 Decision Problem
3.2 Certificate and Verification
3.3 P and NP
3.4 Polynomial-time Reduction
3.5 NPC and NP-hard
3.6 Approximation Algorithm (extra)

Remark:
- Contents are neat, since only the basics are introduced
- Contents are well-structured
- The three major proof techniques in this course, namely diagonalization, pumping lemma and reduction, are all based on contradiction/contrapositive

Personal comments:
The course could become much more abstract when going to part 2. But I think this course is still much more manageable than 3711 because you won't be required to know a large bunch of algorithms or write a very tedious proof (especially induction proof...) or dry-run on some large example (e.g. graph with at least 10 vertices).

This course does not quite focus on practical aspect, e.g. scalability, language design, paradigm, pattern, reusability, etc. Even though there is a part talking about time complexity, the distinction between efficient and inefficient is whether the algorithm runs in polynomial time or exponential time, but not whether it is O(n) or O(n^3). In other words, O(n^1000) would be considered efficient in this course.

However, this course focuses on more primitive, or you can say, philosophical things. What is the mathematical definition of regular expression (the thing that you might use to search strings in some specific form)? What does it mean for a device to compute something? Is there anything that a computer cannot compute? What does it mean for a problem to be hard? What problems are the hardest among hard problems?

This course will give you a completely different mindset to think about computer and a better appreciation of abstract maths. Indeed, I found this course to be beautiful (or lovely?) when all the nontrivial things/concepts are developed just based on the basic naive set theory.
comment_teaching:
The professor is certainly not the best presenter, but his approach of teaching is simple and intuitive. Attending the lectures did clear up some concepts for me.

The teaching of TA is also quite good. He is quite attentive to students' comments to improve his teaching.

BTW
At the start and at the end of semester, the professor raises some interesting comments about the course. One of them goes like this: "This course is kind of useless to your research or anything unless you really want to know the concepts. For PG students, they have to take this course anyway, but I would advise UG students to take other courses." (He later added, "I do not mean UG students cannot handle this course. Just that there are more interesting things out there to explore.")

This is my first time hearing a professor to actually discourage students from taking a course (well, in other courses you might hear something like "you should think whether you want to proceed" instead of "go to take other courses instead"). My opinion is that, if you love abstract thinking, or if you hate programming, or even better, if both, then go for this course.
comment_workload:
Attendance:
You need to be present in Zoom, but if you like, you can afk. I do advise you to pay attention, though.
(The professor would give out some answers for homework 3 and 4 at the end of two of the lessons, with the recording switched off.)

4 Homework:
Intermediate difficulty. If you don't know how to do, go google or find source (or listen to the professor's hints/answers).

3 Presentation:
You need to design your slide, write a script, and then do a recording. It is okay to just read aloud your script (better in a natural way, of course).

The workload should be quite manageable.
comment_grading:
The grading scheme of this semester is rather different:

Attendance - 10 points (Mean 8.5/10)
Written homework x4 - 20 points (Mean ~90/100)
Part 1 presentation - 30 points (Mean ~85/100)
Part 2 presentation - 25 points (Mean ~89/100)
Part 3 presentation - 25 points (Mean ~89/100)
========================
Total - 110 points

The grading curve itself is quite nice. Full in attendance, almost full in homework, getting mean in all three presentations => A-

The course seems rather tempting, right? No exam, just present something, try to get a mean score, and then you have an A range.

But you should note that, a large proportion of students are PHD students who probably have more presentation experience (in thesis defense) than UG students, since this course is primarily for them. Also, judging from the Zoom meeting, many of them are NDS who are probably good at maths. So, don't treat the course too lightly.

The presentation scores are concentrated around the high 80s/90s. It is a bit competitive, but I think as long as you really comprehend the concept, generalize the details and give a big picture, and say something intelligible, then the TA should not be harsh in giving your grade. In other words, you don't need to have extraordinary presentation skills to obtain a high mark.

The professor seems to like this kind of grading scheme. So there is a chance that he retains this scheme even after the pandemic has gone.

He couldn't start our server from the project and he didn't give any meaningful feedbacks but also being aggressive on us saying "you are violating the rules of the project" since he cannot open it. We have tried to help but it didn't work. Again, shout out to Wentao for handling the problem.
comment_workload:
Overall, pretty flexible. If I didn't take this course, I probably will get a really really hard time for this semester. I had so much trouble for other courses.

For labs, you get a lot of time finishing each and one of them. Some of the labs deadline are in 2 weeks or even 3-4 weeks.

There is not much you need to do for studying the exams. You can practice on VSCode on this. I think that is not going to do much.

The project workload really is based on how you and your groupmate can do. And make sure to start it early. If you have all that, then it should be easy to get full marks or higher score than us.

My group mate and I were busy and I was in a situation where I have to finish 7 HWs and 1 lab in two weeks and I did not even start working on the project. The worst part is, I did most of the work by myself and I have to work on the project without sleep during that week and the time for the project was supposed to be pretty flexible! I did the pixel art, most of the coding. My groupmate was not very helpful either. He didn't know how to use github or how to add features properly in the project and I had to spend a lot of time to figure out what he did while figuring out how to do everything on my side. I HAVE TO DO WHAT HE WAS SUPPOSED TO WORK ON! The only thing he contribute the most is the video recording. I couldn't believe I was able to finish the project honestly. Also, Javascript made me spent so much time to debug since it will never tell you what is the issue. Not even console.log can save you! Never trust anyone who says "Javascript is an elegant language".
comment_grading:
This is not absolute graded. Most likely I will not get A or above but I think it is really not that hard to get a really good grade.

There are 6 labs with 4 hand in labs.
The first one is not required. And lab 5 and 6 are combined into one hand in lab.
The total weighs 7% + 7% + 7% + 9% = 30%

I think it is really easy to get full marks on this. I will explain this on the workload session.

Project grading is also 30%. Most got full marks. I got 265/300. If you care, you can look at the last paragraph of me yapping on why I didn't get full in workload session. We made a game of players trying to mine as much minerals as possible and whoever gets higher score wins. The score is based on the rarity of the minerals and the amount. If you are interested, let me know in the comments and I will post the files here.

Exam: 40%. I think it is easy to get 70+ marks since it is open book and most of them are similar to the labs. The only issue is that the exam question can be very long and sometimes confusing. But really easy to get very high score. I got 84/100.

I got a B+ in the end. Honestly, it is quite expected. Probably like 15-20 people have A range. others just B range or below.

Separated into 2 half.

First half is frontend. Things like HTML, CSS, JavaScript and jQuery is taught, and they talk about concepts like module pattern and promise. The beginning is pretty easy if you learn them in other course before. The part about promise may take some time to understand.

The scope is everything taught in the course. Open book exam. Calculator allowed.

Course Project (30%): Group project to create your own multiplayer game, with some specifications like a live view of the opponents gameplay, account system and results screen. Final (40%): The only part of this course that isn't good. The final is a 95 minute long, COMP1021 style exam, it is open note however, so just print out everything. comment_content:
Refer to the 2022 Spring Review for Gibson for the detailed breakdown of lecture materials.

One point that I dislike about the contents, apart from being quite out-dated, is that the topics are not inter-related from class to class. It sometimes jumps to JS, then jumps back to more on CSS, then more on JS, then HTML forms, then backend, then suddenly more on frontend... A bit hard to follow the logic. But if you can clear up your mind and understand everything, it should be fine with the ireegular order of topics.

Also, this course is perhaps one of the few decentralized course in HKUST. Different professors will introduce way different topics. Therefore, suggest you look into other reviews to comapre the detailed contents that each teaches if you think the topics will be one of your concern for enrollment and if you can waitlist into this course.
comment_teaching:
Firslty, showing my background, I have taken various "famous" professors in CSE, such as Dave Rossiter, Desmond, Kenneth, DY Leung, etc. This is the first time (and last, ofc.) taking Gibson's class. Maybe I expected too much, or maybe I thought his teaching would be identical to Rossiter's, anyhow, I don't feel his lecture enjoyable. I will say he is an average instructor.

The professor did have his heart to teach, and prepare pretty well for each class. However, I fell asleep in almost every lecture. Probably due to the fact that lecture time is TuTh 5-6pm, and in LT-K where the cusions are very nice. The professor tried to make jokes from time to time but I don't think them particularly funny.

Also, this is a bit personal, but I just don't like Gibson's intonation. His accent is fine, pronunciation is claer, but his way of presenting linkages between sentences make me hard to concentrate and to digest what every single word that he speaks.

After the add-drop, there are about only 10 students in LT-K where the enrollment size is in full capacity 80. This also shows that Gibson may not be as enjoyable as we expect.

Even though, he delivers the content and maintains the logistics well. One extra comment is that the TA graded the lab and project really, really slow. It took nearly two months to grade the first lab.
comment_workload:
Not bad. Labs have videos released where you can just follow all and write 2-3 lines to get full score.

The last lab may need to spent more time but is fine. The project is the most heavy part where you need to spend days and nights with your groupmates to work on it.

One key tip: Actually not many groups make wonderful games even Gibson showed many samples at the beginning. Follow all the grading criteria and secure most of the scores before making some cool stuff. Fyr, my game is just an extension of a lab because we did't have enough time to make it nice, yet it fulfills all grading criteria and just lose 10 out of 250 points on "gaming experience".
comment_grading:
Labs 3 x 10% (4 choose 3 best)
Project 30%
Final Exam 40%

The four labs are hammer game, jumping game, gem game and chatroom. Project is to develop a web game with real-time connection between two players. (Yes..... I don't know why the professor is so obsessed with games. Anyway, they are fine)

Labs are easy. Follow the lab videos and check with the grading scheme to score full.

Project is tricky, in the sense that you may think everyone is super good, doing cool games. Indeed, it's not the case. Follow the marking scheme and do every single element and then you will be already top 10%. This is not a game programming course at the end of the day.

Final exam: A comp1021 style of final exam, tricky but easy questions. Open book open notes closed internet and closed programming. However, I don't know what's the point of testing us syntax-related stuff and some for-loops or some functions in javascript. Or maybe there is no other things to test at all? Idk.

My grade:
Labs: Full 30% (means are higher than 95% for each lab, and considering choose 3 out of 4, mean for total lab should around full)
Project: 240 out of 250 (mean 230)
Final Exam: 93 (mean 68.4)

Letter grade: A+
Very good indeed. I was expecting an A only.

comment_content:
HTML, CSS, JavaScript, jQuery, SVG, XML, AJAX, PHP, Bootstrap, JSON, Cookies, Node.js, everything you can name related to web development. The content is very hands-on and practical, and I'm glad that I finally have a COMP course that teaches me how to code...

The course is beginner-friendly and no prerequisite knowledge in web programming is needed. Dave has amended the course topics to remove technologies that are obsolete and include those that are more recent and commonly used. It still does not cover the more advanced technologies such as React, AngularJS and Vue.js, but this is fair enough given the limited time for the course. If you wanna be a full stack developer you probably also need to learn Linux, Apache/Nginx, depending on which stack you would like to specialize in. But I guess you'd need to explore them by yourselves.
comment_teaching:
The COVID-19 pandemic has made us switch to online learning this semester. However, so far I think Dave has done a very good job in delivering course materials and helping us consolidate what we've learned. Dave made "high-efficiency" videos that are clear, concise and suitable for lazy guys like me. He also provided a Zoom session every week and asked a few basic questions to help us revise what we've just learned. Some questions involved tricky points we easily miss out and asking questions is indeed a very nice way to bring them out.

PS It was so funny that Dave forgot to escape the characters in his chatroom code example and somebody else in class easily did an XSS hacking during his demo LMAO. Then a message box kept hovering over the web page.
comment_workload:
The workload mainly lies on the lab assignments and the course project.

There were a total of 5 labs, but only lab 2 to lab 4 needed to be submitted. Since it's online instruction they've basically become assignments with strictly defined marking schemes.

🔷 Lab 1
A number guessing game introducing basic CSS, JavaScript and DOM handling.
🔶 Lab 2
A hammer game involving jQuery and the use of timer.
🔶 Lab 3
A obstacle jumping game involving SVG and animations.
🔶 Lab 4
A movie catalog allowing users to search and sort Marvel movies from an XML database. Mainly involved PHP, Bootstrap and XML handling.
🔷 Lab 5
A single-page application allowing users to view, filter, sort and add Pokémon. Mainly involved PHP, Bootstrap, jQuery, XML and AJAX.

🔷 Hand-in Lab
🔶 Self-Learning Lab

Most of the labs were easy and could be finished in a couple of hours. Mean score were crazily high though, so make sure don't lose any easy marks here.

Everything else is simple. but all evil lies in the course project! We can either do it alone or form a group of 2 - 3 people. The topic can either be an extension from the lab (probably can only score an okay-ish mark if we do that) or any other fancy topics we like. At the end we've gotta submit to link to the main page of our website and make a video to introduce what it does / make an appointment to demonstrate to Dave real-time. The difficulty actually depends on the selection of the topic and the group mates. It's still very challenging, however, since the project is indeed assessed in terms of complexity and most of the time the content taught in class is just not enough for a complex project. Specifically the server-side programming such as PHP and Node.js is only taught in brief and there is a lot we have to explore by ourselves if we wanna do a project that includes server-side programming.

But overall the workload is lighter than most of the other COMP courses.
comment_grading:
💻 30% - 3 Hand-in Labs (Each 10%)
✏️ 30% - Late Midterm
💻 40% - Project (8% Proposal, 32% Project Deliverable)

Extra 0.6% for every question you answer right in the Zoom review sessions, and he asked 48 questions in total. So a maximum of 28.8% bonus points could be added on top of your overall score. That's a hell lot of points!

The midterm was a little online Canvas quiz consisting of 15 multiple choice questions, randomly drawn from a super-set of question database. Everyone would get a slightly different subset of questions. It was conducted quite late towards the end of the Spring semester, in May, so it actually covered most of the lecture materials for the whole semester. Open-book and open Internet. But only Chrome was allowed and of course no messaging. The midterm was easy but tricky, with the mean landing on only 61.7/100. We've got some interesting questions though, like one asking us to go to a web page and check the expiry date of a cookie, and another one asking us to send a HEAD HTTP request and check the last modified date of an HTML file. This would only be possible when we have an online midterm like this. But on the other hand Dave also liked to ask "eye test" questions such as spotting a missing speech mark, which I personally think are silly since we now have powerful IDEs that can easily fix all these.

Mean = 61.7/100
SD = 17.5
Max = 92.4
Min = 14.2

As for the project, 8% of the total score is allocated to the project proposal. As soon as you've written something intelligible, you should be able to get it. The remaining 32% is for the actual project deliverable. You can either do great in a project with only client-side programming, or do okay-ish in both the client-side and server-side programming to get full score.

Mean = 26.6 / 32 (Does not include 0's)
SD = 8.4

I got 86.67/100 in the midterm, 22.8/28.8 for bonus Zoom questions, 28/32 for the project (got some bugs ☹️), and full marks in the lab assignments.

Final grade: Pass
So I dunno what I actually got. 🤷‍♂️

comment_content:
Previous code: COMP 4901U (https://ust.space/review/COMP4901U).

4 Major Parts: Lexing, Parsing, Type Checking and Lowering (Code Generation). All of them are fundamental to compilers.

Although the "official" prerequisites are COMP 3021 or 3031, I suggest taking the following before you take the course:

- COMP 2611. This is for code generation.
- COMP 2711. This is for necessary discrete math basics. Proofs, Induction and Sets.
- COMP 3031. This is for the Scala basics and a broader view on programming languages, which are what we want to compile! Moreover, there are some overlapping that will make your life easier.

Believe it or not. Just enjoy the content.
comment_teaching:
Professor PL teaches in a decent way, just like the recursive descent parser. I think he did explain all concepts that he wanted to present, but some of them didn't appear in a clear way in the lecture notes, (e.g., LL(1) Parser). I suggest you directly asking Professor PL or the TAs as they are willing to answer your question.

Edit: Oh no! I forgot to mention our kindest TAs!

In this semester Anto and Tony were the TAs for grading assignments/exams and presenting tutorials. Anto was mainly presenting the tutorial and Tony sometimes were invisible (I've heard that he went to Japan quite often).

The tutorial materials are really well-organized and clear; doing them is more helpful than doing past papers. In the first few tutorials, there were many exercises that couldn't be done in one tutorial section. The rest tutorials are great as the TAs actively saw our progress and put the question that we hadn't yet solved to the next tutorial.

The tutorials really helped a lot in understanding the materials. I'd suggest all CSE courses to introduce such tutorials to solve exercises. The tutorial exercises are usually more difficult than the final exam, so if you managed to understand all of them, then your final exam should be good.

comment_content:
Maybe we can start with a story: In Spring 2024-25, COMP4211 updates its prerequisite as a surprise, they added COMP3211 to the prerequisite to support the new AI major. Anyway, naturally, I decided to enroll COMP3211 at that time and because I had a time clash during COMP4211 time anyway.

However, the prerequsite is then removed (to be enforced again in 2026 Spring -- they said (which is in fact, NOT enforced but only including COMP2211 as a prerequisite (reasonable in my opinion - Desmond's course is good anyway), and I can enroll (COMP2012 and stats/prob course only). Later, at the end of add drop period, my time conflict is also resolved. I enrolled COMP4211 on the last day of add-drop period. I was quite nervous as the first assignment is already there and I got 2 weeks to rush it (no skeleton code?) and the slides looks so intimidating (for a year 2, just finished COMP2211).

Anyway, rip COMP4211 Fall 2025 waitlist... (full because people thought the prerequisite will change to include COMP3211 in the next semester, which creates a long chain of prerequisite)

== Content ==
In my opinion the course is indeed a very BFS (breadth-first search) of machine learning. You will be introduced to many topics and probably it helps your maturity (?) in understanding more advanced machine learning topics. I do wish there are more small implementation practice of each topics but well.. 2 big assignments + project is quite a lot no?

Also, I generally recommend that you have COMP2211 background and linear algebra understanding (I took linear algebra in the same semester, but I learnt a good amount of it before) before taking the course.

Anyway the content are as follow: (Prof hjx largely borrow slides from Prof DY, I think this is the standard machine learning course scope, maybe you can expect to see these in other offerings (after Prof DY retires...) and I think this topic scope is good)
Some of the topics are quite challenging for general CSE students (a lot of math to scare you in the slides - but actually they are not tested heavily. Or rather, actually they are OK topics, but the notations in the slides are just cryptic, so you can't really understand it by looking at the slides once)

I will give some note one which one is math heavy and quite hard (with this topic scope, I think it generally starts off math heavy, and then relatively light after some time, and then slightly hard again around the end of the semester)... the course as a whole should not be too intimidating and should not scare you off.

1. Linear Regression (You might recall this from your MATH2411 (if you did take it), but now we generalize this for n-dimension) - moderate amount of math
2. Logistic Regression & Multiclass Classification - moderate amount of math
3. FFN (Feedforward Neural Network) - cryptic notations amount of math (compared to COMP2211), you may want to try deriving backpropagation once and write it on your cheatsheet for exams; multivariable calculus might help but not neccessary -- I think it was not tested math heavy in my semester
4. Deep Neural Network - moderate amount of math, it gets more chill from here
(from here on.. generally you won't see slides packed with equations except for SVM)

5. Convolutional Neural Network - not too hard, COMP2211 repetition + explanation of ResNet
6. Recurrent Neural Network - not too hard, but note that the math expressions are to be cheatsheet'ed -- just understand them on high level/heuristically is fine
7. Principal Component Analysis - moderate amount of math, please recall eigen{values,vectors} from linear algebra
8. Autoencoders - not too hard
9. Clustering - not too hard
10. GAN (Generative Adversarial Network) - moderate amount of math, mostly understand heuristically -- btw I find this part quite interesting, since this is the first time generative model is introduced
11. Transformers - moderate amount of math and unintuitive at first, really recommend watching: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eMlx5fFNoYc
12. SVM - Support Vector Machine (but actually just a hyperplane) - the most cryptic slides out of all - (quoting Prof hjx: "No one really uses this nowadays") - need time to understand and please learn about plane equations if you haven't before -- but I don't think there is a need in understanding the equations fully
13. Decision Tree and Random Forest - not too hard
comment_teaching:
I think the teaching is the "above average" when you reach COMP4xxx courses (maybe same-ish quality as Prof DY). Prof hjx teaching is quite clear and he will provide pretty good amount of intution and interesting insights for each topics. I think he is also very welcoming to answer questions.

Unfortunately, at this point of CS, I think Prof in general will not derive each equation for you in great detail. Prof hjx teaching pace is slightly on the slow side (He was slightly behind Prof DY's progress a few times.. 😅)

I did write this review after taking Prof hjx COMP4901B (I think it's also another good course to introduce/BFS you to LLMs). His teaching style seems to be (review some part of the previous lecture for 20-30 mins), then start new topic.

Just some side note but Prof hjx said a lot of "out of scope" (maybe because he usually teaches COMP5212 -- PG machine learning) and he did say "out of scope" and advertise COMP4901B when teaching about transformers.

Piazza is useful for asking questions too - including for assignments - (asked lecture questions in batches a few day before the final exam, Prof DY thankfully answers me and clarified most of it)
