I think you can read Grading

The workload is in general more on the heavy side. But I think it's quite worth it.

Anyway, just a note, I did COMP3511 and COMP3711 in the same semester. Still survived (?).
comment_grading:
The grading is reasonable and quite good -- seems to be criterion referencing? But well the scheme is not a large amount in final exam

== Grading Scheme and Assignment Comments ==
> 10% Quiz ( There are usually 1-2 quiz per lecture, to be done on canvas after lecture -- but it is quite 'mean' that there are no drop policy :( -- wrong 1 question will deduct final grade; there are about 20 quizzes.. but ok, most are easy if you understand the lecture) -- only those after add drop counts
> 10% programming assignment 1
No skeleton code??? Write yourself on google colab from scratch... but questions are quite clear and reasonable. I actually end up even having sufficient time to do all the bonuses :) -- Please come to the tutorial if you want some guidance to do some part of the questions
> 10% programming assignment 2
This semester it is about Joint Energy Model (another way of implementing generative model --- it's not explained it lecture at all and has strange equations -- but anyway I think this is expected in comp4xxx no 😅?)
> 7.5% written assignment 1
Reasonable workload, if you do the written assignment you will have a thorough understanding of the lecture. There is a question to actually derive the n-dimension case of linear regression (so that's why the derivation is skipped in lecture)
> 7.5% written assignment 2
Also reasonable workload, also helps to understand lecture topics
> 15% project
So, here's the thing with this offering. I think when Prof hjx teaches COMP5212, he makes a kaggle competition. So now, instead of a free project, we have a kaggle competition. The task is about NER (Named Entitiy Recognition) in NLP.
To be honest, I am quite thankful that this is the case (I'm quite bad at making good free project ideas), and it is good to have this "guided" experience to make a project, but I do think that the assignment makes me try many different approaches, only to realize that it's not the way it's supposed to be done at the end.
Me and my groupmate agreed that we should start gradually according to the topics introduced in class, we tried RNN, transformers, even find some embeddings online.. what happened? they indeed pass the baseline but it cannot surpass lower quartile of the competition😭. The solution? Go find a pretrained model on huggingface (time wasted on experiments: a lot). Yeah.. so that's how.. (anyway we did try different pretrained models too), even then I still get lower quartile for the kaggle competition. I get around mean because of the good report. Anyway, it doesn't matter because most people got 95+ grade in the project anyway.
The sad thing is that pretrained model is introduced slightly later when transformer is taught and there are no small examples to show us how to do fine-tuning (I just learned that this is a thing from the course -- the bitter lesson is I now understand how good they are).
Anyway, my general comment is a harder project is a good addition to the course (I do think I still lack ability to make AI projects because of this project scheme), but maybe make it as less stressful and give more guidance if possible.
> 40% Final Exam
Under Prof DY, you can believe when he say there are not much math in the exam. There's even less math (but there is still some) compared to the written assignment. You do need to understand the heuristic view of all topics in general.

However, the exam is very rushed (Prof hjx warned about this), there are about 12 MCQ (as the first question on all the topics) + ~10 long questions on almost every topic IN 2 HOURS!
This is the most time-packed exam I have ever seated.

When I see the question (those with a lot of math and very looong) I was like: "oh shoot - will I F this course?" -- my general suggestion is to look at all questions first, and tackle those that looks easy, then come back to the questions you have skipped, for me it suddenly clicks (and because I have more time to think).

The math-related questions are mostly on the first few topics on traditional machine learning. For the other questions, you can expect similar to written assignments in terms of difficulty. There are also True or False with "provide reason"

At the end I still got time to double check everything :)

=== Grades ===
My grade is in (...)

Quiz: Wrong 2 out of all :) -- almost everyone got quiz correct (pls don't forget to do it even if you skip lecture, set an alarm or something if this is the case)
PA1: (100/100) [Mean=92.69, Lower Quartile=93, Median=99.5, Upper Quartile=100]
PA2: (100/100) [Cannot find for now, but expect classic PA distribution]
WA1: (100/100) [Cannot find for now, but generally close to full for everyone]
WA2: (100/100) [Cannot find for now, but generally close to full for everyone]
Project: (96.77/100) [Mean: 95, Lower Quartile: 93.18, Median: 95.5, Upper Quartile: 97.63] -- I guess need to put close attention to report too
Exam: (83.5/100) [Mean: 54.8, Lower Quartile: 39.5, Median: 53, Upper Quartile: 70.38]

btw PA grading is more generous than expected, no need to be very concerned. Try to do all bonus if you can (can give +10 marks at max, but grade still capped at 100)

This grade yields an A. (quite happy as a year 2 in this course I guess and considering all that had happened?)

comment_grading:
The course grading is criteria referencing (simply saying, norm-referencing in UST), said by DY.

The grading scheme is as follows:
- 10% Inclass Quizzes
- 20% Written Assignments
- 10% Programming Assignment 1
- 15% Programming Assignment 2
- 15% Midterm Exam
- 30% Final Exam

The grading scheme is a bit focused on written assignments and programming assignments. Therefore, you need to work well on daily assignments not to get a bad grade.

The statistics for exam are shown below:

Midterm Exam
- Lower Quartile: 61
- Median: 70
- Upper Quartile: 78
- Mean: 67.14
- Standard Deviation: 16.68
- High: 96

Final Exam:
- Lower Quartile: 40
- Median: 53
- Upper Quartile: 67.5
- Mean: 52.9
- Standard Deviation: 17.8
- High: 89.5

The midterm exam paper is distributed to us, which allows us to take paper home. This is not bad for us. But for final exam, there is no final paper checking since DY tells us that the primary purpose of the final exam is assessment rather than learning, he tells me individually that HKDSE does not allow for paper checking (but needs to pay money for reviewing), this is a pretty odd reason honestly.

The final letter grade is pretty much surprising me. My midterm exam score is at Lower Quartile and my final exam is at Upper Quartile, with other assignment scores full yield a B+. However, my friend gets a total overall course score 0.45 higher than me and gets an A-. It is confirmed that the cutoff is between my score and my friend's score. comment_content:
I won't type out all the topics taught here as others have already done so. I think the scope is good. BUT the problem is that with most of the students lacking the necessary mathematical background (e.g. multivariable calculus and linear algebra[not the simple ones taught in MATH2111]) and not including courses like MATH2023 etc. as prerequisite, prof. just teach the simplest concepts for each topic. In the SVM part, you can even see there are almost no deviations for all the formulae in the notes, so you can basically only memorize or write them on your cheat sheet. This course, combined with 2211 and 3211 are just the basic intro. to the AI field, so if you want to dive deeper, take other AI courses (e.g. COMP4471, COMP4901[depends on what courses are available]) that are more specific in a certain area.

Moreover, I don't understand with a double in the amount of topics covered in the final exam (compared to midterm, which stops at RNN), we are still only allow to bring one double-sided A4-size cheat sheet. By the way, to those who want/need to take this course, I highly recommend creating the cheat sheet by yourself cuz during which, you will need to read through all the notes and select the most important ones to write down. This process already helps you revise on the topics. Nowadays, with the help of LLM and the web, I don't think it will be hard to get real-time explanation on hard/unclear concepts.

Furthermore, prof. says that the final exam is an assessment, not an opportunity for learning and he and the TAs cross-checked on the marking a few times, so no paper checking. I don't even understand this logic... At least give out the solution for people to have a chance to review on what they have done wrong.

comment_workload:
Three weeks are given for each assignment.

Bonus parts exist for you to recover the missing points that you have made in the normal parts, cap at 100, i.e.
your PA final mark = min(total marks you earned in PA, 100)

Besides coding, there are also some inline written questions for the PAs. As a side note, in my case, one bonus inline question in PA2 requires some explanation using some linear algebra derivations, such as span, column spaces, etc. So you probably need to pick up the math knowledge that you have learned before when answering these bonus parts. They don't care about your background so you may even see something out of syllabus.

If you exclude the bonus part for PAs, then the PAs are manageable.

If not, a total disaster. For PA1, you need to build the whole thing by yourself. No skeleton code is given. For PA2, it is the first time designed by the UGTAs. Yes, UGTAs. So a lot of mismatch between the guidance and the marking scheme. Also, you would need to start this one as early as possible. Since I completed the whole bonus part to play safe, this takes up all 5 GPU hours free quota on Colab so either you have your own GPU or you need to open multiple accounts to do it. I don't understand why the teaching team does not reserve some GPUs from the department/ITSC for this, which my COMP4901B lecturer did.

For the WAs, it is okay if you understand the concepts well but the workload depends on your personality. If you are striving for perfection for your answers like me, the problem is that they never expect you to think deeply and thoroughly, unlike 2711 and 3711. I use the 2711 and 3711 way (i.e. defining a bunch of notations to get a mathematical representation of something, etc.) to answer the questions, which is extremely tedious and leads to twenty something pages for each WA. Their solution is just 4-5 sentences for each sub-question...
comment_grading:
According to prof. 's answer on Piazza, this course is not using norm-referenced grading. (So I assume absolute grading?)

They are generous in grading.

I guess almost every student should get full marks on the PAs and WAs, given that they have put some effort in finishing these assignments. Yet, I do not understand why only the PAs have bonus part but not for the WAs. Isn't coding much easier compared to writing for engineers?

With the help of AI chatbots and searching in the lecture notes, I also don't think the in-class quizzes would be a problem. These quizzes are on Canvas during the lecture period and act as a mean of taking attendance, so you can basically skip some of the lectures if you do not have the time due to midterm or hw. BUT the thing is that the correctness of the quizzes matters... I don't even understand why.

So the only places that differentiate you from other students will be the 2 exams (No project this sem). Even with cheat sheet, the math is mostly not assessed (except the part for calculating number of params, output sizes). You will be tested on your understanding on the concepts. For example, something like will changing the margin lead to a change in the hyperplane, will K-Means give zero SSE given a certain condition. You will need some logical deductions based on clear conceptual understanding to arrive at the answer, so good luck on this part! The part the cheat sheet can help you is answering something like what will be the impacts of applying such techniques, what techniques can be used to reduce overfitting.

For both exams, I get the UQ and for others, I get the full marks, so if prof. is really generous, I could possibly get a A-.

comment_content:
I do not recommend this course.

I give this an A simply in comparison with my review of 3211 and the width of the topic. The depth of which the content is explored or discussed honestly warrant a B or C in my opinion.

Detailed list of content is exactly the same as the past offering so it will not be repeated here.

In almost every topic, you can feel that DY cannot teaches the whole thing due to the math foundation of an average CS student (or rather, the lack thereof). This renders almost all theoretical explanation or discussion extremely weak.

For example, the explanation of linear regression uses the manual and tedious calculus based approach (which arguably uses more math to justify) instead of the much more elegant and easy pure linear algebra proof. If the easiest topic in the course have such weak exploration and math foundation, then i think you can imagine how nonexistent the other math used in the course really is...

As a result, many of the topic becomes extremely shallow and have a math "explanation" that is no different to gibberish to people with no foundation, to me,  the (despite the possibility of proper explanation) (looking at you SVM)

So, what this course teaches is really just yappy empirical results that you basically just takes the prof's word for, which is extremely unsatisfactory and makes your understanding no better than chatgippity with a Medium subscription.

I highly recommend any one who wishes to actually learn machine learning to take many of the other available courses like math 4432 or comp 4471.... This course really feels more like 2211 honours than anything....

comment_teaching:
Both Prof. He Junxian and Prof. Yeung Dit Yan are good (I went to both sessions in this week). Personally, I prefer Prof. He. I will give some details below.

Prof. He's lectures may be one of my best learning experience in UST. I found his lectures very exciting and fruitful, without wasting a single minute. You can tell from his lecture that he is not only very knowledgable in this field, but also good at presenting. He likes to give many examples to make the concepts easier to understand. His lectures are fast-pased, and he usually add more content that is out of the slides (he also distribute his own slides which has some extra pages). The quality of his lecture really make me feel that I'm studying in MIT in Aisa XD.

Prof. Yeung, compared to Prof. He, tends to stick to the slides more and give slower and more detailed (sometimes seeming unnecessary to me) explanations. He always explains things very clearly. As for me, however, I found his tone a little bit to flat and the pace is somehow too slow, which makes me sleepy (or maybe because his lecture is earlier >.<). But I'm sure many people would like his lectures.

In general, they are both good, kind and devoted to teaching, but I would recommend Prof. He if you can catch up and feel comfortable with more fast-paced lectures.

By the way. I think Prof. He may be one of the three instructors that I like the most at HKUST so far. The other two are Desmond (usually teaches 2012/2211, and 1023 in the next semester) and Sunil (usuallly teaches 2711H/5712). For these three good guys, I would enroll their classes even though I may not be very interested in the content, just because they are teaching. (Yes, I'm doing commercial :D)

For the tutorial part, I think the TAs did not do a good job, at least in the add-drop period. Few people listen to them and the tutorials are not so helpful. I would prefer learning by myself. Good news is that there is no attendance for this part.
comment_workload:
I think it depends. The workload is high undoubtedly, but it can still vary depending on what courses you have taken before. The official pre-requisites of the course are:
- [Before Spring 2026]
{COMP 2012 AND a statistics/probability course}
- [Starting from Spring 2026]
{COMP 3211 AND a statistics/probability course}
However, I think those are not enough. I think the courses/content below will help you understand the content or tackle the programming parts of this course and therefore reduce your workload. If you don't have any of them, then you may have a very hard time.

- A python programming course (necessary)
Of course I don't mean COMP 1021. That one is too simple, sometimes naive. I think you will need COMP 1023 (starting from Fall 2025) or COMP 2211 here. They will equip you with the necessary skills that you will need in PAs and project.

- A linear algebra course (necessary)
I think this is VERY important. In lectures, they may assume that you have learned linear algebra and use things like inner product of vectors and also matrix multiplication. Actually, linear algebra is a fundamental tool in ML. If you don't know any linear algebra, you may run into big trouble and miss some beautiful ideas.

- Some statistics/probability knowledge (maybe necessary)
I think you don't need a whole course for this part. Considering the syllabus of MATH 2411 and MATH 2421 only have a little part of overlap in the basic things, I think what needed in this course is also basic. Anyway, I haven't seen the need to take a whole MATH 24x1 for this course. However, now that they put it in the prerequisite, I think you'd better take one. (they rejected a lot of waivers this semester)

- Some multivariable calculus knowledge (not that necessary?)
MATH 2011 / MATH 2023 is good. I find it helpful in gradient descent part.

- Some basic ideas about AI (not necessary but I would highly recommend)
COMP 2211 is good enough for this part. If you take it before 4211, you will find some content of 4211 easier to understand. Things you learn in 2211 labs and PAs will also help you in 4211.

You may have noticed that I didn't mention COMP 2012 and COMP 3211, which are in the old/new official prerequisites. Actually, I can't see any point to put them in the prerequisite. For OOP, one of COMP 1023 or 2211 is definitely enough. For AI basics, COMP 2211 will be enough (and even better than COMP 3211, which is actually more about some old things). Anyway, try to be used to the unreasonable prerequisites in UST :(
(Extra note: it seems that they will not approve any waivers for COMP 3211 in the future, so make your plans early)

---------
p.s. Well, should I write all these things in the workload part? >.<

I'm writing the review at the beginning of the semester, so I'm not able to write about the distribution and so on. I will provide some general descriptions below:

- In-class quizzes: 10%
1-2 MC on canvas quiz. Basically the things that are taught in the lectures. Actually this is not really in-class because you can finish them after class. (In this semester, L1 by Prof. Yeung is at TuTh 9:00-10:30 and L2 by Prof. He is TuTh 10:30-12:00. The quiz is due on 14:00 on the same day of each lecture. Don't forget to do it after class!) However, there is one problem. The quiz questions seem to be made by Prof. Yeung and some of the content is only mentioned in L1 but not L2. Sometimes this is annoying. Most of the questions are easy, though.

- Written assignments: 15%
Haven't started yet.

- Programming assignments: 20%
PAs are somehow crazy and time-consuming. PA1 is basically about using some popular python libraries such as pandas, matplotlib and sklearn. However, you should write everything from scratch and tackle many many problems. It also needs a report. Make sure you start early.

- Group project: 15%
Each group contains 2 people. The project is a kaggle competition about name entity recognition in NLP. You can choose any model you like. Your score will depend on the rankings on kaggle. However, most people got very close scores here because the f1 score of everyone's models are pretty close.
Actually, I think the project part can be improved, because it seems that almost everyone ended up in fine tuning pretrained models. The various models you learn in lectures are somehow all useless with the dominant solution just there.

- Final exam: 40%
2 hours, closed books with 1 cheatsheet. Time is very limited because there are so many questions. The form of the questions are like the quiz and written assignments. I cannot make many comments here because they did not allow us to check the papers, so I have no idea where I got wrong.

No midterm! Yay~~~

---

I think most of the people got close to full in quiz, PAs, written assignments and even project, so the course is somehow all in final. I have to say I don't like this scheme.

I forgot 2 quiz, got full in all PAs and written assignments, 99.6/100 in project and 72/100 in exam. The letter grade is A-. I guess they give close to 25% A range because my final score is just above UQ.

comment_content:
Similar to COMP 4421, the contents were an (excellent) breadth-first look into a field - in this case, Machine Learning. The included topics were sufficient for using ML for a wide variety of tasks. Since this was a UG course, DY deliberately used a bit less Math (and took away some of the more complicated proofs), which I appreciated. The additional topics provided in the lecture notes also give you a good place to start looking deeper into the content.

Although the official prerequisites were OOP and one of statistics or probability, there was also slight multivariable calculus involved (but that is possibly completely unavoidable in the field of ML in general). COMP 2211 can serve as an excellent baseline to help you learn the basics, but is not completely necessary if you're confident in your ability to pick up Python libraries at speed.

Since the PAs and the project involve significant amounts of Python, COMP 2211 flattens the learning curve a bit for that portion (by exposing you to NumPy and other useful libraries earlier). Given the increasing difficulty of COMP 2211 (and its exams), this might prove to be even more of an advantage.

For more depth, you can consider taking other courses which specialize in a specific area of ML: COMP 4221 for NLP (though beware of grading by Victor), COMP 4222 for Graph ML, COMP 4471 for Computer Vision (though the workload is possibly even higher than this course), COMP 4901Y (foundation models) and COMP 4901Z for Reinforcement Learning (which is much more mathematical than this one).

The contents did not vary from the last offering, and were as follows:
Introduction
- Definitions of ML and its terms (Training, Testing)
- Supervised vs Unsupervised Learning
- Definition of Reinforcement Learning
Linear Regression
- The Linear Regression Function (and its derivations)
- Squared Loss
- Nonlinear extensions (Polynomial regression and extra input dimensions)
- Overfitting and solutions (L1, L2 Regularization, Cross-validation)
- Regression Evaluation Metrics (MSE, R^2)
Logistic Regression
- Binary, Multiclass and Multilabel Classification
- The Logistic, Sigmoid and Softmax Functions
- Network Representation of Logistic Regression
- Likelihood, Cross-Entropy Loss and Class Probabilities (for both Binary and Multiclass Classification)
- Minimizing Loss (Learning) by Gradient Descent
- Regularization for Logistic Regression
- Performance Metrics for Classification (Precision, Recall, Accuracy and F1-Score, confusion matrix)
Feedforward Neural Networks (FNNs) (or the Multi-layer Perceptron)
- Layered Representation of a FNN
- Activation Functions (Sigmoid, tanh)
- Vanishing Gradient
- The Forward Pass and Backpropagation
- Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) or Batch Gradient Descent
- Regularization for FNNs
- Momentum in training FNNs
Deep Neural Networks
- Saturation and Non-saturating Activation Functions (Rectifier (ReLU), Leaky ReLU, ELU)
- Parameter Initializers (Xavier for Sigmoid, He for the ReLU family)
- Internal Covariate Shift and Batch Normalization (BatchNorm)
- Dropout as a Regularizer
- Data Augmentation (mainly on Images)
- Optimizers (AdaGrad, RMSProp, Adam) and their properties
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
- 2D Convolution
- Convolutional Layer and Stride
- Feature Maps (and their sizes)
- Pooling (Max, Average Pooling)
- Short Introduction to different CNN Architectures (AlexNet, VGG, GoogLeNet, ResNet, DenseNet)
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
- Motivation (Sequential or Variable-Length Data with Dependencies)
- Recurrent Units and Memory Cells
- Unfolded Representation of a RNN
- Sequence-to-Sequence tasks and the Encoder-Decoder Architecture
- Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)
- Long-Sequence Learning (Gradient Clipping, Vanishing or Exploding Gradients)
- Long-Short Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)
- Attention Mechanism (Context Vectors and (Learning) the Attention Weights)
Principle Component Analysis (PCA)
- The Dimensionality Reduction Task
- Feature Selection vs Feature Transformation
- The PCA Algorithm (Covariance, Linear Projection and Principle Component computation via eigenvalues and eigenvectors)
- Data Preprocessing and Computation Efficiency of PCA
- Percentage of Variance (PoV) and the Scree Plot
Autoencoders
- The Bottleneck Layer
- Simple Autoencoders for Dimensionality Reduction
- Deep, Stacked, Denoising Autoencoders
- Convolutional Autoencoders for Image Segmentation
- The U-Net Architecture
Clustering
- Partitional Clustering with k-Means (The k-Means Algorithm, Potential Problems of k-Means, Guaranteed Convergence and the Elbow Method of Cluster Number Determination)
- Extensions of k-Means (k-Medians, k-Mediods)
- Hierarchical Clustering (Agglomerative vs Divisive)
- Distance Measures (Single-Link, Complete-Link and Average-Link Proximity)
Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs)
- Generative Modelling
- Implicit Generative Models (Matching of Generated Distribution with True Data Distribution)
- GANs (Structure, Cost Functions, Minimax Formulation, Backpropagation)
- Alternate Cost Function for Generator (The Saturation Problem)
- Deep Convolutional GANs
- CycleGAN for Style Transfer
Transformers
- Encoder-Decoder structure for Transformers
- Parallel Data Flow in Encoders
- Self-Attention
- The Vector, Query and Key Matrices in Self-Attention
- Multi-headed Self-Attention
- Positional Encoding
- Residual Connections and Layer Normalization (LayerNorm)
- The Decoding Step
- Pre-Trained Transformer Models (BERT for Token or Sentence Prediction, Feature Extraction)
Support Vector Machines (SVMs)
- The (Canonical) (Optimal) Separating Hyperplane
- Inequality Constraints of SVMs
- Primal and Dual Optimization
- Support Vectors
- Discriminant Function
- Slack Variables and Soft-Margin SVMs (Penalty Terms for Hyperplane-Crossing Data)
- Kernel Methods (Kernel Functions, Basis Functions, Kernel Matrix)
- The Polynomial and RBF Kernels
Decision Trees and Random Forests
- Decision Tree Algorithm
- Best Attribute Selection
- Information Gain and Impurity Measures (Entropy, Gini Index)
- Overfitting on Decision Tress and Pruning
- Ensemble Methods (Boostrapping (Bagging), Random Forests)

The contents were indeed vast, but not covered in great depth - most contents were introduced in a few slides only.
comment_teaching:
DY was a pretty good presenter, with clear diction and a good grasp of what to cover in each lecture. Lecture slides were well-designed and to-the-point. Announcements and Piazza questions were usually promptly replied to by both the teaching team and the TAs-in-charge (though if your question is too obvious from their perspective, you might be asked to refer to previous posts or lecture materials). I also appreciated DY's willingness to answer questions after each lecture.

One nitpick I have with the course is that some topics are introduced from a Math perspective first before the intuition or the use-case for such a topic. It is not something that I personally prefer, but this is purely a personal preference. I do think there is room for teaching ML by explaining ML from an intuitive perspective first, which might enhance my understanding.
comment_workload:
The workload for this course was generally very high, but the work was generally meaningful for enhancing your understanding of ML.

The written assignment took around a week to finalize. Each PA consumed at least two weeks of my time to complete every task, and the project workload was rather high (for our group) as we chose an ambitious topic.

Please plan your work accordingly and perhaps try to take the course in a semester with generally low workload.
comment_grading:
Grading (and perhaps content-wise), the course can actually be split into a Theory part and a Practical part.

The grading scheme is as follows:
10% In-Class Quizzes (MCQs)
10% Written Assignment (Problem Set)
25% Programming Assignments (12.5% Each)
30% Group Project
35% Final Exam

Note that passing the course requires passing the final exam by achieving some undisclosed minimum mark.

Theory Assessments
=================
Theory assessments mainly focused on your understanding of the course materials, and the logic behind them. No coding is required for every assessment in this list.

In-Class Quizzes
--------------------------
The in-class quizzes were 0-2 multiple-choice questions due on the same day as a lecture, with one submission allowed only. Some of the questions were reused from previous semester, while others were completely new. They mainly examined either direct understanding, or the changes in a model given a variation on the model or the conditions of the model taught in class. They were not particularly difficult, and the mean ranged from 0.9 to 1 (the full mark is 1).

Written Assignment
--------------------------
The written assignment contained questions from most of the topics in class, and the difficulty of the questions ranged from pretty easy (number of trainable parameters in a transformer layer) to rather confusing (proof of saturation of the original GAN cost function). DY suggested that you should finish the relevant questions after the conclusion of a single topic, but I did not follow his advice.

The written assignment was released very early in the semester, and due near the end of the semester.

Written assignment statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
0/88.75/89.7/93.5/97/100/100

Exams
--------------------------
Exams were extremely theory-focused with little to none dry-run type questions. They mainly assessed your understanding of the course material by asking you to provide solutions to common ML problems, explain the pitfalls of specific situations or give short explanations of ML theory topics. In particular, there was almost no Math involved except a single question.

A 2-sided A4 cheatsheet was allowed, but proved not very useful for me.

Exam statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
20/46.75/57.59/59/70.25/93.5/100

Side note: Paper checking was not allowed, with the following reasons (which I disagree with): double marking by TA and DY ensured correctness (not a guarantee); exam was an assessment and not a learning opportunity (???); paper checking is not required by the university (true). This left a sour taste despite the generous grading.

Practical Assessments
=================
Practical assessments mainly focused on your ability to apply the concepts you have learnt in the course in a Python environment. This is where the course (with the help of labs) teaches you about Python libraries often used in ML such as NumPy, matplotlib, seaborn, scikit-learn and tensorflow.

Of note: PyTorch was not introduced in this course, although its use was permissible in the course project. If you wish to learn PyTorch and have a low-ish semester workload, consider taking COMP 4471.

Programming Assignments
---------------------------------------
Programming assignments were moderately difficult. Each programming assignment had bonus parts which were mandatory in the previous semesters (perhaps the workload was too high). Both the Python code and a written report was required for each assignment.

PA1 was on data preprocessing techniques, linear and FNN-based regression, logistic regression and FNN-based classification on a given (labeled) dataset. The PA was mainly based on the pandas and scikit-learn libraries. The bonus was on performance enhancement by preprocessing validation and hyperparameter tuning.

No skeleton code was provided for this assignment, so reading and understanding the documentation of the libraries used was also part of the assessment. This PA had 22 tasks and 4 bonus tasks.

PA2 was on image generation and classification using Tensorflow and Keras. Tasks included dataset description, ResNet block and model building, classifier training, Joint-Energy Model (JEM) implementation, training and testing. Since JEMs were not part of the course contents, extra time was required to understand the idea behind JEMs. Limited hints were provided on Piazza.

While skeleton code was provided, reading and understanding the code took significant time. There were 10 written tasks, 8 code tasks and 12 bonus tasks in this PA.

Each PA was released around three weeks before the due date. You are strongly advised to start each PA as soon as possible as model training and understanding the description of each PA takes significant amount of time. In particular, PA2 used a larger amount of resources than what was available on my (gaming) laptop, and necessitated the use of Google Colab for training.

PA statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
12.5/94/92.06/99.5/100/100/100
0/92/91.49/100/100/100/100

Group Project
---------------------------------------
The group project consisted of two parts: a written proposal and a final product submission. The project was a 2-person project, with a 3-person group due to an extra student.

The topics of the project were entirely self-proposed, with emphasis given to the novelty of the application more than the actual results.

The written proposal was a 1-2 page document describing the problem to be solved, previous work on the topic, dataset description and expected computing resources required. The proposal was graded on completion as long as all aspects were reasonably clear.

The final product submission consisted of a 15 minute YouTube video, presentation slides used in the video, a 15-20 page project report and your project source code.

Although the stated requirements for the report were 15-20 pages, I have heard of cases of underlength reports being acceptable - as long as the explanations for each part (dataset description, ML tasks, ML methods, techniques and libraries used) were clear, I believe there should not be any serious issues. Do remember to include table of contents for both your slides and the report, since part of DY's grading was based on that.

"Good programming practice" such as documentation was required for your source code.

The YouTube video should describe your project - it could be a paraphrase of your project report if you are so inclined. DY expected a "serious technical presentation", so video editing skills required should be minimal. 10% deviations were acceptable in our semester.

Note that "proper division of labour" is also a part of the grading scheme, so try to ensure that the division of labour in your report is as close to equal as possible.

comment_content:
The contents are standard. The lecture slides are well-written, well-organized and content-rich. You can refer to the topic list and the subparts of each topic below:

1. Introduction

2. Linear Regression
- Linear Regression Function, Squared Loss and Linear System
- Non-linear Regression (+ Polynomial Regression)
- Regularization (L1, L2, p-norm)
- Mean Squared Error (MSE) and R^2 Score

3. Logistic Regression
- Sigmoid and Softmax Functions
- Class Probabilities, Likelihood and Cross-entropy Loss
- Gradient Descent Learning
- Confusion Matrix, Accuracy, Precision, Recall and F1 score

4. Feedforward Neural Networks (MLP)
- Forward Propagation, Backward Propagation, Weight Updating
- Activation Functions (Sigmoid, Hyperbolic Tangent (tanh))
- Vanishing Gradient Problem
- Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
- Regularized Loss Function and Momentum

5. Deep Neural Networks (DNN)
- ReLU, Leaky ReLU and Exponential Linear Unit (ELU)
- Xavier and He Initialization
- Internal Covariate Shift and Batch Normalization
- Dropout
- Data Augmentation
- AdaGrad, RMSProp and Adam

6. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
- Convolutional Layer v.s. Fully Connected Layer (Dense)
- Stride
- Pooling, Average Pooling and Max Pooling
- Common CNN Architectures (AlexNet, VGGNet, GoogLeNet, ResNet)

7. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)
- Recurrent Units and Memory Cells
- Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT)
- Short-term/Long-term Dependencies
- Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)
- Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU)
- Attention, RNN Encoder-Decoder, Context Vectors and Attention Weights

8. Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
- Dimensionality Reduction
- Feature Selection/Transformation
- Data Variance
- First and Second Principal Components
- Linear Projection
- Data Preprocessing
- Proportion of Variance (PoV)

9. Autoencoders
- Bottleneck Layer concept
- Simple Autoencoders, Deep Autoencoders
- (Stacked) Denoising Autoencoders
- Convolutional Autoencoders
- U-Net Architecture

10. Clustering
- Feature-based/Similarity-based/Partitional/Hierarchical Clustering
- K-Means Clustering and the Elbow Method
- Variations of K-Means: K-Medians and K-Medoids
- Hierarchical Clustering: Agglomerative Approach and Divisive Approach
- Cluster Proximities: Single-link Proximity, Complete-link Proximity and Average-link Proximity (+ Distance Matrix)

11. Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)
- Generative Modeling
- Generators and Discriminators (+ Loss Function)
- Minimax Optimization Problem
- Deep Convolutional Generative Adversarial Network (DCGAN)
- Conditional GAN
- CycleGAN Architecture, Style Transfer and Cycle Consistency Loss

12. Transformers
- Encoder-Decoder Architecture
- (Multi-head) Self-attention
- Positional Encoding, Residual and Layer-normalization
- BERT Architecture

13. Support Vector Machines (SVM)
- (Canonical) Optimal Separating Hyperplane and its Inequality Constraints
- Primal Optimization Problem (PO) and Dual Optimization Problem (DO)
- Discriminant Function
- Hard-margin SVM and Soft-margin SVM
- Soft-margin SVM: Slack Variables, Relaxed Separation Constraints, Penalty and Hinge Loss (+ PO and DO)
- Kernel Methods: Basis Function and Discriminant Function (+ DO)
- Polynomial Kernel and Radial Basis Function (RBF) Kernel

14. Decision Trees and Random Forests
- Impurity Measure and Information Gain
- Entropy
- Decision Tree Ensembles, Bagging and Random Forests

(About 80% of the subparts mentioned above are within 1-2 slides so the content is not that heavy)

Referring to past offerings of this course by DY, Reinforcement Learning is scrapped out this semester. Maybe they are planning to offer a brand new course dedicated to this topic :P

A for content in my opinion.
comment_teaching:
I attended Shimin's lectures and watched a couple of DY's lecture videos. Both of them are kind enough to answer student's questions.

Shimin did his best to derive math equations related to the topic, making it look like a MATH lecture. He also went through the in-class quizzes with students after he completed explaining the lecture content. So it was free 10% for L2 students who stayed till the end.

DY's explanations were clear along with the well-designed lecture slides. I was able to catch up on later chapters a couple of days before the final exam by watching his RVCs. He also makes announcements once an assignment is released and frequently answers students' questions via Piazza, showing that he is very responsible.

Both of them deserve an A for teaching in my opinion.
comment_workload:
The course workload was exceptionally high.

Both the PAs and the project were extremely time-consuming to complete, given the large amount of tasks to complete within 3 weeks for each assignment/project. I skipped a lot of lectures (including other courses) and took nights to get them done. Training models (especially in PA2, maybe also the project) and recording the statistics took the most time so you should start the PAs and projects as early as possible. Based on this, I would argue the course credits should be 4-5 when it comes to workload.

I decided to give a D to the workload, considering that the assignments did provide me with a lot of training on Python and various ML modules, which reinforced what I learnt previously (COMP2211), as well as "D" standing for "DY".
comment_grading:
- 10% In-class Quizzes
- 10% Written Assignment (Problem Set)
- 25% 2 Programming Assignments (10% for PA1, 15% for PA2)
- 20% Project
- 35% Final Exam (Passing the course requires passing the exam)

====================

In-class Quizzes (10%):
Being F2F, the in-class quizzes are in Canvas instead of Zoom polling, with one MCQ per quiz. Also, as there are two sections in this semester and both have lectures on Tuesdays and Thursdays, the deadline for each quiz is set to 23:59 on each lecture day. If they keep this setting in the future, you will not miss out on the lecture quizzes. As the settings are not like iPRS, you can argue it does not require attendance. Last but not least, you are only given one attempt for each quiz.

Statistics: As most people got full in the quizzes, you can assume that it is a must to get all the quizzes correct.

====================

Programming Assignments (25%):
About 3 weeks were given to complete each programming assignment. You are required to submit the source code and a written report, where the manual specifies both code tasks and written tasks. For your reference, there were 27 tasks (code + report) in PA1 and about 30 tasks (12 for code, 18 for report) in PA2. Some code tasks and report tasks overlap as they require you to implement (and run) the code and record (and explain) the findings and observations.

- PA1 (10%) covered the topics of Linear Regression, Logistic Regression and MLP
- PA2 (15%) focused on image classification using CNN, as well as style transfer and data augmentation

The PAs were heavily implementation-based so they make sure you are well-trained in terms of ML-related Python libraries such as Sklearn and Tensorflow, as well as some common ones like NumPy, Pandas and Matplotlib. Thus, it would be very challenging for students with little background in these Python libraries. I suggest you take COMP2211 (*insert Desmond advertisement*) before this course, so you get yourself familiarized with the libraries. That said, the depths of the PAs were good, with their workload aside.

A slight issue about the PAs is that the instructions in the manuals were quite vague for some tasks, requiring you to check out Piazza posts constantly for clarifications. That said, the TAs were kind enough to clarify most of the requirements that were questioned. I hope the clarity can be further improved in the future.

Statistics:
(Low, LQ, Median, UQ, High | Mean | Max)
PA1: 0, 89.5, 95, 97, 99.5 | 89.61 | 100
PA2: 0, 88, 98.5, 99.5, 100 | 87.86 | 100

Unless the code implementation has problems, you are most likely to get full marks in the code part, given that you implemented the code correctly and kept the corresponding output in your .ipynb files. In the report part, make sure to present your findings properly.

====================

Written Assignment (Problem Set) (10%):
A total of 8 questions, covering topics from Linear Regression to PCA, were released midway through the semester and given about a month to complete. The questions were mostly extending some of the math equations (such as the generalized linear system in linear regression and the F-measure) or concepts introduced in lectures, along with some simple calculations (e.g., calculating PoV). Overall, the problem set was straightforward.

Statistics:
(Low, LQ, Median, UQ, High | Mean | Max)
0, 88.5, 93, 96.5, 100 | 89.22 | 100

==========

Project (20%):
You are required to form a group of 2 (both in the same section) to propose an ML-related topic on your own and work on it. Considering starting the project right after the deadline of the proposal submission (2%), you are given about 3 weeks to submit the following items:
1. Project report
2. Source code
3. Presentation slides
4. Presentation video (upload to YouTube)
Note that 13% goes to the report and source code and the remaining 5% goes to the presentation-related items.

Statistics:
(Low, LQ, Median, UQ, High | Mean | Max)
Proposal:
95, 100, 100, 100, 100 | 99.49 | 100
Report, Code, Slides, Video:
72.7, 83.28, 86.9, 89.55, 98.3 | 86.58 | 100

Basically, you get full for the proposal if there is no obvious issue.
For the report, DY expects it to be about 20+ pages, so get everything explained in detail (the same goes for the video, which was required to be 15 minutes long in this semester)

==========

Final Exam (35%):
The exam only took 2 hours, consisting of 11 questions. It was close-book and close-note, but a piece of A4 cheatsheet (both sides) was allowed.

The questions were mainly conceptual, with a limited portion of light mathematical calculations (although DY claimed that they could not exclude the possibility of dry-running MLP). Therefore, I would say the exam paper was well-designed and meaningful (as in challenging but not tedious, unlike what happened in COMP4221).

Assuming that everyone got full in the quizzes and the project proposal, we have an overall mean score of 79.5939.
An overall score of approximately 87 yields an A-.

comment_workload:
Workloads are fine for the PAs, which have 2-3 weeks to complete for each. However, note that no skeleton codes are provided and you need to start everything from scratch starting from packages installing and importing. Some labs may be useful for references though.

Each PA took me around two full days to work on the codes and an extra  day for writing up report. If you aim for perfection, make sure to do each step carefully and write your report clearly according to the guidelines. Minor typo in the code may make huge difference or even a complete wrong in your final submission. Start early because random bugs are likely and training time can be long for ML assignments.

The workloads suddenly increased steeply towards the end of the semester, where we have our Project 1 due in week 12 and Project 2 due in week 13. These two projects take some times in report writing and making powerpoint slides for filming the video if you aim for excellence. For me, I worked overnight for two days to complete Project 1.

You will get two NQA coupons (i.e. late days) for the whole semester where you can use on any assignment or project.
comment_grading:
This grading scheme was for an online semester. No exam was scheduled for my semester.  It is not likely to happen for a F2F semester, so please reference to other threads if needed.

PA1 10% - Multi-layer Perceptrons (MLP) on some toy datasets
PA2 15% - Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) on image classifier
PA3 15% - Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) on fake news text detector

Zoom Poll 10% - Each lecture one or two poll(s), half point for incorrect, full point for correct, no point for missed poll

Project 1 30% - Work on one of the topics as asked (Not a "freewill" project). Download the dataset on your own and do whatever models you like. Make a report and video present afterwards. (Group of 2)

Project 2 20% - Extended learning. On your own, read papers on one of the topics asked  (again, not totally free choice), then film a 30-mins youtube video and teach your fellow students on the advanced topic. Video links are released to the rest of the class after the end of semester. (Single-person project)

comment_content:
1. Overview

This is an introductory course on machine learning. In short, you will build models that learn useful patterns from features in dataset.

Teaching Syllabus:
- Artificial neural networks (Perceptron, deep networks, CNN, RNN, GAN)
- K-means Clustering
- PCA (short)
- Q Learning

TL;DR In case you don't have the time to read this 3400+ words review, just remember one thing: Don't register the course when James is teaching.


2. Coverage

If you have taken COMP 4471, congratz! You won't be able to learn anything from the course. The course content is basically a subset of COMP 4471 with a short intro to k-means clustering and PCA.

There are also some common stuffs in a standard ML course that is not covered.
- Regularization Techniques (e.g. dropout, weight decay, ...)
- Model Selection (e.g. bias-variance tradeoff, error metrics)
- Hyperparameter Tuning

Although the content suggested by the other review may be too difficult/theoretical, it would be good to add things like deep Q network or ensembling. Introducing some of the state-of-the-art research also would make the course more interesting.


3. Content Difficulty

The funny thing is although COMP 4471 covered more topics than this course, it covered them in more details. Even in COMP 1942, for the k-mean clustering and the PCA, it covered them step-by-step instead of going through them briefly.

As mentioned in other comments, COMP 4211 do not feel like a 4000-level course. It would be nice to go into more details in some of the parts. For example, it is interesting to see a sketch proof of perceptron convergence theorem (which I did in COMP 4901K).


4. Usefulness

The lectures covered basic concepts of machine learning but it is far enough from a real life machine learning project. It would only be useful if you want to take more machine learning courses in the future. But why bother registering this if you can learn everything from other courses.

Clustering, PCA - In COMP 1942
Q-Learning, RL - In COMP 3211
Perceptron, Deep Learning - In COMP 4901K

The machine learning research field is very active. However, there is minimal discussion on state-of-the-art techniques. Better to take COMP 4471 for that.

If you want to learn the basics on deep learning, take the course by Andrew Ng in coursera (like everyone suggested in the Reddit). You will benefit far more than taking this course.


5. Disconnection between Lectures & Assignments

To be honest, the project would have been a good project if the instruction was clear and the techniques used were introduced in the lecture. It is a good learning experience to implement a fast R-CNN from scratch (with PyTorch). However, this is only possible given the following:
- I took the coursera Deep Learning and Tensorflow in practice specialization beforehand.
- I had some (but not a lot) experience tuning hyperparamenters from the COMP 4901K project.
- I got a decent computer at home (i5-4460+GTX960, not too powerful but suffice).

The lecture does not cover any of the following:
- hyperparameter tuning (how to tune? what to tune? techniques?)
- debugging deep models (gradient checking? try a small dataset? visualization?)
- potential issues and their solution (vanishing/exploding gradient, dying ReLU)
- dealing with large datasets (how to scale up? baselines?)

How are we suppose to complete the project without learning anything above? Also, it would be much better if some object detection concepts (e.g. object proposal) are covered in the lecture.

P.S. I was taking COMP 3211, COMP 4421, COMP 4901K in the same semester. I did not take other machine learning related course (including COMP 4471) in any semesters.

comment_workload:
1. Demanding Final Project & HW

The final project (topic 2) is an object detection system with Fast R-CNN. We have to follow the Fast R-CNN paper (with some modifications) to implement a turbulence detection system. The main overheads are the following:
- We have zero idea of Fast R-CNN before the project (so we need to read the papers)
- The instructions are vague, ambiguous and sometimes contradictory
- Object Proposal generation is time-consuming
- We don't have enough manpower and CPU/GPU power to debug everything

We spent at least 6 days full-time working on the project. Two or three days are even in the exam period! We would consider ourselves as some of the top students in the CS department (at least in terms of GPA). Even we put so much effort in the project, we did not manage to get a good result. Anyway, it is the best among all groups. I feel so tired and stressed out for the project. I don't wanna touch it anymore.

I will post the link to our project repo and powerpoint after obtaining permission from my groupmates.

The HW is also quite long if you do try to spend time tuning the hyperparameters. However, the effort seems not to be credited as the final performance is not calculated in the marking scheme. (although it is mentioned in the instructions the performance will be considered)


2. Deadlines on Unusual Days

The deadline of the final project is the last day of the exam period (22nd Dec). Glad that the final of my last subject is on 17th Dec so I can spend a few days full-time on the project. It would have been okay if the project had been released early. Sadly, it is released in late November so you have to deal with a shit heavy workload project alongside with your exam and end-of-semester work.


3. Required Attendance

Attend the lecture or lose 10%. And that 10% difference will probably cost you a nice letter grade. :) The prof/TA didn't mention how the attendance is calculated so you are forced to attend every lecture to prevent point deduction. Combined with the fact that the lecture is boring and without verbal interactions, it feels quite tiring to attend all the lectures.

The craziest thing is the professor move the last 4 lectures (presentation sections) to Dec 24 (Christmas Eve). Attendance is compulsory and we have a peer review form to fill in. That means, we have to listen to presentations of the same two topic for 5 hours and grade each of them. Great Christmas gift! Thank you professor!


4. Writing Follow-up Emails

I don't want to count how many emails that we have sent to the TAs/professor. Sometimes, they like holding emails. Sometimes, they like to rephrase our questions and treat that as an answer. We got to a point that feeling sending emails asking for clarifications won't help at all. So, we implemented some parts of our project not following the exact instructions to make it work (or work better).


5. Writing Grade Review Email (Updated: Jan 24 2021)

We have sent a grade review request email to the department due to the unfair grading scheme of the project (refer to Grading section for details). It took us almost 5 days to collect data, drafting the email, asking people to sign, etc. To be honest, I feel so exhausted that I still have to deal with this fu*king course after the end of semester.

Link to email:
https://docs.google.com/document/d/118up9RW5ZoThLqWPuZkfD0XWIGr9Fq1UQjJRM3av8q8/edit?usp=sharing
(Names are removed for privacy reason)

Previously, we put our contact method in this review for fellow victims to provide statistics, and sign the letter if they want. I would like to say a big THANK YOU to those who have provided statistics to us, especially those who have helped us signing the email. We finally manage to have 9 fellow students (out of 78) signing the email.

To start off, we got a bullshit response by James. The department simply forward our email to James, and ask him to respond to the points. Let me translate his response: "I think my scheme is good. You dumb as*es won't get your grades changed too much even if I change the scheme anyway." Attached is his reply (Warning: Please take care of your mental health. Seek help if needed). We filed another request for the issue that our teammate dropping out of the course after the group formation deadline. And, we got an even more ridiculous response from James (He said we should hire another freerider for better grades WTF). No other response from the department.

I was fu*king hopeless at that point. It was going to end horribly. There were argues among our group. My groupmate even sent a not-so-polite email to ask the department for a fair judgement (which I can't completely agree). Clearly, James is a very clever "old seafood" (slang in Cantonese, meaning who have lots of experience in the workplace).

The department responded us that they has just started conducting initial investigation. They said it was hard to get James' response. lol The department called a meeting (without James) to listen to our POV & evidence. Then, there were no news ... until I found my grade updated in SIS a week after.

We didn't really receive any official response from the department/James for the grade review request. The result is far from ideal and far from what we have expected (refer to grading section [5]). Sorry again for those who are still getting a bad grade. I don't have the energy to follow up this bullshit... I need some rest (and time to do my FYP. Sorry Desmond! :C)


6. It was just a Blackhole (Updated: Jan 13 2021)

All in all, this course was a fu*king blackhole. You put in effort, you got back nothing. You want grades? Don't put in efforts. Don't try to make sound argument with James. Pray to god gifting you a freerider in your group (which can easily pull your grades to 100) was enough.

I gave up my exam revision time. I gave up my Christmas Eve holiday. I gave up time doing my FYP. I gave up my sleeping time. The time that I spent in this course was way more than I did in COMP 2012H (which is famous for high workload). And... sigh... I don't know what to say anymore.


7. Writing Course Review (Updated: Jan 9 2021)

I spent a lot of time writing this review. Please share it with your friends so there will be less people suffering from this kind of bullshxt. If you did take the course with us or COMP 4331 this semester, you can help by giving (anonymous) feedback to the university.

http://qa.ust.hk/student_feedback.html

Lastly, I would like to thank each of you who had helped to upvote and share the review. Without your help, we would not be able to find fellow victims to provide statistics. And, this review would not have been that impactful.
comment_grading:
0. Preface

Please leave me a message if you find a course that has worse grading than this one. I will go say sorry to James.


1. Grading Distribution

I think I don't need to say anything about how bullshit is this grading distribution. The numbers speak for themselves. Highest score in group project + full points everything else (98.2% overall) => A. Do you think 96.2% a good score? It is an A-. :D

My score and statistics:
Assignment 1 (10%): 10/10 (Mean: 9.3)
Assignment 2 (10%): 9/10 (Mean: 6.9)
Assignment 3 (10%): 10/10 (Mean: 8.1)
Mini Project (20%): 20/20 (Mean: 17)
Final Project (40%): 95.5/100 (Mean: 65.3)
Participation (10%): 3/3 (Mean: 2.6)
Overall: 97.2% => A
(Statistics from Canvas, other stats such as SD are not released)
(Note: 95.5 is the highest group score for the project mentioned by the TA in email)

Groupmate 1:
Assignments, Mini-project & Participation: 60/60
Final Project: 95.5/100
Overall: 98.2% => A

Groupmate 2:
Assignments, Mini-project & Participation: 58/60
Final Project: 95.5/100
Overall: 96.2% => A-

A friend from another group:
Assignments, Mini-project & Participation: 57/60
Final Project: 83/100
Overall: 90.2% => B+

More from this goop thread: https://hk.goop.ai/topic/ImuK/James-kwok-%E5%A5%BD%E7%88%9Bgrade

Hall of Poor Grades:
85 B-
86 B
87.5 B
89 B
89.74 B+
90.2 B+
90.5 B+
92 B+
92.24 B+
96 A-
96.2 A-
96.5 A-
97 A
97.2 A
98.2 A
(From PMs and goop)


2. Effort Required to Achieve the Grade

Please refer to the workload section. Want bad grades? Want stress? Good. Take this course. Work on the course materials full time and get a nice letter A.


3. Grading Transparency

There is no information regarding to the final project grading, which takes up 40% of the whole course. We have zero idea on how it is graded. The TAs try to avoid answering anything related to the grading (refer to attachments). Is it really true that we need a perfect score to get an A+? No one will ever know.


4. Contribution Scheme (Updated: Jan 5 2021)

From the new information that we have collected, we found there is a project score adjustment for groups that have unfair contributions. The formula is

individual score = min(100, group score * (self contribution/expected contribution))

The problem lies in a person can get a score much higher than the group score if the contribution is very unfair. This situation is not hypothetical. There is a 4-person group that has the following result:

Member A: Contribution 38.04% Personal project score 100
Member B: Contribution 22.57% Personal project score 76.84
Member C/D omitted as it is not important to the calculation
Group Project Score: 76.84 / 90.29% = 85.1
(Names are anonymous for privacy reason)

With some simple calculations, we can find Member A can get 129.49 points in the project if there is no cap (maximum of 100 score) applied! With such formula, Member A still get 100 points when their group project score is lowered to 65.72!

So, what went wrong? (in case you are still not convinced that there is a problem)

a) The effort and the score that can be obtained is NOT a linear relationship. For example, if you choose proj1, you can easily get >60 score by spending three or four hours modifying a pretrained convnet without much tuning. (I received a PM saying he soloed proj1 in one day and get 70+.) However, if you aim for the 80~90 range, you must spend quite a few days tuning the model or implement a much complicated model (proj2).

b) Groups with less than 4 person are severely penalized. According to the formula, for a 3 person group, each member are expected to take up 33.33% work each instead of 25% work. Our group was a 4 person group but one of our members dropped the course (smart choice!) so it becomes three. We pretty much have to do 25.0%/3=8.33% extra work without any rewards. Not to mention the results of our project would be much better if there is a fourth member helping us.

c) It is impossible to get a group score of 100. There is a peer review component and it is impossible to get everyone to give you full points. As a member of the highest scored group, I would say it is impossible to get a significantly better result given the short time period.

Do you understand what's the problem now? Ironically, the professor seems to be confused (or didn't even care/read) after we sent a long explanation to him (at least 4x longer than above). Refer to attachment for evidence.


5. Grade Review (Updated: Jan 24 2021)

We have sent two separate grade review requests regrading the grading:
a) the unfair contribution scheme (as mentioned above, refer to workload section [5] for details)
b) groups that have student(s) dropping out of the course are expected to contribute more (not calculated as freeriders but less members)

In short (more on workload section [5]), the grade review request is partially success and all members in my group have 1 subgrade up. We didn't find anyone else having their grades updated (plz let me know if you do).

Updated grades:
Myself (97.2%): A => A+
Groupmate 1 (98.2%): A => A+
Groupmate 2 (96.2%): A- => A
We failed to find other student who have their grade updated.

Although our group had our grades updated, I think this is still far from ideal. I apologize to those who have helped us fighting back the grades. We failed to get you all having a fair grade which I feel bad. I don't feel happy at all getting back the A+. To be honest, I don't really care if I do not get back the grade (that subgrade won't pull me up a lot anyway). We filed the request because the scheme is unfair - everyone should be assessed in a fair way. Most importantly, we want that stupid stubborn James to know HE IS WRONG.

I will keep the grading as "E" due to the following:

a) The scheme is still not transparent. We don't know how the revamped scheme work. I only received an email saying my final grade is updated to A+. I didn't receive other information regarding the appeal result.

b) The grading cutoff is still high. My teammate who got 96.2% total still results in an A. One may say there are courses with similar cutoff. However, the points can be easily deducted due to careless mistakes in assignments. I got 1% of course grade (10% of an assignment) deducted due to a single line of function call missing.

c) It is still unfair. It is understandable that it is not desirable to update the grades of everyone (especially those who may get their grades down). Despite that, there are much more thing that James and the TAs can do. A mass email explaining the situation to everyone? A less strict cutoff to balance the problem of the scheme? James did NONE of the these. He didn't even bother to solve anything before the department told him to.

comment_content:
In short: This course should be renamed "SVM and a little bit of some other stuff" and made into a 2000-level course.

The course description mentioned much more content, but here are what were actually teached:
- SVM
- Neural network
- Reinforcement learning
- K-means clustering
AND THAT'S ALL.

Only SVM is taught with a 4000-level difficultly and depth.
Neural network: Perceptrons, adaline, deeper network and computational graph, CNN. These are fine. Questions like "how to combat overfitting", "why CNN over fully connected" are not answered.
RL: Policy/value iteration + Q learning.

There are SO MANY THINGS that I can think of and worth teaching in a 4000-level ML course: VC theorem, PCA, actor-critic, Boltzmann machine, etc.

My suggestion: Cut the CNN part and leave it for 4901J. Teach faster, please. You are teaching year 3/4 CS students, not kindergarten kids.
comment_teaching:
Lecture: Teaches very slowly with unnecessarily and sometimes stupid questions. I think the instructor tries to make the class more active but honestly he is wasting too much time on that. Didn't go after midterm.

Lecture notes: Unstructured and over-simplified. Might be usable in a classroom interaction setting but putting more details definitely helps with revision.
comment_workload:
Workload is not heavy. Assignments are unnecessarily complicated on non-important parts. Final exam is ridiculous.

Assignment 2/3: No skeleton code. Wasting time to build structure of loading data, training network and validation instead of the actual algorithm.

Exam: Full of numerical calculation. More than half of the questions are basically "dry run this algorithm and write all intermediate steps".
Another question is "here is a neural network with weird kernel size, padding and stride, compute its output size at every layer" and there are 6 layers. Part b: Now some those kernel size, padding and stride are changed. Do the exact same thing again.
There is more. Question: Draw the computational graph. Little did I know, he expected a graph with ~30 nodes with detail for every single multiplication and addition.

The exam is designed for a computer, not a human. Questions related to ML concepts accounts for I think ~10% of the total marks. Ridiculous.

comment_content:
1. Introduction (e.g. feature extraction, supervised vs unsupervised learning, multi-class learning)

2. Linear Support vector machine (SVM)

3. nonlinear SVM (kernel)

4. overfitting (cross validation)

5. perceptron (AND/OR/XOR)

6. Adaptive linear element (adaline)
[HIGHLIGHT: stochastic gradient descent]

7. hidden units (nonlinear RBF, sigmoid unit, ReLU)

8. back-propagation

9. convolutional neural network (CNN, LeNet)

10. introduction to Reinforcement learning (Markov decision process)

11. policy and policy evaluation

12. policy iteration, value iteration

13. Q learning

14. generalization and function approximation

The rating is given as the content is hard. Please do not underestimate its difficulty. You may not easily build AlphaGo in a course just like Rome wasn't built in a day.

A lot more basic machine learning (ML) concepts, such as regularization, regression, rule-based learning/classification, Naive Bayes classification, kNN, k-means & hierarchical clustering, decision tree, random forest, hidden Markov model are not covered in this course because several lessons are cancelled. It is quite a pity that genetic algorithms, recurrent neural network (RNN) and LSTM are not included here.

comment_grading:
Well what can I say for this? See the earlier post if you want to know the full story. Long story short, the entire grading policy simply could not differentiate students by their understanding on the course material.

Grading scheme: (Mean/LQ/Median/UQ)
15% Assignment 1 (90.8/97/98/100)
15% Assignment 2 (88.11/85/100/100)
20% Project (83.21/90/90/100)
20% Midterm (97.74/96.75/100/100)
30% Final (42.17/37/42.5/51)

As you can see, this is a sh*tty distribution. Everyone just get 100 in every single part of the course except in the final exam where the prof decides to screw us all over. Made a careless mistake in your assignment? Say goodbye to your A/B range.

The "project" is simply running a given python script and write several lines of code yourself, definitely not the PA kind of work.

The final exam is so legendary that I will never forget. The moment I went into the exam venue and sit down at the chair, I was surprised by the sheer thickness of the exam paper which is supposed for a 3-hour examination. Turns out it was double-sided and ends up with like 40 pages in total, which already seem quite dramatic for a 3-hour exam.

The content of the exam is purely evil and designed to screw us over. Have you tried doing a matrix multiplication? Then you know how annoying it is to do that for higher dimension matrix such as 4x4. Guess what? You need to do it multiple times just for a single sub-question for 5 marks in the scaled dot-product attention part. Basically you need to do a 4x4 x 4x4, then apply softmax, and do a 4x4 x 4x4 again! At this point, you have done (7+4+7)*16 = 288 add/multiply and applied the exponential functions for 20 times! Congrats, you earned yourself 3 marks now! Now, do it for a few more times for the other sub-questions ^_^

You think that is it? Try BPE for ~15-20 characters and 5 lines of text and run it for 8 iterations and that would grant you another 3 points ^_^

Did you recite the pseudo-code for multiple algorithms such as graph-based dependency parsing and apply it to 15 nodes interconnecting with 4-5 arrows with one another that you can barely see which arrow is which one? Or did you recite that Naive Bayes classifier algorithm in pseudo-code? No? Too bad, 30 marks gone.

Don't want to handle codes? No problem, let's circle back to math, just draw a SIMPLE computational graph for a 2-2-1 MLP structure with a maximum of two items for each inter-step (e.g. v1 = w1x1 + w2x2 + b1 would need to split into 4 nodes: a=w1x1, b=w2x2, c=a+b, d=c+b1, imagine how many nodes you would generate for the entire MLP structure with just a simple 2-2-1, just imagine you are a CPU running assembly code), and write the respective partial differentiation for like 30 nodes. For this you would get 2 points!

The entire lecture theater is just filled with gasps, sighs and non-stop cursing. To be honest I have no idea what the final grading will be like, or what will happen next. At this point I don't think there's anything they can do to justify what they did to us in the final exam.

comment_grading:
The most disastrous part of the course is definitely the assessment and grading.

The grading scheme is as follows:
30% Written Assignments (15% each)
20% Midterm Exam
20% Course Project
30% Final Exam

Somehow, each and every part of this grading scheme went wrong.

The written assignments were the least disastrous parts of the entire assessment scheme, although there were trivial in the sense that much of what is asked of you should really be done by a computer instead of being done by hand. Assignment 1 had you count bigrams and calculating probabilities by hand - extremely tedious and could be done by computers. Assignment 2 was worse - you had to calculate multiple matrix products, which could definitely be done better by a computer. In fact, most of us used Numpy to do assignment 2, which clearly showed that the assignments were of limited utility.

The midterm exam was a landmark in the history of "worst midterm exams". It was a direct copy of Assignment 1 (with a few numerical tweaks), with the relatively more difficult question completely deleted. The midterm was scheduled for an hour - most people walked out after half an hour. The low difficulty ended up creating one of the most impressive box plots I've seen - the median for the midterm was 100.

Let me repeat: The midterm exam of a 4000-level COMP course has a median of 100. That is perhaps one of the most impressive things in my 3 years of study here. I've attached the box plot for your reference (I have set the what-if score to 0).

The course project is another example of poor assessment design. 20 marks were awarded for simply running the given code. Another 50 to 60 marks are for modifying 1-2 lines of code. Although the project did give us some experience with NLP in Python, what we learnt was effectively absolutely nothing - given that the code was given, and none of the libraries used were ever explained in any depth whatsoever.

All of the above (constituting 70% of the course marks) had a median of 100. This means that 50% of all students (roughly) had full marks before the final. This makes it impossible to distribute grades. So what does Prof Wei do?

He sets the most insane final exam that I have had the misfortune of sitting through. At least for COMP 3021 (Prof Shen version), the questions were hard but definitely conceptually interesting. The paper was fair as it at least examined how clear your concepts are. Prof Wei's paper occupied the other end of the difficulty spectrum, falling squarely into the field of brutally and unnecessarily hard due to sheer tedium. The final featured 8 questions, and was ~40 pages long since the paper was double-side printed. The questions were designed to be tedious and impossible to complete in the allotted period of time. One of the questions asked us to dry run the BPE algorithm 8 times. Another asked us to multiply 4x4 matrices together, manually apply softmax to it, and multiply it with another matrix again. Repeat these steps 3-4 times with minor modifications, and you will have gained a total of 15 marks out of 100, despite the number of numerical calculations required. Another 15 mark question required you to write down the pseudocode for Naive Bayes twice with minor modifications, and dry run it on 5 sentences. The level of number crunching required in the exam was extraordinary, and completely irrelevant to the course aims. Some of the questions also required you to recite some useless definitions (I don't think anyone needs to know the definition of a language model, nor do they need to recite the psuedocode of the Naive Bayes classifier). The exam felt like a useless trivia memorization test and a human calculation test stapled together in 20 sheets of paper - an extremely poor reflection of our ability.

Needless to say, I am deeply disappointed by the course grading scheme.

"Fun" notes:
I heard at least half the hall swear at the exam paper during the final (that includes me). Someone shouted "Professor, do you hate us?" at the end of the paper. It was one of the loudest exams I've sat through - mainly because everyone is swearing to some degree.

I have attached the estimated LQs and UQs, as well as the official mean and SD.

Update post-solution release: I am literally speechless.
- Doing 3 4x4 matrix multiplications: 1/100;
- A 4-page solution: 3/100;
- Making a 27x27 table with different probabilities: 2/100.
Well done!

I am incensed by the released solution: Prof Wei didn't even bother typing out any of the definition questions. For the 4x4 matrices he used Numpy (when we could not). This made me question whether he actually did his paper after setting it. Well done!!

comment_workload:
DO NOT TAKE THIS COURSE!

Mid-term/Final:
No mid term and final for this semester, so you don't need to memorize how Dekai Wu is spelt. (To be honest, I think every CS student in HKUST knows his name since he was quite infamous for his teaching, just look at the other reviews)

Assignments:
Assignments are due in one week ~ 1.5 weeks, and it is quite hard to finish them in time especially if you are year 4 with FYP or took a lot of courses in that semester.

Assignment 1: Dijkstra Anagram
Assignment requirement is to write a program to do Dijkstra algorithm for generating anagram.
This first assignment is okay, not too difficult.
But for getting the 5 points bonus it is quite hard, since your program need to run fast enough within 20s for 14 character length word, which required you to use some sort of pruning to achieve that efficiency.

Assignment 2: HMM
Assignment requires to write a program for Hidden Markov Model backward forward algorithm.
This assignment is hard, since the concept and equations of backward and forward algorithm and HMM is quite difficult and complicated. So you need to spend a lot of time to understand it.
And with the complicated equations, debugging takes a long time.

Assignment 3: Feedforward POS Tagger
Assignment requirement is to loop through the scientific method for feed forward part of speech tagger.
This assignment is easy in terms of coding, since for every hypothesis, just a few lines of code is needed to be changed.
The annoying part is that you would need to write observations of the training set, description of hypothesis, and do error analysis for each hypothesis.
And because I didn't know whether the answers are correct or not, I tried to write as long as possible for the description and explanation, which I used a lot of time to do. I don't know whether it is necessary to write so long, but in the end I got 100/100

Assignment 4: RNN
Assignment requirement: create bidirectional RNN, LSTM, and bidirectional LSTM model for tagging part of speech for the training set with the starting code of RNN.
I would say this is the most useless and annoying assignment because the machine learning framework used in this assignment is LEGO, which is developed by Dekai and his TAs.
Why the hell would you ask the students to use C++ and your own LEGO machine learning framework, but not python with state-of-the-art machine learning framework such as pyTorch or Tensorflow?
Not to mention that because the framework is developed by Dekai and his TAs, there are no documentation that you can refer to understand how LEGO actually works.
So you would spend a lot of time trying to understand the starting code with the comments, do trial and error to understand how different function works and the output of different variables.
Basically, this assignment teaches you to use LEGO machine learning framework, which you would never ever use again in your whole life. (Unless you decide to do postgrad and become his TA)
