comment_workload:
The most heaviest workload course in HKUST. Even heavier than all LANG courses or SOSC and HUMA common core. There a three assignments.
The first assignment will waste you about 5 days to complete.
The second assignment will waste you about a week to complete it.
After the deadline of second assignment, the third assignment will release. The workload is three times of the second assignment, which mean you have to use about 3 weeks to complete a single assignment individually.
Even worse, there are many system maintenance. Your assignment progress will force to stop during maintenance. Eventually, the time for you to complete the assignment will reduce.
Another serious issue is an assignment will divide to three parts. Each part will release each week, which mean you cannot finish the next part before it release. So, you won't be able to plan finishing all parts during its release.
As assignment 3 is released at the last of the semester. you will be very busy.

comment_content:
The content of this course is supposed to be rich, giving students ideas what NLP is.

There are 3 assignments, midterm and final in the course.

The assignment in this course is full of idealism that Dekai wishes the TA to design yet the TA has no time to do it at all. I personally felt bad for the TA as
1. The TA don't even have any materials sent by Dekai
2. The TA is asked by Dekai to asked question that he never mention and define in class (Search Bias), so basically the TA doesn't know what does it mean either.

All in all, there are 3 assignments in this course for this semester, in which assignment 1 was a easy and basic one, and I already get lost in assignment 2 and 3 as I have no way of catching up and understanding his ideas.

As a sidenote, the assignment 3 was broken up into Part A, B and C and suppose we would hand in part by part. This caused a lot of confusion to the class at first and the deadline was unclear at the first.

The Midterm consists of discussion in class about ethnics and some basic hazards he thinks AI could do to humanity (not strictly related to NLP but that's his style), some POS tagging which he spend only one lecture and giving no exercise to us and suppose we will achieve 100% marks from that after weeks of introduction. Seriously is this set up to fail us? I don't know, but most of us can't even understand the POS tag basics during that lecture.

Let's be clear that I did not study for the final because I have already given up the course by that point. It consists of a lot of pseudo programming using C++ syntax and also some question yet again discussed in class.

comment_content:
Machine Learning in graphs.

It covers graphs from basics like graph representations, nodes/edges, bipartite graphs, directed/undirected, strongly connected components. This part is similar to COMP3711.

After that, it will move on to more about graph features, such as node centrality, degree and closeness. This part is similar to COMP4641. It also covers graph isomorphism (like what you learnt in high school chemistry).

Then, it goes deeper into the unique contents of this course. It covers graphs embeddings, using shallow encoding, random walks, etc.

After that, Graph Neural Networks. CNN, RNN and LSTM are briefly covered and more focus are on GNNs, including message propagation and aggregation. Graph Convolutional Layers are also introduced. This part is similar to COMP5214 or equivalent.

Then, more on analysing GNN, including powerfulness of different aggregation methods, how to encode relations, etc.

After that, homogenous graphs and knowledge graphs, which are just extension of the previous topics having different labels at nodes.

Finally, Recommender Systems using statistical and graphs representation. This part is similar to COMP4332.

At the end of the course, it is wrapped up with self-supervised learning in graphs and Generative Adversarial Networks. This lecture just briefly covered the topics and this part is similar to COMP5214.


Comments:
Allow me to make an analogy here. You entered a Nike, and it sells you apples, but the apples are quite delicious.

The topic of this course is called Machine Learning with Structured Data. I was quite excited when enrolling into this course coz I was thinking I will learn the representations of different data, probably on some smart databases or whatever, then further use Machine Learning to analyse the “structured data”. While it is true that graphs is undeniably one of the most important data structures, and graphs can generalise many things, I still think it will be too ridiculous to cover only graphs neural networks but not others in this course.

Probably that’s because the professor shifted his research focus to graphs. Formerly he loves NLP and I doubt whether this course would be all texts and RNN and Bi-LSTM if it was offered before, lol.

However, having that said. I still like the course contents. Just because a Nike shop sells apples, that does not mean the apples are bad. Indeed, there is no any other course in HKUST covers graphs machine learning in such depth and it is pretty interesting yet potentially useful. The most mimic course maybe COMP4641 but not much deep GNNs are covered there. COMP5214 just covers graphs in only 1-2 lectures only.

Therefore, if you are here to learn general ML, don’t take this course, as images, texts, numerical and statistical data are all omitted in this course. However, if you already had prior MLs in CV or NLP, and is eager to learn more on the social and graphs aspects, you are recommended to take this course.
comment_teaching:
Chen Qifeng’s style of teaching I would say. I have taken Qifeng’s 5214 before and I find their teaching styles very similar. Mainland students may think them enjoyable due to many mathematical formulas on slides and a thick chinese accent.

I personally lose concentration quite easily due to the professor’s monotone reading aloud and complex formula in the slides that probably take me much more time to digest compared with his time used to explain the maths behind.

He is willing to answer questions, same as Qifeng, and loves to chat with students (usually in Putonghua) after classes on the concepts that he has covered.

I can tell he is intelligent and enthusiastic, but that does not necessarily make him a good instructor. Indeed, his logistics announcements are a bit vague and it is hard to guess what being required for each task. comment_workload:
It can be light or high, depending on you. And most ridiculously, it does not affect your final grade!

Since you can get full in reading notes even you wrote 3 sentences (but I wrote 5+ pages with proper citations for each note). Ofc, I got full, just everyone does too.

For the project, I spent three overnights for coding and two more overnights for documenting my report. I made my report pretty with proper formatting and citations. Yet, seemingly you get 9x score whatsoever.

So, the actual workloads really depends on how much you want to learn and efforts you like to pay. Grading is not a concern of your workload.

Oh, and in terms of learning, it is more about “research in this field”. If you like doing research and reading others’ papers, then this course is for you. But if you want more applications, maybe through programming assignments, then I am sorry to tell you that there is no any PA throughout the semester. I would say this part is even worse than Chen Qifeng’s class because you can still learn a lot from its PAs regardless of his poor lecture delivery. Of course, the PAs in Qifeng’s are designed by Stanford or MIT (that may be even better tho).

If this course is to be offered again, I would really suggest changing the reading notes to PAs for students to do some practical work to make sure they can catch up the lectures, and grading the projects more seriously to better differentiate students’ ability other than solely the final exam.

Talking about the final exam, it asks more than half of the problems on “how many parameters”, which is basically English reading comprehension over a passage and making careful conclusions. For the remaining half, they are more related to the lecture concepts, graph theories or a few points the the professor highlighted in class. So you can still make some revisions for the final.

The final exam is at least fair at the end of the day.
comment_grading:
Good grading indeed. I am not sure about the exact grading distribution but he said he will give max A-range allowed.

However, the grading scheme is a bit nonsense and that’s why I give a “B” here.

15% reading notes x 3 (5% each)
35% project code and report
10% project presentation
40% final exam

Reading notes and project codes are due the same day in mid-Nov where almost everyone got full in reading notes. It seems the TAs do not really mark them but give full credits for efforts. I would have put more time on project if having known that.

Project report is also a case where many groups fall in similar range of scores. The highest was 98, upper quartile was 97, my group got 96 and the mean was 94.8.

Project presentation is probably one thing that differentiate students, where I got 93/100 with no statistics released. However, this is not a LANG course so I am not sure why it should be the case.

Nonetheless, the final exam is the major differentiator. I scored 83 where the UQ was 78 and the mean was 62.2
**Note: the final exam is pretty tough indeed. I wrote 3-hours non-stop and my brain hurts after it.

From what you can see, my final score should be at around the top 20 percentile of the class. And having such score maps to a straight “A” but not “A-“. Very good grade indeed.

Yet, there is no assignments, no other quizzes to differentiate students’ ability. If one plays luck and does well in the final, he may get A+. Therefore, I do not like about the grading in terms of the scheme, even though I got the second best possible letter grade.

comment_content:
Despite that the 'official' previous course code is COMP4901K, which is 'DL for NLP', the content of this course is totally different from 4901K.

Below are the list of contents:
1. Course Intro and Graph Representation (Stuff from COMP2711H/3711)
2. Node level and edge level feature design (what happens before DL, feature engineering)
3. PageRank (just make sure you understand eigen-vectors and power iteration)
4. Graph Laplacian (This shit is pretty hard and requires decent Linear Algebra knowledge)
5. Network Representation Learning (Shallow embedding stuff, basically a bridge lecture before diving into GNNs)
6. GNN (mainly GCN + GraphSAGE)
7. Spectral domain understanding of GCN (relies heavily on Graph Laplacian and Graph Fourier Transform, I personally think this is the hardest lecture)
8. General GNN framework
9. GIN (most expressive msg passing GNN)
10. Heterogenous graph and Knowledge graph (RGCN, TransE, TransR, DistMult, etc.)
11-12. Recommender Systems
13. Advanced topic (Adversarial attacks + SSL)

Most of the content is copied from CS224W from Stanford (just like COMP4471 copies from CS231N), and mainly focuses on ML on graphs (GNN, etc.). Since it's mostly from Stanford, the content is really decent, in depth, and practical. The professor did add something that was not covered in CS224w, and that is the spectral domain part. And it turns out to be the hardest part.

I don't think the official prerequisite is really helpful, just make sure you know the followings then you'll be fine:
* Graph Representation (COMP2711H/3711)
* Basic ML concepts (COMP4211/4471 or any ML course; I think COMP2211 might even be enough)
* Linear Algebra (MATH2111/2121/2131)
comment_teaching:
Yangqiu is really a rare young assistant/associate professor who is mainly engaged in scientific research but can still give lectures seriously and clearly. Many young and bright assistant/associate professors just really can't teach (and I have taken several of those kind of courses, it's completly self-learning), they just focus on their resarch and does a terrible job in delivering lectures.

Yangqiu can explain concepts clearly and logically. And his English is pretty good. After each lecture, as long as you stay focused, it's not hard to get the takeaway. If there are questions, yangqiu is willing to answer all of them after class as well. In fact, this semester, after every lecture, there is always a heated discussion and Q&A session between yangqiu and a huge bunch of students. (I personally quite enjoy this atomsphere, it's really been a long time since I last experienced this in a university course)

The TAs also did a great job in preparing the tutorials. And their English is decently good as well. Man it feels great to attend lectures/tutorials where the presenter knows how to speak English
comment_workload:
The workload is pretty small except for the project. Basicaly all the workload lies in Final Revision + Project Completion. And if you chose a simple project topic, then basically the only workload is to revise for the final.

I do think that the workload should be slightly intensified by adding some homework or PA. I mean, CS224W actually has both written assignments and PA. Without these, sometimes I really don't know if I understood the materials decently or not. There lacks a way for self evaluation. And this was particularly frustrating when preparing for the final exam since we had no pastpaper considering this is the first offering. The homework or PA could have served as a reference for us to self-evaluate our understanding and do some excercise for preparing for the exam.
comment_grading:
I think the grading is just normal.

Scheme:
Reading notes 15%
Project code and report 35%
Final project presentation 10%
Final exam 40%
(That's right, no HW or PA. Reading Notes refer to reading notes after reading papers.)

Probably most students get full in reading notes.
As for the project and presentation, not everyone gets full, but the standard deviation is very small in a way that the difference between the upper quartile and lower quartile is rougly just 5 points out of 100. (However my group got slightly below mean for the project which I really don't understand. I think we had chosen a meaningful project topic with a dencent amount of coding and experiments and analysis, I don't quite understand why we would fall in the 'below-mean' region. But since the std is very small and not differentiating among students, we didn't regard it as a big deal. Confused though....)

As usuall, what determine the grades is mainly the final exam. The professor said that he will give nearly 25% A range, and most students will get B, only a few will get C or below.

(============= correction ==============)
Ok it seems that what determine the grades is not just the final exam.
One of my group mates, who got 80 in the final exam, got a B+, but another student (not in the same group with me), who also got 80 in the final exam but received 'high' in the project report got an A. So the bins are extremely tight and even just a few more points in any of the components can boost your subgrade.

It turns out that the cutoff for A range was 89.5. And the A range given was almost 25%. My poor group mate got a B+ with an overall of 89.45 :(

I personally know more than 10 students in this class, all of whom are top students. Considering there are in total only 58 students yet the A range is strictly controlled under 25%, it's exceedingly competitive. (In Chinese: SO 卷)

comment_content:
Contents were somewhat of a mixed bag - some of the content was quite applicable to other fields such as machine learning, but some of it is rather useless even in the context of search engines or information retrieval.

The contents are as follows:
Introduction to Search Engines (SE) for Web and Enterprise Data
- Types of SE (Federated, Meta, etc)
- History of SE
SE Business Model
- Advertising (Adsense, Adword, etc)
- Business History of SE
TFxIDF*
- Retrieval Models (Boolean, Vector Space)
- Computing TF, DF, IDF
Vector Space Model*
- Similarity Measures (Cosine, Jaccard, Dice, etc)
- Term Weights and Term Independence
Inverted Files*
- Indexing and Postings Lists
- Insertion and Deletion Speed of Inverted Files
- Extensions for Inverted Files
Extended Boolean Model
- Soft Boolean Operators
- P-norm Model
PageRank*
- History of Ranking Algorithms (WISE, HyPursuit)
- The PageRank Algorithm
HITS Algorithm*
- Authority and Hub
- Convergence Criteria
- Normalization of Results
Performance Evaluation of Information Retrieval (IR)*
- Evaluation Metrics (F-measure, Precision, Recall, Fallout)
- Graphs of Evaluation Metrics (Precision-Recall)
- Other Metrics (Novelty Ratio, Mean Average Precision, etc)
Benchmarking
- TREC
Stopword Removal and Stemming
- Affix Removal (Porter's Algorithm)
- Copora-Based Statistical Stemming (Peak-and-Plateau, Entropy, etc)
Relevance Feedback*
- Query Modification
- Document Modification
- Manual, Automatic, Explicit, Implicit Feedback
Personalized Web Search*
- Eye-tracking Experiments
- Clickthrough Analysis
- Preference Mining (Click > Skip Above, Click > Skip Previous, etc)
Index Term Selection
- Zipf's Law
- Term Discrimination Value
Discovering Phrases and Correlated Terms (See COMP 4221 :( )
- N-gram Models
- Collocation and Cocurrence
- Pointwise Mutual Information
Enterprise SE
- Enterprise Challenges
- Requirements of Enterprise SE

I've marked the topics which Kenneth mentioned might be long questions with an asterisk (*).

If you enjoy learning how search engines work, this might be very interesting for you - for me it was mostly OK, but not very useful information. There are a lot of details about tradeoffs of SE design which were not examined, making revision very painful.
comment_teaching:
Teaching does not exist per se. I did not watch the recorded videos as they were similar to COMP 3111(H) in style and quality. The slides themselves were OK (at least they communicated what they were supposed to) and you can use them to complete most of the post-lecture quizzes.

The TAs were mostly responsive to my emails, but the specification for the project was still quite unclear, and required multiple questions to clear up. The element of subjectivity still makes me give this a C.

Kenneth himself only appeared three times throughout the course - once for the introductory session, once for the final Q&A, and once for the final. He gave enough hints for me to at least have some idea of what to revise in the mountain of content, which was quite appreciated.

P.S. The labs were still utterly useless. This time, the labs used code from 1997 and libraries from 2004. Well done!
comment_workload:
Workload was mostly reasonable, and mainly came from the project.

Project phase 1 required your group to design and implement the inverted file and the indexer, and phase 2 required you to implement the search engine. Both phases took significant amounts of work (at least if you did not use the code from 1997). You can use any programming language, but the labs are in Java. I have seen successful implementations in Typescript and Python though.

The rest of the workload was trivial. The quizzes took next to no effort, and the attendance part wasted some of your time but was nothing too onerous. The final exam should only take you 2-3 days to revise.
comment_grading:
Kenneth's "generous grading" still exists in this course - but the problem of low grade differentiation still exists.

Grading Scheme:
20% Post-Lecture MCQ Quizzes (2 Attempts, Answers Not Shown)
40% Project
- 10% Phase 1
- 30% Phase 2
10% Attendance
30% Final Exam

The main grade differentiator is the final exam and the project. Though everyone scored well in the project, the mark distribution meant that 1 mark in the project could affect your grade a lot. (I have seen A+ to B+ in 3-4 marks). At least the paper was somewhat well set (looking at you, Victor).

Attendance (for a pure online delivery course) is for going to 3/5 project Q&A sessions. The requirement was not stringent, but I still found the idea of attendance in a course with only lecture videos rather funny.

I have attached the collected grading statistics. Note that you cannot add LQs/UQs together, so the totals are for your reference only.

Note: You can join the HKUST Coursera for this course to gain all the quiz answers for free.

comment_content:
This is the first 4000 level COMP course I take. And honestly, words cannot describe how disappointed I am to the content of this course. This was a 4000 level COMP course so I was expecting the course to be difficult. I was expecting a lot of math, going in depth into data mining algorithms, stuff like that. But this course shows me that the 4 is just a number.
There are a few problems with the course content. First of all, there's just too little content. I planned out my study schedule and I would skip lectures and study 3 hours for every course per week. This works for other courses. I would usually spend the full 3 hours every week and be able to catch up on the content.
But for this course, only one and a half hour is needed. The content is so little its only 1.5 credits worth. Which is ridiculous considering this is a 3 credit course.

Second problem with this course is how little depth it has. I mean what he taught for each of the algorithms are just scratching the surface. I have taken COMP2211 and gotten A+. And basically the content of naiive bayes, mlp, cnn are taught in much less detail than COMP2211. I actually just recalled my knowledge from COMP2211 for the exams. I learned nothing new. I mean I actually don't know why he included rnn in the syllabus. It's actually funny because rnn is such a complex algorithm and bro just has one slide per gate (output gate, input gate), and each slides shows a graph and a formula. And doesn't explain anything. And then rnn was never in the exam. I mean what's the point of including rnn in the content then if you are not actually gonna teach it and test it.

Another issue is with the slides. Some slides are literally incomprehensible. There's a slide on edge betweenness, talking about an algorithm that measures edge betweenness. To this day, I have no idea what that slide is talking about. AI was suggesting its the Brande's algorithm? But the word "Brande's algorithm" never appeared on the slides. And I have no idea what the slides is talking about whatsoever. I spent at least 5 hours on that slide I think and I gave up at the end. Guess what? It appeared in the exam. Awesome. And for reference its probably not because I am dumb because I got A in comp3711 just reading the slides.

I have attached some slides as reference. Btw this is the first time I saw filler slides. Just some slides that are not relevant to the content (You can check the community slides). I just thought it was really funny.
comment_teaching:
Disclaimer: What I write here only comments on the slides. I have not even attended a full lecture of James Kwok so I would not comment on how he teach. (I was so bored I left the first lecture after half an hour in front of the whole class)
This is my third year in UST. And honestly I have seen a lot of professors. There are very passionate and helpful professors, those who handwrites their notes, and those whose teaching quality is questionable. But I have never doubted the professionalism of these professors. I thought that each professor must have been one of the top scholars in the world to be able to secure their spot as a professor in a world renowned university.
But from James Kwok's slides, I just don't see it. In the neural network related topics, I actually just doubted if he actually understood the topic himself. He either doesn't know what he is doing, or he just don't care about teaching so much he made such crap slides. I believe the reason would be the latter? But the the slides for the other topics are better. So I don't know what's going on. He literally has so little content in his course. If he actually go in depth to teach any of the topics, he could easily double the content he has in this course and make it a normal comp course workload.
if you compare the neural network slides to the comp2211 neural network slides, you would realise comp2211 probably has around 3-4 times the content of this course. I thought the other topics like decision tree, hierachical clustering, association analysis slides are better (but also very brief ofc), but my friend said comp1942 taught them in way more detail. If this course is worse than a 1000 and 2000 level comp course, should it be 0 level instead? :) Comp0331 lol.

The content in the course is of course very manageable as well, since its only about 1.5 credits worth of content.

I am just gonna make a conclusion for my long ass review. So basically I learned very little and left with a bad grade. DO NOT make the same mistake as me. I advice against anyone who wants to take comp4331 when James Kwok is teaching.
comment_grading:
HAHAHAHAHAHA worst grading I have experienced. WOW!!!
The weightings are as follows:
Assignment 1 8%
Assignment 2 9%
Assignment 3 8%
Midterm 25%
Final 50%
Only mean and standard deviation of the exams are given. So here are the statistics:
Midterm exam: Mean->54.5 SD->22.3
Final exam: Mean->58.5 SD->19

My mark:
Assignments nearly full
Midterm: 84 +1.3sd
Final 72.75 +0.75 sd
I mean overall I should be around +0.93sd right? If this was any good grade normal comp course, my mark would be between A- or A. 0.93sd should be top 18% in a normal distribution.
Guess what grade I got. B+. I GOT B+ SCORING +0.93SD. Wow. What can I say. Apparently I am 0.2 away from the A- cutoff. HAHAHA. Amazing.

But after calming down and thinking about it once again. I think there's two reasons why I got B+. First one being that this course does not have a good grading. James Kwok maybe just doesn't give a lot of A range.
But the second reason is probably because the distribution is not normal. For those who are more sensitive towards math, a near 20 sd is not normal at all. In fact, I think the distribution would be close to a bimodal distribution. When going to paper checking, I saw many people who got 80 something in both midterm and final, and also a lot of them getting very low marks like 20 30 something. And this is just really weird to me. It also hints on the teaching quality of James Kwok. If a professor teaches well most student should get a mark close to mean. Just saying.

The midterm was pretty easy. It's just telling you to run algorithms, no trick questions. Nothing testing your concepts or math. Honestly I thought the mean would be like 6X so I was shocked initially. The final exam tests way more in depth than what the slides is. It's almost like he is deliberately putting things that are not on the slides so that less people would know how to do the exam. I only survived because I have a very strong foundation in COMP2211 (Thanks Desmond).

comment_content:
1. Intro: Explain the importance, source and types of data; What analysis could be performed on them (how to mine them); Applications.
2. Knowing your data: a. Statistical description of the data: from median to Q-Q plot; b. Proximity Measures: how similar/dissimilar your datum is compared to other data in different cases(i.e. types of data)
3. Data preprocessing: Data cleaning: Data integration; Data reduction (dimensionality reduction); Data transformation
4. Data warehouse: OLAP vs OLTP: from structure to operations; Data indexing
5. Introduction to classification: types of classification, measurement  of data (confusion matrix, F-measures); Selection of Training and Testing sets
6. Decision tree: Algorithm, Purity and Overfitting handling
7. Bayesian classification: Assumptions, Formulas, Zero probability problem
8. K-nearest neighbour: Algorithm, Choosing the distance measure and K, limitation
9. Regression: Linear and Logistic regression: from formula to purpose; introduction to gradient decent
10. Artificial Neural Networks: From perceptron to propagation algorithm
11. Convolutional Neural Network: Structure and properties
12. Recurrent Neural Network: Introducing cell, attention mechanism to Neural Networks

comment_content:
An "introductory" course to data mining and machine learning. Just like any other "introductory" courses, if you have no prior knowledge you'll surely be fucked up. If you're RMBI students or you aren't proficient in Python, DROP THIS COURSE IMMEDIATELY and take ISOM3360 instead.

Key topics include a number of algorithms for frequent pattern mining, classification, clustering, outlier detection, collaborative filtering, etc.

0️⃣ Introduction to Data Mining
1️⃣ Getting to know your data
2️⃣ Data Preprocessing
3️⃣ Frequent Pattern Mining
      🔶 Apriori
      🔶 FP-growth
      🔶 Discretize Continuous Variables
      🔶 Generalized Sequential Pattern (GSP)
4️⃣ Classification
      🔶 K-nearest Neighbor (KNN)
      🔶 Decision Tree Induction
      🔶 Naive Bayes Classifier
      🔶 Bayesian Belief Network
      🔶 Confusion Matrix
      🔶 Ensemble Methods
      🔶 Neural Network
      🔶 Support Vector Machines (SVM)
5️⃣ Clustering
      🔶 K-means Clustering
      🔶 K-medoids Clustering
            PAM
      🔶 Hierarchical Clustering
      🔶 Density-based Clustering
            DBSCAN
      🔶 Fuzzy Clustering
            Fuzzy EM
      🔶 Probabilistic Model-based Clustering
            Univariate Gaussian Mixture Model
      🔶 Subspace Clustering
            Dense Unit-based and Entropy-based Methods
6️⃣ Outlier Analysis
      🔶 Statistical Approaches
      🔶 Nested Loop Algorithm
      🔶 Cell-based Algorithm
      🔶 Local Outlier Factor (LOF)
7️⃣ Collaborative Filtering
8️⃣ Social Media
      🔶 Edge Betweenness
      🔶 K-L
9️⃣ OLAP and Data Cube
      🔶 OLAP Operations
      🔶 Data Warehouse: Materialize View
            Greedy Algorithm
🔟 Web Mining
      🔶 PageRank
      🔶 Hypertext-Induced Topic Search (HITS)

Note that this course is mainly on theory and is grossly unrelated to the assignments and the project. Nobody ever teaches how to code the algorithm and how to use the Python packages. When it comes to the assignments and the project, we all end up in learning all by ourselves.

I sincerely wish that we were taught how to import, manipulate, and visualize data using the NumPy, pandas, matplotlib and scikit-learn packages. Knowing how to do data mining by hand doesn't help sh!t at all.
comment_teaching:
At first Prof CHEN's teaching seemed not bad at all. But I later discovered that he had a notorious habit of just skimming through all these algorithms and casually skipping a lot of the details, and he expected all of us to understand all of them as if we were all genii? I knew there were a lot of genii in the class, but I was deffo not one of them. Thus I instead learned by going over YouTube videos on data mining, and I found this 100x more effective than attending his lectures.

Okay. Just another self-learning course.
comment_workload:
Extremely heavy workload. Prof Chen seems to have very high expectation on us.

💻 20% Assignments
💻 30% Project
✏️ 20% Midterm
✏️ 30% Final

There are a total of 3 assignments in Python. All of them require the use of Anaconda, a distribution of Python designed for the purpose of data mining. The assignments are deffo not easy and we are required to spend some time to self-explore what the heck the tasks are and how to use the scikit-learn packages. Again, it's about the ability to self-learn.

And then the fucking project! It was my worst nightmare ever. The project was a dropout prediction task extracted from the professional KDD-cup competition. We were allowed to form groups of up to 3 groupmates, but even with 3 people we still ate sh!t in the project. The project required us to do the data preprocessing (which spent us about 80% of our time) including loading and manipulating the data into a data structure we needed, feature engineering with discretization, data aggregation and transformation, selecting 3 classifiers for prediction, applying cross-validation for these classifiers, ensembling the classifiers and evaluating the classifiers with confusion matrices. This is the most demanding project I've ever met and you can imagine all my groupmate staying in front of our PCs days and nights coding and debugging and coding and debugging... An excellent opportunity to get hands-on experience of how it feels in the hell.

As for the exams they're basically concept-based written papers covering various algorithms taught in class. No codes. Just data mining by hands, with a definitely insufficient amount of time. They were among the sh!ttiest papers ever as they differentiated us based on our speed instead of our understanding of the concepts.
comment_grading:
Not bad. The TAs have been quite lenient in marking our assignments and projects. But as most of us score high in the assignments and the projects, exams become the determining factor of our grade.

✏️ Midterm
Mean = 37/60

✏️ Final
Mean = 75/100, SD = 15

I got 36/60 in the midterm, 78/100 in the final, 98/100 in the project and averaged around 90 in the 3 assignments. Overall I got around 82.7% and this translated into a B+.

As a tip, don't be in despair when your codes do not run. I encountered quite a few times the situation when the codes in my assignments and project did not run at all, but I still managed to secure 90-ish by outlining the logic in my comments, explaining how I approached the problem, writing some partial codes, and most importantly, submitting PUNCTUALLY. Don't submit late even if you cannot finish the assignments. You might be surprised at how many marks you've secured by submitting partial codes with some explanation in the code and the report. Another thing is that if you've used Python packages outside of those suggested by the TAs (which is totally fine), there is no way the TAs could verify whether your codes could actually run or not.

comment_content:
Important concepts of data mining and big data are covered well. Good introductory course if you are curious in big data (how come diapers and beers are often bought together on a supermarket?) or looking to be a data scientist like me.
Assignments cover both written and programming (1st assignment in c++ while 2nd one in java or c++). Then midterm and final as usual.
The course was more on understanding the concepts than mathematics which I was expecting. You would find more questions using mathematics on the assignments while the exams would have more conceptual questions (like predicting how the result would look like given this input and this technique then explaining why).
comment_teaching:
Lectures are okay. Sometimes he tries to make jokes and when I get it I laugh (when I don't get it I just smile). But when he goes really technical, it could be rather boring. Sometimes he tries to show live demos of the materials over websites but mostly fails because the demo is using Java Applet. The lecture desktop does not allow it to run.
One time he was demoing a concept by googling his name. Somehow he stumbled to a website containing reviews about his teachings. He was surprised and spent a minute reading the review in front of the class. That was the best lecture session of this course in my opinion.
comment_workload:

Usually you need to implement one of the machine learning techniques (the easy ones). You should also make some documentation about the program (comments on code, how to run, etc.).

grading: I got an A+ in this course.

But it turns out to be a very unsatisfactory performance to Professor Song. **BE CAUTIOUS ABOUT USING HIS RECOMMENDATION LETTER.**

He agreed to write for all the schools I selected, and at that time everything seemed perfect. I trusted him with my applications, including the schools I dreamed of the most and almost all my safety schools, all with his letter. As a course recommendation, it was naturally expected not to be too strong, but it would never have been a bad one. It should be safe using his letter, I thought. I was very wrong.

And I was rejected, one after another, even by the safety choices I thought would never turn me away. In the end I was rejected by almost all of the universities that received his letter. I did something like an ablation study with my applications when I used the letters. The ones with Professor Song’s letter turned out **MUCH** poorer than the others, **FAR WORSE** than anything I submitted with letters from other professors. What was going on became painfully OBVIOUS, didn’t it?

In the end, I still do not understand why he would do this. I never had any personal confrontation with him, and I cannot think of ANYTHING I might have done to offend him. He never showed any dissatisfaction toward me, at least not when I was present. I am also confused why he did not simply decline when I asked for a letter, or give me ANY sign of his dissatisfaction, so that at the very least I would not have used his letters for the schools I wanted most, as well as my safety schools.

In the end, I wish I had never reached out to him, and perhaps never taken his course at all.

comment_content:
There are some difficulties with the content design, which makes the course content not a 4000-level course. The students consist of people from CS, CPEG, DSCT, and RMBI. If you take a look at the pre-requisite, either COMP4211 or ISOM 3360 can fulfill the pre-requisite. However, these two courses are at completely different levels of difficulty and you can also imagine that RMBI students are less trained in coding and math. Eventually, the professor has to teach some  ML content in a very simple way, otherwise, it will be too hard for RMBI students.

The content is broken because there is a great duplication to other ML courses. And other topics are just not taught in-depth enough. But please don't blame things on the professor and the TAs, they work really hard. A better solution is to design two separate courses for CS CPEG DSCT and RMBI.
comment_teaching: The TAs and the professor worked really hard to answer students' questions. Professor Song also taught the content very clearly and often share his knowledge regarding what sort of techniques are used in the industry. I think this is the most precious part since only Professor Song, who has so much connection with the industry, can share these stories.

3 assignments 1 presentation and Final.

But the PAs are quite easy. Not heavy.

The presentation is like talking about what we did in PA, not really a workload.
comment_grading:
There are 3 assignments, 1 presentation, and a final.

The grading of assignments is a bit broken because the variance of the grade in different assignments varies too much. I think the TAs notice this problem as well. Anyway, this part including the presentation wouldn't affect much of the grade. I actually quite enjoy working on these assignments.

The final is the main factor of the overall grade. The truth is that everyone has a different perspective on the difficulty. What I must say, however, is that the questions, the difficulties, and the amount of workload to finish the exam are very similar to the past papers.

comment_content:
This course covers the fundamental knowledge for text and graph data mining in the context of building a recommender system. If you have taken a basic ML course but are new to its applications for text or graphs, this course is for you.

Since this course is designed for both CS and business students, the teaching favours breadth over depth. If you would like to understand the theory behind the various ML methods covered, consider taking a different course. Also, the pacing is quite slow, so you may get bored if you are already somewhat familiar with ML.

Note that although there is a tentative schedule (see below), the topics are not necessarily covered in order; some of the contents even belong to multiple topics.

[Topics]
1. Introduction
2. New Applications of Big Data Mining
3. Introduction to Deep Learning
4. Text Mining in Recommendation
5. Graph Mining in Recommendation
6. Heterogeneous Information Mining in Recommendation
7. More Techniques and Applications
8. Conclusions
comment_teaching:
The instructor goes into the course material in much detail, especially whenever math is involved, so that business students can still follow the content. If you come from a CS background, it is easy to lose focus; unless you want the bonus marks, I recommend you just skip the lectures and review the recordings at 2x speed.

He tries to keep the class lively by allocating some time to Q&A sessions, in which he asks questions to the class and allows a variable number of students (sometimes more than 10) to answer them for bonus marks.

Each tutorial is split into two parts: a brief review of the topics covered in the lecture, and an overview of example Python code implementing those concepts. However, for the most part, the TAs did not explain the code in detail, so you might as well just go through the code yourself.
comment_workload:
There are 3 group projects and 1 presentation, but assuming that you have found a group of 4, the workload is actually quite minimal. Indeed, most of the work is spent on hyperparameter tuning.

I was lucky enough to be in a group of 4 that distributes work evenly, so I only spent around 16 hours in total.
comment_grading:
[Projects (50-60%)]
There are a total of 3 group projects. Students can form groups of 1-4 which remain the same throughout the whole course.

Each project corresponds to one of the aspects of a recommendation system: sentiment analysis, social network mining, and rating prediction.

In each project, the group is required to develop a model for the given task and write a report on the process. The model accounts for the majority of the score and is graded based on its performance compared to predefined baselines. The report accounts for the remainder of the score and is graded based on content.

[Presentation (10%)]
At the end of the course, each group is required to conduct a 3-minute presentation to the rest of the class about one or more of their projects.

The presentation is graded on: content clearness, description of methods, experiments and components, insight sharing, and time management.

[Final Exam (30-40%)]
This is a 3-hour open-book written exam. Students can type out their answers, and even search the internet, but no communication is allowed.

The exam consists of a small number of T/F questions, with the rest being short questions. Some of the short questions are straightforward, such as doing hand calculations for TF-IDF, convolution, and node2vec; others involve understanding ML models that make use of the layers (in Keras terms) taught in class. Note that these models can range from fairly simple ones, such as vanilla Bi-LSTM, to very complicated ones introduced recently; it would do well to be proficient at literature review on ML papers in general.

While the mean compared to last year may suggest otherwise, the difficulty of the exam has indeed been reduced: the SOTA models featured in this year's exam are much easier to understand.

I would like to emphasise the importance of being able to run model.summary() in your head: in this year's exam, out of 100 marks, 12 are earned from determining the tensor shape and 30 from calculating the number of parameters. Honestly, I feel that whoever designed this paper has some obsession with this task; it would be great if they could come up with other ways to test our knowledge.

[Bonus Marks (+2%)]
As mentioned in the Teaching session, students can earn bonus marks by participating in the Q&A sessions. Each question answered earns 1% of the overall grade, up to a maximum of 2%.

[My Grades]
Note that the mark distribution between the projects and the final exam depends on the difficulty of the final exam (higher difficulty results in larger weight for the projects). There has been no announcement about this since then.

I am not aware of any grading split between CS and business students this year, but just in case: I enrolled in this course under COMP.

Projects: 296/300 (Mean: 289.3)
Presentation: ??? (Mean: ???)
Final Exam: 79.9/100 (Mean: 57.4, Max: 91.0)
Bonus: +1%

Grade: A+

comment_content: This course will expose students to new and practical issues of real world mining and managing big data. Data mining and management is to effectively support storage, retrieval, and extracting implicit, previously unknown, and potentially useful knowledge from data. This course will place emphasis on two parts. The first part is big data issues such as mining and managing on distributed data, sampling on big data and using some cloud computing techniques on big data. The second part is applications of the techniques learnt on areas such as business intelligence, science and engineering, which aims to uncover facts and patterns in large volumes of data for decision support. This course builds on basic knowledge gained in the introductory data-mining course, and explores how to more effectively mine and manage large volumes of real-world data and to tap into large quantities of data. Working on real world data sets, students will experience all steps of a data-mining and management project, beginning with problem definition and data selection, and continuing through data management, data exploration, data transformation, sampling, portioning, modeling, and assessment.

comment_content:
This course focuses exclusively on CPU shading with no coverage of GPU-related content. In my view, its objective is to introduce fundamental graphics concepts rather than teach modern computer graphics in a practical sense.
    The teaching materials and assignments are outdated— the assignments are adapted with minor revisions from those used at other universities (I saw a "2001 copyright" notice in the skeleton code for the coursework).
    Back to the course content: as stated in the course description, it primarily covers the following topics: display technologies; scan conversion; clipping; affine transformations; homogeneous coordinates and projection; viewing transformations; hidden surface removal; reflectance and shading models; ray tracing; spline curves and surfaces; hierarchical modeling; texture mapping; and color models. It covers the fundamentals and is quite comprehensive overall, but precisely because the content is overly basic—and computer graphics is an extremely fast-evolving field—you’ll need to do some extra work if you want to delve deeper into it.
comment_teaching: CK is a really nice professor and his lectures are pretty engaging. The only minor downside is his accent and the occasional pauses in his English. Since each class doesn’t cover that many key points, he goes into great detail on a lot of things.
comment_workload:
There are four project HWs in total, all coded in C++ with OpenGL — and it’s an OpenGL 2.x version I remember. The first project is about image processing, the second focuses on matrix transformations, the third is implementing a basic ray tracer, and the fourth covers curves and animation.
    All the basic requirements are really straightforward; if you’re not aiming for a high grade, the workload for this course is surprisingly light. But each project has a ton of bonus points up for grabs, and these are a must for scoring top marks (though you don’t have to nail all of them — just doing a portion is fine).
    That said, honestly, a lot of these bonus problems are actually really valuable for practical use. The inverse kinematics (IK) system in the second project, for example, is widely used in the 3D animation industry.
comment_grading: The grading criteria are fully transparent,  it’s not very hard to get an A range —but  you need to put in a lot of time to earn the assignment bonuses.

comment_content:
The content is a breadth-first exposure to the major techniques of image processing.

Topics:
Fundamentals
- Images as functions
- Quantization
Enhancement in the Spatial Domain (Over Pixels)
- Image Histogram and Applications (Contrast Stretching, Compressing, Histogram Equalization, Histogram Specification)
- Intensity Transformation (Gray-level Transforms, Bit-Plane Slices)
- Spatial Filtering and Convolution (Gaussian and Median Filter, Unsharp Mask, Laplacian Kernel)
Enhancement in the Frequency Domain (Over Frequencies)
- (2D) (Discrete) Fourier Transform
- Smoothing and Sharpening Frequency Filters (Notch, Lowpass, Highpass, Butterworth, Gaussian, Ideal Filters, Ringing Effect)
Restoration and Filtering
- Diffusion Filtering (For edge or coherence enhancement) (Not tested)
- Random Noise (Gaussian, Rayleigh, Gamma, Uniform and Exponential Noise; Salt and Pepper Noise)
- Spatial Filters for Noise Removal (Harmonic, Contraharmonic Mean Filters; Median, Max-Min Filters; Adaptive Filters)
- Frequency Filters for Noise Removal (Bandpass, Bandreject and Notch-pass Filters)
- Restoration based on Degradation Function (Inverse, Minimum MSE Filters)
- Grey-level Assignment (Nearest-Neighbour, Bilinear Interpolation)
Morphological Image Processing
- Dilation and Erosion on Images
- Opening and Closing,
- Boundary Extraction, Connected Components and Skeleton
Color Image Processing
- RGB, CMY and HSI Color Spaces (and their inter-conversions)
- Tone and Color Correction
- Color Smoothing, Sharpening and Segmentation
- Edge Detection
- Effect of Noise on Color Channels
Image Segmentation
- Hough Circle Detection
- Edge and Line Detection
- Global Thresholding (Iterative and Statistical Approaches)
- Expectation Maximization (EM)
- Snake and Active Contour Methods
- Watershed Segmentation
Image Registration (Positional Matching)
- (2/3D) Object Translation via Matrices
- Non-Affine 2D Transforms
- Measurements of Registration Accuracy (Sum of Squared Differences, Sum of Absolute Difference, Correlation Coefficient, Mutual Information)
- Simple Optimization Methods (Powell's Method)
- Feature-based Image Stitching (SIFT with RANSAC)
Image Compression
- Redundancy and Fidelity Measures
- Encoder-Decoder Pair for Compression (Not related to Machine Learning)
- Huffman Encoding
- Lossless Predictive Coding
- Lossy Compression
- Transform Coding (Discrete Cosine Transform) and Quantization
- JPEG Compression
Image Feature Detection
- The Harris Corner Detector
- Local Binary Patterns and Rotational Invariance
- SIFT
Image Classification
- Bag of Words and Feature Extraction
- Clustering
- Principle Component Analysis for Dimensionality Reduction

Although the lectures did provide a solid mathematical background for those who prefer it, it was not strictly necessary to actually understand all the Math. Being able to understand (intuitively) what each algorithm does is far more important (at least for the course assessments). If you feel lost, try to think what each technique does on a group of pixels instead of staring at the stack of second derivatives often seen in the lecture notes - it helps with the thinking process significantly in my own experience.
comment_teaching:
Albert was a decent teacher who did manage to explain things clearly, but was sometimes unable to gauge the difficulty (and hence the time taken) when delivering the course materials.

As an example, he spent around the same time for teaching binary number conversion and some multivariable calculus-based derivation of one of the image kernels used. Coupled with my less-than-stellar Math background, this definitely confused me significantly.

Overall, the teaching was fine - perhaps more Math-focused lectures are simply not my cup of tea.
comment_workload:
Workload was moderate in general, as per a typical COMP course.

Assignments took around a week to do on-and-off, and I did not spend too much time on preparing for both exams - going through the material once and understanding what is being done should generally be sufficient.
comment_grading:
Grading was... surprisingly bad for a COMP course? It was definitely a surprise (not a positive one) after grade release.

The grading scheme was as follows:
30% 3 Programming Assignments, 10% Each
[20, 25]% Midterm Exam
[45, 50]% Final Exam

The weight percentages for the exams were given as a range in the introductory PowerPoint, and they might be dynamically adjusted with respect to our performance. Although I have sent an email to the teaching team to request the exact breakdown after the semester, I did not receive a reply.

Assignments
=============
In general, the assignments were not particularly easy. They required significant time to work out the correct implementation in NumPy (although vectorization is not a requirement) or reading the OpenCV docs. Taking this course with a friend could help with checking the validity of your outputs, since (especially for Assignment 1) the expected outputs were not provided and minor implementation mistakes can lead to large output variations.

Assignment 1 was on general image processing tasks such as bit plane extraction, histogram equalization and writing your own convolution implementation using NumPy.

Assignment 2 was on color space conversion and image segmentation using the Hough circle detection algorithm and morphological image processing.

Assignment 3 was on image thresholding using EM maximization, the SIFT and RANSAC algorithms for feature detection and image stitching, and the implementation of JPEG compression. This was the most difficult of the trio since the math behind EM maximization took me significant time to comprehend.

Annoyingly, some of the assignments used minor amounts of Google Colab specific code (beyond the standard mount/change directory loop). If you wish to do your assignments locally, you might have to write some short adapters for the missing functionality.

Assignment Statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
1: 0/7.5/7.5/8/8/8/8
2: 0/9/8.87/10/10/10/10
3: 0/85.25/87.25/95/100/100/100

Exams
=============
Both exams tested conceptual understanding and (surprisingly) memory over raw mathematical proofs. Most of the questions mostly asked about the effects of an algorithm on an image or dry-running an algorithm on a small image, then asked you to think of the possible effects if the algorithms are slightly tweaked. There was also one question on NumPy (simple semantics only) for each exam.

In general, the final exam was more computational than the midterm (probably due to the longer time limit). The computations required were well within the range of acceptable work though (unlike COMP 4221).

Exam Statistics:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
Midterm: 27/58.9/67.59/68.8/77/93/100
Final: 27.64/49.93/55.56/55.03/64.18/80.1/100

Overall Grading
=============
Scoring slightly above the estimated median yielded a B-, which is fair but less than expected - usually this translates to a B for non-Honors COMP courses in my experience.

Attached please find the approximated score distribution for your reference.

comment_content:
This course is mainly about standard techniques in image processing. I quite like the syllabus of the course. It covered stuff from a wide range of difficulty and some of the methods introduced is quite interesting. For example, you can segment an image with a lazy snake that tries to minimize his energy level!

List of Topics:
Topic 1: Image representation, MATLAB programming
Topic 2: Spatial domain image enhancement
Topic 3: Frequency domain image enhancement
Topic 4: Image restoration and filtering, Non-linear filtering
Topic 5: Morphological image processing
Topic 6: Image segmentation
Topic 7: Image registration
Topic 8: Image compression
Topic 9: Face detection and recognition
Topic 10: Feature descriptors

Don't get confused by the segmentation/object detection/face recognition part. This course focused on more traditional image processing based methods instead of state-of-the-art deep learning stuff. Although some of the stuff may seemed old-fashioned, they are still being widely used as of today.

Some of the research topic by the professor was also included. Nevertheless, the exam only focused on the standard stuff so you don't need to worry too much.
comment_teaching:
I don't know why but I like the professor's voice lol. Anyway, I think he did a good job in teaching. He tried to explain the slides with annotations and drawings which should not be too difficult to catch up for most students. However, he did speed up a bit in the late half of the semester so you may want to rewatch the video if you miss some of the points.

The professor did answer the question asked in class. He also don't mind to explain something again if we got confused on the concept. Overall, he is a nice prof. that explains things clearly.
comment_workload:
There are total of three assignments. The assignments are not easy. You may need some time to do experiments and think deeper on how to implement the algorithm. (Simple loops will be too slow!) As the implementation is not that straight forward, there might be unexpected issues coming up. Do reserve two or three days for each of the assignments.

No past papers were given. However, do spend some time to try out (and maybe speedrun) the examples given in lectures. It is especially important in this online setting as most will got his/her pace slowed down in online exams. Try to be conservative in time management too as you cannot go back to the previous question. In fact, I got shocked when the AdaBoost (a standard but long question, pure computation) question comes up as the last question.
comment_grading:
The grading seems quite nice from the statistics although I need more data to judge that confidently. If I assume the SD is similar to a normal COMP course exam (18~20), I would be ~1-1.2 SD above mean, which normally would be an A- or A. And, the highest score is 97 which suggests high score should be possible (not for most people tho :C). I am quite surprised to get an A+. Also, my friend got B+ with 0.5 SD above mean (with the same assumption) so it should be slightly better grading than average.

I didn't check my exam paper. But, I don't have enough time to finish the last question on AdaBoost (a standard question appeared in the assignment) and that question worth 13 points. I would say if you are not too careless, a good grade should be possible.

comment_content:
The course website: https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp4431/. Any CSE student should be able to access it.

Briefly, the content consists of 6 major parts:

1. Multimedia Representation
2. Musical Instrument Digital Interface (MIDI)
3. Audio Processing
4. Image Processing
5. Video Processing
6. Multimedia Compression

The coverage of the course is wide and shallow. If you have taken a lot of CSE or ECE courses, then over 50% of the material is already trivial to you and the other 50% is just an extension of what you already know. If you enjoy playing around with Adobe Photoshop and/or Premiere Pro and/or After Effects and/or Audition, then it is almost nothing to learn for you.

It is not a content-intensive course compared to other COMP 4000 level courses (alright, I have only taken 2 4000 level courses). I think it should be a 2000 level course or even a 1000 level common core course.

The content feels like COMP 2211, but less calculation and more conceptual information. I love the style because I can always understand what it is talking about.

Attached is my cheat sheet used in the exam (kind of like my own summary).
comment_teaching:
It's Gibson. You can always trust his teaching.

comment_workload:
1 hour off-line activity lecture per week. Most questions are solvable by ChatGPT. I spent time reviewing the material for the first few weeks, but after that, I realized that it is too time-consuming.

~2 hour off-line lab session per week. I never attended it. You should only attend it if you don't know JavaScript and HTML.

1 Lab Assignment per week (9 in total, excluding public holi-"weeks"). For most of the labs you don't have to submit it in the week. You submit it in a group with other few labs. Each lab took me ~1 hour to complete.

1 Mini Project. I spent a long time on it but surprisingly that it seems Gibson doesn't like my project. My suggestion is that don't spend too much time on it because it is quite subjective. The mini project is actually separated into 4 parts and usually people get full marks for 3 of them. The remaining 1 part is the subjective part and it contributes only 5% of the course grade. Reviewing material for the final exam should be more important than working on it.
comment_grading:
I don't know much about the grading because I got the highest in the final exam. I went to the paper-checking session. I got 2 marks back, but suddenly, I found the TA graded a wrong answer as correct, and I lost 2 marks. So, eventually, the marks didn't change.

In my own perspective, I think the exam questions are not as difficult as mentioned in the other reviews. When I was checking the paper, I found that I really made some silly mistakes:

- I forgot to add abstract value after taking square then square root.
- I didn't notice that in the question description it mentioned "at the center".
- I used a ruler to measure the frequency diagram, but Gibson said I measured it wrong.

Believe it or not, almost all questions are direct that don't require a multistep inference. You just think what he wants to examine you, and the answer is quite straightforward.

comment_content:
This is a course to teach you how to process audio, image and video in a digital way. You also need a few skills on JavaScript and HTML (not that demanding like COMP4021). Dave's MOOC notes and videos will be provided for self-studying so you don't actually need to take COMP4021 as well. As a 4000-level course, this is definitely academically easier than others.

The course contents can be divided into mainly 5(/6?) parts:
0. Basics
- HTML and JavaScript
- Multimedia Representation - Sampling and Quantization

1. MIDI
- MIDI features and controls

2. Audio
- Basic Waveform Generation and Frequency Domain
- Sound Generations (Additive Synthesis/Frequency Modulation)
- Post-processing effects (Reversal/Amplitude control/Tremolo/etc.)
- Echo and Reverberation
- Karplus-Strong Algorithm
- MIDI and Music synthesis

3. Images
- Colors (RGB/CMYK/HSV)
- Monadic image processing (Negation/Grayscale/etc.)
- Image Dithering
- Histogram processing
- Local operations (Blur/Edge/Sharpening)
- Kuwahara Filter

4. Video
- Video processing (Reverse/Transitions/Motion blur)
- Motion Tracking and Shaking Camera Removal
- Chroma Key Effect (something like green screen)

5. Compression
- Lossless/Lossy Audio compression
- Lossless/Lossy Image compression
- (Lossy) Video compression

For the Labs, each part will have a set of lab(s).
The first Lab 0 is self-studying lab for HTML and JavaScript with nothing to do and submit.
Lab 1 is about MIDI, which requires to make a MIDI keyboards.
Lab 2, 3, 4 are about Audio which are done on the same materials. Lab 2 is basic audio generation, Lab 3 adds post-processing effects and sound generation, and Lab 4 adds more post-processing effects and Karplus-Strong Algorithm.
Lab 5, 6, 7 are about Image which are also done on the same materials. Lab 5 is about basic processing and comic color, Lab 6 adds dithering features and histogram handling, while Lab 7 adds filtering (e.g. blur).
Lab 8 is about Video, which needs us to implement certain processing effects.

There are also a mini group project to do, which is an extension to the Lab work. You can choose 1 of the 4 Labs to work with, and various features for each topic could be chosen. Some projects requires only 2 people, while others need 3 people.

Overall, the contents are very ordered and well-organized, so that you won't get lost when you are learning or reviewing. The contents are not very challenging, yet interesting to learn.
While doing the lab works, you would also find it funny and relaxing to apply the effects and see the results. I would say this course is not boring at all!
comment_teaching:
[Warning: Spring 2020 is conducted in online lecture mode, so some of the reviews might not be applicable afterwards]

Gibson is quite enthusiastic at teaching, right? You should know his style of teaching if you have taken 1000-level course from him. 喼神喎。

Originally we had Mon and Wed lectures. However, this semester the course is switched to blended learning mode.

On weekend the materials and (pre-recorded) lecture videos of next week will be released. There are video questions to answer, in which you need to watch lecture videos to answer, because sometimes Gibson just asked some silly questions which are related to things only appear in the video.
Each question is worth 1 point of that week. Sometimes the videos could be as long as 1 hour. This is tiring but the explanations are very clear.

There will be no Lecture on Monday, and Wednesday lecture becomes activity lecture. Gibson will make some review, and ask pop quizzes related to materials that week.
These questions would be similar to the materials, or somehow extensions on the contents. Each pop quiz question is worth 2 points, while survey (don't care about correctness) is 1 point.

Labs are using pre-recorded video (also by Gibson) and lab page. Most of the steps are easy to follow and implement, while some harder parts require you to think and code by yourself.

Actually Gibson did a great job to explain contents. The flow is fluent and clear. Of course the notes are very clean and easy to follow, which is the same as those 1000-level courses. You should take it if you want a high-quality teaching.

The process includes figuring out the algorithm, implementation, debugging and recording the video. You just need to take little time out from your busy works, and hope there is no free-rider(s) occurred.

comment_grading:
25% A and 25% B! As long as you submit your video (just don't submit trash), you can get at least a B (B-range = B/B+ if my information is correct).

If your video is amazing, you will get an A-range (To my understanding: A-range = A/A+). One easy way to check if your video is good: show it to your friends. If they enjoyed it, you cook!
Usually, people will make content related to HKUST, COMP Professors, anime, and random memes. Humor is a plus for your work.

More objectively, to ensure your work is good, you have to complete any of the following:
1. Stunning visual + Mid music
2. Mid visual + Stunning music
3. Decent visual + Decent music
So, you will be at a slight advantage if you have some musical background. Some crazy people (e.g., me) will make music rearrangements and compositions. But no worry, most students won't focus on music, and put most of their efforts into visual -  fancy effects.

Tips:
1. Commentary is important. Show ALL your efforts! And try not to do it like "PowerPoint style", or just replaying the video AGAIN, but in the editor interface. That is boring and not effective. If you have time, I recommend that you edit it with some effort, it'd be good if you add some memes in it ^^

2. Find a good partner if possible. You can save 50% time if the partner is responsible, as you know, video editing is time-consuming.

3. Avoid the following popular content unless you have sth really amazing. Marvel Revengers (esp. the Thanos theme), Spider-Man, Minions, Harry Potter, Donald Trump, Rickroll.
Anime is good, but try to pick scenes that are well-known.
PV & Vocaloid is a very popular choice. Think about how you can make your video stand out. Btw please tune your Vocaloid, or otherwise it will sound like a 3 am door squeaking.

4. You can sing. But don't ruin our ears.
And you can also act/perform! Usually, this will not cause a problem to the audience :D

5. The standard of MIA1 is much lower than MIA Final Project. Your video should be much more complex by the time it goes. Utilize AI tools like deepfake, vocal splitters, or stuff like that!

3 assignment and 1 final project, as well as a documentary for each of them.

In this part I am going to share how to get a high marks in this course:

Lets talk about the music part first:
For me I DON'T have any music background. I know some other comment mentioned that if you do not have any music background then do not take this course. But I totally don't agree. In all my assignment, I use very shitty music for my video, for example, I simply stick the jojo music(pillar man) and star wars theme song tgt without any editing in the first assignment, but I get upper quartile. In the second assignment, I simply stick spideman music, chipi chipi chapa chapa dubi dubi daba daba, and the jojo music tgt, but I get upper quartile again. The third assignment is much more worse, I simply stick the Onegai muscle and the loli kami requriem tgt, which receive much negative comment from the peer and TA, but still get upper quartile again. As you can imagine, the music must be extremely bad, but still can get a quite satisfactory result. So don't worry if you really don't have any music background.

this part I will talk about the content:
As I have mentioned, although I don't have a very great music for all my assignment, but why I still get a good result? It could be probably because my content is crazy enough.
For the first assignment, the content is about Darth Maul kill the master of obi wan, and suddenly Bruce Lee jump out and use dual  light saber to kill the Darth Maul. Crazy enough right? more to come...
For the second assignment, the content is about different Memes character dance in the AGT, such as toby maguire, Ricardo milos, and fat black guy. The video is so crazy in a way that the whole lecture theatre laugh a lot.
For the final project, I deepfake different CS professor on my face and fight the "course monster" in UST. Again many students in the lecture theatre laughed so hard, and the professor love it so much. He even sent it to Desmond, Gibson and David Rossiter to watch our video. the craziest thing is that professor Horner plays the video to the secondary school students who are visiting UST. (So Kam)

Therefore I think the professor prefers creative, interesting and even crazy content more than a good music. Try to put more time on how to make a crazy video!!

Additionally I think coming up with a completely fresh video type is very important.In the lesson, professor will show some great masterpiece from previous semester. And you can see that actually many different genre of video has already been made by other students, such as anime remix, self-made music with customized MV, lip-sync music video and so on.....

I am not saying these genres is not good, but since professor and students had already seen too many these type of video, so it could be hard to excel from other student's masterpiece, not to mention it can be boring to the professor. Therefore coming up with a completely new type of video that previous student do not make can help you to excel easily. For me, I make a low-cost action film with my teammate in the final project, in which no students had made this type of video before. So the students are surprised and the professor loves it so much.

Besides, I think funny video can get a great mark easily compared with very cool music. Since your work will also be graded by your peers and the TA, so you also need to think how to make the video attractive enough for the student to watch (if the video is not funny enough, the student will simply play their phone and ignore your masterpiece T_T ) So try to attract your peers using some funny scene. (I personally suggest putting memes in the video)

Therefore in conclusion, if you think you are a crazy person or can easily come up with some funny idea, this course is very suitable for you and you can probably get a good grade. If you think you are not crazy enough, then watch more memes in IG. Those reels can drive you crazy and may give you idea on how to work on the assignemnt

comment_content:
4 video projects, with 1 main video and 1 video describing your video, so total 8 videos. Each video 1-2 minutes long, the last one can go longer to 3 minutes. Although many people go beyond the time limit, which is allowed, it's better to make it shorter, as long as you can maximize the WOW factor.

Music is an important part of the course. You need music for every video. You don't have to compose your own music. Just find some online and do some remixing or editing to make it fits the video.

No essay, quiz, midterm and exam. Just 4 video project with 8 videos.

Topics:
1. Parady Movie Mashup (10%)
2. Character Mashup Remix (15%)
3. Villain Lyrics Video Remix (20%)
4. Multiverse Surprise / Deepfake Remix (45%)

You have to think of the contents yourself, and there are some freedom in choosing the topic. So, you don't need to follow the above topic strictly, especially for the last video project. Besides, you can use whatever software you want for making your video. Generative AI is also allowed.

For the rest 10% of marks, they are from peer review and research rating. Peer review means you rate other people's work. And research rating is to do surveys and record yourself playing the given music game (Osu). The research review may be different for different semester.
comment_teaching:
During lectures, you just watch other people's work and rate from 1 to 5, and professor will also comment on the videos. You can learn and inspired from the work of others. Skills for video and audio editing will be released as notes and you read by yourself. Not much technical things or theories to talk about, it's more about your creativity.

Horner Andrew is a nice and generous professor. He allows late submission as long as he get enough video to play during the lecture. And he also allows flexibility in the topic, so you can do whatever you like.
comment_workload:
Varies a lot, depends on how much effort you want to put into your video. If you want a high grade (A range), workload can be pretty high because there are many professional students in the class. Otherwise, it is low, as you just need to submit short videos with simple editing.

No attendance is taken, but since you need to review others work, you have to attend lecture, unless you don't care about the 10% mark.
