comment_content:
The most fun I had in UST from a course, truly (just put JOJO/UST meme reference).

The entire course is about editing videos and remixing music to create something new and inspiring. Prof. Horner encourages us to include surprise elements (e.g. creative concepts, unexpected plots and techniques).

Each lecture revolves around Prof. Horner showing past submissions for inspiration in the upcoming assignment, or playing submissions for us to give a peer rating.

There are 3 assignments and a final project for a total of 4 submitted videos. 1-2 mins for assignments and 2-3 mins for the final video, and also a commentary for each submitted video with around the same length. Some general instructions are given for each video like the theme (e.g. music remix, green screens), but you can also just do anything that you want (given that you have the idea).
comment_teaching:
TBH there aren't much taught content in the course. You are expected to learn video/music editing skills by yourself and create a final product. If you have a busy semester (FYP) or are uninterested in media editing, then it is probably better to not take this course.

However the prof does play some videos from previous semester and explain what is good about them and some interesting techniques. He also gives comments on each submitted video after they are played in class. He replies quickly and comments on your video if you submit via email.
comment_workload:
Here are the due dates:
Assignment 1 - Feb 13 (1 week later if you joined during add drop)
Assignment 2 - Mar 1
Assignment 3 - Mar 22
Project - Apr 20

The due dates are pretty tight to get around, as all of the workload is concentrated on the first half of the semester. After you submit the project, the only things left are research ratings (really light) and attendance. You will probably be extremely busy if your FYP due date is on top of the project due date (like me💀).

The workload largely depends on the what you want to achieve in your video. My second and fourth video had ambitious scopes(many green screens/voice acting/ memes) and took me way longer than expected to complete. I spent probably around 80 hours on all of the assignments combined with learning the necessary techniques.

If you have zero/little prior experience in video editing/music remixing and you want to produce something good, then the workload will be very heavy.
comment_grading:
Grading weights:
Assignment 1 (10%) 4.57/5 (movie mashup)
Assignment 2 (15%) 5.01/5 (green screen mashup)
Assignment 3 (20%) 4.46/5 (Villain lyric video remix)
Project (45%) 4.72/5 (Multiverse surprise/ Deepfake/ generally anything)
Peer & research ratings (10%) 22/25

Mean is around 4.17 for assignments and 4 for project. UQ is generally around 4.5.

If you submit via canvas only (no email), he won't play it in class but you also get less marks (and no feedback from him).

You will get a grasp of your marks after the prof give comments in the class. Generally, he always give positive feedback so there are a few things to look out for to know whether you will really get a high mark. If he replay certain parts of your video to give further comments, or he speaks about unique elements in your submission, then you will (probably) get a good mark. Just speaking from experience, the TAs also gives you marks so his comments doesn't reflect everything.

Attendance is counted in peer & research ratings. There will be a peer evaluation quiz (multiple choice) on canvas for each class and you will give a mark for each video.

Research ratings are just online research hosted on Qualtrics. You are required to record the session and use a headphone in a quiet room. each rating takes about 30-40mins and you do 4 of them for 4 bonus marks in that 10%.

Overall I expected around B+/A- but prof. Horner surprised me with an A+ (my only A+ haha and my third assignment wasn't that great). I would say the grading is quite lenient.

If you have no experience and interest in video editing/music remixing, then idk if you can get a good grade (A range). But generally the more work you put in your video, the better the marks will be.

FYI link to all my creations for this course:
https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1NhlVkcbY2yCnd1aBR_wXau6xwrWohAVa?usp=sharing

comment_content:
This experiential project-oriented music and video technology course will provide hands-on experiences in creating music videos and movie/game trailers by creatively remixing music, recutting video, and applying effects such as time and frequency distortion, time warping, reverse, filtering, and phasing. Students will create music remixes, music/video/actor mashups, and plot twists in their music video storytelling. It will give students a chance to work with emerging video technologies such as deepfakes. Whether students have music or video background or not, this course will provide them a chance to create like a sound designer and director. For each of their music video creations, students will also create a behind-the-scenes commentary video that describes what they did and how they did it. They will share their creations and commentaries in class, and learn from and be inspired by one another.

Course web: https://course.cse.ust.hk/comp4441/
(with instructions for assignments and the final project provided)
comment_teaching:
Horner is a very kind prof who frequently praises his students.
He is understanding and allows for a postponed deadline if you send an email in advance.
During class, we appreciate videos created by other students and watch inspiring student assignment videos from previous years. Horner provides feedback for each video, mostly pointing out strengths and innovative aspects, and rarely mentioning weaknesses.

Tutorials are recorded videos made by TAs, and do not require face-to-face attendance. They cover some video and audio editing techniques, and sometimes include music theory knowledge.
To be honest, I hardly watch these videos because unlike other courses, the tutorials do not teach the exam material, but rather just one of the many ways to achieve the final result. If you know other techniques, it is perfectly fine. AI face-swapping and lip-syncing are often taught, but I have never used them.

By the way, I do not think that the grading of this course is biased towards video and audio. If you think "I got a bad grade because my audio was bad, even though my video was good, and this course is called Computer Music," it is probably because your video is not as good as you think it is in the eyes of others (or your audio is sooooooo bad that it is beyond acceptable, but this is rare).
comment_workload:
The workload varies from person to person.
However, according to the course design, if you do not delay the deadline, the workload will be staggered from other courses.

    Any unreliable peer evaluation score will be omitted.
        e.g. All score are max/min, peer evaluation with extremely short time spent on finishing the survey.
    Each assignment is graded by "3 base mark + maximum 2 bonus mark".
        3 base marks will be obtained once you submit the assignment in Canvas.
        2 bonus marks can be obtained if you also submit the assignment to Professor's email (horner@cse.ust.hk) to allow playing your piece in the class.
        The 2 bonus mark is the score mentioned above, which is judged by professor, TAs, and peer evaluation.
    Peer ratings + Research ratings (10%)
        After the add-drop period, we will take attendance from the peer evaluation survey.
        Here are some conditions those may not be taken attendance.
            Late submission for peer evaluation survey (Please finish the survey within the day it started.)
            Your peer evaluation score has extremely low correlation score to other coursemates'. (e.g. Giving the max score to every pieces)

comment_content:
You watch videos; you make videos, and that's it. Should you try harder? Yes, this is one of the most rewarding class to try hard on since it is all about your own creations. Do you need to try hard? No, just make sure that you enjoy working on the videos.

If you are interested, you can Google for COMP 1943 and COMP 4441 to get a feel for the course by looking at some previous work (some people uploaded their work online).

Grading scheme and Assignment topics, may be very similar to later semesters:
10%: Peer & research ratings
10%: A1. Mashup Trailer, Try think of some wacky video + music combination.
15%: A2. Anime Music Video. If you have good music taste, you can do well by simply syncing your video to the music/lyrics.
20%: A3. Romantic theme. Well, I do pretty bad here due to a tacit problem.
45%: A4/Final project. Deepfake MV, yes, you need to use deepfake to do it. You just need to run the completed google colab notebook available online.

The real content quality depends on your fellow classmates. I say it was generally good in this semester, but personally I would say quite a lot of the creations are unnecessarily long.

Almost the same the same experience as comp1943, so you can also refer to those review.
comment_teaching:
The professor and TA give you some nice and encouraging words for your submissions. They are friendly people that you can casually chit chat with them in the zoom chatroom. I personally did not ask for any suggestion so I wouldn't comment on their real teaching quality.

Well, the real instructor for this course is actually google and yourself, and the amount of things you learn depends on yourself.

Basic audio engineering and video effect are not difficult (but can be time consuming) and you can follow tutorial on Youtube to do those. A difficulty is that you need to first come up with using those techniques in the first place. You probably need to consume a lot of Youtube content and remember the cool things about the videos that you have watched.

Well, if you insist to take the course even though you are not particularly interested, you can try making random meme videos to get by. You can do well enough (>=mean) in this course by trying to make your work intentionally bad and broken since your fellow classmates and the instructor/TAs are weird people.
comment_workload:
4 assignments + their commentary video/direct cut, peer review on each assignment, attend an survey for their research. Not a meaningful number but I had spent 3-15 working hours on each assignment (solo).

YOU NEED TO SUBMIT YOUR FIRST ASSIGNMENT BEFORE THE END OF ADD DROP PERIOD. This is probably good for you to know if you are really interested in doing this course.

I would say attendance is required since you need to rate your fellow classmates' work reasonably. They said that they would check if you were spamming.

You should treat everything they said to be optional as requirement. They have a list of optional things in assignment description, you better do at least 1 of them if you can't think of anything special on your own.

As mentioned in other reviews, music knowledge is really nice to have, but In truth, I have not learned or tried anything meaningful about music for the assignments. And my video editing skill is pretty load even at the end.

Suggestions for average people to get above mean:
- Do not go beyond the 1-2 min time limit for each video if you are not very good.
- Do try to sync your video in your music as often as possible.
- Do add sound effects (adding sound and/or editing them) and video effects.
- If you want higher score, example trick: find a midi version of the music, and add other instrument layering on where you want to emphasize (playing the exact same note as provided in the midi file) and see how it sounds. It will sound good 80% of the time.
- Lastly, do try to come up some new idea on your own. It does not need to be good, just sharing it in your video will improve your grade significantly.

The previous examples shown in class often show inspiring ideas, and quite a lot of them do not demonstrate any special skills in video editing and music. So, maybe creative idea is more important? I can't say.
comment_grading:
Do not take this course for the grading, there are some even better options for you. (Like COMP4631, 30% A range + only 8% C range, lowest is C+, basically 0 workload)

Let me give you some idea on score numbers since no one has mentioned it. In original wordings,
3: base mark, granted by submission
3.5: somewhat inspiring
4: inspiring
4.5: very inspiring
5: super inspiring

So, you can imagine that the difficulty to get higher score is growing exponentially. From my friends and my own experience in comp1943 (last sem) and comp4441, Below are what the score actually means:

3 - 3.5 means your work is not even understandable. (people are giving you some bonus score purely for sympathy)

4 means your work is not interesting. In truth, it means you basically get the lowest score.

4.2-4.3 means your work is average. B to B+

4.5 means your work is pretty good. An weighted average of 4.5 should be an A- already, given that you complete all the other things.

Beyond 4.5 is A to A+ territory. Say averaging at 4.6 can already be an A+.

The highest score each time is around 4.9/5, and it sometimes even goes beyond 5/5 lol.

comment_content:
The content is very interesting, basically about music remixing and video cutting. I am not sure if you will still use the applications after the course. It does not require any prior knowledge in music but you will be FUCKED UP if you really do not have any music knowledge.

Let’s talk about the first assignment, it is just mixing music, adding sound effect to a video trailer. Sounds fun and relaxing right? Indeed it was, but your grade will be horrible  afterwards if you do not put efforts in modifying the pitch, chord, bpm, etc. Well I know nothing about music but I felt my work was at least medium in terms of the way I cut the narrative, music and video to tell a totally new story but you know what this is a computer music course,  not a computer video or computer story course. What you need is MUSIC. I didn’t realize that early enough before the end of the add drop period.

If you know nothing about MUSIC, I will say congratulations! Have fun in the course.
comment_teaching: There is literally nothing taught during lessons but just appreciating the excellent from previous students, and watching and rating your peer’s work. The tutorials will teach some application and techniques for music remixing and video cutting like audacity, band lab, davinci resolve, etc.
comment_workload: Workload is fine because there is not too many things to learn but just 3 assignments and 1 final project though you need to spend days for each assignments (well depends on how you are talented in music but I am shitty on that so).
comment_grading:
I know nothing about how the works were graded but if you failed to deliver an amazing music, good luck with your grades. Sometimes you will see the professor praised something in your work but you ended up getting shitty grades, sometimes the professor said nothing and just skipped to the next video after playing yours but you ended up getting not too bad grade.

I never got above mean and was for most of the time just slightly below the mean. While I am typing the comment, the project score is just released. 4/5 with mean 4.2/5 and the project counted for 40% of the course grade. My teammate and I really put effort and tried hard to improve the music and do the video cutting but anyway. Who the hell knows how the work was graded.

I won’t say it is a good grade course, at most a very normal cs course grading. Don’t come to the course if you are not TALENTED IN MUSIC. There are lots more 4000-level good grade cs course out there. My intention to write the comment is NOT to tell how bad the course it is, but to make you think twice before taking the course especially if you don’t know anything about music as there will be no theory taught in the course telling you which music is good and which is bad to give you an idea about how to do your assignment better. And don’t be influenced by the other comments though I know there are indeed many crazy people like even DJ taking the course but still, the grading for students with no music background is not good in my opinion or maybe just I am not suitable for the course.

I thought I would be graduating without a letter C on my transcript. Thank you COMP4441 for helping me to add more diversity in the grades I got. Thank you for almost dragging me down from the 1st class honor cut off.

comment_content:
in-class: we just watched videos and filled the peer review form to give the scores to that students.

If you are shy to play your videos in front of all students, you can choose to submit privately which means your work will not show to all. But, there will be some bouns marks if your show your work to all. This is a trade of between getting higher marks and being no-showing work.
comment_teaching: not much teaching in class. But, the tutorial will teach you some useful skills for mixing sounds so that you can come up with your own videos.
comment_workload:
There are only 5 videos submission are needed.

professor called the assignments as Mission Impossible Assignments (MIPA)

4 short videos (MIPA1, 2, 3, 4)
You need to create short video last for 30 sec to 1 min

1 final video
3 mins video

All 5 videos, you can choose to do it in group of 1 or 2 member(s)
comment_grading:
VERY GOOD grade

NO exam, NO mid term

Marks will be given by Professor, TA and peer review form (students)

Weight:
Assignments: 60%
Final Project: 40%
Peer Evaluation: 10%
So, 110% in total

comment_content:
I took this class online and I love it you literally can get your creativity out as a destress and get good grades while suffering for other boring courses and get bad grades. If you're a creative person and like to edit videos this should be easy for you!! I definitely recommend you to take this class. There's another class that's literally the exact same Comp1943 you should take that too

For Comp4441:
You have to do 4 'Mission Impossible' Assignments (MIA) 60% of your grade (each 15%) where they give you four themes and you have to produce a video with sound editing and video syncing.

MIA 1: Wow Mashup trailer - basically remake a movie trailer and make it look legit
MIA 2: Going back to the start - basically incorporating the element of 'reverse' in your assignment
MIA3: I like to move it move it mashup remix
MIA 4: 1000 years romance remix - love song/sad ballad remixes

And for the MIA 5 Final project is 40% of your grade so you better use lots of time doing this.

MIA 5 (FINAL PROJECT): Final fight mashup /your own choosing project but you will have to get approval from prof and TAs

Each of these you have to provide the 'commentary' which is literally you make another video letting them know how you made this final product and show them at each point in the video what did you do in regards to video editing or sound mixing.

All these assignments will be showned in class and everyone will have to do this peer evaluation because it counts towards 10% of your final grade... WHICH i have no idea how that works because that will be 110% but this was on the syllabus so you should do the peer evaluation it's just a canvas quiz that you can do anytime before the due date.
comment_teaching:
In the lectures, they don't teach you anything it's just showing past examples of these MIAs and showing the student's MIAs so that people can do the peer evaluation.

They have tutorials to teach you some skillsets but I never attended because I already know what I can do and can not do and I work around it on my video final products.

If you don't know anything about video editing and sound mixing you should take those tutorials by TAs they have different topics every week like removing vocals of a song and leave the background audio.. cutting videos with the apps they recommend etc.
comment_workload:
The workload was fine for me. It's all projects and the peer evaluation so I'm not ticking the quiz box because it's not really a quiz..

They give you a week and a half for each assignment and I used one to two days to brainstorming the storyboard of the video and find audios and mix them and match it up to the video and cut it!

Depending on whether you're skilled in video editing and sound mixing... To me, it was pretty easy and I just used any apps I can download off the internet.

They don't provide paid video editing/sound mixing apps for you it's pure just what you can download off google.
comment_grading:
For all these I got MIAs I got around 4.27-4.6 out of 5.

I have no idea how they grade it but I know that they have like 3-4 TAs and the Prof where each of them looks for different things in your product. Some look for good syncing in video recording, some look at your effort put into producing the final product, some look for coolness in sound mixing, some look for overall 'WOW-ness' in creativity and process of producing the final project. The peer evaluation by everyone in the class also counts towards your grade of the MIA. So It's very subjective and they don't tell you how they came to the grade.

but yes I got A in the end! I could've put more effort into it but with minimal effort an A is already very good to me

comment_content:
Music editing and video editing. You can use whatever software you want, suggested music editing software for beginners is Audacity.

You are given a topic to create a music video on every two weeks.

You have to submit your work to canvas, but you are also allowed to email your work to the professor for bonus marks. He says bonus marks but if you add up the % of possible marks together it's like almost 200%, and it's on a curve so you need to get those bonus marks. He shows the videos he gets emailed in class, and you basically spend every lesson watching videos made by others.
comment_teaching:
Well there's not much teaching involved other than the TAs teaching some basic music editing with Audacity, which I used. Otherwise you learn from watching other people's videos.

Every class is just watching videos that other people made.

It's a good idea to get inspiration from others, or just look online for professional level videos.

Professor Horner always praises your work, I've only heard him not say anything positive about one video because honestly no one knew what was going on. He's a really positive professor.
comment_workload:
Workload depends on what grade you want. There is an assignment every two weeks, and a final project which is basically another assignment but slightly longer.

You want an A range? You gotta put more time and effort into your music remixing/creating and videos. I put an average of about 10 hours per assignment but I got an A+ so ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

You wanna chill? You can just put something together in a few hours, and probably get a B/B-

If you put like bare minimum effort but still do something you'll probably get a C range
comment_grading:
He tries to give as high a grade as possible. Honestly he is the nicest grader. I'm pretty sure he gives as many As as he is allowed to.

I tryharded this course because I find it fun. Got an A+

You will learn as you go, just try to learn from the videos other people made and use their techniques in your own work. Also listen to what the professor finds good/interesting.

comment_content:
Do whatever you want, Just put your favorite game , anime ,music into your work to show you passion.  

To be honest, this course is pure experimental learning course. There is nothing to teach. 99% are based on self-learning.

If you expecting actual music theory, here is not the place, here is more like a intro video edit and music composition course which teach you about the WOW factor.

There are manly 5 assignment.
1. Game Trailer mashup
2. Reversing video
3. Punk /Jazz / (i dun understand music anyway)
4. Romantic story
5. Final Battle

So no background is required, no programming is needed in general (unless you need). But skill in video and audio edit will be advantage. Also if you can make your own art, it will be a super advantage.
comment_teaching:
There are only 2 parts of learning in the course.
1. TA may teach you about some trick on Audacity and some online music making tools. (many dun understand at the end, just take it easy)
2. looking at others work (you can learn about how others compose their piece by looking at their  director commentary)

Yet , the TA are super qualify for their work.
comment_workload:
It depends on your effort, basically each assignment is a 2 week project. Some like to solo it, some like team up in 2. I was in a team of 2 , still each work spent us 1 week.

If you are fast editor and have great idea in mind then maybe a day can complete a video already, which is not my case. Normal video editing are very common and will be boring. Best approach is try to include some feature thats other is hard to replicate.
For example super heavy video editing (Meme level), meme looping (brain wash level ,), original animation (Yes, artist will have advantage,手書, MikuMikuDance).

Your work will not rank low if you did something unique. But yet it is time consuming, think about you need to learn After Effect, Premiere Pro, MMD, 3D modeling in a week in order to out win others. Trust me it worth it. Also imagine when the professor show MMD to department, it is a kind of achievement.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading,the grading system is based on roughly 60~70% given by professor/ TA based on your effort (It is extremely important to make good director commentary, it is even more important than your work, some even make ppt for it, If you work is not impressive but it shown you did put a lot of time on it you should still in 4 range).
Whereas the rest of the percentage are from other students in the course rank by canvas quiz, so dun just try to impress the prof , but use theme that will get everyone (Note, TA and prof are Otaku too ~)

I got A+, technically the full mark for each is 5
I got , 4.4,4.1,4.6,3.8,5 respectively.
This is a relative low mark compare with others, (some say their works never get below 4.5 UwU)

Tips, use some old material that will let other rmb their childhood, like old meme/ anime song.(e.g.一万年と二千年前から阿姨洗铁路~, Bad apple these works). Ah also in my semester JOJO reference and Kamen Rider everywhere

comment_content:
This course is mostly about video and sound editing. Every two week or so you would need to submit a video based on the theme given out. There are 4 assignments and 1 final project (which is similar to a normal assignment, just a longer duration). this semester the themes are:
1. No theme (just make a mashup video with a song, you would need to submit this before the first lecture)
2. Reversing video
3. Rhythmic (?)
4. Romantic story
5. Final Battle

As the description said, having a musical background certainly helps but it's not necessary.
comment_teaching:
As the start of each assignment the professor would show some works from previous semesters to give you an idea of what to do (except the first assignment). Besides that, you would be learning how to use your video/audio editing software mostly by yourself. Ask the TA's for tips if needed.

Overall it's a pretty casual course.
comment_workload: The amount of workload depends on how polished you want you final product be. 1-2 hours per day for 3-4 days usually get the job done for me but there are people who spends much more time on it I think. No pressure though.
comment_grading: The scores of your assignments and final project comprise of grading from TAs and professor, as well as peer evaluation. As long as your video is decent it won't be hard to get a good grade.

comment_content: Very interesting way to teach the course. Every week, we submit 1 assignment (1-2 mins) of audio/video based on a theme set by Professor. He discusses the theme briefly and shows us  few examples in class for inspiration. Themes range from romance, horror, reverb, spooky, alien, etc. A dream course for people with creative minds and/or a musical background.
comment_teaching:
Hands down, the best, most creative, most approachable and interesting professor in the department. He makes a lot of effort to discuss examples and is very open to students' interpretation of the assignments.

Being an experiential based course, there are no lecture slides and no typical lectures.

I learnt an amazing set of skills from this course.
comment_workload:
Continuous but light-ish workload.
Doing well in assignments does require a bit of work but its great once you develop (or already have) an interest in the work.
comment_grading:
50% weekly assignments + 50% final project.

Final project is very similar to weekly assignments except he expects a higher quality of work and we have to present to the class and discuss our approach.

Final project score is not released. If you love the course, A range should not be a problem.

comment_content: Interesting course, not too much difficult technical stuff about music for a non-music background. But basically listening to assignment audios in the class. Project-based course. Provide students with hands-on experiences in creating music and soundtracks with a wide range of emotional characteristics for a variety of situations in computer games and videos.
comment_teaching: The professor is very enthusiastic and knowledgeable about music.
comment_workload: One assignment every week but easy to do: "You will share your musical creations and learn from one another, allowing you to dive in and create music that modulates the moods of listeners."
comment_grading:
9 Mission Impossible Assignments (50%), Project-Presentation (50%),  +Bonus(Inspiring Assignment Work, Insightful Participation, Lecture Attendance)

Audit the course so I don't know or care much about the grading...

comment_content:
The course begins with basic contents, from waveforms, acoustics, and to the tools used(CSound/PVan) to analyze/synthesize sounds.
Really easy to follow and most of the contents taught will be practiced through lab sessions using CSound.
When it gets to FM/AM synthesis and wavetables, it may be easy for ones with electronic musical background but for those without any musical background, there are tons of youtube tutorials explaining such concepts so it shouldn't be too hard.
Applications of such knowledge to real music production is minimal. These are mostly theoretical parts and mostly aren't used in fields such as music production(but maybe in research?)
comment_teaching:
The professor is quite chill, will answer any questions you may have. When asking questions related to computer music but outside of class coverage, he will gladly provide any reading materials he can find.
Most contents are very basic and only scratches on the surface of computer music so I cannot comment on how well such knowledge is taught. Just ppt slides will not be enough to understand the concepts though.
comment_workload:
Just labs using C-Sound(basically a walk-through in lab) is all until final project.
You have to decide on your own to find what to do for your final project and present it to the class at the end.
comment_grading:
I guess very generous. I took this course as a freshman without any  prior knowledge in coding. Still got a fairly good grade from midterms and the freedom of choice to do whatever I can for the final project is really a big plus.
The professor will respect your ideas and gladly mark it in accordance with the course contents.

comment_content:
The course introduced a lot of content about game programming. It covers a wide range so not too in-depth, but many important ideas are introduced.

Slides & Title
1. Overview
[course admin, group project]
2. Introduction
[motivation, mechanics, history, categories, platforms]
3. Design
[design theory, process, production]
4. Game AI 1: Adversarial Search
[minimax, alpha-beta, advanced search]
5. Game AI 2: Agents
[game agents, finite state machines]
6. Game AI 3: Pathfinding
[pathfinding, A*]
7. Graphics: Introduction
[GPU evolution, pipeline basics]
8. Graphics 2: Rendering Basics
[rendering pipeline, phong illumination, texturing]
9. Graphics 3: WebGL
[overview, syntax, glsl, shaders]
10. Physics 1: Point Masses
[introduction, projectile motion, collision response]
11. Physics 2: Rigid and Soft Bodies
[numerical simulation, rigid body forces, soft bodies]
12. Physics 3: Collision Detection & GPU
[collision detection, spatial data structures, GPU Physics]
13. Networking
[protocols, latency, reliability, bandwidth, security]
14. Sound
[digital audio, game sound, game music, audio teams]
comment_teaching:
The slides were provided, and easily accessed. There was a clear timeline showing all important dates which was super useful.

The exam was scheduled in-between most other mid-terms and final exams, so it is comfortable to prepare.

Lectures are mostly repeatings stuff in slides but I think that is just the norm. The lecture contents are explained pretty well so I think it is good.

Even thought the project can be in groups, it can also be done solo. So you don't have to worry about free-rider. Just so simple and reasonable.
comment_workload:
I think in terms of workload, having only one exam and one project makes it pretty manageable, especially comparing it with other 4000 level course.

The project is also kindly separated into multiple phases so even if you are a deadline fighter like me, the workload will still be manageable.

The project can be in small groups but also done solo. So you can be in charge of your own amount of work by adjusting your games feature.

For the project, we need to write documents about game design, make presentations and record videos to demonstrate progress and result, so if you are comfortable with editing and recording / presentation, this part should also be fine. Not having to present is a huge plus because you can record multiple times, or just edit out some of the problematic lines.
comment_grading:
The following is our grading scheme

30% Exam
10% A1: Game proposal
10% A2: Game progress
43% A3 & A4: Game submission and report
7% Class polls and project Q & A participation
audit The audit requirement is answering at least 75% of in-class polls

I didn't do well in the exam but did pretty well with the game project. => B

The final project doesn't have to be really fancy (should be complete and playable). It focuses more on the technical parts so as long as you can demonstrate you techniques, with emphasis on Graphics / AI, you should be fine.

I think the grading on project is more kind than I expected, because in my opinion my game sucked, but the grade turned out pretty well.

comment_content:
Various topics in modern game development were covered. However, due to limited course hours, most things were introduced conceptually without further and practical touch.
The course is project-based and you will have to create your own game as the course project, which is also the most interesting part of the course. It is particularly exciting to share your progress and results with classmates, and have a look at others. You are encouraged to question others during the sharing session as well as answer the questions from your peers. Pretty like a game jam in some sense.
Depending on the platform, engine, and API you choose, the course offers a great opportunity to exercise your coding and game-design ability. As for me, I learn to use OpenGL to build graphics apps from scratch, which I did not in COMP4411 lol.
comment_teaching: Pedro is a very good instructor. He explains things clearly and is always helpful. You can also seek his advice and help after class though he is usually busy. The only pity is that the content is a bit too conceptual that too few details were taught while too many concepts were mentioned, but I guess that's mainly because of limited course time.
comment_workload: It depends on the complexity of your game.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme:
30% Exam
10% A1: Game proposal
10% A2: Game progress
43% A3 & A4: Game submission and report
7% Class polls and project Q & A participation
The final exam is scheduled a few weeks before the final period. I nearly missed the exam as I skipped too many lectures, and got a very bad result as a consequence. Class participation is also important so don't hesitate to ask questions during sharing sessions.
Personally not very satisfied with the grading scheme because the exam weights are a bit too heavy for a project-based course. The worst thing is that the grading of the project, i.e. your game, is too lenient(?), and most people got nearly full marks though some of the works look not very challenging, especially from a programming perspective.
Receive a B- at last, so sad.

comment_content:
The scope of this course is big, covering every basic aspect of making a game, namely basic game design, AI, computer graphics, physics, networks and sound. You may take a look at the course website to have a look at the slides. The important parts are AI, Graphics, and Physics, which have to be investigated to make your game work. Network and Sound are just the add-ons for most cases, especially when most of us are just starting game-makers.
You will get to work on a game project and make your own game with your teammate(s), where lots of stuff can be learnt by doing.
comment_teaching: Professor Pedro is a very nice guy, he has been very communicative and would like to give students all kinds of advice. Though the course material itself is a bit plain, he makes good explanation of things with enthusiasm most of the time. So fair teaching.
comment_workload: Super heavy, start early with your teammate(s), do not wait for the course progress, since Pedro would most likely cover material without too many details, and you will need to do most of the research to make your code work by yourself.
comment_grading:
The grading is fair, taking in to account an exam and the whole project process, which gets graded in seperated parts.
First, the exam itself is just plain knowledge, study the course material, remember it is also important to your final grade, even if it is nothing comparable with the game project, in terms of both workload and difficulty.
Then, the project, there are altogether three phases:
1. You will first do a proposal, which is basically writings, make sure to include details, even if you may not be super clear about what you game will be like, leave blanks when you are really not sure, but graders are concerned with the level of detail you provide in this phase, so that they know you have put in some efforts in designing the game.
2. Then, it is the progress report.  The time allowed for this part is around 1 month. Where you should have built the skeleton for your project, so that graders will know how your game functions and looks like as a prototype, it should be noted that graders want to have a holistic view of your game, so try to finish most of the basic AI, graphics, or physics!
3. Lastly, the final game, it is somewhat fierce competition, because most of us have the determination to make games, so try to present a both creative and good-looking(that means, in terms of the technical aspect of the game, like adding some advanced cg/AI/physics features, by yourself). It seems still possible to get good grade even if you are not the best.

comment_content: This course is about build your own game from scratch. It provides information regarding rendering, graphics programming and also some game AI logic. It covers a lot of topic but mostly only the surface of them. However, I think that for a introductory course for game programming, it gives us a better picture of how we make the games.
comment_workload: The workload all surrounding how good you want your game to be. For us we create a FPS adventure game which requires lots of animations and rendering figures. We invest a lot of time in this course in order to produce good quality games.
comment_grading: The grading for this course is actually highly dependent on your final's result because normally people all receives relatively high grade in their projects. Therefore, although the percentage of final is pretty less, it is actually the part that can shows the differences.

comment_content:
The course teaches some usual programming techniques and algorithm that are useful in games.
Most aspects about Game Programming are covered. However, each part lacks in-depth discussion and clarification.

Students are usually required to form a 2-people group at the beginning of semester and starts planning, and submit a final game with a video demo at the end of semester.

WebGL, Three.js and physi.js are commonly used to illustrate the concept but they are not essential, which means they are not included in the final exam and not a fixed requirement in the game.

A quick peek about the content (excluding the intro and overview):
1. Game Design
2. Introduction to Graphics
- most of them covered in COMP4411
3. Rendering & WebGL Basics
(Light source, Phong illumination model, Texture mapping, Shading, etc)
4. Physics
(Physics simulation, Projectile, Explicit Euler Integration, collision detection, Quadtree/Octree, BSP Tree)
5. Networking
6. AI
(Minimax Algorithm with alpha-beta pruning, A* Search, etc)
7. Sound and Level Design
comment_teaching:
Pedro is a good instructor. He prepares many examples of different game including bugs and cheats to make his lecture more interesting. He explains things clearly and he holds review session to clarify the lecture content before final exam.

The only cons of him is he replies email slowly and occasionally, grades and feedback are also released slowly. It's better to approach him face-to-face.
comment_workload:
Depends on your DETERMINATION.

The workload comes solely from the game project, with 3 presentations reporting your proposal, progress and final product.

Note that the game project is done FROM SCRATCH. Marking is done based on playability and complexity. Pedro sees how much is done by YOUR CODE importantly. Comparing to using a strong game engine and a tons of libraries/templates, Pedro likes students applying the graphics algorithm and write challenging code themselves. In my semester I gave up Unity and adopted ThreeJS by writing literally EVERYTHING by myself and my beloved teammate, and the final grade is satisfying.

Therefore, my advice is that your effort is directly proportional to your grade.
comment_grading:

It is normal to hear students getting 13/15 in final game but eventually getting B range or below as there are always some insane students making incredible game and raising the marking standard.

comment_content:
This is a broad UG-level course to HCI, so the overall content is quite general. Mostly the example of the technology is not super fancy one, but you can learn more through the application. A brief outline is right here.
1. Human-Centric Design
2. Understanding Humans
3. Design Thinking Process (Empathize-Ideate-Define-Prototype-Testing)
4. Multimodal Interaction
5. Ubiquitous Computing
6. Evaluation Process
7. Extended Reality
8. CSCW and social computing
9. Crowd Computing
10. Computing for Good (Persuasion & Accessibility)
The most special part is that it also mentions some psychology of humans and some design principles. And no code is mentioned in the lecture, so it is comfortable to listen the lectures. Overall it is worth for designers and programmers to take. (I know some programmers may need some times to adopt the design thinking mindset, not solution-driven but problem-driven. It may be “time-consuming” but it is a great chance to cooperate with other programmers and designers.)
There are 3 lab sessions to assist you for the projects (so you only need to attend those labs). TAs will show how you can build your prototype (this year they teach AI voice chatbot and unity). Of course you can use your own way to make the prototype, as long as you can show the effect you want.
The course outline and project descriptions is uploaded as a reference.
comment_teaching: Prof Ma is quite nice. She will ask questions during the lecture, and reply emails very fast. If you have any questions, she also feel free to answer. The teaching team is also helpful.
comment_workload:
As you see from other comments, there are many assignments and small tasks in this course.

There will be some (around 4-5) pop-up in-class exercise at the end of the lecture, and you need to submit by the end of that day or next day after the lecture. As long as you submit your work and finish it by following the lectures notes, you can get your participation marks. It is a good chance for review but it is quite messy. (The most tricky part is that prof wont upload the whole instruction to canvas. If you skip the lecture then you will have no idea about the exercise, Better to have a friend with you)

For the projects, there are many components. I will try my best to list out. Except Project1, we need to have a presentation with a demo video (around 8 minutes), no need live demo so relax. During the presentations, you have to give marks to other groups as peer evaluation. (Yes, your marks are from teaching team and other classmates. ) And after the presentation, you have to update your own portfolio page by putting your reflection and contribution. (You can build the page whatever you can, like Wix studio, Google Sites, guihub, Framer etc)

And for the final video paper, it is pretty chill. You just show your idea for the given topic and present it through a video. No need to be so practical (no need to make a demo), just need to be fun and creative.

Overall I think the workload is large but manageable. Don't be deadline fighter, and don't think you can finish the project within a few days. Start earlier must be helpful at all. This course also helps you to build a portfolio page, and you can learn from other students (There are many exchange students in the class!). It is still worth to take, no matter you are programmer or designer.
comment_grading:
This course is using experimental learning.
Project1 - 5%
Project2 - 20%
Project3 - 20%
2 in-class midterms - 35%
Final Video Paper - 10%
Participation - 10%

You can pick your own groupmates. Prof have asked if we want same teammates for all 3 projects or change teams for each projects (we picked the first one). The group size depends on the class size (this year we have 5 ppl per group). And the teaching team doesn’t give a clear rubric for the projects.

Both midterms are open-notes. No need to write codes. The weighting of the 2 midterms are slightly different. Midterm1 only allows paper notes and midterm2 you can bring a laptop to watch a video regarding to the question.

For your reference:
Project1 - 4.5/5
Project2 - 17.84/20
Project3 - 17.15/20
Midterm1 - 73/100
Midterm2 - 81/100 (Mean: 81.8; SD: 13.4842)
Final Video Paper - 72.5/100 (Mean: 67.3; SD: 8.69)
Participation - 7.5/10
Total: 81.2 (Letter grade: A-)

I think the course is quite competitive, as everyone get very high marks in all assignments. If you want a better score, you may need study hard in midterms.

comment_content:
Content is classic computer science meets humanities. Design paradigms, a method for how to develop an app starting with getting user needs.....interviewing (though you don't actually have to interview) brainstorming, mindmapping, prototyping etc etc.

Lectures with 1000s of pictures and video links to cool projects (some outdated from 2010s). But the content is not like a core subject where you need to strain yourself a lot to remember it. If you go to class and engage with it, you can find it interesting.

In reality you end up memorizing a few key terms for design (Gestalt principles etc) and the whole step by step need finding to product process. To get a good grade on the exams, you need to write fast and alot, but its not that hard. (its mostly common sense stuff e.g point out the design flaws of the mcdonalds kisok using principles from class, draw out a new UI for this website highlight the principles you used)
comment_teaching: Teacher is passionate and kind, she goes through the slides and asks simple questions for a discussion. When you get to an actual tangible theory part e.g a definition or a pros and cons, best to note it down as that is testable.
comment_workload:
A lot of work every week or 2 weeks if you want an A, there is constantly a diary entry, an exercise or a group work to do but they are not very hard just a bit long.

If you get a lazy group you will suffer (or if you don't care you can all coast and get a B- or something). Just do the slides early and think of an interesting idea.
Compress the notes down to a few pages per lecture for the exams.
comment_grading:
Open book midterm 1) You have 1h20 so you need to write as fast as possible, honestly anything as long as it makes sense will get marks.
Open notes with laptop midterm 2)1h20) Your Laptop is used to watch a video which there is a question on, and to store notes to use. The rest is like midterm 1 a bunch or writing/designing questions that you need to write quickly/ scan your notes for a definition or example answer.

Randomly,  there will be an exercise  to do related to the class due next day. That is only known to people who went to class (unless you have a friend to tell you). This is the 'participation grade' 10% there where 4 tasks for this sem.

Group projects are peer reviewed so its a bit meh, you end up getting around 14-18 /20% depending on how much effort your group put in.
Video project (solo) is 10%, and peer reviewed.

comment_content:
2019 Fall Projects
- project 1: interactive ad
- project 2: VR/AR
- project 3: robot (program Pepper/Nao robot to be used for educational purpose)
- video paper: collective well-being

This course is super hands-on. You get a chance to try out different much-hyped techs. This is great in two ways: you get to find out whether you are interested in these fields, and you can have a rough idea of the power and limitation of these techs.

This course is also a good chance to build your portfolio.
comment_teaching:
Killer Features
- Design Process Drill: for each project, we went through the process of selecting user group, identifying problems, brainstorming solutions, creating storyboards, speed-dating with users, prototyping, getting user feedback, etc. Gradually helps me build up a good habit.
- Teamwork Training: each project is a chance to practice and improve my teamwork skills. How do we come to an agreement? How do we divide up work? How do we keep track of progress? These soft skills, unlike hard technical knowledge, will be valuable no matter what job you do in the future.

Also
- teacher: friendly, interactive
- lecture: lots of videos and pictures, no equations
- classmates: stacked; and lots of international students
comment_workload:
- high workload (many hours)
- they are enjoyable hours though (if you enjoy project more than assignments and exams)
- better take it when your other courses are not heavy
comment_grading:
Grading is fair.
Not easy grade, but fair.
Reflects quality of my projects.

comment_content:
The content is interesting and covers all the basics of HCI. It provides a solid overview and explains things like prototyping and user testing as well as more advanced concepts later on. It covers a lot of ground, but is not really able to go in depth.
The projects allow students to put everything into practice.
comment_teaching: The lectures are nice. Not particularly exciting, but usually interesting and relevant.
comment_workload: The group projects require a lot of teamwork and efficient planning is key. It is very easy to spend many hours on small things that will hardly impact your final presentation, since you have very limited time to present and the grade for the project only relies on the presentation.
comment_grading:
Feedback and grading of assignments and presentation is very unclear. Since it depends mostly on peer evaluation of a short presentation, oftentimes it is not so much about the work that was actually put into the project but simply whether the presentation was funny. And while it is of course important to present results well, I think it is sometimes hard to put a  lot of work into user testing and prototyping only to briefly glance over all that in favor of making a few jokes to get good peer grades.
Essentially, to get a good grade the presentation needs to be fun and interesting, while the content is somewhat secondary.

comment_content:
The content mainly focuses on how you can take a humanistic approach while designing tech products.

There is a lot of idea generation, brainstorming, market research and the prototyping.

There is a lot of interesting course content, but it is not very applicable to the projects that we do in class, good for future reference though if you are interested in this field.
comment_teaching:
Professor Ma is very experienced in HCI and has put in a lot of effort to design this course. The project prompts are always interesting.

Classes are fun with a lot of videos and image-based content, sometimes there are activities like design thinking, brainstorming etc.

My only contention would be that professor does not give feedback on the projects. A lot of students worked very hard on their projects, and they never get any feedback, only ambiguous scores from the audience (other students), which is never reflective of whether your idea was actually good or not.
comment_workload: Quite doable if you have a good team.

comment_content:
List of topics:
1. Perception and Color
2. Basic Visualization Principles (e.g. show full scale, show full context..)
3. Data and Visual Channels
4. Task Abstraction
5. Rules of Thumb (e.g. eye beats memory; overview first, zoom and filter, details on demand...)
6. Trajectory (Time and Space) Visualization
7. Multi-dimensional Data Visualization
8. Text Visualization
9. Graph Visualization
10. Validation and Evaluation
11. Narrative Visualization

====Optional Topics=====
12. BitExtract - Financial Data Visualization
13. Explainable AI
14. Flow Visualization
15. Volume Rendering

Topics 1 - 5 are about general concepts. For example, when designing visualization, you better obey the rule 'Eye beats memory' by showing as many things simultaneously on the screen as you can, so as to cater for the human's incapable short-term memory.

Topics 6 - 9, 11 are about various advanced visualizations (not just some simple line graph or bar chart) that you can choose when doing design. One very typical example is parallel coordinates, which are very useful when your dataset consists of many dimensions (google it and you'd see what it is).

Topic 10 introduces research methods to measure/investigate how effective your visualization is. Topics 12 - 15 are more advanced, and are not required in exams (you may want to use these materials in the project and see if professor and TAs would be impressed; in this semester, no group had ever used these materials)

Teaching materials (slides) are basically a collection of works of various researchers (at different universities or organizations). Because of this, the styles and outlooks of these slides are varying from topic to topic, i.e. inconsistent.
IMPORTANT: Almost all topics have more than one slides, but not every slide is used, and even when that slide is taught, it is possible that some of its parts are skipped during lectures. This essentially means you might not want to skip the lectures so that you can know what unnecessary things can be filtered out when revising for the exam.

Overall speaking, contents are not many and not deep. (In some sense, even 1000-level COMP courses teach deeper...) But personally I am quite interested in some of the topics, so I still give a 'B' to this column.
comment_teaching:
Professor's teaching varies with topics as well.

If the professor is not interested in that particular topic, or the professor finds that topic boring (you can tell from his teaching and facial expression), then the teaching becomes more boring.

On the other hand, if the professor knows the topic well or is interested in that topic (say, when he is introducing the project that he was also in charge with), he talks deeper and makes more jokes.

Although the overall teaching is not exemplary, I still give this column a 'B' because the professor is much more effective when answering our questions individually.

Both TAs are generally approachable, especially the local one.

(By the way, each lecture is 1.5hr long, but usually the professor just finished in 1 hr. The remaining time is for in-class ex., if any)
comment_workload:
Very light, compared to other courses. Three reasons:

1. No attendance taken.
- In-class exercises and lab exercises need not be done right before the lesson ends.
- In-class exercises dead at 11.59pm the same day of the lecture
- lab exercises have around a week to finish
- So it is not a problem even when you never show up for the whole semester
- Plus it is not mandatory to do all of them

2. No midterm.

3. Workload of project is usually manageable
- Normally if your group is not aiming very high, the workload would be okay.
- Personally, the biggest challenge is to manage the workload of other courses so that you can have time doing the project of this course...

So in some situations and sense, this course even has a lighter workload than 1000-level COMP course. But I do encourage you not to skip all the lectures and labs, because you might still need them for final and project.
comment_grading:
10% exercise (in-class exercises and lab exercises)
10% TopVis competition
30% Final Exam
50% Group Project

EXERCISE:
- A total of 10 in-class exercises and 8 lab exercises
- Finishing 9 out of 18 of them would give you a full 10% (even when you just do all lab ex. + 1 in-class ex., or just do all the in-class ex.)
- Basically everyone would get this 10%.
- Some in-class exercises would appear (only with slight modification) again in the final exam.
- Lab exercises are mainly done on Google Collaboratory and Observable Notebook.

TOPVIS COMPETITION:
In around the end of April, you need to select/search for a visualization that you think is great, then write a report (about 2 pages is ok) and give a 2-min presentation on it. Basically everyone would get this 10% as well.

FINAL EXAM:
7 problems in total.
- The first problem consists of 10 MC questions. Not asking for specific details, but some general concepts.
- The second and third problems are mainly about the concepts of topics 1-6. One question is about suggesting several time-series visualizations to solving a specific problem, which is related to topic 6.
- The forth to the seventh questions are about visualization designs. We need to sketch (and draw) the outline of visualization and give (annotated) explanations. Try to design a visualization that is effective for comparison purposes and that does not violate the 'eye beats memory' rule of thumb.
- The paper is not difficult, but somehow you need to guess what requirements are set by the professor for the visualization design problems.

GROUP PROJECT:
PHASE I (20% of project mark)
- Need to form a group (~4 people) before the end of February
- Need to give a proposal presentation (8min) in around late-March
- Need to tell what data you have collected, what task you have formulated, and what visualization design you have chosen; you can talk about some initial designs and attempts as well, if there are any

PHASE II (80% of project mark)
- Need to give an 8-min presentation on your final product in around early-May
- Need to describe and explain your visualization design, and what findings can be derived from them. It would be good if these findings are interesting (to the professor and TAs).

Things NOT to do:
- You better avoid using line graphs, bar charts, or any such kinds of simple visualizations; a quote from the professor: "If I can use Excel to achieve these things, then why do I even take this course?"
- You better not say anything like "there is no particular finding" from your visualizations; another quote from the professor: "If there is no meaning, then what is the meaning of your visualization? Your visualization has no meaning as well!"
- The above two quotes are comments that are given by the professor to some groups (poor guys...). You can tell the professor can be quite mean when you've "got on his nerves". So you really don't do the above two things in your presentation!

Things to do:
- If you think your visualization is complicated enough, present this complication clearly so that professor and TAs could give "sufficient appreciation" on this aspect. (A point made by the local TA)
- Usually each group would do at least 4 different kinds of visualizations in total. It would be good if you can integrate all these visualizations together as a system (e.g. a change on one visualization would automatically lead to changes reflected in other visualizations)
- Four criteria for grading the project:
1. The application of knowledge and skill from the course (e.g. have you violated some rules of thumb)
2. Sophistication (complication)
3. Findings (are they major? interesting?)
4. Presentation (yes.. a good presentation would also do you a favor)

ROUGH STATS:
Final: mean ~69, sd ~14 (out of 100)
Project: min ~25, mean ~34-35, max ~45 (out of 50)

Why do I say this course is good-good grade?
Although I am 1SD above mean in final, but final contributes less (30%) to the grading than the project (50%), and our group just got a mean score for the project. So I think I would definitely be in B range, or if there is a miracle, I might got an A-. But it is an A. A 4.0 GPA. I mean wtf. Totally unexpected, really.

You just need COMP2011 to take this course?
For the project, no. Please no. C++ is not useful in this course. You should at least have some basic knowledge on HTML+CSS+JavaScript. If you are an expert in (or acquainted with) front-end web development, or data mining and AI techniques, that would be a great plus!

Tools that might be useful for the project: PowerBI, R (ggplot2/ggvis), Python (Matplotlib, seaborn, ...), JavaScript (react/vue/angular/node), python web server, relational database (MySQL...), mapbox, ......
(So COMP1021 is more useful? Neither)

But for the concepts part (or final exam), you really don't need any prerequisite (even for graph visualization). Because as I said, professor doesn't teach deeply, i.e. many technical parts are just skipped.

Who should avoid taking this course?
People who extremely hate / are not good at presentation. There are 3 presentations throughout the course (two group presentations + one individual), held in late-March, late-April and early-May. This is somehow different from regular COMP courses.

comment_content:
Contents were mostly directly imported from Stanford University's CS231N: Convolutional Neural Networks for Visual Recognition, with some small amount of additional topics from the instructor's own research.

The course goes into more depth in the Math behind Deep Learning (DL) when compared to COMP 4211 (Machine Learning). If you are more interested in the Math behind modern ML, then this is the better course for you. However, if you only want a broad overview of modern ML, COMP 4211 is a better choice.

At any rate, the contents were as follows:
Image classification
Regularization and optimization*
Neural Networks and Backpropagation*
Convolutional neural networks*
CNN architectures*
Training neural networks*
Recurrent neural networks*
Attention and Transformers*
Video Models
Detection and segmentation
Visualizing and understanding
==== Midterm Coverage Up to Here ======
Self-supervsied Learning
Generative models
PixelRNN/CNN
Variational Autoencoders*
Generative Adversarial Networks*
3D Vision
Normalizing Flow and Diffusion Model
Graph Neural Networks
Robot Learning
Generative Video Synthesis

Much of the slides were a direct copy of CS231N.

Though the contents were indeed useful and is close to the current state-of-the-art of Computer Vision, there was no incentive for you to actually learn the materials after the midterm unless you were interested in the content or will be doing research in the area in the future.

Topics covered by COMP 4211 are marked with a asterisk (*), but this course covered most of them in more mathematical detail than COMP 4211.
comment_teaching:
I only attended the first and the last lecture (for project presentation).

Qifeng's lessons were basically slide-reading and were not very interesting. While he was knowledgeable in the field (I found a few of his papers when searching for information for our project, and he did talk about some of his research in the later lectures), self-learning from either videos of CS231N or doing the assignments were much more effective ways to learn the content in my opinion.
comment_workload:
The workload is somehow on par with, or even higher than the COMP 4211 by DY.

Each PA took up all of two weeks (and some late days) to satisfactorily complete. As you were expected to both write Python to accomplish some of the tasks and to complete written questions, both derivation of the required formulae and actual implementation took significant amounts of time.

Since the midterm was open electronic notes, little time was spent on revising for it on my end.

A large proportion of the workload stemmed from the project. Our group did not have enough time during the semester to dedicate to the project, which caused us to defer much of the work until the study break. It took us around 5 full days to train the model, evaluate the results and write the report, and we ended up using 4 late days as well. Please do not defer the workload of the project to the end of the semester like us (i.e. do not deadline fight the project) for the sake of your own health and/or mental stability.
comment_grading:
Grading was reasonably generous.

The grading scheme was as follows:
36% 3 Programming Assignments, 12% Each
29% Midterm
35% Project
- 2% Project Proposal
- 3% Milestone Report
- 4% Interim Presentation
- 20% Final Report

8 late days were allocated for the programming assignments as a whole, and 8 late days were allocated per group for every component of the project (for a total of 16). A maximum of 4 late days can be used on any given component.

I used 1 late day for PA1, 4 late days for PA2 and 3 late days for PA3. Our group allocated 4 late days to the project milestone and 4 late days to the final report.

Programming Assignments
======================
Continuing the trend, programming assignments were also directly copied from Stanford's CS231N. They were quite challenging to complete in general, and you should expect to spend at least two weeks on each assignment. Each assignment included both written inline questions (in the provided Jupyter notebooks) and (vectorized) Python code implementations.

PA1 was on K-Nearest Neighbour Classifier, Support Vector Machines, Softmax Classifer, Two-Layer Neural Networks and Image Feature Extraction.
PA2 was on Multi-Layer Fully-Connected Neural Networks, Batch and Layer Normalization, Dropout, Convolutional Neural Networks and PyTorch.
PA3 was on Image Captioning for RNNs and Transformers, Generative Adversarial Networks, and Self-Supervised Learning. Bonus tasks included a LSTM-based implementation of the RNN part.

The assignments were not on the same level of difficulty as COMP 4211 - while COMP 4211 PAs were mainly training you on the use of popular ML libraries, you are expected to implement many of the functions from these libraries from scratch with only NumPy. The entire PA1, the majority of PA2 and some of PA3 are done in this manner. Possibly the most challenging part was figuring out the (vectorized) implementations of backpropagation for each layer with NumPy only - often, derivation of the correct formulae took me more time than actually writing the code. The most difficult part for me was the implementation of convolutional and spatial group normalization backpropagation in PA2.

You are strongly advised to reserve sufficient time for each assignment. Though solutions can be found online, they are often extremely optimized (and thus hard to understand).

No statistics were released for each programming assignment.

Midterm
======================
Midterms were a mix of multi-answer MCQs, some short questions, and two long questions. MCQs were mostly trivia which can be found on the lecture notes, but some required understanding of the materials over direct copying. Short questions tested the hypothetical outputs of some of the ML models presented with slight variations. One long question was on receptive fields of CNNs, while the other was on gradient descent on K-Means.

The difficulty of the midterm mainly stemmed from two places: the short time limit (one lecture, or around 90 minutes) and the tricky MCQs. The midterm was open electronic notes (but no communication was allowed), so memorization was not a significant factor.

The midterm statistics was as follows:
Low/LQ/Mean/Median/UQ/High/Max:
0/40/53.17/56/65/90/100

Final Project
======================
The project was spread over the entire semester with four graded components - a project proposal, a milestone report, an interim presentation and a final project report (with code).

The project proposal was a ~200 word short description of your project, in which you briefly outline the problem, dataset and proposed model. After you have confirmed the topic of your project, this should not take too long to complete. In our semester, the project proposal was due around mid-October.

The milestone report was 2-3 pages, and should cover your literature survey, technical approach (in greater detail than the project proposal) and preliminary results. It was due around early to mid-November.

The interim presentation was scheduled after the end of all of the taught topics, and was a 4-5 minute presentation on your current progress. The PowerPoint slides must be submitted in advance before the first groups present. The PPTs were due around mid- to late November.

The final report was supposed to be formatted with the given LaTeX template, and should be structured as a Computer Vision Conference-style paper. It (along with your project code) was due before the end of the study break.

Since our group did not have sufficient time to work on the project, our interim presentation and milestone report did not include any notable results. Much of the work was done around the end of the semester (see the Workload section).

The distributions for all the project components were not disclosed.

Overall Grading
======================
Since most of the statistics are missing (not good practice in my opinion), I have no basis for comparison with other courses. Scoring ~90 per project component, ~90 per PA and UQ for the midterm was sufficient for a B+ in my case.
