3 Programming Assignments (from Stanford cs231n, typically implementing different core components to vision models and applying it to a dataset like CIFAR10)
1 Midterm (Open Note, could use notes/materials on your computer and any other links on the course website)
1 Final Project (Any computer vision topic, groups of 2-3)
comment_grading: You'll basically get 100 on all the assignments and the final project (as long as you put at least some effort in). Grading all comes down to performance on the midterm which typically has one or two hard questions that will determine how well you do. The mean for the midterm is typically around 50-60. Despite this, the professor actively tries to give as many people a good grade as possible.

comment_content:
A large part of content comes from Stanford's CS231n and Michigan's EECS 498-007 / 598-005. Professor will also share his research related to Computer Vision, which is impressive and inspiring.

Content:
1. Image classification
2. Loss function and optimization
3. Introduction to neural networks
4. Convolutional neural networks (CNN)
5. Training neural networks
6. Deep learning hardware and software
7. CNN architectures
8. Recurrent neural networks (RNN)
9. Attention and Transformers
10. Detection and segmentation
11. Generative models
12. Visualizing and understanding
13. Video Models
14. Self-supervised Learning
15. Deep 3D Vision
16. Learning-based Sensing Technology
17. Invertibility in Image Processing and Restoration
18. Other Research Review...

Very fruitful content and advanced knowledge involved.

Coursework:
1. Programming Assignments x3, each 12%
2. Midterm Exam, 35%
3. Project: 29%
- Proposal: 2%
- Milestone: 3%
- Presentation: 4%
- Report: 20%

Having late policy which allows you to share 8 late days for assignment and each up to 4 days. For group project, you can have a group up to 3 people and share another 8 late days in total.
comment_teaching:
During the lectures, cqf mainly just reads the slides and add some explanations to some complicated concepts. However, his voice is too soft and I can even hear better in zoom rather than sitting in the classroom, which is ironic. Also, his delivery skill is not good such that it is quite difficult to understand what he wants to present even the concepts or contents are easy.

There are some lecture videos on Youtube of CS231n. You may want to watch those videos to understand the major content of this course, which is recommended. The lecture slides are also mainly come from CS231n.

There are Lab session for helping students to understand the coding in deep learning and help them for finishing the assignments. You can watch the lab videos if you want on the course website and visit the related tutorials posted, although those hyperlinks are mostly outdated or 404 not found. D:

Assignments are copied from CS231n, you can find many useful reference on web. However, the assignments are using outdated version of Tensorflow and PyTorch (Tensorflow v1 D:). You may need to set up your own environment and even modify the code of utilities provided.

The research of cqf is inspiring and impressive, which may be favorable for those passionate in Computer Vision.
comment_workload:
Average or High.

For those who are new to deep learning, you may need time to follow the contents and understand it. If you have the relevant knowledge on it, then the workload should not be high for you.

Assignment is not very difficult. You can refer to Lab assignment help session or online resources for helping you to finish it.

Midterm is quite difficult to me as I don't have any knowledge in machine learning before. It is open book so that you can view your notes on your notebook, which saves your time from reciting those concepts and architectures.

Project workload is varied and depended. You can choose any  topic you want related to Computer Vision. The most time-consuming part is the final report. It needs to be written in CVPR style for 6-8 pages.
comment_grading:
Based on curve, generally the grading is good for me.

My score:
Assignment: Full for all (mean for each assignment is around 90-95 / 100)
Midterm: 74/100, mean 70.33
Project: Full for Proposal, Milestone and presentation (whether many students full for these parts also). 93/100 for final report  (mean is around 92.x)

Final grade: B+

comment_content:
A combination of Stanford's CS231n and Michigan's EECS498-007. The professor also shares some of his research results regarding computer vision.

Youtube has a collection of Stanford CS231n offered in Spring 2017, which I highly recommend watching while studying this course. The syllabus is basically the same.

Three Python programming assignments. Can be easily sourced from various Github pages. Coding from scratch may take days and requires proficiency in NumPy and PyTorch. Luckily you can learn these packages when doing homework with these references.

The midterm is conventional. No coding and mostly chain rule. I attach one of the past papers to give you an idea.

The project requires 1-3 people per group. It could be a realization of a conference paper, some experiments on a new dataset, etc. etc. At last, you need to write a research paper of 6-8 pages in CVPR style.
comment_teaching:
Professor Chen mostly reads the slides. But since the slides are of high quality, I think it's not a bit of a problem.

There is a lab session. TAs will go through homework and introduce some usage of PyTorch of Tensorflow. Totally optional.
comment_workload:
Average workload.

Each assignment takes less than a week to finish. However, more time is needed if no prior Numpy or Anaconda experience.

Midterm takes about a weekend to review.

Project workload largely depends on the topic your group chooses. I would say it's time-consuming to write 6-8 pages in CVPR style. Just go check one of the CVPR papers to get a grip of what that means.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme:
Assignments: 3 * 12% = 36%
Midterm: 35%
Project:
--Proposal: 2%
--Milestone: 3%
--Presentation: 4%
--Report: 20%

There is a late policy in this course. Assignments share a total of 8 late days with up to 4 late days per assignment. Project share a total of 8 total days as well.

Curve grading but fair. Here are some statistics of me and my friends:
96/100: A+
94/100: A
92/100: A-
The bins seem tight, but since everyone is doing great in assignments and projects, the only difference lies in midterm. Get prepared!

comment_content:
大部分內容同 stanford 嘅 cs231n 同埋 michgen 嘅 Deep Learning for Computer Vision 非常相似。呢兩科網上都有片睇。十分推薦想讀呢科嘅人去睇下自己仲唔仲意，或者當預習。最尾professor會介紹下佢自己個研究。所以內容會比果兩科多啲同新啲。

功課方面一共三份同 cs231n 差唔多嘅功課，網上好多 source。最後一份 project，最多3人一組。final report係論文格式，6-8 page，當鍛煉下寫paper。

一個 mid term 冇 final。多數學嘢，冇coding。mid term嘅source就好難搵，睇題目非常基礎，比如問下RNN入面gradient點計出來，計下CNN嘅output size。mid term算難。
comment_teaching: zoom上課就有啲悶。呢幾年網上嘅ML或者tensorflow嘅course都多，推薦唔明嘅時候搵搵呢啲。或者問下TA。
comment_workload:
三份功課每份內容都多，就算睇source都要一段時間先睇得明，精通python有優勢。

三份功課共享8日delay 但每樣最多4日，通常後兩個會用到幾日。project嘅 proposal, milestone, final report都一樣8日delay，我就用晒去 milestone 同 final report。
comment_grading: 整科 weighted total 有 91+ 有A。我估mid term同project係容易拉大差距嘅地方，整體grade算好。

comment_content:
COMP 4521 offers a broad, practical introduction to mobile app development with a strong emphasis on the Android ecosystem. The course blends lectures, tutorials, labs, and assignments, but the real value lies in its hands‑on, implementation‑driven approach.
What the Course Covers

The curriculum is surprisingly comprehensive for a single-semester course:
- Android Fundamentals: Activities, Intents, UI interaction, and the overall Android app structure.
- Jetpack Compose: Modern declarative UI development using Kotlin — a major focus of the course.
- Local Data:
        > DataStore Preferences for key-value storage
        > Room for relational databases
- Cloud Integration: Firebase Authentication, Firestore, Realtime Database, Cloud Storage.
- Core Mobile Features: CameraX, GPS, multimedia, notifications, biometrics, gestures.
- Networking: RESTful APIs using Retrofit (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE).
- Architecture: Activity lifecycle, state management, MVVM, ViewModel, StateFlow.
- Navigation: Multi-screen apps with Navigation Compose.
- Cross-Platform: Light intro to Flutter and React Native.
- AI/ML: Basics of ML + mobile ML tools like TensorFlow Lite and ML Kit.

Tools & Environment
- Kotlin as the primary language
- Android Studio, emulators, Gradle scripts, resource management
- Students must be comfortable with debugging, project structure, and build tools

Assessment Weight
Group Project 60%
Assignment 15%
Lab                 15%
In-class Quiz 10%

Labs are practical and straightforward (e.g., fixing bugs, building a currency converter with Compose).
The Group Project is the main highlight — a full mobile app with proposal, progress reports, final report, and presentation.
comment_teaching:
The course is taught in a weekly Saturday morning session, but attendance is not taken — and it shows. Fewer than 10 students typically show up, and it’s very possible to skip the entire semester and still do fine.

Lectures are monotone and quite boring, but the material itself is easy to self-study. With modern AI tools, you can honestly learn most of the content faster on your own.

The instructor is kind, responsive, and professional — quick email replies, timely feedback, and even holiday greeting emails. TA presence is unclear; you may never see them.

Overall: teaching style is dry, but the instructor is supportive and approachable.
comment_workload:
Extremely light.

-> You can just skip all lectures without any consequences.
-> One day of revision is enough for the quizzes, and the two quizzes have non‑overlapping syllabi.
-> Group project takes some time, but manageable — especially if you reuse ideas (e.g., your FYP proposal) and have good group mates.
-> Labs are simple and can be “vibe-coded.”
-> Individual assignment is an app comparison; using HCI principles (e.g., from COMP 4461) makes it easy.

Suggested comparison topics include:

    KMB vs Citybus (I did this)

    HK bank apps

    KFC vs McDonald’s

    Any two apps of your choice

Overall: very manageable workload, especially compared to other COMP courses.
comment_grading:
My grades: (Stats aren't published)

    Proposal: 4.5 / 6

    Presentation Video: 10 / 12

    Final Project: 32 / 42

    In-class Quiz 1: 4.17 / 5

    In-class Quiz 2: 4.5 / 5

    Labs: 5/5, 4.5/5, 4.75/5

    Individual Assignment: 13 / 15

Final Grade: A‑

The grading seems fair and not overly harsh. If you put in reasonable effort for the project and do the quizzes, an A-range grade is very achievable.

comment_content:
As a person with no mobile app development background who didn't take the COMP3021 Java course, the content of this course is extremely difficult for me to pick up. Every lecture he gives talks about topics that are new, and it all sounds confusing to me. So, I never attended his lectures after the add-drop period and watched the recordings only to prepare myself for the in-class quizzes.

If you are someone who already knows how to do mobile app development, feel free to omit my post...
comment_teaching:
I think he assumes everyone knows how to do Java Programming, and due to Kotlin is somehow similar to Java, he just spent a very short amount of time teaching us the syntax (Or more precisely, he just used a Kotlin program to demonstrate how that program works).

The time I write this review is during the midterm period, and I can say I still have no idea how to code in Kotlin and make it a runnable application. Throughout this long period, I used GenAI to generate my code (Yes, GenAI is allowed in this course). And tbh I felt lost.

There is only ONE 3 hour long lecture per week. It is acceptable that we take a 10-15 minute break during the lecture. But the thing is, he only keeps us for 2 hours per lecture. That means the time we can listen to him is only half the lecture time for a normal course! So on one hand I cannot understand the content he delivers, and another hand is he simply won't give us more details about the topics he is covering.

In my point of view, he is just there providing you some overall briefing but not teaching you the real bits of knowledge.

Oh, the two TAs are also awesome! They can fly through 80 pages of slides within 40 minutes in their tutorials, good job:)
comment_workload:
Not that heavy if you know what you are doing and you know how to code.

Few labs + 2 in-class quizzes + a project is all the coursework.

Even though I am using AI to "assist" my coding, I can finish each lab within 2 hours, and the project took us only a few days to code the overall structure. I think the whole project can be done within 2 weeks.

comment_content:
Topics:
1. Introduction & Environment settings
2. Activity & View
3. Checkbox & ImageButton
4. RadioButton & ScrollView
5. Spinner & UserPreference
6. Intent
7. Google Service
8. Multimedia
9. Advanced Topics (SQLite, notification, etc.)

Basic Android development stuff. Although I have learnt Flutter to develop app in my internship but I still found them quite useful and interesting as it is a different aspect.

The development platform is Android Studio, with base language Java. If you take COMP3021 you will have very large advantage (Sarcastically, Charles Zhang's COMP3021 fuxked me last year but it helps me now. If you want to know more about this visit COMP3021 comment page)

However, even though you haven't take COMP3021, the course content is still quite simple. It contains very less theory. Most of the things are straightforward, high-level and practical. The code is not hard to comprehend as well. You will not find any problem even for self-studying. Due to the high workload from FYP and other courses, I don't have much spare time to have the 3 hours lecture weekly, but I still managed to handle the course content.

I would say, the difficulty of this 4000 level COMP course is somehow equivalent to 3000, or even 2000 level COMP course only. A student who just finished COMP1022P or COMP2012 is able to pass this course as well (In reality you generally can't because of the prerequisite, although I don't see any necessity to have the knowledge of 2611 and 3511 for handling this course)
comment_teaching:
New lecturer since Prof. Muppala retires recently.

A very nice guy, aside from grading (describe later). He is a local lecturer so his does not have much accent. His voice is quite soft but the pace is fine. However, in the lecture, he just go through the concept and the demo code in the lecture notes once while they are quite self-explanatory. As
a result, the overall pace of the lecture is quite slow. Therefore, it is not really necessary to go to the lecture.

Ah yes, for this semester, the lecture time is FRIDAY NIGHT 7PM - 10PM. I can only say, who will be that willing to have a lecture on Happy Friday night till 10PM? Fortunately, the prof is kind, he provides Zoom real-time lecture as well as recording so you can just have real-time lecture at home or watch the recording at an time you want.

He is really kind, he gives out many time in the lecture for the student to run the code and he always gives time for break as the lecture time is 3 hours. He also extends the deadline of all the project components for one week because students request it. I praise his entire effort in this course...

aside from grading.
comment_workload:
1. Group project
Make an app in Android Studio. You must use this (The base language taught is Java, but I don't know whether Kotlin is ok). You can't use other things like Flutter.

Three phases:

1.1 Initial application idea
Write short report to draft the design of your app.

1.2 Project proposal
Write a proposal to introduce the design structure of your app

1.3 Final product
Final implementation of the project, which includes three parts: Source code, video presentation, final report.

The workload of the project depends on the complexity of your project. My group used two days to finish app idea and proposal respectively. We used around two weeks to finish the final product part (We just start this part after the FYP presentation). It looks like we make a good product with this amount of effort, but guess what, I got fuxked by the exam. GG nah hah.

2. Final exam
Most of the thing have been mentioned in "GRADING". Straightforward "COMP3111 style" exam, stupid as fuxk. It is close-book but it allows you to bring a one-sided cheatsheet. Remember to put everything into the cheatsheet then you are good to go.

3 parts:
a.) MC (40%)
20 MC questions which ask trivial concept, such as what is the use of a specific component, that can be directed referenced from the lecture notes (i.e. your cheatsheet)

b.) Coding (45%)
Gives you the screen layout and some specification, then you need to "hand-write" the entire code block in Main function into the answer booklet.

The features that is requested is trivial, such as pressing a button then show some image, etc. There are 4 questions, you only need to pick 3 of them to answer. Basically, it is not a big problem if you jot down the function into the cheatsheet. The only problem is that it is exhausting for my hands

c.) Essay (15%)
3 topics on Android development topic. You only need to pick one of them to answer. Write less than 250 words

The topics for this semester is:
i. Advantage of Android App Development in Business
ii. How to plan for a successful Android App Development project
iii. How to develop a secure Android App (Kind of? I forget a little bit)

Last reminder: In the exam, try to be extremely careful otherwise you will get fuxked like me as well.
comment_grading:
1. Project (50%)
-> 1.1 Initial app Idea (10%)
-> 1.2 Project proposal (15%)
-> 1.3 Final product & presentation (25%)
2. Final Examination (50%)

Final grade: B+

Everything in this course is fine, but when it comes to grading, it falls apart. Probably it is not hard to get decent grade in this course and I get decent grade as well, but the experience just annoyed me anyway.

For this semester, the grading scheme add one more thing: A final exam. The prof claimed that this is used to make the distribution become more even. Unfortunately, this the beginning of a nightmare. It completely backfires.

Firstly, let me ask you, if there is a COMP course that contains a project and an extremely easy, low fault tolerance exam, which COMP course will you come up?

COMP3111 right?

Yes, the grading style of this course is very similar to COMP3111. Why? Because the exam is so fucking easy. MC questions ask straightforward concept question (Sth like what is the use of a specific component or if you want to print a string on console then which function should you use). Coding questions only ask us to implement trivial features as well. The exam time limit is 3 hours, but people start leaving 1 hour after the exam starts.

What is worse than 3111 is that, in 3111, at least you need to recite the exercise or "Hints" that Kenneth gives you because the exam is close-book right? However, for this course, the exam allows you A CHEATSHEET. That means, after you put everything from the lecture notes into the cheatsheet, the MC question can be directly referenced from the cheatsheet. You can directly copy the function into the coding question as well. That further narrows the performance difference between students. I can imagine that even 1 MC will already cost you a subgrade.

One powerful evidence is that, I got B+ eventually. That sounds not really bad right? But my project partner gets an A+. I mean like, WTFFFFFFFF??????????????

This means either, we have different score for project, which is less likely as the prof does not know the division of labor; or I screw up the final exam. However, although I don't have much self-satisfaction, I would say I know over 85% of the question in the exam (Most of the people should have at least percentage as well). At least I didn't struggle a lot and I am able to do it smoothly. I just can't understand how large is the score difference between us. If this exam had not existed like in the past, probably I would have gotten A range.

Another thing which is even more annoying than 3111 is that, this course's grading is non-transparent as fuxk. There is nothing call transparency in this course. It is just a solid concrete wall which blocks everything.

At least Kenneth will post all the score and statistics for you right? But this prof did not even publish any numerical score or statistics or project feedback for us. Our score is like being blocked by a 國防布 (For non-Chinese speaker just treat this as solid wall which blocks everything). I cannot see anything about my performance or the overall performance (完全沒有畫面.jpg). I can't even know how bad I have done in the exam. The only thing I know is that, I get a B+, and me and my teammates differs from a LETTER GRADE.

I don't know who propose the idea of having an exam on this course. It is either the prof himself or he is forced to do that by CSE department, but I would say anyway: This exam is stupid.

Probably this prof is new to this university and he doesn't know the hidden rule of this school: "An easy exam out of generosity will backfire on the student" because it will become a carefulness testing with low fault tolerance anyway.

Although I genuinely think the prof does not deliberately do all these things, anyway, stop making any "COMP3111 style" exam and grading (Easy content, low fault tolerance) in the future. You can either test more difficult things to differentiate ability or just wiped the exam out.

The prof claims that the exam can make the performance distribution more even, NO, IT'S NOT. Everyone just score high with small score differences. This type of exam does not differentiate the ability of students. It only differentiate how careful students are. Is that really the thing that you want to differentiate? What is the point?

Sorry for being emotional and typing a bunch of junk in this part, but I am quite disappointed. I think this is the closest chance that I can get an A range in COMP course (alongside 3111, which I get B+ as well) but for both times I get fuxked by a stupid carefulness testing exam. Well, at least I get my first and last  A+ from CORE1501 and FYP (Thank you Desmond...), as well as my first and last dean list in this school, which comforts me a lot @@

comment_workload:

As for the final, again, just copy everything from the lecture notes down on the cheatsheet, and you will be good to go.
comment_grading:
This course prior did not have a final exam, however it seems the school saw fit to add a 50% Final Exam to this class this semester, alongside with the project.

App Idea (10%): Think of an app with your groupmates (2-3 people per group, you can also do it solo), write a short post on CANVAS explaining it.

Project Proposal (15%): Similar to the app idea, but to explain in it a proposal, that also goes over the planned technical implementation of things.

Course Project (Including Presentation) (25%): After finishing the project, write a report (reusing the project proposal mostly) about the implementation and results, and to make a video explaining the app that you have built.

Examination (50%): COMP1021 style exam. Since the contents covered in the lecture are so limited, you can write the entire course on the 1 page cheat sheet provided. In the exam, there are 20 MCQs, 3 short questions and 1 final essay. For the MCQ and short questions, if you have prepared the cheat sheet well you can answer them easily. And for the "essay", you have to write stuff about "What are the advantages of Android development for businesses" or "How to develop a secure Android app", since it's only 250 words at most, you can just write whatever. (Average: will be very high, will update later)

Letter grade released without any details, very opaque.

comment_workload:
Just a project, but we have to complete it in 3 phrases.

1. Initial Application Idea - just brainstorm any idea of the app you want to build on 1 page. Can be different from real project.

2. Proposal
- Description of the application structure including activities, services, and broadcast receivers
- Details of the data storage including design of the content provider
- Details of the specific Android features like multimedia, graphics, location, maps etc. that are used in the application
- Expected results from your project

3. Demo, Report and Code

Note that you are not required to necessarily use Android Studio to build your app. From what Prof. said, React Native, Ionic and iOS are also welcomed.

I used Ionic and consumed a week to complete the entire project (development + testing), so the workload is light for me. But if you are not familiar with app development, I suggest you start coding earlier.
comment_grading:

As the course is project-based, the grading is absolute. For your reference, 88% in the total marks might receive an A-.

comment_content: The course covers three main areas requiring solid ELEC background and SSH skills for Raspberry Pi work. Signal processing involves chirps, frequency domain, FFT, and TDOA concepts (ELEC1200 knowledge helpful but rusty for many). Wireless communication covers protocols like WiFi, ALOHA, and LoRa (ELEC3120 beneficial but not essential as professor explains thoroughly). Human activity recognition introduces machine learning with sensor data, taught by a different professor making it harder to follow.
comment_teaching: Labs 1-3 focus on signal processing using FFT codes, sin/cos functions, and Doppler effects to generate graphs and determine 2D object positioning - setup is lengthy but straightforward. Labs 4-6 explore LoRa features, Arduino coding for retransmission, and campus signal coverage testing across floors 3-5. Labs 7-8 provide PyTorch introduction and model improvement exercises for higher marks.
comment_workload: Extremely high workload, especially overwhelming for students with limited programming background beyond basic Python. The LoRa and PyTorch sections are particularly brutal without prior experience, requiring significant time investment to understand packet structures, ACK protocols, and machine learning implementations. Projects start dangerously late in the semester with zero budget provided, forcing students to rush through complex implementations while scrambling for resources. The combination of individual lab assignments demanding full concept mastery and group projects requiring coordination creates a perfect storm of stress. Success heavily depends on forming strong study groups for homework discussions and securing reliable, knowledgeable groupmates - having a disappearing team member makes the already challenging assignments nearly impossible to complete solo. Students often find themselves pulling all-nighters trying to bridge knowledge gaps while simultaneously working on multiple demanding assignments.
comment_grading: Assessment includes class participation (10%), midterm exam (30%), individual lab projects (30% total, 10% each), and group project (30%) covering proposal, execution, demonstration, and report. The open-notes midterm is quite difficult, covering lab content like Free Space Path Loss with incomplete derivation explanations, plus questions on chirps, ALOHA, and FSPL concepts.

comment_teaching:
Unlike many other COMP courses in UST that focus on theory, the instructor mainly puts his focus on the real-world use cases. His teaching style avoids dense mathematical proofs in favour of actionable insights (for example, he would go onto Etherscan to show a random transaction to demonstrate lecture content)

If you want to dive deep into the cryptographic primitives like SHA256 or merkle trees, or the theory behind consensus algorithms like Paxos or Nakamoto, then this course might not be the best choice for you.

Imo, if you know nothing about blockchain and you're curious to know more about it (as a beginner course), this course is a great choice for you.
comment_workload:
Lightweight if you compare it to other courses like COMP3711 (just my opinion because I'm not really good at all those theory stuff).

Overall only 4 assignments, 1 individual project, and 1 open-book final exam.

No tutorials or whatsoever, and the professor replies to your email really quickly if you have any difficulties or confusion regarding lecture materials.

No attendance, which is probably the reason why not many students attended the class (it's my lunch time for this semester T.T).

Have lecture recordings, meaning you can skip the lectures if you want (I'm not encouraging you to do it tho :] )

The assignments are lightweight (just to test your basic understanding of the materials taught within the past 2-3 weeks), which could probably be done within an hour.

The project basically asks you to make a simple DApp from scratch, for which you can ask for assistance from AI. Imo, since AI is so powerful these days, thinking about your DApp idea (whether it's innovative or useful) would be more important than the programming part itself.

The final exam is open book, which means you can print all the lecture notes into the exam, and almost all answers in the exam can be found in the lecture materials. If you read through all the lecture materials in detail before the exam, it shouldn't be too challenging for you.

Hope this review helps.

comment_content:
This course is about how to carry out various group activities (auction,lottery,voting ...) without trusting anyone and without leaking any more information than needed (no one needs to know that you have voted for Alice, they only need to know that your vote is valid).

Real world currencies like USD are controlled by the government, if the government doesn't like you they may freeze you bank account. They also know the details (who, when ...) of your transections so if the government gets hacked there'll be privacy issues. Cryptocurrencies attempts to solve this problem by removing the role of government (decentralization) and instead everyone is equal. However, as you probably noticed, there'll many issues e.g. Can Bob spends the $1 that Alice owns ? Can Alice deny the existence of a past transection ? You'll learn the concepts needed (digital signature, blockchain ...) to answer those questions in this course.

Once you have such a decentralized and secured cryptocurrencies, there're many things you can do like auctions, money laundering, gambling ... This course will teach you the techniques to design secured and privacy-preserving protocols for them. (Note: we're only interested in the theory, don't do illegal stuff IRL, you can still be caught like developers of Tornado Cash)

You'll also have the opportunity to implement those protocols using solidity programming language and simulate using VM, it's very fun !

One of the best teachers I've ever met. Amir teaches by drawing and handwriting (with colors) on his digital notebook, this keeps my attention and helps me understand much better.

Before introducing a new concept, he'll first try to solve the problem with only old concepts and discuss the weaknesses of the approach. This ensures a smooth transition between topics and also motivates you to learn.

Amir is an expert and is also very active in this field, occasionally he will share some of his and TA's recent papers with us. I can see his passion both in research and teaching.

If you have difficulty understanding a concept or want clarification, feel free to email him. Typically he'll reply in the next working day and will provide helpful guidance.

comment_content: The course covers the classic seven-layer OSI model from the physical layer all the way up to the application layer (though these days people more often discuss things in terms of the TCP/IP four-layer model). It goes into fairly detailed explanations of the data link layer (things like Ethernet and ARP), the network layer (IP protocol along with routing protocols such as RIP, OSPF, and BGP), the transport layer (with a strong focus on TCP and UDP, especially TCP’s congestion control mechanisms), and several typical application-layer protocols (HTTP, DNS, SMTP, and P2P systems like BitTorrent). Occasionally it also touches on some more advanced topics such as Quality of Service (QoS), multicast, and IPv6. Overall, the course systematically walks you through the entire journey of how data packets travel from your device to the destination, helping you really understand how the different layers of the Internet work together underneath the surface.
comment_teaching: He is a very enthusiastic teacher who is also deeply familiar with the subject matter. I think he is one of the rare truly good professors at HKUST. If you're willing to chat with him about the course content after class, he is more than happy to spend a lot of time discussing things with you.
comment_workload: The workload for this course is very light. The assignments each take only about 2 hours, and there are just three of them throughout the entire semester. There’s also one project that lasts a month, but you can realistically finish it in 2–3 days.
comment_grading: Compared to other COMP courses at HKUST, COMP4621 is noticeably more lenient when it comes to grading and final marks. The exams, however, are quite unique each time—the professor personally designs all the questions, so you can’t rely too heavily on memorizing past papers from previous years. For review, the best preparation is to thoroughly work through the end-of-chapter exercises in the textbook, as those problems essentially serve as the main question bank for both the midterm and final exams.

comment_content:
Nothing special. Just standard 5 layers of network. The content and slides mainly follows the book Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach.

The first half of the course (application layer & transport layer) is practical and somehow interesting. However, after the midterm, the content (network layer & link layer) seems to become more and more boring. As far as I'm concerned, The second half of the course seems not so useful unless you want a job that is highly related to (the bottom of) network.

p.s. The professor of this semester is the designer of COMP 2611, so it is not surprising that this course is like another 2611 to me.
comment_teaching:
Satisfactory teaching. The professor can explain things very clealy. He usually includes a lot of examples, drawings and simile to help us understand the contents better. Generally speaking, his teaching is slow-paced, so I think is not hard to keep up with him in lectures. Bad news is that he failed to finish everything in time, so he posted some extra videos for us to watch. He is also willing to answer questions.

Lab tutorials, however, are completely useless. The TAs just read everything on their slides, without any passion. I found reading their slides myself far more efficient than listening to the TAs. I guess their research area has nothing to do with computer network.

Recordings are available on canvas. Although the prof claimed that he will not release the recordings if few people go to his lecture, we ended up getting all of them.
comment_workload: Please refer to the grading part for details. I think the workload is very low for a 4xxx-level course. You can treat the workload as another COMP 2611.
comment_grading:
### Grading Scheme

- Homeworks: 20%
- Midterm: 25%
- Project: 20%
- Final: 35%

### Homeworks

3 homeworks in total. Each of them takes around 1-2 hours. Some of them may ask you to summarize a protocol taught in class, while some ask you to self-study certain new topic and give answers to some questions never mentioned in class. For me, it is very hard to get high marks, possibly because I am a lazy guy who don't like to write too many words for a question >_<

stat:
- Homework 1: Low=45.0 LQ=52.00 Mean=54.58 Median=56.00 UQ=56.00 High=60/60
- Homework 2: Low=43.5 LQ=49.13 Mean=51.99 Median=52.50 UQ=55.38 High=59/60
- Homework 3: Low=28.0 LQ=45.13 Mean=46.86 Median=47.75 UQ=50.00 High=56/60

### Project

The project is a chatting room with file sharing functionality. The coding is done in C. I spent ~2 days on it. Basically you need to use the things taught in labs to fill in the blanks of the skeleton code. I think this project is not harder than the one in COMP 2611, mainly because C is at least better than assembly ;)

They provided 5 test scripts for us to test our implementation, so it is not hard to get a high score. There is also one bonus task (2%), so you will see the highest score is 22 out of 20.

stat:
Low=4 LQ=19.6 Mean=18.82 Median=UQ=20 High=22/20

### Exams

Generally speaking, the questions in the exams are much trickier than the examples in lectures. For some questions, you need to be kind of smart and also some math capability to get to the point.

The questions come from various but reasonable sources. At the end of the midterm, I heard some student saying that they have seen a question in a past paper, although I don't know where they get the past papers because the prof never released any. The prof also claimed that a question in the midterm comes from the exercises in the textbook. Unfortunately, I didn't check the textbook and messed up the midterm.

As for the final, I remember that one question was very similar to an example in the extra videos. Another question comes from the addition notes at the end of some slides. Besides, the prof mentioned in class that some content will probably appear in the final, and it did. Therefore, I feel that you may need to pay attention to all those details in the course to do well in the exam.

By the way, you can print all the lecture slides and bring them to the exam. However, you cannot add additional marks or notes on them. Calculators are allowed but proved not useful.

stat:
- Midterm: Low=19 LQ=47 Mean=56.06 Median=54.25 UQ=67.88 High=85.5/100
- Final: Low=0 LQ=61.5 Mean=66.61 Median=67 UQ=77.5 High=90/100

### Others

Although attendance is not included in the grading scheme, the prof randomly take attendance in lectures and tutorials. You also need to submit some quiz after each lab session, which also does not appear in the scheme. I have no idea whether these things are included in grading or not.

### My Scores

- Homework 1: 45/60
- Homework 2: 51/60
- Homework 3: 47/60
- Project: 22/20 (with bonus)
- Midterm: 71.25/100
- Final: 90/100

Letter grade: A+

The grading is very good, I would say.

comment_content:
Networking is a must for CS students. Focus on UDP, TCP, IP, and Socket, they’re common in job interviews. The first half is engaging, covering protocols like HTTP, DNS, and P2P networks. The later part (post-midterm) feels like OS and is less interesting.

Course Syllabus
Introduction (3 lectures)

Introduction to Networking

Network Architectures

Application Layer (5 lectures)

Application Layer Protocols: HTTP, SMTP, DNS, Streaming

Peer-to-Peer Networks and Applications (BitTorrent)

Transport Layer (7 lectures)

The TCP/IP and Internet Architecture

Transport Protocols: TCP and UDP

Reliable data transfer
TCP Congestion Control

Network Layer: datapath and

Network Layer Control (7 lectures)

Network Protocol: IP

Internetworking

Switch/Router Architecture
Packet Forwarding/Scheduling and buffer management
Network Routing and Routing Algorithms

Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)

IP Version 6 (IPv6)

Link Layer and Local Area Networks (3 lectures)

Link Layer Issues Error detection and Framing

Multiple Access Protocols: Aloha, CSMA

Ethernet

Bridges and Switches

Textbook
James F. Kurose and Keith W. Ross Computer Networks: A Top Down Approach Featuring Internet, 7th/8th Edition, Pearson,  (6th Edition also is fine but it lacks some small parts)

Larry L. Peterson and Bruce S. Davie, Computer Networks: A Systems Approach, Morgan Kaufmann Publishers

W. Richard Stevens, Bill Fenner and Andrew M. Rudoff, UNIX Network Programming Vol. 1, 3rd ed., Addison-Wesley.

comment_content:
You learn how the internet works and how computers communicate with each other. The course covers the five Internet layer stack and teaches from the top to bottom (except the lowest one physical layer). There might be some extra contents such as wireless networking, security, etc. and you might also learn something about hacking.
About 90% concepts and 10% probabilities. Some stuff from COMP3711, 3511, and MATH2411 are also there. Four written assignments and one programming project. Then midterm and final.
Programming project was quite challenging. Basically you need to make a multi-threaded server-client using java from scratch (no skeleton).

comment_content:
Content is rich(i), but if you're looking for hands-on stuff, you'll be disappointed in this course, except for the few labs on basic web attacks and binary attacks. Why disappointed? Because the majority of the course focuses on a high-level view, utilising offensive Kali tools (which automate a lot of the hacking process) and defensive tools (which automate a lot of the filtering and protection). IMO, Ricci does a good job at striking a balance between practice and theory; even though there's A LOT of content (and there’s not enough time to cover everything in one sem).

(Disclaimer) Personally, I've already experienced hands-on attacks and fun in the Firebird CTF courses (COMP2633-4633). But cyber reality is far from just CTFs. Hence, I took this course to get a holistic understanding of attacks and countermeasures, learn Kali hacking tools, and familiarise myself with Windows. (Note one of the keywords in my title: vocational.) A lot of enterprise machines use Windows, and I haven't used Windows in a decade, so the opportunity to use Windows again was nice.

Seriously, if you want to have fun with cyberattacks and mess around with vulnerabilities and low-level shenanigans, I suggest taking 2633-4633 instead of this course. It's much more rewarding, exhilarating, and addicting (but more time-consuming). The “hacking” labs of 4632 are a joke compared to what you learn in 2633-4633 (assuming you put in effort, ofc).

Those who are saying "oh 4632 bad because PHP sucks" are missing the point. For one thing, PHP is pervasive in the business world due to WordPress and other popular CMSs. Any self-respecting security professional or web engineer should be aware of that. WordPress plugins have so many potential vulnerabilities, to the extent that some bug bounty ecosystems dedicate themselves to WordPress.

Another thing: technology constantly evolves—no surprise. What you take out of this course shouldn't be the specific technology, but the underlying processes, reasoning, and attitude. Like- why do you think they teach C++ in 201X and not Python? Because pointers. Raw, efficient algorithms, stripped of any garbage collection or automated process. Low-levelness. Memory models. Stack and heap. Do you know how many people abuse Arduino String over std::string_view or std::string? Software. Enginerring. Tehcnology.

Also don't focus on the version and tool that much. Do you think the C++11 they teach is enough for jobs? Companies are moving to C++17 now (if not C++20), and C++23 is around the corner. Not to mention the libraries/tools—Boost, Qt, clang, CMake—those are also a thing. One of my friends once complained about Flutter: "Haiyah, version updated. Need to learn so many things again." (He's been using Flutter for a decent while.) So don't get hung up on the tech. Focus on the processes and underlying concepts instead.

Side note, and back to PHP: this was the first time I played with a traditional pure server-side language (well, Python Flask doesn't count here), so it was an interesting experience learning how it works and seeing how crappy its documentation and vulnerabilities truly are (I mean old PHP; PHP 8 is much better). Come to think of it, a lot of server side software (Flask, Django, etc.) are based on the same principle of substitutions and evaluations. I suppose another benefit of learning old tech is being able to critique it more precisely by understanding its flaws.

Thanks for reading my rant. And yes, I recommend entering this (and any) course with a right attitude (hopefully you've gather that from the last few paragraphs).

Syllabus hasn't changed; see other reviews.

One thing they don't cover in the syllabus are modern cybersecurity fields: blockchain/web3, AI, IoT. These were briefly covered by group presentations though, so props to the groups that went there.
comment_teaching:
I won't discuss much here. A large portion was taught to me by our good friends Google and ChatGPT.

I only listened half-heartedly to Ricci's lectures while I worked on the labs. I think Ricci is a passionate individual. He has his own cybersecurity firm and all that. He's well-grounded in the industry. He's nice. Raises awareness of cybersecurity, recent cybersecurity news, and different fields (although biased towards forensics, i.e. his company/research). He takes time to answer our questions and prods us to think further (esp. during after-class questions).

One of the TAs delivered well. If you know, you know. The other TAs... well... they could improve.

To echo a few points:

- TAs don't prepare well for labs unless it's their assigned lab. So make sure to ask for the right TA.
- Sometimes asking the people around you is more effective than asking TAs. Don't hesitate lah.

So don't (solely) rely on TAs.

Also, the lab/hw marking is rather lazy. Feedback is ambiguous, and that’s *if* they give any feedback at all. Dropping this from an A to a B.

comment_teaching:
Extremely boring tone.  

Also, the lecturer has extremely poor time management skills. The normal lecture time including the lab is 10:30-2:20. However, it overran almost every time, and there was a time when he finished the class at 3 pm. In my personal situation, I had another class at 3 pm, which meant that I did not have any time to grab some food for lunch, and I had to suffer from extreme hunger until my other class ended every Friday.

The professor may defend himself by saying that we can actually leave the classroom earlier and watch the Zoom lecture video afterward. However, please be noted that the quality of the Zoom video is extremely poor. Sometimes the screen shown on the Zoom video did not match the content. This may be caused by the fact that the Prof/TA only shared the screen of one of the applications but forgot to switch the screen sharing in Zoom when they were using other applications. This led to the fact that I can only listen to the recording, but I am not able to see what the Prof/TA is actually doing on the screen. Moreover, the soundtrack of the Zoom video is also of extremely poor quality. Sometimes the prof walked away and talked to the entire class, but the Zoom video could only vaguely record his sound. I could not hear what he said entirely when he walked away from the mic.

You may say that this is a face-to-face class, so it is normal that the quality of the Zoom video is poor. So, because of the poor quality of the video, I have to sit in the class physically until the class ends so that I can understand the course material.

I strongly suggest that if the lecturer still insists to hold this course next year, either the course should start at 8/9 am so that students can enjoy their lunch after the class or it should start at 12/1 pm after everyone finished their lunch. Also, you should put in the class descriptions saying that “It is expected that there will be huge delays in the class time, so please do not arrange another class or activities right after this class”. In my situation, I still have a 40mins gap so at least I can give up my lunch to attend the class. But what if other people have something to do at 2:30 pm?

Furthermore, most of the TA are extremely unknowledgeable. Most of the time, only one of the TA has prepared for the lab beforehand. The other TA did not understand the lab and was not able to answer my questions during lab time.

I want to put teaching grade Z. E is not low enough.

comment_content:
Very fruitful and comprehensive, but quite hard to understand all the things.

Week 1: Introduction to Cyber Security
- Set up virtualization environments like Azure Cloud Lab, AWS and CSE VM

Week 2: Virtualization and Network, WiFi Security and Encryption
- Analyze network activities, PCAP
- WiFi cracking

Week 3: Network Basics
- Set up AWS for web server

Week 4: Network Security and Attack
- Introduce vulnerability scanning tool, Nessus

Week 5: Web Application Programming
- Develop a simple web application using HTML, PHP and SQL

Week 6: Web Application Hacking
- Introduce common web application vulnerabilities

Week 7: OS System Security
- Privilege escalation and introduce Netcat

Week 8: IAM, Authentication and Authorization
- Password cracking, access control security and 2FA

Week 9: Application and Software Security
- Use buffer overflow to override authentication

Week 10: Network Security Defense Mechanisms
- VPN, AWS Network Security Group and Snort IDS

Week 11: Incident Handling and Web Log Analysis
- Introduce Elasticsearch and analyse Apache log
comment_teaching:
In this semester, we had lessons in Zoom.

Ricci was a good guy and had passion, though, his tone was really too boring.

Lecture notes and actual teaching content had great difference. He liked to mention some concepts that did not exist in the lecture notes or lab when he saw some wordings on the notes (these concepts even appeared in the final exam). The most challenging thing was, it's not possible to concentrate on the 4-hour lessons all the time (lecture+lab), so probably some important concepts could be missed.

Lecture notes and lab were not well designed too. The key content and relatively less important information were not balanced in the lecture notes. The instruction, requirement and questions in the lab were not clear enough.
comment_workload:
No midterm, but the workload was still quite heavy for me.

Weekly 4-hour lecture+lab really, really tortured us.

We could have few days to finish each lab work, but they were not easy.

2 assignments counted only 10% for the grade but they consumed a lot of time.

The instruction and requirement for group presentation were not clear. Although it's officially called "project", it's more likely a "paper referencing assignment", but in group.

comment_content:
The course includes these things in each week (number represents the week):

1. Basic concepts on IT Security / Setup VM environment
2. CentOS VM / Kali Linux / WiFi analysis and cracking / Wireshark / Virtual network
3. DNS / LAN and WAN / Directory services / SSL TLS / POODLE / WWW and FTP
4. Nmap scanning / sniffing / DoS attack / DNS info searching / Email attacks
5. PHP / HTML / CSS / Javascript / SQL / Web authentication / MySQL
6. OWASP 2017 top 10 attacks (teach 3 only, SQL injection / XSS / CSRF) / Web session management
7. System and kernel attack / patch management / Metasploit / reverse shell
8. IAM / Authentication / Authorization / Password cracking / Federated Auth.
9. Application security / Buffer overflow / Application firewall / secure code review / malware and virus
10. Network defense mechanisms / firewall / IDS / honeypot / VPN / Snort
11. Log analysis with Elastic Search / Kibana / Attack tracing

WoW, I cannot imagine a student can master all of them within ONE SEMESTER! The content is so rich, I suggest you to better think twice before you study this course as you must suffer if you did not learn half of them before. Despite the content is very useful, it is a bit too much.

Also, the technology used in some lessons are awful. Like the others said, PHP 5.4... I also don't know why I should use 2 hours to complete several html pages and write CSS in my Lab 5. It is a security course...

comment_content:
This course is pretty similar to COMP3071 and robotics team's ENGG396x series, in the sense that you are given credits for contributing to a school team. You are not expected to passively learn from the course, so I would suggest you think this course as 2 free credits, and you are actually playing for the Firebird CTF team instead. However, I would assume that if you are looking at this review, you are probably very interested and dedicated to CTF and Firebird to the point that you would like to sacrifice like 2 hours each week for the team (sounds like a religion which is kinda scary but trust me it feels good (this is not drugs (trust me bro))), so this "suggestion" would not really matter.

As one of the 8 helpers each year, you will be required to do 2 things. Firstly, helpers will join a few (like 1-2) very large CTFs in Hong Kong as 2 teams. HKCERT CTF is a must basically every year, and there might be a few more CTFs which results will be used in the grade bargaining process. This year we joined HKCERT CTF and PwC Hackaday (which a certain CSE professor who is not Alex banned us from "officially" representing UST), in both of which we got excellent results. These competitions are very precious in the sense that you gain experience of joining on-site CTF competitions, collaborating with your fellow helpers, and meeting and sharing with other universities' CTF players (and possibly earning a lot of prizes and adding a bunch of stuff in your CV).

Secondly, if you look at the course schedule, you will find out that this course's timeslot always 100% overlaps with COMP2633. This is completely intentional, as you will be attending the same zoom meeting with COMP2633 students. However, while they are the ones listening, you will be the one teaching them. You are expected to teach 2 sessions, but might have to teach a few more depending on what categories your year's helpers play. This is a very rare opportunity to participate in the teaching process which is something that you have probably not done before (unless you have had some tutoring jobs before), and you can gain much confidence and experience after this. If you really like designing, this is also a chance for you to make your own challenges for the first time, and actually becoming an experienced CTF player.

Definitely consider enrolling into the course if you are invited, this is a literally once-in-a-lifetime opportunity that you will never get again if you missed it. (You can only become a helper in the same year as becoming a core member)
comment_teaching:
Same as COMP3633, Alex is not teaching in this course. You are the one who teach COMP2633, so there is virtually nothing I can write for the teaching part. However, there are weekly meetings for demonstrating your COMP2633 presentation in front of Alex and other helpers, organizing Firebird CTF, etc. that lasts for an entire year. Although this course will be finished in the fall semester, your duty actually carries over to the spring semester, where you will be the one grading new COMP3633 students' presentations.

Alex, as we all know, is a very nice and friendly guy. He gives us a lot of freedom in teaching and organizing Firebird CTF, and is very willing to offer help and suggestions. Definitely a great professor <3
comment_workload:
As mentioned in the content part, all you have to do is to join a few CTFs (which is basically 0 workload considering you do not have to prepare anything) and teaching COMP2633 students.

Compared to COMP3633 in which you have to make your own slides from scratch, you can inherit slides from previous years' helpers, so all you really have to do is to review the slides, edit the parts that you think are not good enough (you can choose to not edit anything if you think the slides are good), and preparing yourself for presenting the slides. If you are used to presentations or have survived COMP3633 (which you must already have), you should not need to worry about that. Just treat these "presentations" as sharing what you know and learnt and stay casual, then you will easily survive the seemingly horrifying 2-hour teaching sessions.

As to why my rating and TrebledJ's rating in workload are very different, please keep in mind that TrebledJ is the one who made all the reverse engineering slides from scratch. Compared to me who inherited his slides and only modified it a bit, it makes much sense that he would give a B-C while mine is a solid A.

-----------

To COMP2633 students looking at this review and wanting to complain about how "bad" the teaching method in COMP2633 is, I would have to say we really tried our best. Teaching time is very limited (4 hours per week is a VERY short amount of time even if it sounds like a lot) and there are many many things to learn in cybersecurity, so all we can do is to introduce you to some of the most basic techniques. You will have to self-learn to actually gain knowledge (and solve your homework), which is very different from the spoon-fed type of education you have had in other courses. This is how CTF works — you learn everything by yourself and apply those knowledge to solve difficult challenges ahead of you. There is no such thing called spoon-feeding in the CTF world considering that there are nearly infinite concepts, techniques, CVEs and challenge ideas, and there is no way one man can put all of them into their head. Each CTF is a process of self-learning, and this is what the real purpose of CTFs is — to encourage cybersecurity enthusiasts to keep learning in the neverending world of cybersecurity.

If you still believe our teaching method is terrible, why not join us and change it all? You are the one who will be teaching after all, and we are all looking forward to see you in COMP4633.

comment_content:
Read TrebleJ's review on COMP4632 (https://ust.space/review/COMP4632/view/oruPcRaTdoF2qkWW1QmxGndK2RGi8ide) before complaining about how abstract the course content is. Again, if you only want to try hands-on hacking, go for COMP2633/3633/4633 instead.

Week 1: introduction to cloud and security concept
- CIA triad, security pillars
- data center operations, physical security
- 5 essential cloud characteristics, IaaS/PaaS/SaaS, deployment model

Week 2: identity and access management (IAM)
- user account IAM, user group, roles
- authentication/authorization/MFA
- policy settings
- Azure AD

Week 3: network security and workload security
- TCP/IP, network segmentation, VLAN
- attack and defense in cloud network layers
- security defense mechanisms and attack methods
- workloads in cloud computing

Week 4: data security
- data storage, data classification
- data life cycle
- key management
- difference between service models

Week 5: application security, DevOps, PaaS security
- OWASP top 10
- DevOps, CI/ID pipeline
- secure system development life cycle (SDLC)
- PaaS, FaaS, infrastructure as code
- container security, microservices (Docker, Kubernetes)

Week 6: SaaS security, cloud risk management
- SaaS cloud security, SaaS security principles
- cloud governance and compliance
- cloud risk management, cloud audit, cloud privacy

Week 7: advanced cloud security topics
- advanced IAM (IAM, SSO, OAuth, OIDC)
- resilience, BCP, DRP
- cloud security incident handling, log analysis

Week 8: presentation and exam

There are a lot of content covered in lectures, most of which are high-level, abstract and theoretical. I personally find most of the theoretical parts interesting as you get to understand the backbone core concepts of cloud security, but the business management parts just do not really appeal to me. I wouldn't complain about including these parts though since this part speaks a lot about how real companies practise cloud security.

I would say this course is pretty much an industrial training as a whole. The course content sticks to the syllabus of cloud security certificates, and Ricci says that finishing this course would be enough for us to pass CCSK (if I remember correctly). The reason that this course is filled with theoretical content is probably because these are what cloud security certificates test you, and pretty much how training is carried out in the industry.

Labs are way more practical than the lectures as you basically learn how to use AWS. Starting from week 2, there are 3-4 labs per week, each of which is about one service provided by AWS. (Ricci enrolled all students into AWS academy to let us do the labs provided by it) Doing them definitely can improve your skills in using AWS and actually experience and learn more technical details of certain concepts taught in lectures.
comment_teaching:
Each week there is only 1 class on Saturday, which is separated into 2 3-hour sections in the morning and afternoon. Lectures are taught by Ricci, while labs are conducted by TA Sunny (UST graduate (quite fresh), active in FYS discord server) and eWalker staff Charles (also UST graduate (quite fresh), active in Firebird discord server). Although the timetable on course quota website says that there is a dedicated lab section, it is actually merged with the lecture section (same as other Ricci courses), and labs are carried out during lectures.

Ricci is infamous for having a boring tone, and I can completely feel the aura. Every lecture I went to the classroom, listened to his teaching in the morning, and could not help falling asleep in the afternoon. His voice was probably the best medicine for curing insomnia. However, as a certified professional trainer in the industry, he knows a lot about all topics covered in the course. Therefore, instead of strictly following the powerpoint slides, he would skip a bunch of less useful slides (and leave us to read on our own), explain the core concepts of important slides in detail, and then move on to talk about a lot of useful stuff not included in the slides. Content in both the skipped slides and stuff not in slides are still included in the exam, so make sure to jot down some notes if needed. I would argue his style of teaching is way closer to industrial training than normal lectures, so the ones who are interested would like his teaching while the ones who are uninterested would despise it. Again, do not approach Ricci's course in a way similar to approaching other courses.

Sunny and Charles are both friendly, willing to answer questions and most importantly, good at teaching. They demonstrated the labs in a very easy-to-follow step-by-step way and explained extra concepts in labs very clearly. Their English is quite good as well, so you would never run into a situation where you struggle to comprehend what language they are speaking.
comment_workload:
The workload is not the lightest, but certainly not heavy at all.

There are 3-4 labs per week, and all you have to do for each lab is to follow the lab sheet step by step. The lab sheets even tell you what button you should press on which web page, so it should not be hard to follow their instructions. Each lab takes around 30 minutes to 1 hour to finish depending on the number of checkpoints, and most of the time you can finish all the labs in class if you decide to not listen to Ricci and do the labs on your own. Notice that AWS academy generate random IDs and names for cloud components for the labs when starting lab instances, and these IDs must be consistent throughout checkpoint screenshots or else the grader cannot recognize which ID belongs to which component. Therefore, if you find that something is wrong with your checkpoint submission after stopping your lab instance, you will essentially have to redo the entire lab. Remember to double check your checkpoint submission files before stopping the labs instances, or else you would become me and have to redo 2 labs from 0 in week 2.

The project is a much heavier workload compared to the labs as the time is quite tight for the scale of the project. You basically have less than a month for all of planning, writing proposal, implementing the app, writing report, making powerpoint slides for presentation and filming presentation video. My group vibe coded a CTF platform from scratch (given that we are all from Firebird) while one group literally cloned an online repo for web design and just added cloud functions to it, and both are accepted. Of course, the more cloud features your app has, the higher the score you get. However, I believe the focus is not on the app itself, but rather the cloud security audit plan. Ricci specifically told us that you can leave vulnerabilities in the app untouched, but you have to mention them if your cloud security audit plan contains items of related topics. You can simply copy from existing plans in the industry, but you have to evaluate your app using each item in your plan instead of GPT-generating your evaluation. Therefore, while spending time on perfecting your app is good, do not forget to put some effort on doing the audit plan. After all, this is a cybersecurity course, not COMP3111.
comment_grading:
Labs: 30% (6 weeks of labs in total, 5% each)
Participation: 10% (2% per week, capped at 10%)
Project: 30%
Quiz: 15% (2 quizzes in week 3 and 6)
Final exam: 15%

Labs:
Ricci will provide lab sheets for each of the labs. The lab sheets are basically copied from instructions in AWS academy labs (obviously, given that we are simply doing labs it provides), except Ricci added checkpoints in them. Most of the checkpoints require you to submit screenshots or files from the AWS console or terminal, but a few of them are MC questions (copied directly, again, so you can easily find their answers on google). You can double check your submissions with online videos to ensure you get full score for labs, except some labs are quite buggy and you have to take extreme measures (such as restarting the lab and doing the lab from the beginning and forging unobtainable CSV files due to a bug in lab, AWS ACADEMY PLEASE FIX YOUR LABS) to get some of the checkpoints.

Participation:
Simply answering a question gives you participation. Ricci or TA will hand you a small piece of paper for you to write your name on, and they will collect them back and add your participation score after each class. Since there were very few students enrolled, fewer attended the class and basically no one wanted to answer questions, it is quite easy to get full score here.

Project:
3-5 students form a group to design and build a cloud application in any cloud service provider environment. Aside from the app itself, you would also need to develop a cloud security audit plan and checklist of your app environment, then include it in your project report and presentation. Ricci expected us to submit a ~40-minute video containing the content of the project report and a demonstration of the cloud app, and then play it during group presentation, but only my group did this while the other 2 groups presented on the spot. The presentation format does not affect the grading though, only the content matters.

Quiz:
Each quiz contains 15 MC questions, some are single choice while some requires you to choose more than 1 answers. The quizzes are somewhat hard for me in the sense of abstract wordings, but since they are open book most people get really close to full marks.

Final exam:
50% MC questions, 50% long questions. I have taken a cybersecurity certificate exam before, and the MCs to me look exactly like what the certificate exams asks. The long questions are like 10% reading comprehension and 90% blowing water, and none of us even knew what we answered after the exam. The exam is open book as well, but it is basically useless except for a few MC questions which are actually asking you "what is this term/concept".

My scores:
Participation: 10/10
Labs:
- Week 2: 5/5
- Week 3: 4.97/5
- Week 4: 5/5
- Week 5: 5/5
- Week 6, 7: god knows
Quiz:
- Week 3: 14/15
- Week 6: 13.42/15
Project: god knows, groupmates carried very hard so I think our group should be the highest in class
Final exam: god knows

Final grade: A+ (??????????)

There are only 11+1 (audit) students in class and Ricci always tries to give grades as good as possible, so a staggering number of 3 people got A range. (A range is maximized, thank you 25% A range rule) 2 people I knew got B+, so I guess Ricci might have just spammed B+ for most students.

Although Ricci always gives maximized grades, the one thing that keeps me from giving an A for this part is how close everyone's scores are. From the stats, you can see that most students' scores are nearly full, so it comes down to project and final exam, scores of which are both (intentionally) unreleased. To get A range, not only do you need to sweat the project as hard as you can (or by vibe coding a large and complicated project), but you also have to know how to use your common sense and blow water. Even with all these, the small class size (which happens to every Ricci course) can still easily cause you to lose your A range. Run away immediately if you only care about A ranges, do not gamble with your CGA. If you want to study this course, I would recommend treating this course like a hobby class and care less of your grades.

comment_content:
Four points to conclude this course (TLDR):
1. Good Grading
2. Easy Midterm and Final
2. Light Workloads
3. Interesting Contents
4. Good Teaching and Clear Lecture Notes

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
This course is about analyzing the social network data and the reason behind the formation of different social network.

Interesting Contents
The contents of this course are quite funny. (e.g. Using some qualitative technique to find out the key person in a social network)

And it is composed by three parts,

1. Network properties and models
    a. Social media and networks
    b. Basic network properties and web graph
    c. Small-world phenomenon
    d. Community structure in networks
        i. Weak ties
        ii. Structural holes
        iii. Network communities
    e. Centrality Measures
    f. Network Formation
         i. Power-Law
        ii. Mathematics behind
        iii. Preferential attachment
2. Users behaviors and influence
    g. Cascading Behavior
    h. Viral Market
         i. Disease spreading
        ii. Viral marketing
        iii. Models of information spread
   i. Influence Maximization
   j.  Signed Networks
        i. Structural balance
        ii.  Status Theory
   k.  User Evaluations
   l. Homophily and Social influence
3. Social networking services and social factors in system design
   m.  Recommender systems
   n.  Link analysis: HITS and PageRank

The Prerequisites are useless. The algorithms and the mathematics in this course are not complicated.

If you have already learned some simple discrete math, calculus, statistics and linear algebra such as binomial distribution, expectation ,variance, matrix operations, integration and limit, graph theory (e.g. DFS, BFS), direct / indirect proof, eigenvalue and eigenvector. It is enough for you to handle all in this course.

The contents of this course are almost the same as the course CS224W in Standford and some of the lecture notes are copys of that.
http://web.stanford.edu/class/cs224w/

This curriculum sequence systematically covers the core knowledge and technologies of cloud computing and distributed systems, starting from foundational concepts and progressing to advanced practical applications and cutting-edge topics. It opens with logistics, cloud computing’s core concepts, fundamental principles and service models, then dives into virtualization—the underlying technology of cloud computing—and cloud storage systems. The course then shifts to classic distributed computing frameworks, elaborating on MapReduce and its implementation Hadoop, as well as MapReduce algorithm design, before moving to the upgraded Spark framework with two dedicated sessions on its programming practice. Subsequent modules explore specialized distributed computing fields: graph analytics and distributed machine learning, followed by the mainstream cloud computing paradigm of serverless computing. The curriculum concludes with advanced topics, offering an in-depth exploration of frontier directions in the field, forming a complete learning path from basics to practice, from classic frameworks to emerging technologies.

comment_content:
This course goes over the basics and theory of cloud computing and big data systems (as titled). Personally, it wasn't particularly rewarding. Its high enrollment is probably due to it being a BDT minor compulsory course. I believe the syllabus this semester, coordinated by GUO Song, is slightly different to previous offerings. Below is an over-simplification and heavily-opinionated list of topics introduced in this course. (I am not responsible for certain inaccuracies)

Topic 1: Overview & Course Logistics
- There are a lot of data!
- Course Logistics (duh)
- How to read a paper (He wanted to introduce this to us)

Topic 2: Fundamentals
- What is Cloud? (Cloud big!)
- Cloud definitions and characteristics (Cloud big!!)
- Amazon EC2 (Our sponsor)
- Why Cloud? Cloud cheap! (In certain contexts)

Topic 3: Service Models and Challenges
- Cloud deployment models (Public or private? Or maybe both?)
- Cloud service models (Something-aaS)
- Issues of Cloud
- Challenges Facing Cloud Providers

Topic 4: Basic Data Analytics (A lot of COMP 2211 content)
- Basic Concepts (Take COMP 2211)
- Predictive Analytics (Take COMP 2211 and/or COMP 4211)
- Descriptive Analytics (Take COMP 2211)

(btw I think GUO Song is teaching COMP 2211 in Spring 2024-25)

Topic 5: Cloud Storage Systems
- How does Cloud store data? What are problems faced?
- Details about Cloud Storage Systems

Topic 6: Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)
- What is HDFS?
- Design
- Limitations
- uh oh there is Java code

Topic 7: MapReduce
- Parallel processing is faster and better (probably)
- Functional Programming (COMP 3031!) (No seriously PPL saved my ass)
- Map (duh)
- Reduce (duh)

Topic 8: MapReduce Algorithm Design
- Zen of MapReduce (idfk)
- PseudoCode! (Important for exam)
- Oh there's also the Combiner aside from Mapper and Reducer
- Time to be mean (MapReduce to calculate mean)
- Bigram Counting - Pairs and Stripes (uh... COMP 4221 I guess)

-- Midterm syllabus ends here --

Topic 9: Hadoop
- We're back! (Kind of)
- Hadoop is better than MapReduce from scratch
- Get a job (Hadoop job)
- MapReduce in Hadoop

Topic 10: Virtualization
- What is virtualization? (COMP 3511)
- History
- How does it work?
- Implementations
- VM & Containers

Topic 11: From MapReduce to Spark
- Hadoop is great, except not really
- Apache Spark is great! (Why Java when you can Python?)
- Map and Reduce in Spark (Functional Programming but easier)

Topic 12: Spark Internals
- RDD
- Spark Architecture
- YARN
- Shuffling
- Spark vs Hadoop MapReduce

Topic 13: Spark Programming
- PySpark API
- RDD with Python (e.g. rdd.map(...).map(...).reduce(...))

Topic 14: From RDDs to DataFrames
- DataFrames means less code (probably)
- Spark SQL
- More Python programing!

Topic 15: Graph Analytics
- Graph (COMP 2711/COMP 3711)
- PageRank (COMP 4321)
- Doing these in Spark
- Dijkstra's Algorithm (I know, I also thought 'wtf')
- Parallel BFS (fun) (not)
- Application

-- Final syllabus are everything above, with a focus on the latter half --

Overall, if you aren't taking BDT, I wouldn't particularly recommend this course. It is, however, a decent course if you want a low-ish workload 4000 COMP course and you don't really care much about learning.
comment_teaching:
GUO Song was a decent professor. He clearly has the heart in teaching, but unfortunately his lectures are Mon 1330-1450 and Fri 0900-1020, which means people don't show up to his Friday lectures much. He talks at a moderate pace in my opinion with a clear accent. He does articulate his b, p, t and ks louder than other consonants. He also often draws demonstrations of concepts on the whiteboard, which is beneficial for understanding. He seems to be happy to answer any questions from students, and also encourage people to make appointments face-to-face with him instead of using emailing as the may way of communication after-class. Given the content of this course, I believe the delivery was pretty good.

I would, however, say that the TAs of this course aren't particularly good. They make announcements later than I would like them, whether it's exam details or HW logistics. This course uses GitHub classroom for HW and project submissions, and I would really appreciate if they actually updated details instead of copying them from previous semesters.

For HW2, the TA half-screwed up the autograding, where a lot of students encountered a timeout and got 0 points. After the appeal period, however, the statistics of the HW increased significantly. I would've appreciated it a lot more if it didn't give me a heart attack over initially receiving a 0 on my HW2.

C mostly because of TAs. GUO Song himself is at B.
comment_workload:
Workload is low for a 4000-level course.
The HWs are annoying but they can be finished within half a day usually. Plus, if you know where to look, sources are available.
The workload of the project wasn't particularly high, but it heavily depends on your topic.

B because it isn't low per se, but it isn't particularly energy-draining.
comment_grading:
Statistics below are given in the format of: Mean/High/Upper Quartile/Median/Lower Quartile/Low
The grading scheme:

1. 10% In-class Quizzes and Participation
No details were particularly announced beforehand, but GUO Song created 3 in-class quizzes. They were in the format of the exams (except shorter). These fully open-book quizzes (except the first one) are essentially attendance checks. You get 1 if you submitted, 0 otherwise. They act as a guide to prepare you for what the midterm and final exams will be like.

Statistics are not completely disclosed, but:
Quiz 1 (Full: 1 point)
0.96/1/1/1/1/0
Quiz 2 (Full: 1 point)
Unknown, LQ is probably 1 though
Quiz 3 (Full: 1 point)
0.98/1/1/1/1/0


2. 20% Midterm
MCs, short questions and long questions. Mostly on concepts. Pseudocode-writing is required for MapReduce. Completely open-book as long as materials are hard-copies.
Stats (Full: 100 points):
67.1/91/76/70/59.5/0

3. 24% HWs (each 8%)
HW1 is about Amazon EC2. Forces you to try it out. Beneficial for project.
HW2 is about MapReduce in Java. Requires Linux VM/Mac/Linux for docker because of Hadoop. Skeleton is given and implementation isn't much.
HW3 is about linear regression with PySpark. Recommended to run on DataBricks. Useful to try out PySpark for final exam
Stats:
HW1 (Full: 8 points)
7.74/8/8/8/7.5/5
HW2 (Full: 8 points)
6.65/8/8/8/6/0
HW3 (Full: 8 points)
7.87/0/8/8/8/8

4. 16% Project
2-4 person project with a self-selected topic. Grading depends on group size. Lots of people did machine-learning-related projects. A report is required.
Stats (Full: 20 points):
16.77/20/18/16.5/15.5/14

5. 30% Final Exam
Very similar to midterm. Large proportion (~40-50%?) is on PySpark programming (handwriting code/output). Completely open-book as long as materials are hard-copies.
Stats (Full: 100 points):
59.88/16/53/61/71/89

Letter grade seems to be quite nice
My assignments and in-class quizzes got full; Project below LQ; Midterm < median a tiny bit; Final > UQ a tiny bit; This yields a B+.
