comment_content: Big data systems, including Cloud Computing and parallel data processing frameworks, emerge as enabling technologies in managing and mining the massive amount of data across hundreds or even thousands of commodity servers in datacenters. This course exposes students to both the theory and hands-on experience of this new technology. The course will cover the following topics. (1) Basic concepts of Cloud Computing and production Cloud services; (2) MapReduce - the de facto datacenter-scale programming abstraction - and its open source implementation of Hadoop. (3) Apache Spark - a new generation parallel processing framework - and its infrastructure, programming model, cluster deployment, tuning and debugging, as well as a number of specialized data processing systems built on top of Spark. By walking through a number of hands-on labs and assignments, students are expected to gain first-hand experience programming on real world clusters in production datacenters.

comment_content:
The course guides you through a journey from old statistical models to the latest neural transformer models for various NLP tasks. The course put more focus on the machine learning part instead of NLP. The topics are introduced in a chronological order which would make the intuitions clear and logical. The most interesting thing is some of the newer models actually incorporate classic techniques or their variants. For example, in few shot learning, we can borrow the concept of distance/similarity from KNN to determine which is the most similar.

Some other things that I liked:
- Introductory topics are discussed in depth while advanced topics are discussed in brief. A good balance between depth and breadth.
- Handful of tools introduced in tutorials. (but the code quality is not that good)
- Supplementary PG materials are provided for reference. (I didn't read any of them tho)

Syllabus is approximately the same as the past offering.
Lecture 1: Introduction
Lecture 2: Introduction to NLP
Lecture 3: Introduction to NLTK
Lecture 4: Vector Space Model
Lecture 5 and 6: Introduction to Classification
Lecture 7: Naïve Bayes
Lecture 8: Perceptron, Error-Driven Classification
Lecture 9: Logistic Regression
Lecture 10: Neural Networks
Lecture 11: CNN
Lecture 12: Language Models
Lecture 13: Neural Language Models
Lecture 14: Word Embeddings
Lecture 15: Sequence to Sequence Learning
Lecture 16: Attention Models
Lecture 17: Knowledge Graphs and Its Applications in Healthcare
Lecture 18: Machine Learning For NLP: Past, Present, and Future

It seems that the prof. has responded to the previous review by:
- Cutting 1 intro lecture (if the 3 lectures intro from previous review is true)
- Emphasizing the connection between different parts, especially between logistic regression, perceptron and naive bayes (which I found interesting)

In any case, it would be much better than COMP 4211 by James Kwok which you will literally learn nothing. :)
(Feel free to refer to my COMP 4211 review)
comment_teaching:
Prof. Song is a nice and passionate instructor. He also knows the field well so he is able to give a good introduction to the current trend or some of the state-of-the-art techniques in the NLP field.

Sometimes, he would ask us for opinions on the course. It seems that he is willing to improve. For the last lecture, there is not enough time to accommodate all students to answer questions for the bonus. He is kind enough to open up extra sections for us to discuss with him.

[Review WIP, currently short and messy, will update soon]

Disclaimer: I attended every lecture in real-time, and face-to-face (while mixed mode was adapted).

(P.S. One fun fact: I have written 20+ reviews in USTSPACE. If you have read those reviews too, you will find my English is bad. The only two vocab that I used for describing good instructors are "nice" and "passionate". lol)
comment_workload:
Prof. has reduced the workload for the current semester by cutting down the number of project and cancel the midterm. The workload is light compared to most COMP electives.

Lab Assignments:
Although lab attendance is not compulsory, there is one lab assignment after each lab. Each one will take you 10~15 minutes only as you only need to copy and paste the code from the lab materials.

Project:
The project is easy. It is a named entity recognition task on a covid-19 dataset. To get a full score on the model component (which weight 80% of the project), you only need to build a model to pass a weak baseline provided (see Grading section). The first model that I built already passed the full score baseline. I was quite shocked to be honest. It was basically a 2-layer bidirectional 64-units LSTM with Adam (default hyperparameters by Tensorflow).
Our final model is a scaled up version of the first model described above with the addition of convolutional layers. Although the architecture is mostly the same, my groupmate and I spent some time trying a lot of things to make the model work well. It was quite fun to mess around actually. (Remember the times when we first learn programming?) You can refer to our presentation slides (section below) for details if you are interested.

Project Presentation:
Basically we copied everything in the report to the slides. We prepared too much slides in fact.... It was a 6 minutes presentation and the TA will stop you if time is up. So, we don't have time to present all of the slides. I think it is a good idea to post the slides here so the slides remaining won't be wasted lol.
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1JyzasK0mh_85pF07rLOr-sL4Vw6aJd6e/view?usp=sharing

Final Exam:
Only a single year of past paper is given (due to the course has been offered only once before). Despite of that, I spent quite some time revising the exam. My strategy was to practice COMP 4471 papers (practicing derivatives is a must) then fine tune on Standford cs224n papers. Then, I coded up simple programs for all the standard computation questions given in the past paper to be used in the exam.
The exam is relatively long. Due to my poor multi-variable calculus background, I did not manage to finish my derivation properly in the derivative question. (The notations are not well defined, and there are wrongly transposed matrices everywhere.) Do try to write something tho coz I still got full points on the question despite of that.
I would say my strategy worked quite well. The simple programs that I wrote speeded up my calculations by a lot. I also feel more comfortable when answering other questions. There is no way to finish the paper on time without practice and tools. My advice would be do try to find some problems from other courses (in terms of ML, to do transfer learning!) and prepare some tools before the exam. And, remember to write steps so you won't get tons of points deducted like me. :(
comment_grading:
The prof. said he could give a larger A range due to relatively low enrollment in the course. However, I don't have any data to support this. And, consider my personal score and the statistics, the grading is not particularly good. I would say take this course for content but not for grades.

Score breakdown and statistics:
Lab Assignments (30%): 24/24
Final Project (20%): 99/100 (Mean: 90.4)
Project Presentation (10%): 9.2/10 (Mean: 8.2)
Final Exam Part 1: 50/55 (Mean: 36, High: 53)
Final Exam Part 2: 36/45 (Mean: 23.9, High: 43)
Final Exam Overall (40%): 86/100 (Mean: 59.9)
Bonus: +2%
Overall: 95.4% => A
(All statistics are from Canvas)

9 point were deducted in exam part 2 purely due to missing steps in the answer sheet. However, it is not explicitly mentioned steps are required. It feels so bad to lost points because of that. That might be the reason that I lost the subgrade.

Another thing is my group got the highest test accuracy among 20+ group in class. Sadly, there is no bonus for that. From the presentation, most (if not all) groups pass the baseline model by TA for full points in the model part. The top model (from our group) is having 3.7% higher accuracy than the full points baseline.

"The test acc of 80%, 90%, and 100% baselines are 83.4%, 88.2%, and 89.9% respectively.
The top 5 test acc are as follows: 93.6%, 92.72%, 92.68%, 92.61%, and 92.23%."

Few points that I considered to give "B" for the grading:
- Not easy final exam with mean ~60 while there are quite a number of student that hold up the top grades
- Bonus is given. However, it seems to me that it did not pull up my grades.
- The project grading scheme fails to award students that performed very well.

I will try to ask some of my friends for more statistics.

comment_content:
Five points to conclude this course (TLDR):
1. Grading is OK
2  Easy Midterm and Final
3. Middle to Heavy Workloads
4. Rich Contents but not organized well
5. Poor Teaching and Poor Lecture Notes

-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Unlike the course COMP4221 taught by Dekai. This course focus on solving the NLP problem via Machine Learning / Deep Learning. But, it is good to take COMP4221 before taking this course in order to have some basic ideas of NLP. This course will skip some fundamental NLP and directly go to NLP in ML.

Rich Contents
The following topics are covered:
1,2,3 The first 3 lectures are all wasting time introduction....

Before Midterm (NLP + Traditional Machine Learning)
4. NLTK (Tokenization)

5. Vector Space Model (WordNet, Word Similarity, Vector Space Model, Bag-of-words, Zipf’s Law, Stemming, Stop words, TF-IDF)

6. Classification (Classification, Feature Selection, Loss Function, Evaluation Methods , Training Size, Paired t-test, Bayes risk, type of classification methods)

7. NaiveBayes (NaiveBayes Classifier, Laplace Smmothing,  K-Fold Validation)

8. Perceptron (Preceptron Algorithum, Perceptron Learnability, Convergence, Perceptron-Mistake Bound, Averaged Preceptron)

9. Logistic Regression (Logistic Regression, Newton Method, Stochastic/Batch/Mini-Batch Gradient Decent, Adam, Learning Rate, Decaying learning rate)

After Midterm (NLP + Deep Learning)

10. Neural Network (Feed Forward Network, Dropout, L1/2 Regularization, Activation Function, Back Propagation, Batch Normalization, Learning Rule, Over fitting, Gradient Checking, Convolutional Layer, Pooling Layer, CNN for NLP)

11. Language Model (N-Gram Model, Maximum Likelihood estimation, Smoothing, Perplexity)

12. Neural language models (Probabilistic Language Models, Log-Linear Model, Recurrent Neural Network, Back Propagation in RNN, Vanishing/Exploding Gradients, Bi-directional RNN, LSTM, GRU)

13. Word Embedding (Cross Entropy Loss, Count Based Approach, Word2vec(CBOW), Glove)

14. Seq to Seq Model (Machine Translation Model, Encoder-Decoder, BLBU)

15. Attention Mechanism (Different Attentions, Self-Attention, Transformer Network)

16. Information Retrieval and Knowledge Graph

17. Future of NLP

comment_content:
(Note: This course is being rebranded as COMP2633.)

If you're an escapist and loathe some of the more annoying classes of UST (looking at you math3033), then this course is just right for you... maybe. If you, like me previously, have never touched the field of cybersecurity before and you're even the tiniest bit interested, this course is for you.

This is the first in a series of three courses (COMP4901N/O/P, now rebranded as COMP2633/3633/4633). So if you do well in this course, you'll get to advance and learn/experience potentially more. Since this course is introductory, content goes for more breadth and less depth.

After the first couple weeks, you'll be asked to select either Track A (pwn/binary/reverse) or Track B (web/crypto/misc)–more on these below. Just keep in mind that the tracks are merely a formality. You can cherry pick whatever challenge and topic you want: e.g. maybe you like playing reverse and crypto but hate pwn and web.

Everything in this course (CTFs in general, actually) is rather hands-on. So if you rather hack something than learn whatever a second derivative telescoping triplosive Bodeman-Weiestrass clopen set is... then just clicky click the enrol button.

To give you an idea what topics are available:

- pwn/binary: study assembly, compiler/processor nuances
- reverse: discover what an app is doing under the hood
- web: study web browsers, php, frameworks
- crypto: break ciphers, do meth, get high
- misc: image/audio/disk/packet nuances and other fun little things
comment_teaching:
Taught by students, for students.

Similar structure to Robotics Team. You enter as a trainee. You get filtered (selected). You get filtered some more. Finally you ascend into the upper echelons and gain the opportunity to preach your wisdom to the next generation of lost sheep.

I'd say the teaching was mostly good? Some were quite good, while some were ungood. But overall, I'd say at the same level, or even better than a lot of professors.

The best teacher, however, is experience. Get your hands dirty, run the scripts, and hack away as you escape from the reality of UST.
comment_workload:
This is quite variable as it depends on experience, knowledge, which track(s) you choose, the support you receive from the seniors, and most of all... endurance. How much are you willing to suffer to find the flags?

If you have experience with the web and networking, you may find the web challenges easier to comprehend.

If you have experience with assembly, you may find the pwn/binary/reverse challenges easier to crack. Having taken COMP2611, I found these challenges were generally easier to navigate.

Regardless, you're here to learn. If you want to rank high and advance to COMP4901O (now COMP3633), you'll need to sacrifice quite a bit of time. If all you want is to pass, you just need to submit attendance flags (which are basically free); but IMHO you would just be wasting your time.

But eh, just have fun.
comment_grading:
Reasonable grading. Submit attendance flags. Do your homework. Profit.

Assuming people don't cheat and share flags (which you shouldn't), then there is a strong correlation between your effort and your grade.

I won't talk too much about stats here. Not much has changed so you can just check the older reviews for details.

comment_content:
A course about how to play CTF.
Capture the Flag(CTF), "flags" are secrets hidden in purposefully-vulnerable programs or websites. Competitors steal flags from the organizers (jeopardy-style challenges). (Copy from Wiki)

So simply speaking, this course is about practical hacking skills! It will be a great course if you are interested in treasure hunting and hacking.

It includes almost every "basic" technique in CTF competition. Although those are basic skills in CTF, the techniques are not basic at all in the aspect of computer science. You'll have to learn as simple as SQL injection, or as difficult as how to buffer overflow to get a remote shell. Just many aspects of computer science. For details, you can refer to Kenny128's review.

There will be also different local or internal CTF competitions that you can play with your classmates. This would be the time where you show the CTF skills that you have learned in the class!
comment_teaching:
All the senpais are really yasashii (except Sam, because he made so many ribble challenges that require guessing xD). Although the pace of teaching would be pretty fast due to the limited time that you may not be able to understand some of the contents. You could always watch the Zoom recording to watch the class again, again, and again.

The most important point is that there is an official Discord group where you can chat with the senpais and your classmates. You can ask them about the contents of the class if you don't understand. They will be very willing to answer and explain to you. You can even ask your homework if you really can't solve it, they will give you hints too. ;)

While actually, you can chat anything in the Discord group. (Then will you find that all of us are talking about anime, VTubers, memes www) Just treat all of us are friends and the atmosphere are very good. OuO
comment_workload:
To be honest, the only workload would be doing the homework. The homework is not difficult if you could understand the contents taught in the class. It just requires a lot of time to think/guess (and Google. Yes, trust me, Google is your BEST friend) the correct approach to hack the machine and get the flag.

For me, I usually start to do the homework immediately after the class. Sometimes I finish the homework by doing it few hours continuously, while sometimes I need to take more time so that I will have to spend time doing it half of a day in the next day. Great to spend time if you feel boring (like if you feel all the courses in UST are easy for you) or if your parents ask you why you seem to be having lots of free time in UST.

To conclude, try harder, Try Harder, TRY HARDER, then you'll find the flag : D
comment_grading:
Good grading, just P/F.

50% - Attendance
50% - Track A in-class exercises and homework
50% - Track B in-class exercises and homework

So technically, the maximum score that you could get is 150%, where 60% would be a pass. By getting a full score in attendance and doing some in-class exercises (In-class exercise will be demonstrated in-class so you could get the flag by copying it), you must get a P grade so it's very difficult to get an F.

However, if you aim for a more advanced course - COMP4901O, you will have to get a better performance in order to be invited to join the more advanced course.

comment_grading:
If you want to learn something, you are still welcome to participate in the course even if you cannot officially register into the course due to time clash. Everyone is treated equally.
(Should be on Friday at night, but there may still be collision with lectures until 6, or labs from 6-8?)

P/F, 0 Credits (← Don't affect your grade in any way)

Attendance (50%) + Exercises & Homework (50% * Total no. / No. from single track)
This semester consists of 11 weeks and 10 (Exercise + Homework) sets with 2 track-shared sets, so Exercises & Homework constitute a total of 50% * 18 / 10 = 90%
And thus the maximum possible score is 140%.

For the non-track-shared part, half of the exercise / homework are from Track A, while the other half are from Track B. You are supposed to focus on one track, but better if you manage to do it all.
* Exercises are those demonstrated in class *

Passing Score: 60% ← Just submitting every attandance flag and in-class exercise flags is already giving you far more than just pass.

To enroll into COMP4901O (and COMP4901P), you may need a higher score and perform satisfactorily in internal CTF (← No exam, Yay!)

comment_content:
There are two major components in this course: CTFs and presentations.

CTFs are the fun part. You can think of them as fun little assignments. This year, our entire class of 15 participated as one team (Firebird) across four CTFs. I'd say we did fairly well.

Onto presentations. In the first half of the course, expect lectures from seniors/experts which cover more advanced topics of cybersecurity and mindset. In the second half, expect classmates to wow you with their quickly-gained expertise or incompetence regarding one cybersecurity topic (which you get to choose, first-come first-serve).

The topics we covered were:
- Advanced Pwn
   - Linux Kernel Pwn
   - ret2dl_resolve
   - House of Force
   - FSOP
   - DNS Rebinding
- Advanced Reverse
   - Advanced Angr
- Advanced Web
   - Expression Language Injection
   - GraphQL
   - Request Smuggling
   - JS Prototype Pollution
   - NoSQL Injection
- Advanced Crypto
   - Lattice-based Attacks
   - Post-quantum Crypto
- Misc.
   - Windows/Azure AD Attack
   - Advanced OSINT
   - Sandbox Escape

comment_content:
I have to admit this is a content-rich course, but somehow too rich for a normal student to fully take in. This course covers all aspects that you need to know to construct a simple compiler. The content overall can be summarized into 4 huge parts:
- Lexer
- Parsing
- Type checking
- Code generation
Also, there are some trivial parts like regular expressions (you may have learnt this from other courses) and some storage stuff (I missed this lecture due to some Ph.D. interviews but this content has not appeared in the final exam)

Lexer and Parsing are not so hard, what you need to focus is regular expression (if you have not learnt that in your discrete math but I have so it is relatively simple for me to understand) and LL1. LL1 is very hard, especially when professor automatically assume everyone can get the trick after knowing how to compute first and nullable. You MUST attend the tutorials to get LL1 tricks or you will die hard in your final prep period. (I missed LL1 tutorial but I asked TA to teach me again before the exam)

I really suggest you give up on type checking since it is not for normal human beings to understand when reviewing and taking the exam. The question in the final for type checking is to differentiate A range dudes, and it took me an hour to fix the first sub-question and I didn't even understand what the * I had written after several minutes. It is really hard, but the lectures are kind of simple to understand. It is two different stories for you to understand in the lecture and solve that question in the final exam.

Code gen is not so hard. Maybe in the ending of the semester, the clever-as-god professor finally realize that we are stupid so the content are simpler than before. Questions are also easy if you have learnt computer organization before and it is even easier since you do not need to care about those registers. WebAssembly will help you deal with that.

If you are truly interested in this course, I think you will enjoy the course flow because I think the organization of this course flows smoothly. But if you are not so interested in this and you are just selecting this course so that you can full fill some credits in your track like me, I really suggest you think twice before you make the decision since you will feel much pain when in the final prep period you will find that even you have consumed all the recordings, you still cannot crack those past papers.
comment_teaching:
I truly admit the professor is very dedicated in making this course but there are points that are not satisfactory.

- Important points not emphasized. For example, LL1 is difficult, so I think in the lecture this part should be emphasized.

- Some very hard topics integrated into the contents but finally not so helpful to the final exam. I think those points should be marked out or only covered at the end of the lecture or the last lecture. Some concepts are too hard and not helpful at all.

- Tutorials are good, but some early tutorials are not covering all the problems. I think in the later semesters (if there're still students selecting this course), the tutorials should be released as homework and then the tutorials should be focusing on teaching.
comment_workload:
Work load is super heavy. This course took me over 60% of time to learn in this semester, but the outcome is not satisfactory at all.

1) Scala / Java programming
2) Discrete Math / Some complex logic
3) Basic language principle
Or: You have a lot of time to spare.

Some tips to minimize your pain:
1) Focus on tutorial problems and past papers (especially those papers with lecture no converted to 4121). They are much much more helpful than slides in the final period.
2) Ignore some difficult concepts. I spent a huge amount of time on concepts, but they are not helpful in cracking the finals. Concepts are important if you can comprehend them during the class or within 10 mins. Or ignore them.
3) Ignore Type Checking in your final review. If you crack all other problems and write something for Type Checking, you may get around median.
4) It is almost impossible for you to be easy in this semester if you choose this course. Work hard and good luck anyway. You can learn something from this course, whether they are knowledge or lessons :)

comment_content:
The content basically covers everything you need to implement a compiler. I will say it is not an easy course. Some even claim it's PG-difficulty, although I disagree.

- Formal languages, regular expressions, lexing
- Context free grammars, parsing LL(1) grammars, Pratt parsing
- Name analysis
- Type theory, type checking/unification, let polymorphism
- WebAssembly crash course, codegen
- Compiler optimisation, control flow graphs

After this course, you'll probably have the prerequisite knowledge to create your own language for real and learn toolchains like LLVM.

I highly recommend you to have the following pre-requisites before you take this course:
- Comfortable with discrete math (If you got through COMP 2711 or 3711, you are probably fine)
- Open to learn new math concepts such as inductively defined sets, lattices
- Prepared to learn functional programming
- Some computer architecture (COMP2611, even if taken in the same semester, is enough)

If you lack some of these, then you may struggle. As always, try out the course during add-drop period and have a go at the first lab.

By the way, the entire course is taught in Scala. At first, it's quite tricky to learn. But, after a while, you will fall in love with algebraic data types and pattern matching. It will also probably make you a better programmer.

P.S. I heard the course will become a standard COMP offering in the future. If so, it will probably be one of the hardest standard COMP electives for UG.

UPDATE: It appears this course will tentatively be offered in Spring 2024: https://cse.hkust.edu.hk/ug/courses/

UPDATE 2: This course will be converted into an official course: COMP 4121, and will be offered starting Spring 2024. As of writing it is yet to be approved, but it should be added soon.
comment_teaching:
Professor Parreaux (LP) is a great instructor. His teaching is clear, and the lecture slides provide ample detail for when you want to go back and revise something.

Lectures are up to 2 hours long (though usually only 90 minutes), with a 10 minute break in the middle. While he sometimes may go too fast during class, he is extremely patient and willing to answer questions during the break or after class.

The real heart of this course is the tutorials, delivered by the TAs. The tutorials have attendance, but skipping one regardless is a death wish. This course is an oddball in UST, in the sense that the tutorials are actually really hard and you **will** be confused and exhausted. This is not necessarily the fault of the TAs or the professor -- the content is just inherently hard. However, the tutorials are quite interactive, and you are encouraged to work in groups (although we all know that isn't happening in HKUST CSE :P)

As for the labs, I only attended one or two of them as they seem to just be a Q&A / support session if you need help with the labs.

Moreover, all the TAs and LP himself are reachable through a class Discord server.

Overall, great teaching.

Also, shout-out to LP for making special arrangements to accommodate students who had a time clash to still register the course!
comment_workload:
The bulk of the workload comes from the labs.

The labs are challenging. Basically, you are building towards a full-blown compiler in Scala for a mini-language called Amy. Each lab builds one stage of the compiler:
- Lexer
- Parser
- Type checker
- Codegen
The first lab is an interpreter which is designed to get you acquainted with the language. It is unrelated to the compiler.

Each lab builds on the previous one. You are expected to write your own test cases. You will be given feedback on your labs so that you can fix bugs and whatnot (which you should do), but solutions to the labs are NOT released. If you discover any bugs, fix them before the rest of your compiler breaks.

Most labs are doable in a day. However, some really hard labs like the parser and codegen lab took me a few days to complete.

--

There will also be some additional workload from trying to understand the content and tutorial questions outside of class.

--

The bonus project was released on the last day of semester, and was due at the end of the finals. If you plan to do it, be prepared to work on it alongside your finals.
comment_grading:
The grading scheme is as follows:

60% labs
- 5 labs, each graded /10, worth 12% each
    - For each lab, major mistakes deduct two points, while minor mistakes deduct one point
    - In my experience, mistakes from the same root cause will not be penalised twice (grading is done manually)
40% Final Exam

Moreover, there is an optional bonus project graded out of 100% that can recover up to 50% of the total lab marks. You extend the compiler in some cool way in groups of 1-4 for this project. The actual formula for the lab score is:

min(60%, Your lab score + 30% * project score)

Note that attendance is required for the tutorials. I don't actually know what happens if you skip a tutorial, but I wouldn't recommend finding out. :P
--

PROJECT

Again, fully optional, and is only for the purpose of making up lost lab points. You're given a long list of project ideas (or you can come up with it on your own), and you implement it by yourself or in groups. The projects have a hard upper limit on the team size depending on the difficulty.

It seems you can get a good score on the project as long as you implement everything carefully and write lots of relevant details in your report.
--

FINAL EXAM

The final is quite hard. It's full of theory and a bit of programming (also, there are some proof questions). I had to stare at a question for 30 minutes before having any idea what it was trying to say. However, the format is quite similar to the tutorials, and LP provides a lot of past papers for your self-study.

The final was originally out of 80 (+5 bonus), but the max score was truncated to 70 due to the difficulty this semester.

Stats: Low  LQ  Median  UQ  High  |  Mean

Final exam (truncated, /70):
18  32.13  38.25  53  71  |  42.58

The labs usually have a median of around 8 and always an upper quartile of 10.

--
LETTER GRADE

Overall 98.3% -> A+
Getting around upper quartile will result in an A/A-.

comment_content:
I won't say too much, as most of what I planned to write has already been covered by other reviews.

comment_teaching:
Very clear teaching, guidelines, lab descriptions, lecture notes.

Lectures are relatively interactive, which I find somewhat of a rarity. Tutorials actually introduce new content and are intellectually challenging, so I strongly recommend attending, even if it's through Zoom. Plenty of examples and exercises all-around.

LP is very nice. Made the project optional. Gives detailed feedback in labs. Responds reliably to emails. Gives breaks during lectures.

Only annoyance I have is that Labs 3-5 weren't graded until after the semester was over, so there wasn't any feedback on some compiler stages. :( But this doesn't stop me from giving teaching an A.
comment_workload:
5 Labs. 1 Project. 1 Final.

In each lab, we're tasked with implementing a stage of a compiler pipeline to compile a subset of Scala. The skeleton provided is a full-blown Scala project. Be sure to take advantage of the testing library and write test cases.

The first several labs are a breeze (interpreter, lexer, parser). The last few labs are much more difficult (type checking, codegen). Especially codegen. This one requires a lot more thought.

The project is fun. You form up to groups of 3 and implement any compiler extension to the compiler you finished from the labs. Also, did I mention LP made the project optional?

The final was nice and intellectually challenging. LP provided past papers from EPFL (although those were much easier).

A word of caution though. Usually, each lab would build upon a previous lab (e.g. the parser stage depends on the lexer stage). Likewise, the project depends on the compiler you've implemented for the labs. So it's better to fix bugs early on, or else the rest of your compiler will suffer. Make sure your test cases have good coverage of all the rules. If you're taking this course without any friends (as I did), good luck.

comment_content:
One of the richest CS UG course in HKUST content-wise. Challenging like COMP3711 but in a good way.

A must-take if you are looking to pursue PG in CS, and you should audit just for the things you'd learn. Half the class is PG anyways.

Basically a clone of the course offered in Swiss Federal Institute of Technology Lausanne (EPFL) under the same name, but theirs is worth 6 credits.
https://edu.epfl.ch/coursebook/en/computer-language-processing-CS-320

Understanding discrete math, theory of computation, functional programming, computer architecture are extremely useful but not required.

You write a compiler for a Scala-like functional programming language to WebAssembly in Scala in the lab.
comment_teaching:
The instructor is new to teaching in UST and makes some typos, but he is super nice and willing to change the weighting if needed. He handles everything from lecture, tutorials to labs. I only met the TA once in the final.

Every lecture is like a talk you'd find in a developer conference, extremely information dense and detailed. Don't ever skip one because without it you won't even understand the question in tutorials and final.

The instructor is also open for discussions during mid-lecture breaks and after class.
comment_workload:
Would have been worse than B had the project not changed to optional last minute.

Labs are challenging but manageable. The libraries he gave you are in .jar file which makes language server features unavailable. You better clone the GitHub repositories of those libraries and look at the examples yourself.

Final is pretty tough, but being open-book helps. Print the WebAssembly examples and I don't think it's hard to pass.

I decided to still do the project, where you add an extra feature to the compiler you have built in the 5 labs. Form a group of 1 to 3 depending on the difficulty of your feature. This part is super intensive, done during the final period without much help, you basically need to figure out everything yourselves.

With the project, workload is more like D towards the end. However, the high number of PG students means he can give more A than other courses and hard work does have returns.
comment_grading:
The course is definitely not easy, following are the weightings:

60% over 5 lab assignments
40% final exam
[optional] project

The project can recover up to 50% of marks lost in the lab assignments, so basically:

40% * final + min(60%, labs * 60% + project * 30%)

This was not the intended weighting but the one we got because the project late-semester was too heavy.

Labs mean are around 80%
final mean is 55/80
87 is actually the highest possible score and some crazy guy managed 82

Overall 92% => A

Original weighting as follow:

40% final
25% lab (5% each)
35% project

I gave A for grading because hard work will be reflected in the grade, not because it is easy to get good grades.

comment_content:
Very comprehensive and well-organised course materials for beginners. The first half of the course is more theoretical. The second half is more practical with Solidity programming.
Professor will release the lecture videos on YouTube. Attendance is not compulsory but highly recommended.
comment_teaching: One of the best teachings I have seen in this university. Professor is an expert in this domain and explains everything clearly and slowly. He is willing to reply to students’ emails.
comment_workload:
Ustspace has a weird definition on workload. A is very light, B is light, C is average.

Reasonable workload. Since there is no midterm or final, the assignments and the project are relatively more challenging. The marking is forgiving such that you can resubmit once for each assignment (not project) after receiving feedback. Students are required to finish the project on their own without any discussion with peers. That's why it requires weeks to complete the project.

As announced by the professor, a few students had been caught plagiarism in the assignments. This fact is a huge deterrent to cheating in this course.

The teaching is decent and normal. Nothing special, and nothing bad.

Syllabus:
- Hash Functions and Public key cryptography
- Double spending problem
- Bitcoin and proof of work

comment_teaching:
One of the best lecturers in the CSE department for sure. Answers your questions by email quickly, gives great feedback, and has very fluent English.

Homework questions (especially bonus questions) may not be topics taught in the class, but of course, we are CS students, and this is a 4000-level course. We should at least be proficient at googling and not rely on the lecturer spoon-feeding us all the answers for the homework, especially as there are no exams in this course.
comment_workload:
No exams, 7 assignments (coding + report), and 1 project. There are a lot of bonus points in the homework and in the final project. If you don't do the bonus, the workload is quite low. The bonus questions usually are trickier and may not be taught in the class.

The workload is for sure way lower than Prof Golin's COMP3711 or COMP3711H class, even including the bonus questions if you know how to research and use google. Keep in mind that there are no exams in this course, so the number of assignments is completely justified.
comment_grading:
Doing all the required assignments and the final project perfectly will give you 100 points. However, there are also bonus points (up to 25). I received over 100 points, but the official grades are not out yet. There are rumors that the grading scheme will be changed by the CSE department, but we will have to wait and see.

If we use the original grading scheme as agreed at the beginning of the semester, it is a very good grading course.

UPDATE:
How the f*** is 114 A-???????? I did not do the bonus points knowing for sure I will get over 100 points. Now I get A- WOW.

UPDATE 2:
They finally changed my grade to A+.

comment_content:
1.Hash Functions and Public-key Cryptography
2.The Double-spending Problem
3.Bitcoin and Proof-of-Work (PoW)
4.Proof-of-stake and other alternatives to PoW
5.Programmable Blockchains
6.Introduction to Ethereum and Solidity
7.Implementing Smart Contracts
8.Commitment Schemes
9.Auctions and Escrows
10.Re-entrancy and Out-of-gas Vulnerabilities
11.Incentivization Bugs
12.Verifying Correctness of Smart Contracts

The first half of the course mainly focus on theories of blockchain, like cryptography schemes, hash functions and the basic working principles of blockchain.
The second half is to teach you to code your smart contract with solidity. This language is very similar to  C++. Writing codes is the easiest part even if you get a B- in COMP2011, understanding the cybersecurity concepts would be much more important.
comment_teaching: The prof.'s English was very fluent and had almost no accent. He did not use any textbooks or materials, but directly wrote  the corresponding content while lecturing. There was no difference between the lecture session and the lab session. Both of them are simply lectures. The concepts were explained in detail, but they were not enough for completing the assignments.
comment_workload: As I mentioned in the teaching part, the content taught during the lectures are not enough for you to complete the assignments, especially for the first 3 of them. You have to do a lot of research on yourself to discover the answers, from the Internet forums, the bitcoin white book and even some research papers. If you aim full marks on the assignments, then the workload for each of them will be as much as an COMP3711 assignment.
comment_grading:
Grading is the most wonderful and dramatic part of this course. It is a This is an EVEN MORE OUTRAGEOUS AND EXAGGERATED DISASTER then COMP3021!!!

There is no midterm and final exam. Instead, there are 7 assignments and 1 final project. Each assignment carries 10% of your total grade and the final project worths 30%. For each of the assignment, every student got a chance of resubmit if they did not obtain full mark. Apart from this, you can obtain a 15% bonus for assignments and 10% for the final project.

It is hard to tell the actual mean scores for the assignment part as everyone could resubmit. But obviously, it would be very close to 100%. The mean score for the final project is 27.9/30.

My scores:
Assignments 70/70 + 4 bonus.
Final Project 25/30 + 5 bonus.
Overall: 100/100

I thought I would definitely get a A+ for this course. But guess what, the department changed the grading scheme at the final moment. The full mark for this course is updated into 125, and grades will be distributed by a standard way of "拉curve". So my marks become 104/125.

This is much more ironic than the situation in COMP3021. I don't know why there's no one even discuss about it, as there are 125 students in this coures, even more than COMP3021.

comment_content:
Covered the complete process of training, finetuning, inference of large language models. Involve considerations of almost everything you should know to develop / improve a LLM (theoretically):
gradient descent,
gpu architecture and scaling,
multi-gpu training and inference,
fast inference techniques,
prompt engineering and finetuning,
RAG and agent,
LLM evaluation

Rather than dive deep into certain topics, this course gives students an overview of a large area. Taking such a course is much more beneficial than searching on internet or reading paper by myself.

This course covered a lot of recent advancements in LLM training and inference and included many research papers published within one year. A paper list is provided after each lecture, but watching lecture videos is much more efficient to fully understand core idea of a paper than reading myself.
comment_teaching: Never went to lectures but watched all recordings. Despite terrible background noise in the videos, the lectures and ppts still helped me a lot in understanding relevant topics.
comment_workload:
Almost 0

It may be a little hard to understand the content at the very beginning, (for me, especially the scaling law), but after some effort one can

The homework is more about environment setup instead of problem solving. Almost no coding is required. The TA provided all codes and you only need to set up your computer environments to run them.

For the homework, you need do the following by yourself (probably by asking AI) if you haven't done before:
run basic machine learning scripts,
setting up vm and generate ssh keys,
apply for TACC platform access and run inference on the docker.
comment_grading:
Homework 20%
Midterm 30%
Final 50%

Exam is not difficult and sample paper is provided. The most important part is the multi-gpu training and inference.

comment_teaching: Professor Yuan makes a genuine effort to ensure that students understand the material. He is always willing to explain concepts thoroughly, both during and after class. Since some of the topics can be challenging to grasp at first, you may need to go beyond the slides and take time to digest the content on your own or with the help of AI.
comment_workload: To be honest, this course is quite light in terms of workload. Each homework usually takes less than an hour to complete. Since there are no past papers or sample papers, you don’t have much extra material to go through. Just focus on the lecture slides and prepare a good cheatsheet before the exams — that’s usually enough.
comment_grading:
The grading for this course is quite generous. Professor Yuan aims to give around 25% of students an A-range grade, and he does make that happen.

The homework assignments are relatively lenient. Professor Yuan encourages the TA to award full marks as long as there are no obvious mistakes. Even if your answer differs from the reference solution, it may still be considered correct if it’s reasonable.

Both the midterm and final exams are not particularly difficult. Most students can score nearly full marks on the multiple-choice sections. To do well in this course, it’s important to develop a solid understanding of the different types of parallelism, which are central to the content.

Personally, I got full marks on all homework assignments and scored 93 on both the midterm and final, which earned me an A+ in the course.

The content is more like a computer engineering course instead of a computer science course. If you want to learn about what is the thought behind how to improve Machine Learning under Large-Scale, then DO NOT choose this course. According to the COMP4910 information to employer page, the participating industrial company is supposed to compose a workload similar to a FYP project. I am hugely disappointed that the school does not examine and communicate with the industrial party to ensure a relevant and coherent set of workload is assigned in actuality.

In my experience, none of the person in the team (team formed and started way before I joined) is aware of COMP4910's nature, obviously they have no interest in catering your reporting needs since the campus relationship team who signed up for this is irrelevant (they don't make money 🗿) . Therefore, you are just a regular employee to the project manager and you may experience the following scenario that will fuck up your reporting.
1. Being assigned to deal with project management work, have fun doing clerical work (spreadsheets, presentations).
2. Being assigned to multiple projects. I was told that this project (in their pov) is scheduled to be completed a month or two after I commence.

You should have communicated! The industry and the school could both have said that. In this hypothetical situation they are well aware that they have authority (def. the power or right to give orders, make decisions, and enforce obedience) and your only option is say haha yes and suck it up. Any refutation will lead to bureaucracy and coercion. It's just a cruel fact that in this powerplay, students are the one with the lower hand and spring pocket fully exposed. A mirror of the numerous times when your grade suffer when the course logistic fucked up. Understandably, both of them have incentives to appeal to each other, so if there is an issue that result in conflict of interest u are the one to blame with an arbitary reason, will you refuse complying to this "resolution" and risk graduation? Probably not. Knowing that u will do whatever the fuck the company assigned and you are not able to switch to another company, I honestly can't imagine how a person can write up an COMP FYP report when the company assign no code task what so ever. "In this COMP project I participated in 5 irrelevant projects, I wrote meeting minutes, make spreadsheets and presentation decks?" your graders may be sympathetic to your difficulties but they also have to suck it up (comply to the grading scheme).

Overall my experience so far aren't as bad, the scenario above are just worst case. By pure luck, I'm assigned a piece of software (unlike another CS intern being assigned business bullshit) and the project completion date is changed for an unnamed reason. But I do hope by this time I have conveyed the fact that your project's presentation is highly dependent on the commitment of the participating industrial company towards upholding the non-binding pact betweem them and ust.

comment_content:
Contents for each FYP/FYT can vary greatly. They can range from theory (design and implement type systems) to practical (drone search for missing people), and a smaller scope (online barcode generator) to large systems ((further development of) ZINC). Since each project can involve completely different skillsets, it is hard to quantify what "content" each project will involve (except involving CS in some way).

In lieu of comments on the actual content, I'll like to offer a few of my own tips and observations on the FYP process itself. Our FYP is on building a pair of mobile applications, so your flow for more theoretical FYPs/FYTs might differ greatly from mine.

Pre-FYP (Early Feb - Early May)
==========================
General Notes
---------------------------------
While the FYP has not officially started yet, you can think of the following criteria before the official launch of the FYP:
1. Your potential groupmates, if you wish to do a FYP (skip this for a FYT);
2. Your potential supervisor; and
3. Your potential topic.

Having any one of these would be rather helpful for your planning process (or your mental health). Having any of these helps find the other pieces far more easily (since others would like to work with a well-planned project idea / team), and makes the searching process less anxiety-inducing.

While the official FYP informational seminar is held around mid-April each year, you can definitely form groups / talk with supervisors about potential topics or supervision even before this. This means that if you already have someone (no matter if they are a supervisor or potential groupmates) you want to work with, approaching them earlier helps you secure a team or a supervisor. Some popular supervisors only take a limited number of projects, so this becomes even more vital if you want to work under them.

Remember to actively reach out to supervisors before bidding for a project, since projects need to be accepted by the supervisor (and is not first-come-first-served). Most supervisors prefer to gauge your interest and capabilities before choosing your group.

FYP or FYT or Co-op?
---------------------------------
First of all, a FYT requires a 3.7 CGA. While I am unsure about whether this can be waived, an FYT means a solo project (at a minimum), which might be difficult to handle if you already struggle with normal COMP courses. FYTs tend to lean towards the theoretical side, but there are also some (limited) practical FYTs. The requirements are pretty much the same as a FYP, so the difference in overall workload is not significant.

Co-op projects require you to apply to the external company directly, and also requires you to find a UST supervisor. Co-ops have a minimum CGA requirement which varies per year (ours was 3.0), and are not required to produce a video (a poster is required instead). Some FYPs are sponsored by the industry as well if you want to do an industry-adjacent project but do not want to work full-time. You also get to skip the required internship (COMP 1991). (As I have not applied for a Co-op, I am unable to share too many details.)

The choice is up to you if you fulfill the requirements for applying to all three options. You can choose theoretical FYTs if you want to continue onto research (MPhil, PhD), or Co-ops if you want to have some working experience.

Topic Selection
---------------------------------
Desmond believes (and I concur) that you should choose a topic that interests you greatly. The FYP is not a light undertaking, and choosing something that you hate (or is only lukewarm towards) can mean burnout in the later stages of the project when the workload gets immense.

You do not necessarily need to know the tech stack (or the required technical knowledge) thoroughly before the project, though it would help immensely. While you're not encouraged to take up projects with which you have zero knowledge on (especially the theory-based FYPs), your ability to learn is far more important than your existing skillset (unless you already have years of software development experience or published papers under your belt).

Groupmate Selection
---------------------------------
Choose someone you want to work with (and preferably have worked with). This helps reduce the friction when you start working. You have to deal with them for the next ten months, so avoiding people you actively dislike is pretty much crucial.

Ideally, your group should have a mixture of strengths, but this is often impractical:
- Someone good at organizing and writing (for the reports);
- Someone good at presenting (for the final presentation); and
- Someone (at least somewhat) knowledgeable in a technical niche that your project uses.

Ultimately, you need to trust your groupmates and communicate effectively in order to make everything run smoothly - this is perhaps even more important than having a strong technical background.

+AI?
---------------------------------
For +AI students, the EMIA department should approve all projects marked with +AI (not necessarily the Artificial Intelligence tag). If you want the project to have said tag, consider telling your supervisor to add the tag. You can fulfill the requirements of EMIA 4990 by finishing a short survey.

Early FYP (Jun - late Aug/late Dec)
==========================
The main task in this time period is planning and structuring your FYP. This includes understanding the requirements and the scope of the project, experimenting with various tools, and designing your tech stack.

In particular, figuring out exactly what your project entails is important. While you have a short project description and the title, the concrete details are often not confirmed. This means that spending some time with your group to hammer out the features is a worthwhile endeavour. Limiting your scope to something manageable is also important; scope creep can make your project extremely difficult to complete on time.

Since there are often multiple competing libraries or approaches to complete a specific task, understanding the limitations and advantages of each approach can greatly speedup your development process (and helps you complete your literature review; see below). This does not necessarily need to be finalized by this time, but making sure what you wish to do can actually be accomplished using current technology is important. Ideally, you can finish the development environment setup and write some simple proof-of-concept code for the libraries you intend to use.

Around mid-August to early September, you will need to start writing your proposal report. The proposal consists of the objectives, the literature review, the design and implementation and the test plan of your project. Most of the objectives and the literature review is reused throughout; the other sections can have less concrete details, and can be changed as your project progresses.

The reason why the Early FYP and the Mid-FYP time period is so variable is due to your workload and motivation during the Fall semester - if your Fall courses have overly intense workloads, it is possible that you will not accomplish a lot during the semester.

You will also need to submit the Individual Ethics Essay and two monthly reports in the Fall semester.

The monthly reports need to be signed and graded by the supervisor, and should not take up a lot of your time; point-form statements on your current progress and future work should do. Most supervisors understand that you are busy, so grading is generally generous.

The ethics essay is an essay on the ethical implications of your FYP, which is graded by Noor, the communication tutor. (More on Noor in the teaching section). Since the grading by Noor is rather arbitrary and hard to predict, doing your best is the only advice I can offer. (Grades can range from A+ to C from my experience, and does not seem to be correlated with your writing abilities.)

Mid-FYP (Sept/Late Dec - Early Feb)
==========================
The winter semester is where most people start putting serious amounts of effort into the FYP. Implementation of the features often start during this time, and a sizable chunk of development time is spent here. Do not expect to have a lot of spare time during the winter semester, especially if you choose a supervisor which is relatively... hardworking.

At this stage, communication with your groupmates becomes more important (unless you have very clearly separated your work). You might want to meet a bit more before the Spring semester, where the majority of the workload hits.

You will need to submit a monthly report for both December and January, and the progress report (due mid-February). The progress report only requires minimal edits to the proposal report for the non-technical sections and the design (unless anything specific changed in these sections). For each implementation section, update the previously written work with the work that you have completed and future plans for that part. You might want to define your evaluation more concretely at this stage - try to add quantitative measurements to your project if applicable, or start user testing to gather some concrete feedback if you are building an application of some sort.

Late FYP (Mid-Feb - Early May)
==========================
After submitting the progress report, you should critically evaluate your project and figure out what cannot be completed within the two months left - it is normal for teams to cut some of the planned features if the project itself is too ambitious.

The major milestones remaining are the self-assessment report and the final report (both due mid-April), code submission (due late April), the final presentation (due late April to early May; usually on Saturdays) and the video trailer (due early May). There are a lot of deadlines, so be sure to space the work out evenly and make realistic assessments of how much work you are able to complete.

Note that you do not need to complete everything by the final report submission date nor by the code submission date - I knew of teams which were still implementing features the night before the final presentation. However, the information contained within the report should at least be truthful by the date of the presentation.

The major changes to the final report are the discussion and evaluation sections; they are usually written to evaluate quantitatively how well you have accomplished the objectives. If your project is a piece of software, you might want to collect user feedback from user acceptance testing; this helps shore up any weaknesses that your project has.

The self-assessment report is only in play if there are freeriders; otherwise, point form will do.

For the final presentation, make sure to include live demonstrations (if possible), record videos for parts which are not, and prepare for any tricky questions that the second reader may ask.

The video trailer is only three minutes; consider making a narrative if you want a good grade. Otherwise, a simple recap of your project does the trick.
comment_teaching:
Desmond (Certified Good Guy ™️)
==========================
Desmond was incredibly supportive throughout the project period, and was very helpful throughout (though perhaps a bit "chur"). We met biweekly (once per two weeks), and he provided detailed advice and useful suggestions for our project throughout. He kept up with our project progress and was able to grade our work very quickly. A++ la ha!

Desmond specializes in building software systems; other professors may have different specialties according to their research interests or area.

Communication Tutors
==========================
You will need to meet with the communication tutors twice during the semester; once before the progress report and once before the final report. Half of the FYP teams are tutored by Noor and half the FYP teams are tutored by Ada at the time of writing.

Since Noor did not have a solid technical background, she was sometimes unable to understand our project (but attempted to provide advice as if she did). The suggestions were somewhat questionable and sometimes downright incorrect. My advice would be to smile and nod throughout, and be thankful you only have to meet twice.
comment_workload:
The only course which I can consider an F in workload appropriate.

The workload increases exponentially if your project is large, if you don't know some of the important tools of the project, or if you discover that you want to implement feature(s) which require you to basically refactor everything.

It is, again, impossible to evaluate whether the workload is too high since each project is different - I can only tell you my subjective experience.

However, the workload is solidly 12 credits when distributed throughout (in my own opinion). It was 2 credits for Summer (project planning), 2 credits for Fall (slight amounts of work) and 8 credits for Winter + Spring semester (when everything is on fire).

This is without a doubt the highest workload per credit course for non-independent work courses in CSE (exceeding COMP 2012H with a very large margin). Not to say that the work is not meaningful, but the workload was indeed immense.

To all who are going to work on FYP: I wish you every success, and good luck! It is a long (but rewarding) journey.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme
================
The grading scheme has an advisor component and a second reader component, and the grades for each component are weighted in a 60:40 ratio (60% advisor, 40% second reader).

For the advisor component:
Project proposal report - 5%
Individual ethics essay - 5% (graded by the communication tutor)
Project progress report - 20%
Final project report - 35%
Oral presentation/thesis defense - 30%
Monthly reports (Oct, Nov, Dec & Jan) - 5%

For the second reader component:
Project proposal report - 5%
Project progress report - 15%
Final project report - 35%
Oral presentation/thesis defense - 40%
Poster session / Video Trailer - 5%

Actual Grading
================
From what I can tell, actual grading is rather generous and clustered around the A range, though getting an A+ might be difficult. If your supervisor and second reader are typical CSE professors, there should not be any undue problems provided that you did your work. Some second readers may have special requirements which deviate from the norm though, so make sure to do your research and ask around.

comment_content: The course title "Advanced Artificial Intelligence" is significantly misleading. Rather than covering recent advanced developments, the curriculum is almost entirely on traditional, rule-based Reinforcement Learning and classical planning, which is better suited for an introductory or "Classical AI". While the instructor attempts to modernize the material by mentioning recent breakthroughs like GPT and OpenAI, these additions feel superficial, lacking any technical substance.
comment_teaching: The lecture quality is severely lacking. The instructor’s delivery is monotonous and difficult to hear due to unclear articulation and low volume. Furthermore, the "in-class competition" designed to mimic a Turing Test was particularly poorly executed. The software system was plagued by technical bugs, leading to an unfair and frustrating rating experience for students.
comment_workload: The volume of questions was excessive, failing to respect the time constraints typically expected at this level. While "sample questions" and A3 cheat sheets were permitted, the actual exam bore no resemblance to the provided practice materials, calling the utility and authenticity of the study resources into question. Thus, do NOT recommend.
comment_grading: The grading policy is unreasonably harsh for a PG-level course. Several students received grades below a B, which is critical as it prevents the course from counting toward PG graduation requirements. The final exam was poorly designed; rather than testing advanced conceptual understanding on AI, it relied on sheer volume, containing far too many questions of Classic AI and traditional planning.

comment_content: The course content is extremely outdated and doesn't live up to its name, "Advanced Artificial Intelligence"—it's not advanced at all. It's practically useless for your current AI studies. Plus, the course is rather boring.
comment_teaching: The teacher spoke softly and had an unclear accent, making it difficult to understand what he was saying. Moreover, the entire class was so boring it made me want to sleep because I couldn't hear clearly and the lectures weren't very good. I highly do not recommend it.
comment_workload: The course consists of three assignments and two projects. Attendance at the project is mandatory; otherwise, you will not receive a grade of B or higher. Attendance at the other classes is not required.
comment_grading: Some of my classmates got a C in this course. The grades weren't high, and the exam was quite difficult. However, they provide last year's questions and allow you to create an A3 cheat sheet, but last year's questions are completely different from this year's. I don't even know if last year's sample questions are genuine.

comment_content: Content is very old school, but it can be an interesting experience if you want to see different sides of AI. It covers topics like search algorithms (genetic programming, simple agents, etc) and game theory, which are not what is typically covered in AI courses. So if you are interested in those topics, this course can be of interest to you. But if you are interested in modern machine learning or deep learning, then you probably should avoid this class. It will only cover some very basic aspects of deep learning, and may not be that useful.
comment_teaching: The professor can sometimes go on long tangents. But I find him. clear in presenting the main ideas. The lecture also covers most of the concepts in assignment and final, but does not go into much details. So you should study on your own a lot if you want to keep in pace with the class.
comment_workload: Workload is fairly light, since grade is based very heavily on the final exam.
comment_grading: The assignment is fairly similar to the final exam, so it can be good practice. But both the assignments are much more difficult than what is taught in class. So a lot of self learning may be a good idea.

comment_content: I just took the first class, but I would say the class material is really outdated. The material is old school 20 years ago. Attendance – 10%

Final Exam – 20%

comment_content: The material covers fundamental Python topics such as if/else statements, for loops, lists, dictionaries, basic classes, functions/methods, and recursion. It also includes Turtle graphics, a library that lets you create drawings by controlling “turtles” (which act like brushes or pens) through code. The content should be very straightforward for anyone with prior programming experience. However, be cautious with Python’s data types since the language is type-optional, and type hints won’t be taught or tested in exams.
comment_teaching: Alex Lam, the professor, teaches well and often repeats key points to ensure clarity, recognizing that programming concepts can initially be confusing and need reinforcement. That said, I personally found the lectures unnecessary since I already had programming experience and skipped most of them, so I can’t comment much on the teaching overall.
comment_workload: The workload is very light. There are 7 labs plus an introductory lab; only 3 labs are submitted and graded. The non-graded labs are optional but worth reviewing since their content often appears on the final exam. The labs are easy as long as you can follow instructions and apply basic programming skills.
comment_grading:
There are two grading schemes:

24% midterm + 36% labs + 40% final
OR 42% labs + 58% final
The course includes 7 labs plus an introductory lab. Three labs are graded out of 100. The labs themselves are relatively easy, but you must be meticulous because there are usually no partial marks—one small error causing your program to fail a test case can cost you up to 20% of that lab’s score (this actually happened to me). Lab averages typically range from 90 to 100 with a standard deviation of 8 to 10.

The midterm is fairly simple if you’re careful and review your answers. Since the exam is remote, you should have enough time, especially if you have prior programming experience. The midterm average was about 71.9 with a standard deviation around 21.4.

In contrast, the final exam is very challenging. Time is tight, leaving little room to double-check answers. The questions are tricky and sometimes misleading—for example, multiplying a number by 2 when the number is actually a string, not an integer. Extreme caution is required. This semester’s final average was 48.57 with a standard deviation of 19.29.

One strategy is to treat the final as a typing test by quickly typing out the code they don’t allow you to copy. If you can type fast (90-100 words per minute), this approach can reduce stress and improve performance.

comment_content: The contents are comprehensive, covering (1) standard machine learning topics like linear regression, logistic regression, and simple generative models (Naive Bayes), Bias-variance trade-off;  (2) Deep learning models like FFN, CNN, RNN, and Transformer; (3) Deep generative models like VAE, GAN, diffusion models; (4) Reinforcement learning. Each parts are accompanied by hands-on or written assignments to deepen your understanding. So, after studying this course, you should have a good command of basic ideas of machine learning and deep learning. The course is taught using a probabilistic perspective, so a good understanding of probability theory would be beneficial.
comment_teaching: The teaching is good and the explanation is intuitive. The teaching materials and plan are well-designed. The instructor has posted recorded videos that you could watch after class. Also, he is available and responsive on Piazza to answer questions by himself.
comment_workload: The workload is substantial, comprising 4 written assignments and approximately 5 hands-on assignments to complete. A similarity score exceeding 40% will give you a penalty on the grade, so you need to be careful to answer the questions in the assignment.
comment_grading: Though the final exam is open-book and you may bring up to four cheat sheets, I found the questions not trivial; you need to have a good understanding of the materials covered in the class in order to answer the questions and get high grades. The final exam consists of 64% of the final grade, which I think is a little bit large.

comment_content: It covers almost all machine learning models, such as bert, transformer diffusion, cnn, rnn, lstm, etc., and reasoning all the formula foundations and model foundations in detail. You need to follow him carefully in the early stage of each class; otherwise, you will understand more and more. (Utilitarianism speaking,) There is no need to attend this course in class. The ppt covers a very wide range of derivations, and the teacher will complete the derivations in great detail. However, most of the questions in the exam are very fragmented explanation questions. You need to ensure that you can basically cover all the knowledge content. Derivations are a "super bonus point" for the exam.
comment_teaching: A very, very, very, very, very, very, very responsible teacher. The lesson preparation is completely serious, and the homework is strictly formulated according to the teaching schedule. Listening to his classes is a kind of enjoyment
comment_workload: The workload is heavy, with 4 WA and more than 5 HA. The HA assignments are not difficult, but some of the WA questions can be quite challenging. Since it's an electronic assignment, and considering the time spent on typing formulas, some of the proof questions in WA can be postponed for about two hours. I think it's highly likely that I won't be able to solve them during the exam. Sure, part of the reason is that my foundation in mathematics and science is not good enough. Surprisingly, the connection between the exam and the regular homework is very small. The types of questions in the regular homework account for less than 10% of the exam and are almost never included. Even if they are included, the content is extracted from a single homework question and condensed into a 2-point short question, There are approximately three sub-questions. 2+2+5=9 points is included in the homework.
