comment_workload: 四次作业，一次project，在kaggle上面，做一个简单的线性回归，算MSE，来进行排行，这个project需要达到baseline会比较好，四次的作业还挺难的
comment_grading: 给分目前暂不确定，还没出分呢。考勤占比10%，作业4次36%，project14%，半期考20%，期末20%

comment_content: All theoretical things you need to know about machine learning.
comment_teaching: Very good teaching with both examples and theoretical inference. The professor takes courses from cmu and Stanford as reference.
comment_workload: A little bit large. The first assignment  took me 14 hours, and the second 6 hours, the third 8 hours. The fourth is en exercise before exam. There’s also a project, an easy midterm exam and a little bit hard final exam. But in all, the assignments are more difficult than the exams but discussions with classmates is permitted.
comment_grading: The professor is generous when giving grades. I got more than B+.

comment_content: Rich and fulfilling. Introduction to major learning paradigms and techniques, basic applied statistics and information theory, decision trees, neural networks, Bayesian classification, kernel methods, clustering, density estimation, feature selection and extraction, hidden Markov models, reinforcement learning, case-based learning, model selection and various applications.
comment_teaching: The teacher explained very clearly, but the pace of the class was a bit tight. More extra effort is needed to better absorb the knowledge learned in class.
comment_workload: The coursework comprises a total of four written homework assignments, one coding assignment, a midterm exam, and a final exam.
comment_grading: nice. The teacher is very kind

comment_content: This course encompasses a diverse range of topics, each of which could potentially necessitate several hours of in-depth instruction. Consequently, the pace may seem somewhat hurried as the professor endeavors to impart the essential knowledge and key points within the allotted time frame. However, efforts have been made to ensure that the core concepts are communicated effectively despite the compressed timeframe.
comment_teaching: Professor He's teaching is commendable, as he possesses the ability to elucidate all the course materials with clarity and thoroughness. What sets him apart is his willingness to share his personal insights and perspectives, enhancing students' understanding. Nevertheless, due to the extensive nature of the content, achieving a comprehensive grasp may necessitate additional effort outside of class.
comment_workload: The coursework comprises a total of four written homework assignments, one coding assignment, a midterm exam, and a final exam. These written homework assignments serve as a valuable tool for reinforcing class content and fostering deeper understanding of the topics covered. Although they require a dedicated time investment, the effort expended is well worth it in terms of enhancing learning outcomes.
comment_grading: nice

comment_content:
The content covers most of the tranditional machine learning models and goes deep into them, providing you with a comprehensive understanding of the details behind. This courses is more difficult than previous machine learning courses but you can definitely learn a lot from it.
Basically it covers Linear Regression, Logistic regression, Exponential Family, Generalized linear models, Kernel Methods,Kernel methods, SVM,Generative Models,Naive Bayes, MLE, MAP,Generalization, bias-variance tradeoff,Clustering, EM,PCA before midterm.

And Probabilistic Graphical Models,HMM,Neural Networks, backpropagation,Transformer, Variational autoencoder,GANs, Reinforcement Learning, Language models after midterm.

This courses covers much topics while each topic may worth several hours to teach. So it will be a bit rush, but professor tried to deliver all the key points to you in limited time.
comment_teaching: Professor He's teaching is really good. He can explain all the content clearly and in details. The most important thing is that he will tell you his own understanding about the stuff.  However, the content is too much, if you want to make everything clear, you may still need to take some effort after class.
comment_workload:
In total there are 4 written homework + 1 coding assignment + midterm exam + final exam.
The 4 written homework helps us review the content in class and extend a bit to learn the topic deeper. It takes some time to do them but it worth spend time.

The professor said the exam will be much easier than homework.
comment_grading:
The professor said the grading will be generous.

And homework has some bonus questions for us to improve the grade. If you master the content in class, you can answer the bonus questions.

comment_content: Enough, covers most of the necessary techniques. And these are quite inspiring and helped me with my academic research.
comment_teaching: The new professor, so not as good as an experienced one. But overall, he is trying his best to give us a good lesson, I really understand it is not easy for a new professor.
comment_workload: a little time-consuming. A lot of maths about the ground theory, we can understand that machine learning itself is not a black box. the problem is only from deep learning.
comment_grading: Good, very fair. And I like this course and the instructor style.

comment_content: The course is well-organized. The topics were presented in a logical order. The course covers a wide range of useful topics.
comment_teaching: The professor is an excellent teacher who does a great job of explaining complex. The classroom atmosphere is very positive, and the professor is approachable and eager to solve the confusion of students.
comment_workload: There are several assignments in this course that require you to do mathematics derive or coding. While these assignments can take some time to complete, they are worth the effort. They are very effective in helping you develop important skills and concepts, and completing them has helped me better understand the course material.
comment_grading: If you complete all the assignments and projects seriously, you can certainly earn a good grade in this course. These tasks are very effective in helping you develop important skills and concepts. The exam is also manageable if you review the main knowledge carefully.

comment_content:
Introduction to Machine Learning
Math basics: Linear algebra, Calculus, Probability
Linear models: Linear Regression, Logistic Regression and SVM
Convex Optimization: Smoothness, Lasso Regression, Gradient descent, stochastic gradient descent and its variants
Kernel methods
Tree-based method
Deep Learning:
Convolutional Neural Networks
Recurrent Neural Networks
Transformer and NLP
His research field in Machine Learning
AutoML
Trustworthy Machine Learning
comment_teaching: The content of the course has little to do with the assignment, written assignments are related to convex optimization.
comment_workload:
3(written)+2(programming) assignment
written assignment: derivation, convex, smoothness, hessian matrix, Newton method, lasso regression
programming assignment: CNN, LSTM
comment_grading: Grading is good.

comment_content: his course encompasses a wide range of machine learning topics, providing you with a comprehensive understanding of the underlying theory. By completing this course, you will acquire a solid foundation in machine learning.
comment_teaching: Due to the extensive coverage of topics in this course, the teaching pace may be relatively fast.
comment_workload: The workload is a little bit heavy. It requires 4 written assignments first. I choose not to present so I have to finish 6 hands-on assignments as well.
comment_grading: The examination for this course is conducted with closed books, meaning you won't have access to external references. However, the exam itself is not overly challenging. By diligently following the guidance of the instructor and thoroughly reviewing the lecture notes, you can expect to achieve a high grade.

comment_content: The contents are quite comprehensive and the instructor covered almost every aspect in Machine Learning.
comment_teaching: I think the teaching part can be further improved. Dr. Cheng definitely knows what he's talking about but sometimes it's hard to interpret to students. Maybe he can talk louder next time.
comment_workload: The workload was fine. There are 5 assignments with mathematical problems and programming problems. Each may take around 2-3 days. There are no midterm. And you need to conduct a machine learning-related project and do the presentation. And there is a final.
comment_grading: You can definitely get high grade as long as you go through the slides thoroughly and listen to the recordings. The first two assignments are quite challenging since they are all mathematical problems. But the following ones become easier with programming problems.

comment_content: This course includes valuable information on machine learning topics such as probability, regression, RNN, CNN, Transformer, GAN, VAE, and RL. It is highly beneficial and applicable.
comment_teaching:
Nevin is an excellent teacher, and his courses are straightforward to understand. However, he did mention in the first class that his course is not suitable for students without prior knowledge of machine learning. Nonetheless, I believe it could be beneficial for beginners who are willing to put in the effort to learn.
And I think his website about the course is quite clear for students with a lot of useful information about course info, announcements, course materials and RVC videos.
comment_workload: I find the workload a bit burdensome. The homework and coding labs require a significant amount of my time almost every week. However, Nevin presented an alternative - an opportunity to work on a machine learning project. Those who choose to take on the team project(2 to 3 students as a team) can avoid completing the ongoing coding labs.
comment_grading:
4 Written Assignments: (4x5%)
Projects: (30%)
Final examination: (50%)
I received a score of 5/5 on most of my homework assignments.
My performance on the final exam was close to the average score but a little higher.
That's A-

comment_content: This course covers a wide range of topics in Machine Learning. It also introduces new research in the relevant areas. The main focus of the course is the theoretical foundation behind all the ML techniques, which means you need to understand the equations and algorithms thoroughly.
comment_teaching: Prof. ZHANG's course materials are well-prepared. Basically all the questions in the final exam are covered in the course material. He also provides the recordings of the lecture which are useful for revision.
comment_workload: The workload is reasonable. All the homework are not very time-consuming. If you have experience in writing research papers, the programming assignment should not be very challenging since there are many existing code online that can be reused and modified.
comment_grading: Since this is a PG course the average grading is relatively higher. Around 50% of all students can get A range. The evaluate criteria includes written assignments, programming project/assignments, and a final exam. Students can choose from complete a group project or finish multiple programming assignments. The written assignments are not very hard and the contents are covered in the course material.

comment_content: The course contents are nicely designed, covering both theoretical foundations and high-level ideas. The focus of the course is on deep learning techniques such as CNN, RNN, Transformer, VAE, and GAN, as opposed to traditional machine learning algorithms. Brief introductions to algorithms in recent papers are also provided for each topic. It is recommended that students have some basic knowledge of machine learning before taking this course as many topics are covered. The course provides helpful tutorials that teach about programming and hands-on homework. Programming homework gives students the skeleton code, so students know where to start.
comment_teaching: Prof. Zhang is an excellent teacher who provides wonderful intuitions in this course. He delivers the core concepts in more detail and uses a tablet to write notes, which is helpful for students to follow. The course also utilizes Piazza to facilitate question-asking. In addition, the TAs also provide great support to the students. Overall, the course is well-organized and provides a great learning experience.
comment_workload: Completing the homework and project assignments for this course can cost quite some time, but the good thing is that you can learn from the well-designed materials. The most time-consuming aspect of the course is reviewing the class materials because of the packed course contents.
comment_grading: The final grade for the course is determined by the final exam, which accounts for 50% of the total score, homework, which accounts for 20%, and the project, which accounts for 30%. For the project, students have the option to complete multiple programming assignments or work on a team project related to their chosen topic. The final exam is a reflection of the amount of effort put into the course.

comment_content: Course are useful, well prepared, and could be considered as complete deep introductory materials to provide mathematical and implementation know-how of fundamental progression in machine learning.
comment_teaching: Prof. Zhang explained the materials very well. His knowledge is so deep and he went as far as giving an accurate analogy to illustrate his point, and make sure that his points are delivered well to the students.
comment_workload: HWs and HAs can be too much for people without basic math and Python, but I reckon the workload is still doable even for people with such conditions. I didn't take the project path, so I can't comment on that.
comment_grading: The grading is generous. 50% will get an A range, and 90% will get a minimum B, I think, but the Prof notes that we are working hard also for other things, so he tries not to make us worried too much about the final exam.

comment_content: At a high level, this course can be splitted into two modules: fundamentals and recent researches. The fundamentals cover some mathematic basics (e.g., derivatives, matrix calculation, probability theory) and why machine learning works from theory level. This part is derived from UCLA CS 260. The other part focuses on recent advances in deep learning (e.g., transformers, GANs, semi-supervised learning).
comment_teaching: Unsatisfactory. It can be observed that the instructor tries to make things clear. However, most of the time he would waste on the simple concepts and nearly skipped on what's unfamiliar to me (e.g., graph theory). Even some times the instructor receive no response for the question "If someone understands, please raise your hand", leaving an awkward classroom atmosphere.
comment_workload: The workload can also be splitted into two categories. For programming projects, you may Google it or search Github for the original answers (as I've said in the content section, it basically copies UCLA CS260). It contains just some simple prediction algorithms. However, for the written homework, it would cost a lot of efforts to dig into it since it is proofs to some of mathematical concepts with few reference you can refer to.
comment_grading: I think it is like a binary grading. The final is not difficult. For assignments, as long as you can "complete" them, there will be full marks (since the expected outcome for programming and proofs is obvious), otherwise the marks would be low.

comment_content: The topics in the syllabus were quite interesting. Few topics covered are Theory of generalisation, SVM, DNN, etc.
comment_teaching: He partially copied his slides from the course by Prof. Yaser (Caltech), but was not very good at explaining them. I also found it hard to understand what he is speaking as he speaks in a very low voice.
comment_workload: Not much workload. Few assignments, one project, and one exam. The instructor never told or have any useful feedback on the project proposal, but was very quick to criticize the final report. I would have preferred if he has given some feedback earlier so we can improve.
comment_grading:
No consideration was given to whether the student understands the topic, but only whether the answer is ditto copy of what the instructor wishes to see there. Using any other valid definition than what was presented will lead to deduction in marks.  

No explanation is usually given on many assignments like why they deduct marks until you ask explicitly, and even then your request could be ignored indefinitely.

I also found that he himself doesn't understand many concepts, and is extremely hostile toward any sort of discussion about grading.
This was the first course of his career, and I hope he will becomes a better teacher in the future.

comment_content:
The topic includes basic machine learning concepts, linear regression, generalization, RL concepts, generative models, unsupervised learning, etc.

The lecture discusses mostly about theory and the intuition of the machine learning concepts. It is okay to not fully memorize the technical details as long as you understand the intuition.
comment_teaching: Prof Nevin explains the concepts well although sometimes I find it lacking and not clear (maybe due to accent issues).
comment_workload:
If you have experience with coding, then the workload can be good. The other questions in the homework about ML concepts can be easily answered.

In order to have good result in the exam, it is important to undestand the intution of the concepts.
comment_grading: I thin the grading is okay. Most students can get an A based from the other students I asked.

comment_content:
Well Organized, up-to-date content. Nevin updated the content every year actually.

Little programming thing is involved in the lecture; most of the things are about the theory side (but useful).
comment_teaching: Clear enough; easy to follow with a lot of handwriting maths.
comment_workload:
Okay for me. But some students complain the workload is too high with the writing assignments, program assignments, the project, and the final exam.

The questions in the final exam are similar to those in the writing assignment.
comment_grading: Fair enough for most students. Not difficult to get an A for a PG student.

comment_content: The course covers basic concepts of machine learning. The contents are easy to follow and useful.
comment_teaching: The professor and TA are good at teaching and their delivery is really nice.
comment_workload: The workload is not too light, but not too heavy. Assignments and final exam are not too hard while the project is quite time consuming and hard to get a great score.
comment_grading: The professor is very generous. It's easy to get B+ as long as you follow the workload and your exam score is not too low.

comment_content: The content is very useful I think. It is fundamental for all PG students, not only people who work in the machine learning area.
comment_teaching: Nevin Zhang teaches very clearly on both the overall picture and the details. I like him very much.
comment_workload: Very little workload. It is easy to finish the homework and projects. If you do not want to cost too much time, please choose this.
comment_grading: Good grade, easy to get A or above even for me. I think if you want to promote your GPA, then you should absolutely choose this course.

comment_content: Covered many basic aspects of ML, from basic calculations such as backwardpropagation and gradient descent to specific structures such as CNN, LSTM, GAN.
comment_teaching: Nevin is really a good lecturer. He prepared slides carefully, maintained a good pace in class, and answered questions in time.
comment_workload: Not too much. Written assignments are basic, if you follow the lectures. You need to work on a group project, but you can choose virtually any topic related to ML. Final exam is not so hard, either.
comment_grading: Grading seems good as far as I am concerned.

comment_content: I took this course in UG. I would say that this course is the same as what I took. This course will help the student to understand the basic concepts of  machine learning and deep learning. It is very fun to take this course.
comment_teaching:
The professor Nevin L Zhang is nice and give his point accurately;
However, the handwriting is hard to recognize.
comment_workload: Workload is not very high. Totally can handle it. No mid-term, only project and final exam.
comment_grading:
The grading includes 4 assignments and the mid/final exam.
This course only touches on the basics of neural networks-based computer vision. 90% of student can get B+.

comment_content: There is no pre-defined syllabus, but rather the lecturer attempts to adapt the teaching to the background of the students. The contents of the class has consisted of mainly: discussion of ChatGPT, anagram/bigram models, inductive bias, learning vs. performance components of ML, search, Hidden Markov Models, etc.
comment_teaching: The class is very much an interactive and discussion-based class. If you are an engaged student that likes to ask questions and think out loud, you make like this course. If you are student who wants to just listen and take notes, I don't recommend this course. For several lectures, the professor simply never came, and the TA was forced to hold a lecture.
comment_workload: The workload is relatively light since most of the term since the lecturer does not assign much homework, however he will occasionally assign some task to think about and hand-in. The final project may take a lot of time, or may take little time, depending on the difficulty of your project. The lecturer promotes choosing something different and conduct new research, however this did not seem compulsory.
comment_grading: You are graded on your in-class participation (not exactly attendance), forum participation, mid-term exam as well as a final project. I do not know the grading ratios unfortunately.

comment_content: The course covered all fundamentals in data-base management systems. You can attend the course even if you do not have any prior knowledge in Databases.
comment_teaching: The teaching was excellent. The professor asked multiple questions throughout the lessons which personally helped me keep up with the course.
comment_workload: You will not have a large workload. I recomment however to attend lectures so that you will keep will keep in touch with the course.
comment_grading: Grading is not bad considering the fact that the course only gives a final exam and an assingment consisting of writing a short survey and making a presentation.

comment_content: Ralational Model, SQL language and Database Design. This course covers most of the basic content about database and some advanced design in database. Some of them might be related to the algorithm and computer architecture. Not an easy course if you never learn related courses before.
comment_teaching: Prof. Dimitris explain every thing very well and give many examples for better understanding.
comment_workload: No homework after class. You just need to submit a 5-page survey on the providing topic or any topic related to database, which takes about 3-4 days to finish. And you also need to make a presentation based on the survey and take the final exam. 50% final exam, 20% presentation, 20% survey, 10% class participation.
comment_grading: The grading is good I think.

comment_content: Basic DB knowledge ranging from the use of SQL to transactions. If you have a CS/SE background, most things should be covered in your UG courses. Some interesting advanced topics were mentioned but not covered in the final exam.
comment_teaching: Prof. Dimitris explained everything very well (at least much better than my UG lecturer) and also recalled your memories of other knowledge.
comment_workload: Only need to prepare for the presentation (with the survey report) and the final exam, which is easy for PG students. No homework, but you may not be familiar with the questions in the final exam if you are not from CSE.
comment_grading: 50% final exam, 20% presentation, 20% survey, 10% class participation.

comment_content:
Basically follows the classic DB textbook "Database System Concepts". The contents covered were quite UG-level (SQL, relational model, query optimization, join algorithms, transactions, and concurrent control...)

2/3 of the semester were course lectures and 1/3 were student presentations. Only contents in lectures were examed. No attendance requirement.
comment_teaching:
Dimitris is good at explaining things in a clear way.
Apart from his teaching, he is quite nice. He tries his best to accommodate all your (reasonable) requests on presentation and survey submission arrangements.
comment_workload:
No assignment or project. One survey (around 3000 words required) and one presentation (less than 15min) in a DB-related research area. One final exam.

P.S. Dimitris's advice on the survey and presentation:  "Independently of your topic, focus on the general problem and methodologies. Try to avoid technical details and complicated math"
comment_grading:
Have no idea with the final grading scheme, but I think it is fair. My final exam score ranked around top 25%, and I got A-.

The exam questions were standard, which covered all aspects I mentioned and focused on basic concepts and approaches.

comment_content: Old, repetitive, and with some mistakes in the slides. The slides are not enough to fully understand the concepts. You can search for them or attend the class to fully understand them.
comment_teaching: I suggest attending the lectures as well. The instructor takes attendance sometimes (maybe two to three times in the whole semester). I'm not sure if they matter. 10% of each session is useful; the rest is not.
comment_workload:
2 homeworks which were easy and 1 bonus homework which was easier.
No programming assignments.
The midterm and finals need some studying but they're also openbook (you can bring the slides to the exam).
comment_grading: Not bad in grading. It's easy to get B+ and with a good understanding of the basics you can get an A.

comment_content: The content is not too much or too less. It does not cover web security but some other most common computer network basis. Application layer, transport layer, network layer, link layer and physic layer.
comment_teaching: The professor is very nice and gives a lot of examples to help students understand in class.
comment_workload: There are 2 mandatory assignments to gain 30% scores. The third assignment is optional if you are happy with your first two assignments.
comment_grading: The grading is overall good. It seems everyone could achieve at least B, as long as no cheating.

comment_content:
1.Introduction
Introduction to Networking
Network Architectures

2.Application Layer
Application Layer Protocols: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, CDN, Streaming
P2P Networks and Applications

3.Transport Layer
The TCP/IP and Internet Architecture
Transport Protocols: TCP and UDP
Reliable data transfer
------------------------------------ ↑ midterm
TCP Congestion Control

4.Network Layer: Data path and Control
Network Protocol: IP
Switch architecture, Packet scheduling and buffer management
Network Routing and Routing Algorithms
Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)
IP Version 6 (IPv6)

5.Link Layer and Local Area Networks
Link Layer services
Multiple Access Protocols: Aloha/Ethernet
Bridges and Switches
comment_teaching: I think it's good. The lessons are clear and interesting, and the content isn't difficult to understand.And even if you can't attend the lectures for some reason, reviewing the slides before the exam will be enough to help you easily learn most of the content.
comment_workload:
There are three assignments in total, each with a relatively small number of questions, which is very manageable. Assignments account for 30% of the final grade. Only the two highest-scoring assignments are counted, meaning one assignment can be skipped. Overall, the workload on assignments is minimal.
If you never attend classes and haven't studied any related content, you can still prepare for the exam by reviewing your homework and slides before the exam, and the workload remains not heavy
comment_grading:
I believe that if you spend a normal amount of time preparing before the exam (or you master the basic content of the slides and assignments), it's difficult to get a score significantly lower than average. However, getting a high score isn't easy either.

Midterm exam is obviously more difficult than final exam. But getting a score above 80 on the final exam isn't always easy. This depends on luck and the grading criteria.

comment_content:
Typical contents about top-down computer network.
The teaching material is the classic top-down book.
However, I dont think you can learn something new if you used to take UG computer network course, especially in mainland china which might cover far more contents.
comment_teaching:
BBB teachs much more better than instructor at my undergraduate host institution.

And also, he teachs much more better than most of my UG CSE cources' instructors.

And the cost is the progess of the course is too slow.
comment_workload: Two assignments. Its a very low workload. And the content of the course is very simple. BBB will teach much more less content even than a UG computer network course.
comment_grading: If you want to spend some time on reviewing, you can get a good score at the final. BUT, more PhD students do not have time to review.

comment_content: 就是自顶而下课本的5个layer，不过最后没有讲完，只讲到了第六章。
comment_teaching: 老师讲课很认真
comment_workload: 不错的工作量，2个HW
comment_grading: 期末考了链路算法，DV算法，CRC，ARP，第一道大题考的略杂有点说不清楚考了啥，总体上难度很适中，但期中考试的强度确实不低，需要提前认真复习。会出课本上的题目，想拿高分把课后题刷一遍，再看看往年卷(可早至2003年)就没什么问题。要看课本，光看PPT绝对不行。但是期末考乱改分，有个TA连数都算不对，我说他改错了他也不给改，说已经过了平均分不用担心

comment_content: stuff hasn't been changed for 15 years, except the powerpoint was cut down by the publisher, and he will go back and teach u those stuff very in-depth
comment_teaching:
good, very very good, it's better than the one you can find online which only talk about how stuff works, but he tries to explain why stuff are designed like this from a kinda researcher's (?) perspective (because he was indeed in that era when networking was being designed and stuff)

but lmao
he tried to only release the lecturer recordings if there was sufficient attendance
then he tried to only release recordings just before the midterm, like 1-2 days
then after the midterm the attendance was so low (like only 5 people attended) that he just gave up and released all recordings
i feel bad lol
comment_workload: meh
comment_grading:
One of the midterm questions was like, you want to send 9 segments with go back n and selective repeat, no lost acks, window size 3, but a loss event happens every 5 packets, then how many segments do you need to send all 9 of them for each algo, draw a time space diagram

He made the midterms very tough

Final easier, but a lot of traps
Like ARP algorithm is link layer algorithm, the way each switch knows about where each node came from, how distance vector are not passed between nodes which don't have a direct edge (consider a 4 node graph)

comment_content:
Basically, just finish some after-class homework. The content is a bit similar to the undergraduate course, and it is not difficult.

Principles of computer network architectures and communication protocols; the OSI
reference model; switching and multiplexing techniques; data link, network, transport and
application layers; LAN and medium access protocols; network programming.
Prerequisite(s): COMP 3511; Exclusion(s): COMP 5621, ELEC 3120, ISOM 3180;
Background: Probability and statistics
comment_teaching: The content of the lecture is average, but fortunately, the assessment is not strict.
comment_workload: The overall pressure is not big, or even very small, if you just want to pass this course.
comment_grading: The score is also good. Most of them can be passed. Finish your homework.

comment_content: The content is about the basic knowledge of network, even if you have never learnt about the network.
comment_teaching: The teaching is clear with many examples and comparisons. It's easy to understand. The instructor is nice.
comment_workload: There is no coding project, just assignments with some TF, selection, and 1-2 short-answer questions. The quiz is also in the format of time-limited assignment. The workload is not heavy.
comment_grading: The grading is normal. I got the score above the average in the assignments, quiz and exams, and I got a B+.

comment_content: Include application layer, transport layer, network layer and link layer, ranging from small local area networks (LANs) to vast global networks like the Internet, enabling seamless communication and collaboration. The link layer was not introduced in class.
comment_teaching: Very good, as it is detailed and demonstrates extensive thinking and depth of understanding. It effectively conveys a thorough analysis and reflects a strong grasp of the subject matter.
comment_workload: Only three assignments without presentation or project. The assignments were very easy and it took about one hour to complete each.
comment_grading: The final exam was easy while the middle-term was difficult, but the final grade was acceptable.

comment_content: 整体上课一般，可能需要课后自己多学习；上课内容整体简单，但是期中和期末考试的内容beyond the content in class.
comment_teaching: 老师上课比较慢，这次都没上完，最后给video自己看
comment_workload: 比较小，但是考试很难
comment_grading: 高分别想，但是过B，把要求都做了基本都能拿到

comment_content: The course covered basic concepts of computer networking, which were quite similar to what I learned during my undergraduate studies. For students already familiar with networking fundamentals, the content was easy to grasp. However, it could have benefited from more advanced topics to challenge students further.
comment_teaching: The instructor's teaching was adequate, though I did not attend many sessions due to the introductory nature of the material. The lectures were clear but lacked the depth that would engage students more interested in exploring advanced aspects of networking.
comment_workload: The workload was light, mainly consisting of homework assignments and an exam. The assignments were not particularly difficult and didn’t require extensive effort, making it manageable for students balancing multiple courses. Overall, the course stress level was low.

For example, missing a small detail like multiplying by two for the full-duplex and half-duplex concepts could result in significant point deductions. This felt a bit unreasonable, as it emphasized memorization over understanding.

He uses a variety of teaching methods, including interactive discussions, real-world examples, and thought-provoking questions, to stimulate deep thinking and foster a dynamic learning environment.

comment_content:
The course is structured into classical cryptography (about 2/3) and cyber security (about 1/3).

The course syllabus consists of the following topics:
• History of cryptography
• Design and analysis of one-key ciphers
• Public-key cryptography
• Partially homomorphic encryption
• Hash functions, keyed hash functions
• Digital signature
• Data origin authentication
• Data integrity
• Nonrepudiation
• Anonymity on the Internet
• Secret sharing
• Key management and public key infrastructure
• Cryptographic protocols
• Email security
• Web security
• Network security
• Distributed systems security
• Firewalls
• Virtual private networks.  

The reason for my bad content grading is that the course content seems outdated and a lot of interesting more recent topics are completely missing, for example:
• Fully homomorphic encryption
• Zero-knowledge proofs (e.g. SNARKs)
• Multi-party computation
comment_teaching: Instructor didn't seem to have a lot of excitement which might be as a result of teaching the course for so many years. He basically reads out the lecture slides. However, he is happy to answer any questions patiently.
comment_workload:
There were 4 assignments in total, each can be solved in several hours. Some assignments require a good foundation in number theory, which you are required to learn by yourself if you are not familiar with it.
For the exam, a good understanding of the course material was necessary, but the final exam was open book so the workload for final exam preparation was low.
comment_grading:
• Lecture attendance 6%
• Four assignments 44%
• Final exam 50%

Grading is fairly generous. I would consider this course to be an "easy A" course.

The final exam was open book, i.e. you were allowed to bring a laptop in offline mode so you could use any downloaded or paper documents.

comment_content: The contents provide good introduction to basic security concepts and standard (but quite old) cryptographic primitives, as well as some antiquated real world security systems like PGP and IPSec. Introduction to more state of the art security and cryptography research such as ZKP and post-quantum primitives would have been appreciated.
comment_teaching: Lectures are good, with some minor interactions with students in class. Lectures are easy to understand, but deeper mathematical concepts are skipped over in favor of higher level presentation of security systems.
comment_workload: Attend lecture. Spend about 30 minutes per week on assignments. Study a little for final exam. This is an easy course.
comment_grading:
Assignments - Easy to get full score
Attendance - Just attend most classes
Final Exam - Easy final exam, mostly non-mathematical problems. Exam is open book, and computer notes are allowed (no internet - wifi must be turned off)

comment_content:
First half is on cryptographic primitives (symmetric key cipher, assymetric key cipher (RSA), Digital Signature Scheme (DSS), secret sharing scheme, etc). Second half is on real-world security system (IPSec, IKEv2, Keyberos, SSL, SSH, etc).

Surprisingly, there is no formal treatment to security.

I personally dislike the second part and would rather want it to be on some modern crytographic tools like zero-knowledge proof, blockchain, etc...
comment_teaching: The professor is experienced and nice but his intonation is not the best among CSE. The professor mentioned that he tried to make the course less technical this semester. I am not sure what changed, but it's still somewhat technical, not in the sense that the mathamatics is difficult, but the fact that the lecture talks about some intricate details of some protocols (e.g., which protocol must support AES, which protocol do not require both sides to support any cipher, which protocol needs an alert protocol, and which modes does a protocol have, etc......). Those are actually not that interesting for beginners.
comment_workload: The workload is very tiny. Only 4 assignments which are quite easy. However, I actually hoped that there are more assignments given so that I can understand the protocols in real world security system better, especially on IPSec, which is quite technical.
comment_grading: The grading is promised to be nice. The final examination is not difficult. I personally think that this course is a popular choice for PG only because it's an easy course and the grading is nice...

comment_content:
First half: Cryptography

1. One key (symmetric) cipher
1.1 One-key block cipher
1.2 Design Idea
1.3 AES
1.4 Stream Cipher
1.5 Key Management

2. Public key cipher
2.1 Introduction
2.2 RSA and ElGamal
2.3 PKI
2.4 Homomorphic Encryption

3. Hashing
3.1 Keyed hash function
3.2 SHA

4. Security Protocols
5. DSS
6. Secret Sharing

Second half: Network Security

7. Application layer
7.1 PGP
7.2 Kerberos

8. Transport layer
8.1 SSL
8.2 SSH

9. Network Layer
9.1 IPSec, AH, ESP
9.2 IKEv2

10. Other
10.1 VPN
10.2 Firewall
10.3 Anonymity (Onion routing, etc.)

I like the first half. The design of lecture notes is very clean and easy to follow. Some contents are insightful, especially the lecture about homomorphic encryption. To understand the content of this part, it suffices to know about the number theory part in COMP2711 (plus a little bit of quadratic residue and discrete indices) and some basic concepts in abstract algebra, such as the Galois field (which is kind of an abstraction of modular arithmetic).

I don't quite like the second part, however. The lecture slides contain many subtle details about each protocol, but explanation is not so detailed, and sometimes even lacking. You need to hear the professor's words carefully in order to understand. The contents did not provide much insight, but just some plain details, unfortunately. If there is more focus on attacking these protocols, like COMP3632, then it might be better.
comment_teaching: Although very detailed, the teaching pace is very slow, and the tone makes the lecture so boring. After attending 10 lectures, I cannot bear anymore and skip all other lectures.
comment_workload:
3 Assignments
Don't know how to do? Google or find source

May need 1 to 2 days to finish

Not much to prepare. You can even prepare the exam during the exam time, if you want :)   I actually only watch the relevant part of lecture video not until the 4-hour exam.

Btw. Professor always emphasizes that this course is tough and many maths are involved. Don't be scared by this statement. Even an undergraduate student like me can handle this course quite handily.
comment_grading:
3 Assignments: 45%
Project: 20%
Final Exam: 35%

The three assignments basically ask you some simple proofs (or attack) and some concepts which can be subtle or tricky. The proofs and attacks are similar to proofs in number theory, so if you are comfortable with number theory, then they are kind of trivial. However, you might need sources and Google to answer the conceptual questions. Assignment 3 did not have source and IMO is the trickiest one.

Project basically asks you to compare public key cryptography with identity-based encryption. You don't really need to understand the technical details to write a good report, although it is interesting to dive deeper. Technical details might involve some number theory which is a liitle bit more advanced than those in the lecture (e.g. Jacobi and Legendre symbol), and also ellpitic curve cryptography.

Final Exam consists of only 9 MCs in 4 hours, but they are quite tricky. Some MC actually requires you to do proof, but it is still all-or-nothing because if you got the proof wrong in the halfway, then you got the wrong choice anyway. Other conceptual/factual questions are tricky in the way that you have to read the statement and comprehend the concept very carefully. Even though there is sufficient time for you to think carefully and repeatedly, it is still difficult to get a correct choice.

The grading scheme is really good. Basically everyone could obtain at least 90% in assignments and project, so the final mainly determines your grade. Even if you got only 1 answer correct, you could still get a B. Yes, B seems to be the lowest grade you can get other than F. Roughly speaking, the median grade is A-, and it can be obtained by answering 5 questions correctly. However, gettting A and A+ are difficult, and you need to answer at least 7-8 questions correctly in order to obtain it.
